Title: Pound (move)

This page focuses on the move "Pound." For information regarding the unit of weight, refer to the "List of Pokémon by weight." Known in Japanese as はたく (Pound), this Normal-type damaging attack made its debut in Generation I. Its sole function is to cause damage, lacking any additional effects. Except for Generation IV, King's Rock influences Pound across all other generations. Within Generation III Pokémon Contests, Pound serves as an initial move in a combination, enabling DoubleSlap, Faint Attack, and Slam to achieve twice their usual appeal when performed immediately afterward.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Jigglypuff employs Pound as its neutral special attack, transitioning to its side special starting with Super Smash Bros. Melee. This move primarily functions as an alternative method for recovery, given that Jigglypuff's up special, Sing, offers no assistance in regaining height. Furthermore, Pound deals additional shield damage, making it an effective tool for swiftly shattering opponents' shields.

In the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer, specifically within the segment titled "A Conk on Cranidos's Cranium," Platinum's Piplup was noted to possess knowledge of Pound, though its actual usage was never depicted. Similarly, Professor Birch's Treecko was officially stated on the website to know Pound, yet he was similarly never observed performing the attack.

Among all Generation I moves, Pound holds the distinction of being the first in terms of index number sequence. Before the 1.2.0 update for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Pound also occupied index positions 875 and 876. These entries served as temporary placeholders for the attacks Psyblade and Hydro Steam, respectively. Finally, when utilized within the Distortion World in Pokémon Platinum, the move Secret Power adopts Pound's animation.

Title: Karate Chop (move)

First appearing in Generation I, Karate Chop (known in Japanese as からてチョップ Karate Chop) is an offensive Fighting-type attack. Before Generation II, however, its type was Normal. This move inflicts damage and inherently possesses a heightened chance of landing a critical hit. In Pokémon Contest combinations, Karate Chop serves as a valid second move, granting its user an additional 3 appeal points if Focus Energy was employed during the preceding turn. Similarly, within a Contest Spectacular combination, it awards three extra appeal points when Focus Energy was performed in the turn before. Its core effect involves causing damage and having an elevated critical hit probability, which stands at 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad game series. However, despite its description, Karate Chop mistakenly lacked its boosted critical hit rate within the Rescue Team series. During the episode 'The Cave of Mirrors!', Mirror Ash instructed his Hawlucha to execute Karate Chop; however, due to his inherent timidity preventing an attack, Hawlucha was never observed performing the technique. Within the roster of moves featured in Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, Karate Chop stands as one of four moves whose type was altered in Generation II, alongside Gust, Bite, and Sand Attack. All of these moves were classified as Normal-type during Generation I.

Title: Double Slap (move)

Double Slap (Japanese: おうふくビンタ Round Trip Slap) was designated as DoubleSlap before Pokémon X and Y, and is a damage-dealing Normal-type multistrike attack introduced in the first generation. DoubleSlap delivers damage by striking the target between 2 and 5 times per usage. Initially, there was a 37.5% chance for 2 hits, a 37.5% chance for 3 hits, a 12.5% chance for 4 hits, and a 12.5% chance for 5 hits. Although only its initial strike could land as a critical hit, all subsequent blows would inflict the identical amount of damage. Solely in its debut generation, DoubleSlap would cease immediately upon shattering a substitute. Bide and Counter would only register the move's final strike.

Later, all individual strikes began to deal damage independently, allowing any of them to be critical hits. DoubleSlap subsequently gained the ability to continue attacking even after breaking a substitute. If the user held a King's Rock, the concluding strike had a chance to cause the opponent to flinch. When the target possesses an Ability that activates upon contact, each strike is counted separately, enabling the Ability to trigger multiple times. Pokémon endowed with the Skill Link Ability will unfailingly hit five times when executing DoubleSlap, provided it does not miss. If the user is equipped with a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every consecutive hit possesses an equal chance to induce flinching in the foe.

DoubleSlap is usable as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Pound was previously used. The hit distribution was later modified to a 35% chance for 2 hits, 35% for 3 hits, 15% for 4 hits, and 15% for 5 hits. The average cumulative base power, factoring in the inherent 15% miss chance, now totals 39.5. Should a Focus Sash activate before the final strike is delivered, the next strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still repeatedly prevent fainting, but each opportunity is distinct. Sturdy will activate with every hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. If the target possesses Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will trigger these Abilities. Should the target be holding a Rocky Helmet, the user receives damage for each strike.

Double Slap inflicts damage by hitting the target 2-5 times per use. Every hit undergoes a separate accuracy check; however, the move will proceed even if a strike fails. In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's critical-hit rate is reduced to 2%. Starting in Super Mystery Dungeon, the move concludes if a Reviver Seed activates. In "The Escape," Green's Jigglypuff, Jiggly, was confirmed to know Double Slap by her Pokédex. Within Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, despite its moniker, this move, like most multistrike attacks, can connect with the opponent up to five times. Nevertheless, its Japanese, Chinese, Korean, and French names do not denote a specific number of strikes.

Title: Comet Punch (move)

For those seeking the attack known as Comet Punch in Japanese, please refer to Meteor Mash. Comet Punch (Japanese: れんぞくパンチ Consecutive Punch) is a damage-dealing Normal-type multistrike move that made its debut in Generation I. It is assigned as TM108 within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used, Comet Punch inflicts damage by striking the target between two and five times. Historically, its probability distribution for hits was 37.5% for two strikes, 37.5% for three, 12.5% for four, and 12.5% for five. Initially, only the first strike could land as a critical hit, with subsequent hits dealing identical damage. Previously, the move would conclude immediately upon destroying a substitute. Bide and Counter calculate their damage solely based on the last strike.

Currently, each individual strike holds an independent chance of being a critical hit. The move is now capable of continuing its assault even after shattering a target's substitute. Should Comet Punch trigger a Focus Band held by the target, no subsequent blows can cause a knockout. At one point, if the user held a King's Rock, only the final strike possessed a chance to inflict flinching upon the opponent. Every single strike independently has a chance to activate an Ability that triggers upon physical contact. Should Effect Spore induce sleep in the attacking Pokémon, the move will cease instantly. Similarly, if the target's Focus Sash is activated, no further strikes from Comet Punch are capable of knocking out the Pokémon.

Subsequently, when holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every consecutive hit gained an equal probability of causing the opponent to flinch. The probabilities for its number of hits were later updated to: 35% for two hits, 35% for three, 15% for four, and 15% for five. Its average cumulative base power, factoring in a 15% inherent miss chance, is now 47.4. In specific scenarios, should a Focus Sash trigger prior to the final blow being landed, the very next strike will subsequently cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains the capability to repeatedly activate and prevent fainting, with each instance of activation being independent. If the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1, the Ability Sturdy will activate with every single hit. Every individual strike of Comet Punch will successfully trigger Abilities like Weak Armor, Stamina, or an item such as a Rocky Helmet. However, defensive moves like Spiky Shield and King's Shield will only activate a single time per use of Comet Punch. The Ability Parental Bond specifically does not activate when Comet Punch is employed.

From the Mega Dimension DLC onward, Comet Punch once again becomes a selectable option. Its base power received an increase, elevating from 18 to 30. In specific game instances, Comet Punch invariably delivers five hits. Furthermore, when utilized as a Plus Move, it unfailingly connects six times. Generally, Comet Punch delivers damage by striking its target between two and five times per application. Although each individual hit undergoes a distinct accuracy check, the move will persist even if a particular strike misses. Its critical-hit ratio is diminished, specifically being 1% within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series. In Super Mystery Dungeon, if a Reviver Seed becomes active, the execution of the move will conclude. During Mountain Time, Benji's Ledian received the command to use Comet Punch, but was unable to perform the action. In the main series of games, Comet Punch stands as one of only two moves with a base power not divisible by 5, the other being Fury Swipes. Pin Missile previously constituted a third such move until its base power was elevated to 25 in Generation VI. Kangaskhan's Pokédex entry indicates its proficiency in executing attacks with Comet Punch.

Title: Mega Punch (move)

Known in Japanese as メガトンパンチ (Megaton Punch), Mega Punch is an offensive Normal-type technique that debuted in Generation I. It served as TM01 in Generation I and was later designated TM00 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Sharing the same type and a comparable name, it functions as a conceptual counterpart to Mega Kick. This move deals direct damage and lacks any secondary effects. In Pokémon Contest combinations, Mega Punch can be utilized as the second action, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Focus Energy or Mind Reader was performed on the preceding turn. In the episode "Charizard Chills," Ash instructed his Charizard to use Mega Punch, but the Pokémon disobediently declined to execute the command. A translation error in the English dub of "A Tyrogue Full of Trouble" depicted Kiyo ordering his Hitmonchan to use Mega Punch; however, in the original Japanese rendition, he had commanded Hitmonchan to use Mach Punch. Notably, Pokémon such as Torchic and Togekiss are able to learn Mega Punch despite being devoid of visible arms.

Title: Pay Day (move)

Originating in Generation I, Pay Day (Japanese: ネコにこばん, or "Coin for a Cat") is a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage. It served as the exclusive signature attack for Meowth and Persian during Generations II and III, a distinction that continued solely for Meowth from Generations IV through VI. This move is also known as TM57 within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!.

Beyond inflicting damage, Pay Day causes coins to be strewn across the battlefield. The quantity of these scattered coins scales directly with the attacking Pokémon's level. Players collect all scattered coins, receiving the corresponding money, provided they secure victory in the battle or terminate a wild encounter using a move that compels the opponent to flee (like Roar or Dragon Tail). The money obtained from Pay Day doubles if any of the player's Pokémon participates in the battle while equipped with an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense; this bonus, however, is a one-time application, unaffected by multiple Pokémon holding these items. Possessing several Pokémon with these items does not yield any additional monetary increase.

Coins are not collected if either combatant flees or uses Teleport, unless the player themselves forces a wild Pokémon to flee. Similarly, no coins are gathered if a wild Pokémon concludes the battle with a move that forces the player to escape. Collection also fails in link battles, Max Raid Battles, Tera Raid Battles, or any battle facility engagements.

Typically, Pay Day causes coins to scatter, with a value equivalent to twice the user's level at the moment of execution, maxing out at 200 coins for a level 100 user. Initially, from Generation I to IV, Pay Day would scatter coins upon striking a substitute, but notably failed to do so if the attack caused the substitute to break. However, a change in Generations V and VI meant Pay Day no longer scattered coins when hitting a substitute, even if the substitute was destroyed. Subsequently, starting from Generation VII, Pay Day once again scatters coins, even when the move successfully shatters a substitute.

Within Generation I, a hard cap of $999,999 is imposed on the total Pay Day coins collectible per battle. Beyond this threshold, while the game continues to indicate coin scattering with each Pay Day use, the accumulated coin value within the game's system will not increment further. Upon the battle's conclusion in Generation I, the player retrieves all coins dispersed by both their own Pokémon and any opposing Pokémon.

From Generation II onwards, the move's coin output was revised to five times the user's level per use. Furthermore, players can only collect coins generated by their own Pokémon. Should the user gain a level during battle, the collected money amount is now computed based on the elevated level, rather than the level at which Pay Day was initially executed. When a Pokémon with the Parental Bond ability utilizes Pay Day (e.g., via Skill Swap), coins are only scattered on the initial hit. The move Happy Hour further amplifies the money collected, as does Prize Money Power.

For users exceeding level 100—a state not typically permissible in standard gameplay but achievable through methods like the old man glitch—Pay Day functions as normal up to level 127, dispensing a maximum of $254 per usage. At level 0, this move would scatter $0, though no corresponding message appears. Yet, if the user's level falls between 128 and 255, Pay Day instead scatters money calculated as (level−128)×2. Examples include scattering $0 at level 128, $100 at level 178, and $254 at level 255. Pokémon exceeding level 100 cannot be transferred from Generation I to Generation II, as a message indicates the Pokémon is abnormal; however, such a Pokémon can be acquired in Generation II via arbitrary code execution, like the Coin Case glitch.

In contrast to Generations I and II, Generation III onward sees Pay Day award the anticipated money for Levels 101-255 (five times the level, reaching a maximum of $1275 per use). Consistent with prior generations, a level 0 user would scatter $0, without any message notification. Pokémon above level 100 can be acquired using arbitrary code execution methods, such as viewing a glitch Pokémon's animation or employing a glitch move from Glitzer Popping. A minor glitch in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire causes an incorrect money amount to be displayed when an Amulet Coin is held (affecting Levels 1-100 as well). Despite this, the accurate sum is still received post-battle. This display error was rectified starting with Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen and in all games thereafter.

Additionally, Pay Day inflicts damage. Should an opponent be defeated by this move, it will drop Poké (in addition to any held item). This particular effect does not activate if the target is recruited. The secondary effect of Pay Day does not manifest in all dungeons; for instance, it is absent in the mazes of Super Mystery Dungeon. In the Super Smash Bros. series (including Super Smash Bros., Super Smash Bros. Brawl, Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate), a Meowth emerging from a Poké Ball employs Pay Day to launch coins at adversaries.

The Japanese appellation for the move is an idiom, signifying "to cast pearls before swine." Visually, the move's animation features koban, gold coins from feudal Japan's Edo period, identical to the coin prominently displayed on Meowth's head. During Generation I, Pay Day represented one of merely three non-glitch methods for acquiring currency after all Trainers had been vanquished, the others being selling items (obtained using Coins after all other items were sold) and re-engaging the Elite Four and Champion. Within Generation I battles, Pay Day could facilitate earning up to $999,999. Achieving this maximum, however, necessitated deploying the move a minimum of 5000 times within a single battle. An example strategy for reaching the monetary cap in this generation involves a Mew equipped with Pay Day, Rest, and Transform confronting a wild Ditto. After Ditto assumes Mew's form, both Pokémon will possess an identical moveset, encompassing Pay Day, alongside Rest for HP recovery. The opponent inherently possesses infinite PP, while the player's Pokémon can perpetually replenish its own PP by repeatedly transforming into the adversary and re-acquiring its moves.

Title: Fire Punch (move)

Known in Japanese as ほのおのパンチ (Flame Punch), Fire Punch is a Fire-type attack from Generation I that inflicts damage. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it is designated as TM31. It shares thematic and functional similarities with Ice Punch and Thunder Punch, serving as a direct counterpart to these moves.

Within Pokémon Contests, utilizing Fire Punch as an initial move in a combination grants double the standard appeal points for Ice Punch or ThunderPunch if executed on the subsequent turn. Conversely, when concluding a combination, the user secures an additional 4 appeal points if Ice Punch, Sunny Day, or ThunderPunch preceded it.

This move's Power Points (PP) saw a reduction from 15 to 10, while its likelihood of inflicting a burn on the target significantly rose from 10% to 30%. Fire Punch deals damage and holds a possibility of leaving the target burned. Before its update to Version 2.10.0 on June 28, 2021, its in-game description noted it "Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned."

In the narrative "A Cheeky Charizard Change-Up I," Crystal's Alakazam was stated to possess Fire Punch, though its actual usage was never depicted. Certain Pokédex entries describe Emboar as capable of "throw[ing] a fire punch." Curiously, this claim first appeared in Pokémon Black, even though Emboar could not learn Fire Punch itself until the release of Pokémon Black 2 and White 2. The Japanese Pokédex entries render "fire punch" as 「ほのおの パンチ」 (or 「炎の パンチ」 in its kanji form). This phrasing is nearly identical to 「ほのおのパンチ」, the Japanese title for the move Fire Punch, differing only by an inserted space. Likewise, English Pokédex text presents "fire punch" in lowercase, contrasting with the title case used for specific moves such as "Fire Punch."

Title: Ice Punch (move)

Hailing from Generation I, Ice Punch (Japanese: れいとうパンチ Freezing Punch) is an offensive, damage-dealing Ice-type move. It appears as TM35 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Regarded as a counterpart to Fire Punch and Thunder Punch, it shares similar names and effects. Beyond dealing damage, Ice Punch previously carried a 10% chance of freezing the target.

Starting with Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), Ice Punch underwent revisions: its PP was reduced from 15 to 10, and it gained a 30% chance of inflicting frostbite, the modern replacement for the frozen status. Prior to this update, specifically before June 28, 2021, official descriptions stated the move 'deals damage and may leave the target frozen' and that it 'Has a very small chance of leaving the target frozen.'

In Pokémon Contests, Ice Punch can initiate a combination, causing Fire Punch and ThunderPunch to score double appeal if used next. Alternatively, it can finish a combination, awarding the user 4 bonus appeal points if Fire Punch or ThunderPunch was used previously.

Crystal's Alakazam was mentioned knowing Ice Punch in the manga 'A Cheeky Charizard Change-Up I,' though it was never seen using the move. During the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire pre-release, Blaziken was depicted using Ice Punch. However, the Official Japanese Pokémon website later issued an apology after the games' launch, confirming Blaziken could not, in fact, learn the move.

Title: Thunder Punch (move)

This Electric-type attack, which deals damage, debuted in Generation I. Before Pokémon X and Y, its name appeared as ThunderPunch. Its Japanese name is かみなりパンチ, also translated as Thunder Punch. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it functions as TM23. With comparable names and effects, it serves as a conceptual counterpart to Ice Punch and Fire Punch.

Initially, Thunder Punch inflicted damage and carried a 10% probability of leaving the target paralyzed. During this period, Electric-type Pokémon could not be paralyzed by the move. Later, it gained the ability to paralyze Electric-type Pokémon. However, Thunder Punch once again became unable to paralyze Electric-type Pokémon due to their renewed immunity to paralysis. Its Power Points (PP) were decreased from 15 to 10, and its chance of paralyzing the opponent was increased from 10% to 30%. Presently, the move delivers damage and holds the potential to leave the target paralyzed. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021), its in-game description read: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target paralyzed."

In Pokémon Contests, using Thunder Punch as the first move in a combination causes Fire Punch and Ice Punch to score double the standard appeal when executed on the subsequent turn. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Charge, Fire Punch, or Ice Punch was utilized in the preceding turn. Within Contest Spectaculars, linking Thunder Punch with Charge in the prior turn awards the user an additional three appeal points.

Within "To Master the Onixpected!", a videotape viewed by Ash and Brock depicted Bruno instructing his Hitmonchan to employ Thunder Punch; however, Misty shut off the television before the move's performance was depicted. Melmetal is internally coded to acquire this move upon evolving in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Nevertheless, this cannot occur because Meltan is unable to evolve in the core series.

Title: Scratch (move)

Originating in Generation I, the Normal-type attack known as Scratch (ひっかく in Japanese) deals damage to its target. It serves as a straightforward offensive maneuver, lacking any additional effects beyond its direct damage output. Within Pokémon Contests, Scratch has utility as an initial step in a combination; employing it causes subsequent uses of Fury Swipes or Slash on the following turn to yield twice the standard appeal points. Conversely, it can conclude a combination, granting the user an extra 4 appeal points when preceded by Leer in the immediately prior turn. During the episode "Classroom Training!", Jeremiah issued a command for his Skuntank to employ Scratch; however, the Pokémon was rendered incapable of performing the attack, having been immobilized by James's Carnivine's Vine Whip. Furthermore, in "Zap!! A Rising Beam of Light," Henry stated that his Grookey, Twiggy, possessed knowledge of Scratch, though there has been no visual confirmation of him utilizing it. The official website listed Maizie's Teddiursa as knowing Scratch, yet her Pokémon has likewise never been depicted performing the attack.

Title: Guillotine (move)

Originating in Generation I, Guillotine (known in Japanese as ハサミギロチン, or Pincer Guillotine) is a Normal-type attack renowned for its one-hit knockout potential. When successful, this move consistently deals precisely 65535 HP in damage. It possesses a base accuracy of 30%. A direct hit from Guillotine will shatter a target's Substitute. In contrast to other attacks that disregard the standard damage formula, Guillotine respects type immunities, rather than circumventing them. It fails to impact any foe whose current Speed statistic surpasses that of the user. Presently, Guillotine inflicts damage equivalent to the opponent's current HP.

Its accuracy is now determined by a specific formula: the attack invariably misses if the user's level is lower than the target's (with Speed having no bearing on success). When the user's level exceeds the target's by 90 or more, Guillotine achieves 100% accuracy (even if calculations would yield a higher percentage for differences of 91+). This formula establishes an approximate 30% (76256) accuracy at equal levels, increasing by roughly 0.78% (2256) for each level the user is above the target. This calculated accuracy is influenced by both the user's accuracy and the target's evasion stats.

Notwithstanding its listed 30% accuracy, Guillotine's actual hit chance is determined by an alternative formula: it begins at 30% when both Pokémon share the same level, increasing by 1% for every level the user surpasses the target. Should the user be at least 70 levels superior, Guillotine becomes a guaranteed hit. Conversely, if the user's level is inferior to the target's, the move always fails. This particular hit chance is distinct from conventional accuracy; it remains unaffected by accuracy/evasion stats, and immune to modifiers like Gravity, Wide Lens, and Compound Eyes.

Furthermore, it cannot strike Pokémon in a semi-invulnerable state. Nevertheless, abilities and effects that ensure move hits, such as No Guard and Lock-On, will influence Guillotine's success, provided the user's level is not below the target's.

In Pokémon Contests, Guillotine serves as a viable second move in a combination, awarding the user 2 additional appeal points if ViceGrip was executed during the preceding turn. Moreover, it participates in Contest Spectacular combinations, yielding an extra three appeal points when Lock-On, Mind Reader, or Miracle Eye were employed in the turn before. During Max Raid Battles, if directed at a Dynamax Pokémon protected by a mysterious barrier, Guillotine will consume two segments of the barrier's durability. Otherwise, this move invariably fails when used against Dynamax Pokémon. Tera Raid bosses are impervious to Guillotine.

This attack eliminates the opponent in a single blow. Before the game Gates to Infinity, Guillotine's knockout mechanism involved inflicting "calamitous damage" (Japanese: とてつもない タメージ, or tremendous damage), which the game internally registered as 9999 damage. Furthermore, it proved ineffective against Ghost-type Pokémon.

In the narrative *Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming*, Emerald's Pokédex disclosed that a Pinsir, on loan from the Battle Factory, was proficient in Guillotine. Interestingly, both the Axew and Pawniard evolutionary families, alongside Kartana, are capable of learning Guillotine, despite none of these seven Pokémon possessing the pincers implied as requisite by the move's description and its Japanese name. Conversely, the animated series depicts Pawniard and Bisharp executing this move by assailing their adversaries with their sharp blades.

Apart from Smeargle, no other Pokémon capable of learning Guillotine can also learn any of the alternative one-hit knockout attacks. Moreover, unlike its one-hit knockout counterparts, Guillotine is unique in that no Pokémon sharing its Normal type can ever learn it.

Title: Razor Wind (move)

For the exploration group featured in the Mystery Dungeon titles, refer to Team (Mystery Dungeon)#Team Razor Wind. Known as かまいたち (Kamaitachi) in Japanese, Razor Wind is a Normal-type attacking technique first seen in Generation I. It served as TM02 during Generation I and will be TM145 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon selection, Razor Wind initiates a charging phase, displaying the message "<Pokémon> made a whirlwind!" and having no other immediate effect. During the subsequent turn, the move executes, dealing damage, consuming PP, and being registered as the most recently utilized attack. After choosing Razor Wind, the Pokémon cannot be switched out until its execution is either completed or interrupted.

Initially, Razor Wind possessed an accuracy rating of 75%. Should the move fail to execute completely, no PP will be expended, nor will it be considered the last move performed. If an opponent employs Mirror Move during the turn Razor Wind's user generates its whirlwind, Mirror Move will replicate the attack the user performed directly prior to Razor Wind (or fail if no such move exists). Conditions like sleep, freezing, binding, and flinching merely postpone Razor Wind's execution without canceling it. In Pokémon Stadium, Mirror Move can copy Razor Wind during either of its two turns of execution.

Subsequently, Razor Wind's critical hit ratio was enhanced. Later, if its success was hindered by ailments like flinching, paralysis, confusion, or sleep, the entire move would fail outright instead of just being paused. Its accuracy was then standardized to 100%. However, it no longer possessed an increased critical hit ratio. The charging message was altered to "<Pokémon> whipped up a whirlwind!". When used in multi-opponent encounters, it targets all adjacent adversaries. The move's critical hit ratio was re-increased at a later point. Holding a Power Herb allows the user to execute Razor Wind in a single turn.

From the Mega Dimension DLC onward, Razor Wind became selectable once more. It was subsequently reclassified as a multistrike move with 40 power, characterized by a lengthy wind-up but unleashing 5 tracking projectiles. As a Plus Move, it releases 6 projectiles instead. The user acquires the "Razor Wind" status for one turn before unleashing the attack on the subsequent turn.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's damage output is doubled upon final calculation. It also features an elevated critical hit rate (50% in the Rescue Team series, 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series). Its two-turn nature prevents it from being linked with other moves. Furthermore, Razor Wind is unusable within Pokémon Towers in the Adventure Squad series.

Despite its in-game description in FireRed and LeafGreen claiming an increased critical hit ratio, Razor Wind actually lacked such a bonus in Generation III. Curiously, despite its Generation I debut, no Pokémon learned Razor Wind by leveling up until Generation III. In Generation II, players could only teach Razor Wind to Pokémon by trading with a Generation I game. Among all special moves, Razor Wind holds the distinction of having the lowest index number. The Japanese appellation, Kamaitachi, directly references the Japanese yōkai that shares its name. This yōkai is a weasel-like creature, often portrayed with keen claws, riding on wind gusts, and inflicting cuts on people's legs. Ironically, Sneasel, a Pokémon inspired by this yōkai, cannot learn Razor Wind. In Generation VIII, Razor Wind stands as one of only two charging-turn moves rendered unusable, the other being Sky Drop.

Title: Swords Dance (move)

This Normal-type attack, known as つるぎのまい (Swords Dance) in Japanese, was first introduced in the game's inaugural generation and inflicts no direct damage. It served as TM75 from the fourth generation up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is also present as TM75 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When utilized, it provides a two-stage enhancement to the user's Attack stat. This boost consequently elevates the user's offensive capabilities for a duration of five turns. More specifically, it causes the user's Attack stat to rise by two levels. In Pokémon Contests, initiating a combination with Swords Dance allows Crabhammer, Crush Claw, Cut, False Swipe, Fury Cutter, and Slash to achieve twice their usual appeal score when performed on the subsequent turn. Activating its Z-Power with a Normalium Z transforms it into Z-Swords Dance, which additionally resets all of the user's reduced stats. Snatch can intercept this technique.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, status-altering techniques like Swords Dance were exclusively accessible to opposing Pokémon, never becoming available on those captured. Boss Aegislash was observed employing Swords Dance. Before version 2.38.0, its designation as a buddy move was B Swords Dance. The anime episode "ZigZag Zangoose" depicted Nicholai instructing his Zangoose to perform Swords Dance, an action it ceased upon detecting Jessie's Seviper. The work Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King is mentioned; in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Battle CD 07 features a Machamp possessing Swords Dance, despite it being a move Machamp is normally incapable of learning. Before Generation V, Shedinja could obtain Swords Dance via leveling up if its pre-evolution, Nincada, was permitted to evolve solely at level 25, the point at which Ninjask acquires this particular move. Among all moves that alter a Pokémon's stats, Swords Dance possesses the highest number of variations, totaling eight when counting the original form.

Title: Cut (move)

This entry focuses on the battle move known as Cut. For information regarding its counterpart used in the overworld, please refer to Cut (Field Move).

Introduced in the first generation of Pokémon games, Cut (Japanese: いあいぎり Iai Cut) is a Normal-type attack that deals damage. Throughout Generations I to VI, it served as HM01, later becoming TM93 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. While its data exists within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the move cannot be chosen during combat, as no Pokémon currently available in those titles can acquire it. Nonetheless, Metronome possesses the capability to randomly select Cut. A Pokémon with the Sharpness ability will see Cut's power boosted by 50% when using it.

In various titles, Cut functions as a field move, necessitating possession of a specific Gym Badge for its out-of-battle application. These required badges include the Cascade Badge, Hive Badge, Stone Badge, Forest Badge, or Bug Badge. From Generations I through VI, and also accessible via the Hidden Moves Pokétch app, Cut's primary overworld function was to clear obstructive cuttable plants that impede the player's path. These cleared trees regenerate either upon the player's departure from the current area or upon resetting the game. Historically, in certain generations, players could only remove such vegetation by selecting the move directly from the party menu. Subsequently, an alternative method emerged: players could simply interact with the plants and press the A button, which would prompt a dialogue inquiring about cutting the plant.

Presently, Cut is no longer usable outside of battle, with the singular exception of its activation through the Hidden Moves Pokétch app in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, where it is used without being taught to a Pokémon. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, an alternative Secret Technique called Chop Down is employed to fell small trees. Cuttable plants themselves are absent from Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, Ultra Moon, Sword, Shield, Legends: Arceus, Scarlet, and Violet.

Across Generations I, II, and III, Cut could additionally be utilized to clear patches of tall grass. Within Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, a sealed cavern in Ruin Valley, marked with the braille word "CUT," can be opened by applying the move Cut to its entrance, granting passage to the Dotted Hole.

As a component of Pokémon Contest combinations, Cut serves as a suitable second move; performing it after Swords Dance in the preceding turn awards the user 2 bonus appeal points. In the game Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can learn Cut from a Scyther found in Withered Wasteland. This enables Ditto to slice through plants and wooden structures, and to harvest mature flora. Consuming any type of bread can temporarily enhance Cut's power in Pokopia, allowing Ditto to affect distant targets and sever even robust materials like chains and iron fences.

Bill's Farfetch'd was listed as knowing Cut on the official website, though no depiction of it using the move has ever been observed. A glitch in Generation I allows players to save and exit while standing on a previously cut tree; upon resuming, the player character will remain positioned on that same cut tree. This phenomenon is replicable with Pikachu in Pokémon Yellow, and can also occur with Pikachu in Celadon Gym in Yellow after specific battles.

Cut is not a mandatory move for completing the main storylines of Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, their subsequent remakes, Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, or Pokémon X and Y. The Japanese designation for Cut, いあいぎり (Iai Cut), draws its name from iaido, a Japanese martial art. This name specifically alludes to the martial art's technique of unsheathing a blade, striking an adversary, and then seamlessly re-sheathing the weapon. In the animated series, Cut held the distinction of being the inaugural HM move to be featured.

Title: Gust (move)

This entry focuses on the attack Gust. For information concerning the field maneuver, consult Gust (Field Move). Designated in Japanese as かぜおこし (Stir Up Wind), Gust is a damage-dealing Flying-type move, first appearing in Generation I. Before Generation II, its classification was Normal-type. Gust possesses the unique ability to strike a Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable turns of Fly and Bounce, applying double the damage if successful. Gust's effects are now influenced by King's Rock, with the exception of Generation IV. From Generation IV onwards, when targeting a Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly or Bounce, Gust's *power* is doubled instead of its *damage* being doubled (resulting in virtually the same effect). This particular interaction was extended to Sky Drop beginning in Generation V. In Triple Battles, Gust is capable of striking non-adjacent opponents. The move delivers damage. It has the capacity to target enemies in the air due to moves such as Fly or Bounce, and if it does, will deal double the damage. Within Pokémon GO, Gust functions as a Fast Attack, made accessible since November 5, 2020. Fantina's Drifblim was officially disclosed on the website as knowing Gust, although she was never depicted employing it. Among Flying-type moves, Gust holds the distinction of being the most commonly acquired via leveling up. Gust is one of four moves that underwent a type alteration in Generation II; the others are Karate Chop, Bite, and Sand Attack. All these moves were classified as Normal-type in Generation I. Despite its descriptive text indicating that the wind is generated by wings, certain Pokémon lacking wings are also capable of learning Gust. Gust stands alone as the sole special move possessing 35 PP, which represents the maximum PP count among all special moves.

Title: Wing Attack (move)

Originating in Generation I, the Flying-type attack known as Wing Attack (つばさでうつ Wing Strike in Japanese) is a damage-dealing technique. This particular move causes direct harm without any secondary effects. Initially possessing a power of 35, its power later increases to 60. In the unique context of Triple Battles, Wing Attack can strike foes not positioned immediately adjacent to the user. An oversight occurred exclusively within the Japanese release of Pokémon Stadium (the series' second Japanese installment), where Wing Attack's in-game description incorrectly identifies it as a Normal-type technique. During the episode 'Try for the Family Stone!', the character Mitchell made an unsuccessful endeavor to teach Wing Attack to his Murkrow. It currently holds the highest PP-to-power ratio among all moves, a record previously belonging to Extrasensory before its PP reduction in Generation VI.

Title: Whirlwind (move)

This entry pertains to the move Whirlwind. For information regarding the TFG starter set, please consult Whirlwind (TFG). Whirlwind (Japanese: ふきとばし, or "Blow Away") is a Normal-type move that does not inflict damage, first introduced in Generation I. It held the TM04 designation in Generation I and is assigned TM094 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A.

In wild Pokémon encounters, if either Pokémon successfully employs Whirlwind, the battle is instantly concluded. Conversely, in Trainer battles, Whirlwind has no functional effect. Initially, Whirlwind possessed 85% accuracy and standard priority. It could strike Pokémon even when they were in the semi-invulnerable phase of Dig. A probability of failure existed if the user's level was less than the target's level, ranging approximately from 10% to 25%, depending on the specific levels of both Pokémon. This chance of failure generally increased as the target's level surpassed the user's. Specifically, the failure chance was determined by the following formula: FailureChance=⌊Leveltarget4⌋Leveltarget+Leveluser+1.

From Generation II onward, in Trainer battles, Whirlwind now compels the target to switch with a randomly selected Pokémon from its Trainer's party. Should no other Pokémon be available for the target to switch with, Whirlwind will fail. The move's accuracy was boosted to 100%, though its priority was simultaneously reduced to -1. However, if Whirlwind was used before the opponent could make its move, it would fail. Whirlwind gained the ability to hit Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly, but could no longer affect those using Dig's semi-invulnerable turn. The level-dependent chance of failure no longer applied in Trainer battles. Nevertheless, in Trainer battles, Whirlwind invariably failed if executed prior to the opponent's action. The move would also consistently fail if used by the player against the Red Gyarados or in attempts to escape the forced wild Pokémon encounters at Team Rocket HQ. Whirlwind would fail when targeting Pokémon with the Ability Suction Cups or those rooted by Ingrain. It no longer failed simply for not being used last. It could also no longer strike Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly. The calculation for failure when the user's level is less than the target's was slightly altered; specifically, for a random integer X between 0 and 255, Whirlwind would fail if a certain relation was true [relation not provided in original text]. An approximate formula could estimate this failure chance, though this approximation might diverge moderately from the true probability for low user and target levels.

In subsequent generations, in wild Pokémon battles, Whirlwind now unfailingly misses if the user's level is lower than the target's. In Trainer battles, a successful hit by Whirlwind will now always succeed in its effect, regardless of either Pokémon's level. The move became susceptible to being reflected by Magic Coat and Magic Bounce. If a wild Pokémon battle features multiple combatants on both sides, Whirlwind will fail if a player's Pokémon attempts to use it on a wild opponent. However, if a wild Pokémon employs it against the player, it will compel the target to swap with another Pokémon from the Trainer's party, regardless of the target's level. Whirlwind now bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring a hit unless the target is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves such as Dig or Fly. It can now strike through Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, but is blocked by Crafty Shield. In Contest Spectaculars, Whirlwind forms part of a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if Spikes, Stealth Rock, or Toxic Spikes was utilized in the preceding turn. When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal into Z-Whirlwind, the user's Special Defense attribute increases by one stage. Using Whirlwind on a Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon will result in its failure.

In specific spin-off games (e.g., Pokémon Mystery Dungeon), Whirlwind targets the frontal enemy, propelling them back by 10 tiles. Should the affected foe collide with a wall, they sustain 5 HP of damage; if they strike another Pokémon, both take 5 HP damage. Any Pokémon pushed into an impassable tile will be warped away. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, status-altering techniques, including Whirlwind, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Pidgeotto and Pidgeot were observed employing Whirlwind. The description for Whirlwind, prior to Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), stated: "Forces the opponent's sync pair to switch out."

In the episode "The Ninja Poké-Showdown," Ash instructed his Bulbasaur to use Whirlwind, a move it cannot legitimately learn. This constitutes a dubbing error; in the original Japanese version, Ash merely commanded Bulbasaur to "blow away" Aya's Venonat's Stun Spore. During "All Things Bright and Beautifly!," Chaz asserted his Venomoth knew Whirlwind, yet its usage of the move was never depicted. A glitch in the Japanese editions of Pokémon Red, Green, and Blue caused the exclamation mark to partially overwrite the text box if the player's Pokémon used Whirlwind on a wild Pokémon possessing a 5-character name. This issue was rectified in Yellow with the addition of a line break. A glitch present in FireRed, LeafGreen, and Emerald would cause a roaming Pokémon that concluded a battle by deploying Whirlwind to be registered as defeated, leading to its permanent disappearance. Nevertheless, since Raikou, Entei, Suicune, Latias, and Latios are unable to learn Whirlwind, this scenario is impossible under normal gameplay conditions. The Nintendo Switch versions of FireRed and LeafGreen saw this glitch corrected. The Latin Spanish appellation for Whirlwind, Torbellino, corresponds to the European Spanish name for Whirlpool, and conversely (Remolino denotes Whirlwind in European Spanish and Whirlpool in Latin Spanish).

Title: Fly (move)
The attack known as Fly (そらをとぶ in Japanese) made its debut in Generation I as a Flying-type offensive maneuver. Its Technical Machine (TM) assignments include TM76 in Pokémon Sun and Moon and Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, TM14 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, and TM94 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

When Fly is chosen, the Pokémon ascends, entering a semi-invulnerable state; only Bide and Swift are capable of striking it during this phase. On the subsequent turn, Fly deals damage, consuming its Power Points (PP) and registering as the most recent action. Once initiated, the user cannot be switched out unless the move is either interrupted or brought to completion. Should Fly fail to execute fully, no PP is spent, and it does not count as the last move. If an adversary employs Mirror Move during the user's airborne turn, it replicates the move performed just prior to Fly's activation (or fails if no such move exists). Complete paralysis or self-inflicted confusion damage will interrupt Fly. Furthermore, if complete paralysis occurs while the Pokémon is semi-invulnerable during Fly, this status persists until the user either swaps out or fully completes the move.

Beginning with Pokémon Stadium, Fly enables the user to evade Bide. Full paralysis now causes the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly to reset. Mirror Move can duplicate Fly during either of its two execution turns. Additionally, Pokémon are susceptible to being captured even while in Fly's semi-invulnerable stage.

In this generation, Gust, Thunder, Twister, and Whirlwind are now capable of hitting the user during Fly's semi-invulnerable phase, with Gust and Twister dealing amplified (double) damage. A Pokémon airborne via Fly can also be struck if it was previously affected by Lock-On or Mind Reader, and then targeted on the subsequent turn, provided the attacking move is not Magnitude, Earthquake, or Fissure. Swift and Bide no longer independently connect during Fly's semi-invulnerable state. As of this generation, if a Pokémon ascends with Fly but the encounter concludes before it can descend (e.g., a wild Pokémon using Teleport or Self-Destruct), the PP for Fly is already consumed on the first turn, with no further PP deduction upon the move's second-turn impact. Exclusively within this generation, a glitch prevents Attract, Curse, Foresight, Mean Look, Mimic, Nightmare, Spider Web, and Transform from hitting targets in Fly's semi-invulnerable turn when Lock-On or Mind Reader are active. Furthermore, moves cannot decrease the stats of targets in this airborne state; status moves like String Shot will fail, and secondary effects from moves such as Bubble will not trigger.

This generation allows Sky Uppercut to strike the user during Fly's semi-invulnerable turn. Magnitude, Earthquake, and Fissure now successfully hit a Pokémon using Fly when the target is affected by Lock-On or Mind Reader, behaving as they normally would. Fly's base power saw an increase from 70 to 90. Its execution is prohibited under the influence of Gravity, and should Gravity activate on the same turn Fly was initiated, Fly will be nullified.

In this generation, the user cannot evade attacks from Pokémon possessing the No Guard ability during Fly's semi-invulnerable phase, nor can it evade attacks if it itself has No Guard. Should Gust or Twister connect during this semi-invulnerable turn, the move's base power is doubled, rather than the damage dealt (producing an almost identical outcome).

Hurricane, Smack Down, and Thousand Arrows are now able to strike the user during Fly's semi-invulnerable turn. If Smack Down or Thousand Arrows successfully lands, the Fly user is grounded, thereby canceling the move. If Gust or Twister hits during the semi-invulnerable turn, the actual damage dealt is doubled, rather than the move's power (yielding an effect largely indistinguishable from the prior mechanic).

Holding a Power Herb allows the user to perform Fly in a single turn. In Triple Battles, Fly can strike non-adjacent adversaries. While airborne, the user is immune to targeting and attacks until it descends. However, Hurricane, Gust, Twister, and Thunder are unable to hit an airborne target. Historically, Fly functioned as a field move, facilitating instant travel to previously explored areas. Most potential destinations for Fly were cities and towns, though other notable sites were accessible. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the PokéNav Plus's Town Map permitted players to fly to any marked area, landing before a relevant sign or a suitable location on routes or at landmarks. Beyond the Party menu, Fly could also be activated via the AreaNav. Commencing with Generation VII, Fly ceased being a field move; its traversal role was succeeded by Poké Ride Charizard Glide in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon; the Secret Technique Sky Dash in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!; the Flying Taxi in Pokémon Sword, Shield, Scarlet, and Violet; and the Pokétch's Hidden Moves app in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Within Generation I, the use of Fly in the overworld resets the step counter governing HP loss for poisoned Pokémon. Consequently, a poisoned Pokémon loses 1 HP precisely four steps after Fly's most recent activation.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Fly bestows the "flying status" upon the user on its initial turn. While airborne, the Pokémon is immune to most attacks, with exceptions including Gust, Thunder, Twister, and Sky Uppercut. Projectiles also pass through the user as if its space were unoccupied. On the second turn, the user strikes the foe directly ahead. Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Fly's damage is doubled post-calculation. As a two-turn attack, it cannot be linked with other moves. Fly is unusable within a Pokémon Tower in the Adventure Squad series. When employed by a Gigantic Pokémon in Gates to Infinity, Fly possesses a base PP of 20, an intrinsic accuracy of 90%, and allows the user to target any enemy within the current room.

In the Super Smash Bros. franchise, Fly serves as Charizard's up special maneuver. Charizard envelops itself in flames, spinning upwards to gain altitude and harm adjacent opponents. It functions as a recovery tool for returning to the platform. Moltres's Trophy mentions its use of Fly upon being summoned from a Poké Ball; however, this depiction more closely resembles natural avian flight than the actual move Fly. According to the Smash Bros. DOJO!!, the official Super Smash Bros. Brawl website, Mew, Celebi, and Jirachi all employ Fly when departing the screen after emerging from a Poké Ball, yet only Mew can acquire this move in the main Pokémon games.

Pokémon GO features Fly as a Charged Attack, introduced on July 3, 2020. In cooperative battles, a user ceases its airborne state upon executing a unity attack. Earlier descriptions include: prior to Version 2.11.0 (July 28, 2021), "If the user is hit by a sync move while flying, it will stop flying."; prior to Version 2.23.5 (August 8, 2022), "If the user is hit by a sync move or max move while flying, it will stop flying."

Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto gains access to the move Glide after learning it from a Dragonite in Sparkling Skylands. This move seems to derive from Fly. It enables Ditto to shapeshift into a Dragonite and glide through the air by holding R during a jump, consuming Ditto's PP continuously throughout the transformation.

In the English rendition of "Fighting Flyer with Fire," Ash instructed his Charizard to use Fly; the Japanese equivalent simply had him commanding Charizard to ascend. Regardless, Charizard could not perform the action due to a wing ailment. In the episode "Blossom's Dream," Blossom directed her father's Charizard, Char-ly, to employ Fly, but the Pokémon defied her, unwilling to obey anyone but its rightful Trainer. Shelly's twin Vibrava were officially stated online to possess knowledge of Fly, although they were never depicted utilizing it. Similarly, Courtney, Tabitha, and Blaise's Swellow were indicated on the official website to know Fly, but their use of the move has never been observed. A number of Pokémon renowned for their extensive flight capabilities cannot acquire this move, including Yanmega, whose HeartGold Pokédex entry asserts its effortless capacity to transport an adult mid-flight. Conversely, certain Pokémon, like Vullaby, are able to learn Fly despite their Pokédex entries stating they

Title: Bind (move)

Originating in Generation I, Bind (Japanese: しめつける Bind) is an offensive Normal-type technique. Initially, this move possessed an accuracy of 75% and inflicted damage across two to five turns. The distribution for this duration was a 37.5% probability for 2 turns, an identical 37.5% for 3 turns, and a 12.5% chance for either 4 or 5 turns of sustained impact. While only the initial strike could land as a critical hit, all subsequent hits throughout its duration dealt an consistent amount of damage. For the entirety of Bind's effect, the target was rendered incapable of attacking, including the very first turn, provided the user initiated the attack first. This incapacitation even applied if Bind struck a substitute. During the period of damage infliction, the message "<Pokémon>'s attack continues!" would appear. While Bind was active, neither the user nor the target could select alternative moves (choosing "Fight" would only result in the user continuing its attack and the target doing nothing), though both parties retained the option to switch Pokémon. No explicit message signaled the final turn, requiring players to select "Fight" to verify Bind's conclusion. Damage stemming from Bind's ongoing duration was resolved after other recurrent damage. Should the user switch out before the turn count expired, the target would remain unable to attack during that specific turn, having missed its opportunity to select a move. If the target switched out prematurely, Bind would automatically be deployed against the newly incoming Pokémon, consuming an additional PP from the move. Even if Bind had 0 PP under such circumstances, it would still execute, and its PP would then reset to 63, factoring in full PP Ups. Notably, a missed Bind could still nullify the Hyper Beam's typical recharge turn. Furthermore, if Bind's user attacked before a Hyper Beam user during a recharge turn, and Bind missed, the Hyper Beam user would automatically activate Hyper Beam that turn. If Hyper Beam had 0 PP in this situation, it would nevertheless be used, and its current PP would afterwards roll over to 63, applying all PP Ups. Despite being unable to inflict damage upon Ghost-type Pokémon, Bind could still immobilize them for its entire duration. Upon successful use against a Ghost-type, the message "didn't affect" would nonetheless appear, and Bind's animation would not play on the initial turn (but would on subsequent turns).

In Pokémon Stadium, the mechanics diverged: players could select a move on each turn of Bind's effect. If the target switched out before the duration ended, the incoming Pokémon would not be automatically attacked; instead, the user's turn would simply be skipped. Bind would only negate Hyper Beam's recharge turn if its own attack was successful.

Starting in Generation II, the target became capable of attacking during Bind's duration, functioning normally. Instead, Bind now inflicted 1/16 of the target's maximum HP as residual damage for two to five turns, in addition to the damage dealt by the initial hit. The battle log would display "<user> used Bind on <Pokémon>!" Bind also gained the ability to trap the target, preventing switching and escape. If the user switched out, the trapped target would be freed. A Pokémon ensnared by Bind could free itself by employing Rapid Spin. In wild encounters, if a wild Pokémon used Bind, the player's Pokémon could still escape if it possessed the Run Away ability or held a Smoke Ball, though these did not allow the player to switch out the Pokémon. The in-game text also changed to "<Pokémon> was squeezed by <user>'s Bind!".

For Pokémon Contest combinations in Generation III, Bind could serve as a second move, granting 3 bonus appeal points if ViceGrip was used in the preceding turn.

In Generation IV, if the user of Bind held a Grip Claw, the move's duration would consistently be 5 turns. Also, a Pokémon affected by Bind could switch out if it was equipped with a Shed Shell.

Generation V brought further alterations: the displayed text became "<Pokémon> was squeezed by <user>!". Bind's accuracy was enhanced from 75% to 85%, and its sustained effect would now persist for either 4 or 5 turns. When the user held a Grip Claw, the duration was extended to a fixed 7 turns. The base end-of-turn damage inflicted by Bind was augmented from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. Furthermore, if the user was equipped with a Binding Band, this residual damage would further intensify to 1/6 of the target's maximum HP.

In more recent contexts, Bind deals damage and can apply the bound status condition (formerly known as Constriction status) to the adversary. With this status, the Pokémon is unable to move (though it may still attack) and cannot exchange places with the team leader. The affected Pokémon loses 5 / 3 HP every two turns, and the condition lasts for 4-6 / 6 turns.

In the story of Cerulean Blues, Kim's Tentacruel was ordered to use Bind but couldn't execute the command before being repelled by Misty's Gyarados. It's also noteworthy that Tentacruel was legitimately unable to learn Bind in Generation III. Despite binding moves evolving from Generation I to Generation II, Bind's description in Pokémon Stadium 2 mistakenly asserts that it renders the target immobile, a misstatement also found for Fire Spin and Wrap.

Title: Slam (move)

Known in Japanese as たたきつける (Slam), this Normal-type attack causes damage and made its debut in Generation I. Within Pokémon Contests, Slam functions as the second move in specific combinations, awarding the user an additional 2 appeal points when preceded by Pound during the prior turn. An instance from "A Cacturne for the Worse" featured an unnamed Coordinator instructing their Azurill to perform Slam, though the execution was prevented. When employed on standard terrain in the Generation III titles, Secret Power adopts Slam's visual animation. The Spanish localizations of the Generation I games initially rendered Slam incorrectly as Portazo, which translates to "door slam." This translation oversight persisted across subsequent game releases until Generation VI, when it was rectified to Atizar.

Title: Vine Whip (move)

Originating in Generation I, Vine Whip (Japanese: つるのムチ Vine Whip) is an attack of the Grass type, purely designed for dealing damage. This offensive technique simply inflicts harm, devoid of any secondary effects. Initially, it possessed a Power stat of 35 and a PP count of 10. Within Pokémon Contests, Vine Whip functions as the second component of a combination, granting its user 4 additional appeal points if Growth was successfully performed during the preceding turn. Subsequent revisions saw its base Power increase from 35 to 45, while its PP was boosted to 25.

As Ivysaur's Up Special move in Super Smash Bros., Vine Whip is noteworthy for its tether functionality. Furthermore, in the exclusive Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Vine Whip serves as the Power Card associated with Bulbasaur. This card allows the Bulbasaur player to seize one building (either a Poké Mart or Pokémon Center) from an opponent's property whenever they construct one. The stolen building must then be placed on a legally appropriate property owned by the Bulbasaur player. If no suitable location is immediately available, the player may hold onto the building until an appropriate property is acquired. In keeping with all other Power Cards, this specific ability is usable only once per game. Finally, Venusaur is shown utilizing Vine Whip in the Generation III wireless mini-game, Pokémon Jump.

Title: Stomp (move)

This offensive maneuver, known in Japanese as ふみつけ (Trample), debuted in Generation I and is classified as a Normal-type attack. Stomp delivers damage and carries a 30% probability of making the opposing Pokémon recoil. However, it cannot induce flinching if the target is protected by a substitute. Upon striking a Pokémon that has previously utilized Minimize, Stomp will inflict twice the normal damage. In certain contexts, if Stomp connects with a foe that previously used Minimize, its base power is doubled rather than the final damage output (achieving a nearly identical outcome). Conversely, in other game iterations, should Stomp land on a Pokémon that previously employed Minimize, the *damage dealt* is doubled, distinct from its power (with a practically equivalent result). Stomp can be used as the second action in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the performer 1 additional appeal point if Leer was performed on the preceding turn. Furthermore, if the adversary has used Minimize, Stomp's accuracy check is bypassed, guaranteeing a hit, unless the target is currently in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. A Steenee possessing knowledge of this move will undergo evolution into Tsareena upon leveling up. Fundamentally, this attack deals harm and carries the potential to make opponents flinch. An older description, preceding Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), stated: "Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch."

Title: Double Kick (move)

First appearing in Generation I, Double Kick (Japanese: にどげり Double Kick) is a Fighting-type attack that inflicts damage through multiple strikes. It hits the target twice during a single execution. In earlier generations (specifically Generations I-V), only the initial strike was capable of landing as a critical hit, yet the subsequent strike would always deal an identical amount of damage. During Generations I-V, if the first strike successfully shatters a substitute, the move immediately ceases. Furthermore, Bide and Counter would only register the second strike of this move. From Generation II to IV, when the user held a King's Rock, only the concluding strike possessed an opportunity to make the foe flinch.

From Generation V onwards, when the user carries a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every individual strike has an identical probability of causing the opposing Pokémon to flinch. In Generation VI and beyond, each individual hit inflicts damage autonomously, allowing either strike the potential to be a critical hit. Additionally, if the initial strike destroys a substitute, Double Kick will now continue to land its second hit.

For targets possessing contact-activated Abilities, each separate hit counts distinctly, thereby allowing such an Ability to trigger two times. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, retains the capacity to activate multiple times to avert fainting, with each instance of activation being entirely separate. If the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1, Sturdy will trigger with every hit received. Similarly, if the target possesses Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will cause the respective Ability to activate. Should the target be equipped with a Rocky Helmet, the user will incur damage for each individual strike landed.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Double Kick possesses a lowered base critical hit rate of 3%. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, if a Reviver Seed activates, the execution of the move will be terminated.

Within Pokémon GO, Double Kick functions as a Fast Attack and has been accessible since September 1, 2022.

In the episode Curtain Up! Fight the Fights!, Hop commanded his Wooloo to perform Double Kick, but the Pokémon was defeated prior to executing the attack. In File 3: Giovanni, Red instructed his Jolteon to employ Double Kick; however, Jolteon was knocked out by Giovanni's Rhyhorn's Thunderbolt before it could initiate the move. During Valiant Venomoth!, Green ordered her Nidorina, Nido, to use Double Kick. Nevertheless, Lorelei's Cloyster utilized Blizzard to disperse a Sleep Powder cloud generated by Sabrina's Venomoth, redirecting it to strike Nido, which consequently put her to sleep and prevented her from using Double Kick.

Title: Mega Kick (move)

Originating in Generation I, Mega Kick (Japanese: メガトンキック Megaton Kick) is a Normal-type offensive maneuver designed to deal damage. Its Technical Machine designation was TM05 in Generation I, later becoming TM01 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. For Generation II, it served as the signature move exclusively for Hitmonlee. With the same type and a comparable moniker, it functions as a conceptual counterpart to Mega Punch. The move's primary effect is inflicting damage, devoid of any secondary attributes. In Pokémon Contests, Mega Kick can be deployed as the second part of a combination, granting the user 4 extra appeal points if Focus Energy or Mind Reader was activated on the prior turn. When employed by Kris & Totodile/Croconaw/Feraligatr, Mega Kick adopts the Water type, owing to their passive skill Water Shift. Similarly, Sygna Suit Giovanni & Nidoking's utilization of Mega Kick renders it a Ground-type attack that never misses, thanks to their passive skill Giovanni's Cunning. In the English localized version of The Punchy Pokémon, Giant's Hitmonlee is said to use Mega Kick, a dubbing error where the Japanese original featured Jump Kick. Notably, Dusknoir, despite its lack of visible legs, can acquire this move, a vestige from its pre-Evolution, Dusclops, which possesses limbs.

Title: Jump Kick (move)

Jump Kick (Japanese: とびげり Dropkick) is a direct damage-inflicting Fighting-type maneuver that debuted in Generation I. Before Generation IV, this move was recognized as one of Hitmonlee's signature attacks. Initially, Jump Kick possessed a power rating of 70. Should the move fail to connect, its user would sustain 1 HP in crash damage. When directed at a Ghost-type Pokémon, the attack would consistently register as a miss. Across Generations I through III, if a Pokémon employing Jump Kick acted first and subsequently fainted from its own crash damage, the opposing Pokémon would neither attack nor be affected by ongoing damage during that turn. The crash damage mechanics were later revised to inflict 1/8 of the damage the move would have otherwise dealt. Furthermore, the user would incur crash damage if Jump Kick was defended against by moves like Protect, but not if blocked due to type immunity (in which case only the type immunity message was displayed). A subsequent modification adjusted the crash damage to be 1/2 of the projected damage output. Jump Kick serves as a valid second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the performer 6 bonus appeal points if Mind Reader was utilized during the preceding turn. Its base power was later amplified from 70 to 85. The move is rendered unusable when Gravity is in effect. A significant alteration allowed the user to suffer crash damage even from type immunity, with the damage being half of the target's maximum HP in such instances. However, crash damage is circumvented if Jump Kick fails because no remaining targets are present. The move's base power received another boost, climbing from 85 to 100, while its PP was simultaneously decreased from 25 to 10. The current crash damage calculation is consistently half of the user's maximum HP, rounded down.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Jump Kick causes damage, and if it misses its intended target, the user endures recoil damage. Specifically, within the Rescue Team and Explorers series, this recoil damage equates to ½ of the damage that would have been inflicted. From Gates to Infinity onwards, the recoil damage mechanism was updated to be 25% of the user's maximum HP.

Title: Rolling Kick (move)

Known in Japanese as まわしげり (Roundhouse Kick), Rolling Kick is a Fighting-type attack that first appeared in Generation I, dealing direct damage. Until Generation VII, this move was characteristic of Hitmonlee and Hitmontop, although other Pokémon could acquire it through breeding. Besides its damaging effects, Rolling Kick has a 30% chance to cause the target to flinch. It is important to note, however, that a target protected by a substitute cannot be made to flinch by this move. Furthermore, King's Rock no longer affects Rolling Kick.

Title: Sand Attack (move)

Sand Attack (Japanese: すなかけ Sand Attack), which was referred to as Sand-Attack before Pokémon X and Y, is a non-damaging Ground-type maneuver that debuted in Generation I. Initially, prior to Generation II, it was classified as a Normal-type move. This move's effect is to reduce the target's accuracy stat by one stage. In Generation I, while a Normal-type move, Sand Attack was not affected by immunities to Normal-type attacks. Additionally, in the Generation I core series games, an in-game opponent's use of Sand Attack carried a 25% chance of failure. In Generation II, having become a Ground-type move, it similarly disregards immunities to the Ground type. In the Generation II core series games, when an in-game opponent outside of the Battle Tower used Sand Attack, the move had a 25% chance to fail.

Sand Attack can initiate a Pokémon Contest combination, causing Mud-Slap to score double the normal appeal if utilized in the subsequent turn. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Mud-Slap or Sandstorm was previously used. Furthermore, it is part of a Contest Spectacular combination, providing the user three extra appeal points if Sandstorm was employed in the prior turn. When powered up by a Groundium Z into Z-Sand Attack, the user's evasiveness is raised by one stage. This move, which lowers the target's accuracy by one stage, is affected by Magic Coat.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Sand Attack, were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any caught Pokémon. Boss Wimpod was observed using Sand Attack. The anime episode "When Pokémon Worlds Collide!" revealed through Paul's Pokédex that his two Starly knew Sand Attack. In PW11, Diglett was mentioned to use Sand-Attack, though its usage was not depicted. Similarly, in PW17, Giovanni's Diglett was referenced as using Sand-Attack but was not shown performing it. As detailed in Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder, Sand Attack is one of four moves (alongside Gust, Bite, and Karate Chop) to have its type modified in Generation II, making it the only status move to do so in that generation. It is also one of only five status moves in total to have its type changed between generations, with the others being Charm, Curse, Moonlight, and Sweet Kiss.

Title: Headbutt (move)

This offensive technique, known as ずつき in Japanese, is a Normal-type attack that first appeared in the inaugural generation of Pokémon games. It inflicts damage upon its target. It is designated as TM01 in *Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!* and *Let's Go, Eevee!*, as well as in *Pokémon Legends: Z-A*. Beyond dealing damage, Headbutt possesses a 30% probability of making the opposing Pokémon flinch. Subsequently, Headbutt gained utility as a field move, enabling trainers to interact with small trees. This interaction could dislodge a wild Pokémon, causing it to fall out. The state of slumber for the encountered Pokémon was contingent on the in-game time. Certain species, such as Pineco and Heracross, were exclusively obtainable through this specific approach. Within Pokémon Contests, Headbutt functions effectively as the second step in a move combination, granting the performer an additional 2 appeal points if Focus Energy was executed during the preceding turn.

However, in *Pokémon Diamond*, *Pearl*, and *Platinum*, Headbutt ceased to function as an overworld action. Its capability to be used on trees in the overworld was reinstated in *Pokémon HeartGold* and *SoulSilver*. A key change was its applicability to all trees, though only a subset of these would actually produce Pokémon. Any tree capable of yielding a Pokémon would consistently do so when Headbutted. Furthermore, any Pokémon discovered via this method would no longer be asleep, irrespective of the time. The former system of tree categories was replaced by location-specific wild Pokémon encounters. Additionally, special trees were introduced, each providing a Pokémon that typically wouldn't be found through Headbutt in that particular zone. Upon obtaining the National Pokédex, trainers could also encounter non-native Pokémon from the Hoenn or Sinnoh regions through this technique.

In the *Rescue Team* and *Explorers* series of games, Headbutt inflicts damage and has a 25% chance to cause the target to flinch. Before version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its description stated: "Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch." Within *Pokémon Pokopia*, Ditto possesses the innate ability to use Headbutt, which enables it to butt trees, causing Berries to descend. A flashback scene in "In the Pink" depicted Misty's attempt to instruct Togepi in the use of Headbutt. During the episode "Nice Pryce, Baby!", Pryce commanded his Dewgong to perform Headbutt, but the attack was interrupted by a Swift before it could connect with the adversary. Interestingly, in *Pokémon Pokopia*, Ditto can utilize Headbutt in its human guise, despite never having the capacity to learn this particular technique.

Title: Horn Attack (move)

Known in Japanese as つのでつく (Horn Thrust), Horn Attack is a Normal-type offensive technique that causes damage and debuted in the first generation of games. This attack exclusively inflicts damage, devoid of any auxiliary properties. In Pokémon Contest combinations, employing Horn Attack first will cause both Fury Attack and Horn Drill to earn double the standard appeal if executed on the subsequent turn. Conversely, it can conclude a combination, granting the user an additional 4 appeal points if Leer was performed on the turn prior. The episode "The Water Flowers of Cerulean City" contained a reference to Lily's Goldeen knowing Horn Attack; despite this, no visual depiction of Goldeen performing the move has ever occurred. Notably, Horn Attack was the very first technique demonstrated in the animated series, utilized by a Nidorino in the debut episode. Intriguingly, Omanyte could acquire this move in Generation I, even though it possesses no visible horns or tusks. The probable explanation for this lies with its evolved state, Omastar, which is adorned with shell spikes.

Title: Fury Attack (move)

Known in Japan as みだれづき, or 'Disturbed Stab,' Fury Attack is a Normal-type offensive technique capable of striking multiple times. It made its debut in Generation I of the Pokémon series. Upon execution, this move deals damage by striking the opposing Pokémon anywhere from two to five times. The probability of strikes is distributed as follows: a 37.5% likelihood for two hits, an equal 37.5% chance for three hits, and a 12.5% probability for both four and five hits, respectively. Initially, only the inaugural strike could land as a critical hit, though all subsequent impacts would inflict identical damage. Solely within this generation, Fury Attack's sequence would terminate instantly upon shattering a substitute. Furthermore, moves like Bide and Counter would register only the final strike of Fury Attack.

In a later evolution of the mechanic, every strike began dealing damage autonomously, allowing each one to potentially land as a critical hit. Similarly, the move's assault was updated to persist even after overcoming a substitute. When the user holds a King's Rock, the concluding blow carries a possibility of causing the target to flinch. Should the target possess an Ability that triggers on contact, each individual strike contributes to its activation, allowing it to trigger repeatedly. For Pokémon equipped with the Skill Link Ability, Fury Attack unfailingly delivers five hits, provided the move does not miss entirely. Moreover, if the user is equipped with either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every successive hit gains an independent and equal opportunity to induce flinching in the foe.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Fury Attack functions as a potential second move, awarding the user an additional 2 appeal points if Horn Attack or Peck was deployed in the preceding turn. The probability distribution for hits was later adjusted to 35% for two strikes, 35% for three, 15% for four, and 15% for five strikes. Its current average total base power, factoring in a 15% inherent miss chance, stands at 39.5. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike being delivered, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its capability to repeatedly prevent fainting, though each activation attempt operates independently. The Sturdy Ability will trigger with every impact if the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1. Furthermore, if the target carries Weak Armor or Stamina, each individual strike will cause that Ability to activate.
