In instances where the target holds a Rocky Helmet, the user sustains damage for every single strike delivered.

As a core mechanic, Fury Attack delivers damage by striking the foe between two and five times per action. While each individual strike is subject to its own accuracy check, the overall move sequence will persist even if one of these checks results in a miss. In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's critical-hit rate sees a reduction, specifically to 1%. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, the activation of a Reviver Seed will terminate Fury Attack's execution.

Title: Horn Drill (move)

Originating in Generation I, Horn Drill (Japanese: つのドリル), a Normal-type attack, stands as a signature one-hit knockout (OHKO) maneuver. In its debut generation, it was identified as TM07. When successfully executed, Horn Drill delivers a fixed 65535 HP damage, effectively eliminating the target in a single blow by matching the damage output to their current HP. Initially, its base accuracy was listed at 30%. A striking hit from Horn Drill is also capable of breaking a substitute.

Uniquely among moves that bypass the standard damage formula, Horn Drill does not ignore type immunities. In its initial functionality, Horn Drill would fail to affect a target if the opponent's current Speed stat surpassed the user's.

The mechanism for calculating Horn Drill's accuracy has since evolved. Presently, if the user's level is inferior to the target's, the move's attempt will invariably fail (with Speed no longer influencing its success). However, should the user's level be 90 or more levels greater than the target's, Horn Drill achieves 100% accuracy, even if the underlying formula would generate a percentage exceeding 100% for differences of 91 or higher. This formula establishes an approximate 30% (76256) accuracy when both Pokémon are at the same level, increasing by roughly 0.78% (2256) for each level the user is above the target. This specific accuracy calculation remains susceptible to adjustments from accuracy and evasion stats.

In contrast, a separate method exists where Horn Drill's hit chance, despite its 30% listed accuracy, is determined by a different formula. Under this rule, the accuracy begins at 30% for Pokémon of identical levels and gains an additional 1% chance to hit for every level the user exceeds the target. If the user's level is at least 70 levels higher than the target's, Horn Drill becomes guaranteed to hit. Conversely, if the user is a lower level than the target, the move consistently fails. Notably, this particular hitting chance is distinct from standard accuracy: it is unaffected by accuracy or evasion stats, nor by external modifiers like Gravity, Wide Lens, or Compound Eyes.

During Generation II's core series games exclusively, Horn Drill, if it missed, could be countered by Counter for maximum damage. This interaction was removed in Pokémon Stadium 2, where Counter could not retaliate against it.

Horn Drill cannot strike a semi-invulnerable Pokémon. Nevertheless, effects that ensure move hits, such as No Guard and Lock-On, will still enable Horn Drill to connect, provided the user's level is not lower than the target's.

In Pokémon Contests, Horn Drill serves as a second move in a combination, awarding the user 2 bonus appeal points if Horn Attack was performed on the preceding turn. It also participates in Contest Spectacular combinations, granting an additional three appeal points if Lock-On, Mind Reader, or Miracle Eye was used immediately prior.

When engaged in a Max Raid Battle against a Dynamax Pokémon, Horn Drill's effect varies. If the Dynamax Pokémon is protected by a mysterious barrier, a successful Horn Drill will deplete two bars of the barrier's strength. Otherwise, Horn Drill will always be ineffective against Dynamax Pokémon.

Functionally, Horn Drill is designed to defeat its target in a single hit. In games predating Gates to Infinity, a successful Horn Drill would knock out the target by inflicting "calamitous damage" (Japanese: とてつもない タメージ, meaning "tremendous damage"), which was internally coded as 9999 damage. Furthermore, it inherently fails against Ghost-type Pokémon due to type immunity.

Despite the move's name implying a requirement for a horn, certain Pokémon defy this expectation. For instance, Nidorina and Kantonian Ponyta, which do not possess horns but develop them upon evolving, are capable of learning Horn Drill. Similarly, Tyrunt and Tyrantrum, Pokémon without horns, can also learn this move.

Title: Tackle (move)

This entry focuses on the combat technique known as Tackle. For information regarding its field application, consult Tackle (Field Move). Tackle (Japanese: たいあたり Body Blow) is a damaging Normal-type attack first introduced in the inaugural generation of Pokémon games. This move simply inflicts damage without any additional effects. In Pokémon Contests, Tackle can be utilized as the second step in a combination; if Defense Curl, Harden, or Leer was executed in the preceding turn, the user will earn an extra 4 appeal points. Tackle's base Power was subsequently boosted from 35 to 50, and its Accuracy improved from 95% to a perfect 100%. When employed by Scottie/Bettie alongside Caterpie/Metapod/ButterfreeEgg, this move transforms into a Bug-type attack, owing to their Bug Shift passive skill.

In the anime episode "When Pokémon Worlds Collide!", Paul's pair of Starly were noted by his Pokédex to possess Tackle. Similarly, in "Different Strokes for Different Blokes," Paul's Stantler was confirmed via his Pokédex to know Tackle.

Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer

In the episode "Buddy," Hop stated that his Wooloo knew Tackle, though it was never depicted using the attack. During PW11, Geodude was said to have known Tackle, but its usage was unobserved.

Pokémon HeartGold SoulSilver: Pokédex Completion Comic

"The Secret of Kangaskhan" in the manga revealed that Blue's Venomoth knew Tackle according to his Pokédex. Green's Blastoise, nicknamed Blasty, was revealed to have known Tackle as a Wartortle via Red's Pokédex in "Wartortle Wars." Additionally, the official website indicated that Green's Squirtle (Blasty), Yellow's Caterpie (Kitty), Ruby's Mudkip (Mumu), Blaine's Rapidash, and Joey's Rattata all knew Tackle, despite never being shown performing it.

Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder

Distinct from other generations, all initial partner Pokémon from the Unova region are capable of learning Tackle. The absence of Pound and Scratch in Pokémon Legends: Arceus and Pokémon Legends: Z-A has meant that all Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, and Kalos first partner Pokémon have also had opportunities to learn Tackle. In the animated series, Tackle stands out as the most frequently employed move by Pokémon that are unable to learn it within the games. It is also the most commonly acquired move that is not taught through TMs, TRs, or Move Tutors. From Generation I onwards, various game Pokédex entries describe Rhyhorn as possessing formidable tackles. However, Rhyhorn itself could not learn the move Tackle until Generation VIII. This description first appeared in Rhyhorn's Japanese Pokédex entry for Pokémon Red and Green, an entry that remained untranslated until its re-use in Pokémon FireRed. Rhyhorn's Japanese Pokédex entries accurately feature Tackle's Japanese name, 「たいあたり」, or its kanji equivalent, 「体当た」.

Title: Body Slam (move)

The offensive Normal-type technique known as Body Slam (のしかかり Lean On in Japanese) was introduced in Generation I. This move inflicts damage and carries a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon paralyzed. However, Electric-type Pokémon are now immune to paralysis, meaning Body Slam cannot inflict this status condition upon them. Should the target have employed Minimize, Body Slam's power doubles, and it becomes an unavoidable attack, bypassing accuracy checks. This guaranteed hit is only circumvented if the opponent is in the semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. When utilized by Scottie/Bettie paired with Igglybuff/Jigglypuff/WigglytuffEgg and possessing the passive skill Fairy Shift, Body Slam transforms into a Fairy-type attack.

Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed." During the English dub of "Charizard Chills," Tad instructed his Poliwrath to execute Body Slam; however, the attempt was interrupted. In the original Japanese broadcast, Poliwrath was instead commanded to use Counter. Similarly, in the English dub of "Pokémon Double Trouble," Luana directed her Marowak to employ Body Slam, whereas the Japanese version featured Marowak being ordered to use Double-Edge. "Pruning a Passel of Pals!" depicted an anonymous Coordinator's Wailmer being commanded to perform Body Slam, but Dawn's Buneary froze Wailmer with an Ice Beam before the move could be carried out. Emerald's Sceptile was noted to have used Body Slam off-panel in "Susceptible to Sceptile." From Generation IV onward, the move Secret Power adopts Body Slam's animation when executed within a building environment.

Its Chinese designation, 泰山压顶 (Tàishān Yādǐng), constitutes a chengyu (成语 chéngyǔ)—a four-character idiomatic expression or proverb. This specific chengyu literally means "bearing down with the overwhelming weight of Mt. Tai," referring to a 1500-meter (5000-foot) peak located in Shandong province.

Title: Wrap (move)

Should you be searching for the recurring Team Skull Grunt from the animated series, please refer to Rapp. Wrap (Japanese: まきつく, or Wrap Around) is a Normal-type damage-dealing attack first introduced in Generation I. Initially, Wrap possessed an accuracy of 85%. This move inflicts damage over a duration of 2 to 5 turns. The probability breakdown for these turns was 37.5% for 2 turns, 37.5% for 3 turns, 12.5% for 4 turns, and 12.5% for 5 turns. Only the first hit could land as a critical hit, but each subsequent hit dealt an identical amount of damage. Until Wrap's effect concluded, the target was rendered unable to attack, including on the starting turn, provided the user of Wrap acted first. Wrap's immobilizing effect persisted even if it struck a substitute. While damage was being inflicted, the message "<Pokémon>'s attack continues!" would appear. During Wrap's ongoing effect, neither the user nor the target could select moves (choosing "Fight" would cause the user to keep dealing damage and the target to do nothing), though both Pokémon retained the ability to switch out. No message was provided on the final turn, leaving players unaware of Wrap's end unless they selected "Fight" to view the move list. Damage from Wrap's continuous duration was applied after any other recurrent damage. If the user withdrew before the turn duration elapsed, the target would remain unable to attack during that turn. If the target switched out prematurely, the user would automatically deploy Wrap against the incoming Pokémon, deducting an additional PP from the move and applying the standard miss chance. Should Wrap have 0 PP at such a moment, it would still be used, with its current PP rolling over to 63, fully applying all PP Ups. Even a missed Wrap would negate the recharge turn typically required for Hyper Beam. Furthermore, if Wrap's user attacked before a Hyper Beam user during a recharge turn, and Wrap missed, the Hyper Beam user would automatically activate Hyper Beam during that same turn. If Hyper Beam had 0 PP under these circumstances, it would still be used, its PP then rolling over to 63, with full PP Ups applied. Although Wrap could not harm Ghost-type Pokémon, it still prevented them from attacking for its duration. A successful Wrap against a Ghost-type would still display "didn't affect" the Pokémon, and Wrap's animation would not play on the initial turn, but would during subsequent turns of its effect.

In Pokémon Stadium, players could select a move during each turn Wrap was active. If the target switched out before Wrap ended, the incoming Pokémon was not automatically attacked; instead, the user's turn was skipped. In this game, Wrap would only negate Hyper Beam's recharge turn if it successfully connected.

Subsequently, the target became able to attack normally during Wrap's duration. The move then began to inflict 1/16 of the target's maximum HP as damage for two to five turns in addition to the initial damage dealt upon use. The probabilities for attacking for 2, 3, 4, or 5 turns became an equal 25% for each duration. The text "Enemy <Pokémon> was WRAPPED by <user>!" would appear. Wrap also gained the ability to trap the target, preventing switching and escape. If the user switched out, the target would be freed. A trapped Pokémon could free itself by using Rapid Spin. While the target was being hurt, the message "Enemy <Pokémon>'s hurt by WRAP!" was displayed. After the effect concluded, the text "Enemy <Pokémon> was released from WRAP!" would appear. If a wild Pokémon used Wrap on a player's Pokémon, the player could escape if the affected Pokémon had the Run Away Ability or was holding a Smoke Ball, though these items did not permit switching the Pokémon out. Following successful use against a wild or foe Pokémon, the text "Wild/Foe <Pokémon> was WRAPPED by <user>!" would appear. While it inflicted damage, the message "Wild/Foe <Pokémon> is hurt by WRAP!" was shown. Upon cessation, "The wild/foe <Pokémon> was freed from Wrap!" would be displayed. If the user of Wrap held a Grip Claw, the duration would consistently be 5 turns. Additionally, a Pokémon holding a Shed Shell could switch out even when affected by Wrap. The message "The wild/foe's <Pokémon> was wrapped by <user>!" would appear after successful use. During the damage-dealing phase, "The wild/foe's <Pokémon> is hurt by Wrap!" was seen, and upon its end, "The wild/foe's <Pokémon> was freed from Wrap!" would be displayed.

In a later update, Wrap's accuracy was changed from 85% to 90%, and its duration became fixed at four to five turns. If the user held a Binding Band, the end-of-turn damage inflicted by Wrap increased from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. Subsequently, if the user of Wrap held a Grip Claw, its duration became 7 turns. The end-of-turn damage from Wrap was generally increased from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. Furthermore, if the user was holding a Binding Band, Wrap's end-of-turn damage would increase to 1/6 of the target's maximum HP.

In the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, Wrap functions as a status move, inflicting the 'Wrapped' status on enemies and the 'Wrap' status on the user. Both of these statuses prevent movement and stop the Pokémon from taking any actions. A Pokémon that is Wrapped takes 6 HP damage every two turns. The 'Wrap' and 'Wrapped' statuses last for 0-5 turns. Wrap can be linked with another move, but if it is the initial move in a link, the second linked move will not be utilized. As a status move, Wrap is susceptible to Taunt. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Wrap deals damage and has a chance to inflict the 'Bound' status. Pokémon afflicted with the 'Bound' status are unable to move (though they can still use moves) and lose 3 HP damage every two turns. This status lasts for 6 turns.

In the English dub of the anime episode "Playing with Fire," Harrison was depicted commanding his Steelix to use Wrap, a move it cannot legally learn. The original Japanese version saw Harrison instructing Steelix to use Bind instead. In the movie "The Rise of Darkrai," Baron Alberto's Lickilicky was shown to know Wrap, as its Trainer used the move while transformed into Lickilicky. Orm's two Shuckle were listed as knowing Wrap on the official website, but they were never seen using the move. Despite the binding moves' function changing between Generation I and Generation II, Wrap's description in Pokémon Stadium 2 still erroneously states that it makes the target unable to move. This same inaccuracy also applies to Bind and Fire Spin.

Title: Take Down (move)

This offensive maneuver, known in Japan as とっしん (Charge), belongs to the Normal type and made its debut in Generation I of the Pokémon series. Its Technical Machine designation was TM09 during Generation I, and it is presently identified as TM001 in Generation IX. When executed, Take Down causes harm to the target, with the attacker subsequently enduring recoil damage equivalent to one-quarter (¼) of the damage successfully dealt. Should the Pokémon employing Take Down act first and then be knocked out by the resulting recoil, the opposing Pokémon will neither make a move nor suffer any ongoing damage within the same turn. Recoil damage sustained from Take Down in a preceding turn has the potential to be negated if the adversary fails to execute an action on the subsequent turn. Prior game mechanics dictated that no recoil damage was incurred if Take Down successfully shattered a substitute. Furthermore, in the Stadium series, the user avoided recoil entirely if Take Down delivered a knockout blow to an opponent. However, current implementations ensure that the user still suffers recoil damage even when breaking a substitute. In Pokémon Contests, Take Down functions as the second component of a combination, granting 6 additional appeal points if either Focus Energy or Harden was performed in the preceding turn.

This aggressive move always inflicts damage, resulting in self-inflicted recoil. Specifically, in the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, this recoil amounts to one-eighth (⅛) of the user's maximum HP. Beginning with Gates to Infinity, this self-inflicted damage was altered to 10% of the Pokémon's maximum HP.

Beyond the main series, in the original Super Smash Bros., summoning a Poké Ball could release a group of Beedrill, which would then fly diagonally across the screen, performing Take Down. These Beedrill were superseded by Unown in Super Smash Bros. Melee, with the Unown capable of traversing the screen from any cardinal direction. Later, in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, the Poké Ball Pokémon Eevee executes Take Down by charging repeatedly across the stage, assailing combatants. Within Pokémon GO, Take Down functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible since February 9, 2018.

In the English dubbed version of the episode "The Mystery is History," Ritchie instructed his Pupitar, named Cruise, to employ Take Down, even though Pupitar was incapable of learning this move at that point in time. The original Japanese version of the same episode featured a command for Headbutt instead. During the events of "Silly Scyther," Bugsy directed his Heracross to use Take Down, yet the Pokémon was prevented from performing the attack due to the influence of Encore. Furthermore, in "Mewtwo Comes Through," it was disclosed that Yellow's Golem, Gravvy, possessed knowledge of Take Down. On the official website, it was stated that Steven's various Beldum were aware of Take Down, although their usage of the move was never depicted.

Title: Thrash (move)

The Normal-type damaging technique, Thrash, known in Japanese as あばれる (Violent Struggle), made its debut in Generation I. This move delivers continuous damage for 3-4 turns, boasts a base power of 90, and comes with 20 PP. The cost of PP is only incurred on the turn Thrash is first initiated. Upon calling the move, the user becomes locked into Thrash and is unable to swap out, regardless of whether the attack misses. Subsequent to its full execution, the user enters a state of confusion due to the resulting exhaustion. Should the ongoing duration of Thrash be interrupted (for example, by complete paralysis or self-inflicted damage from confusion), the move ceases instantly. Confusion only afflicts the user if Thrash runs its full course without any interruption to its duration. Conditions like sleep, freeze, binding, and flinching will merely suspend Thrash's duration rather than interrupting it entirely.

In instances where Thrash's accuracy is influenced by alterations to accuracy or evasion, any accuracy reduction is reapplied during each subsequent automatic turn. Its accuracy then reverts to normal when the move is next manually chosen. However, if Thrash fails to hit because of the 99.6% accuracy glitch affecting 100%-accuracy moves when no other accuracy or evasion modifications are active, or when such modifiers nullify one another, its accuracy remains unchanged. Furthermore, if Thrash misses on the turn it is initially chosen, its secondary effects do not come into play. Within the Stadium titles, upon the conclusion of Thrash's duration, a message indicates that the user will be confused for a period.

During multi-opponent encounters, the user designates itself as the target, yet strikes a randomly chosen adjacent foe with each iteration of the move. Thrash is suitable as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, awarding the user an additional 4 appeal points if Rage was performed on the preceding turn. Its base power is augmented to 120, while its PP is cut to 10. The move's execution is disrupted if it fails to connect due to missing, the user being asleep, paralysis, freeze, flinching, a target's Protect, or immunities like Ghost-type or Wonder Guard. Even if a disruption takes place on what would have been Thrash's concluding, confusion-triggering turn, the user nonetheless becomes confused. This move both inflicts damage and subsequently confuses its user once the duration completes.

Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the user performs three attacks, each facing a random direction (which may include repeating directions), striking any Pokémon, whether ally or foe, currently in front of them. Should the Pokémon be repositioned during execution, for instance by Vital Throw, it will persist in attacking from its updated location. Beginning with Gates to Infinity, Thrash instead delivers 2-3 consecutive hits to an enemy positioned directly in front. The Spanish localizations of Generation I games initially translated Thrash vaguely as Golpe (meaning "strike"), a name carried over until Generation IX, when it was finally changed to Saña.

Title: Tail Whip (move)

Tail Whip (Japanese: しっぽをふる Tail Wag) is a Normal-type ability that causes no direct damage, first appearing in Generation I. This move functions by diminishing the Defense stat of all nearby opposing Pokémon by a single stage. In the core series titles of Generation I and II, when an in-game challenger outside the Battle Tower employed Tail Whip, it possessed a 25% likelihood of failure. Tail Whip can be utilized as the concluding move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 2 additional appeal points if Charm was executed on the preceding turn. When boosted by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Tail Whip, the user's Attack stat is elevated by one stage. This technique invariably decreases an opponent's Defense by one level. Magic Coat has an effect on this particular move. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, Tail Whip included, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Pikachu, Vulpix, and Ponyta were observed using Tail Whip. Green's Blastoise, named Blasty, was confirmed by Red's Pokédex in "Wartortle Wars" to have known Tail Whip during its time as a Wartortle. Joey's Rattata's proficiency in Tail Whip was disclosed on the official website, though it was never depicted using the move. Similarly, a conman's Wooper were officially stated to know Tail Whip online, but no on-screen usage was ever shown. Several Pokémon in the past (e.g., Flareon, Marowak, and Tauros) were capable of simultaneously mastering both Tail Whip and Leer, despite their identical functional outcome. With Morpeko as the sole exception, all such Pokémon eventually learned only Tail Whip, irrespective of possessing a very short tail (like Rhyhorn) or no tail whatsoever (such as Snubbull).

Title: Poison Sting (move)

Originating in Generation I, Poison Sting (どくばり Poison Needle in Japanese) is a damage-dealing Poison-type attack. It causes harm and initially possessed a 20% chance of afflicting the target with poison. Its current iteration, however, provides a 30% likelihood of leaving the opposing Pokémon poisoned. The in-game text for Poison Sting currently states: "Poison Sting deals damage and may leave the target poisoned." Prior to Version 2.10.0, its descriptive text was: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target poisoned." Within the narrative of Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Glalie, an undisclosed Pokémon owned by Guile Hideout deployed Poison Sting. Nido, Green's Nidorina, was confirmed to possess Poison Sting on the official web site, though her usage of the move was never depicted.

Title: Twineedle (move)

Known in Japan as ダブルニードル (Double Needle), Twineedle is a Bug-type damage-dealing move featuring multiple strikes, first introduced in Generation I. Before Generation V, it was exclusively Beedrill's signature attack. When used, Twineedle inflicts damage by striking the target two times per application. The second hit carries a 20% chance of inflicting a poisoned status, unless the target is a Poison-type Pokémon. Initially, only the first strike could score a critical hit, with the second always dealing an identical amount of damage. Twineedle would immediately cease if its initial strike successfully broke a substitute. Both Bide and Counter abilities would only register the damage from this move's second strike. Over time, each individual strike began to deal damage independently, and both gained the capacity for critical hits.

Due to a programming oversight, Twineedle once possessed the unusual ability to poison Steel-type Pokémon. Later changes allowed Twineedle to continue its attack even if the first strike shattered a substitute. Consequently, if the initial strike broke a substitute, the subsequent strike retained its potential to poison the target. In specific contexts, if the user was holding a King's Rock, the ultimate strike gained a chance to make the target flinch. Eventually, Twineedle lost its unique capacity to poison Steel-type Pokémon.

From a certain point, both of Twineedle's strikes were given an independent 20% chance of poisoning the target, resulting in an overall 36% chance of poisoning. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the delivery of the final strike, the subsequent strike will nonetheless cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, can still activate multiple times to avert fainting, with each activation opportunity being distinct. The Sturdy ability will trigger with every hit if the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1. For targets with the abilities Weak Armor or Stamina, each individual strike will activate the respective ability. Twineedle became generally affected by King's Rock mechanics. Specifically, when holding either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, each successive hit was granted an equal probability of causing the target to flinch.

As a fundamental aspect, Twineedle delivers damage by striking the opponent 2 times per use. Each individual hit also undergoes a distinct accuracy check, though the move's execution persists even if one hit misses. Additionally, Twineedle retains the potential to inflict poison upon the adversary. In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's critical hit rate experiences a reduction, specifically to 2%. Prior to the release of Super Mystery Dungeon, if a Pokémon was knocked out by the initial projectile, the subsequent projectile could then target a new Pokémon and would still be performed even if a Reviver Seed activated.

Its official description before Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) read: "Attacks twice in a row. Has a small chance of leaving the target poisoned." Exclusively within Generation II, Twineedle held the distinction as the sole move capable of poisoning a Steel-type Pokémon, a phenomenon stemming from Steel-types being coded to only resist Poison-type moves, not all sources of poison.

Title: Pin Missile (move)

The Bug-type attack known as Pin Missile (Japanese: ミサイルばり, Missile Needle) debuted in Generation I as a damage-dealing, multi-hit technique. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was cataloged as TM07. When employed, Pin Missile strikes the opponent between two and five times. During its initial generation, the probability distribution for hits was 37.5% for two strikes, 37.5% for three, 12.5% for four, and 12.5% for five. Only the initial impact could register as a critical hit, with subsequent hits dealing identical damage. Exclusively within this generation, the move would cease immediately upon shattering a substitute. Furthermore, Bide and Counter would only register the final strike of Pin Missile. It possessed a base power of 14 and an accuracy rating of 85%.

From Generation II onward, individual strikes of Pin Missile were capable of dealing damage independently, allowing any of them to land as a critical hit. Also, the move would no longer terminate prematurely if it successfully broke a substitute. In Generation II, equipping the user with a King's Rock offered a chance for the final strike to induce flinching. This mechanism was later refined in Generation III, where both King's Rock and Razor Fang granted an equal flinch chance on *each* consecutive hit. Generation IV introduced the Ability Skill Link, which guaranteed Pin Missile would strike five times, provided the attack did not miss.
In Generation V, the hit probability distribution shifted to 35% for two strikes, 35% for three, 15% for four, and 15% for five. The average total base power, accounting for a 15% chance of missing entirely, was established at 36.9. Concurrently, if a Focus Sash triggered before the final strike, the subsequent hit would result in the defending Pokémon's fainting. A Focus Band, however, could still activate multiple times to prevent a faint, with each activation opportunity being independent. Pokémon with the Ability Sturdy, possessing 1 HP, would have the ability activate with every hit. Additionally, if the target had Weak Armor, each strike of Pin Missile would activate its effect.
Generation VI brought a significant update, increasing Pin Missile's base power to 25 and its accuracy to 95%. With these adjustments, its average total base power, assuming no misses, reached 79.2. When factoring in the 5% inherent miss chance, the overall average total base power became 73.6. Generation VII introduced the Ability Stamina, which would activate with every strike Pin Missile landed on a target possessing it.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Pin Missile behaves differently; it strikes a single time, subsequently afflicting the target with splinters for 4 turns when used with Strong Style, 2 turns with Agile Style, or 3 turns otherwise. In this specific game, Pin Missile invariably lands 5 hits. Within the Pokémon Rumble series, employing Pin Missile as a Plus Move guarantees it will strike 6 times.
In the Mystery Dungeon series, Pin Missile delivers damage by striking the target 2 to 5 times. Each individual hit undergoes a distinct accuracy check, although the attack persists even if a particular strike misses. Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad installments, the move's critical-hit rate is lowered to 1%. Before the release of Gates to Infinity, should a targeted Pokémon faint mid-attack, any remaining projectiles could redirect to a new foe. However, beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, the activation of a Reviver Seed by the target will cause the move to conclude.
Regarding other media, the anime episode "The Bicker the Better" featured Jessie instructing Dustox to use Pin Missile, which was later identified as a dubbing inaccuracy; the original Japanese script called for Poison Sting. In the manga, specifically PW31, Jolteon was depicted as having used Pin Missile, although only the move itself was visually represented.

Historically, preceding Generation VI, Pin Missile held the unique distinction of being the sole move with a base power of 14. Furthermore, across the core series games, it stood as one of just three moves – alongside Comet Punch and Fury Swipes – whose base power was not a multiple of 5. Notably, all these moves share the characteristic of being multistrike attacks.

Title: Leer (move)

A non-damaging Normal-type attack, Leer (Japanese: にらみつける Glare) debuted in Generation I. This technique lowers the Defense stat of all adjacent adversaries by one stage. Within the Generation I and II core series titles, Leer had a 25% probability of failing when employed by an in-game opponent not located in the Battle Tower. As the initial action in a Pokémon Contest combination, Leer empowers Bite, Faint Attack, Glare, Horn Attack, Scary Face, Scratch, Stomp, and Tackle to achieve double their standard appeal score if performed immediately afterwards. Alternatively, it can conclude a combination, awarding the user 3 bonus appeal points if Rage or Scary Face was executed in the preceding turn. When transformed into Z-Leer by a Normalium Z, the user's Attack stat increases by one stage. The move's primary function is to reduce the target's Defense by a single stage. Magic Coat affects this particular move. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, Leer included, were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss versions of Natu, Patrat, Lillipup, Pansear, Axew, Rufflet, Vullaby, Pancham, Yungoos, Stufful, and Jangmo-o were observed utilizing Leer. Any reductions in stats or other effects applied by these moves impacted each target hit. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021), its description read: "Lowers the target's Defense." This particular buddy move was designated as B Leer before version 2.38.0. Ash instructed his Totodile to use Leer in the English dubbed version of Playing with Fire!. In contrast, the original Japanese version had him command Scary Face. Pearl's Chimchar, named Chimler, was listed on the official website as knowing Leer, though it was never depicted using the move. Professor Birch's Treecko also appeared on the official website as knowing Leer, but its use was never showcased. Maizie's Teddiursa was likewise revealed on the official website to know Leer, yet she was never shown executing the technique. The Japanese translation of Leer's name, Glare (にらみつける), also serves as the English name for a separate move.

Title: Bite (move)

Known in Japanese as かみつく (Bite), this damage-dealing Dark-type maneuver first appeared in Generation I. However, it was classified as a Normal-type move prior to Generation II. Originally, as a Normal-type move in Generation I, it inflicted damage with a 10% probability of causing the target to flinch. Upon its reclassification to Dark-type in Generation II and subsequent generations, its flinch chance increased to 30%. This type alteration also led to it being categorized as a special move in Generations II and III, only to revert to a physical move from Generation IV onwards.

In Pokémon Contests, Bite serves as the second component of a combination, granting the user 1 additional appeal point if either Leer or Scary Face preceded it. Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad game series, Bite's usage is impeded by the Muzzled status condition. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description stated: "Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch."

The English dub of The Legend of Thunder! features Hun instructing his Steelix to employ Bite, a move it is incapable of legitimately learning. This contrasts with the original Japanese version, where Hun consistently commands Steelix to use Crunch throughout the special, a detail preserved in both versions thereafter. During the episode A Timeless Encounter!, Professor Kukui directed his Litten to use Bite; however, it was overcome by Ash's Torracat's Fire Blast before the attack could be unleashed. In the manga segment 'Shocking!! Reunion in the Forest' from Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, Casey's Arrokuda, named Kilo, was noted for knowing Bite, though he was never depicted performing the attack. Similarly, Red's Eevee, Vee, was indicated on the official website as knowing Bite, yet this move was never demonstrated by him.

Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder.

Bite stands as one of only four moves that underwent a type alteration in Generation II, alongside Gust, Karate Chop, and Sand Attack. While all these moves were initially Normal-type in Generation I, Bite uniquely transitioned to a type that itself debuted in Generation II. Furthermore, Bite holds the distinction of being the sole move whose damage category has shifted on two separate occasions. This unique progression stems from its classification as Normal-type (and thus physical) in Generation I, then Dark-type (and consequently special) throughout Generations II and III, before ultimately becoming a physical move again from Generation IV onward. Notably, in Generation III, when Secret Power is deployed within a cave environment, its animation mirrors that of Bite.

Title: Growl (move)

Introduced in the first generation of Pokémon, Growl (known in Japanese as なきごえ Cry) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move reduces the Attack stat of all proximate adversaries by a single stage. Certain Abilities grant immunity to this Attack reduction, specifically Hyper Cutter, Clear Body, White Smoke, and Full Metal Body. Furthermore, Soundproof Pokémon are immune to Growl's effects, given its classification as a sound-based move. When enhanced by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Growl, the user experiences a one-stage increase in their Defense stat and can execute the move even if previously hindered by Throat Chop. In Pokémon Contests, Growl serves as a viable second move in a combination, awarding an additional 2 appeal points if preceded by Charm in the preceding turn. Historically, Pokémon shielded by a substitute were immune to Growl's effects. In the initial two generations of core series games, specifically when an in-game rival outside the Battle Tower employed Growl, the technique carried a 25% chance of failing. This inherent chance of failure for CPU opponents has since been eliminated. Currently, Growl is capable of affecting Pokémon even when they are protected by a substitute. Its targeting mechanic extends to all enemies within the same room, diminishing their Attack by one stage. Growl is subject to both the Muzzled status condition and the effects of Magic Coat. As a technique reliant on sound, it is consequently blocked by the Soundproof Ability. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Growl, alongside all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by captured Pokémon. Boss versions of Charmander, Spearow, Nidoran♀, Meowth, Cubone, Phanpy, Bidoof, Chingling, Litten, and Popplio were observed utilizing Growl. Any stat reductions inflicted by Growl are individually applied to every affected target. Gold's Togepi, known as Togebo, was shown to possess Growl via his Pokédex in the Gligar Glide segment. Ruby's Skitty, Kiki, was officially listed online as knowing Growl, though no depiction of her actually employing the move ever occurred. Similarly, wild Kricketot were stated on the official website to know Growl, yet were never visually confirmed performing it. Despite the sound-based move mechanic being formalized only in Generation III, Growl's position in the move index number aligns it closely with other Generation I moves that are retrospectively considered sound-based, such as Roar, Sing, and Supersonic.

Title: Roar (move)

Roar (Japanese: ほえる Roar) is a non-damaging Normal-type technique first introduced in Generation I. It was designated as TM05 from Generation II through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and also in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When successfully utilized in a wild Pokémon encounter by either combatant, the battle automatically concludes. Conversely, in a Trainer battle, Roar initially possessed no effect. It boasts 100% accuracy and standard priority. Roar could initially strike Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable phase of Dig. If the user's level was less than the target's level, a variable chance of failure existed for Roar, roughly between 10% and 25%, depending on their respective levels. This failure probability typically escalated as the target's level increasingly surpassed the user's. Specifically, the chance of failure was calculated as follows: FailureChance=⌊Leveltarget4⌋Leveltarget+Leveluser+1

Subsequently, in Trainer battles, Roar's effect shifted to forcing the target to switch out with a randomly chosen Pokémon from its Trainer's party. Should no other Pokémon be available for the target to switch with, Roar would then fail. Its priority was adjusted to -1. Roar also lost its capability to hit Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable turn of Dig. The level-dependent failure chance no longer applied to Trainer battles. However, in Trainer battles of certain generations, Roar would invariably fail if used before the opponent had executed its move. The move consistently fails if a player attempts to use Roar against the Red Gyarados or to escape the mandatory wild Pokémon encounters at Team Rocket HQ. Roar was later classified as a sound-based move. Consequently, it fails when employed against Pokémon possessing the Soundproof ability (due to its sound-based nature), or against a Pokémon with Suction Cups or rooted by Ingrain. The previous condition causing it to fail if not used last was removed.

The calculation for Roar's failure chance when the user's level was lower than the target's was later revised. Specifically, for a random integer X between 0 and 255, Roar fails if the following relation is true: The chance of failure can be approximated by the following formula. However, this approximation may diverge moderately from the true probability for low user and target levels. In wild Pokémon battles from later games, Roar will now always fail if the user's level is less than the target's. Simultaneously, in Trainer battles, Roar will always succeed if it hits, regardless of either Pokémon's level. Roar became susceptible to being reflected by Magic Coat and Magic Bounce. In wild Pokémon battles involving more than one Pokémon per side, Roar will fail if used by the player's Pokémon against a wild Pokémon. Conversely, if a wild Pokémon employs it against the player, it will compel the target to switch with another Pokémon in the Trainer's party, irrespective of the target's level. Roar now bypasses accuracy checks to ensure a hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of another move (such as Dig or Fly). It gained the ability to strike a target even if safeguarded by Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, but will still fail if the target is shielded by Crafty Shield.

Roar can contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Spikes, Stealth Rock, or Toxic Spikes was used in the preceding turn. If utilized by the player's Pokémon in an SOS Battle, Roar will fail. When powered up into Z-Roar by a Normalium Z, the user's Defense stat increases by one stage. Throat Chop cannot prevent a Pokémon from using Z-Roar. Roar fails when directed at a Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon. In certain spin-off titles, Roar targets the enemy in front, blowing them back by 10 tiles. Should the enemy collide with a wall, they incur 5 HP of damage; if they hit another Pokémon, both take 5 HP damage. A Pokémon forced onto an impassable tile will be warped away. This move is influenced by the Muzzled status condition. Being a sound-based move, it is blocked by Soundproof.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Roar, were exclusively used by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any caught Pokémon. Roar was notably seen as a move used by Boss Growlithe and Stoutland. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) read: "Forces the opponent's sync pair to switch out." Blaine's Growlithe was officially listed on the website as knowing Roar, but was never actually shown using the move. Although the sound-based move mechanic itself debuted in Generation III, Roar's entry in the move index is located near other Generation I sound-based moves: Growl, Sing, and Supersonic. A glitch in FireRed and LeafGreen would cause a roaming Pokémon to vanish entirely from the game if it used Roar. This glitch practically affected Raikou if the player's first partner Pokémon was Squirtle, and Entei if Bulbasaur was the chosen starter. (Suicune remained unaffected because it does not know Roar when encountered as a roaming Pokémon.) This same glitch was present in Emerald, but was not observable in normal gameplay because the Eon duo could only learn Roar via TM. This particular glitch was rectified in the Nintendo Switch re-release of FireRed and LeafGreen.

Title: Sing (move)

The term "Sing" primarily refers to this Pokémon move. For the Pokémon Music Collective song, consult Sing (song).

Known in Japanese as うたう (Sing), this non-damaging Normal-type move was introduced in Generation I. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it holds the designation of TM111. Pokémon possessing the Abilities Insomnia, Vital Spirit, Shields Down (when its shield is active), or Comatose are impervious to sleep. Furthermore, Pokémon with Soundproof are unaffected by Sing, as it is a sound-based attack.

Historically, in the main series handheld games, Sing could bypass a substitute; however, it no longer affects targets protected by one. During the Generation II core series games, if an in-game opponent (outside of the Battle Tower) utilized Sing, the move carried an additional 25% chance to fail, distinct from its standard miss chance.

Within Pokémon Contests, Sing can initiate a combination, enabling Perish Song and Refresh to achieve double the usual appeal when executed on the subsequent turn. When empowered by a Normalium Z into Z-Sing, the user's Speed stat gains a single stage. Notably, Throat Chop does not hinder a Pokémon from performing Z-Sing. In Contest Spectaculars, Sing also serves as a component of a combination, granting an extra three appeal points to specific moves—Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap—when they are used on the following turn.

The primary effect of Sing is to induce sleep in the opponent. Its effectiveness can be influenced by Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition. As a sound-based move, it is inherently blocked by Soundproof.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Sing serves as Jigglypuff's up special. It induces sleep in all grounded opponents near Jigglypuff, with airborne players remaining unaffected. The duration of this sleep effect increases with the damage the opponent has accumulated. Uniquely for an up special, Sing offers no recovery.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Sing, were exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Sing was notably employed by Boss Igglybuff. When utilized by Rosa (Champion) & Meloetta, Sing becomes a Psychic-type move, an effect triggered by their passive skill, Psychic Performance.

In the special Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Jigglypuff's designated Power Card is Sing. This card allows the player controlling Jigglypuff, once per game and at the conclusion of their turn, to immediately take an additional turn, ostensibly by making all other players fall asleep.

A notable event in Pokémon Yellow involves a Trainer in the Pewter City Pokémon Center whose Jigglypuff performs Sing, causing the player's Pikachu to appear asleep. Although the player's Pikachu temporarily halts following them until spoken to or until the player leaves the Pokémon Center, Pikachu is never actually afflicted with the sleep status condition.

Despite sound-based moves not receiving an official flag until Generation III, Sing's move index number in Generation I is situated closely with other moves recognized as sound-based: Growl, Roar, and Supersonic. Sing stands out as one of a select group of moves exhibiting differing accuracy values across game titles, possessing a 55% accuracy in the core series games compared to 45% in Pokémon Masters EX.

Title: Supersonic (move)

Debuting in Generation I, Supersonic (Japanese: ちょうおんぱ Ultrasound) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no direct damage. Its primary effect is to induce the confused status condition upon the target. This move will fail if the foe is already confused or possesses the Own Tempo Ability. While it historically could not affect a Pokémon protected by a substitute, Supersonic can now strike opponents even if they are behind one. When transformed into Z-Supersonic by a Normalium Z, the user's Speed stat is boosted by one stage; Throat Chop cannot prevent a Pokémon from unleashing Z-Supersonic. Supersonic directs its effect at the immediate opponent, causing them to become confused. It is susceptible to deflection by Magic Coat and is influenced by the Muzzled status condition. Being a sound-based move, Pokémon with the Soundproof Ability are immune to its effects. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Supersonic, along with all other status moves, was exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any caught Pokémon. The move was notably employed by Boss versions of Zubat, Golbat, Venonat, Ledyba, and Loudred. In the episode 'The Whistle Stop', Arielle's six Ledyba were mentioned to possess Supersonic. Additionally, Skyla's Swoobat was shown to know Supersonic during an imaginary "Air Battle" in 'Cilan Takes Flight!'. Despite sound-based moves not receiving an explicit flag until Generation III, Supersonic's move index number from Generation I positions it near other early sound-related techniques such as Growl, Roar, and Sing.

Title: Sonic Boom (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Sonic Boom (Japanese: ソニックブーム Sonic Boom) is a Normal-type damage-dealing attack. Prior to Pokémon X and Y, its name was formatted as SonicBoom. When successfully executed, Sonic Boom consistently inflicts exactly 20 points of damage. It possesses no secondary effects and completely disregards both type weaknesses and resistances. Historically, type immunities did not affect Sonic Boom, allowing it to strike Ghost-type Pokémon. However, this move is now subject to type immunities, meaning it typically fails to hit Ghost-type Pokémon.

Only in Generation II was its base power shown as 20 rather than a dash (—). The specific fixed damage output of Sonic Boom varies across different games. Within the Rescue Team series, Sonic Boom delivers 55 HP damage. From the Explorers series onward, this move's damage output shifts to 20 HP.

*Important note:* The animation for this move in Korean Generation II versions matches its Japanese counterpart. For the Japanese and Korean releases of Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, SonicBoom displays a shock wave striking the adversary. Conversely, Western localizations of Sonic Boom utilize the Gust animation, which depicts a tornado impacting the opponent.

Due to the inherently low HP of low-level Pokémon, Sonic Boom is prohibited in Little Cup and Little Battle formats. Despite its evocative name, Sonic Boom is not categorized as a sound-based move. The core series animation for this move bears a resemblance to the similarly named "Sonic Boom" technique executed by the Street Fighter character Guile.

Title: Disable (move)

Known in Japanese as かなしばり (Temporary Binding), Disable is a Normal-type status move that causes no direct damage, first appearing in Generation I . The Ability Cursed Body operates with similar mechanics to Disable. This technique temporarily prevents an opposing Pokémon from utilizing a particular move. Should the afflicted Pokémon possess no other usable moves with PP remaining apart from the one that is disabled, it will resort to using Struggle. For example, if a Pokémon is compelled to employ a specific move due to effects like Encore or holding choice items, but that designated move is disabled, Struggle will be used.

Despite being disabled, the affected Pokémon retains the ability to use the move via Sleep Talk, Metronome, or Mirror Move. An ally's Assist also holds the potential to call forth the disabled move. Disable will fail if the target already has one of its moves rendered unusable by this effect. Furthermore, Pokémon under the influence of Aroma Veil are immune to Disable's effects.

The duration of Disable's effect varies across generations, but it universally concludes if the Pokémon is switched out, faints, or the battle concludes. During Generation I and II, the countdown of turns for the effect to wear off only progresses when the affected Pokémon makes an attempt to use a move. Turns spent in conditions such as flinching, being fast asleep, frozen solid, recharging, or bound do not contribute to the Disable duration in these generations. However, beginning with Generation III , every turn is counted toward the Disable duration, irrespective of whether a move attempt is made. This effect cannot be passed on through Baton Pass.

The specific move to be disabled is chosen randomly from the target's available moves that still have more than 0 PP. Should the target have 0 PP for all of its moves, Disable will not succeed. In the originating generation (Generation I), Disable's effect can persist for a period ranging from 0 to 7 turns. While a move is disabled, the text "disabled!" replaces its PP display, and the target cannot select or use that move until the round after the effect ends (trying to select it simply triggers a "move is disabled" notice). If the target does manage to pick the move (e.g., if it knows the move twice via Mimic, or if the user of Disable is faster and disables the move the target was planning for that round) and attempts its execution before Disable wears off, the turn will be wasted, accompanied by a "move is disabled" message. Given that the check for a disabled move occurs after the duration is reduced, it is possible for Disable to last only 0 turns for the move the target was about to use, resulting in the effect ending immediately and still allowing the target to use that move within the same round.

Even when Disable's attempt is unsuccessful for any reason, applying it to a Pokémon currently using Rage will still cause its rage to accumulate. Pokémon that are locked into multi-turn moves like Thrash, Petal Dance, or Rage will be unable to attack until Disable expires. The counter for Thrash/Petal Dance will not decrease during this period, resuming its normal reduction once Disable dissipates. If an opponent is in the process of charging up a move (such as Razor Wind, Skull Bash, Sky Attack, or SolarBeam) and that move is disabled before its full execution, the move will fail, and the charging status will be reset. Nevertheless, if the Disable counter was set to 0 turns, the effect will terminate prior to the opponent's second turn, allowing the move to be executed completely as normal. In this generation (Generation I), if a Pokémon gains a level in battle and replaces its disabled move with a new one, the newly acquired move will inherit the disabled status.

In Pokémon Stadium, Rage will only build if Disable is successful. The PP display of a disabled move does not change to "disabled!" in Stadium, though attempting to select it still prompts the "move is disabled" notice. From Generation II onwards, Disable's targeting mechanism changed to disable the *last move* the target used. Under this new rule, it fails if the target has not yet used any move or if its last move was Struggle. If the target is currently locked into a consecutively executing move and it becomes disabled before completion, the move sequence will be interrupted. If the target has already acted within that turn before the move is disabled, it remains locked into the move and will attempt to use it again on the subsequent turn, where it will then be unsuccessful. When Disable is boosted by a Normalium Z to become Z-Disable, all of the user's lowered stats are reset. If the target's last used move was a Max Move, Disable will not take effect.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Disable inflicts paralysis upon the foe. From Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Sky, the move is subject to the effects of Magic Coat. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity and subsequent titles, Disable seals the move the target most recently utilized while on the floor. Mewtwo incorporates Disable as its down special move in Super Smash Bros., where instead of preventing a move, it stuns the adversary, creating a brief window of vulnerability. This particular iteration of the move functions only if the opponent establishes eye contact with Mewtwo and is grounded; otherwise, they receive only weak knockback. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Disable, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Kadabra and Slowbro were observed using Disable in this game. In PW18, Slowpoke was revealed to possess knowledge of Disable, though it was not depicted using it. Blue's Venomoth was shown to know Disable via his Pokédex in The Secret of Kangaskhan.

Title: Acid (move)

Known in Japanese as ようかいえき, or Corrosive Liquid, Acid is a Poison-type damaging technique that debuted in Generation I of the Pokémon series. Beyond simply inflicting damage, this move initially possessed a 33.2% probability of decreasing the opponent's Defense stat by a single stage. However, when executed by a non-player character within the game, an extra 25% chance of the secondary effect failing was present, bringing its actual success rate to 24.9%. Subsequently, Acid's ability to lower Defense was adjusted to a 10% chance per usage. The 25% failure chance for in-game opponents persisted, resulting in an effective 7.5% chance for the Defense drop in those scenarios. Notably, this added failure chance did not apply if moves like Lock-On or Mind Reader were active, nor was it present in the Battle Tower. Later, the additional chance for Acid's secondary effect to fail when an in-game opponent used it was removed entirely. The move's secondary effect was then altered to a 10% chance of reducing the target's Special Defense stat by one stage instead of Defense. Within Triple Battles, Acid's targeting scope is limited to only adjacent opposing Pokémon. In the context of the Rescue Team and Explorers series, Acid inflicts damage and lowers the target's Sp. Defense by one stage, although it specifically reduced the Defense stat in Red and Blue Rescue Team. Its use is also influenced by the Muzzled status condition in the Rescue Team and Explorers series. Prior to Version 2.10.0, the in-game description for its additional effect simply stated: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def." Despite the strong reactions real-world acids have with most metals, Acid, being a Poison-type attack, typically has no effect against Steel-type Pokémon in the games.

Title: Ember (move)

This entry pertains to the attack Ember. For information regarding the specific Sevii Islands location, consult Mt. Ember. Known in Japanese as ひのこ (Sparks), Ember is an offensive Fire-type technique that made its debut in Generation I. When used, Ember inflicts damage and carries a 10% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon with a burn. In Pokémon Contests, Ember functions as a follow-up move in a combination; it grants an additional 4 appeal points if Sunny Day was executed during the preceding turn. It causes harm and might inflict a burn. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated it had "a very small chance of leaving the target burned." During the English localized version of "Take the Lombre Home," a wild Solrock was referenced as employing Ember, despite being unable to legitimately acquire this move. This constitutes a dubbing inaccuracy, as the original Japanese script specified Fire Spin instead. Ember has been featured in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer, Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, and Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~. In the episode "Fast-Thinking Froakie," Y instructed her Fletchling, named Fletchy, to utilize Ember, though the move's execution was never depicted. At that point, Fletchling could not learn Ember until its evolution into Fletchinder. Sun's Torracat, Dollar, demonstrated knowledge of Ember in "A Photoshoot and the Abandoned Thrifty Megamart" by employing Inferno Overdrive. Despite this confirmation, Dollar has yet to be observed using Ember directly. On the official website, Green's Moltres, Blaine's Growlithe, The Masked Man's Houndour, and Courtney's Ninetales were indicated to possess Ember, yet none of these Pokémon were ever illustrated performing the attack. Additional mentions include Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder and Pokémon HeartGold SoulSilver: Pokédex Completion Comic. The Latin American localization of the original series rendered Ember as "Ámbar," which translates to "amber," rather than "ember." Furthermore, in "Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire," Ember was erroneously referred to as "Sorpresa," which is the Spanish equivalent of Fake Out.

Title: Flamethrower (move)

This entry discusses the move Flamethrower. For information regarding the TFG starter set, consult Flamethrower (TFG). Flamethrower (Japanese: かえんほうしゃ Flamethrower) is an offensive Fire-type maneuver that inflicts damage, having made its debut in Generation I. It was designated as Technical Machine 35 from the third generation through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, as well as in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it could be found as TM37. Flamethrower deals damage and carries a 10% probability of afflicting the target with a burn condition. Within Pokémon Contests, Flamethrower functions effectively as the second step in a combination, awarding the user an additional 4 appeal points if Sunny Day was executed during the preceding turn.

In the context of Gates to Infinity, when unleashed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Flamethrower begins with a base PP of 20, possesses a base accuracy of 90%, and necessitates a charging turn prior to its execution. When Charizard emerges from a Poké Ball in the original Super Smash Bros. and Super Smash Bros. Melee, it unleashes Flamethrower in a sweeping, side-to-side motion. Upon Charizard's inclusion as a playable fighter in Super Smash Bros. Brawl, Flamethrower became its Neutral Special. The flame's trajectory can be subtly adjusted vertically, and its intensity and reach diminish with continuous application. Its textual description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) stated: Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned.

Within the special Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition game, Flamethrower serves as Charmander's unique Power Card. A player controlling Charmander is permitted to, at any point, confiscate a property from another player and return it to the bank. Should the seized property possess any Poké Marts or a Pokémon Center, its previous owner liquidates these structures back to the bank for half their construction price. This specific Power Card's effect, like all others, can only be activated once per game.

Manga appearances include: Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~ and Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder. In A Tale of Ninetales, Red directed Blue's Charmeleon to employ Flamethrower, but Charmeleon's lack of deference toward Red resulted in the command being ignored. During Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming, Noland instructed a Mawile, rented from the Battle Factory, to use Flamethrower, yet the Pokémon was unable to perform the move due to a Choice Band, which Emerald's rental Linoone had applied via Trick. In Regeneration!! The Power of the Sun and the Moon!, Plumeria's Salazzle was commanded to use Flamethrower, but Lillie's Cutiefly's Stun Spore paralyzed it, preventing execution of the move. Further appearances are noted in Pocket Monsters Diamond and Pearl: Regigigas Major Capture Operation!! and Pokémon HeartGold SoulSilver: Pokédex Completion Comic.

Title: Mist (move)

First appearing in Generation I, Mist (Japanese: しろいきり White Mist) is an Ice-type move that causes no direct damage. Its initial function was to shield the user from stat reductions caused by opposing status moves until the user was switched out; however, damaging moves with secondary effects that lowered stats, such as Acid or Psychic, could still affect the Pokémon's attributes. This protection did not negate existing stat modifications nor prevent the user from decreasing its own stats. Previously, Haze could clear the Mist effect. The move itself would fail if its effects were already active, and it provided no additional benefit when Guard Spec. was already in effect.

In its evolved form, Mist now safeguards the user from all opponent-induced stat decreases, including those from damaging moves, with the protection lasting until the user switches out. Furthermore, Haze is no longer able to clear the Mist effect, and its benefits are transferable via Baton Pass. Currently, using Mist establishes a protective field effect on the user's side of the battle for five turns, safeguarding all Pokémon present on that side, including any switched in after its activation. This field effect is dispelled if any Pokémon on the affected side is struck by Defog. While under the influence of Mist, protected Pokémon cannot have their stats lowered by other Pokémon (even allies), whether through moves or Abilities (such as Intimidate). However, Pokémon are still able to decrease their own stats.

When empowered by an Icium Z into Z-Mist, the user completely restores its HP. In the Mystery Dungeon series, Mist inflicts the Mist status condition, which prevents other Pokémon from lowering the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, evasiveness, and accuracy (encompassing stat multipliers from moves like Charm, Aurora Beam, Memento, and Screech). This status persists for 11-14 turns, or 21 turns. In the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, an enemy's use of Defog will prematurely end this status. Pokémon Battle Revolution showcases an animation upon the conclusion of Mist's effect. Guard Spec. provides an identical effect to Mist, even featuring the same on-screen message: "the ally was shrouded in mist".

In Pokémon Yellow, Vaporeon is technically set to acquire both Haze and Mist at level 42; nevertheless, due to a programming error that prohibits a Pokémon from learning two distinct moves at the same level, Vaporeon is unable to learn Mist in Yellow unless through Pokémon Day Care. Mist seems to function as a stat-protection equivalent to Haze, sharing the same type, PP, and Z-Move effect. Their Japanese names are also strikingly similar, and they are typically learned at comparable levels.

Title: Water Gun (move)

Originating in Generation I, Water Gun (Japanese: みずでっぽう Water Gun) is a damage-dealing Water-type attack. In Generation I, it was assigned the Technical Machine number TM12. This offensive maneuver inflicts damage without any accompanying secondary effects. As the second component of a Pokémon Contest combination, Water Gun grants the user 4 bonus appeal points if Mud Sport, Rain Dance, or Water Sport was utilized in the preceding turn.

Regarding Squirtle's Neutral Special move, Water Gun involves charging up and then expelling water at an adversary. This particular application pushes foes away but does not cause damage. In the game Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can learn Water Gun from a Squirtle found in Withered Wasteland. This enables Ditto to spray water to revitalize arid soil and wilted plants, as well as to douse fires. The move can receive a temporary power boost by consuming soups, which permits Ditto to charge it for a wider area of effect.

In the episode "Shapes of Things to Come!", Paul's Azumarill was instructed to execute Water Gun, but it was defeated before the command could be carried out. From "Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~ In Wartortle Wars," Red's Pokédex revealed that Green's Wartortle, Blasty, knew Water Gun. On the official website, Yellow's Seadra was confirmed to know Water Gun, though it was never shown performing the move. The glitched Pokémon MissingNo. possesses the unusual ability to acquire two instances of this move simultaneously. Notably, Water Gun was the first move depicted in the Pokémon Adventures manga.

Title: Hydro Pump (move)

Known in Japan as ハイドロポンプ (Hydro Pump), this Water-type attack made its debut in Generation I, dealing direct damage without any additional effects. In Pokémon Contests, it serves as the second move in a combination, awarding the user an extra 4 appeal points if Rain Dance was performed in the preceding turn. Blastoise executes this maneuver by unleashing a pair of smaller water jets instead of a singular, powerful stream. Within Gates to Infinity, when a gigantic Pokémon employs Hydro Pump, it possesses a starting PP of 20, a base accuracy of 90%, and necessitates a charging turn before its execution. From Super Mystery Dungeon and subsequent titles, Hydro Pump penetrates foes. If a gigantic Pokémon utilizes it, the attack also breaches walls. Before version 2.38.0, this particular buddy move was identified as B Hydro Pump.

Earlier descriptions of this move, specifically before Version 2.20.0 (as of April 27, 2022), stated its power augmented when the target was confined. Another previous description, also before Version 2.20.0 (from April 27, 2022), indicated its strength grew when none of the target's stats were boosted. Notably, unlike certain sync moves, this particular attack was incapable of being upgraded to 6★ EX from its initial launch on April 30, 2020, for a duration of 263 days, finally gaining the upgrade option on January 18, 2021.

Across the Super Smash Bros. series, Hydro Pump makes several appearances. In both Super Smash Bros. and Super Smash Bros. Melee, Blastoise, emerging from a Poké Ball, employs Hydro Pump to inflict harm upon and repel rival combatants with multiple, non-reflectable blasts. In Super Smash Bros. Brawl and Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, Kyogre takes Blastoise's place, though its water jets do not injure foes. Squirtle, as part of Pokémon Trainer's Triple Finish Final Smash, utilizes Hydro Pump in Super Smash Bros. Brawl and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate. Furthermore, Greninja's up special move in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate is Hydro Pump; Greninja employs a pair of Hydro Pumps for self-propulsion, capable of pushing adversaries back. This dual application allows Greninja to move in two distinct directions based on simultaneous input.

In the specialized Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Hydro Pump is designated as Squirtle's unique Power Card. This grants the player using Squirtle a single-game opportunity to dismantle either the Poké Marts or the Pokémon Center situated on a chosen property. The original owner of those structures then claims half of their construction cost from the bank.

Official website disclosures indicated that Yellow's Omanyte, Omny, and Ghetsis's Seismitoad possessed knowledge of Hydro Pump, although neither Pokémon was ever depicted executing the technique. Dawn's Piplup made an unsuccessful attempt at using Hydro Pump in PDP09. Black instructed Gyarados to deploy Hydro Pump during "A Spectacular Battle To Save Ampharos!!," but Jasmine's Steelix incapacitated Gyarados before the move could be performed. Multiple Kids' WB! promotional materials feature the move's name erroneously spelled as Hydropump.

Prior to Pokémon X and Y in 3D titles, Blastoise's usage of this attack involved two streams emanating from its cannons, a distinct visual from the single stream produced by all other Pokémon. This unique animation for Blastoise was absent from Pokémon X and Y onwards, until its reintroduction upon Blastoise's return in The Indigo Disk. Beginning with Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the move's sound effect reverted to its original Generation I audio. Ash's Squirtle's depiction of Hydro Pump within Pokémon the Series pays homage to Gamera's signature spinning flight. This specific rotational movement subsequently inspired Blastoise's Rapid Spin animation in Pokémon UNITE and Pokkén Tournament DX.

Title: Surf (move)
Known in Japan as なみのり (Surfing), Surf is a Water-type attack that inflicts damage, making its debut in Generation I. It is accessible as TM94 in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it functions as TM47. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it is assigned TM95. When an opponent is in the semi-invulnerable phase of Dive, Surf can strike them. This move inflicts double damage across all generations, with the exception of Generation IV, where its power is instead doubled, yielding a largely identical outcome. Should a Cramorant possessing the Gulp Missile Ability execute Surf (or Dive), it will capture prey and transition into a different form, provided it hasn't already caught prey or isn't currently Dynamaxed.

In a Double Battle scenario, Surf targets both enemy Pokémon. For Pokémon Contests, Surf serves as an initial combination move, making Dive earn double its standard appeal when used immediately afterward. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user an extra 3 appeal points if Dive or Rain Dance was performed on the preceding turn. Furthermore, in Double or Triple Battles, Surf now impacts all adjacent Pokémon, including allies. Its base power has seen a reduction from 95 to 90. Beyond damage, Surf can also dismantle stalagmites appearing in battle backgrounds.

Across all main series titles up to Generation VI, including Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Surf functions as a field move, enabling players to traverse water bodies outside of combat. During Generation I, activating this move solely involved selecting it from the party menu while the player stood on land facing a water tile. From Generation II onward, players gained the option to initiate Surf simply by interacting with a water tile using the A button. A player actively surfing can dismount and walk by stepping onto dry land. An alternative method to stop surfing involves facing a land tile and re-selecting Surf from the party screen. While riding a Pokémon on water, encounters with both Trainers and wild Pokémon are possible (though in Generation I, only specific locations yield Pokémon discoverable through surfing). The moves Waterfall, Whirlpool, and Dive are exclusively usable outside of battle when the player is currently surfing. Starting with Generation III, players also gained the capability to fish for Pokémon while surfing.

Surf is indispensable for completing nearly all games where its out-of-battle utility is present, with Pokémon Black and White being the sole exception. This technique is crucial for accessing certain cities, towns, and eventually the Pokémon League, home to the Elite Four. Every game features a distinct musical theme for Surf, which overrides the standard overworld music when the move is activated outside of combat. Specific games mandate possession of a particular gym badge for Surf's out-of-battle use; these include the Soul Badge, Fog Badge, Balance Badge, Relic Badge, Fen Badge, or Rumble Badge.

Surfing velocity has fluctuated across different titles: In Generations I and II, as well as in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, it matches walking speed. In Generation III, and from Pokémon Platinum onward, the speed aligns with running. Notably, in Generation VI, riding Kyogre while surfing is quicker than average, and riding Sharpedo doubles the usual speed. When Surf is employed outside of combat, the player assumes control of the Pokémon used for surfing. A universal aquatic Pokémon silhouette typically represents most or all Pokémon in a particular game. For Generations I and II, the player character's sprite is absent while surfing, with only the Pokémon visible. However, from Generation III onward, the player character is also depicted visibly riding the surfing Pokémon.

In Generation I, the surfing graphic for nearly all Pokémon mimics the appearance of Seel and Dewgong (this graphic also serves as the party icon for fifteen Water-type Pokémon). Generation II largely portrays surfing Pokémon using Lapras's party icon, adopting the player's color scheme (red for male players, blue for female players). In Pokémon Yellow, Gold, Silver, and Crystal, should Pikachu utilize Surf outside of combat, its unique sprite depicts a surfing Pikachu. If a Pichu evolves into Pikachu or a Pikachu into Raichu while currently surfing, the surfing sprite persists until that Pokémon next employs Surf. Uniquely in Generation VI, specifically surfing on Lapras will render the player mounted on Lapras itself, diverging from the standard generic form. Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire feature distinct surfing models for Kyogre, Wailmer, and Sharpedo; Kyogre, moreover, boasts increased speed and a larger hitbox, while Sharpedo travels at double the normal pace but precludes fishing. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, when Surf is engaged through the Hidden Moves app, the player is depicted riding a Bibarel.

Various games feature water currents that compel a surfing player to move in a predetermined direction. These are observable in the Seafoam Islands, Hoenn's Routes 132, 133, and 134, and Unova's Route 17. Conversely, some titles present shallow water and puddles where players walk rather than surf; examples include the shallow areas of Shoal Cave and certain puddles on Hoenn Route 120. In Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, specific fast currents in the Sevii Islands render Surf unusable.

In Generation I, upon the precise moment Surf is activated from the party menu (transitioning between land and water, or vice versa): Pokémon placed in the Day Care do not accrue experience. This action does not contribute to a poisoned Pokémon's HP loss occurring every four steps. The step counter for Repel, Super Repel, and Max Repel remains unchanged. Nonetheless, moving directly from water to land *does* register as a standard step. A Generation I glitch prevents Surf's out-of-battle use (and Bicycle use) in a new game if the player saves at Cycling Road before starting it. This restriction persists until the player performs one of the following actions: faints (in battle or overworld), uses Dig, Teleport, or Fly outside of battle, utilizes an Escape Rope, or enters and exits the Cycling Road.

Beginning with Generation VII, Surf ceased to be a field move. In Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, its previous functions were transferred to the Ride Pokémon Lapras and Sharpedo. Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! offer the Secret Technique Sea Skim as a replacement. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, water travel becomes possible after upgrading the Rotom Bike.
