Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl reinstate Surf's field utility, though it is now accessed through the Pokétch rather than a party Pokémon. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the player's Koraidon or Miraidon navigate water using their Swimming Build or Aquatic Mode, respectively.

Surf deals damage and specifically inflicts double damage upon targets in the midst of using Dive. Within Super Smash Bros. Brawl, Piplup summons and rides a wave, displacing any combatants in its path. Those enveloped by the wave are propelled until Piplup vanishes or they are forced off the stage. Piplup continues to employ Surf even when airborne. In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, Oshawott executes a move with comparable results. As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Surf has been available since December 8, 2017. Before version 2.38.0, this buddy move was identified as B Surf. Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can utilize Surf after acquiring it from a Lapras on Bleak Beach. This enables Ditto to transform into a Lapras, allowing unhindered passage across water bodies. Ditto's PP steadily diminishes throughout this transformation.

Lucy's Milotic was shown to possess Surf via Emerald's Pokédex in *Just My Luck...Shuckle*. Lance's pair of Dragonair were indicated to know Surf on the official website, though they were never depicted performing the move. Certain Pokémon capable of learning Surf, such as Barboach, seemingly lack the physical attributes necessary to carry a human across water. Junichi Masuda, in an interview, disclosed Surf as his preferred move.

Surf and Fly stand as the sole HM moves that gain special move status for specific Pokémon, namely Pikachu. However, as of Generation VIII, Pikachu can acquire this move through TM/TR, removing its "special move" designation. In Generation VI, Surf and Fly shared the distinction of possessing the highest base power among all HM moves. Before Generation VI, Surf alone held this highest base power. Using Secret Power in the ocean during Generation III triggers Surf's animation. An unimplemented item exists in Red, Blue, and Yellow, which would have enabled water traversal identical to Surf. Muddy Water's animation mirrors Surf's, but with brown water instead of blue. In the Stadium series, Colosseum, XD, and Battle Revolution, when Pikachu uses Surf, a surfboard appears underneath it, and when Raichu uses Surf, it surfs on its tail.

Title: Ice Beam (move)

A damage-dealing move of the Ice type, Ice Beam (れいとうビーム, or Freezing Beam in Japanese) made its debut in Generation I. It served as TM13 during Generation I, then again from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and will also feature in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned TM55. Beyond simply causing damage, this attack carries a 10% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon frozen solid. In the context of Gates to Infinity, a Gigantic Pokémon utilizing Ice Beam will find it has a base PP of 20, a base accuracy of 90%, and necessitates a turn for charging before execution. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated it "Has a very small chance of leaving the target frozen." As a buddy move, it was previously identified as B Ice Beam until version 2.38.0. The English dub of The Mandrin Island Miss Match incorrectly referred to Prima's Jynx's Ice Punch as Ice Beam. Furthermore, in Lapras Lazily, Blue's Pokédex indicated that Evan's Lapras knew Ice Beam. Emerald's Pokédex later confirmed that both Crystal's Starmie and Lucy's Milotic possessed the move Ice Beam in Just My Luck...Shuckle. Mitsumi made mention of her Glaceon employing Ice Beam in A New Battle Begins!, though its use occurred off-panel. While its freezing effect appears quite frequently in the animated series, its actual in-game probability of freezing an opponent remains low at 10%. Remarkably, nearly all Ice-type Pokémon are capable of learning this technique, with the notable exceptions of Snom, Chien-Pao, and Frost Rotom.

Title: Blizzard (move)

This entry focuses on the move Blizzard. For information on the weather phenomenon, refer to Hail (weather condition). Known in Japanese as ふぶき (Blizzard), this Ice-type attack debuted in Generation I and inflicts damage upon its target. It was designated as TM14 from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the TM51 slot.

Blizzard's primary effect is dealing damage, accompanied by a 10% probability of freezing the adversary. Its accuracy stood at 90% in Generation I. A notable regional difference existed: in the Japanese versions of the Generation I core series games, Blizzard possessed a 30% chance to freeze. This likelihood was subsequently decreased to 10% for Pokémon Yellow's Colosseum 2, all Stadium titles, and every international Generation I release. The English manual for Pokémon Yellow inaccurately stated that "Blizzard's probability of freezing changes by 10%" when used in Colosseum 2, which was a mistranslation, as the actual change only affected the Japanese version's higher freeze rate.

In Double Battles, Blizzard strikes both opposing Pokémon. For Triple Battles, its effect is restricted to adjacent opponents. Its base power underwent a revision, moving from 120 down to 110. During Horde Encounters, this move targets all adversaries simultaneously.

Specific environmental interactions affect Blizzard: it always connects, bypassing accuracy checks, when used during hail or snow conditions, unless the target is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. However, a glitch in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl caused Blizzard to have a 30% chance to hit targets using Protect or Detect even during hail; this was rectified from Pokémon Platinum onward.

Blizzard is categorized as a wind move, meaning it will trigger the Wind Power or Wind Rider Abilities of any Pokémon it strikes. Its mechanics bear a resemblance to Thunder and Hurricane, which coincidentally also share a 70% accuracy in turn-based battles. Visually, a small, snowy wind effect materializes beneath the target upon the move's wind-up, intensifying into the full blizzard for delayed damage.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Blizzard serves as a valid second move, granting the user 4 extra appeal points if Hail or Powder Snow was previously used. Similarly, in Contest Spectaculars, it can form a combination, adding 3 bonus appeal points if Hail was employed on the prior turn.

Its functionality varies in other games:
*   In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Blizzard, like all other moves, affects only a single target.
*   For the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Blizzard inflicts damage and may freeze foes. Since Explorers of Time and Darkness, it targets all enemies within the same room and never misses during Hail.
*   A Gigantic Pokémon wielding Blizzard in Gates to Infinity will find its base PP set at 20 and base accuracy at 90%.
*   In Pokémon Masters EX, specific passive skills such as Eusine & Suicune's Piercing Gaze and Red (Champion) & Articuno's Journey from Pallet ensure Blizzard never misses. This sync move became eligible for 6★ EX upgrades on December 25, 2023, 1580 days after its initial release on August 29, 2019. Older descriptions in Masters EX include "Never misses during a hailstorm. Has a moderate chance of leaving the target frozen." (prior to Version 2.10.0, June 28, 2021) and "This move's power increases when the target is frozen." (prior to Version 2.20.0, April 27, 2022).

Beyond battle, Blizzard can dislodge Berries from Berry trees visible in battle backgrounds. Every Ice-type Pokémon, with the sole exception of Snom, possesses the capability to learn Blizzard.

Title: Psybeam (move)

Psybeam (Japanese: サイケこうせん Psyche Beam), a Psychic-type damaging attack, made its debut in Generation I. Currently, trainers can acquire it as TM016 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This attack delivers direct damage and carries a 10% probability of leaving its target confused. In Pokémon Contests, Psybeam functions as the second move of a combination; performers receive an additional 3 appeal points if Calm Mind had been executed on the preceding turn. Generally, Psybeam inflicts damage and has the potential to leave the target confused. An earlier in-game description, prior to Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), stated: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target confused." Intriguingly, Black's Musharna, nicknamed Musha, from Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~ was confirmed to know Psybeam on the official website, though no battle footage ever depicted him utilizing it. Generations VI and VII saw Psybeam incorporate an enhanced iteration of the sound effect originally heard in Generations I and II.

Title: Bubble Beam (move)

Originating in Generation I, Bubble Beam (バブルこうせん in Japanese, also known as Bubble Beam) is a Water-type offensive technique that deals damage. Before Pokémon X and Y, its name was consistently written as BubbleBeam. In its debut generation, it was assigned Technical Machine number 11 (TM11).

Beyond inflicting damage, BubbleBeam initially carried a 33.2% probability of decreasing the foe's Speed statistic by a single stage. However, if an in-game adversary employed it, an extra 25% chance of its secondary effect failing was present, bringing the effective chance down to 24.9%.

Currently, BubbleBeam possesses a 10% chance to reduce the target's Speed stat by one stage. When utilized by an in-game opponent, this still includes an additional 25% chance for the effect to be nullified, leading to an actual success rate of 7.5%. These opponent-specific failure chances do not apply if Lock-On or Mind Reader are active, nor within the confines of the Battle Tower. More recently, Bubble Beam's secondary effect no longer faces an additional chance to fail when employed by an in-game opponent.

In Pokémon Contests, Bubble Beam serves as a viable second move in a combination, awarding its user one additional appeal point if Rain Dance was performed on the preceding turn. Within certain game mechanics, Bubble Beam inflicts damage and has the potential to reduce the opponent's Travel Speed by one stage.

Regarding descriptive changes for a sync move: Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Speed." A description from prior to Version 2.20.0, dated April 27, 2022, mentioned: "The more the user's Sp. Def is raised, the greater the power of this move." This particular sync move was not eligible for promotion to 6★ EX until March 29, 2021, a span of 578 days following its initial release on August 29, 2019. The 6★-EX effect description, valid before Version 2.18.5 on March 15, 2022, read: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents."

Within the narrative of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Sky - Beyond Time & Darkness, Corphish, a member of Wigglytuff's Guild, claimed proficiency with Bubble Beam, though he was never depicted performing it. During the episode "Enter The Dragonite," Ash directed his Squirtle to employ Bubble in the dubbed version; however, in the original Japanese rendition, the command given was for Bubble Beam. The move's animation is also referenced in relation to "Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom"; specifically, when Secret Power is used on a pond in Generation III, it takes on the visual appearance of Bubble Beam.

Title: Aurora Beam (move)

Aurora Beam (Japanese: オーロラビーム Aurora Beam) is an Ice-type damaging maneuver that first appeared in Generation I. Initially, Aurora Beam inflicted damage and carried an 85/256 (~33.2%) chance of decreasing the opponent's Attack stat by one stage. If deployed by a computer-controlled adversary during this period, there was an additional 25% likelihood for the effect to fail, resulting in an effective success rate of 24.9%.

Currently, Aurora Beam possesses a 10% chance to lower the target's Attack stat by one stage. Prior to this change, when executed by an in-game opponent, the move still maintained a supplementary 25% chance for its effect to be negated, culminating in an effective chance of 7.5%. This particular failure condition, however, did not apply when Lock-On or Mind Reader were active, nor within the confines of the Battle Tower. As of more recent generations, the move's secondary effect is no longer subject to an additional failure chance when utilized by an in-game foe.

Beyond inflicting direct harm, Aurora Beam is also capable of spiritually reducing the target's Attack. This unique reduction halves the Attack stat, allowing it to stack down to 1/128, and notably operates distinctly from conventional stat stage alterations. This precise characteristic is shared exclusively with the moves Charm and Memento.

In Pokémon GO, Aurora Beam functions as a Charged Attack, having been made available since February 16, 2017. A noteworthy incident occurred in "Zygarde Appears" where Y's Xerneas, named Xerxer, under Essentia's control, was commanded to unleash Aurora Beam, only for the attack to be interrupted by a strike from X's Pinsir, Rute. It's worth mentioning that the Pokémon Stadium Official Player's Guide from Nintendo Power mistakenly asserted that this move also held a possibility of freezing the opponent.

Title: Hyper Beam (move)

For details on the Japanese merchandise line sharing this name, consult the Hyper Beam collection entry. Known in Japanese as はかいこうせん (Destruction Beam), Hyper Beam is a Normal-type offensive maneuver originating in Generation I. It served as Technical Machine (TM) 15 from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, as well as in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned TM48.

This attack deals damage, but mandates a recovery turn immediately following its use, rendering the user immobile during that period. The compulsory recharge is bypassed under several conditions: if the move fails to hit, if it shatters a substitute, if it defeats the target, if the user becomes the focus of a binding move (even a miss), if the user flinches, or if the user falls asleep after the attack concludes but prior to their recharge phase. Nevertheless, being frozen does not exempt the Pokémon from Hyper Beam's subsequent recharge requirement. Should the user be afflicted with confusion, the turn spent recharging Hyper Beam will not reduce the duration of their confused state.

In a scenario where an opposing Pokémon initiates with a binding move that misses, yet the other Pokémon is in the midst of recharging from Hyper Beam, the Hyper Beam user will automatically unleash the move again that turn instead of recovering. Even if Hyper Beam's Power Points (PP) are depleted to 0 in this specific instance, the move will still execute, after which its PP will reset to 63, benefiting from any applied PP Ups to reach its maximum. During the recharge period, adversaries can still employ items on the user, in which case the user's recharge will be postponed until the subsequent turn.

Within Pokémon Stadium, Hyper Beam mandates a recharge phase following every activation. In this game, a recharge is invariably required if Hyper Beam connects, even if it faints the opponent or breaks a substitute, unless the battle concludes on that same turn or the attack itself misses. The notorious binding move glitch has since been rectified. For Pokémon afflicted with the Truant ability, the recharge effect and the "loafing around" turn coincide. Hyper Beam's damage category transitioned from physical to special, concurrent with the introduction of its physical counterpart, Giga Impact.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, instead of a recharge, Hyper Beam impairs the user's action speed. Within the Aquapolis e-Reader minigame Mighty Tyranitar, Tyranitar employs Hyper Beam to obliterate rocks launched from an active volcano. Distinct from the core series titles, this iteration of Hyper Beam requires no charging and permits continuous use.

Hyper Beam causes damage. Should the attack land successfully, the user is inflicted with the recoil status for a single turn (before Gates to Infinity, this effect was the Paused status), making them unable to act on their subsequent turn. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon and Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, Hyper Beam detonates a 3x3 explosion centered on the target (or a wall), harming both allies and foes according to the standard damage formula, and demolishing any walls or items within its radius. When deployed by a Gigantic Pokémon, the move's reach extends up to 10 tiles in front of the user, penetrating both enemies and walls (without destroying the latter), thereby impacting all adversaries within its 10-tile trajectory.

It transforms into a Dragon-type attack when utilized by Lance & Dragonite, Lance & Dragonair, and Lance & Kingdra, thanks to their Dragon Shift passive skill. For Arc Suit Lance & Dragonite, courtesy of their passive ability The Caped Dragon Master, it functions as a Dragon-type move, requires only two move gauge slots, and possesses perfect accuracy. It becomes a Bug-type move when executed by Alder & Volcarona and Arc Suit Alder & Volcarona, attributed to their respective passive skills Bug Shift and The Wandering Champion. Lance (New Year's 2021) & Gyarados unleash it as a Flying-type attack because of their Flying Shift passive skill. Blue (Classic) & Aerodactyl's Rock Shift passive skill causes it to be a Rock-type move. When Blue & Arcanine employ it, Fire Shift makes it a Fire-type move. Iris (Fall 2022) & Naganadel's Poison Shift passive skill renders it a Poison-type attack. It is a Water-type move when Scottie/Bettie & GyaradosEgg use it, owing to their Water Shift passive skill. Volo & Togepi utilize it as a Fairy-type move thanks to their Piqued Curiosity passive skill. Additionally, Arc Suit Lance & Dragonite's passive skill The Caped Dragon Master ensures it is a Dragon-type move. For Arc Suit Alder & Volcarona, their passive skill The Wandering Champion also makes it a Bug-type move. Irida (Anniversary 2025) & Typhlosion's Soul-Eating Space passive skill causes it to manifest as a Poison-type move.

Mewtwo is confirmed to possess knowledge of Hyper Beam, though its only recorded use was in the radio drama The Birth of Mewtwo, meaning no visual depiction exists of its execution. Baron Alberto, in his Pokémon form, was noted to know Hyper Beam, a fact inferred from his Lickilicky's mastery of the technique. Dahlia's Togekiss was mentioned as knowing Hyper Beam in Exit Empoleon, but the move was never actually displayed. In GDZ66, Shu instructed Dragonair to execute Hyper Beam, but ultimately revoked the command.

A strong correlation exists between the TM learnsets of Hyper Beam and Giga Impact; most Pokémon capable of learning one via TM can also learn the other, and vice-versa. Both Hyper Beam and Giga Impact are accessible via TM for numerous fully evolved Pokémon that are TM-compatible, encompassing even those that gained additional evolutions later, like Porygon or Seadra. Uniquely in Generation I, the Dratini evolutionary line constituted the sole non-fully evolved or single-stage Pokémon able to learn Hyper Beam, albeit exclusively through leveling up rather than via TM, potentially an oversight.

A Pokéquiz segment on Kids' WB!'s Pokémon: Orange Island Adventures, broadcast during Pokémon Food Fight!, displayed Hyper Beam's name incorrectly as "Hyperbeam." The animated series' Brazilian dub has attributed numerous alternate names to Hyper Beam, including Hiper-farol, Hiper-fusão, Super-chama, and notably Raio Solar, which often leads to misunderstanding as it is also the Brazilian dub equivalent of Solar Beam. Particularly in its initial seasons, the Dutch dub of the animated series frequently mistranslates the move as "Hydro Straal," meaning "Hydro Beam." Both the Generation I titles and Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen feature a Cinnabar Gym quiz question inquiring whether TM28 contains "Tombstoner" or "Tombstony." In the Japanese originals, the move is rendered as しねしねこうせん (Shineshine Kōsen), a playful reference that can be interpreted as "Die-Die Beam" or "Die! Beam," creatively echoing Hyper Beam's Japanese designation, はかいこうせん (Hakai Kōsen), or "Destruction Beam." From Generation VI onward, the move's sound effect reverted to its original auditory signature from Generations I and II.

Within Pokémon Masters EX, Hyper Beam holds the distinction of being the inaugural usable move demanding four move-gauge slots. It made its debut with Lance & Dragonite on December 26, 2019.

Title: Peck (move)

The Flying-type attack known as Peck (つつく Peck in Japanese) was first introduced in Generation I and is capable of inflicting damage. Beyond its standard damage output, Peck possesses the unique ability to strike foes not directly next to the user during Triple Battles. When utilized as the opening move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Peck enhances the score of subsequent Drill Peck or Fury Attack uses, causing them to earn twice the typical appeal on the following turn. In the narrative 'Perturbed by Pachirisu,' Platinum's Prinplup was shown to have employed Peck, although its execution occurred outside of direct view. During 'Piplup's Special Training,' Piplup received instructions to perform Peck, yet it opted to unleash Bubble Beam instead. Certain game entries within the Pokédex describe Pidgeotto as a creature that "will fiercely peck at any intruder." Curiously, despite this description, Pidgeotto does not have the capacity to learn Peck.

Title: Drill Peck (move)

Known in Japanese as ドリルくちばし (Drill Beak), Drill Peck is an offensive Flying-type maneuver that first appeared in Generation I, delivering direct damage. This straightforward attack's sole purpose is dealing damage, lacking any auxiliary effects. Within Triple Battles, this move possesses the capability to strike foes that are not immediately adjacent. In Pokémon Contests, Drill Peck qualifies as the concluding move in a combination; employing it after the move Peck in the preceding turn awards the performer an extra 4 appeal points. Regarding its sync move variant, the option to elevate it to 6★ EX status became available on November 16, 2020. This date marked precisely 340 days following its initial release on December 12, 2019. Before Version 2.18.5, implemented on March 15, 2022, the 6★ EX enhancement specifically caused the sync buff to double upon the inaugural use of a sync move.

Title: Submission (move)

Submission, known in Japanese as じごくぐるま (Hell Wheel), made its debut in Generation I as a Fighting-type attack that inflicts damage. During Generation I, it was designated as TM17. While Submission deals damage, its user simultaneously suffers recoil damage amounting to 25% of the total damage inflicted upon the adversary. Should the Pokémon employing Submission act first and subsequently faint from its own recoil, the opponent will not execute a move or endure persistent damage for the duration of that round. Nevertheless, the Submission user remains susceptible to recurrent damage even if the opposing Pokémon is knocked out. Recoil damage taken by the user from Submission on the preceding turn can be negated if the target fails to make a move on the subsequent turn. Breaking a substitute with Submission prevents the user from incurring any recoil damage. Within the Stadium games, no recoil damage is suffered if Submission successfully faints the target. Conversely, the user will still incur recoil damage even when Submission shatters a substitute. In Pokémon Contests, Submission functions as the second move in a combination, awarding its performer 6 additional appeal points if Mind Reader was performed during the preceding turn. Another mechanic dictates that Submission causes damage while its user endures recoil equivalent to ⅛ (or 10%) of their total maximum HP. The Japanese name of this technique, じごくぐるま or Hell Wheel, draws its inspiration from the manga Jūdō Icchokusen, penned by Ikki Kajiwara. Its English appellation is derived from the professional wrestling concept of a submission hold—a diverse range of MMA or jujutsu-influenced grapples like armlocks, leglocks, or chokeholds, performed with the intention of forcing an opponent to yield or become too incapacitated to continue fighting. However, this thematic origin does not align with the move's portrayal in the animated series. Among all moves that inflict recoil, Submission boasts the highest base PP while simultaneously possessing the lowest power. The in-game descriptions for Submission within Pokémon Stadium and Stadium 2 erroneously declare it as the most potent Fighting-type assault; in truth, High Jump Kick surpassed it in base power during Generation I, a status later held by Cross Chop and Dynamic Punch in Generation II. Notably, Submission stands as the sole Fighting-type attack that results in recoil damage upon a successful hit.

Title: Low Kick (move)

Should you be seeking the move known as Low Kick in its Japanese form, please refer to Low Sweep. Low Kick (Japanese: けたぐり Kick Down) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type technique that was first introduced in Generation I. It bore the designation TR07 in Pokémon Sword and Shield and currently functions as TM012 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. In its original iteration, Low Kick had a power rating of 50, an accuracy of 90%, and carried a 30% likelihood of causing the target to flinch. It was not possible for Low Kick to make a target protected by a substitute flinch. Presently, Low Kick boasts a perfect 100% accuracy, and its damage output is now contingent upon the target's weight, dealing intensified harm to heavier opponents. Its power is computed as follows (it is important to note that the games utilize weight in kilograms for all thresholds, not pounds): Low Kick no longer possesses the capability to induce flinching in the target. Across games where individual members of the same species exhibit variable height and weight, Low Kick disregards these individual characteristics, instead accounting solely for the base weight attributed to the target's species in the Pokédex. This move will fail if it is deployed against a Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon. The damage inflicted by Low Kick directly correlates with the target's mass; the greater the weight of the target, the more substantial the damage dealt. For the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, the damage calculation procedure is as follows. In the English dubbed version of 'Ash and Trip's Third Battle!', Trip's Timburr's Low Sweep was mistakenly identified as Low Kick, a misattribution stemming from the fact that Low Sweep is named ローキック Low Kick in the original Japanese version. The genesis of this move is rooted in the sumo technique "ketaguri," a specific kimarite wherein the attacker executes a sidestep at the tachiai, then sweeps the opponent's forward leg from the interior while simultaneously striking their shoulder or pulling an arm. Despite being learnable by the Poliwag evolutionary line via leveling up in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, they were unable to acquire Low Kick through TR07 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. This inconsistency was subsequently corrected when Low Kick made its return as TM012 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Counter (move)

Counter (Japanese: カウンター Counter) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move that debuted in Generation I, where it occupied TM slot 18. Should the user have sustained damage exceeding zero from a Normal-type or Fighting-type assault (excluding Counter itself) just prior to its execution, Counter retaliates by inflicting double that damage upon the foe. Otherwise, the move fails to connect. Despite delivering Fighting-type damage, its efficacy is not subject to type matchups, enabling it to strike Ghost-type Pokémon. When Counter fails because the user took no Normal or Fighting-type damage, the on-screen message indicates a miss.

This move carries a priority of -1. In any given round, if the adversary does not employ a move with similarly reduced priority, Counter will always execute last, irrespective of either Pokémon's speed attribute. However, if the opponent *also* utilizes a low-priority move in the same round, the turn order between the two will follow standard speed mechanics. Should the user fall asleep or become frozen during the turn Counter is deployed (or attempted), its negative priority status remains unaltered. This status is subsequently cleared only upon the user's awakening, defrosting, or withdrawal from battle.

Counter possesses the capacity to land a critical hit, yet the damage output itself remains unaffected by this occurrence. It specifically retaliates against only the final blow of a multistrike move or the concluding strike of a binding attack. Invocation via Metronome invariably results in a miss. Should both active Pokémon simultaneously attempt to use Counter in the same turn, both attempts will be unsuccessful. Damage absorbed by a substitute is eligible for Counter, reflecting the amount that would have been inflicted directly upon the user.

In certain situations, neither swapping out nor executing a multi-turn attack will erase the record of the last damage sustained, enabling Counter to even retaliate against its own prior use or self-inflicted recoil. Furthermore, if the foe consumes an HP-restoring or battle item within the same turn as Counter's deployment, Counter will target the item. More precisely, Counter fails (and reports as a miss) under these conditions:
*   The opponent's immediately preceding move held a power of 0 (note that all damaging moves, even fixed-damage ones like Seismic Toss, possess non-zero power).
*   The opponent's immediately preceding move was neither Normal-type nor Fighting-type, or was Counter itself.
*   The last move used in the skirmish inflicted no damage, unless it was a status move that specifically does not overwrite the data concerning the last damage-dealing attack. Such non-resetting moves include: Conversion, Haze, Whirlwind, Roar, Teleport, Mist, Focus Energy, Supersonic, Confuse Ray, Recover, Softboiled, Rest, Transform, Light Screen, Reflect, PoisonPowder, Toxic, Poison Gas, Stun Spore, Thunder Wave, Glare, Substitute, Mimic, Leech Seed, and Splash.

A desynchronization between players occurs if, after a Normal or Fighting-type attack dealt damage, a Pokémon attempts a different type of attack, gets fully paralyzed, and the foe uses Counter. Similarly, desynchronization also arises if damage was dealt by another attack type, a Pokémon then selects a Normal or Fighting-type attack but experiences full paralysis, and the opponent responds with Counter. Within the Stadium series, however, various actions such as switching, experiencing full paralysis, or initiating a multi-turn move *do* clear the record of the last damage taken. This means Counter cannot retaliate against its own prior use or recoil damage if the opponent makes no move on their subsequent turn.

From Generation II onwards, Counter's mechanics evolved: it now targets any physical move, returning double the damage inflicted upon the user. It ceases to retaliate against hits absorbed by a substitute. Additionally, Ghost-type Pokémon become fully immune to Counter's effects. Interestingly, it retains the ability to counter Hidden Power, irrespective of its specific type, and can even counter Beat Up, despite Dark-type moves not being classified as physical at the time. Exclusively in the core series titles, one-hit KO moves like Fissure and Horn Drill (though notably not Guillotine) can be countered for maximum damage if they failed to connect.

Within Double Battles, Counter targets the last opponent who inflicted physical damage upon the user. It is incapable of affecting allied Pokémon. When the user takes no physical damage, the message now explicitly indicates the move 'failed'. As a Pokémon Contest combination move, Counter earns the user 2 extra appeal points if Taunt was performed in the preceding turn. Starting from Generation VI, Counter lost its ability to counter Hidden Power, as Hidden Power transitioned to a special move. In Triple Battles, Counter can strike non-adjacent foes if such a foe was the most recent attacker. If a physical attack hits the Counter user for 0 damage (for example, False Swipe when the user of Counter already has 1 HP), Counter itself transforms into a physical move with 1 base power. In Contest Spectaculars, it can form a combination, granting three additional appeal points if Encore, Taunt, or Torment was employed during the previous turn.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Counter bestows the "Counter" status condition upon the user. This status reflects all damage from physical attacks (including standard A-button attacks) back to adjacent opponents, matching the damage received. Peculiarly, in Explorers of Time and Darkness, despite the in-game Physical/Special categorization, Counter reflects damage from types that were physical *before* the Physical/Special split. This status persists for 7-12 / 6 turns. Pyukumuku, when summoned from a Poké Ball, deploys Counter whenever an adversary approaches too closely, then bids farewell. It is also uniquely throwable as an item. As a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO, Counter has been accessible since February 16, 2017. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Counter was exclusively an opposing Pokémon's ability and unobtainable for player-caught Pokémon; it notably featured as a move used by Boss Lycanroc (Midnight Form).

The Japanese episode 'Charizard Chills' depicted Tad instructing his Poliwrath to prepare Counter, which was then interrupted. Curiously, the English dub rendered this as 'Body Slam'. Across various game localizations, the move's name suffered mistranslations; in Spanish and Italian, it was erroneously rendered as Contador and Contatore respectively (implying 'counter' as a noun for counting). These errors were rectified in Generation VI games to Contraataque and Contrattacco (correctly meaning "counterattack"). The Spanish animated series' dub also corrected to Contraataque, though the Polish dub repeated the initial mistake. In Generation I, Counter held the distinction of being the sole move with decreased priority. Notably, Gengar in Generation I was capable of learning Counter, despite its usual immunity to both Normal- and Fighting-type attacks, which were the only types Counter could retaliate against at that time. Consequently, Gengar could only effectively counter damage from Seismic Toss and Bide, both of which bypass type immunities.

Title: Seismic Toss (move)

A Fighting-type damage-dealing move, Seismic Toss (Japanese: ちきゅうなげ, meaning Earth Throw) made its debut in Generation I. It was designated as TM19 during Generation I and later as TM15 in the games Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This attack delivers damage equivalent to the user's current level. Despite being classified as a Fighting-type move, its specific typing does not influence the amount of damage it causes. Historically, Seismic Toss disregarded type immunities, allowing it to affect Ghost-type Pokémon. However, the game mechanics have since changed, and it is now susceptible to type immunities, thus generally failing to hit Ghost-type targets.

For Pokémon Contests, utilizing Seismic Toss as the second move in a combination grants the user 2 bonus appeal points if Fake Out was performed on the previous turn. Furthermore, it contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding an extra three appeal points if any of the moves Entrainment, Hold Hands, or Play Nice was executed in the prior turn.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, its functionality has varied: in the Rescue Team and Explorers titles, Seismic Toss inflicted damage equal to the user's level. However, starting with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, it transitioned into a status-category move that no longer deals damage. Instead, it launches its target (whether an ally or an opponent) at another Pokémon or a trap. In dungeon environments, enemy Pokémon are capable of using Seismic Toss on other foes to propel them towards the player character.

In Pokémon UNITE, Charizard's Unite Move, Seismic Slam, shares similarities with Seismic Toss.

The anime episode "A Triple Fighting Chance!" depicted Maylene commanding her Machoke to use Seismic Toss, but Ash's Staravia achieved victory before the move could be executed. In "Pikachu's Plan," Ash instructed his Charizard to perform Seismic Toss, though the move's execution occurred off-panel. Across the animated series, Pokémon employing Seismic Toss are frequently shown orbiting the Earth. This visual theme is also present in the games, specifically Generation II, Pokémon Battle Revolution, and Generations VI through IX, where a globe is integrated into the move's animation. This depiction stems directly from its Japanese name, "Earth Throw."

During the initial seasons of the Dutch dub, Seismic Toss was called "aardbeving," which is actually the Dutch word for Earthquake. Likewise, in the Latin American dub during the Pokémon BW: Rival Destinies season, Seismic Toss was referred to as "Tiro Vital," the Spanish equivalent for Vital Throw.

Title: Strength (move)

The combat technique known as Strength (Japanese: かいりき, meaning Super Strength), a damage-dealing Normal-type move, first appeared in Generation I. This particular attack delivers damage without any additional secondary effects. It served as HM04 across Generations I through VI, before transitioning to TM96 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Notably, in Generation VII, Strength was designated as Machamp's signature move.

Beyond battles, Strength offered a valuable field utility, enabling trainers to manipulate boulders. In certain game installments, activating this ability required possessing a specific Gym Badge, such as the Rainbow Badge, Plain Badge, Heat Badge, Mine Badge, or Cliff Badge. Once Strength was activated, players could reposition boulders by walking into their tile, which would then push the boulder into the adjacent tile, provided that tile was unobstructed by another boulder or a wall. Numerous games integrated puzzles necessitating the strategic placement of these Strength-activated boulders. These challenges often involved clearing a blocked path, gaining access to an otherwise unobtainable item, or even activating floor-mounted buttons to remove other obstacles. Furthermore, in locations like the Seafoam Islands, Ice Path, and Blackthorn Gym, boulders could be pushed into holes, causing them to drop to the floor below.

Should a player exit the current area, or even simply move far enough away for a boulder to go off-screen, most boulders within that area would revert to their initial positions, meaning the puzzle would need to be re-solved upon return. An important exception existed for boulders pushed through holes; these remained on the lower floor and did not reset. If a player maneuvered a boulder incorrectly, perhaps trapping it in a corner from which it couldn't be moved further, progress would halt until the puzzle was reset to allow for a fresh attempt.

Strength’s out-of-battle mode would automatically deactivate upon leaving an area, for instance, by passing through a door. When navigating multi-level caves, ascending or descending stairs was considered leaving the area, often requiring players to reactivate Strength multiple times to tackle boulders on different floors. Saving and reloading the game also resulted in Strength deactivating, although any moved boulders in the immediate vicinity would retain their altered positions.

The method of activating Strength evolved over time. Originally, it could be initiated from the party screen at any moment, even if no boulders were present. Later changes allowed activation only by interacting with a visible boulder and pressing the A button, and only when a boulder was actually nearby. Boulders themselves also changed; they became considerably larger and were primarily designed to be pushed into holes to create traversable paths, rather than simply pushed into corners or around obstacles. Once pushed into a hole under these newer mechanics, these boulders typically remained in place on subsequent visits and did not require re-pushing.

In more recent generations, Strength’s out-of-battle capabilities have been largely removed. In Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, these functions were transferred to the Ride Pokémon Machamp. Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! introduced the Secret Technique Strong Push as an alternative for similar tasks. Pokémon Sword and Shield explicitly confirm that Strength cannot be used outside of battle. However, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the move's field utility was restored, operating as it did in Generations IV through VI, though it was accessed via the Pokétch rather than being taught to a Pokémon.

A specific application of Strength’s field effect occurs in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, and their remakes Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, where its use in the Desert Ruins grants entry to a chamber housing the Legendary Pokémon Regirock.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Strength's behavior varies. In the Rescue Team and Explorers titles, it functioned as a status move that propelled the Pokémon directly in front across the room. If the launched Pokémon struck another hostile foe mid-flight, both would incur 10 HP damage as the initial target bounced off. As a non-damaging move in these games, it was susceptible to Taunt. However, from Gates to Infinity onward, Strength became a standard damaging attack in the Mystery Dungeon series.

In the title Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can learn Strength from a Machoke located in Rocky Ridges, empowering it to push heavy objects.

Curiously, when Secret Power is used inside a building in Generation III, its visual effect mirrors the animation of Strength.

Despite HM moves being unavailable for out-of-battle use in the Orre region, Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness features three areas with puzzles akin to Strength challenges. The S.S. Libra and Cipher Key Lair each contain areas blocked by large wooden crates, which players must push to clear paths, bridge gaps, or activate switches to advance or obtain items. These crates, much like pre-Generation V Strength boulders, would reset if the player left and re-entered the area. The third puzzle, on Citadark Isle, involves pushing massive metal blocks down short cliffs to impede lava flows, allowing for progression and item acquisition. Unlike the crates, these metal blocks, once correctly positioned, do not reset.

A glitch in the Generation IV core series games caused a Pokémon's battle sprite to shift two pixels to the right each time it performed Strength's animation.

Machamp’s designation as Strength’s signature user in Generation VII coincided with its new role as a Ride Pokémon, which directly took over the out-of-battle functionality Strength had offered in prior generations.

In Japanese, the name かいりき (Kairiki) for Strength is also used in the category name for Machamp and its pre-evolutions, forming かいりきポケモン (Kairiki Pokémon). Additionally, in both Mandarin and Cantonese Chinese, Strength shares its name with Machamp: 怪力 (Guàilì) and 怪力 (Gwaailihk), respectively.

Title: Absorb (move)

For the skill featured in Pokémon Shuffle, consult Skill (Shuffle) → Absorb. Known in Japanese as すいとる (Absorb), this Grass-type damage-dealing attack debuted in Generation I. It stands as the initial entry in a series of three Grass-type HP-restoring techniques, succeeded by Mega Drain and Giga Drain. Absorb causes harm to the opponent, and the user recovers HP equal to up to 50% of the damage inflicted, with a minimum gain of 1 HP, and never exceeding the Pokémon's maximum HP. Initially, Absorb possesses 20 PP. In localized Generation I titles, if a Substitute is broken by Absorb, the user gains no HP. Conversely, in the Japanese Generation I games, the entire Pokémon Stadium series, and all Generation II releases, Absorb unfailingly misses against a foe protected by a Substitute. Subsequently, Absorb was revised to correctly restore the user's HP when harming a Substitute, even upon its destruction. Against a Pokémon possessing the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user experiences HP loss equivalent to the amount it would have recovered. As part of a Pokémon Contest combination, Absorb functions as a second move, granting the user an additional 2 appeal points if Growth preceded it in the prior turn. Its Power Points (PP) were later augmented to 25. Holding a Big Root causes the HP regeneration to rise by 30%, resulting in 65% of the damage inflicted being restored as HP. However, Big Root does not amplify the damage Absorb inflicts. This move becomes unusable while the user is afflicted by the Heal Block status. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Absorb's base power was adjusted from 20 to 40, concurrently with a PP reduction to 15. Subsequently, its power reverted to 20, and its PP returned to 25. Generally, Absorb deals damage and restores 50% of that damage as HP to the user. Exclusively in Adventure Squad, the HP recovery rate is altered to 20% of the damage dealt. Only in Gates to Infinity does the user's healing rely on the target's HP loss, with any damage exceeding the target's remaining HP not contributing to the recovery. Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Absorb is impacted by the Muzzle status condition. An older description, preceding Version 2.10.0, outlines its additional effect as: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target." Diamond's Turtwig, named Tru, was officially listed on the website as knowing Absorb, though he was never depicted utilizing the technique.

Title: Mega Drain (move)

Mega Drain, known as メガドレイン in Japanese, is a Grass-type damage-dealing move originating from Generation I. It was designated as TM21 during Generation I, and later as TM53 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move occupies the middle position among three Grass-type HP-draining attacks, preceded by Absorb and followed by Giga Drain. Mega Drain delivers damage and concurrently restores the user's HP by an amount equal to up to 50% of the damage inflicted upon the target (never less than 1 HP), potentially bringing the user up to its maximum HP. Initially, Mega Drain possessed a base power of 40 and 10 PP.

Within the localized Generation I games, if this move shatters a substitute, the user gains no HP. However, in the Japanese Generation I games, all iterations of the Pokémon Stadium series, and Generation II games, Mega Drain invariably fails to connect against a target protected by a substitute. Subsequently, Mega Drain gained the ability to correctly heal the user when harming a substitute, even upon its breakage. Should the target possess the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user will instead suffer HP loss equivalent to the amount it would have normally recovered.

In Pokémon Contests, Mega Drain can serve as the second move in a combination, granting the user 1 additional appeal point if Growth was executed in the preceding turn. At one point, its base PP saw an increase to 15. When the user is equipped with a Big Root, the HP regeneration rises by 30%, resulting in 65% of the dealt damage being restored. It's important to note that Big Root has no effect on the damage output of the move. Furthermore, Mega Drain becomes unusable if the user is afflicted by Heal Block.

Specifically in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Mega Drain underwent a power adjustment from 40 to 75, while its PP reverted to 10. This alteration rendered it functionally identical to Giga Drain, a probable design choice given Giga Drain's unavailability in those titles. Subsequently, Mega Drain's power was restored to 40, and its PP returned to 15. The move deals damage and provides HP restoration to the user, recovering up to 50% of the damage dealt to the target, even accounting for excess damage.

Exclusively in Adventure Squad, the healing amount is adjusted to 20% of the damage inflicted. Conversely, in Gates to Infinity alone, the user's HP recovery is calculated based on the target's HP loss, without considering excess damage.

During the dubbed rendition of Battling with Elegance and a Big Smile!, Clemont remarked on Sawyer's Treecko utilizing Mega Drain following their Gym battle. The original Japanese script, however, cited Razor Leaf, an attack Treecko is unable to legally acquire. Lastly, in The Ariados up There, Gold's Sunkern, Sunbo, was shown to know Mega Drain, though Gold's command for the attack was cut short.

Title: Leech Seed (move)

This Grass-type technique, known in Japan as やどりぎのタネ (Mistletoe Seed), made its debut in Generation I as a non-damaging ability. In Contest Spectaculars, it forms a combination: an additional three appeal points are awarded if Rototiller was executed on the preceding turn. When enhanced by a Grassium Z, transforming into Z-Leech Seed, this move fully restores any of the user's diminished stats. Leech Seed targets the opponent positioned directly ahead, afflicting them with a specific status condition. Under this condition, the affected Pokémon suffers a loss of 10 HP or 5 HP at the conclusion of every other turn, simultaneously restoring an equal amount (10 HP or 5 HP) to the Pokémon that initiated the move. This status effect persists for either 11-12 turns or 6 turns, or until the Pokémon that employed Leech Seed is knocked out. Notably, creatures of the Grass type are immune to becoming affected by Leech Seed, and Magic Coat can reflect this technique. Its appearance extends to Super Smash Bros. Melee, where Leech Seed is among the various random effects produced by Togepi's Metronome attack; any character within the affected zone will sprout a flower on their head, causing continuous 5% damage at brief intervals. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Leech Seed, along with all other status moves, was exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and unavailable for acquisition by captured Pokémon, with an example of its use there being by opposing Sunkern. Despite its in-game inability to affect Grass-type Pokémon, the animated series has depicted Grass-type Pokémon being afflicted by it, a curious point given that its Japanese name, "Mistletoe Seed," explicitly refers to a parasitic plant that siphons nutrients from a host.

Title: Growth (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Growth (Japanese: せいちょう Growth) is a non-damaging Normal-type move. Notably, its typing shifts to Grass in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. In its standard application, Growth boosts the user's Special Attack stat by one stage; prior to the release of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, this was its sole stat-enhancing effect. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Growth now also elevates the user's Attack stat by one stage, alongside the Special Attack increase, resulting in a one-stage rise for both. Under the effects of harsh sunlight, Growth's power intensifies, causing it to elevate both Attack and Special Attack by two stages apiece.

As an initial action in Pokémon Contest combinations, Growth sets up several other moves. If Absorb, Bullet Seed, Frenzy Plant, Giga Drain, Magical Leaf, Mega Drain, Petal Dance, Razor Leaf, SolarBeam, or Vine Whip are performed immediately afterwards, they receive double their usual appeal. For Contest Spectacular combinations, Growth offers an additional appeal point if Sunny Day was executed during the preceding turn. When amplified by a Normalium Z into Z-Growth, the user gains an additional stage increase to its Special Attack stat.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Growth, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and was unobtainable on any Pokémon captured by the player. Specifically, Boss Bellsprout and Snivy were observed utilizing Growth in this game. Despite its Normal typing, Comfey stands as the singular non-Grass-type Pokémon capable of learning Growth through leveling up. Beyond event distributions, Illumise and Numel are also able to acquire this move, though solely by breeding with a Pokémon of the Grass type.

Title: Razor Leaf (move)

Originating in Generation I, the Grass-type attack known as Razor Leaf (Japanese: はっぱカッター Leaf Cutter) functions as a damage-dealing technique. This particular move inflicts damage upon its target and is characterized by an elevated likelihood of scoring a critical hit, manifesting as 50% in the Rescue Team series and 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. A Pokémon possessing the Sharpness ability will see Razor Leaf's power amplified by 50% when executing the move. In multi-opponent skirmishes, Razor Leaf strikes every adjacent adversary, with the probability of achieving a critical hit determined independently for each opponent it affects. Furthermore, this move is capable of eliminating grass patches that appear in the background environments of battles.

Within Pokémon Contests, Razor Leaf serves as the concluding move in a combination, awarding the performer 3 additional appeal points if Growth was employed during the preceding turn.

For Ivysaur, Razor Leaf functions as a side special move. Upon activation, a solitary leaf is launched along a somewhat random, though mostly straight, path, inflicting both damage and knockback upon any target struck. Chikorita deploys Razor Leaf after emerging from a Poké Ball in Super Smash Bros. Melee and Super Smash Bros. Brawl. Similarly, Snivy utilizes Razor Leaf upon its release from a Poké Ball in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U; notwithstanding this, Snivy is unable to legitimately acquire this move in the core series titles. Earlier iterations, specifically before version 2.38.0, referred to this buddy move as B Razor Leaf.

During the episode "Battling with Elegance and a Big Smile!", Clemont made observations regarding Sawyer's Treecko's Razor Leaf following their Gym contest. Nonetheless, Treecko lacks the capability to learn Razor Leaf, and in the dubbed version, this was substituted with Mega Drain, a technique Treecko can legitimately master. The official website disclosed that a wild Nuzleaf possessed knowledge of Razor Leaf, though it was never depicted performing the move. Likewise, a wild Sewaddle was stated on the official website to know Razor Leaf, yet its usage of the attack remained unseen.

Within the Pokémon Adventures manga's Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom arc, Razor Leaf stands as one of three attacks upgraded into "new" moves, alongside Thunderbolt and Thunder. Notably, it holds the distinction of having the most extensive "move" name across all media, dubbed "Razor Leaf Dia Special Mach One and Only" by Diamond.

Throughout Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire, the Finnish and Latin American localized versions, likely erroneously, employed identical translated designations for both Razor Leaf and Leaf Blade. This occurred despite the fact that the translation was a more precise match for Leaf Blade, and the original series had previously utilized a distinct, arguably more appropriate, translation for Razor Leaf. An analogous situation arose in the Brazilian Portuguese dub, where the names of Razor Leaf and Leaf Blade were congruent between the episodes "Clamperl of Wisdom" and "From Cradle to Save." The reason for this initial occurrence remains unclear, considering Leaf Blade was identified as Lâmina de Folha up to "The Garden of Eatin'."

Title: Solar Beam (move)

Known as ソーラービーム Solar Beam in Japanese, this damage-dealing Grass-type attack, introduced in Generation I, was officially styled as SolarBeam until Pokémon X and Y. It featured as TM22 across numerous titles, from Generation I up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and notably reappeared as such in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the TM45 slot.

The inherent mechanism of SolarBeam involves a two-turn sequence. On the initial turn of selection, the move performs no action other than signaling that the user is "taking in sunlight." The actual damage output occurs on the subsequent turn, at which point a PP is consumed, and the move is registered as the last one executed. Once SolarBeam is chosen, the user becomes unable to switch out until the move is either fully performed or interrupted. Should SolarBeam fail to execute completely, no PP will be deducted, nor will it count as the last move used.

Regarding interactions, if an opponent employs Mirror Move while the user is gathering sunlight, Mirror Move will replicate the attack the user performed immediately prior to initiating SolarBeam (or fail if no such move exists). Conditions such as sleep, freezing, binding, and flinching will merely suspend SolarBeam's duration without disrupting it entirely. A unique rule in Pokémon Stadium allows Mirror Move to copy SolarBeam on either of its two turns of execution.

Certain weather conditions modify SolarBeam's behavior. When used under harsh sunlight, the move requires no charging turn and attacks instantly. Conversely, its power is halved if unleashed during rain, hail, fog, or a raging sandstorm. Interestingly, if the user is prevented from attacking with SolarBeam during harsh sunlight by status conditions like flinching, paralysis, or confusion, PP will still be deducted. This behavior stems from SolarBeam's fundamental design as a two-turn move. However, if SolarBeam's success is generally thwarted by disruptions such as flinching, paralysis, or confusion, the entire move will now be canceled rather than simply paused. Strong winds, however, exert no influence over Solar Beam's power.

An equipped Power Herb enables SolarBeam to be executed in a single turn by consuming the item. This item consumption is bypassed if harsh sunlight is already present.

In Pokémon Contests, Solar Beam functions as a potential second move in a combination. The user earns 4 bonus appeal points if Growth or Sunny Day was utilized on the preceding turn. For Contest Spectaculars, it can also form part of a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if Sunny Day was used just before. A subtle change in animation now displays the charging half of Solar Beam even during sunny weather, a visual that was previously absent; the actual attack, however, still follows immediately.

A significant power adjustment occurred in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, where Solar Beam's base power was increased from 120 to 200. In general mechanics, the user acquires the "Solar Beam status condition" during the first turn and then releases the attack on the subsequent turn. In Sunny weather or Extremely Harsh Sunlight, the charging phase is skipped, and the move fires instantly. Its damage suffers a reduction in Rain, Heavy Rain, Hail, or a Sandstorm.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series (specifically Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad), Solar Beam's damage is doubled at the conclusion of damage calculation. This damage multiplier is negated in Rain, Hail, or a Sandstorm, effectively halving the overall damage dealt. As a two-turn move, it cannot be linked with other attacks. Furthermore, Solar Beam is prohibited from use in a Pokémon Tower within the Adventure Squad series.

Ivysaur incorporates Solar Beam as a component of Pokémon Trainer's Final Smash, "Triple Finish."
Prior to Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), the description for this move in a related context stated: "The user gets ready to attack. Using this move again will cause the user to attack. No other actions can be taken while ready to attack. The preparation period is skipped if the weather is sunny, and the user can attack right away."

Manga references include its mention in *Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King*. In *Just My Luck...Shuckle*, Crystal's Rapidash was revealed to possess Solar Beam according to Emerald's Pokédex. Gold instructed his Sunflora, Sunbo, to use Solar Beam in *All About Arceus VIII*, though the execution happened off-panel. An anomaly in Generation I permitted Exeggcute to learn SolarBeam by leveling up, distinct from its general TM availability.

Title: Poison Powder (move)

The nomenclature "Poisonpowder" redirects to this entry; for information concerning the Pokémon Play It! Version 2 Theme Deck, please consult the "Poisonpowder Deck (TCG)" article. This non-damaging, Poison-type attack, known in Japanese as どくのこな (PoisonPowder), made its debut in Generation I. Its former spelling was "PoisonPowder" prior to Pokémon X and Y. Poison Powder presents as a poisoning counterpart to Sleep Powder and Stun Spore, sharing identical or highly similar attributes such as their accuracy, Contest group, appeal, effects, Japanese names, and animation. These analogous moves are also typically acquired at comparable levels or through a similar progression.

The move's primary objective is to induce the poisoned status condition upon the target, provided they are susceptible. Pokémon of the Poison and Steel types are inherently immune to Poison Powder's effects. A unique mechanism in the Generation II core series games dictated that when an in-game opponent (excluding those within the Battle Tower) employed Poison Powder, the move possessed a supplementary 25% chance to fail, in addition to its normal chance to miss. Targets possessing the Ability Immunity are likewise unaffected by Poison Powder.

Within Pokémon Contests, Poison Powder functions as the second move in a combination, bestowing the user with 3 bonus appeal points if Sweet Scent was performed in the preceding turn. Furthermore, for Contest Spectaculars, Poison Powder initiates a combination, causing Hex, Venom Drench, and Venoshock to yield an extra three appeal points when utilized subsequently. When amplified by a Poisonium Z into Z-Poison Powder, the user's Defense stat undergoes a one-stage increase.

In more recent contexts, Grass-type Pokémon, those with the Overcoat Ability, and Pokémon equipped with Safety Goggles are now immune to Poison Powder. In *Super Mystery Dungeon*, specifically, Grass-type Pokémon also exhibit immunity, as this move is categorized as a powder-based attack. This move is susceptible to reflection by Magic Coat.

During *Pokémon Rumble Rush*, all status-altering moves, including Poison Powder, were exclusively employed by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any captured Pokémon. Boss versions of Gloom, Venomoth, and Weepinbell were among those observed utilizing Poison Powder in this game. In the English dub of 'A Tent Situation,' James commanded his Victreebel to use Poison Powder; notably, no such move order was issued in the original Japanese version. Blue's Pokédex confirmed that his Venomoth possessed Poison Powder in 'The Secret of Kangaskhan.'

Title: Stun Spore (move)

Debuting in Generation I, the Grass-type technique known as Stun Spore (しびれごな, or Numbing Powder in Japan) is a non-damaging attack. It shares significant similarities with Poison Powder and Sleep Powder, appearing to be their paralysis-inducing counterpart. This kinship is evident in their identical or highly similar accuracy, Contest group, appeal, effects, Japanese nomenclature, and animations. Furthermore, these three moves typically follow analogous learning patterns or are acquired at comparable levels. Stun Spore's primary function is to inflict paralysis upon the target.

Exclusively within the Generation I handheld titles, Stun Spore possessed the unique ability to bypass a substitute. A particular alteration in the Generation II core series games meant that if an in-game opponent (outside of the Battle Tower) attempted to use Stun Spore, the move had a 25% chance of outright failure, beyond its standard accuracy check. Pokémon possessing the Limber Ability are immune to Stun Spore's effects.

In Pokémon Contests, when deployed as the second move in a combination, Stun Spore grants the user 2 additional appeal points if Sweet Scent was executed during the preceding turn. Currently, Pokémon of the Grass and Electric types, those with the Overcoat Ability, and creatures equipped with Safety Goggles are all impervious to Stun Spore. In Contest Spectaculars, Stun Spore contributes to a combination that awards an extra three appeal points to Hex and Smelling Salts if either is used immediately afterwards. When enhanced by a Grassium Z to become Z-Stun Spore, the user experiences a one-stage increase in its Special Defense stat.

This move targets all adjacent opposing Pokémon, afflicting them with paralysis. Stun Spore is susceptible to Magic Coat's effects. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Stun Spore, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured creatures. Notable Boss Pokémon observed utilizing Stun Spore in Rumble Rush included Parasect, Hoppip, Shroomish, Budew, and Scatterbug.

During the English dub of the episode 'Jump for Joy!', a group of wild Oddish was referenced as employing Stun Spore; however, the original Japanese script featured Sleep Powder, a discrepancy further complicated by the dub retaining the sleep-inducing effect despite the altered name. Red's Victreebel was revealed to possess Stun Spore in 'A Hollow Victreebel', though its actual usage was never depicted. Notably, Stun Spore holds the distinction of being the sole Grass-type move capable of paralyzing an opponent.

Title: Sleep Powder (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Sleep Powder (Japanese: ねむりごな Sleep Powder) is a Grass-type technique that inflicts no direct damage. It closely resembles Poison Powder and Stun Spore, functioning as their sleep-inducing counterpart; this similarity is evident in their identical or highly comparable accuracy values, Contest group classifications, appeal scores, in-game effects, Japanese names, and animations. Furthermore, Pokémon typically learn these moves at similar levels or via analogous acquisition patterns. Exclusively within the Generation I handheld titles, Sleep Powder uniquely possesses the capability to affect a target even when shielded by a substitute. In the Generation II core series games, when an in-game challenger (excluding those in the Battle Tower) deploys Sleep Powder, the move carries an additional 25% chance to fail, beyond its regular chance to miss. Targets possessing the Abilities Insomnia, Vital Spirit, or Sap Sipper are immune to Sleep Powder's effects.

As the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Sleep Powder awards the user 1 bonus appeal point if Sweet Scent was performed during the previous turn. Grass-type Pokémon, those with the Overcoat Ability, and Pokémon holding Safety Goggles are all impervious to Sleep Powder. Additionally, Pokémon benefiting from the Sweet Veil Ability are also unaffected by this powder move. When used as part of a Contest Spectacular combination, Sleep Powder causes specific follow-up moves—Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap—to grant an extra three appeal points if utilized in the subsequent turn. If powered up by a Grassium Z into Z-Sleep Powder, the user's Speed stat is boosted by one stage. The primary effect of Sleep Powder is to cause the opponent to fall asleep. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, Grass-type Pokémon cannot be affected by it, as it is categorized as a powder move. This move can be reflected by Magic Coat.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-category moves, including Sleep Powder, were exclusively used by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any captured Pokémon. Boss versions of Venusaur, Victreebel, and Jumpluff were observed using Sleep Powder in that game. Red's Victreebel was revealed to know Sleep Powder in "A Hollow Victreebel," though it was never depicted performing the move.

Title: Petal Dance (move)

The move known as Petal Dance (Japanese: はなびらのまい Petal Dance) is an offensive Grass-type technique that made its debut in Generation I. Until Generation II, it was an exclusive capability of the Oddish evolutionary line. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is identified as TM151.

Petal Dance delivers damage over a span of 3 to 4 turns, boasting a base power of 70 and 20 PP. Its PP cost is incurred solely on the initial turn it is activated. Once initiated, the Pokémon employing it becomes locked into using Petal Dance, even if it fails to connect, and cannot be switched out. Upon the conclusion of Petal Dance, the user experiences confusion, a result of fatigue. Should Petal Dance's execution be interrupted (for instance, by full paralysis or self-inflicted damage from confusion), its effect ceases immediately. Confusion only afflicts the user if Petal Dance completes its entire duration without interruption.

Conditions such as sleep, freezing, being bound, or flinching will momentarily halt Petal Dance's duration without ending it. When modifiers to accuracy or evasion impact Petal Dance's precision, any reduction in hit chance is reapplied for every subsequent automatic turn. Normal accuracy is restored only when the move is explicitly chosen once more. Should Petal Dance miss because of the 99.6% accuracy glitch affecting moves with 100% accuracy, specifically when no other accuracy or evasion alterations are present, or if such modifiers nullify one another, its precision remains unchanged. If Petal Dance fails to connect on the turn it is initially selected, its secondary effects will not trigger.

Within Stadium games, once Petal Dance concludes, a notification confirms the user's confusion. Here, Petal Dance's duration is shortened to 2-3 turns. During multi-opponent battles, the user targets itself, yet strikes a randomly chosen adjacent foe with each individual use of the move. Petal Dance serves as a viable second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, awarding the user an additional 4 appeal points if preceded by Growth.

Petal Dance's base power later saw an increase to 90, with all other aspects remaining identical. Subsequently, its power climbed further to 120, alongside a PP reduction to 10. The move is disrupted if it is unsuccessful due to missing, or if the user is afflicted by sleep, paralysis, freeze, or flinching, or if it is thwarted by a protective move. Even if a disruption takes place on what would have been Petal Dance's concluding turn, which typically causes confusion, the user still becomes confused. Later still, Petal Dance's base power was lowered to 90, and its accuracy adjusted to 85%. It ceased its multi-turn operation and user confusion, instead causing the user to become fixated on employing Petal Dance.

In certain iterations, Petal Dance functions as a multi-strike move with 40 power, inflicting damage three times to foes surrounding the user and causing confusion following its successful execution. Should the user flinch during its wind-up phase, Petal Dance will initiate a cooldown period, but the user will avoid confusion. This move also inflicts damage by striking the target 2-3 times per usage (or 2-5 times before Gates to Infinity). Confusion afflicts the user once the move concludes. It is noteworthy that in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time & Darkness and its follow-up, Sunflora was noted to possess knowledge of Petal Dance, though it was never depicted using the ability in gameplay.

Title: String Shot (move)

Known in Japanese as いとをはく (Spit Thread), String Shot is a Bug-type status move that originated in the first generation of games. It has the effect of reducing the Speed stat of its targets by a single stage. Specifically within Generation I and II core series titles, if employed by an in-game opponent beyond the confines of the Battle Tower, String Shot possesses an additional 25% failure rate on top of its standard accuracy check. During multi-opponent engagements, this maneuver affects all neighboring adversaries. As a starter move in a Pokémon Contest combination, String Shot enables Spider Web to yield twice the standard appeal points when performed immediately thereafter. Currently, String Shot's effect has been enhanced to diminish the targets' Speed stat by two stages. Moreover, it serves as a component in a Contest Spectacular combination, leading Electroweb, Spider Web, or Sticky Web to grant an additional three appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. When amplified by a Buginium Z into Z-String Shot, the Pokémon's own Speed stat experiences a one-stage increase. In another context, this technique reduces the target's Travel Speed by a single stage. Magic Coat is known to affect this particular move. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, String Shot, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured species. Boss Grubbin notably employed String Shot in that game. During the episode The Treasure Is All Mine!, Sebastian commanded his Spinarak to execute String Shot against James; however, James's Mime Jr. intervened by disorienting both Sebastian and Spinarak with Teeter Dance before the move could be unleashed. While Janine's Ariados was confirmed to possess String Shot on the official website, its actual use of the move was never depicted. Similarly, Bugsy's Metapod was instructed to deploy String Shot in Let's Aim For The Goal!, though the move's execution was omitted from view. Uniquely among Bug-type status maneuvers, String Shot stands as the sole one with an accuracy rating below 100%.

Title: Dragon Rage (move)

Known in Japanese as りゅうのいかり (Dragon Rage), this Dragon-type offensive move first appeared in Generation I, dealing damage. During Generation I, trainers could obtain it as TM23. When successfully landed, Dragon Rage consistently deals precisely 40 HP in damage. This move lacks any additional effects and disregards type matchups like weaknesses or resistances for damage calculation, though it still respects type immunities. Exclusively in Generation II, its base power appeared as '40' rather than the usual '—' symbol. In Pokémon Contests, Dragon Rage serves as an opener for a combination; if followed by Dragon Claw, Dragon Dance, or Dragon Breath on the subsequent turn, those moves will earn double the standard appeal. Alternatively, it can conclude a combination, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point if either Dragon Dance or Dragon Breath was executed in the preceding turn. The effect of King's Rock now applies to Dragon Rage, with Generation IV being the sole exception. This attack always deals a predetermined amount of damage to its target. Within the Rescue Team series of games, Dragon Rage inflicts 65 damage on opponents. However, starting with the Explorers series, the damage output decreased to 30. Super Mystery Dungeon implements a variable range for the move, contingent on the user's level. If the Pokémon is below level 40, its range is limited to a single adjacent tile. Between levels 40 and 80, it expands to strike any foe within a fan-shaped zone directly ahead, provided those foes are within the unit's line of sight. Upon reaching level 80 or higher, the move's fan-shaped area of effect in front of the user can target any enemy, even if they are not visible. In the episode "The Dragonite's Imperial Wrath," Clair commanded her Dragonair to unleash Dragon Rage, yet the attack itself was not depicted. Notably, Dragon Rage stands as the sole Dragon-type move available in Generation I. Nevertheless, since it ignores type match-ups, no moves actually inflicted Dragon-type damage during Generation I. Due to the inherently low HP of Pokémon eligible for the Little Cup, Dragon Rage is disallowed in that format within Pokémon Stadium 2 and Pokémon Battle Revolution. Only Growlithe, Arcanine, and Mew, all non-Dragon-type Pokémon, were capable of learning this move, and exclusively during Generation I. Aside from Z-Moves, Dragon Rage holds the distinction of being the sole Dragon-type move unavailable for use in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Fire Spin (move)

Known in Japanese as ほのおのうず (Flame Swirl), Fire Spin is a Fire-type offensive technique that made its debut in Generation I. Initially, it possessed a base power of 15 and an accuracy of 70%. This move applied continuous damage over a period of 2 to 5 turns. The likelihood of these durations was: 37.5% for 2 turns, an equal 37.5% for 3 turns, and 12.5% for both 4 and 5 turns, respectively. While only the initial strike could register as a critical hit, every subsequent turn's damage output remained consistent. Should the Fire Spin user act first, the affected opponent was prevented from attacking for the entire duration of the move, beginning with the turn it was initiated. This inability to attack persisted even if the target utilized a substitute. During its damage-dealing phase, the battle message "<Pokémon>'s attack continues!" was displayed. Throughout the move's active turns, both the user and the target were restricted from selecting new moves (choosing "Fight" made the user maintain the damage output and the target remain idle), although both parties retained the option to switch out. The final turn provided no specific notification, meaning players had to select "Fight" to view their move list and confirm if Fire Spin had concluded. Damage incurred from Fire Spin's ongoing effect was calculated subsequent to any other recurrent damage. Should the attacker withdraw prior to the duration's end, the foe remained unable to attack for that specific turn. If the afflicted Pokémon swapped out before the turns expired, Fire Spin was automatically re-applied to the newly introduced Pokémon, consuming an extra PP and retaining its standard miss probability. Even if Fire Spin had 0 PP in this scenario, it would still be executed, and its PP would then reset to 63, factoring in all PP Ups. A missed Fire Spin still nullified the recharge turn typically imposed by Hyper Beam. Furthermore, if Fire Spin's user acted before a Hyper Beam user on a recharge turn, and Fire Spin missed, the Hyper Beam user would automatically unleash Hyper Beam that same turn. Should Hyper Beam have 0 PP under these circumstances, it would still be fired, after which its PP would cycle to 63, incorporating all PP ups.

Within Pokémon Stadium, players were granted the ability to choose a move on every turn that Fire Spin was active. Should the opponent swap out prematurely, the incoming Pokémon did not face an automatic re-application; instead, the user's subsequent turn was forgone. The negation of Hyper Beam's recharge turn occurred only if Fire Spin successfully connected. The affected Pokémon was now capable of attacking throughout Fire Spin's effect, behaving as normal.

From Generation II onwards, instead of preventing attacks, Fire Spin began to inflict 1/16 of the target's total HP as damage for two to five turns after its initial direct hit. The chance of it attacking for 2, 3, 4, or 5 turns became uniformly set at 25% for each duration. The message "<Pokémon> was trapped!" would now be displayed. It furthermore bound the target, hindering both switching out and escaping. If the user swapped out, the trapped Pokémon was released. A Pokémon under this trapping effect could free itself by using Rapid Spin.
