When a wild Pokémon employed Fire Spin against a player's Pokémon, the player gained the ability to flee if their affected Pokémon possessed the Run Away ability or was equipped with a Smoke Ball. These methods, however, did not permit the player to switch out the Pokémon. The accompanying text also altered to "<Pokémon> was trapped in the vortex!"

If the Fire Spin user held a Grip Claw, the move's duration was fixed at 5 turns. Additionally, a Pokémon afflicted by Fire Spin could switch out if it held a Shed Shell. Fire Spin qualified as a secondary move in Pokémon Contest combinations, granting the user 3 extra appeal points if Sunny Day was performed in the preceding turn.

In a later update, Fire Spin's power was increased to 35 and its accuracy to 85%, with its duration now consistently lasting four to five turns. When the user was equipped with a Grip Claw, Fire Spin's effect would invariably persist for seven turns. Should the user hold a Binding Band, the damage dealt at the conclusion of each turn escalated from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. The in-game message further evolved to "<Pokémon> was trapped in the fiery vortex!" A subsequent enhancement raised Fire Spin's turn-end damage from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum health. If the user was holding a Binding Band, this specific damage further increased to 1/6 of the target's maximum HP. Ghost-type Pokémon were immune to being trapped by Fire Spin.

In more recent main series titles, Fire Spin generates a persistent environmental effect that periodically damages foe Pokémon caught within it. As a Plus Move, its area of effect expands. Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad game series, Fire Spin causes damage and has a chance to apply the Constriction status to enemies. This status prevented the enemy from moving (though they could still attack) and caused a loss of 5 HP every two turns, persisting for 4-6 turns. From Gates to Infinity and beyond, the move instead had a chance to impose the Fire Spin status for 6 turns, which made the enemy immobile (while still permitting attacks) and deducted 3 HP every two turns. In Pokémon GO, Fire Spin functions as a Fast Attack, introduced on February 16, 2017.

During the episode "Pikachu's Plan," Ash commanded his Charizard to employ Fire Spin, although its execution occurred off-screen. The official website disclosed that Charon's Heatran possessed knowledge of Fire Spin, yet it has never been depicted performing the attack. In "Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder," Yū's Infernape was noted in PBK2 to know Fire Spin but was not seen utilizing it. Notwithstanding the alteration in binding moves' mechanics from Generation I to Generation II, Fire Spin's description in Pokémon Stadium 2 incorrectly maintains that it immobilizes the target, an inaccuracy that also extends to both Bind and Wrap. Notably, Fire Spin is the sole Generation I Fire-type move incapable of inflicting a burn, consequently making it the only one unable to thaw a frozen foe (before Generation III).

Title: Thunder Shock (move)

Known as でんきショック (Electric Shock) in Japanese, and previously stylized as ThunderShock before Pokémon X and Y, this Electric-type offensive technique debuted in Generation I. This attack delivers damage and possesses a 10% likelihood of inflicting paralysis upon its target. Initially, Electric-type Pokémon were immune to ThunderShock's paralyzing effect. Subsequently, the move gained the ability to paralyze Electric-type Pokémon. For Pokémon Contests, ThunderShock functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the user 4 bonus appeal points if Charge was executed in the preceding turn. More recently, Electric-type Pokémon have once again become impervious to paralysis, thus Thunder Shock can no longer paralyze them. In Contest Spectaculars, using Thunder Shock as part of a combination grants the user an extra three appeal points when preceded by Charge. The move deals damage and carries the potential to leave opponents paralyzed. Its base critical hit chance is lower than typical moves (set at 2% within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series). Pikachu utilizes Thunder Shock as its personalized neutral special 3, replacing Thunder Jolt. This variant travels less distance and is weaker than Thunder Jolt, yet it concludes with an electrical burst that inflicts greater damage. A description from before Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) noted: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target paralyzed." Several English dubbing discrepancies exist: In "Lights, Camera, Quack-tion", Katrina's Raichu was ordered to use Thunder Shock, though the Japanese version specified Thunder. In "Hello, Pummelo!", Drake's Ditto (transformed into Pikachu) was instructed to use Thunder Shock, but the Japanese original had it use Thunderbolt. Furthermore, in "Trouble's Brewing", Satsuki's Jolteon was commanded to perform Thunder Shock in the English dub, when the Japanese dialogue actually indicated Zap Cannon. Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~

Title: Thunderbolt (move)

An Electric-type attack, Thunderbolt (known in Japan as １０まんボルト, or 100,000 Volts) made its debut in Generation I, inflicting damage upon opponents. It was designated as TM24 in the first generation, again from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and once more in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the TM36 slot. This move can be enhanced into one of two unique Z-Moves: Stoked Sparksurfer, when used by an Alolan Raichu equipped with Aloraichium Z, or 10,000,000 Volt Thunderbolt, if a Pikachu in a cap is holding Pikashunium Z.

Thunderbolt not only deals damage but also carries a 10% probability of causing paralysis in the target. Initially, Electric-type Pokémon were impervious to paralysis from Thunderbolt. Later, this immunity was removed, allowing Thunderbolt to paralyze Electric-type Pokémon. Within Pokémon Contests, Thunderbolt functions as the second step in a combination; employing it after Charge provides the user with 4 additional appeal points. Its base power subsequently saw a reduction, dropping from 95 to 90. Electric-type Pokémon regained their immunity to paralysis, rendering Thunderbolt unable to affect them with the status condition once more. In Contest Spectaculars, Thunderbolt forms part of a combination, yielding three extra appeal points if Charge was utilized in the preceding turn. This attack inflicts damage and possesses the potential to leave the opponent paralyzed. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its description noted "a very small chance of leaving the target paralyzed."

Until version 2.38.0, this particular "buddy move" variant was identified as B Thunderbolt. When all slots of the user's move gauge are consumed, this attack experiences a 25% increase in its power. This power boost is applied prior to any increases stemming from skills. A table would illustrate the varying powers that Best Buddies Thunderbolt can possess at move level 1, without skill application.

In the specialized Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Pikachu's designated Power Card is Thunderbolt. A player controlling Pikachu can, once per game, relocate all other players' tokens to Zapdos, then roll the dice to collect quadruple the resulting number in Pokémon Dollars from every player, irrespective of Zapdos ownership. Should Zapdos remain unowned by any player, the Pikachu controller is also granted the option to acquire it from the bank.

In the episode "Tie One On!", Macy's Starmie was referenced as having employed Thunderbolt against Jackson's Poliwhirl in an unseen battle. During "Betrayed, Bothered, and Beleaguered!", Tony instructed his Electabuzz to unleash Thunderbolt; however, Ash's Riolu incapacitated it before the order could be carried out. The nomenclature of this attack aligns with the category assigned to both Electivire and Zebstrika. Notably, Thunderbolt is unique in its ability to transform into more than one exclusive Z-Move: specifically, Alolan Raichu's Stoked Sparksurfer and the Pikachu in a cap's 10,000,000 Volt Thunderbolt.

Its Japanese designation, １０まんボルト (100,000 Volts), aligns with details found in certain Raichu Pokédex entries, which describe its electrical discharges as reaching or sometimes surpassing 100,000 volts. A number of other moves share this Japanese naming convention, indicating specific voltage levels. Within Pokémon Adventures, Red's Pikachu, Pika, executed an impromptu technique named MegaVolt (Japanese: １００まんボルト, or 1,000,000 Volts), which, at 10^6 volts, is ten times more powerful than Thunderbolt. The exclusive Z-Move for Pikachu in a cap, 10,000,000 Volt Thunderbolt (Japanese: １０００まんボルト, 10,000,000 volts), measures 10^7 volts, making it 100 times the voltage of Thunderbolt. While not following the identical naming structure, Gigavolt Havoc (Japanese: スパーキングギガボルト, Sparking Gigavolt) includes the metric prefix "giga," signifying 10^9 (1,000,000,000), which implies it possesses 10,000 times Thunderbolt's voltage.

In handheld game renditions, Thunderbolt is shown descending onto the foe; however, in console titles, its portrayal mirrors the animated series, with the electrical bolt striking directly forward. Despite this visual alteration, its in-game description maintained "a strong electric blast crashes down on the target" up until Pokémon Legends: Arceus. The battle animation for Thunderbolt in Pokémon Red and Green featured numerous shorter flashes, a contrast to the international Pokémon Red and Blue's animation. This animation was presumably simplified to mitigate the risk of epileptic seizures. Certain copies of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire experienced game freezes during the animation sequences of Thunderbolt or Thunder. This issue could be circumvented by disabling battle effects within the options menu. In Pokémon FireRed, LeafGreen, and Emerald, the audio for Thunderbolt or Thunder could loop indefinitely until the game was reset.

Having been a consistent element of Pikachu's move pool since the fifth episode, Thunderbolt stands as one of, if not *the*, most frequently showcased attacks across the entirety of Pokémon the Series. Furthermore, as divulged in "Climbing the Walls!", it holds the distinction of being Ash's preferred move. Lastly, Thunderbolt is one of three attacks, alongside Razor Leaf and Thunder, that received "new" move enhancements in the Pokémon Adventures manga.

Title: Thunder Wave (move)

Thunder Wave (Japanese: でんじは, or "Electromagnetic Wave") is a non-damaging Electric-type attack that originated in Generation I. It was designated as TM73 from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it could be found as TM16. This move boasts 100% accuracy and always inflicts paralysis on the targeted opponent. Thunder Wave's effect is contingent on type matchups, typically failing to impact Ground-type Pokémon. This type consideration extends to altered typings; for instance, if modified by Normalize, Ghost-type Pokémon would gain immunity. Exclusively within the Generation I handheld games, Thunder Wave could bypass a target protected by a substitute. In the Generation II core series titles, when utilized by an in-game opponent outside of the Battle Tower, the move carried a 25% chance of failure. Pokémon with the Limber Ability are unaffected by Thunder Wave.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Thunder Wave serves as the second move, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Charge was performed in the preceding turn. For Contest Spectacular combinations, using Thunder Wave can set up Hex and Smelling Salts to provide an extra three appeal points when executed on the subsequent turn. Ground-type Pokémon holding a Ring Target can be successfully targeted by Thunder Wave. Due to their inherent immunity to paralysis, Electric-type Pokémon can no longer be paralyzed by Thunder Wave. When amplified by an Electrium Z into Z-Thunder Wave, it boosts the user's Special Defense stat by one stage. Broadly, the move's function is to paralyze the immediate foe. Magic Coat can reflect this move.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Thunder Wave, along with all other status moves, was exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any caught Pokémon. Boss Minun was observed using Thunder Wave in that game. Marnie & Liepard's passive skill, Piercing Gaze, guarantees that Thunder Wave never misses when they use it. Pikachu employs Thunder Wave as its custom neutral special 2, a substitute for Thunder Jolt. While Thunder Wave deals less damage than Thunder Jolt, it compensates by briefly stunning the adversary.

In the English dub of "The Winner by a Nosepass," Roxanne instructed her Nosepass to use Thunder Wave, differing from the original Japanese version where Zap Cannon was commanded. This discrepancy was later corrected within the same episode. Wattson mentioned in "Watt's with Wattson?" that his Magnemite knew Thunder Wave, but it was never shown performing the move. The English dub of "Manectric Charge" depicted Wattson ordering his Manectric to use Thunder Wave twice; however, in the original Japanese version, the second command was for Shock Wave. Another dubbing error occurred in "Coming Full-Festival Circle!", where Zoey's Mismagius was told to use Thunder Wave in the English version, but Shock Wave in the original Japanese. In "Best Friend...Worst Nightmare!", Ren's Magnemite, Francois, attempted to use Thunder Wave but was thwarted when a wild Gengar entangled its body with cables. Emerald's Pokédex in "Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming" confirmed that a Linoone rented from the Battle Factory possessed Thunder Wave.

Thunder Wave is distinctive as the only status move that considers the type chart for its effects. Consequently, it is the sole status move that cannot hit Ground-type Pokémon, even when other status moves are affected by Electrify. In Generations II and III, Glare also took type matchups into account. A dungeon sharing the name Thunderwave Cave, or partially so in its French, German, and Korean localizations, appears in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, as well as its remake, Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX. The Japanese representation of the move's name uses the kanji 「電磁波」.

Title: Thunder (move)

Thunder, known as かみなり (Lightning) in Japanese, is an Electric-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation I. It was available as TM25 across various titles, from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it could be taught via TM38.

Initially, Thunder had a 10% probability of causing paralysis but was ineffective against Electric-type Pokémon. This mechanic evolved, granting Thunder a 30% chance to paralyze any target, including Electric-types. Subsequently, its base power was adjusted, decreasing from 120 to 110. Later, Electric-type Pokémon once again became immune to paralysis, making Thunder unable to paralyze them.

Its accuracy is significantly influenced by weather conditions. During rain, Thunder bypasses standard accuracy checks, guaranteeing a hit unless the foe is in the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Dive. Conversely, harsh sunlight drastically lowers its accuracy to 50%. Notably, Thunder can strike Pokémon even while they are in the semi-invulnerable states of Fly, Bounce, or Sky Drop.

In Pokémon Contests, Thunder serves as a follow-up move; using it after Charge, Lock-On, or Rain Dance awards the user 2 bonus appeal points. For Contest Spectaculars, employing Thunder after Charge or Rain Dance yields an extra three appeal points. A unique glitch in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl allowed Thunder, when used in rain, a 30% chance to hit targets shielded by Protect or Detect. Generally, Thunder shares functional similarities with Hurricane and Blizzard, all of which coincidentally possess a 70% accuracy rating in turn-based battles.

The move's animation features an electricity effect appearing under the target's position, followed by a delayed thunderstrike that damages enemies in the vicinity. Within Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, when unleashed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Thunder boasts a base PP of 20 and a base accuracy of 90%.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Pikachu utilizes Thunder as its down special move, calling down a thunderbolt to strike ground-based opponents. Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U altered this, with a cloud materializing to fire the thunderbolt, reducing its range and making the attack act as a Meteor Smash. Pichu also wields Thunder as its down special in Super Smash Bros. Melee; it functions similarly to Pikachu's version but strikes multiple times, with the caveat that Pichu takes damage if hit by its own thunderbolt.

Certain trainer-Pokémon pairings ensure Thunder never misses: Ash & Pikachu (due to Ash's Passion), Iono & Kilowattrel (with Piercing Gaze), and May (Champion) & Kyogre (thanks to Sure Hit | P-Moves & S-Moves Crit). A previous in-game description, current until June 28, 2021 (Version 2.10.0), stated it never missed in rainy weather and had a moderate chance of paralysis. Historic glitches include potential game freezes during Thunder or Thunderbolt animations in some Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire copies (resolvable by disabling battle effects), and a sound loop issue for Thunder or Thunderbolt in Pokémon FireRed, LeafGreen, and Emerald until a game reset.

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, Thunder, alongside Thunderbolt and Razor Leaf, is one of three attacks presented as enhanced "new" moves. Curiously, Pokémon Stadium 2 incorrectly displayed Thunder's paralysis chance as 10%, despite it having been updated to 30% in Generation II.

Title: Rock Throw (move)

Known in Japanese as いわおとし (Rock Drop), Rock Throw is a Rock-type offensive maneuver that made its debut in Generation I of the Pokémon series. This attack simply inflicts damage without any supplementary consequences, possessing a precision rating of 65%. In Pokémon Contests, Rock Throw serves as a foundational move for a combination; initiating with it will cause Rock Slide and Rock Tomb to earn double their standard appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. In Super Smash Bros., the Pokémon Onix deploys Rock Throw when summoned from a Poké Ball, ascending briefly before releasing multiple rocks downwards. As depicted in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer, the move Secret Power adopts Rock Throw's visual effect when employed on a rocky terrain during Generation III games. Subsequently, starting with Generation IV, Secret Power additionally showcases the Rock Throw animation when utilized within a cave environment.

Title: Earthquake (move)

Known in Japan as じしん (Earthquake), this Ground-type attack delivers damage and first appeared in Generation I. It was designated TM26 from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Conversely, it was available as TM41 within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move's primary function is to deal harm, lacking any additional effects. Should a Pokémon be in the semi-invulnerable state of using Dig, Earthquake can strike it, applying double its usual power to that specific target. All nearby Pokémon, including allies, are affected by Earthquake's reach. Within Pokémon Contests, Earthquake serves as an initiating move in a combination, enabling Eruption and Fissure to achieve twice their standard appeal if performed immediately afterward. Currently, when Earthquake connects with a Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable stage of Dig, the damage itself is technically doubled rather than the move's power, which yields an almost identical outcome. Its power is cut in half when striking grounded Pokémon while Grassy Terrain is present. Similar to Bulldoze and Sludge Wave, Earthquake creates an area of effect for its damage that originates from the user. In its execution, it harms all Pokémon present in the space (including allies), with the exception of the user. Those in the midst of using Dig incur twice the standard damage. Regarding this sync move, 6★ EX potential was unlockable only after November 23, 2020 – a span of 327 days from its initial release on January 1, 2020. In the narrative of Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Emerald's Pokédex indicated that a Glalie—specifically, a Golem that Noland had rented from the Battle Factory—was proficient in Earthquake. While Cynthia's Garchomp was publicly stated to know Earthquake on the official website, its use of the move was never depicted. Every fully evolved first partner Pokémon from generations preceding Generation V is capable of learning Earthquake via TM26; however, Venusaur only gained this capability starting in Generation III. Beginning with Generation V, the move's learnability by fully evolved first partner Pokémon became restricted to Fire-types from odd-numbered generations and Grass-types from even-numbered generations. Following the 2004 Chūetsu earthquakes, a particular episode in Japan, which was set to showcase a Whiscash employing Earthquake, was prohibited from airing. Consequently, neither Earthquake nor its analogous moves, Fissure and Magnitude, have made any appearances in the animated series subsequent to this event. In contrast, Bulldoze premiered a full nine years afterward, specifically in Clemont's Got a Secret!. Curiously, in Pokémon Emerald, Cooltrainer Dianne encountered in Victory Road possesses a Lanturn with Earthquake, despite the fact that Lanturn cannot legitimately acquire this move in any game generation. The sole Ground-type Pokémon unable to master Earthquake are Nincada, Palpitoad, Silvally, Toedscool, and Toedscruel. Should a player instruct an Aerodactyl in Earthquake within a Generation II game, transfer it to Generation I, and then deploy it in Pokémon Stadium, the Aerodactyl will exhibit a glitch, performing an exclusive animation involving rapid spinning in its spot.

Title: Fissure (move)

The Ground-type attack known as Fissure (Japanese: じわれ Fissure) first appeared in Generation I, serving as a one-hit knockout (OHKO) technique. During Generation II, it held the distinction of being the signature move for Diglett and Dugtrio. It is designated as TM27 in Generation I and will be TM154 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When Fissure successfully lands, it unfailingly inflicts exactly 65535 HP damage. Its fundamental accuracy rating is 30%. A successful Fissure strike will effectively break a target's Substitute. In contrast to other attacks that circumvent the standard damage formula, Fissure does not bypass type immunities. Moreover, this move will not connect with a target whose current Speed stat surpasses the user's current Speed stat.

The contemporary accuracy of Fissure in the main series is determined by a specific formula: it will always fail if the user's level is below the target's (the Speed stat no longer affects Fissure's success). If the user's level is 90 or more levels higher than the target's, Fissure's accuracy becomes 100% (even though the formula would yield values over 100% for a difference of 91 or more). This formula dictates that accuracy begins at approximately 30% (76256) when both Pokémon share the same level, and it increases by roughly 0.78% (2256) for each level the user is above the target. This calculated accuracy is influenced by the standard accuracy and evasion stats. Fissure can also currently affect Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable phase of Dig.

Exclusively within the Generation II core series games, a Fissure that missed could be countered by Counter for maximum retaliatory damage. However, Fissure is not susceptible to Counter in Pokémon Stadium 2.

Despite having a listed accuracy of 30%, Fissure's actual probability of hitting a Pokémon in certain contexts is determined by an alternative calculation: accuracy starts at 30% when the Pokémon are at the same level, and becomes 1% more likely to hit for every level the user is higher than the target. If the user is at least 70 levels higher than the target, Fissure will always connect. Conversely, if the user is a lower level than the target, the move consistently fails. This chance to hit is not considered true accuracy; it is unaffected by accuracy and evasion stats, nor by other accuracy modifiers such as Gravity, Wide Lens, and Compound Eyes. Additionally, Fissure cannot hit a semi-invulnerable Pokémon in this context. Nevertheless, effects that guarantee moves to hit (e.g., No Guard and Lock-On) still influence Fissure, provided the user's level is not lower than the target's.

In Pokémon Contests, Fissure can be employed as the second move in a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Earthquake was used in the previous turn. It also forms part of a Contest Spectacular combination, providing an extra three appeal points if Lock-On, Mind Reader, or Miracle Eye was utilized in the preceding turn.

When used in a Max Raid Battle against a Dynamax Pokémon protected by a mysterious barrier, Fissure will deplete two bars of the barrier's strength. Otherwise, Fissure will always fail against Dynamax Pokémon. Tera Raid bosses are entirely unaffected by Fissure.

Regarding Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Fissure does not function as an instant knockout attack; instead, it deals damage normally. Consequently, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, since Fissure is not a one-hit knockout move, Ground-type Pokémon (including Groudon) will receive STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus) when using it.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Fissure typically defeats the target in one hit. Prior to Gates to Infinity, Fissure would knock out targets by dealing "calamitous damage" (Japanese: とてつもない タメージ tremendous damage), internally registered as 9999 damage. Unlike in the core series games, it cannot strike a Pokémon in the middle of Dig in Mystery Dungeon. Furthermore, it will fail against Flying-type Pokémon and those possessing the Ability Levitate. In the current Mystery Dungeon titles, Fissure now inflicts damage equal to the target's current HP.

Due to the 2004 Chūetsu earthquake, this move, alongside Earthquake and Magnitude, has not been featured in the animated series since episode AG101 (which remained unaired globally). After defeating Giovanni at the Viridian Gym in Pokémon Red, Blue, and Yellow, he mentions his creation of TM27, which teaches Fissure in Generation I. In Pokémon Yellow, a title heavily influenced by the animated series, a Youngster on Route 9 owns a Sandshrew that knows Fissure, mirroring A.J.'s Sandshrew from the anime.

Notably, Fissure is the sole physical one-hit knockout move that does not require direct contact.

Title: Dig (move)

This entry focuses on the combat technique Dig. For details on the field action Tunnel 1, please consult Field Move (Ranger). Dig (Japanese: あなをほる Dig Hole) is a Ground-type attack move, capable of inflicting damage, that first appeared in Generation I. It served as TM28 from Generation I through Generation VI, as well as in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and is slated to be TM28 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was designated as TM10.

Upon selection, the user submerges underground, attaining a state of immunity to nearly all incoming attacks during the initial turn, which is known as a semi-invulnerable turn. On the subsequent turn, Dig proceeds to deal damage, its PP is expended, and it is registered as the last move performed. Once Dig has been chosen, the user is unable to withdraw from battle until the move is either interrupted or fully carried out. Should Dig not reach full execution, its PP will not be deducted, nor will it be counted as the most recently used move. If the user holds a Power Herb, this item is consumed, allowing Dig to complete its effect within a single turn. During this semi-invulnerable phase, the user is also protected from damage caused by sandstorm and hail weather conditions.

Dig can be halted by factors such as full paralysis, infatuation, or self-inflicted harm resulting from confusion. Certain moves, however, are still effective against a Pokémon in this semi-invulnerable state: Earthquake, Magnitude, and Fissure all affect a Pokémon digging underground. From Generation II onward, Earthquake and Magnitude inflict double damage when striking an underground target. Exclusively in Generation I, Bide (except in Pokémon Stadium), Whirlwind, Roar, Swift, and Transform also affect the user. Beginning with Generation VI, Toxic successfully lands when utilized by a Poison-type Pokémon. A partially invulnerable Pokémon can also be hit if either it or the attacking Pokémon possesses the No Guard Ability, or if it is under the effects of Lock-On or Mind Reader.

If Mirror Move targets a Pokémon currently in its semi-invulnerable turn, Mirror Move will duplicate the action that Pokémon performed immediately prior to using Dig (or fail if no such action can be copied). Instruct is unsuccessful when used on a Pokémon whose previous move was Dig. Should the user experience full paralysis during Dig's mostly-invulnerable turn, the semi-invulnerable status will not be reset until the user either switches out or fully executes Dig. In Pokémon Stadium, conversely, full paralysis will reset Dig's semi-invulnerable turn. Mirror Move can replicate Dig on either of the turns it takes for the move to execute. Pokémon could be captured during Dig's semi-invulnerable stage.

Dig's base power was adjusted from 100 to 60 in Generation II. In Generation II only, a specific glitch caused moves such as Attract, Curse, Foresight, Mean Look, Mimic, Nightmare, Spider Web, and Transform to miss targets in Dig's semi-invulnerable turn when Lock-On or Mind Reader were active. Furthermore, moves were unable to lower the stats of targets during Dig's semi-invulnerable turn (meaning status moves like String Shot would fail, and secondary effects of moves like Bubble would not trigger). Pokémon can no longer be caught while in Dig's semi-invulnerable phase.

While underground, the user cannot be targeted, granting complete immunity to attacks until it resurfaces. Earthquake cannot hit a target that is underground. When deployed inside a cave or certain buildings, this move transports the player to the last Pokémon Center they used for healing, or to their own residence if they have not yet used a Pokémon Center. No Gym Badge is required to utilize this move outside of combat. Due to an oversight in Pokémon Red and Blue, this functionality allowed escape from locations like the Sea Cottage or the Vermilion City Pokémon Fan Club. This issue was corrected in Pokémon Yellow. Alternatively, when used within a cave or specific structures (including Gyms), it returns the player to the entrance they initially used to access that location. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, Dig cannot be employed outside of battle. However, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it once again functions as an out-of-battle field move, performing as it did from Generations II through VI. Subsequently, Dig's out-of-battle usage is again unavailable.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Dig bestows the "digging" status upon the user during the first turn. While the user is beneath the ground, the Pokémon is immune to all moves except Earthquake and Magnitude. Unlike the core series games, Fissure will not affect Pokémon in the digging status. Projectiles will also pass through the user as if its tile were unoccupied. On the second turn, the user attacks the enemy positioned directly in front. Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's damage is doubled at the conclusion of damage calculation. This move can only be executed on a land tile; it will fail if attempted on any other tile type (including the shallow water found in water dungeons, where the animation will play but the move will still be unsuccessful). Being a two-turn move, it cannot be linked. Dig is unusable within a Pokémon Tower in the Adventure Squad series. When performed by a gigantic Pokémon in Super Mystery Dungeon, Dig strikes targets 10 tiles away instead of 1 and also pierces through both enemies and walls.

In the manga "Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~", specifically in PASV12, Scarlet mentioned teaching her Pikachu to use Dig, though its actual use by her has not been depicted. In Generation I, if Dig was initiated while standing on a warp panel, the spinning animation for the move was identical to that of the warp panel itself. In Generation VIII, when a Pokémon resurfaces after using Dig, its shadow is no longer rendered.

Title: Toxic (move)

This entry details the move Toxic. For the Badge granted by Gym Leader Roxie, please refer to Badge → Toxic Badge. Toxic (どくどく, or Excessive Poison, in Japanese) is a Poison-type technique that inflicts no direct damage, first appearing in Generation I. Its Technical Machine (TM) designations have varied: it was TM06 from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the TM27 slot.

Toxic inflicts a status known as bad poison upon the target, and possesses an accuracy rating of 85%. However, Poison-type Pokémon are immune to its effects. When a Pokémon is subjected to bad poison, the resulting damage is a fraction of its maximum HP, specifically N/16ths, rounded down (though never less than 1). The value of N begins at 1 and increases each time the Pokémon takes damage from either poison or Leech Seed (potentially twice per turn if both are active). Thus, the damage is calculated as N multiplied by the floor of (HPmax / 16).

Historically, after a battle concludes, bad poison would revert to regular poison. Similarly, if a badly poisoned Pokémon was struck by Haze or was withdrawn from battle, its status would also change to regular poison. In Generation I, if a badly poisoned Pokémon successfully used Rest, it would be cured of poison, yet the counter (N) for bad poison damage would not reset. If this Pokémon subsequently suffered damage from burn, Leech Seed, or poison, that damage would still be calculated using the existing value of N, and N would continue to increment by 1 with each instance of damage (though if poisoned again by Toxic, N would reset to 1). A notable exception was Pokémon Stadium, where Rest *did* reset the N counter for bad poison.

Subsequent game iterations brought changes: Toxic and Haze ceased to interact with Leech Seed. Rest was later updated to fully remove bad poison, including resetting its counter. However, if a badly poisoned Pokémon utilized Baton Pass or Heal Bell, the counter would not reset (mirroring Generation I's Rest behavior). Consequently, any subsequent burn or poison damage would be calculated as N×⌊HPmax/16⌋, with N still incrementing by 1 per damage instance (unless re-poisoned by Toxic, in which case N resets to 1).

During the Generation II core series games, when an in-game opponent (excluding those in the Battle Tower) employed Toxic, the move carried an additional 25% chance to fail, beyond its standard chance to miss. Toxic's damage calculations have since become independent of other forms of recurrent damage, meaning it no longer amplifies or is amplified by them. Pokémon with the Ability Immunity cannot be affected by Toxic.

More recent updates have further altered its behavior: A Pokémon afflicted with bad poison by Toxic will no longer have its status downgraded to regular poison if it switches out, nor if the battle ends. However, a Pokémon badly poisoned by Toxic *will* now transition to regular poison once the battle concludes.

A specific interaction, known as the Toxic sure-hit glitch, was observed: if a Poison-type Pokémon executed Toxic, the move would infallibly hit, even if the target was undergoing a semi-invulnerable phase (e.g., during Fly or Dig). Furthermore, any subsequent moves used by that same Pokémon in the same turn would also bypass accuracy checks. This glitch was addressed in version 1.3.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, where, after a Poison-type Pokémon uses Toxic, any further moves in that turn perform their accuracy checks as usual.

In Contest Spectaculars, Toxic serves as a component of a combination, granting Hex, Venom Drench, and Venoshock an additional three appeal points if utilized in the following turn. When enhanced by a Poisonium Z into Z-Toxic, the user's Defense stat receives a one-stage boost. In Max Raid Battles, if Toxic strikes a Dynamaxed adversary, the Dynamax Pokémon is inflicted with standard poison rather than bad poison. Similarly, in Tera Raid Battles, a wild Tera Pokémon hit by Toxic receives normal poison instead of bad poison.

From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, if a Poison-type Pokémon uses Toxic, the move will always hit, even against targets in the semi-invulnerable state of moves like Fly or Dig. The move Toxic is also susceptible to Magic Coat. In some contexts, any Pokémon entering the area directly in front of the user will be badly poisoned. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-altering moves, including Toxic, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by caught Pokémon. Boss Skuntank and Pyukumuku were observed utilizing Toxic in this game.

Visually, in Pokémon Battle Revolution, Toxic is represented by two intertwining purple orbs launched towards the opponent. However, when Spinda or Carnivine perform the move, these orbs exhibit an erratic trajectory. Lore-wise, in Generation I and its remakes (Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, and Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!), Koga reveals that Toxic is a secret, 400-year-old technique passed down through his lineage. In Generation I, Toxic was the sole Poison-type move available via TM.

Regarding localization, German releases of Pokémon Red, Blue, and Yellow used the same message for bad poison from Toxic ("(Gegner) (name) ist vergiftet!") as for regular poison. Later generations rectified this by specifying "badly poisoned" ("(Gegner) (name) ist schwer vergiftet!"). Toxic notably became the first move to relinquish its near-universal TM compatibility, with numerous Pokémon unable to learn its returning TM06 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For instance, Shroomish can learn Toxic via TM in these games, but its evolution, Breloom, cannot (though Breloom can still learn it via Move Reminder). Furthermore, Ekans, Arbok, Zubat, Golbat, Grimer, and Muk, despite learning Toxic by leveling up in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, are unable to learn it via TM in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Confusion (move)

This entry focuses on the Pokémon attack; for details concerning the status condition, refer to Confusion (status condition). Known in Japanese as ねんりき (Telekinesis), Confusion is an offensive Psychic-type technique first introduced in Generation I. This damaging ability carries a 10% likelihood of leaving the target Pokémon confused.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Confusion can be utilized as the initial action, causing Future Sight, Kinesis, Psychic, and Teleport to yield double their usual appeal if executed on the subsequent turn. Conversely, it may conclude a combination, providing the user with an additional 2 appeal points if Calm Mind, Kinesis, or Psychic was performed immediately prior.

Mewtwo employs Confusion as its side special maneuver in Super Smash Bros. This particular move functions as a grab, bypassing shields while flipping the adversary in midair. Furthermore, it possesses the capacity to reflect projectiles, although in Super Smash Bros. Melee, such reflected projectiles would not harm their original sender. This mechanic was subsequently rectified in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U.

Prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, the move's description read: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target confused."

In the English dub of the episode "From Ghost to Ghost," Morty instructed his Gastly to use Confusion, despite Gastly being unable to legitimately learn this move. The original Japanese version of the episode contained no such command. Both Blaine's Mewtwo and a wild Dustox were listed on the official website as knowing Confusion, yet neither was ever observed employing the ability.

The English designation "Confusion" bears a semantic dissimilarity to its Japanese counterpart, ねんりき (Nenriki), which translates to "mindpower." This discrepancy has occasionally led to misunderstandings when depicted in the animated series' English dub. Within the animated series, Confusion's observed effects often precisely mirror those of the move Psychic. Predominantly, when Confusion appears in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, it results in the Defending Pokémon becoming Paralyzed, rather than confused.

Title: Psychic (move)

Known in Japanese as サイコキネシス (Psychokinesis), Psychic is an offensive Psychic-type attack that debuted in the first generation of games. It was designated as TM29 across multiple generations, from Generation I up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the TM40 slot. Mew can transform Psychic into the unique Z-Move Genesis Supernova when equipped with Mewnium Z.

This move inflicts damage and, in earlier generations, carried a 33.2% probability of decreasing the foe's Special stat by one level. However, if an in-game opponent utilized Psychic, there was an extra 25% chance for this stat-lowering effect to be nullified, leading to an actual success rate of 24.9%. Currently, Psychic possesses a 10% likelihood of reducing the target's Special Defense by a single stage. When executed by an in-game adversary, the same supplementary 25% chance of failure persists, bringing the actual success rate down to 7.5%. These failure conditions are bypassed if Lock-On or Mind Reader are active, and they also do not apply within the Battle Tower. More recently, the additional chance for Psychic's effect to fail when an in-game opponent uses it has been removed entirely.

In Pokémon Contests, Psychic can initiate a combination, granting double the usual appeal if Confusion, Future Sight, Kinesis, or Teleport are performed immediately afterward. Conversely, it can conclude a combination, providing the user with 1 bonus appeal point if Calm Mind, Confusion, or Kinesis preceded it in the prior turn. Beyond its contest utility, Psychic functions as a damaging attack that can also diminish an opponent's Special Defense by a single stage.

Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description noted a "very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def." Prior to the Version 2.20.0 update on April 27, 2022, its description mentioned that "This move's power increases when the target is confused." Additionally, unlike certain other sync moves, this particular attack was unable to achieve 6★ EX status from its launch on October 31, 2019, until 907 days later, on April 24, 2022. Another former description, also predating Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022), stated that "This move's power increases when the target is paralyzed." This variant of the attack, distinct from some sync moves, also lacked 6★ EX upgrade capability from its debut on February 20, 2020, for 764 days, until March 25, 2022.

Within the unique Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition board game, Psychic functions as Psyduck's exclusive Power Card. Its ability allows a player controlling Psyduck to seize control of a Professor Oak or Trainer Battle card's effects, and the card itself, immediately after an opponent discloses its actions, effectively nullifying the opponent's use. As with all other Power Cards, this specific advantage is restricted to a single use per game.

During the English localization of the episode "Pros and Con Artists," Grace's Medicham was repeatedly instructed to employ Psychic, whereas in the original Japanese broadcast, the move specified was consistently Confusion. Similarly, in the English dub of "Hail to the Chef!", Rhonda's Mr. Mime was directed to use Psychic, contrasting with the Japanese version where Confusion was the move indicated. It also appeared in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer. In the manga chapter "Leaping Past Lopunny," Mira instructed her Alakazam to unleash Psychic, though its execution occurred off-panel. Dahlia's Togekiss was noted to possess Psychic in "Exit Empoleon," yet the move was never depicted in action. The official website disclosed that Black's Musharna, known as Musha, knew Psychic, despite him never being shown utilizing it. Within "The Secret Of The Fighting Type Pokémon," Black commanded his Xatu to use Psychic, but Gold's Tyrogue swiftly defeated it before the attack could be launched.

Notably, Psychic stands alone as the sole move sharing its English designation with one of the eighteen distinct Pokémon types. Between Generation VI and Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the move's sound effect was an enhanced iteration of its original audio from Generations I and II.

Title: Hypnosis (move)

The Psychic-type technique known as Hypnosis (さいみんじゅつ in Japanese, meaning "Hypnotism") originated in Generation I and does not inflict damage. Exclusively within the Generation I handheld titles, Hypnosis possesses the unique property of being able to strike a foe even if they are protected by a Substitute. During the Generation II core series adventures, if an NPC adversary, excluding those encountered in the Battle Tower, employs Hypnosis, the attack faces an additional 25% likelihood of failure, separate from its standard miss rate. Pokémon possessing the Abilities Insomnia or Vital Spirit are entirely immune to the effects of Hypnosis. This move functions as an initial component in a Pokémon Contest combination, enabling Dream Eater and Nightmare to yield twice their usual appeal points when performed on the subsequent turn. Its accuracy rating was adjusted to 70% in the games Diamond and Pearl, as well as in Pokémon Battle Revolution. However, in Platinum, this accuracy value reverted to 60%. For link battles pitting Diamond and Pearl against Platinum, HeartGold, or SoulSilver, Hypnosis maintains a consistent 60% accuracy, irrespective of which player assumed the role of leader. Furthermore, in Contest Spectacular combinations, Hypnosis can initiate a sequence where specific moves—Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap—grant an additional three appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. When empowered by a Psychium Z Crystal to become Z-Hypnosis, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its Speed attribute. Its standard accuracy stands at 70%, and it induces a state of drowsiness in the target that persists for five turns. Ultimately, this technique causes the opponent to fall asleep. The effects of Magic Coat can redirect this move. Beginning with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, applying Hypnosis to an adversary already in a sleep state triggers a terrified status. A Pokémon afflicted with terror will flee the battle and is rendered unable to utilize any moves or items. Should an opponent employ Hypnosis against one of the player's Pokémon that is already asleep, that Pokémon will be transformed into a puppet. Under the puppet status, the Pokémon will unpredictably strike its allies or consume items. Players are prevented from switching to a Pokémon under this particular status condition. If the leading Pokémon is affected by the puppet status, the player's actions are completely restricted until the condition dissipates. The puppet status remains active until the accompanying sleep condition concludes. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-altering techniques, Hypnosis included, were exclusively wielded by opposing Pokémon and were unobtainable for any Pokémon captured by the player. Boss versions of Poliwag, Poliwhirl, Gastly, Drowzee, and Hypno were observed employing Hypnosis. During the episode "Round One - Begin!", Mandi directed his Exeggutor to unleash Hypnosis, though the Pokémon was incapable of performing the action, incapacitated by pain from Ash's Krabby's Vise Grip. "At Liberty on Liberty Garden" depicted a Team Plasma Grunt's Gothitelle as knowing Hypnosis, but its execution occurred off-screen. Notably, Gothitelle could not legitimately acquire Hypnosis prior to Generation VIII. Aside from Shadow Panic and Shadow End, Hypnosis stands as the sole move possessing an accuracy rating of 60. Disregarding alterations made in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Hypnosis is unique as the only move to have its accuracy adjusted between different titles within the same generation.

Title: Meditate (move)

Known in Japan as ヨガのポーズ (Yoga Pose), Meditate is a Psychic-type status move that debuted in Generation I. Its primary effect is to boost the user's Attack stat by one stage. In Pokémon Contests, Meditate can be deployed as the second move of a combination, granting an additional 1 appeal point if Calm Mind was executed in the preceding turn. When enhanced by a Psychium Z, it transforms into Z-Meditate, causing the user's Attack to rise by a further stage. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Meditate, like all other status moves, was exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured species. Boss Meditite was specifically noted to employ Meditate. Furthermore, in the English dub of the episode "A Meditite Fight!", Shauna's Meditite was directed to perform Meditate, a discrepancy from the original Japanese version where Calm Mind was the instructed move. Finally, an unutilized animation for Meditate is present within the game code of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, featuring distinct visuals and audio compared to its portrayal in earlier titles.

Title: Agility (move)

This entry focuses on the move Agility. For details on the field move, refer to Field Move (Ranger). Known in Japanese as こうそくいどう (High-speed Movement), Agility is a Psychic-type move that debuted in Generation I and inflicts no damage. In Pokémon Contests, Agility serves as the second move in a combination; employing it after Double Team earns the user an additional 3 appeal points. Furthermore, it participates in a Contest Spectacular combination, leading to Baton Pass and Electro Ball granting an extra three appeal points when utilized subsequently. When boosted by a Psychium Z to become Z-Agility, any of the user's diminished stats are fully restored. Agility enhances the Travel Speed of both the user and allies present in the same room by one stage. Within Super Smash Bros. Melee and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, Agility functions as Pichu's up special move. While resembling Pikachu's Quick Attack through its swift, two-directional dash, Agility differentiates itself by harming Pichu instead of inflicting damage upon foes. Pichu is unable to legitimately acquire Agility in the core Pokémon titles. For Pokémon Rumble Rush, Agility, along with all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be learned by any captured Pokémon. Notably, Boss Zebstrika was observed utilizing Agility. During the English dub of "The Ancient Puzzle of Pokémopolis," Brock instructed his Vulpix to employ Agility, despite Vulpix being unable to legitimately learn this move until Generation VIII. The original Japanese broadcast, however, featured Brock directing Vulpix to use Quick Attack. Similarly, in the English dub of "Power Play!", Gary commanded his Umbreon to perform Agility, a move it legitimately cannot acquire. In contrast, the original Japanese script merely had Umbreon use its footwork to evade an attack. Another instance appears in the English dub of "Trouble's Brewing," where Tamao ordered her Umbreon to use Agility, a move inaccessible to it through legitimate means. The Japanese original instead depicted Tamao telling Umbreon to use Quick Attack. Before Generation V, Shedinja could possess Agility by leveling up, provided its pre-evolution Nincada was permitted to evolve solely at level 38, which is when Ninjask acquires the move.

Title: Quick Attack (move)

Referred to as でんこうせっか (Lightning Speed) in Japanese, Quick Attack is a Normal-type offensive technique introduced in Generation I, designed to inflict damage. This maneuver possesses a priority value of +1, causing it to execute before any other attacks lacking elevated priority. Should the user be afflicted with sleep or freeze status during the turn Quick Attack is initiated or attempted, its heightened priority remains active throughout the duration of that sleep or freeze condition. Consequently, this priority status will only be reset the turn after the Pokémon awakens or thaws, or if the Pokémon is withdrawn from battle. However, Quick Attack's increased priority no longer applies to sleeping or frozen Pokémon; instead, the turn order for a sleeping or frozen combatant is now determined by the specific move they choose each round.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Quick Attack serves as a viable second move, rewarding the user with 3 additional appeal points if Double Team was employed during the preceding turn. Consistent with other moves historically granted priority, Quick Attack propels the user in a rapid dash to make injurious contact with the target, although environmental factors might impede this action. While executing this charge, the Pokémon becomes insubstantial, effectively bypassing any projectiles or battlefield hazards it traverses.

Within the context of Super Smash Bros., Pikachu employs Quick Attack as its upward special move. Pikachu executes a swift dash, capable of changing direction twice if the angular divergence is sufficiently wide. Notably, this particular move inflicted no damage in the inaugural Super Smash Bros. game.

When wielded by Ingo (Special Costume) & Accelgor, Quick Attack transforms into a Bug-type attack, owing to their Bug Shift passive skill. Similarly, Lisia (New Year's 2023) & Rapidash utilize it as a Fairy-type move, courtesy of their Fairy Shift passive skill. Before the update to version 2.38.0, this move, when used as a buddy move, was referred to as B Quick Attack.

In the English localization of The Fourth Round Rumble, Jeanette Fisher's Scyther was ordered to perform Quick Attack. However, this constitutes a localization inaccuracy, given that in the original Japanese rendition, Jeanette instructed Scyther to use Swift instead. In the narrative of Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~ (specifically in "Wanted: Pikachu!"), Red's Pokédex indicated that his Pikachu, named Pika, possessed the Quick Attack technique. Later, in "Scizor Defends," Y's Sylveon, Veevee, was noted to be capable of Quick Attack, though its execution occurred outside of visible panels. An official website disclosed that Joey's Rattata was aware of Quick Attack, despite never being depicted employing it. Across the games Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom, Quick Attack frequently forms a component of the F.E.A.R. battle strategy. The initial instance of Ash's Pidgeotto using Quick Attack bore a resemblance to the later-introduced move Brave Bird. Notably, Quick Attack stands as the sole increased priority move introduced during Generation I. From Generation IV onward, Quick Attack has shared an identical in-game description with Aqua Jet.

Title: Rage (move)

Rage (Japanese: いかり Rage) is a Normal-type damage-dealing move that made its debut in Generation I, where it was designated as TM20. Once activated, Rage inflicts damage, and its user becomes locked into repeatedly performing the move; player control is surrendered, and the Pokémon will not cease its continuous assault until it either faints or the battle concludes. The user's Attack stat will escalate by a single stage each time it is struck by an attack or targeted by Disable, with multi-hit moves triggering this boost for every individual hit received. Rage consumes 1 PP upon its initial selection, but subsequent turns using the move do not incur any further PP cost. Its duration is paused, though never terminated, by various conditions such as sleep, freeze, binding, flinching, or self-inflicted damage due to confusion.

Should Rage's accuracy be altered by changes in accuracy or evasion, the resulting decrease will be reapplied consecutively each turn, eventually settling at a minimum of 0.4%. However, the specific 99.6% accuracy glitch affecting 100%-accuracy moves (when no other modifiers are active or when they cancel each other out) will not impact Rage's precision. If Rage misses on the turn it is first chosen, its secondary effects will fail to activate. The move will not attempt to boost the user's Attack if the stat has already reached a +6 stage, nor will it successfully increase Attack if the stat value is already 999. In Pokémon Stadium, Rage's accuracy does not diminish upon missing, and Disable only contributes to Rage's build-up when it successfully connects.

Beginning in Generation II, Rage ceased to be a continuous, battle-long maneuver. Consecutive uses now expend PP each turn, and the damage dealt is amplified by a distinct "Rage counter." This counter initializes at 1 and climbs by one every time the user is damaged by an attack (up to a maximum of 255), resetting if the user stops employing Rage (but not if it misses) or if an item is used. The accuracy-altering effects from Generation I are no longer applicable; missing does not affect Rage's accuracy. When used consecutively, the user's Attack stat now increases by one stage each time it suffers damage from an attack. Furthermore, the Rage counter, which multiplies damage, can now accumulate even if Rage's initial execution misses or fails. In Pokémon Contests, Rage functions as an opening move, causing Leer, Scary Face, and Thrash to double their normal appeal if used on the subsequent turn. It is important to note that the user's Attack stat will only begin to increase after Rage has been successfully employed.

A glitch exclusive to Generation IV allowed a Pokémon affected by Rage to select a move other than Rage, resulting in the removal of all volatile status conditions (e.g., confusion, transform, substitute) except Rage itself. If transform was dispelled this way, the transformed Pokémon permanently retained the moveset it had acquired.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, specifically Rescue Team and Explorers, Rage is a status move that bestows the "Enraged" status condition. This status elevates the user's Attack stat by one stage whenever it takes damage and persists for 5-10 turns. From Gates to Infinity onward, Rage simultaneously inflicts damage and applies the Enraged status. As in prior titles, the Enraged condition still grants a one-stage Attack boost upon taking damage. Its duration in these later games varies: 100 turns in Gates to Infinity, but only 11 turns in Super Mystery Dungeon.

In the English dub of the episode "Charizard Chills," Ash's Charizard's Dragon Rage was erroneously referred to as Rage. The "Lake of Rage" takes its name from this move, with the exception of its French and German localizations. In French, the lake is instead named after Outrage (Colère). In German, it is not named after a specific move; the only move containing "Zorn-" in its German name is Fury Cutter (Zornklinge). Prior to Generation VI, if the final opposing Pokémon successfully employed Self-Destruct or Explosion on a target that had previously used Rage and the target survived, Rage would continue to build despite the battle having concluded.

Title: Teleport (move)

This entry discusses the Psychic-type move, Teleport, which does not inflict damage and first appeared in Generation I. (The Japanese name for this move is テレポート, also Teleport.) For information on the field move, consult Field Move (Ranger).

In Generation I, Teleport was designated as TM30, and in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the designation of TM04. When employed in wild Pokémon encounters, the user makes an escape, thereby concluding the battle. Conversely, in battles against Trainers, Teleport invariably results in failure.

During certain early generations, a chance existed for Teleport to fail if the user's level was lower than the opponent's. This probability ranged from approximately 10% to 25%, varying based on the levels of both Pokémon involved. The likelihood of failure generally increased as the target's level surpassed the user's level. The precise formula for this failure chance was: FailureChance=⌊Levelopponent4⌋Levelopponent+Leveluser+1. While the player's Pokémon could still experience Teleport failure due to the opponent's level, a known bug prevented this level-based failure for wild Pokémon utilizing Teleport.

Teleport would also fail if the user was restricted by a trapping move (excluding binding moves), or when an opposing non-player character (NPC) attempted to use it. Exclusively within this generation, Teleport was unsuccessful in battles against specific trap Pokémon in Team Rocket's Hideout, the GS Ball Celebi, the Tin Tower Suicune (in Crystal), and the predetermined Red Gyarados. Furthermore, its use was ineffective in wild battles that were not Single Battles.

Later, the ability for Teleport to fail due to a user's lower level compared to the opponent's was removed. Subsequently, Teleport began to fail if the user was ensnared by *any* trapping move (now including Ingrain and binding moves) or by an Ability. However, if the user was equipped with a Smoke Ball, Teleport's success was guaranteed, irrespective of trapping moves or Abilities.

In Pokémon Colosseum, due to the absence of wild encounters, Teleport consistently fails. In contrast, within Pokémon XD, it could be successfully deployed during wild encounters found at Poké Spots.

As a Pokémon Contest combination element, Teleport functions as a second move, granting the user a single bonus appeal point if Confusion, Double Team, Kinesis, or Psychic was performed on the preceding turn.

In some contexts, Teleport specifically ignores Ingrain's trapping effect, ensuring success. A Trainer-owned Pokémon holding a Smoke Ball will invariably succeed with Teleport, bypassing trapping moves and Abilities. However, if a wild Pokémon is affected by a trapping move or Ability, Teleport will fail for it, even if it holds a Smoke Ball. Generally, if the user is holding a Smoke Ball, Teleport will succeed regardless of trapping moves and Abilities.

Teleport consistently fails in battles occurring during trials. SOS Battles are categorized as Single Battles as long as only one opponent remains. When amplified by a Psychium Z into Z-Teleport, the user fully restores its HP.

In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Teleport causes the user to switch out and possesses a priority of -6. This move also fails if the user is the sole Pokémon in the party capable of battling. Its effect from Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! is maintained in later games, and it succeeds regardless of the user being trapped. It only concludes a battle when initiated by a wild Pokémon.

Regarding its overworld application, from Generations I through VI, and also in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, using Teleport outdoors transports the player to the last visited Pokémon Center. If no Pokémon Center has been visited, the player is warped to their own house. From Generation IV onward, visiting one's own house establishes it as the current Teleport destination. In Generation I, the player had to heal their Pokémon at the counter for a Pokémon Center visit to register; in subsequent generations, simply entering the location was sufficient.

In Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, upon receiving the Mystery Egg from Mr. Pokémon, the last used Pokémon Center is automatically set to Cherrygrove City. This mechanism prevents players from bypassing the initial rival battle by blacking out or using Teleport to return home directly (though Teleport isn't legitimately accessible at this game stage). Additionally, when exiting the S.S. Aqua and entering either Vermilion City or Olivine City, the last used Pokémon Center is configured to that specific city.

Teleport's overworld use is restricted to outdoor areas, mirroring the constraints of Fly. Unlike Fly, however, Teleport in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver allows players to travel between regions. Exclusively in Generation IV, Teleport cannot be used within towns or cities (with the exception of the Safari Zone Gate), at Mt. Silver, or inside the Pokéathlon Dome.

In some battle contexts, Teleport warps the user to a different spot on the same floor. Mewtwo employs Teleport as its up special move in Super Smash Bros., disappearing before reappearing in the direction specified by the player. Another in-battle manifestation sees Teleport relocating the user to a random position on the battlefield; while the user can select a direction for its execution, this choice does not influence the randomly determined destination.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Teleport, were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Teleport was notably observed as a move utilized by Boss Abra, Cosmog, and Cosmoem.

In the short film "Lights, Camera, Quack-tion," Cleavon Schpielbunk recounted an incident where the Abra cast for his newest movie had teleported away after its co-star, Wigglytuff, proved excessively temperamental.

Teleport stands alone as the only move to have undergone a priority modification within the same generation. Its shift from 0 to -6 represents the most significant single priority reduction for any move.

Title: Night Shade (move)

Referred to as ナイトヘッド (Night Head) in Japanese, Night Shade is a Ghost-type damaging move that was first introduced in Generation I. It is obtainable as TM042 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Before the advent of Generation II, this move was exclusively associated with Gastly's evolutionary line. Night Shade consistently inflicts damage equivalent to the user's level. Despite being a Ghost-type attack, its elemental classification does not influence the actual amount of damage it causes. In Generation I, Night Shade was unique in that it bypassed type immunities, allowing it to strike both Normal-type and Psychic-type Pokémon, both of whom were otherwise immune to Ghost-type attacks in that era. Currently, however, Night Shade now respects type immunities, meaning it generally cannot affect Normal-type Pokémon. Nevertheless, it remains effective against Psychic-type Pokémon, as they are no longer immune to Ghost-type moves. The offensive power of Night Shade always corresponds precisely to the user's current level. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Night Shade is classified as a Charged Attack and has been available to players since February 16, 2017.

Title: Mimic (move)

The Normal-type, non-damaging technique known as Mimic (Japanese: ものまね Mimic) made its debut in Generation I. While it served as Sudowoodo's unique signature attack in Generation II, other Pokémon were nonetheless able to acquire it through the breeding mechanic. This move is designated as TM31 in Generation I and TM125 in the upcoming Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Mimic's core function is to replicate an attack from the opposing Pokémon. The Pokémon employing Mimic substitutes it with the acquired move, retaining it until it is knocked out, recalled, or the current battle concludes. This replication doesn't alter the user's summary screen, which continues to display its original move pool.

When facing a non-player character or a wild Pokémon, Mimic provides the user with visibility of the target's entire moveset, enabling a deliberate choice of which move to imitate. Conversely, during link battles and within both the Japanese and English versions of the Pokémon Stadium titles, Mimic operates differently, concealing the target's moveset and instead randomly selecting a move to copy. Distinct from subsequent generations, Mimic in this era is capable of replicating any selectable move, encompassing the adversary's Mimic, Transform, and Metronome, alongside any move the user itself already possesses. An exception is Struggle, which Mimic cannot imitate due to its inherent unselectability under normal circumstances.

Exclusively in this generation, the replicated move consumes Mimic's own Power Points (PP); consequently, Mimic's PP depletes each time a copied move is utilized. Should Mimic be invoked indirectly by Mirror Move or Metronome, the resulting copied move will instead draw from the PP of the calling move. Utilizing PP-restoring items will replenish PP up to the maximum capacity of Mimic (or Mirror Move, or Metronome, if applicable). A unique oversight in this generation causes moves duplicated by Mimic to exhibit an erroneous maximum PP count. The shown maximum PP reflects that of the imitated move, rather than Mimic's actual maximum. Should a Pokémon employ Mimic after obtaining it via Transform (or if Mimic originates from Mirror Move or Metronome, themselves acquired through Transform), the 5 PP provided by Transform will also be allocated to the copied move, rather than drawing from the Pokémon's intrinsic PP.

Another oversight specific to this generation: if a Pokémon that has used Mimic gains a level and learns a new move during battle, Mimic's effect is nullified. The previously copied move vanishes, and Mimic becomes available for use once more. Should Mimic replicate a binding move, and the target is switched out while its PP is at 0 but before its effect duration concludes, the current PP of the move undergoes a rollover to 63, acquiring full PP Ups. This significantly increased PP total can be utilized normally (with other Mimic-copied moves also drawing from this value), and the user perpetually retains these full PP Ups. Nevertheless, should the move's PP be restored (e.g., through a PP-healing item or at a Pokémon Center), it will revert to its rightful maximum capacity, still accounting for any PP Ups.

In a subsequent update, Mimic was reconfigured to copy the target's most recently executed move. Moreover, utilizing a replicated move no longer depletes any PP from Mimic itself. Any move acquired through Mimic will possess a fixed 5 PP, irrespective of whether PP Up or PP Max items were used on Mimic to enhance its own PP. It became incapable of replicating Sketch, Transform, Struggle, Metronome, or any technique the user already possessed. Additionally, Mimic began to circumvent accuracy evaluations, ensuring a hit unless the adversary was in the semi-invulnerable phase of maneuvers like Dig or Fly.

In a later iteration, Mimic regained the capacity to copy Transform, a change that could precipitate a glitch in early Japanese versions of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl. For this glitch to occur, a Pokémon must employ Mimic to replicate and then utilize Transform, followed by its fainted status. Should the trainer of the Mimic-wielding Pokémon emerge victorious, all moves acquired through the use of Transform are permanently kept post-battle. It copies Me First itself, rather than the move that Me First would call. Eventually, the imitated move came to possess its maximum PP, a departure from the previous fixed 5 PP. However, its ability to copy Transform was subsequently removed. A Rayquaza that replicates Dragon Ascent via Mimic will not fulfill the specific criteria needed for Mega Evolution.

When enhanced by a Normalium Z to become Z-Mimic, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its accuracy. Specifically in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, upon Mimic's complete execution, the user instantly performs the target's most recent action. Mimic is restricted from copying Endure, Protect, or Detect; if any of these was the target's final move, the system defaults to the last *eligible* move instead. Should Nihil Light have been the target's preceding action, the user will execute Core Enforcer. If the target is an inanimate object like a Mega Crystal or if it has not employed any copiable moves, Mimic becomes unselectable.

Two specific Pokémon undergo evolution when Mimic is among their known techniques: Bonsly transforms into Sudowoodo, and Mime Jr. evolves into Mr. Mime. When Mimic is utilized as a Plus Move, the subsequently copied technique also functions as a Plus Move.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Mimic targets the immediately facing foe, replicating their last employed move. Prior to the Super Mystery Dungeon installment, Mimic would instantaneously deploy the foe's last used move back against them. Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, Mimic's function shifted to copying the opponent's last used move for the entirety of the current dungeon floor. The replicated move then becomes available for use starting from the subsequent turn. Not every move is eligible for replication. Within the Rescue Team series, Mimic is unable to imitate itself, Encore, Sketch, or any move that invokes other moves (with the exceptions of Sleep Talk and Snatch). The Explorers series expanded Mimic's capabilities, allowing it to copy Nature Power, Metronome, and Me First.

In Generation III, an error on the status screen falsely indicates Mimic possesses 100% accuracy, despite its true nature of disregarding all accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Screech (move)

Hailing from Generation I, Screech (いやなおと, or Unpleasant Tone, in Japanese) is a Normal-type move that causes no direct damage. Its TM identifier in Pokémon Sword and Shield was TM16. This move inflicts a two-stage reduction on the target's Defense statistic. Transforming Screech into Z-Screech with a Normalium Z results in the user's Attack stat increasing by a single stage. The effects of Throat Chop are ineffective at stopping a Pokémon from executing Z-Screech. A Pokémon that has deployed a substitute cannot be influenced by Screech.

For core series games in Generations I and II, when an in-game opponent (outside the Battle Tower) deploys Screech, it incurred an extra 25% chance of failing, on top of its regular miss rate. This supplementary failure chance for in-game opponents using Screech has since been removed. As of recent updates, Screech is able to bypass substitutes and hit protected Pokémon.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Screech diminishes the target's Defense, setting it to ¼ of its initial value. This particular reduction is distinct from typical stat stages and can be repeatedly applied, potentially reaching a minimum of 1/128 of the original value. However, beginning with Gates to Infinity, Screech's effect in Mystery Dungeon was adjusted to lower Defense to ½ of its original value instead.

Both Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition have an impact on this move. Due to its nature as a sound-based attack, Soundproof will prevent its successful execution. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Screech, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Opposing Kricketune was observed employing Screech in that game.

Its in-game description, prior to the update to Version 2.10.0 on June 28, 2021, read: "Sharply lowers the target's Defense." An interesting detail about Screech is its varied accuracy: 85% in the core series games, but a perfect 100% in Pokémon Masters EX, a rarity among moves. Before the advent of Generation V, Shedinja could legitimately learn Screech by leveling up, on the condition that its Nincada form evolved precisely at level 20, given that Ninjask gains this move upon reaching that level.

Title: Double Team (move)

For information regarding the Trainer classification "Double Battling," please consult Double Team (Trainer class). The technique known as Double Team (Japanese: かげぶんしん, translated as Shadow Divide) debuted in Generation I as a Normal-type maneuver that inflicts no damage. This move holds the designation of TM32, spanning its availability from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, reappearing in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and also featuring in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Utilizing Double Team grants the user a one-stage boost to their evasion stat. When channeled through a Normalium Z, transforming it into Z-Double Team, this ability instead purges all negative stat changes from the Pokémon that used it. In Pokémon Contests, Double Team functions as an initiating move for a combination, enabling Agility, Quick Attack, and Teleport to achieve twice their usual appeal points when executed on the subsequent turn. Given the absence of evasion and accuracy statistics in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Double Team's function diverges: it renders the user intangible at its current location, allowing all incoming assaults to harmlessly pass through and preventing further moves from targeting it for the duration. Distinct from Protect and Detect, Double Team's intangible state ensures Plus Moves inflict no damage, although they will partially disregard protection. As a trade-off, Double Team exhibits a discernible delay before its effect materializes, unlike the immediate activation of Protect and Detect. It serves to elevate the Pokémon's evasiveness by one increment. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated: "Raises the user's evasiveness." Furthermore, as a buddy move, it bore the appellation B Double Team until version 2.38.0. In Super Smash Bros., Lucario employs Double Team as its down Special maneuver. Activating it causes Lucario to generate illusory duplicates, drawing the opponent's attacks, while the true Lucario encircles for a retaliatory strike from the rear. Within the episode "Clefairy and the Moon Stone," Brock instructed his Zubat to execute Double Team, which constituted a dubbing discrepancy; the Japanese original had Brock commanding Zubat to use Whirlwind. A preview for the unbroadcast episode AG101 showcased a Whiscash performing Double Team. Nihei's Venusaur was noted to possess knowledge of Double Team in "A Rush of Ninja Wisdom!". The Japanese designation for this technique, かげぶんしん (kage bunshin), pays homage to a ubiquitous ninja ability frequently termed "shadow clone" in English media, spanning manga, anime, and other fictional works. A prominent illustration of this concept is found in the Naruto franchise, where its protagonist, Naruto Uzumaki, consistently deploys Kage Bunshin no Jutsu as a hallmark ninja skill. Curiously, the Stadium series of games features an incorrect description for the move, claiming it reduces the foe's accuracy. One of Greninja's triumphant post-battle animations in Super Smash Bros. depicts it executing Double Team.

Title: Recover (move)

Originating in Generation I, Recover (known in Japanese as じこさいせい, meaning Self-regeneration) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move replenishes the user's HP by as much as 50% of their total HP, rounded downwards, and possesses 20 PP.
