Exclusively within the Generation I core series, Recover would not execute if the disparity between the user's maximum and current HP resulted in a remainder of 255 upon division by 256 (for instance, values like 255 or 511). This glitch was subsequently rectified starting with Pokémon Stadium and continued to be absent in all subsequent generations. Presently, Recover consistently restores 50% of the user's maximum HP, with the value being rounded upwards. When boosted by a Normalium Z, transforming it into Z-Recover, it gains the additional effect of clearing all of the user's decreased stat stages. The exact percentage of the user's maximum HP restored by Recover can fluctuate across different game iterations. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-affecting techniques, Recover among them, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and were unobtainable for any Pokémon captured by the player. As a boss encounter, Mewtwo, Arceus, and Cryogonal were observed utilizing Recover. It is confirmed that Mewtwo possesses knowledge of Recover. Nevertheless, its usage has been confined to the radio drama "The Birth of Mewtwo," meaning it has never been depicted using the move visually. In the "Just My Luck...Shuckle" installment, Emerald's Pokédex disclosed that Lucy's Milotic was capable of using Recover.

Title: Harden (move)

Known in Japanese as かたくなる (Stiffen), Harden is a non-damaging Normal-type move that debuted in Generation I. When employed, it causes the user's Defense stat to rise by one stage. If empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Harden, the Pokémon gains an additional stage increase to its Defense. Within Pokémon Contests, Harden functions as a pivotal first move in certain combinations, enabling Double-Edge, Protect, Rollout, Tackle, and Take Down to earn double their standard appeal points if used during the subsequent turn. The Pokémon Stadium minigame "Rock Harden" features participants, both player and computer-controlled, as Metapod or Kakuna; their goal is to activate Harden at opportune moments to avoid damage from falling rocks. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Harden, were exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any Pokémon caught by the player. Harden was observed as a move used by adversarial Silcoon, Cascoon, and Seedot. Although the official website disclosed that wild Cascoon and Silcoon could learn Harden, there were no documented instances of them ever actually performing the move.

Title: Minimize (move)

Minimize (Japanese: ちいさくなる, Shrink) is a Normal-type move without offensive capability, first appearing in Generation I. When successfully increasing the user's evasion in Generations I and II, this move causes the user's sprite to be replaced by a small, generic image. In Generation I alone, this transformation to a tiny generic image only takes effect if battle animations are active. When a Pokémon utilizes Transform against a minimized opponent, the user adopts the target's regular sprite, not its reduced form. Conversely, a minimized Pokémon employing Transform will see its own tiny sprite supplanted by the target's standard sprite. Should a Pokémon's sprite become invisible after using Acid Armor, invoking Minimize will restore its visibility as the tiny generic image. If a Pokémon undergoes Dynamax subsequent to using Minimize, moves specifically enhanced against minimized targets will not bypass accuracy and evasion. When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, becoming Z-Minimize, all of the user's diminished stats are reset. Particular moves inflict twice their usual power (Generation IV) or have their damage output doubled (Generations II, III, and V onward) against foes that have executed Minimize at least once. Furthermore, commencing with Generation VI, these specific moves will also disregard accuracy and evasion assessments under these conditions. Should a Pokémon enter Dynamax after having used Minimize, these particular moves will revert to their base damage output. This specific effect is not transferable through Baton Pass. Minimize enhances the user's evasiveness. Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, evasiveness sees an increase of one stage. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Minimize boosts the user's evasiveness by two stages. Minimize is featured as one of Chandelure's designated Pokémon moves. Chandelure temporarily reduces its size, making it invulnerable to attacks until it returns to normal. In its shrunken form, Chandelure can also move to reposition itself, thereby maintaining distance from its opponent. A glitch in Generation I games allowed for the opponent's Pokémon sprite to become a corrupted version of the last-viewed Pokémon in the player's party, triggered by using Minimize, then inspecting a party Pokémon's stats, and finally exiting the menu. Minimize is among a trio of moves that have appeared in a fully concluded animated series arc, yet have not been shown in the English dub. The other two moves comprising this group are Conversion and Ion Deluge.

Title: Smokescreen (move)

Known as えんまく Smokescreen in Japanese, this Normal-type move, which causes no direct damage, made its debut in Generation I. Prior to the release of Pokémon X and Y, its name appeared as SmokeScreen. Its primary effect is to decrease an opponent's accuracy statistic by a single stage. During the first two generations of the core series titles, if a non-Battle Tower in-game adversary employed Smokescreen, there was a one-in-four probability of the technique failing. This move is eligible as the follow-up in a Pokémon Contest combination; should the user have previously deployed Smog, an additional 3 appeal points are awarded. When transformed into Z-Smokescreen by a Normalium Z, the move instead grants the user a one-stage boost to their evasiveness. Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad lines of games, Smokescreen causes the foe to suffer from the Whiffer status condition. A Pokémon afflicted with Whiffer will unfailingly miss every subsequent attack, including ordinarily infallible moves such as Aerial Ace. However, a standard attack will still connect if the user possesses the Sure-Hit Attacker IQ skill. Magic Coat is capable of reflecting this technique. Starting with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon and in subsequent titles in that series, Smokescreen's effect reverts to decreasing the target's accuracy by one stage. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Smokescreen, like all other status-affecting techniques, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured species. Specifically, Boss Charmeleon was observed employing Smokescreen. Furthermore, in Smash Tour, the occasional appearance of a wild Koffing results in it deploying Smokescreen to obscure the entire playing field, rendering most elements invisible until the effect dissipates.

Title: Confuse Ray (move)

This entry pertains to the move Confuse Ray. For details regarding the Festival Plaza facility, please consult Festival Plaza → Haunted houses.

Confuse Ray, known in Japanese as あやしいひかり (Eerie Light), is a non-damaging Ghost-type attack that debuted in Generation I. Players can acquire it as TM017 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. If enhanced into Z-Confuse Ray by a Ghostium Z, the user's Special Attack stat receives a one-stage boost.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Confuse Ray, were restricted to opposing Pokémon and could not be learned by any Pokémon captured by the player. Confuse Ray was notably utilized by Boss Pokémon such as Ninetales, Gengar, Starmie, Chinchou, Lanturn, Xatu, and Ampharos.

In the episode A Family That Battles Together Stays Together!, Lola commanded her Mantine to use Confuse Ray, but the Pokémon could not execute the move because it was held in a Bind by Brock's Onix. Confuse Ray holds the distinction of being the sole Ghost-type move capable of inducing confusion.

Title: Withdraw (move)

"Withdraw" serves as a primary reference point; for the in-battle action of swapping Pokémon, refer to "Recall." Known in Japanese as からにこもる (Withdraw into Shell), this non-damaging Water-type move made its debut in Generation I. Its primary effect is to boost the user's Defense stat by a single stage. When amplified into Z-Withdraw via a Waterium Z, this causes the user's Defense to ascend an additional stage. In Pokémon Contests, it functions as the concluding move in a combination, awarding an extra appeal point if Rain Dance was executed during the preceding turn. Its stat-boosting properties make it susceptible to the move Snatch. In Super Smash Bros., Squirtle utilizes Withdraw as its Side Special technique, retreating into its shell to slide across the arena, harming foes it impacts. Should an adversary land on it, Squirtle becomes inverted and temporarily immobilized. The offensive, spinning nature of its Smash Bros. incarnation contrasts with its typical defensive role, drawing parallels to Squirtle's employment of Rapid Spin in various Pokémon animated adaptations. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, every status-category move, Withdraw included, was exclusively available to adversary Pokémon and unattainable by any captured companions. Specifically, Boss versions of Shellder, Cloyster, and Tirtouga were observed employing Withdraw. In the Pokémon Adventures manga, specifically "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II," Diamond's Torterra, Tru, was confirmed to possess Withdraw, though its execution occurred off-panel. Furthermore, Diamond's Turtwig, also named Tru, was officially listed online as knowing Withdraw, despite never being depicted using the technique.

Title: Defense Curl (move)

The technique known as Defense Curl (まるくなる Curl Up in Japanese) emerged as a non-damaging, Normal-type move in Generation I. It was made available as TM40 during Generation II. Its primary function is to elevate the user's Defense by one stage. Defense Curl also instigates a continuous effect for the duration of the current battle, causing the user's Rollout and Ice Ball attacks to have their power doubled. This specific power bonus does not accumulate with multiple uses, nor can it be passed on via Baton Pass. When utilized as the initial move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Defense Curl enables Rollout and Tackle to earn double their standard appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. For Contest Spectacular combinations, employing Defense Curl grants the user an extra three appeal points if Ice Ball or Rollout are performed immediately afterward. If powered up by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Defense Curl, it bestows a one-stage enhancement to the user's accuracy. The user's Defense stat is augmented by a single stage. The power of Rollout and Ice Ball will be multiplied by two if Defense Curl was the user's most recently performed move. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, Defense Curl included, were restricted to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any caught Pokémon; Boss Geodude, for example, was observed to wield it. In the "Buddy" storyline, Hop indicated that his Wooloo was capable of using Defense Curl, yet its actual deployment of the move has not been witnessed. Furthermore, Green's Jigglypuff, Jiggly, was confirmed to know Defense Curl through her Pokédex in "The Escape."

Title: Barrier (move)

Known in Japanese as バリアー (Barrier), this Psychic-type technique, originating in Generation I, does not inflict damage. Upon use, it bolsters the user's Defense by two stages. When amplified by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Barrier, its effect shifts; it then restores all of the user's diminished stats to their initial state. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Barrier, like all other status moves, was restricted to opposing Pokémon and unobtainable for player-caught Pokémon. Notably, it was a move observed on Boss Mr. Mime and Mew. Within the games Red, Green, and Blue, Lance possesses a Dragonite equipped with Barrier, a curious detail given that Dragonite cannot legitimately learn or possess this move in any of the Generation I titles. To commemorate this specific Dragonite, a variant also knowing Barrier was later distributed as a participation reward during the Kanto Classic Wi-Fi tournament. Curiously, despite their unique in-game mechanics, Barrier, Reflect, Light Screen, Protect, and Safeguard are frequently portrayed in the animated series as if they all share the identical defensive function of Protect within the games – shielding the user from the majority of move effects.

Title: Light Screen (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Light Screen (known in Japanese as ひかりのかべ, or Light Wall) is a Psychic-type status move that causes no direct damage. It functioned as Technical Machine 16 (TM16) from Generation III up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and will again in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned TM06. Historically, Light Screen would double the user's Special stat when an adversary inflicted damage with a special attack. If the user was already under its influence, Light Screen would fail. Critical hits were unaffected by Light Screen's protective effect. Should Light Screen cause the user's special stat to reach 1024 or higher during any battle calculations, it would be reduced via a modulo 1024 operation. Haze was originally capable of nullifying Light Screen's benefits.

Currently, Light Screen remains active for 5 turns and instead doubles the Special Defense of any Pokémon on the user's side of the battlefield. Haze is no longer able to remove Light Screen's effects. Rather than directly boosting Special Defense, Light Screen now technically halves the damage received from special moves; however, it does not reduce damage from moves that deal direct damage. When multiple Pokémon are present on the user's side of the field, all receive Light Screen's protection, but special damage is only reduced by a third, not by half.

The screen effect is dispelled from a Pokémon's side of the field if an opponent employs Brick Break or Shadow Shed against it. This occurs even if the target is Ghost-type and would thus be immune to Brick Break, unless the target is protected from the move or the move fails to connect. Light Screen can be utilized as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Calm Mind was executed in the preceding turn. Furthermore, Light Screen is cleared from a Pokémon's side if a Pokémon uses Brick Break or Defog on its ally (even if that ally is Ghost-type and immune to Brick Break), provided the target isn't protected from the move or the move doesn't miss.

Holding the item Light Clay when Light Screen is deployed extends its duration from 5 turns to 8 turns. The protective barrier is also dismantled if a Pokémon on that side is struck by Brick Break, Defog, Psychic Fangs, or Raging Bull, or when a Pokémon possessing the Screen Cleaner Ability enters the battle. Conversely, the screen persists if the target is immune to the move (for instance, a Ghost-type Pokémon being targeted by Brick Break). Pokémon with the Infiltrator Ability bypass Light Screen's defensive effects when initiating an attack.

Unlike Generations III and IV, Light Screen now consistently reduces damage by approximately a third in Double Battles and Triple Battles, rather than half (specifically, using a multiplier of 2703/4096 in Generation V, or 2732/4096 from Generation VI onwards), irrespective of how many allied Pokémon are shielded. This same multiplier applies to Battle Royals starting from Generation VII. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Light Screen will invariably diminish incoming damage by roughly a third, specifically applying a multiplier of 0.66. When powered up by a Psychium Z into Z-Light Screen, the user's Special Defense stat experiences a single-stage increase. The move Glitzy Glow establishes Light Screen on the user's side of the battlefield.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, the user (and the entire party in Gates to Infinity) gains the Light Screen status condition, which halves damage from special moves. This status typically lasts for 11-12 turns, or up to 21 turns. The status can be ended prematurely if the Pokémon is hit with Brick Break. An enemy's use of Defog in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series will also prematurely terminate the status. A Gigantic Pokémon employing Light Screen in Gates to Infinity possesses a base Power Point (PP) count of 20.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Light Screen, like all other status moves, was exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. It notably appeared as a move employed by Boss Duosion and Reuniclus. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated: "Reduces special damage done to the allied field of play." Pokémon Battle Revolution features a distinct animation for Light Screen when its effect naturally concludes. In the animated series, despite their unique in-game mechanics, moves like Reflect, Light Screen, Barrier, Protect, and Safeguard frequently appear to share a uniform effect, resembling the in-game Protect by shielding the user from the majority of move effects.

Title: Haze (move)

Named くろいきり (Black Mist) in Japanese, Haze is a non-damaging, Ice-type attack that made its debut in Generation I. It is found as Technical Machine 174 (TM174) in Pokémon Scarlet and Pokémon Violet. This move functions by reverting the stat stages of both combatants presently on the field to their base level of 0, simultaneously nullifying stat penalties inflicted by burns and paralysis. Furthermore, it dissipates the influences of Focus Energy, Dire Hit, Mist, Guard Spec., X Accuracy, Leech Seed, Disable, Reflect, and Light Screen for all Pokémon involved in the battle. Moreover, Haze remedies confusion and degrades 'bad poison' to 'regular poison' for both active Pokémon. It also completely clears any non-volatile status affliction from the opposing Pokémon.

However, Haze conspicuously fails to eliminate any non-volatile status conditions afflicting the user itself. It also does not revert a Pokémon's appearance from the generic Minimize graphic to its normal form, nor does it reset either Pokémon's prevailing type, known moves, or species. Should an opponent be cured of freeze or sleep through Haze, that Pokémon will be unable to act during the current turn. A frozen Pokémon that has not yet completed its recharge from Hyper Beam, if unfrozen by Haze, will persist in its inability to attack, switch out, or perform any other action; this state cannot be altered by any means until the Pokémon ultimately faints. Within the Stadium games, Haze uniquely clears any major status conditions from the user.

At a certain point, Haze's functionality was modified to exclusively reset the stat stages of all active Pokémon on the field to 0; it ceased to remove any other effects (such as Focus Energy). However, in another iteration, Haze was again made to clear the effects of Focus Energy and Dire Hit from both sides of the battle. Subsequently, the influences of Focus Energy and Dire Hit were once more rendered unaffected by Haze. When amplified by an Icium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Haze, the Pokémon utilizing it fully recovers its health.

Haze eliminates every stat alteration, encompassing the distinct stat multipliers originating from Charm, Aurora Beam, Memento, and Screech. Any stat reductions induced by Haze are applied individually to each Pokémon affected. Its description, preceding Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), stated: "Returns raised stats of all opposing sync pairs to normal."

In the English localized version of the episode "The Evolution Solution," James commanded his Weezing to deploy Haze. Yet, in the original Japanese rendition, he simply instructed Weezing to counterattack, without designating a specific move. Agatha's Arbok was officially documented as knowing Haze on the official website, though no depiction of it actually using the move ever occurred. Haze serves as a stat-resetting counterpart to Mist, sharing its type, PP, and Z-Move effect. Furthermore, their Japanese names bear a striking resemblance, and they are typically acquired at equivalent experience levels.

Title: Reflect (move)

The Psychic-type move known as Reflect (リフレクター in Japanese), which does not inflict damage, made its debut in Generation I. It was designated as TM33 during Generation I, again from Generation III up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the TM09 slot. Originally, Reflect would double the user's Defense stat if an opposing Pokémon struck them with a physical attack. The move fails to activate if the user is already benefiting from its protective effect. Critical hits and damage caused by self-inflicted confusion bypass Reflect's defensive benefits. Should Reflect cause the user's Defense stat to ascend to 1024 or beyond during any battle calculation, the stat will be subject to a modulo 1024 reduction. When an opponent uses Reflect, it consequently reduces the confusion damage taken by the user. Its effect now persists for a duration of five turns, universally doubling the Defense of all Pokémon present on the user's side of the field. Presently, confusion damage is mitigated by the user's own Reflect, rather than being influenced by an opponent's Reflect. Rather than boosting the Defense of affected Pokémon, Reflect now effectively halves the damage received from physical attacks; however, it provides no reduction against direct damage moves. In scenarios with multiple Pokémon on the user's side, all allies receive Reflect's protection, yet physical damage is merely reduced by one-third, not half.

Reflect's effect is nullified on a Pokémon's side if an adversary employs Brick Break or Shadow Shed against it, regardless of the target being Ghost-type and thus immune to Brick Break, unless the move misses or the target is otherwise protected. Moreover, Reflect is dispelled from a Pokémon's side if a Pokémon uses Brick Break or Defog on its partner (even when the partner is Ghost-type and immune to Brick Break), provided the move connects and the partner is unprotected. As a Pokémon Contest combination move, Reflect serves as a second action, granting the user an additional 1 appeal point if Calm Mind was performed in the preceding turn. Reflect is also removed from a Pokémon's side of the field if struck by Brick Break, Defog, Psychic Fangs, or Raging Bull, or upon the entry of a Pokémon with the Screen Cleaner Ability. Conversely, Reflect remains active if the target possesses immunity to the specific removal move (for instance, a Ghost-type Pokémon being targeted by Brick Break). Holding a Light Clay when Reflect is employed extends its operational period from five turns to eight turns. Pokémon equipped with the Infiltrator Ability can bypass Reflect's effects entirely when launching an assault.

Diverging from Generations III and IV, Reflect consistently imposes a roughly one-third damage reduction (specifically, a 2703/4096 multiplier in Generation V, or 2732/4096 from Generation VI onward) in Double and Triple Battles, irrespective of the number of Pokémon under its protection on a given side. Battle Royals in Generation VII likewise adopt this specific damage multiplier. In the upcoming Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Reflect's damage reduction will consistently be approximately one-third, achieved by applying a 0.66 multiplier. When augmented by a Psychium Z to become Z-Reflect, the user's Defense stat experiences a single-stage increase. Employing the move Baddy Bad establishes Reflect on the user's field. The user acquires the Reflect status condition, which halves incoming damage from physical attacks, encompassing other Attack-stat-based moves like the standard 'A' attack or 'Stick'. This status persists for either 11-12 turns or 21 turns. An early termination of this status can occur if the Pokémon takes a hit from Brick Break. In the Explorers and Adventure Squad game series, an enemy's use of Defog will also prematurely conclude the Reflect status. In Gates to Infinity, Reflect boasts a base PP of 20 when utilized by a Gigantic Pokémon.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-category moves, including Reflect, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Alakazam and Solosis were observed using Reflect. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021) stated: "Reduces physical damage done to the allied field of play." The on-screen message accompanying this move's use saw alterations in its English localization, at least through the first two generations. Japanese versions displayed: "<user>は だげきこうげきに つよくなった！" (<user> became stronger against damaging attacks!), later becoming: "<user>は リフレクターで だげきこうげきに つよくなった！" (<user>'s Reflector made it stronger against damaging attacks!). Following Reflect's re-establishment as a TM move from Generation III onward, numerous Generation I Pokémon lost the ability to learn it via TM33, a capability they possessed in Generation I titles. Consequently, Reflect holds the distinction of being the TM move that has seen the greatest reduction in Pokémon compatibility across generations. In Pokémon Battle Revolution, Reflect features a distinct animation when its effect naturally concludes. Within Generation II games, a modified Celebi Egg glitch involving both parents having Reflect in their fourth attack slot would result in the offspring holding a Teru-sama (Gold and Silver Versions) or a GS Ball (Crystal Version). Should the Teru-sama be transferred to Crystal, it transforms into a GS Ball. When observed in Pokémon Stadium 2, the Teru-sama appears as a GS Ball. Notwithstanding their unique in-game mechanics, Reflect, Light Screen, Barrier, Protect, and Safeguard are frequently portrayed in the animated series as having a shared effect, akin to Protect's in-game function of shielding the user from most move effects.

Title: Focus Energy (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Focus Energy (Japanese: きあいだめ Fighting Spirit Improvement) is a Normal-type move that inflicts no damage. For Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was designated as TR13. Exclusively within the Generation I handheld titles, Focus Energy contains a bug, causing it to permanently divide the user's critical hit probability by 4, rather than executing its intended effect of multiplying it by 4. This effect does not stack; if the user is already affected by Focus Energy, the move will fail.

A distinct critical hit probability formula is employed in Pokémon Stadium, differing from that used in the handheld versions. When Focus Energy is utilized, the critical hit formula is altered, significantly boosting the user's chances of scoring a critical hit (over twice as likely compared to not using it, though the precise boost is contingent on the user's base Speed). So considerable is this enhancement that, within Pokémon Stadium, activating a move with an already high critical hit ratio while under the influence of Focus Energy invariably grants the user the peak 255/256 (approximately 99.6%) chance of hitting critically.

Beyond Generation I handhelds, Focus Energy permanently boosts the user's critical hit ratio by one stage. Baton Pass can transmit this effect. In subsequent generations, its effect was enhanced to increase the user's critical hit ratio by two stages instead of just one. Exclusively in Generation IV, Haze possesses the ability to dispel Focus Energy's effects.

As the initial move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Focus Energy allows subsequent uses of Arm Thrust, Bone Rush, Brick Break, Cross Chop, Double-Edge, DynamicPunch, Focus Punch, Headbutt, Karate Chop, Mega Kick, Mega Punch, Sky Uppercut, Take Down, and Triple Kick on the following turn to yield double their usual appeal points. More recently, the status conferred by Focus Energy became copiable by Psych Up, Transform, or Costar. Furthermore, it functions within a Contest Spectacular combination, where specific moves—Aeroblast, Attack Order, Blaze Kick, Cross Poison, Drill Run, Karate Chop, Leaf Blade, Night Slash, Poison Tail, Psycho Cut, Shadow Claw, Spacial Rend, and Stone Edge—receive an additional three appeal points when executed on the subsequent turn.

When augmented by a Normalium Z, transforming it into Z-Focus Energy, the user's accuracy stat ascends by one stage. In the latest iterations, Focus Energy also fails to activate if the user is already influenced by Dragon Cheer. Upon activation, the user acquires the Focus Energy status condition, which serves to elevate its critical hit ratio.

Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the move guarantees a critical hit for all actions by boosting the user's critical hit ratio to 999% (unless blocked by Battle Armor, Shell Armor, or Lucky Chant), with the status persisting for 3 to 6 turns. From Gates to Infinity onward, Focus Energy elevates the critical hit ratio to 80%, and its condition endures for 3 turns. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-altering moves, including Focus Energy, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss versions of Rattata, Nidoran♂, Nidoking, Machop, Watchog, Timburr, and Bouffalant were observed utilizing Focus Energy.

Title: Bide (move)

Known in Japanese as がまん, or Perseverance, Bide is a Normal-type offensive maneuver first appearing in Generation I. During that generation, it was designated as TM34. Upon choosing Bide, the Pokémon becomes inactive for a randomly determined duration of either two or three turns, during which it cannot select other actions. However, switching out remains an option while Bide is in effect. Subsequently, the move unleashes an attack dealing damage equivalent to double the total damage taken by the user during this waiting phase. Should the user avoid direct attacks throughout the biding period, Bide will ultimately fail on the turn it was meant to inflict damage.

Despite its classification as a Normal-type ability, Bide's damage output is not influenced by type matchups. Consequently, it inflicts neutral damage on both Rock and Ghost-type Pokémon and does not benefit from the same-type attack bonus (STAB). This attack inherently disregards accuracy checks, guaranteeing a hit. It successfully strikes targets even during the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. The total damage accumulated during the biding duration is quantified as the most recent instance of damage dealt. This includes recoil damage from crashing, self-inflicted confusion damage from an opposing Pokémon, and the full extent of damage absorbed by a substitute. Furthermore, if the opponent executes an attack that does not miss, the last damage value is not reset, thereby allowing damage dealt in the preceding turn to contribute to the current turn's accumulation. Status conditions such as sleep, freeze, binding, and flinching merely suspend Bide's duration without canceling its effect.

A notable change in Stadium allows for a move to be chosen during each of Bide's inactive turns. In subsequent generations, Bide fails to connect with any Pokémon undergoing a semi-invulnerable state, such as during Dig or Fly. It ceased to bypass accuracy checks, instead possessing a fixed accuracy of 100. Moreover, it lost the ability to strike Ghost-type Pokémon or those in the semi-invulnerable turn of abilities like Dig or Fly. Should a Pokémon using Bide fall asleep, the move is interrupted and will not resume, even after the user awakens. From this point, Bide exclusively endures attacks for a fixed duration of two turns. Once biding commences, the Pokémon is unable to switch out until the move has fully resolved. The retaliatory strike now targets the last Pokémon that attacked the user, including allied Pokémon.

Exclusively within Generation III, HP reduction caused by Pain Split was factored into Bide's accumulated damage. Later on, Bide gained an elevated priority of +1 for its entire duration, ensuring it activates before moves without priority boosts. It also regained its ability to bypass accuracy checks and to hit Ghost-type Pokémon. However, Pain Split's HP loss ceased to contribute to Bide's damage accumulation. When invoked via another move, Bide's subsequent turns revert to +0 priority. In Triple Battles, the attack strikes the Pokémon that most recently dealt damage to the user, encompassing both allies and non-adjacent adversaries. Subsequently, if Bide is triggered by another move, its follow-up turns adopted a +1 priority.

In certain contexts, Bide bestows a specific 'Bide status' upon the user, rendering them unable to move, attack, or utilize items. This status persists for either 4 or 3 turns, during which any incoming damage is reflected back at a rate of 2× or 1.5× to the opponent directly facing the user. Usage of Bide is prohibited within Pokémon Towers in the Adventure Squad series. The user enters a state of reprisal, causing any damage sustained by them to also be inflicted upon the attacking Pokémon. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Bide was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. It notably featured as an ability of enemy Kricketot. Although wild Kricketot were officially listed as knowing Bide on the official website, they were never depicted using it in-game. Similarly, Orm's pair of Shuckle were also revealed to possess Bide on the official website, yet they were never observed employing the move.

Title: Metronome (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Metronome (Japanese: ゆびをふる, meaning "Wag Finger") is a non-damaging move of the Normal type. Its primary effect is to cause the user to perform a randomly chosen move, which is then recorded as the most recently executed action. Due to Metronome possessing standard move priority, the randomly generated move may be performed at a different point in the turn order than its inherent priority would dictate. For example, Metronome might cause Quick Attack to be used subsequent to a normal priority move, or Counter could be deployed prior to one.

Should Metronome successfully summon Hyper Beam or a binding move, and its PP reaches 0 before the called move concludes, its PP will replenish as if the move itself was used, including any PP Ups. A Pokémon can utilize a disabled move if Metronome generates it. The glitch that allowed Struggle to be bypassed has since been resolved, meaning Metronome's PP can no longer experience this rollover.

Metronome does not apply any targeting preference; it will arbitrarily select an opposing Pokémon that the chosen move is capable of affecting, even if the move typically provides a beneficial effect, like Heal Pulse. If the move is restricted to targeting the field, an allied team, or the user, it will adhere to those limitations. When Acupressure is generated by Metronome, it can either target the user or an ally; however, in a Single Battle, it invariably targets the user.

Initially, Metronome would not select moves that would inherently fail due to the presence of Gravity or Heal Block; however, this behavior was altered, allowing Metronome to now call such moves even if they would ordinarily fail under those conditions. In Rotation Battles, if Acupressure is the chosen move, it will exclusively target the Pokémon that used Metronome. Any Pokémon with Metronome in its moveset is prohibited from appearing in Pokéstar Studio films.

Should Metronome summon a move already known by its holder while the holder is equipped with a Choice Band, Choice Specs, or Choice Scarf, the generated move will become locked by the Choice item. In all other scenarios, Metronome itself becomes the locked move. If a multi-turn move generated by Metronome activates Cursed Body, that specific multi-turn move will be disabled by Cursed Body, regardless of whether the Metronome user inherently knows it; otherwise, Metronome will be disabled instead. When Metronome calls a multi-turn move like Rollout or Dig, and the user possesses a Choice Band, Choice Specs, or Choice Scarf, the move will execute on the initial turn but will subsequently fail and terminate on the following turn.

When amplified by a Normalium Z to become Z-Metronome, the randomly chosen move transforms into its equivalent Z-Move. However, if the selected move is a status move, it will not gain its additional Z-Power effect. Following Metronome's initial finger-wagging animation, the player is not instantly informed of the specific move that has been summoned, even if they issued the command. Instead, the user first adopts the appropriate positioning for the generated move's execution (for instance, if Earthquake is called, the user moves into close range; for Fire Blast, the user returns to the trainer's side; Swords Dance is performed at the user's current location) before the game notifies the player of the selected move, which is then charged and executed. This design choice probably aims to prevent a summoned move from completely missing its target; as Metronome can be used from any relative position to an opponent, the user might otherwise fail to connect with close-range moves if the target isn't already nearby.

Across all mainline Pokémon games, with the exception of Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Metronome is unable to choose a Max Move, a Z-Move, or any of the moves indicated with an '✘' in the subsequent table (whereas moves marked with a '✔' are available in their respective Generations). Within Generation II, if a Pokémon has employed Mimic or Transform, the rule "Moves that the user knows" refers to its active moveset, not its initial one. While moves the user already knows were meant to be excluded from Metronome's pool in Generation III, a coding flaw prevented this restriction from being implemented. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Metronome's selection is limited exclusively to moves introduced in Generation I, with the exception of Metronome itself and the subsequent list of moves. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Metronome's move-calling behavior has evolved from prior titles: it selects a random move from the available options, and the user executes it in their current facing direction.

Although categorized as a status move, Metronome is immune to Taunt in the Rescue Team series of games, a departure from its behavior in the Explorers series. The range of moves Metronome can choose from in these spin-off titles is considerably more constrained than in the main series. Specifically, Metronome can select one from a pool of 19 moves: one potent attack move for each type, plus Fissure. Fissure has a 1% chance of being chosen, while all other moves have an identical probability of being used. The full list of potential moves is as follows: Masterful Metronome has a narrower selection of 11 moves: ten powerful offensive moves and Fissure. Normal-type moves invoked by Masterful Metronome convert into Fairy-type moves, thanks to Volo & Togepi's passive skill, Piqued Curiosity.

Within the Super Smash Bros. series, when Clefairy is released from a Poké Ball, it employs Metronome to replicate an action performed by another Poké Ball Pokémon. In Super Smash Bros. Melee, Clefairy's options are limited to Explosion, Blizzard, Fire Spin, and Gust. Togepi also appears in Melee, utilizing Metronome upon release from a Poké Ball to call Sleep Powder, Leech Seed, Magnitude, Powder Snow, or Night Shade. Togepi's moveset evolves in Super Smash Bros. Brawl, where SmokeScreen replaces Night Shade and Hypnosis replaces Sleep Powder, a set it retains in all subsequent games after Brawl. In Pokémon UNITE, Clefable's Unite Move, Wonder Wish, shares conceptual similarities with Metronome.

The Japanese name of the move alludes to the characteristic wagging finger, suggesting the user arbitrarily selects a move from a repertoire. Conversely, the English name reflects the rhythmic, consistent ticking sounds, much like the musical device it derives its name from. Throughout the animated series, Splash has been the most frequently observed outcome when Metronome is used. The description for Metronome contained inaccuracies in Generations II and III, asserting that the user could perform "any move," despite certain moves being unavailable for selection. This was rectified starting from Generation IV, where the description was updated to "nearly any move."

Title: Mirror Move (move)

Mirror Move, known in Japanese as オウムがえし (Parrot Mimicry), is a non-damaging Flying-type move that debuted in Generation I. This technique utilizes the most recent move directed at the user by an adversary still active on the battlefield. A move successfully copied by Mirror Move is subsequently considered the last move executed. Attacks targeting multiple Pokémon are valid for replication, provided the Mirror Move user was among the designated targets.

Mirror Move will fail under several circumstances: if no moves were previously aimed at the user (by a Pokémon remaining on the field), if the originator of the move intended for replication switches out during the turn Mirror Move is used, or if Mirror Move attempts to copy itself, a Shadow move, or a Z-Move. In Generation II exclusively, Mirror Move will also consistently fail when employed by a transformed Pokémon. Conditions like Paralysis, confusion, recharging, or building up for a multi-turn move do not affect what is registered as the last move used. However, if Mirror Move is employed immediately after an opponent experiences full paralysis, injures itself in confusion, or performs the initial phase of a multi-turn move, Mirror Move will not recognize these as actual moves to be copied.

Within Pokémon Stadium, Mirror Move can replicate multi-turn moves during either the first or second turn of their execution. Should Mirror Move call upon a move that is currently disabled, the Pokémon will still be capable of using it. Mirror Move also possesses the ability to be used on teammates. When replicating a move like Rock Slide, which typically targets only opponents, Mirror Move will execute it without harming allies.

A notable alteration is that Mirror Move now permits its user to simply choose a Pokémon present on the field to imitate its technique, thereby removing the condition that it must have been the last attack specifically directed at the user.

When powered up by a Flyinium Z crystal into Z-Mirror Move, the user's Attack stat increases by two stages. Additionally, the replicated move itself will convert into its corresponding Z-Move. If the copied move is a status move, it will not receive its Z-Power effect.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Mirror Move grants the user the "Mirror Move" status. This status causes all incoming enemy moves targeting the user to be reflected back at the attacker instead, without the user being affected by them. Mirror Move exclusively reflects the effect the move would have had on the user, rather than its complete usage; for instance, moves with multiple targets will still strike their other original targets. This status does not return regular attacks, nor does it work if the attacker is not adjacent, if the move has already been bounced back, or if it originated from cutting a corner. This status typically lasts for 2 to 5 turns.

From Gates to Infinity onward, Mirror Move's function changes to instead target an enemy directly in front and use that enemy's last executed move against them. This iteration of Mirror Move fails if there is no move available to copy.

Despite Mirror Move not having a direct association with flying, its Japanese name, オウムがえし (Parrot Mimicry), provides the rationale for its Flying-type classification and why most Pokémon capable of learning it are bird-based. Even though Mirror Move has been rendered unusable as of Generation VIII, other moves are still programmed to be affected by it in later game installments.

Title: Egg Bomb (move)

Known in Japanese as タマゴばくだん (Egg Bomb), this Normal-type damaging attack first made its appearance in Generation I. During Generation I, it held the Technical Machine slot TM37. Egg Bomb is solely responsible for dealing damage, offering no additional effects. However, this move proves ineffective against adversaries possessing the Bulletproof ability. Furthermore, Egg Bomb functions as the second step in a combination for both Pokémon Contests and Contest Spectaculars; executing it after Soft-Boiled in the previous turn will award the user 4 extra appeal points.

Title: Lick (move)

The move known as Lick (in Japanese: したでなめる, or Tongue Lick) is a Ghost-type attack that inflicts direct damage. Its debut occurred in the first generation of Pokémon games. Beyond its direct damage output, Lick possesses a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon paralyzed. It is notable that this paralyzing effect does not apply to targets that are themselves Ghost-type. This attack functions by dealing damage and simultaneously carrying the potential to paralyze foes. The Muzzled status condition has an effect on this particular move's execution. Before Version 2.10.0, which was released on June 28, 2021, the move's in-game description stated it "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed." Although Maizie's Teddiursa was officially confirmed via the website to possess knowledge of Lick, she was never depicted utilizing the move in practice. Among all Generation I Ghost-type moves, Lick stands alone in dealing direct damage; Night Shade, by contrast, determines its damage output based on the user's level. Furthermore, it is the sole Ghost-type attack from Generation I to factor in the type chart during damage calculations. Within the games Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD, a Pokémon executing Lick would display its tongue during the animation. However, from Pokémon Battle Revolution onward, this particular animation detail was discontinued. Uniquely among all Ghost-type moves, Lick holds the distinction of being the sole ability capable of inducing paralysis.

Title: Smog (move)

Originating in Generation I, Smog (Japanese: スモッグ Smog) is a Poison-type move that deals damage. It has a 40% chance of poisoning the target. In Pokémon Contests, Smog serves as an opening combination move, allowing SmokeScreen to achieve double its usual appeal if performed immediately afterward. Fundamentally, this attack inflicts damage and can cause poison. The description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) read: "Has a moderately good chance of leaving the target poisoned." In Pokémon Stadium and Stadium 2, Smog's stated 50% poisoning chance is inaccurate; its actual probability for this effect is 40%.

Title: Sludge (move)

Originating in Generation I, Sludge (known in Japanese as ヘドロこうげき, or Sludge Attack) is a Poison-type maneuver that inflicts damage. In battle, it has a 40% probability of poisoning the opposing Pokémon. For Pokémon Contests, Sludge holds a dual utility: initiating a combination with this move enables Sludge Bomb to earn twice the standard appeal points if deployed on the subsequent turn. Conversely, it can serve as a concluding move, granting the user an extra appeal point provided Sludge Bomb was utilized in the preceding turn. Generally, this attack deals damage and has the potential to leave the target poisoned. Prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description stated: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target poisoned." A notable dubbing discrepancy occurred in the English rendition of the episode "Here Comes the Trubbish Squad!", where a wild Trubbish was mentioned to use Sludge, whereas the original Japanese version correctly referred to the move as Sludge Bomb. Furthermore, in the game Pokémon Stadium, Sludge was inaccurately described as having a 50% chance of poisoning its target, despite its actual probability being 40%. From Generation V onward, Sludge employs the identical descriptive text as Sludge Bomb.

Title: Bone Club (move)

Known in Japanese as ホネこんぼう (Bone Club), this damage-dealing Ground-type attack debuted in Generation I. It is recognized as a signature technique for both Cubone and Marowak. When activated, Bone Club deals damage and carries a 10% chance of making the target flinch. This flinching effect, however, is ineffective against an opponent shielded by a substitute.

In the context of Pokémon Contests, Bone Club serves versatile roles. As the opening maneuver in a combination, it causes Bone Rush and Bonemerang to accrue double the standard appeal if executed on the subsequent turn. Alternatively, it can finalize a combination, awarding the user 2 extra appeal points if Bone Rush or Bonemerang was utilized in the turn prior. Uniquely, Bone Club is the sole Ground-type move capable of inducing flinching in an adversary.

Title: Fire Blast (move)

Fire Blast (Japanese: だいもんじ Daimonji) is a Fire-type attack, first appearing in Generation I, which inflicts damage upon its target. Throughout Generation I up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it was designated as TM38. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the TM46 slot. This move initially carried a 30% likelihood of burning the opponent after dealing damage. Subsequently, its chance of inflicting a burn was revised to 10%. Generally, Fire Blast has the potential to leave targets with a burn status condition.

For Pokémon Contest combinations, Fire Blast can be utilized as a second move, granting the performer 4 additional appeal points if Sunny Day was executed during the preceding turn.

Beyond the main series games, Charizard employs Fire Blast as part of Pokémon Trainer's Triple Finish Final Smash in Super Smash Bros. Brawl and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate. In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, Mega Charizard X shoots a fireball from its mouth that transforms into the character 大 upon impact, delivering multiple hits as one of its special attacks.

A description of Fire Blast prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated that it "Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned." Furthermore, the passive skill Champion Who Hears the Cheers, exclusive to Arc Suit Leon & Charizard, causes this attack to strike all opposing Pokémon and invariably land as a critical hit.

Fire Blast also appears in other media; for instance, in the manga *Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder*'s "Final Battle: Versus The Salon Maiden," Anabel instructed her Entei to use it, though the move itself was not depicted. The move has been represented across various official merchandise featuring Pokémon in action.

Notably, in Generation I, Fire Blast held the distinction of being the sole Fire-type move available via TM. Its visual representation draws inspiration from the Daimonji bonfire event of Japan's Gozan no Okuribi festival, where a substantial bonfire is ignited in the shape of the character 大, which translates to "big" or "great."

Some graphical anomalies have been observed with this move. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the background occasionally fails to load, resulting in a black display. Another glitch in Pokémon X and Y occurs when a Pokémon with a large hitbox (like Rayquaza) performs Fire Blast in the back alleys of Lumiose City; as the camera retracts, the Pokémon momentarily disappears by clipping through the adjacent building on its right.

Finally, the decorative item known as the Fire Blast Mat takes its name from this move and incorporates the Daimonji character into its design.

Title: Waterfall (move)

This Water-type attack, known in Japanese as たきのぼり (Waterfall Climb), first appeared in Generation I and inflicts damage. It serves as TM98 in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it is TM25. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, players find it as TM99.

Before Generation II, Goldeen and Seaking exclusively learned this technique. Initially, Waterfall simply dealt damage without any additional effects. In Pokémon Contests, if employed as the second move in a combination, it awards the user 2 extra appeal points when Rain Dance was performed the preceding turn. Currently, there is a 20% probability that Waterfall will cause the opposing Pokémon to flinch.

Beyond combat, Waterfall facilitates exploration by enabling the player to ascend waterfalls. Many titles necessitate this ability to navigate areas where waterfalls impede progress. Its activation requires the player to be surfing on water, directly facing a waterfall. Players can initiate its use from the party menu or by pressing the A button when interacting with a waterfall. Before Generation V, every climbable waterfall for this move consistently flowed from north to south (downwards on the game screen).

Some games mandate possession of a specific badge—the Volcano, Rising, Rain, Beacon, or Iceberg Badge—to utilize Waterfall for travel. It evolved into an HM (Hidden Machine) move, still granting access up waterfalls. Descending waterfalls does not necessitate this move. Uniquely in Pokémon Platinum's Distortion World, a specific waterfall can be ascended without possessing Waterfall or the Beacon Badge. This anomaly is attributed to the distorted gravity of that realm, where the player seemingly "surfs" on the waterfall itself.

Its utility outside of battle has varied across generations. For instance, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, it cannot be employed as a field move. However, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, its field function returns, operating as it did from Generations IV to VI, albeit now accessible via the Pokétch instead of being taught to a Pokémon. Generally, in more recent titles, its out-of-battle functionality has been removed.

As an attack, Waterfall causes damage. Starting with the Explorers series, the move also gained the potential to make targets flinch. In the Super Smash Bros. series, Waterfall is Squirtle's Up Special technique. Squirtle utilizes it for recovery and delivers significant knockback towards its conclusion. As a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO, it became available on December 8, 2017. Its description pre-Version 2.10.0 (before June 28, 2021) noted a minor chance of causing the target to flinch. In Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can employ Waterfall after acquiring it from a Gyarados within Sparkling Skylands. This enables a transformed Lapras-Ditto to ascend waterfalls.

Regarding its nomenclature, Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire titles present additional insight. The English title "Waterfall" omits the "climb" aspect present in its Japanese counterpart, "たきのぼり" (Waterfall Climb). Certain in-game descriptions, first appearing in the Japanese Pokémon Stadium, explicitly detail the user charging with sufficient power to ascend waterfalls. English versions of Pokémon Red, Blue, and Yellow provide no such indication due to the absence of move descriptions in those titles. Conversely, the Japanese Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal mention "charging with enough force to climb waterfalls" in its description. The English translation, however, reduced this to merely "An aquatic charge attack," again omitting the climbing detail. Despite this, Clair, upon defeat, clarifies its function: "RisingBadge will enable your Pokémon to use the move for climbing waterfalls."

Visually, various games depict a waterfall appearing during its in-battle execution. This visual cue might misleadingly imply the user summons a waterfall. While prominent in side series like the Pokémon Stadium games, Colosseum, and XD: Gale of Darkness, Squirtle's portrayal in Super Smash Bros. accurately depicts the attack. It holds the distinction of being the first move to acquire HM status a generation after its debut, which occurred in Generation II. Only Rock Smash shares this characteristic, though it didn't retain HM status in all following generations. Uniquely, Waterfall is the sole HM move never exclusively learned via HM. Furthermore, it is the only former signature move to subsequently become an HM.

In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Wallace presents HM05 (Waterfall) rather than a TM. This instance marks the only occasion a Gym Leader distributes an HM instead of a TM. Solely in Generation II, Pokémon climbing waterfalls while surfing perform a rapid spin animation. During Generation VI, when ascending or descending waterfalls on a Pokémon with a distinctive Surfing model (such as Lapras), the screen momentarily fills with a water splash before repositioning the player at the waterfall's opposite side, foregoing a full animation of the ascent/descent. On Battle CD 38 in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Anorith inexplicably possesses Waterfall despite being unable to learn it naturally. It stands alone as the only Water-type move able to induce flinching in the target.

Title: Clamp (move)

Originating in Generation I, Clamp (Japanese: からではさむ, known as Shell Squeeze) is a Water-type offensive technique that inflicts damage. Before the advent of Generation III, this move was exclusive to Shellder and Cloyster. Its initial accuracy rating was 75%, and it possessed a base of 10 Power Points (PP).

In Generation I battles, Clamp inflicted damage over a span of two to five turns. The probability distribution for its duration was as follows: a 37.5% chance for two turns, another 37.5% chance for three turns, a 12.5% chance for four turns, and a final 12.5% chance for five turns. While only the initial strike could result in a critical hit, subsequent turns consistently delivered the same amount of damage. Throughout its effect, the target was immobilized, rendered unable to attack. Should Clamp's user initiate an attack before the target, the target would also be incapable of acting during that same turn. Clamp could even prevent the target's attack if it successfully connected with a substitute. While Clamp was ongoing, neither combatant could select another move (choosing "Fight" would merely cause the user to continue dealing damage and the target to remain inactive), yet both Pokémon retained the ability to switch out. No specific message announced Clamp's conclusion on its final turn, requiring players to check their move list via "Fight" to ascertain its termination. Damage from Clamp's continuing effect was applied after any recurrent damage. If the user withdrew before the duration expired, the target would still be unable to attack for that turn. If, however, the target switched out prematurely, Clamp would automatically affect the incoming Pokémon, consuming an additional PP. Even if Clamp had 0 PP at that moment, it would still activate, and its current PP value would then loop back to 63, reflecting full PP Ups. Notably, even a missed Clamp could nullify Hyper Beam's typical recharge requirement. Furthermore, if Clamp's user acted before a Hyper Beam user during a recharge turn, and Clamp missed, the Hyper Beam user would then automatically execute Hyper Beam that same turn. Should Hyper Beam have 0 PP under such circumstances, it would still be used, and its PP would subsequently roll over to 63, including all PP Ups.

Within Pokémon Stadium, the mechanics diverged slightly: players maintained the ability to select a move during each turn Clamp was active. If a target Pokémon switched out prematurely, the new entrant was not automatically subjected to Clamp; instead, the user's turn was simply skipped. Moreover, Clamp would only negate Hyper Beam's recharge turn if its own attack was successful. The target was also free to attack throughout Clamp's duration and could otherwise act normally.

Generation II introduced significant changes. The target was now able to attack normally while affected by Clamp. In addition to the direct damage from its initial use, it also inflicted damage equal to 1/16 of the target's maximum HP at the end of each turn for two to five turns. The likelihood for these durations became uniform, with an equal 25% chance for two, three, four, or five turns. A message, "<Pokémon> was CLAMPED by <User>!", would now appear. The move also began to consistently trap the target, preventing both switching and escape. If the user withdrew, the trapped target would be freed. A Pokémon caught by Clamp could also break free by using Rapid Spin.

In Generation III, Clamp's effects largely mirrored those of Generation II. A key addition was that if a wild Pokémon employed Clamp against a player's Pokémon, the player could potentially escape the battle if their Pokémon possessed the Run Away ability or was equipped with a Smoke Ball. These methods, however, did not enable switching out the Pokémon. The battle message was also altered to "<User> CLAMPED <Pokémon>!" The duration of Clamp would be fixed at five turns if its user held a Grip Claw. Furthermore, a Pokémon holding a Shed Shell could now switch out even while trapped.

Generation IV saw an increase in Clamp's accuracy to 85, its base PP rose to 15, and its duration adjusted to consistently last four to five turns. In Pokémon Contests, Clamp could serve as the second move in a combination, granting the user 3 bonus appeal points if Rain Dance had been performed in the preceding turn.

In Generation V, if the user held a Binding Band, the damage inflicted at the end of each turn increased from 1/16 of the target's maximum HP to 1/8. Moreover, a Grip Claw would cause Clamp's duration to extend to 7 turns. Later, the inherent end-of-turn damage of Clamp itself was globally increased from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. Consequently, if the user held a Binding Band, this amplified end-of-turn damage would then further rise to 1/6 of the target's maximum HP.

Beginning with Generation VI, Ghost-type Pokémon became immune to Clamp's trapping effect.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, specifically the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad titles, Clamp both dealt damage and could inflict the "Constriction" status condition. This condition incapacitated the Pokémon from moving (though it could still attack), prevented it from swapping places with the team leader, and caused a loss of 5 HP every two turns for a duration of 4-6 turns. From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, the move instead caused the "Clamp" status condition, resulting in a loss of 3 HP every two turns for a fixed period of 6 turns.

Title: Swift (move)

The damaging Normal-type attack known as Swift (Japanese: スピードスター Speed Star) made its debut in Generation I. Across Western releases of the Generation I handheld titles and within Pokémon Stadium, Swift deals damage and guarantees a hit by circumventing accuracy evaluations. This allows it to strike a Pokémon even during the partially invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Conversely, in the Japanese Generation I handheld games, Swift achieved 100% accuracy through a glitch, unless the foe was protected by a substitute. Should a substitute be present, Swift would instead guarantee a hit, even against a target in the semi-invulnerable stage of moves like Dig or Fly. Presently, Swift consistently bypasses accuracy checks to ensure a hit, with the sole exception being a target engaged in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly. In multi-Pokémon encounters, Swift impacts all adjacent opposing Pokémon. When employed by Blue & Arcanine, Swift transforms into a Fire-type attack, owing to their inherent Fire Shift passive skill. Mewtwo's capacity to learn Swift has been substantiated. Nonetheless, its actual usage was confined to the radio drama The Birth of Mewtwo, meaning no visual depiction of it employing the move exists. In The Beginning Of Good Luck?! Pokémon Fortune Telling, Ash's Pikachu received the command to use Swift, yet the move's execution was not displayed. Within Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder In Scizor Defends, Y's Sylveon, named Veevee, was noted as knowing Swift, though the action occurred off-panel. The Japanese designation, スピードスター (Speed Star), also carries the connotation of "Speedster," logically leading to its English translation, "Swift." Before Generation VI, Swift held the distinction as the sole guaranteed-hit attack capable of striking both opposing combatants simultaneously in a Double Battle. As of Generation VI, Disarming Voice also fulfills these same criteria. Every Fire-type first partner Pokémon, alongside their evolutionary lines, is able to acquire Swift through Move Tutors in Platinum, HeartGold, and SoulSilver. Within The Electric Tale of Pikachu, Ash's Pikachu's Swift is alternatively called Speed Star, representing a direct translation of its Japanese nomenclature.

Title: Skull Bash (move)

The Normal-type damaging technique known as Skull Bash (Japanese: ロケットずつき Rocket Headbutt) made its debut in Generation I. During Generation II, this move served as the signature ability for the Squirtle evolutionary line; however, other species could still acquire it through inheritance via breeding. Its Technical Machine designations include TM40 in Generation I and TM146 within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon selection, Skull Bash performs no action during the initial turn, beyond displaying a message indicating the user has lowered its head. The subsequent turn is when Skull Bash deals damage, incurs a PP deduction, and registers as the most recently executed move. After committing to Skull Bash, the Pokémon cannot be withdrawn from battle until the move is either interrupted or completely carried out. Should Skull Bash fail to complete its execution, no PP will be expended, nor will it be marked as the last move utilized. Should an adversary employ Mirror Move during the turn the user is lowering its head, Mirror Move will replicate the action performed directly prior to Skull Bash's initiation (or simply fail if no such move exists). Conditions such as sleep, freezing, binding, and flinching will temporarily halt Skull Bash's duration without causing its disruption. Notably, within the Stadium games, Mirror Move is capable of copying Skull Bash during either of its two execution turns. The initial turn of Skull Bash also grants the user a one-stage increase to its Defense stat. Possessing a Power Herb allows the user to complete Skull Bash within a single turn. The move's base Power value shifted from 100 to 130, and its PP count was adjusted from 15 to 10. On its first turn, Skull Bash inflicts the user with the Skull Bash status condition and elevates the user's Defense (this Defense enhancement persists solely during the charging phase in games predating Super Mystery Dungeon). The attack is then unleashed by the user on the subsequent turn. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers game series, the move's damage output is subjected to a doubling effect at the conclusion of the damage calculation process. As a technique requiring two turns, it is incompatible with the linking mechanic. Use of Skull Bash is forbidden in Pokémon Towers throughout the Adventure Squad series. In Pokémon GO, Skull Bash functions as a Charged Attack, accessible to players since July 9, 2019. Pikachu employs Skull Bash as its side special move in the Super Smash Bros. series, beginning with Melee. This ability allows for charging, resulting in increased damage and knockback, and also propels Pikachu further horizontally, thereby facilitating recovery. Pichu also utilizes Skull Bash as its side special. While analogous to Pikachu's version, Pichu's Skull Bash permits a longer charge time and inflicts even greater damage and knockback, albeit at the cost of slight self-inflicted harm upon its release. Despite this, Pichu is unable to legitimately acquire Skull Bash in the core Pokémon titles. During The Ultimate Test, Nurse Joy instructed a Squirtle, which she had leased from the Pokémon League Admissions Exam instructor, to perform Skull Bash; however, the execution of the move occurred off-screen. The English localized version of Dueling Heroes shows Ash ordering his Totodile to execute Skull Bash, a move it is not capable of legally learning. Conversely, the original Japanese broadcast depicted Ash instructing Totodile to perform Headbutt instead. While Green's Blastoise, affectionately named Blasty, was officially listed on the website as knowing Skull Bash, he has never been visually portrayed using the technique.

Title: Spike Cannon (move)

Known in Japanese as とげキャノン (Spike Cannon), this Normal-type attack debuted in Generation I as a damage-inflicting, multi-hit technique. When used, Spike Cannon strikes the opponent between two and five times, dealing direct damage with each connection. Initially, the probability distribution for the number of hits was as follows: a 37.5% likelihood for two hits, an equal 37.5% chance for three hits, a 12.5% possibility for four hits, and another 12.5% chance for five hits. In its early form, only the initial impact had the potential to land as a critical hit, though all subsequent strikes delivered an identical damage output. Should a Substitute be shattered by Spike Cannon, the move's execution ceased instantly. For moves like Bide and Counter, only the final blow of Spike Cannon was taken into account.

Currently, every individual strike inflicts damage independently, allowing any of them to register as a critical hit. Furthermore, it is now capable of continuing its assault even after successfully breaking a Substitute. When the user holds a King's Rock, there's a possibility for the last strike to make the adversary flinch. Any Pokémon possessing the Ability Skill Link will consistently land five hits with Spike Cannon, provided the move does not miss entirely. Should the user be equipped with either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every sequential hit carries an independent chance to induce flinching in the target. The hit probability distribution has since been updated to: 35% for two hits, 35% for three hits, 15% for four hits, and 15% for five hits. Its average cumulative base power currently stands at 62. In instances where a Focus Sash activates before the final strike lands, the subsequent strike will result in the defending Pokémon's immediate fainting. A Focus Band, however, retains its ability to prevent fainting multiple times, though each activation attempt is distinct. The Ability Sturdy will trigger with every hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. For targets possessing the Abilities Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike from Spike Cannon will activate their respective effects.

The attack causes damage by hitting the target between 2 and 5 times per usage. Each individual hit undergoes a separate accuracy check; nevertheless, the move persists even if one of these checks fails. Its critical hit ratio is reduced, specifically to 2% within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series. Before Super Mystery Dungeon, should a Pokémon faint midway through the assault, any remaining projectiles could redirect to a new target, and the attack would still fully execute even if a Reviver Seed was triggered. Interestingly, in Generation I, Omanyte could acquire Spike Cannon, despite having no apparent spikes. Excluding Smeargle, no Pokémon capable of learning Spike Cannon receives a Same-Type Attack Bonus (STAB) from its use. Furthermore, every Pokémon that can learn this move is, without exception, partially a Water-type.

Title: Constrict (move)

This entry details the attack found in the main series titles. For information on the ability in Pokémon Shuffle, refer to Skill (Shuffle) → Constrict. Known in Japanese as からみつく (Coil Around), Constrict is a Normal-type offensive technique that debuted in Generation I. Constrict causes damage and, in its initial implementation, featured a 33.2% likelihood of decreasing the target's Speed stat by a single stage. If an in-game foe utilized this move, an additional 25% chance for this secondary effect to fail was present, yielding an effective success rate of 24.9%. Currently, Constrict has a 10% chance to reduce the target's Speed stat by one stage. When employed by an in-game opponent, the supplementary 25% chance for the effect to fail still persisted, resulting in an effective 7.5% probability. These opponent-specific failure conditions were nullified when Lock-On or Mind Reader were active, or within the confines of the Battle Tower. More recently, the additional chance for Constrict's secondary effect to fail when used by an in-game opponent has been completely removed. The move itself inflicts damage and retains the potential to decrease the target's Travel Speed by one stage. Among all damage-dealing techniques, Constrict possesses the lowest consistent base power.

Title: Amnesia (move)

Originating in Generation I, Amnesia (Japanese: ドわすれ Memory Lapse) is a non-damaging Psychic-type move. This technique was formerly designated as TR17 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and it currently holds the identifier TM128 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Initially, Amnesia would boost the user's Special stat by two stages. Its present-day effect, however, is to elevate the user's Special Defense stat by two stages instead. Furthermore, Amnesia participates in a Contest Spectacular combination; its use will grant an extra three appeal points to Baton Pass and Stored Power if either is performed on the subsequent turn. When amplified by a Psychium Z, evolving into Z-Amnesia, all of the user's lowered stats are reset. It is important to note that Amnesia's primary function is to increase the user's Sp. Defense by two stages.

Title: Kinesis (move)

Known in Japanese as スプーンまげ (Spoon Bend), Kinesis is a Psychic-type technique that deals no direct damage, first appearing in the initial generation of Pokémon games. This ability is exclusive to Kadabra and Alakazam, serving as their signature move. When employed, Kinesis lowers the accuracy of the opposing Pokémon by one stage. During Generation I and II core series gameplay, Kinesis, when used by a non-Battle Tower in-game adversary, carries an additional 25% chance of outright failure, beyond its standard chance to miss.

Within Pokémon Contests, initiating a combination with Kinesis causes Confusion, Future Sight, Psychic, and Teleport to yield twice their regular appeal when executed on the subsequent turn. This move can also serve as a combination finisher, providing the user with 3 additional appeal points if either Confusion or Psychic preceded it. Utilizing a Psychium Z to transform Kinesis into Z-Kinesis results in the user's evasiveness stat climbing by one stage.

For Pokémon Sword and Shield, Kinesis was unselectable in battle from versions 1.0 through 1.1.1. However, with the release of Version 1.2.0, it became accessible again, operating identically to its behavior in earlier generations. The move itself alludes to the psychokinetic spoon bending acts made famous by the illusionist Uri Geller.

It shares with Barrage and Struggle the distinction of being among the mere three moves originating in Generation I that have yet to be depicted in the anime. For Kinesis, specifically, this non-appearance could potentially be attributed to legal conflicts involving Nintendo and Geller. Even without a full-fledged anime debut, Kinesis did appear during the Professor Oak's Pokémon Lecture bonus segment for EP096, where a Clefairy utilized it through Metronome.

In Pokémon Red and Blue, Kinesis was not a learnable move for any Pokémon, manifesting solely as an unpredictable outcome of Metronome. Starting with Pokémon Yellow and continuing into Generation II, Kadabra begins with Kinesis, thus wild Kadabra in these games innately know the move. Both Kadabra and Alakazam have the option to be taught Kinesis through the Move Reminder feature present in Pokémon Stadium 2. Uniquely, Kinesis stands as the only Generation VIII move that was initially unusable to feature an "unusable move" description until Pokémon Sword and Shield's 1.2.0 update.

Title: High Jump Kick (move)

The Fighting-type offensive maneuver known as High Jump Kick (とびひざげり, or "Jumping Knee Kick" in Japanese) first appeared in Generation I, serving as a damage-dealing ability. Before the release of Pokémon X and Y, its name was spelled Hi Jump Kick. Prior to Generation III, this move was a signature ability of Hitmonlee, though Tyrogue could still acquire it through breeding. Initially, Hi Jump Kick inflicted damage with a base power of 85. Should the attack fail to connect, the Pokémon attempting the move would incur 1 HP of crash damage. However, when targeting a Ghost-type Pokémon, the user was exempt from this crash damage. Across Generations I through III, if the user of Hi Jump Kick moved first and subsequently fainted from its own crash damage, the opposing Pokémon would be prevented from making its attack.

Later, the crash damage mechanic was updated to be 1/8 of the damage Hi Jump Kick *would have* inflicted. Performing Hi Jump Kick against a foe protected by moves like Protect would result in crash damage to the user; however, type immunity alone (which only triggered a message) did not cause crash damage at this point. The crash damage formula underwent further revision, becoming 1/2 of the potential damage. In Pokémon Contests, Hi Jump Kick serves as a valid second move in a combination, awarding an extra 6 appeal points if Mind Reader preceded it. The move's base power saw an increase, escalating from 85 to 100. The execution of this move is impossible under the effects of Gravity. A subsequent rule change meant that the user *could* now incur crash damage from a target's type immunity, suffering damage equivalent to half of the target's maximum HP in such instances. If the move fails because no targets remain, the user will not sustain crash damage.

High Jump Kick's offensive capability was boosted once more, raising its base power from 100 to 130, while its Power Points (PP) were simultaneously cut from 20 to 10. The crash damage mechanism was finalized to consistently inflict half of the user's maximum HP, rounded down, regardless of other factors. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, High Jump Kick deals damage, and its final damage output is doubled. In these Mystery Dungeon titles, a missed High Jump Kick causes recoil damage: half of the *potential* damage in Rescue Team and Explorers, 50% of the user's max HP in Gates to Infinity, and 30% of the user's max HP in Super Mystery Dungeon. For Pokémon GO players, High Jump Kick functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since May 19, 2024. In Pokémon Masters EX, Gloria (Alt. 2) & Cinderace possess the passive skill Fuel Economy 2, which reduces High Jump Kick's cost to just 1 move gauge slot when their sync move is Fire-type. Additionally, their Piercing Gaze passive skill ensures High Jump Kick never misses when their sync move is Fighting-type. A previous in-game description, valid before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated that a failed Hi Jump Kick would cause the user to take damage equivalent to half of its maximum HP.

Title: Glare (move)

For the move whose Japanese name can be translated as Glare, refer to Leer. The Normal-type status move Glare (Japanese: へびにらみ, meaning Snake Glare) debuted in Generation I.
