Before Generation II, it was the exclusive signature move of Ekans and Arbok. Its primary effect is to paralyze the target, and it initially had an accuracy of 75%. While it can be used against Ghost-type Pokémon, Glare is currently ineffective at paralyzing them, unless they are under the effects of Foresight or Odor Sleuth. In the Generation II core series games, when an in-game opponent (excluding those in the Battle Tower) utilized Glare, the move carried an additional 25% chance to fail, separate from its standard accuracy miss chance. Glare functions as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Leer was employed in the preceding turn. Glare's accuracy first increased from 75% to 90%, then further changed from 90% to a perfect 100%. It subsequently ceased affecting Electric-types, as they gained immunity to paralysis. When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Glare, the user's Special Defense stat receives a one-stage boost. This move can also form part of a Contest Spectacular combination, causing Hex and Smelling Salts to provide an extra three appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. The move's essential outcome remains target paralysis. Glare is susceptible to Magic Coat's reflective properties. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Glare, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and unlearnable by captured ones; Boss Ekans, for instance, was observed using it. Interestingly, Drampa, Druddigon, and Helioptile can learn this move, known as へびにらみ Hebinirami (Snake Glare) in Japanese, despite not being explicitly snake-based. This may be linked to the mythological depiction of Western dragons and snakes, particularly the basilisk, which are often shown to immobilize victims through eye contact, either via magical influence or sheer fear. Conversely, despite their classifications as the "Rock Snake" and "Iron Snake" Pokémon respectively, neither Onix nor its evolution, Steelix, have ever gained the ability to learn Glare through any method. From FireRed and LeafGreen through Generation IV up to Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Glare's in-game description states: "The user intimidates the target with the pattern on its belly," a flavor text seemingly tailored for Arbok. In various adaptations like Pokémon the Series, the manga, and the Pokémon Stadium games, Glare is depicted as a literal stare at the foe, whereas Pokémon Horizons: The Series aligns its portrayal of the move with its descriptions found in the majority of core games.

Title: Dream Eater (move)

For information on the e-Reader application, refer to Dream Eater (e-Reader). Known as ゆめくい (Dream Eat) in Japanese, Dream Eater is a Psychic-type attack that inflicts damage, originating in Generation I. It served as TM85 across games from Generation IV up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was TM59. This move is only effective against a sleeping target; if the foe is not asleep, it fails. Dream Eater causes damage, and the user recovers HP equal to 50% of the damage dealt to the opponent (not exceeding the user's maximum HP). Should the attack inflict only 1 HP of damage, the user will still regain 1 HP. Pokémon possessing the Comatose Ability are also susceptible to Dream Eater, as this Ability causes them to behave as if asleep, despite not having the actual status condition. When the user holds a Big Root, the amount of HP recovered is boosted by 30%, resulting in 65% of the damage dealt being restored. It's important to note that Big Root has no effect on the damage output itself. Conversely, targeting a Pokémon with the Liquid Ooze Ability will cause the user to lose HP equivalent to what would have been recovered. If a Pokémon is afflicted by Heal Block, it becomes incapable of executing Dream Eater. Should no other moves be available for use, the Pokémon will resort to Struggle. In scenarios where the user's current HP surpasses its maximum HP upon restoration, the current HP will be capped at its maximum value. Within the Generation I localized game versions, Dream Eater will not restore HP to the user if it breaks a substitute. However, in the Japanese Generation I games and every installment of the Pokémon Stadium series, Dream Eater consistently fails against a target protected by a substitute. For Pokémon Contests, Dream Eater serves as a potential second move in a combination, awarding the user 2 extra appeal points if Calm Mind or Hypnosis was performed immediately before it. Later, Dream Eater gained the ability to strike a substitute and correctly restore the user's HP when inflicting damage upon one, even if the substitute is ultimately broken. In Contest Spectaculars, this move can form part of a combination, granting three additional appeal points if a sleep-inducing status move (excluding Psycho Shift and Rest) was executed in the preceding turn. The move causes damage, and 50% of that damage, including any excess, is returned to the user as HP. It will not work if the opponent lacks a sleep-related status condition. Furthermore, the Muzzle status condition impacts this move. Within Gates to Infinity, the user's HP recovery is calculated solely on the target's HP loss, disregarding any excess damage inflicted. This mechanic reverted to its original behavior in Super Mystery Dungeon. Prior to Generation V, Dream Eater stood alone as the sole HP-draining move immune to Liquid Ooze's effects. It remains the only Psychic-type move capable of draining HP. Among all HP-draining moves, Dream Eater boasts the greatest base power. Uniquely, Dream Eater was the only draining move in Generation III that still failed against a substitute; however, it has functioned against substitutes starting from Generation V. Although strongly linked to Dream Eater, Drowzee and Hypno are unable to learn it via leveling up, except in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Significantly, since Dream Eater is not a TM in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Drowzee and Hypno cannot legitimately acquire it in those titles, even though both Pokémon and the move itself are featured.

Title: Poison Gas (move)

The term どくガス redirects here; for the Pokémon Shuffle Skill "Poisonous Mist," which shares the same Japanese designation, see Skill (Shuffle) → Poisonous Mist. Poison Gas (Japanese: どくガス Poison Gas) is a non-damaging Poison-type move, first introduced in Generation I. It inflicts the poisoned status condition on its target, possessing an accuracy of 55%. However, Poison-type Pokémon cannot be affected by this move. In the Generation II core series games, when an in-game opponent (excluding those in the Battle Tower) utilized Poison Gas, the move had an additional 25% chance to fail, separate from its normal chance to miss. In Double and Triple Battles, Poison Gas's accuracy is increased to 80% and it now targets all adjacent opponents. This move can also contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, causing Hex, Venom Drench, and Venoshock to yield an extra three appeal points if used on the following turn. When powered up by a Poisonium Z into Z-Poison Gas, it causes the user's Defense stat to rise by one stage. Poison Gas consistently leaves the target poisoned, and it is affected by Magic Coat. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Poison Gas, were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any captured Pokémon. It notably appeared as a move used by Boss Koffing and Stunky. Any status conditions inflicted by this move apply to each target successfully hit. The description for this move in Pokémon Masters EX, prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), read: "Leaves all opposing sync pairs poisoned." In the English dub of "A Tent Situation," Ash commanded his Muk to use Poison Gas; in the original Japanese version, he simply called out Muk's name.

Title: Barrage (move)

Originating in Generation I, Barrage (たまなげ in Japanese, meaning "Ball Throw") is a multistrike maneuver of the Normal type that inflicts damage. This attack serves as the signature technique for both Exeggcute and Exeggutor. When employed, Barrage deals damage by striking the chosen opponent between two and five times. Specifically, there is a 37.5% probability of striking twice, an equal 37.5% chance for three hits, a 12.5% likelihood of connecting four times, and also a 12.5% chance of delivering five blows. Initially, only the opening hit had the potential to be a critical hit, though all subsequent strikes would inflict identical damage. Furthermore, this move would cease instantly upon destroying a substitute. For the purposes of Bide and Counter, only the final strike of Barrage was taken into account.

In a later iteration, each individual strike began to deal damage independently, allowing any of them to land as a critical hit. This also meant Barrage could now persist in its assault even after shattering a substitute. When the user held a King's Rock, the opportunity arose for the last hit to make the opponent flinch. Subsequently, if either a King's Rock or Razor Fang was held, every single strike thereafter gained an equivalent chance to induce flinching in the target. The probabilities for the number of strikes were later adjusted: 35% for two hits, 35% for three hits, 15% for four hits, and 15% for five hits. Taking into account a 15% base miss chance, the average overall base power for this move stands at 39.5.

Should a Focus Sash be triggered prior to the final strike, the subsequent hit is guaranteed to cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its ability to activate multiple times to avert a KO, with each activation opportunity being distinct. For a defending Pokémon with a maximum HP of 1, the ability Sturdy will be triggered by every single hit. Likewise, if the target possesses the abilities Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will independently activate their effects. Nevertheless, Barrage proves ineffective against opponents endowed with the Bulletproof ability. Fundamentally, Barrage remains a move that delivers damage by hitting the target 2-5 times per use. Each individual strike undergoes its own accuracy verification, yet the move will proceed even if one of these checks results in a miss.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad game series, the move experiences a reduced critical-hit rate, specifically set at 2%. Super Mystery Dungeon sees its range extended, striking a fan-shaped zone reaching up to five tiles distant. Should a Reviver Seed be activated, the execution of Barrage will cease. Barrage stands as one of only three Generation I moves, alongside Kinesis and Struggle, that has not yet made an appearance in the animated television series. Notably, it is also the sole move showcased in Pokémon Origins that has not been included in the main animated series.

Title: Leech Life (move)

Known as きゅうけつ (Suck Blood) in Japanese, Leech Life debuted in Generation I as a Bug-type attack that deals damage. It was designated as TM28 in the titles Pokémon Sun and Moon, as well as Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. The move inflicts harm, and the user recovers HP equal to up to 50% of the damage dealt to the adversary (with a minimum of 1 HP restored), never exceeding the user's maximum HP.

In localized Generation I games, should Leech Life shatter a substitute, the user receives no HP restoration. Conversely, the Japanese Generation I releases, every iteration of the Pokémon Stadium series, and all Generation II titles cause Leech Life to invariably miss if the opponent possesses a substitute. Currently, Leech Life correctly heals the user even when damaging or breaking a substitute. Should the target possess the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user suffers damage rather than recovering health. Equipping a Big Root boosts the HP recovered by 30%, resulting in 65% of the damage dealt being restored; however, Big Root has no effect on the amount of damage inflicted. Its execution is prevented if the user is affected by Heal Block.

Leech Life saw its power augmented from 20 to 80, while its PP was simultaneously decreased from 15 to 10. This modification led to Absorb taking Leech Life's place in the level-up learnsets for the majority of Pokémon. It inflicts harm, and up to half of the total damage dealt to the target, including any excess damage, is returned to the user as HP. Exclusively in Adventure Squad, the move's healing is set at 20% of the damage caused. Only in Gates to Infinity does the user's healing depend on the target's HP loss, disregarding excess damage. The Muzzled status condition impacts this particular move. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description stated: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target."

Blue's Venomoth, in The Secret of Kangaskhan, was shown via his Pokédex to be capable of using Leech Life. Generation VII saw Leech Life's power quadrupled, an enhancement that represents the greatest percentage increase for a move's power within a single generation, and equals Fury Cutter's cumulative power boost when considering multiple increases. Furthermore, this constitutes the most significant power surge for any move since Self-Destruct and Explosion received boosts in Generation II. Nevertheless, this alteration also involved its removal from the early level-up learnsets of numerous Pokémon in earlier generations, with Absorb becoming the replacement.

Title: Lovely Kiss (move)

Hailing from Generation I, Lovely Kiss (known in Japanese as あくまのキッス, or Demon's Kiss) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move stands as the signature attack of Jynx and functions as the sleep-inducing equivalent to Sweet Kiss. Exclusively in the Generation I handheld titles, Lovely Kiss possessed the rare ability to bypass a target's substitute. A peculiar condition existed in the Generation II core series games: if an in-game opponent (outside of the Battle Tower) attempted to use this move, it carried an additional 25% chance to fail, beyond its standard accuracy check.

Pokémon possessing the Abilities Insomnia or Vital Spirit are entirely immune to its effects. Similarly, creatures shielded by Sweet Veil remain unaffected. In Contest Spectaculars, Lovely Kiss can initiate a combination, causing specific subsequent moves – Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap – to grant an extra three appeal points if performed on the following turn. When empowered by a Normalium Z into Z-Lovely Kiss, the user receives a one-stage boost to their Speed stat.

From Versions 1.0 to 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Lovely Kiss was inexplicably unselectable during combat. However, with the release of Version 1.3.0, its usability was restored, functioning identically to its portrayal in prior generations. The move's primary effect is to put the opposing Pokémon to sleep. It is susceptible to Magic Coat and is also influenced by the Muzzled status condition.

Within the game data for Pokémon Gold and Silver, Smoochum is programmed to acquire Lovely Kiss as an Egg Move. Yet, the absence of any male Pokémon within Smoochum's Egg Group that can legitimately learn the move meant it could not be passed down. By Pokémon Crystal, this specific Egg Move designation was removed. The Japanese moniker's literal meaning aligns with the move's visual sequences in the Generation II and III games, as well as Battle Revolution, where a demonic figure descends from the screen's top to impart a kiss upon the adversary. Sweet Kiss featured a comparable animated sequence prior to Generation VII. This same demonic character also makes an appearance in the Generation II animation of the move Spite.

Title: Sky Attack (move)

Known in Japanese as ゴッドバード (God Bird), Sky Attack is a damage-dealing Flying-type move that first appeared in Generation I. In Generation II, it functioned as the signature move of Moltres, although other Pokémon were still able to acquire it through breeding. It is designated as TM43 in Generation I and will be TM153 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon selection, Sky Attack's initial turn yields no action beyond a message indicating the user is glowing. On the subsequent turn, Sky Attack inflicts damage, depletes a PP point, and registers as the last move used. Once Sky Attack has been chosen, the user becomes unable to switch out until the move is either disrupted or fully executed. If Sky Attack is not fully carried out, no PP is deducted, and it does not count as the most recently used move. Should the target utilize Mirror Move during the turn the user is glowing, Mirror Move will copy the move the user performed immediately before initiating Sky Attack (or fail if it cannot). Sleep, freeze, binding, and flinching will suspend Sky Attack's duration without canceling its effect. Within the Stadium games, Mirror Move can replicate Sky Attack on either of the two turns it takes to execute.

Presently, Sky Attack holds a 30% chance of causing the target to flinch, and it also features an elevated critical hit ratio. The in-game message displayed during its charge now reads "<Pokémon> became cloaked in a harsh light!", replacing the previous "<Pokémon> is glowing!". If the user is holding a Power Herb, the charging turn is entirely bypassed. In Triple Battles, Sky Attack has the capability to strike opponents that are not directly adjacent. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Sky Attack's power was increased from 140 to 200. The user enters a specific "Sky Attack" status condition on the first turn, enabling the move's unleash on the next turn. In the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the move's damage is doubled at the conclusion of damage calculation. Its nature as a two-turn move prevents it from being linked. Sky Attack cannot be used within a Pokémon Tower in the Adventure Squad series. Its increased critical hit ratio applies exclusively since Super Mystery Dungeon.

In Pokémon GO, Sky Attack is a Charged Attack that has been available to players since February 16, 2017. Sky Attack possesses the highest base power among all Flying-type moves, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. It also boasts the highest base power of all moves capable of inflicting flinching. Additionally, Sky Attack holds the highest base power among all moves with an increased critical-hit ratio. A Supersonic Skystrike based on Sky Attack also achieves the highest base power of all Flying-type moves. Sky Attack and Brave Bird exhibit similar in-game animations during Generation IV and V; their primary distinction lies in Sky Attack's blue aura compared to Brave Bird's red one. This visual characteristic was inverted in the animated series; Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire portrayed Sky Attack with a red or orange aura, which then shifted to white or yellow in Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, a portrayal maintained until Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon. Notably, in Generation I, Sky Attack was the sole Flying-type move that could be taught via TM.

Title: Transform (move)
For discussions of the ability in Pokémon Shuffle, refer to Skill (Shuffle) → Transform. Regarding the Special Ability found in Pokémon Battle Trozei, consult the List of Special Abilities in Pokémon Battle Trozei.

Known in Japanese as へんしん (Transform), Transform is a Normal-type status move, incapable of inflicting damage, which first appeared in Generation I. The Imposter Ability functions identically to Transform. This move causes the user to assume the form of its target. A Pokémon that has transformed will revert to its original state if it is withdrawn from battle, faints, or when the battle concludes.

When powered up by a Normalium Z into Z-Transform, the user's HP is fully restored. Transform bypasses accuracy checks and consistently connects, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. Type immunities do not affect its execution. Transform fails if the target is shielded by Crafty Shield, yet it successfully impacts targets protected by other moves such as Protect or Detect. It also fails when used on Shadow Pokémon, if either the user or the target is camouflaged by Illusion, if the target has a substitute, or if the target's Ability is Good as Gold. Some of these conditions varied in earlier generations; consult the table below for specifics on when these conditions would not prevent Transform's use. Exclusively in Pokémon Stadium, Transform fails if the target's current species is Ditto. However, in the earlier Japan-only game Pokémon Stadium, Transform works normally against Ditto. As of Version 3.0.1 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Transform fails when used in a wild Pokémon battle by any Pokémon other than Ditto or Mew. This change was implemented to resolve a cloning exploit involving wild Smeargle using the move, though it also impacts player-owned Smeargle. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, Pokémon that know Transform are prohibited from participating in Pokéstar Studios films.

Generally, Transform copies the target's appearance, types, Ability, stats, and moves; the user's HP and level remain unchanged. More precise details are outlined subsequently. *: In Generation II, the Love Ball utilizes the copied Individual Values (IVs) for transformed wild Pokémon. See below.

Transform replicates any alterations to the target's type (e.g., from Conversion or Color Change), Ability (e.g., from Skill Swap), or moves (via Mimic). It also copies types bestowed by Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat. If the target has shed a type due to Roost, Transform will copy the type(s) the target possessed before Roost was employed. Should a dual-type target lose one of its types from Burn Up or Double Shock, Transform will only copy the remaining type. If a target loses its sole type (excluding added types) due to Burn Up or Double Shock, becoming typeless, Transform will replicate the Normal type. The user's level is inconsequential, except in Generations I-IV if the Pokémon gains a level and its stats are recalculated.

Appearance encompasses the Pokémon's sprite or model displayed in battle, covering its species, gender, form, and whether it is Shiny. (Before Generation IV, there was intricate nuance concerning the transformation's coloration.) The replicated stats (Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, Speed) incorporate the final values (as seen on the summary screen) alongside the target's base stats, individual values, and effort values. Aside from the transformation's appearance, gender (both for battle mechanics and the gender symbol post-name) was only copied starting in Generation V, with a minor exception in Generation II. Transform will not alter status conditions or volatile statuses based on the transformed type's immunities. For example, a Pokémon remains poisoned even after transforming into a Poison or Steel-type, and stays seeded by Leech Seed even after becoming a Grass-type.

In Generation I exclusively, if a player's transformed Pokémon deals or receives a critical hit, its untransformed stats are used in the damage calculation. (The calculation correctly employs transformed stats for wild and NPC Pokémon.) In all generations, transformed Pokémon are depicted in their untransformed state in the summary and party screens. (This also applies to their moves, Ability, and stats.) In Nintendo DS and 3DS titles, the move list shown on the bottom screen during battle will also display the Pokémon's untransformed sprite. In Generation I, if either the user's or target's sprite changed due to Acid Armor or Minimize, using Transform will display the transformation's standard sprite.

In Generation I and II core series games, a transformed Pokémon retains its original color palette. In Pokémon Stadium 2 and Generation III games (including Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness), a transformed Pokémon's Shiny appearance depends on whether the user was originally Shiny; from Generation IV onward, it depends on whether the target was Shiny. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, if the Partner Pikachu/Eevee is Transform's target, any clothes they wear will be copied.

Transform copies the target's current form. In Generations III and IV, a transformed Pokémon could change form if it met the conditions for a form change (e.g., Arceus holding a Plate). Since Generation V, transformed Pokémon cannot change forms. Dynamax transformations are not considered forms, meaning Transform will copy the base form of a Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon or Eternamax Eternatus, and a transformed Pokémon can still Dynamax. If Transform copies a Gigantamax Pokémon, it does not copy the Gigantamax Factor, so Dynamaxing will not result in a Gigantamax Pokémon. If a Dynamaxed Pokémon uses Transform, it will fail if the target is Zacian, Zamazenta, or Eternatus. Transform will fail if the target is a Starmobile. If the target is a Titan Pokémon, the user transforms into a regular version of that species. Transform does not copy Tera Types; thus, a transformed Pokémon that Terastallizes will use its own Tera Type. If a Terastallized Pokémon uses Transform, the move will not alter its type. Transform fails against a Terastallized Ogerpon or Terapagos. If a Pokémon Transforms into Ogerpon or Terapagos, it will be unable to Terastallize. If a Pokémon Terastallizes into the Stellar type and uses Transform, it will fail.

When a move is copied, it will have 5 PP, unless its natural maximum PP is lower (such as Sketch). In Generations I and II, the maximum PP is equivalent to the move's inherent maximum PP; in Generations III and IV, each copied move benefits from PP Ups applied to the corresponding move slot of the user's original moves; since Generation V, the maximum PP for copied moves is 5. In Generations I-III, PP-restoring items like Ether can only replenish PP for a move if the Pokémon possessed that move before transforming. For a held Leppa Berry, if a move the Pokémon did not originally know reaches 0 PP, the Berry will not activate.

Transform does not influence the user's Hidden Power (whose type, and power up to Generation V, are determined by the user's original IVs). In Generation II, Attract operates based on the target's original gender. In Generation II only, Mirror Move invariably fails when used by a transformed Pokémon. Telekinesis remains effective even if the target is transformed into a Pokémon that would ordinarily be immune to it. A transformed Pokémon can use Hyperspace Fury if it has transformed into Hoopa Unbound. A Pokémon can utilize a species-exclusive Z-Move if transformed into the correct species and holding the corresponding Z-Crystal, unless it is Mega Evolved. If a Pokémon transforms into Meteor Form Minior, Shields Down will not prevent it from being afflicted with status conditions.

A transformation cannot be transferred via Baton Pass. A transformed Pokémon retains its original species for the purpose of determining if it receives boosts from most Pokémon-specific stat-enhancing items. Only Metal Powder and Quick Powder utilize the transformation's species. For Eviolite's effect, the user's original species is considered, rather than the Pokémon it transformed into. This is relevant when using certain Poké Balls on a transformed wild Pokémon:

In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, during a Pokémon Contest, the Pokémon will transform into one of the other contestants. In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, during a Pokémon Super Contest, the Pokémon will transform into one of the other contestants. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, during a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, when Transform is used, a substitute doll appears on stage, and the user transforms into the doll. The effect of Transform will last for the round it is used in.

Generation I contains several glitches related to Transform. Any transformed wild Pokémon caught will become Ditto, owing to the Transform assumption oversight. If a transformed Pokémon uses Transform again, its previous stats (stored as a backup) are overwritten by its current stats (as they are backed up so that Transform can change the working stats); this constitutes the Transform DV manipulation glitch. If

Title: Bubble (move)

Bubble (Japanese: あわ Bubble) is a Water-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation I. Initially, this move possessed a power stat of 20. Beyond causing damage, it offered a 33.2% probability of decreasing the opponent's Speed stat by one level. If an in-game adversary employed Bubble, an extra 25% chance of its secondary effect failing was present, bringing the actual success rate to 24.9%. Currently, Bubble carries a 10% likelihood of reducing the target's Speed stat by one stage. Should an in-game opponent utilize it, a supplementary 25% chance of the effect failing persists, leading to an effective success rate of 7.5%. This additional failure chance is nullified under the influence of Lock-On or Mind Reader, and it also doesn't apply within the Battle Tower environment. At a later point, the extra failure chance for Bubble's secondary effect, when used by an in-game opponent, was removed. In Double Battles, Bubble strikes both Pokémon on the opposing side. However, in Triple Battles, its reach is limited to only adjacent opposing Pokémon. Within Pokémon Contests, Bubble serves as a viable second move in a combination, granting the performer 2 bonus appeal points if Rain Dance was executed during the preceding turn. The move's power stat was subsequently boosted from 20 to 40. During Horde Encounters, Bubble expands its reach to target every opponent present. It causes damage and has the potential to decrease the target's Travel Speed by one stage. Its in-game description before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Speed." Notably, in the English dub of "Bye Bye Psyduck," Marina instructed her Starmie to unleash Bubble, even though Starmie is incapable of learning this particular attack; the original Japanese script, however, had the command for Bubble Beam. Similarly, in the dubbed "Enter The Dragonite," Ash directed his Squirtle to employ Bubble, but in the Japanese original, Bubble Beam was the move requested. In the episode "Noibat Stands in the Way," Tierno ordered his Corphish to use Bubble, though the Pokémon failed to hear him and thus did not execute the technique. Later, in "The Delivery of Rotom and the Girl," Professor Kukui instructed his Popplio to use Bubble, yet its performance occurred off-screen. Despite this, Popplio cannot learn Bubble.

Title: Spore (move)

This entry focuses on the move Spore. For information on the Ability with the same Japanese name, please see Effect Spore (Ability). Spore, known as キノコのほうし (Mushroom Spore) in Japanese, is a Grass-type status move that first appeared in Generation I. It does not inflict damage. Before Generation III, Spore was exclusively learned by Paras and Parasect.

Specific game mechanics have varied: In Generation I handheld titles, Spore possessed the unique capability to affect targets positioned behind a substitute. During the Generation II core series games, when executed by an in-game opponent outside of the Battle Tower, Spore carried a 25% chance of failure.

Certain Pokémon and conditions grant immunity to Spore's effects. Pokémon with the Abilities Insomnia or Vital Spirit cannot be affected. Furthermore, Grass-type Pokémon, those with the Overcoat Ability, and Pokémon equipped with Safety Goggles are now immune. Pokémon under the influence of Sweet Veil are also protected from Spore.

Beyond battles, Spore plays a role in Contest Spectaculars. When used as part of a combination, it causes subsequent uses of Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, or Wake-Up Slap to earn an additional three appeal points on the following turn. When enhanced by a Grassium Z into Z-Spore, it gains the effect of resetting all of the user's decreased stats.

Spore's primary function is to put enemies to sleep. It targets all opposing Pokémon present in the area, attempting to induce sleep. Notably, Spore holds the distinction of being the sole sleep-inducing status move with a perfect 100% accuracy.

Regarding its availability, Pokémon capable of learning Spore naturally (excluding Smeargle's use of Sketch) have exclusively been introduced in odd-numbered generations, with each such generation adding at least one. A consistent theme among all Pokémon that naturally acquire Spore is their association with mushrooms. This connection stems directly from its Japanese name, Mushroom Spore (キノコのほうし). The mushroom motif is further emphasized by the move's descriptions in Pokémon Stadium and Pokémon Stadium 2, and visually, the move's animation has depicted a mushroom releasing spores since Generation VII.

Title: Flash (move)

This article pertains to the attack known as Flash. (For its counterpart in the Ranger series, refer to Field Move (Ranger)). The Normal-type, non-damaging move Flash (Japanese: フラッシュ Flash) made its debut in Generation I. It served as HM05 across Generations I through III, and later as TM70 from Generations IV to VI, a role it reprised in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

In battle, Flash reduces the target's accuracy stat by a single stage. Its own accuracy is rated at 70%. A unique mechanic existed in the Generation I and II core series games: when Flash was employed by an in-game opponent outside of the Battle Tower, it carried a 25% chance to fail, separate from its inherent miss chance. This supplementary failure condition for opponent-used Flash has since been removed. When channeled through a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Flash, the user's evasiveness will be boosted by one stage.

To utilize Flash as a field move, specific badges were necessary in some titles: namely, the Boulder Badge, Zephyr Badge, or Knuckle Badge. Its primary function outside of combat was to light up dim caves. Players could activate Flash from the party screen provided they were situated in a darkened section of a cave. The illumination provided by Flash persists even when traversing different areas within the same cave (including sections that might naturally be bright). Furthermore, its effect carries over if a player resumes a game session saved within a Flash-lit cave. Upon exiting a cave, however, the area will revert to its dark state should the player re-enter.

Exclusively in Generation I, activating Flash outside of a dark cave still triggered the message "A blinding Flash lights the area!", despite the move having no practical impact. Starting from Generation II, Flash became unusable if it offered no benefit. Typically, Flash only brightens a wide circular area surrounding the player, improving visibility in caves. However, in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, and Pokémon Platinum, it brightens the entire screen, mirroring its effect in Generation I.

In Pokémon X and Y, as there are no dark caves to be found, Flash instead causes the screen to zoom out, revealing a greater portion of the surrounding area than is typically visible. Additionally, it reduces the chance of encountering wild Pokémon. Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire reintroduced areas with reduced visibility that Flash could illuminate. However, in these games, the dark areas appeared dimly lit rather than completely dark, making the move less essential. In X and Y, Flash may be used to zoom out the perspective in the following caves: Flash is no longer usable outside of battle.

For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, players utilize Secret Technique Light Up as an alternative. Nevertheless, Flash made a comeback as an out-of-battle option for lighting up dark caves in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Flash also facilitated entry to hidden areas: in Pokémon Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, it allowed access to a concealed room in the Ruins of Alph. Employing Flash in Pokémon Emerald's Ancient Tomb opened a chamber where Registeel resides. Furthermore, in Pokémon Black and White, Flash provided entry to the 3rd floor of the Abyssal Ruins.

Flash is never an indispensable requirement for main gameplay progression, as dark caves remain traversable even without its assistance. While some puzzles depend on Flash, none are obligatory for full game completion. Notably, Flash stands as the sole Generation I HM move usable outside of combat, yet it cannot be chosen for battle in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Psywave (move)

Psywave, known in Japanese as サイコウェーブ (Psycho Wave), is a damaging Psychic-type attack that debuted in Generation I, where it was designated as TM46. During Generation II, it served as Misdreavus's signature technique, though other Pokémon could still acquire it through breeding.

This move inflicts a variable amount of damage. When utilized by the player, the damage ranges from 1 HP up to 1.5 times the user's level. However, if an opposing Pokémon employs Psywave, the damage can fluctuate between 0 and 1.5 times the user's level. Should a value of 0 be generated during a link battle, a desynchronization glitch occurs: the target's game registers 0 damage, while the attacker's game continues to generate pseudorandom numbers until a value between 1 and 1.5 times the user's level is produced. Furthermore, the game may softlock if a Pokémon at Level 0, 1, or 171 uses Psywave, though such Pokémon are typically only obtainable through glitches. In Pokémon Stadium, Psywave is programmed to always deal a minimum of 1 damage.

The damage inflicted by Psywave, generally ranging from 1 HP to 1.5 times the user's level, is always rounded down. Nevertheless, it is guaranteed to cause at least 1 HP of damage. This move disregards type weaknesses and resistances, but type immunities remain effective. In its original Generation I implementation, Psywave's damage calculation was given by the formula ⌊Leveluser×(10r+50)100⌋, where 'r' is a random integer from 0 to 10. If this calculation results in 0, 1 HP of damage is dealt instead. In another common description, Psywave's damage is computed as ⌊Leveluser×(r+50)100⌋, with 'r' being a random integer from 0 to 100. Again, if the outcome is 0, the move instead inflicts 1 HP.

As a Pokémon Contest combination move, Psywave functions as a second-stage action, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Calm Mind was performed in the preceding turn.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Psywave's mechanics differ. For the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the move's damage output falls between 1/2 and 383/256 (approximately 1.49×) of the user's level. Unlike the main series, it is influenced by type matchups and STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus). Before these modifiers are applied, the move guarantees a minimum of 1 HP damage and a maximum of 199, although 199 is not achievable under normal gameplay conditions. In Super Mystery Dungeon, Psywave's effect is further revised, no longer being affected by type matchups and dealing damage roughly between 1 HP and 1.5 times the user's level.

In the anime episode All Things Bright and Beautifly!, Jessie commanded her Seviper to use Psywave; however, Seviper is unable to learn this move, preventing its execution. Notably, Psywave was linked to two distinct glitches in Generation I, one of which could lead to a desynchronization of games during link battles under normal gameplay scenarios.

Title: Splash (move)

Splash, known as はねる (Hop) in Japanese, first appeared in Generation I as a Normal-type attack that inflicts no damage. Before Generation II, Magikarp was its exclusive original user. This technique possesses absolutely no functional impact. Its activation merely triggers a visual 'dipping' sequence and a message to the player confirming its lack of outcome. The Generation I message displayed was "No effect!". From Generation II onward, the text changed to "But nothing happened.", evolving to replace the period with an exclamation mark starting in Generation III.

Gravity's influence prevents the execution of Splash. Should a Pokémon only have Splash as an available move while Gravity is active, it will resort to using Struggle instead. When amplified by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Splash, it grants a formidable three-stage boost to the user's Attack stat.

Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad installments, Splash propels the user to an arbitrary neighboring tile, potentially traversing corners. Should this movement result in contact with another Pokémon, both combatants suffer 5 HP of damage, and the user reverts to its initial position. However, in Gates to Infinity, this technique again proves entirely ineffectual. Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon redefines its function, allowing the user to leap over as many as three Pokémon directly ahead, landing on the tile beyond them. Even when afflicted by the Stuck status, Splash remains usable, though it offers no remedy for the condition. Furthermore, it possesses the utility to disorient foes actively tracking the Pokémon's path. Conversely, when employed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Splash yields absolutely no result. Apart from exhibiting a brief animation and allowing the user to select its facing direction afterward, Splash carries no inherent function.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, status techniques, Splash included, were exclusively employed by adversaries and unattainable for captured Pokémon. Opponent Magikarp, Hoppip, Spoink, and Cosmog were observed utilizing Splash in this title.

In Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto gains access to the move Jump subsequent to acquiring it from a Magikarp within the Withered Wasteland locale. Given its strong connection to Magikarp, Jump's origins seem to trace back to Splash. This bestows upon Ditto the capability to leap via pressing the R button.

Despite its English designation and the animations present in Generation IV and V implying an aquatic connection, its Japanese name, はねる (haneru), encompasses both 'splash' and 'hop,' with the latter being its more common interpretation. This linguistic nuance clarifies its availability to non-aquatic species renowned for their bouncing or leaping, such as Spoink and Hoppip, and accounts for its incapacitation under the effects of Gravity. Nob Ogasawara, the translator responsible for Pokémon Red and Blue, acknowledged this naming discrepancy and proposed "Flop" as an alternative, but his suggestion was ultimately rejected by superiors.

Within the animated series, Splash frequently emerges as the outcome of Metronome's unpredictable effect. In the main series Pokémon games, Splash, along with the event-only techniques Celebrate and Hold Hands, stands as one of the few moves that possess absolutely no functional impact. Uniquely in Pokémon GO, Splash is the sole acquirable move whose elemental typing differs from its core series counterpart.

Title: Acid Armor (move)

Acid Armor, known as とける (Liquefy) in Japanese, is a non-damaging Poison-type move that made its debut in Generation I. This move primarily functions to sharply increase the user's Defense stat by two stages. When enhanced by a Poisonium Z, transforming into Z-Acid Armor, it additionally resets all of the user's stats that have been lowered.

Exclusively in Generation I, Acid Armor visually renders the user invisible for the remainder of the current battle, though this visual alteration has no actual impact on gameplay. The user's sprite will disappear if battle animations are active, unless the move fails due to the user's Defense having already been boosted by six stages. The invisible Pokémon regains visibility if it utilizes Minimize (adopting its small Minimize sprite), Substitute (being replaced by a Substitute), or Transform (being transformed into the same species as the target). It also becomes visible once more after it faints, is switched out, or when the battle concludes. The Pokémon remains visible within the summary screen.

If a Pokémon already protected by an active Substitute uses Acid Armor, the Substitute itself becomes invisible. Nevertheless, the Pokémon will appear visible the instant it uses any move (because the visible Pokémon emerges from behind the Substitute to execute the move), and the Substitute concurrently becomes visible. The Pokémon also reappears if its Substitute breaks. Furthermore, should a Pokémon attempt to use Transform on an invisible target, the invisibility is disregarded, and the user will become a visible duplicate of the target.

The Defense stat increase provided by Acid Armor returns to its normal level when the user progresses to the next floor or steps on a Wonder Tile.

Title: Crabhammer (move)

Emerging in Generation I, Crabhammer (Japanese: クラブハンマー Crab Hammer) is a Water-type move designed to inflict damage upon its target. Before Generation III, it held the distinction of being the signature attack unique to both Krabby and Kingler. This powerful move carries a base power of 90 and an 85% accuracy rating, also benefiting from an inherently increased critical hit ratio. For Pokémon Contest participants, Crabhammer can serve as the subsequent action in a combination, awarding the user 3 additional appeal points if either Rain Dance or Swords Dance was executed during the preceding turn. The enhanced critical hit probability stands at 50% throughout the Rescue Team series, and 30% within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series of games. In Pokémon GO, Crabhammer has been available as a Charged Attack since its introduction on August 23, 2019. Notably, Ruby's Pokédex confirmed a wild Crawdaunt's knowledge of Crabhammer in the animated episode "Stick This in Your Craw, Crawdaunt! II".

Title: Explosion (move)

Known in Japan as だいばくはつ (Giant Explosion), Explosion is a Normal-type offensive maneuver, first appearing in Generation I. It held the technical machine slot TM47 in Generation I, and subsequently TM64 from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, a designation it also retains in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This attack deals damage but inherently causes its user to collapse. Neither the Sturdy Ability nor a held Focus Band or Focus Sash can avert the user's inevitable fainting. Furthermore, its execution is impossible if a Pokémon with the Damp Ability is present on the battlefield. While its listed base power stands at 170, the target's Defense stat is effectively halved during damage computation (unless its value is already 1), which consequently escalates its true damage output to an equivalent of 340 power. Regardless of whether the assault misses or if the adversary is immune (such as a Ghost-type Pokémon), the user of Explosion will still faint.

In Generation I, should Explosion successfully shatter an opponent's Substitute, the attacker will not faint, though its visual representation will become a blank image. This original sprite reappears if the user possessed a Substitute at that moment, employs Substitute thereafter, or is withdrawn from battle. When an Explosion user acts first and faints itself, the opposing Pokémon will neither execute its own action nor suffer any end-of-turn persistent damage for that turn.

Pokémon Stadium introduced changes: if Explosion demolishes a target's Substitute, the user now faints. Moreover, the base power displayed in Stadium already reflects the Defense-halving effect, showing 340.

Generation II saw Explosion's base power boosted to 250. The target's Defense remains halved, making its effective power 500. From this generation, the user now faints even if the target is shielded by a Substitute or protected by other means. Should the Explosion user move first and faint, the opponent will skip its attack that turn but still endure recurrent end-of-turn effects like Sandstorm. In contrast to its predecessor, Stadium 2 shows Explosion's base power *before* the Defense halving calculation.

Generation III introduced additional consequences: if Explosion fails due to the user's confusion, the Pokémon also takes increased confusion damage. The user's sprite also ceases to vanish after executing the move. If the Explosion user faints itself by attacking first, all other combatants on the field can still act during that turn.

In Generations IV and V, if all designated targets have already been knocked out on the current turn, leading to no available targets when Explosion attempts to execute, the move will fail, and its user will not faint.

Beginning in Generation VI, the temporary Defense reduction caused by Explosion no longer amplifies confusion damage.

Generation VII removed the Defense-halving effect on targets for Explosion. Furthermore, the user now faints *before* inflicting damage, a departure from prior generations. Even if all targets have already fallen this turn, leaving no active targets for Explosion's execution, the user will now faint regardless.

In Generation VIII, the move's mechanics reverted to the user dealing damage to the target *before* it faints.

Explosion can also be integrated into a Contest Spectacular combination, earning the user an additional three appeal points if Block, Mean Look, or Spider Web were performed on the preceding turn. When employed in a Max Raid or Tera Raid Battle, Explosion will simply fail, and its user will not faint.

Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move is reclassified as a status effect, causing the user to first gain an "Explosion" status. The actual explosion then occurs only if the user is attacked on the subsequent turn. Irrespective of the user, the resulting blast inflicts 40 HP damage upon enemy Pokémon and halves the current HP of the player's Pokémon (always dealing a minimum of 1 HP). Fire-types receive half the normal damage. In Super Mystery Dungeon specifically, the explosion instantly defeats affected enemies within range, though Fire-type enemies still only lose 50% of their HP. Damage to the player remains consistent with prior iterations of the game. Additionally, it obliterates all items and wall tiles within its radius in Mystery Dungeon, excluding those on the floor's edge and within locked rooms. However, in Gates to Infinity, it explicitly does not destroy walls. In Mystery Dungeon, this technique cannot be initiated during rainy weather or if a Pokémon possessing the Damp ability is present anywhere on the floor. For Gigantic Pokémon in Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's area of effect remains constant, but since it emanates from the user's central tile, Explosion will impact only the immediate surrounding single tile. Outside of main series, this move is exclusively known by particular opponents, and the sole playable sync pairs capable of utilizing it do so through the "Last Word" skill.

The in-game description before Version 2.10.0 (issued June 28, 2021) stated: "The user faints after using it." This was updated in Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022) to: "The user faints after using this move."

In the manga "Gotcha Where I Wantcha," Emerald's Pokédex confirmed that a Golem, rented by Noland from the Battle Factory, knew Explosion. Black referenced his Electrode employing Explosion in "Farewell To Pikachu?!," though the action itself was not depicted visually.

Explosion stands alone as the only move possessing a base power of 250. It claims the title for the highest fixed base power among all moves, exceeding even Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. Nevertheless, several other moves can achieve superior base power under specific circumstances: Last Respects, for instance, can reach an astounding 5050 power, becoming the highest of all moves, if 100 Pokémon have fainted on the user's side (a feat achievable by repeatedly reviving and fainting Pokémon). Rollout and Ice Ball can attain 960 power following a prior Defense Curl. Stored Power and Power Trip can each hit 860 if the user's stats are boosted by six stages across the board. Rage Fist ascends to 350 power after the user has been struck six times. Furthermore, Spit Up can reach 300 power after three uses of Stockpile. Interestingly, when Explosion is transformed into the Z-Move Breakneck Blitz, the resulting Z-Move actually possesses a lower base power (200) compared to Explosion's native 250.

A peculiar occurrence in Generation I: if either Selfdestruct or Explosion simultaneously defeats the final Pokémon on both sides of the field, the player will experience a normal blackout, yet the victory theme will still play. Self-Destruct, Explosion, and Misty Explosion are distinct as the sole three damaging attacks whose battle animations fully render even if they fail to connect or produce no discernible effect. Notably, every Pokémon capable of possessing the Aftermath Ability can learn Explosion.

Title: Fury Swipes (move)

This offensive Normal-type, multi-hit technique, known in Japanese as みだれひっかき (Fury Swipes), made its debut in Generation I. When employed, Fury Swipes strikes the foe between two and five times, dealing damage with each impact. The probability distribution for these hits was initially: a 37.5% likelihood for 2 hits, an identical 37.5% chance for 3 hits, and a 12.5% probability for both 4 hits and 5 hits, respectively. Originally, only the initial strike possessed the potential for a critical hit, though subsequent blows consistently inflicted an equal amount of damage. Exclusively within this generation, the move would cease prematurely upon shattering a substitute. Furthermore, attacks like Bide and Counter would solely account for the final hit delivered by Fury Swipes. Subsequently, all individual strikes were modified to inflict damage independently, allowing any of them to land as critical hits. The move was also updated to permit continued attacking even after breaking a substitute. When the Pokémon utilizing the move holds a King's Rock, the concluding strike gains a chance to induce flinching in the target. Should the opponent possess a contact-activated Ability, each individual strike is registered separately, allowing the Ability to potentially trigger on multiple occasions. For Pokémon equipped with the Skill Link Ability, Fury Swipes unfailingly connects for five hits, assuming the move does not miss. Later, if the user is equipped with either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every successive hit was granted an equivalent chance to make the foe flinch. In Pokémon Contests, Fury Swipes functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the performer 2 additional appeal points if Scratch preceded it in the prior turn. The revised probabilities for hitting are now: 35% for 2 hits, 35% for 3 hits, 15% for 4 hits, and 15% for 5 hits. Consequently, the average cumulative base power, factoring in the inherent 20% chance of missing, now stands at 44.6. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike landing, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its capacity to repeatedly avert fainting, though each activation chance operates independently. The Ability Sturdy will trigger with every hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. If the target possesses Weak Armor or Stamina, each individual strike of Fury Swipes will activate that Ability. When an opponent holds a Rocky Helmet, the user of Fury Swipes sustains damage for every single strike delivered. As a multi-hit attack, Fury Swipes targets the foe 2-5 times per application. Each individual hit is subject to its own accuracy check; however, the move will proceed with subsequent strikes even if one fails to connect. Its critical-hit rate experiences a reduction, specifically to 1% in the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series. From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, the activation of a Reviver Seed will cause the move to terminate. In the episode "The Breeding Center Secret," Cassidy intended to impart Fury Swipes to a stolen Sandshrew, aiming to enhance its utility. During the English dub of "The Ties That Bind," Ash notably directed his Heracross to use Fury Swipes, a move it is incapable of legitimately learning. However, in the original Japanese rendition, Ash's command to Heracross was for Fury Attack instead. Within the main series games, Fury Swipes stands as one of only two moves possessing a base power that is not a multiple of 5, sharing this distinction with Comet Punch. Pin Missile was once a third such move until its base power was elevated to 25 in Generation VI. With Comet Punch's power subsequently increasing to 30 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Fury Swipes remains the sole move across all core series games to lack a base power that is a multiple of 5. Beginning with Generation V, Fury Swipes holds the distinction of having the lowest accuracy among all multistrike moves, at 80%.

Title: Bonemerang (move)

The technique known as Bonemerang (Japanese: ホネブーメラン, also translated as Bone Boomerang) is a multi-strike, damage-dealing Ground-type attack that first appeared in Generation I. It is recognized as a signature ability of both Cubone and Marowak. Upon use, Bonemerang delivers two separate impacts to its target. Initially, only the first strike was capable of landing a critical hit, with the subsequent strike always dealing an identical amount of damage. Furthermore, in earlier game mechanics, Bonemerang's sequence would cease immediately if its initial strike successfully broke a substitute. The move Bide, however, exclusively registers the second strike of this technique.

Currently, each individual strike deals damage independently, thereby allowing either hit the potential to be a critical hit. Bonemerang will now continue to hit with its second strike even if the first strike shatters a substitute. Previously, if the Pokémon using Bonemerang held a King's Rock, only its final strike carried a chance to make the opponent flinch. Presently, when the user is equipped with either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every consecutive hit possesses an equal likelihood of causing the target to flinch.

In Pokémon Contests, Bonemerang can serve as the opening move of a combination, causing Bone Club and Bone Rush to generate double their standard appeal if performed on the very next turn. Conversely, it can complete a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Bone Club or Bone Rush was executed in the preceding turn. Should a Focus Sash activate before Bonemerang's last strike is delivered, the subsequent strike will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band retains its capacity to activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, though each chance for activation functions independently. Sturdy will be triggered by every hit if the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1. If the target has the abilities Weak Armor or Stamina, each individual strike from Bonemerang will activate these effects.

In Versions 1.0 through 1.1.1 of the Pokémon Sword and Shield games, Bonemerang could not be chosen in battle. However, starting with Version 1.2.0, it became selectable and operates as it did in prior generations. This move strikes the enemy twice. Prior to the release of Super Mystery Dungeon, if a Pokémon was knocked out by the first projectile, the second projectile was then able to target a new opponent and would still be executed even if a Reviver Seed activated. In the exclusive Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Bonemerang is designated as Cubone's Power Card. Once per game, the player controlling Cubone may force an opponent to pay double the rent on one of their properties if they land on it.

Title: Rest (move)

Known in Japan as ねむる (Rest), this Psychic-type, non-damaging technique made its debut in Generation I. It was designated TM44 across many titles, spanning from Generation I through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, including Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the TM05 slot.

Upon activation, Rest induces slumber in the Pokémon using it, simultaneously replenishing its Hit Points to their maximum capacity. For two turns following the execution of this move, the user remains incapacitated by sleep, unable to deploy any other techniques. However, a Pokémon possessing the Early Bird Ability will rouse from its nap one turn sooner, thus regaining the ability to act two turns after employing Rest.

As the user enters sleep, Rest also alleviates any other present non-volatile status ailments, specifically poison, paralysis, burn, or freeze. Conversely, it has no impact on temporary, volatile conditions like confusion, infatuation, or being seeded. Its effects manifest normally even when Safeguard protects the user. Nevertheless, Rest fails to induce sleep if the user possesses the Insomnia or Vital Spirit Abilities, as these inherently hinder slumber. Similarly, it interacts uniquely with Comatose, which treats the Pokémon as perpetually asleep, and Purifying Salt, which confers full immunity to non-volatile status conditions, including sleep.

Exclusively within Generations III and IV, a Pokémon with Soundproof can still utilize Rest even amidst an Uproar. From Generation V onwards, Leaf Guard enables its use under harsh sunlight. Furthermore, it operates if the user is grounded while Electric Terrain or Misty Terrain is active. Should the user be afflicted by Heal Block, Rest becomes unusable; if no alternative moves are accessible in such a scenario, the Pokémon will resort to Struggle.

A unique anomaly in the handheld Generation I titles meant that stat debuffs from paralysis and burn would persist even after Rest cured the underlying status, a quirk mitigable by swapping out the Pokémon. In contrast, Pokémon Stadium and subsequent iterations of the game ensure Rest clears any stat reductions caused by major status conditions. Solely in the handheld Generation I games, a Pokémon badly poisoned by Toxic, if it successfully uses Rest, will be relieved of its poisoned state; however, the Toxic counter remains unreset. Consequently, if the Pokémon subsequently takes damage from burn, Leech Seed, or poison, the calculation will still adhere to the bad poison formula (N * x), with the N value continuing to increment by 1 each instance (though a fresh Toxic affliction would indeed reset N to 1). Additionally, in the handheld Generation I titles, Rest will prove ineffective if the disparity between the user's maximum and current HP yields a remainder of 255 when divided by 256 (for example, values like 255 or 511). Unique to this generation, Rest could be accessed through Sleep Talk, functioning normally to restore HP and reset sleep turns. However, in all subsequent generations, Rest consistently fails when invoked via Sleep Talk, irrespective of the user's HP.

Within Pokémon Contests, Rest can initiate a combination, leading Sleep Talk and Snore to achieve twice their usual appeal if deployed immediately afterward. It may also conclude a combination, granting the user 2 additional appeal points if Belly Drum, Charm, or Yawn preceded it. For Contest Spectaculars, Rest contributes to a combination by bestowing an extra three appeal points upon Sleep Talk and Snore when they are performed on the subsequent turn.

When enhanced by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Rest, this move will clear all of the user's diminished stats. In another context (likely Pokémon GO), Rest recuperates 75% of the user's total HP and induces a drowsy state for four turns. It also causes the user to commence napping for 2-3, 6, or 5 turns, depending on the specific scenario. Upon the termination of this status, the Pokémon's HP is restored, its status conditions are alleviated, and any move seals are removed. Should the user be roused against its will (e.g., by Uproar), these beneficial effects will not manifest.

Snatch can intercept this move. In the Super Smash Bros. series, Rest serves as Jigglypuff's down special. Diverging from its function in the main Pokémon games, Jigglypuff employs this move not for self-healing, but as an offensive maneuver by falling asleep. A substantial accumulation of energy materializes at Jigglypuff's core precisely as it begins to doze, and this stored power inflicts damage upon foes, propelling them skyward. Before Super Smash Bros. Brawl, this attack would also ignite the affected opponent; however, from Brawl onward, it instead affixes a damage-dealing flower to their head. This technique is renowned for its immense potency, frequently resulting in a knockout against opponents in Melee, for instance. Its attack range is remarkably precise, with the collision box situated squarely at Jigglypuff's center. Mere proximity is insufficient; an opponent must practically overlap with Jigglypuff to be struck. After executing Rest, Jigglypuff remains asleep for a brief duration, during which it is incapable of performing any subsequent actions. A missed attack leaves Jigglypuff highly exposed, akin to a slumbering Pokémon being defenseless against incoming assaults.

Within the manga chapter Ursaring Major, Gold observed a wild Slowpoke with knowledge of Rest, though it never utilized the move. In Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Emerald's Pokédex confirmed that a Glalie, temporarily under Noland's command from the Battle Factory, knew Rest. Similarly, in Just My Luck...Shuckle, Emerald's Pokédex disclosed that Crystal's Blissey was capable of using Rest.

This move stands alone as the sole technique that both induces a non-volatile status condition and is self-targeting. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, a friend of Webster suggests that the name "Rest" is an abbreviation of "recover fast." The Japanese localization of this dialogue, however, asserts that 「ぐっすり」 (meaning to sleep soundly) is a compressed form of 「グッドスリープ」 (good sleep).

The English instruction booklet for Pokémon Yellow inaccurately states that Rest's duration is altered to between 1 and 3 turns when employed in Colosseum 2. Moreover, Shedinja's attempt to use Rest will invariably fail, as a non-fainted Shedinja perpetually possesses its maximum 1 HP. Historically, Rest has maintained its status as a widely accessible TM for an unparalleled duration, distinguishing itself as the only Technical Machine featured across every generation released to date.

Title: Rock Slide (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Rock Slide (Japanese: いわなだれ Rock Slide) is a damage-dealing Rock-type technique. It functioned as TM80 across Generation IV to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, as well as in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!, Let's Go, Eevee!, and Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was designated as TM22. Rock Slide causes damage without any additional effects. Furthermore, Rock Slide inflicts damage and carries a 30% probability of making each individual target flinch. During encounters with multiple adversaries, Rock Slide will strike all adjacent opponents. It also possesses the ability to demolish specific natural environmental features present in battle backgrounds. In a Pokémon Contest combination, Rock Slide serves as the second move, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Rock Throw was executed on the preceding turn. Consistent with other techniques in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Rock Slide in that game strikes only a single target. The move generates an array of rock projectiles that descend from above the designated target. This arrangement of rocks becomes fixed upon the completion of the wind-up animation and will not track the target's subsequent movements. Should even one rock connect with an enemy, that foe sustains the full damage of the attack, and the flinch probability is determined then. No additional damage or increased chances to flinch result from a target being hit by multiple rocks. A general summary states that Rock Slide causes damage and might make the target flinch. Prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description specified a "moderate chance of making the target flinch." In the manga 'Just My Luck...Shuckle', Emerald's Pokédex revealed that Lucy's Shuckle possessed knowledge of Rock Slide. Within 'Goodbye to a Peaceful Village!', Ginji and Mudkip merged their Flamethrower and Water Gun attacks to manifest a Rock Slide, subsequently employing it to vanquish Moltres. During Generation I, Rock Slide was the singular Rock-type Technical Machine. In the Japanese iterations of Pokémon Red, Green, Blue, and Yellow, the character who provides TM48 in exchange for a Soda Pop mentions Rock Slide's potential to occasionally cause the enemy to faint (Japanese: たまに あいてを きぜつ させる). The English versions of Pokémon Red and Blue, however, feature this character omitting any mention of fainting, instead stating Rock Slide can "spook the target sometimes." This particular phrasing might be an early allusion to flinching, despite the move lacking this specific effect until Generation II. Notably, this piece of dialogue is entirely absent from the English release of Pokémon Yellow. Champion Lance's Aerodactyl in Generation II is shown to know Rock Slide, notwithstanding its inability to learn this move before Generation III. Rock Slide holds the distinction as the sole Rock-type move capable of inducing flinching in its target. The only Rock-type Pokémon unable to acquire Rock Slide are Cornerstone Mask Ogerpon and Iron Boulder.

Title: Hyper Fang (move)

Introduced in Generation I, Hyper Fang (Japanese: ひっさつまえば, meaning "Certain-kill Fang") is a Normal-type attack designed to inflict damage. Prior to Generation IV, this move was among the signature techniques exclusive to Rattata and Raticate. Hyper Fang delivers damage and carries a 10% likelihood of causing the opposing Pokémon to flinch. When a Pokémon with the Strong Jaw ability utilizes Hyper Fang, its power receives a 50% increase. Although its data is present in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Hyper Fang cannot be chosen for use in battle because no Pokémon within those titles are capable of learning it. Nevertheless, it can be summoned through Metronome. Without a programmed animation, the game will experience a crash if battle effects are enabled and this move is used. In the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the Muzzled status condition impacts Hyper Fang. Despite what its Japanese name implies, Hyper Fang is not a one-hit knockout attack. In Generation VIII, Hyper Fang holds the unique distinction (aside from signature moves) of being the only move exclusively usable via Metronome. An unseen animation for Hyper Fang exists within the code of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, which displays a different visual style compared to earlier games.

Title: Sharpen (move)

Known in Japanese as かくばる (Improve Angles), Sharpen is a non-damaging Normal-type technique first introduced in Generation I. Until Generation V, it was recognized as Porygon's exclusive signature move. This move boosts the user's Attack stat by one stage upon activation. If enhanced with a Normalium Z, it transforms into Z-Sharpen, which provides the user with an additional stage increase to their Attack. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Sharpen, alongside every other status-modifying move, was restricted to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any Pokémon caught by players. Notably, Boss Porygon in that title was observed using Sharpen.

Title: Conversion (move)

The move known as Conversion (Japanese: テクスチャー Texture) made its debut in Generation I, categorized as a non-damaging Normal-type attack. Until Generation IX, it was considered a distinctive move for the Porygon family.

Its initial effect caused the user to adopt the target's current typing. The user's original type(s) are reverted upon switching out, fainting, or the conclusion of the battle. Repetitive use is possible, even if the user and target already share identical types. On the summary screen, Conversion's type alteration does not reflect, displaying the Pokémon's original type(s).

Its function evolved, causing Conversion to alter the user's type to correspond with the type of one of its own moves (Conversion itself included). Initially, it could not adopt the type of a move if that type already matched any of the Pokémon's current types, leading to failure if all moves were of an already existing type. Furthermore, Conversion could not previously be used on Curse to become a ???-type Pokémon, nor would it work if a Normal-type user's only other moves were Shadow moves.

More recently, with Curse redefined as a Ghost-type move, Conversion *can* be employed with it. The current mechanism for Conversion involves changing the user's type to align with the type of the move in its first move slot, regardless of whether that move is selectable in combat.

In certain contexts, Conversion allows the user to change its type to match one of its moves, even if the user already possesses that type. Notably, starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move chosen for this effect is always the one positioned at the top of the user's known moves list. Conversion considers the true type of Hidden Power, Natural Gift, and Weather Ball (though Weather Ball was considered Normal-type before the Explorers series).

When amplified by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Conversion, it confers a one-stage boost to the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats.

During Versions 1.0 through 1.1.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Conversion was not selectable in battles. However, commencing with Version 1.2.0, it became selectable and operated identically to its behavior in the two generations prior.

Reflect Type, a move introduced in Generation V, replicates the effect Conversion had during Generation I. The move description for Conversion in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire contained an error, stating "an own move's type" rather than "a known move's type." This textual inaccuracy was corrected in Pokémon Emerald.

Conversion is among a trio of moves that have appeared in a complete animated series but were never shown in the English dub. Minimize and Ion Deluge comprise the other two.

Title: Tri Attack (move)

Known in Japanese as トライアタック Tri Attack, this Normal-type damage-dealing move debuted in Generation I. It can be found as TM28 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Tri Attack delivers damage and carries a 20% chance of either paralyzing, freezing, or burning the target. Each of these ailments has an equal probability of being inflicted. This attack was notable for its ability to paralyze Electric-type Pokémon, burn Fire-type Pokémon, and freeze Ice-type Pokémon. Additionally, it possesses a 1/3 chance of thawing out a frozen Pokémon. However, the move later lost its capacity to burn Fire-types or freeze Ice-types. In Pokémon Contests, Tri Attack functions as a second-move combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Lock-On was performed during the previous turn. Subsequently, its ability to paralyze Electric-types was removed, as Electric-type Pokémon became immune to paralysis.

In Pokémon GO, Tri Attack is categorized as a Charged Attack, made available starting September 20, 2020. Uniquely, when employed by Skyla (Holiday 2020) & Togekiss, it transforms into a Flying-type move due to their passive skill, Flying Shift. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), its in-game description was: "Has a small chance of leaving the target burned, paralyzed, or frozen." Gold's Croconaw, Pikachu, and Chris's Cyndaquil collaboratively formed Tri Attack by combining their attacks in the episode "The Truth About The Mysterious Whirl Islands!!".

Title: Super Fang (move)

Known in Japanese as いかりのまえば, which translates to Fangs of Wrath, Super Fang made its debut as a Normal-type damage-dealing attack in Generation I. Until Generation IV, this technique served as a signature move for both Rattata and Raticate. It is listed as TM183 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Super Fang inflicts damage equivalent to half of the opposing Pokémon's current hit points, with the value always rounded down. It is guaranteed to deal a minimum of 1 HP damage. Within Pokémon Contests, Super Fang functions as the second move in a combination; using Scary Face on the preceding turn grants the performer an additional 2 appeal points. In Generations III and IV, King's Rock had no effect on Super Fang's outcome. When utilized in certain spin-off games, this attack targets a frontal opponent and cuts their HP in half. A key distinction from the main series is its inability to deliver a knockout to a target with 1 HP; in such instances, the move will instead fail. Its functionality is subject to the Muzzled status condition. In the episode "The Fortune Hunters," Cassidy directed her Raticate to execute Super Fang, but the assault was disrupted by James's Victreebel's Stun Spore.

Title: Slash (move)

This entry concerns the Pokémon move named Slash; for information regarding the Ultra Beast codenamed UB Slash in Japanese, please refer to Kartana (Pokémon). Introduced in Generation I, Slash (Japanese: きりさく Slash) functions as a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage and inherently features an elevated critical hit ratio. In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, this augmented critical hit rate is specified as 50% for the Rescue Team games and 30% for the Explorers and Adventure Squad titles. When used by a Pokémon with the Sharpness Ability, Slash's power is boosted by 50%. This move is suitable as a second action in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 3 bonus appeal points if Scratch or Swords Dance was performed in the preceding turn.

In Gates to Infinity, if a gigantic Pokémon uses Slash, its base Power Points (PP) are increased to 20, while its base accuracy is simultaneously reduced to 90%. Cynthia & Mega Garchomp's passive skill Ground Shift changes this move to a Ground-type. Similarly, Scottie/Bettie & ScytherEgg's passive skill Bug Shift causes Slash to become a Bug-type attack. For Lance & Charizard, their passive skill Dragon's Ascent transforms Slash into a Flying-type move that never misses.

In the English dubs of the anime episodes Tracey Gets Bugged and The Rivalry Revival, Tracey's Scyther was instructed to use Slash. This was an error in localization, as the Japanese version of both episodes featured Cut as the commanded move. Silver directed his Totodile to use Slash in Bellsprout Rout, though the action itself was not depicted on-panel. In Getting the Drop on Gallade I, a Zangoose featured in the Battle Castle was revealed to have Slash, but it was not seen executing the move.

All iterations of this attack involve cutting or slashing motions. With a total of seven Pokémon, Slash is the move learned by more Pokémon upon evolving than any other. Prior to Generation V, Shedinja was capable of learning Slash via leveling up if its pre-evolution, Nincada, was allowed to evolve only at level 31, which is the level at which Ninjask learns this move.

Title: Substitute (move)

For details regarding the physical doll created by this technique, consult the entry for Substitute (doll). Known in Japanese as みがわり (Substitute), this Normal-type maneuver, which causes no direct damage, made its debut in Generation I. It was designated as TM90 from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the TM08 slot. During Generation II, it functioned as Mr. Mime's signature move.
