To perform Substitute, the user sacrifices 25% of its maximum HP (rounded down) to manifest a decoy. The health of this created substitute matches the HP amount lost to form it, with the unique exception of Generation I where it possesses 1 additional HP above the sacrificed amount. Should a Pokémon already have a substitute active, the move will fail. The substitute doll then absorbs any incoming damage that the user would normally sustain until its HP is depleted. While a Pokémon is protected by a substitute, it becomes immune to numerous status-inducing moves from opposing Pokémon, and many secondary effects of damaging moves also fail to impact it. If Snatch is employed as Substitute is used, it will appropriate the move's intended effect.

In the handheld Generation I titles, if a user's HP is precisely 25% of its maximum, executing Substitute will cause it to faint immediately after creation. Conversely, in Pokémon Stadium, the move fails under the exact same 25% HP condition. Substitute cannot be successfully performed if the user has 25% or less of its maximum HP remaining, as this would result in the Pokémon fainting. Consequently, Shedinja, possessing only 1 HP, is invariably unable to use Substitute.

When augmented by a Normalium Z into Z-Substitute, all of the user's decreased stats are reset. In the upcoming Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Substitute's HP cost is reduced to 1/8 of the user's maximum HP. Upon activation, it produces a substitute doll positioned before the user, acting as a barrier. This substitute can be targeted and destroyed by damaging moves, even those from the user itself or other party Pokémon. Only one substitute can be active per party at a given time; if Substitute is used while another party Pokémon's substitute is present, the existing doll will be removed. As a Plus Move, Substitute generates a larger doll that can withstand greater damage before breaking.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series (specifically Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad), Substitute targets an adjacent Pokémon, friend or foe, applying the Decoy status condition. A Pokémon afflicted with Decoy will be preferentially attacked by both allies and adversaries. This status persists for 21 to 50 turns and is susceptible to Snatch. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Substitute functions differently: it inflicts the Substitute status by halving the user's HP. Under this status, the user is immune to enemy targeting but cannot employ moves or items, nor can it naturally recover HP. However, the user is not shielded from room-wide attacks like Earthquake. This effect lasts for 11 turns.

Substitute is featured as Greninja's down special attack in Super Smash Bros., summoning a doll reminiscent of those from the main series, or sometimes a log. This move operates as a counter, striking opponents who attack Greninja during its posing animation. A trophy depicting the substitute doll exists in Super Smash Bros. for Wii U. The text playfully remarks: "If it looks like a Pokémon and it gets attacked like a Pokémon, it's probably a- Oh, no, it could also be a Substitute Doll. This cute plush figure appears out of nowhere when a Pokémon uses the move Substitute. Is that a smile on its face or a grimace of determination? Only the doll knows, and it's not telling."

In Pokkén Tournament, Substitute is called upon by Whimsicott as a Support Pokémon. It materializes three substitute dolls that orbit the Battle Pokémon, intercepting incoming projectiles, and also slightly restores the Battle Pokémon's HP. Furthermore, Substitute Dolls appear independently within the game's Practice Area mode.

Shedinja can learn Substitute despite being unable to execute it in the core series due to its insufficient HP to meet the cost. Nevertheless, in the Mystery Dungeon series, Shedinja can utilize Substitute, as its HP can exceed 1 in those titles. Notably, the animation for Substitute plays even when battle animations are disabled. In Generation I games, a specific glitch occurred: if a player used Substitute, then checked a party Pokémon's stats and exited the menu, the opponent's Pokémon sprite would transform into a glitched version of the Pokémon just viewed. In Pokémon Crystal, if an opponent's Pokémon with a substitute uses Baton Pass, the incoming Pokémon will display a glitchy animation.

Title: Struggle (move) Struggle (Japanese: わるあがき Vain Struggle)
A damaging attack originating in Generation I, Struggle (わるあがき, known as Vain Struggle in Japanese) is a unique move accessible to all Pokémon when their other moves become inaccessible, despite not being a naturally learned technique. This attack deals damage to its target, with the user simultaneously suffering recoil. Struggle can land critical hits. When used in Double Battles, its target is chosen randomly from the opposing side. It consumes no PP for its execution. Although a maximum PP value exists in the game's internal data for this move, it remains hidden from players.

Unlike standard moves, Struggle cannot be taught; instead, a Pokémon automatically unleashes it if commanded to attack while none of its regular moves are available for use. A move's unavailability can stem from various causes, including being suppressed by specific moves like Disable, Gravity, Heal Block, Imprison, Taunt, Throat Chop, or Torment, by the Cursed Body Ability, or by restrictive held items such as a choice item or Assault Vest. Further restrictions might prevent a move's selection under certain conditions (e.g., Belch or Blood Moon), or if the move is disallowed in the active game mode, such as in a Sky Battle. Should a Pokémon be locked into a move by Encore, but that move exhausts its PP, the Encore effect concludes. However, if the Encorded move becomes unusable for any other reason, Struggle will be employed instead.

Struggle is capable of bypassing Wonder Guard. Despite causing recoil, it receives no power amplification from the Reckless Ability. Furthermore, its user incurs recoil damage regardless of whether their Ability is Magic Guard or Rock Head. This move resists being invoked by Assist, Me First, Metronome, or Sleep Talk; it cannot be duplicated via Mimic, Sketch, Transform, or Imposter; nor can it be perpetually re-used by Encore, Copycat, or a choice item, or compelled through Instruct. It is immune to suppression by Disable, Imprison, or Cursed Body, and cannot be successively blocked by Torment.

A Normal Gem held by the user has no effect on Struggle, nor is it consumed. Even when the user's other moves have their types altered (e.g., by Electrify, Ion Deluge, Normalize, or Pixilate), Struggle invariably inflicts typeless damage and does not interact with or deplete any type of Gem. Under normal circumstances, Struggle's PP remains static; it cannot be gained or lost through effects like Grudge or Spite, or by consumables such as Ether or a Leppa Berry.

Outside of Rotation Battles, if a Pokémon must resort to Struggle, choosing the 'Fight' option on the menu automatically commits the Pokémon to this move without requiring additional commands. This prevents the Pokémon from performing Mega Evolution, Ultra Burst, a Z-Move, Dynamaxing, or Terastallizing during that same turn. An already Dynamaxed Pokémon compelled to use Struggle will not convert it into a Max Move. Conversely, in a Rotation Battle, if a Pokémon finds itself forced to use Struggle, selecting 'Fight' presents the move list with Struggle displayed as a unique overlay option. Players retain the ability to rotate to one of their other two Pokémon instead of picking Struggle. Intriguingly, if the Pokémon is capable of Mega Evolution, the corresponding button remains selectable alongside Struggle in Rotation Battles, a feature absent in all other battle formats.

Should a Pokémon using Struggle attack first and faint itself from recoil damage, the opposing Pokémon will neither make its attack nor take ongoing damage during that turn. When Struggle shatters a substitute, the user avoids recoil damage. Furthermore, recoil damage suffered from Struggle on a previous turn can be mitigated if the opponent failed to make a move on the subsequent turn.

Within this particular generation, a persistent inability to use Struggle occurs if any of a Pokémon's unoccupied move slots possess more than 0 PP in the game's internal data. For example, if a transformed Ditto employs Metronome or Mirror Move at least once, and the used move is not in the first slot, a glitch will increment the PP of an unused slot on Ditto. Consequently, in all subsequent encounters, even if Ditto is not transformed, and Transform has 0 PP or is disabled, it remains incapable of using Struggle. This issue cannot be resolved by healing Ditto; however, it can be rectified by using Metronome or Mirror Move a cumulative 256 times (potentially across multiple battles) in the same move slot while Ditto is transformed, which causes the PP count to overflow to 0.

In the Stadium game, Struggle is effective against Ghost Pokémon, and its user receives no recoil damage if it successfully faints an opponent. However, recoil damage is incurred even if Struggle merely breaks a substitute. Recoil damage calculation involves rounding down, but never to less than 1.

In later iterations, Struggle consistently hits, circumventing accuracy checks, unless the adversary is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. It will also trigger a target's held Chilan Berry. Despite its consistent base power of 50 across all generations, Struggle's damage output is not augmented by the Technician ability in Generation IV. Its recoil calculation became subject to standard rounding conventions; for instance, a Pokémon with 201 maximum HP unleashing Struggle would endure 50 recoil (rounded down), whereas 202 or 203 HP would result in 51 recoil damage (rounded up).

Should Struggle occupy a standard moveslot (achievable only through illicit means), a Pokémon can freely choose it once, even if a Choice item is meant to restrict it to another move. Subsequent to this use, if all other moves become unavailable, the Pokémon will try to use Struggle again on subsequent turns, resulting in failure and the message, "But there was no PP left for the move!" As an unlearnable move, Struggle activates automatically whenever a Pokémon attempts to use any of its moves but finds all of them depleted of PP (or if all viable moves are left unselected in Rescue Team DX).

In the Mystery Dungeon series, the user suffers recoil damage equivalent to ¼ of its maximum HP (or 20% in Gates to Infinity), provided Struggle successfully inflicts damage. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers titles, it functions as a Normal-type attack, rather than typeless, mirroring its behavior in the Generation I core series. From Gates to Infinity forward, this attack boasts perfect accuracy. When executed by a Gigantic Pokémon in Gates to Infinity, it can strike adjacent foes within a 1-tile radius of the user, or enemies in a linear path when used by Lugia and Ho-Oh. This version causes 5% recoil damage but is incapable of causing the user to faint. It is assigned 5 power and 20 PP, yet its PP never depletes. Unlike the core series, Struggle in these games does not activate through external triggers like Encore. Also from Gates to Infinity onward, with the exception of the aforementioned scenarios, Struggle is exclusive to team members (or the leader Pokémon in Rescue Team DX); other Pokémon will instead resort to a standard physical attack. Team members will opt for Struggle even if their other moves are unselected.

Holding the final position in Generation I's index number order, Struggle, despite being accessible only through complete PP exhaustion, was assigned stats for Contest, Super Contest, and Contest Spectacular events. In Generation I, it stood as the sole move, aside from Metronome itself, that Metronome could not call. Among all moves causing recoil, Struggle possesses the lowest base power. Its typeless damage makes it the only Normal-type damaging move capable of striking a Ghost-type opponent under typical conditions, except for Generation I where it inflicted Normal-type damage. Alongside Barrage and Kinesis, Struggle is one of three Generation I moves never depicted in the animated series; notably, it was the only one of these present in Pokémon Sword and Shield before version 1.2.0.

Remarkably, some glitch Pokémon, such as Generation IV hybrids, 'Ng'Mp, 'ゥ., and 4, ゥァ (DD), can acquire Struggle through leveling up. If Struggle is illicitly coded onto a Pokémon, its display in Pokémon Stadium 2's lab will be erroneous (unlike in the original Stadium), appearing highlighted as though it were a Generation II move. This visual anomaly does not persist when assembling a battle team. Within Pokémon Stadium, the move's description contains a factual error, claiming the user takes recoil equal to ¼ of the damage dealt, whereas the accurate Generation I value is ½.

A Generation I glitch exists where a Pokémon becomes entirely unable to use Struggle if it previously utilized Transform and subsequently either Metronome or Mirror Move. This renders the Pokémon non-viable for battle until its PP is fully replenished. The Raid Format within the Pokémon Trading Card Game incorporates an optional rule referred to as the 'Struggle'.

Title: Sketch (move)

Known in Japanese as スケッチ Sketch, this non-damaging Normal-type technique was introduced in Generation II. It stands as the signature move of Smeargle. Sketch allows its user to permanently acquire the target's most recent action, replacing Sketch in the process. The move bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring it always hits, unless the opposing Pokémon is within the semi-invulnerable turn of a maneuver such as Dig or Fly. Sketch remains unaffected by defensive abilities like Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield. However, it will fail if used against a target protected by Crafty Shield.

Despite being capable of being sketched, Aura Wheel, Hyperspace Fury, and Dark Void (from Generation VII onwards) cannot be utilized in combat. These specific moves are exclusively usable by Morpeko, Hoopa Unbound, or Darkrai, respectively, or by a Pokémon that has undergone transformation into them. Certain moves are immune to Sketching: Chatter, Struggle, Sketch itself, all Shadow moves, Z-Moves, and a selection of others depending on the generation. Sketch is unable to duplicate Transform or Mimic. When targeting Metronome, Mirror Move, or Sleep Talk, it replicates the move those abilities triggered. Because the move fails if there is no opponent on the field (or the turn concludes immediately in certain generations, such as Generation IV), Selfdestruct and Explosion are also unsketchable. The move may attempt to copy an ability the user already possesses.

In combat scenarios where experience points are not awarded (e.g., link battles or battle facility encounters), if a transformed Pokémon utilizes Sketch, it will permanently integrate the copied move into its repertoire; this phenomenon is identified as the Sketch glitch. Sketch's behavior has since changed: it now directly copies Mirror Move, Sleep Talk, and Metronome, rather than the moves they invoke. Selfdestruct and Explosion can now be acquired if their initial attempt was thwarted by a Pokémon with the Damp Ability present on the battlefield. Exclusively within Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, Sketch gained the capability to copy an action a Pokémon tried to perform but was prevented from doing so by sleep or other ailments. This specific condition permits the copying of Mimic and Transform, moves otherwise uncopyable. In engagements where experience points are unobtainable (for example, link battles or Battle Tower challenges), any alterations to a Pokémon's moveset by Sketch will be reset once the battle concludes. There are documented instances where a Pokémon employs Sketch despite it not being present in its active moveset.

Presently, Mimic and Transform can be copied by Sketch just like any standard move, without requiring their initial failure. When empowered by a Normalium Z to become Z-Sketch, it grants the user a one-stage boost to its Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats. Between versions 1.0 and 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Sketch was unselectable during combat. However, with the release of Version 3.0.0, Sketch's usability has been restored. Dark Void and Hyperspace Fury are now impervious to Sketching, as are the recently introduced Revival Blessing, Tera Starstorm, or the Starmobiles' unique techniques. Even though it is both unobtainable and unusable, Breakneck Blitz is nonetheless cited as a move Sketch cannot copy.

Sketch focuses on the immediate opposing Pokémon, duplicating their most recently executed move and replacing itself with it. The Ginseng boost is not replicated by Sketch. Failure occurs if the target has forgotten its last move, or if that move happens to be Sketch or Struggle.

Within the Rescue Team series, Sketch is unable to replicate moves that summon other moves (with the exceptions of Sleep Talk, Snatch, and Mirror Move); curiously, it can even copy itself if reflected via Mirror Move. Beginning with Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, the move gained the ability to sketch moves that call others. Usage of Sketch is prohibited in Pokémon Towers within the Adventure Squad series. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, attempting to use Sketch on Gigantic Pokémon will invariably result in failure, due to their distinct versions of identical moves. For Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, Sketch among them, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Opposing Smeargle were observed employing Sketch.

In terms of index number order, Sketch holds the distinction of being the inaugural Generation II move. Smeargle acquires Sketch ten times through leveling up, establishing Sketch as the move most frequently learned via this method by a single Pokémon. Furthermore, it is the sole move obtainable more than four times through gaining levels. Excluding Struggle and Shadow moves, Sketch is one of just two moves with a base PP value that is not a multiple of 5, the other being Revival Blessing.

Title: Triple Kick (move)

Triple Kick (Japanese: トリプルキック) is a Fighting-type, damage-dealing multistrike move that debuted in Generation II. Before Generation VII and once again in Generation IX, it was specifically associated with Hitmontop as its signature move.

This attack hits the target up to three times per use. In its earlier main series incarnations, Triple Kick had a base power of 10, with the damage from the second hit being multiplied by 2 and the third hit by 3. Its accuracy was 90%; if the attack did not miss, there was always an equal 33.33% chance for it to land 1, 2, or 3 times.

However, in modern main series games, Triple Kick's mechanics have been updated. Instead of damage multiplication, the base power is incremented by 10 with each successful strike: the first hit is 10, the second 20, and the third 30. Each individual strike now performs a separate 90% accuracy check, and if any of these checks fail, the move immediately ceases. Consequently, there's a 72.9% chance for all three strikes to land (accumulating 60 base power), an 8.1% chance for two strikes (cumulative 30 base power), and a 9% chance for just one strike (10 base power). Factoring in these probabilities, Triple Kick possesses an effective power of 47.07.

Interactions with other game mechanics are as follows: Bide and Counter will only take into account the final strike delivered. For items, a King's Rock or Razor Fang grants each consecutive hit an independent chance to cause the opponent to flinch. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike connects, the very next strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, can repeatedly prevent fainting, with each activation being independent. For Abilities, if the target has a contact-activating Ability, each strike separately triggers it, potentially multiple times. This includes Abilities like Weak Armor or Stamina, which activate per hit. If the user holds a Rocky Helmet, it takes damage for every strike landed. The Abilities Skill Link and the item Loaded Dice modify Triple Kick to have a single accuracy check, ensuring all three hits connect.

In Pokémon Contests, Triple Kick serves as a valid second move in a combination, awarding 4 bonus appeal points if Focus Energy was used on the preceding turn.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, its mechanics vary. In Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad, Triple Kick had a reduced base critical hit rate of 2%. During this era, it would hit three times with separate accuracy checks, and unlike modern main series, a failed hit would not end the move; its power would also escalate with each hit (2× damage for the second, 3× for the third). However, starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's power no longer increases, and if a Reviver Seed activates, the attack will terminate.

Triple Kick shares its description text with Triple Axel. It stands as one of only three multistrike moves, alongside Triple Axel and Population Bomb, where the chance for successive hits to miss is independent of the first strike. Furthermore, Triple Kick is tied with Constrict for the lowest initial base power (10) among all damage-dealing moves, though this only applies to its initial hit.

Title: Thief (move)

For the character in Pokémon: Yeah! I Got Pokémon!, consult Thief (GDZ47). This entry covers the move Thief. Regarding the 'Thief' status condition within Mystery Dungeon titles, refer to Kecleon Shop→Theft. Known in Japanese as どろぼう (Thief), Thief is a Dark-type damaging technique first introduced in Generation II. It was available as TM46 from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Thief deals damage and has a 99.6% chance to appropriate the target's held item, assuming the target possesses one and the user does not. Items cannot be taken if the attack strikes a Pokémon's substitute. Held Mail is immune to theft. Wild Pokémon are capable of seizing held items from the player's Pokémon. However, since Thief was exclusively a TM in this generation, this could only legitimately occur with a wild Ditto using Transform on a player's Pokémon that knows Thief, or a wild Smeargle that acquired Thief via Sketch. In Link Battles, the Battle Tower, and the Trainer House, any stolen items are restored at the end of the battle.

Thief inflicts damage and takes the target's held item (if present). An item cannot be stolen from a Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability. Held e-Reader Berries and Mail cannot be stolen. Even if the user faints while employing Thief (for example, due to the target's Rough Skin), the held item is still successfully acquired. If Focus Band's effect would trigger from Thief's damage, it will activate prior to being stolen. Wild Pokémon are unable to steal items held by the player's Pokémon. In Link Battles, battle facilities (except the Battle Pyramid), and Secret Bases, stolen items are returned post-battle (or at the conclusion of the challenge for the Battle Pike). Within Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD, if an opponent uses Thief, items are not permanently removed.

Pokémon possessing the Multitype Ability are immune to item theft. A Griseous Orb, when held, also cannot be stolen. If the target's consumable held item (such as a Colbur Berry or Focus Sash) would activate due to Thief, it will be consumed before any theft attempt. Should the user faint while executing Thief (e.g., from Rough Skin or Rocky Helmet), it will fail to steal the target's held item. If Thief causes a Pokémon with Sticky Hold to faint, its held item can now be stolen. The move no longer results in permanent item loss from a Trainer's Pokémon; however, players can still permanently acquire items from wild Pokémon.

Thief is now capable of stealing a held Griseous Orb, provided neither the attacker nor the defender is Giratina. Plates are immune to theft if either the user or target is Arceus (Multitype does not influence Thief's behavior here), and Drives cannot be stolen if Genesect is either the user or the target. Thief's base power was adjusted from 40 to 60, and its PP increased from 10 to 25. Furthermore, Thief cannot seize a Mega Stone or Orb if either the user or the target is capable of Mega Evolving or undergoing Primal Reversion with it. Similarly, Z-Crystals are safe from any Pokémon, memories cannot be taken if Silvally is involved, the Rusted Sword is immune if Zacian is present, the Rusted Shield if Zamazenta is present, or a Booster Energy if a Paradox Pokémon (excluding Koraidon and Miraidon) is either the user or target. When employed in a Max Raid Battle, Thief completely fails to execute, dealing no damage, regardless of whether the target is the raid boss or an allied Pokémon. Items successfully taken from wild Pokémon are now placed directly into the player's Bag. A Wellspring Mask, Hearthflame Mask, or Cornerstone Mask cannot be stolen if Ogerpon is either the user or the target. This attack causes damage and takes the opponent's held item, provided the user is not currently holding one. Should the target faint, its item will simply fall to the ground, as is standard.

In the exclusive edition of Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Thief functions as Meowth's Power Card. A player utilizing Meowth can immediately steal a property from an opponent who has just purchased one. This effect, like all other Power Cards, is restricted to a single use per game. Within Pokémon Black and White, Minccino possesses the ability to learn Thief through TM, unlike its evolution, Cinccino. This represents an error that was rectified in Black 2 and White 2.

Title: Spider Web (move)

A non-damaging Bug-type move, Spider Web (クモのす in Japanese) made its debut in Generation II. Before Generation V, it was recognized as the signature attack of Spinarak and Ariados. This move prevents the target Pokémon from switching out of battle or fleeing, which includes evasion via Teleport. It consistently lands, bypassing all accuracy checks, unless the foe is in a semi-invulnerable state from a move like Dig or Fly. The trapping effect of Spider Web remains active only as long as the Pokémon that utilized it stays in the fight.

Initially, it would hit targets even through Protect or Detect. However, it is now blocked by Protect, Detect, and Crafty Shield. An affected Pokémon can still switch out using Baton Pass, in which case the Pokémon that replaces it will also become trapped. Conversely, if the Pokémon that used Spider Web switches out with Baton Pass, the effects of Spider Web will still apply. However, if a Pokémon traps a target with Spider Web and then switches with Baton Pass, the target will no longer be trapped. Additionally, if the ensnared Pokémon is hit by Roar or Whirlwind, it will still be forced out of battle.

When a wild Pokémon uses Spider Web on a player's Pokémon, the player's escape is blocked. Nevertheless, Pokémon caught by Spider Web can still flee (though not switch out) if they possess the Run Away Ability or are holding a Smoke Ball. Pokémon affected by Spider Web can now switch out with U-turn. Furthermore, Volt Switch also enables trapped Pokémon to switch out. Dragon Tail and Circle Throw will also forcibly remove the affected Pokémon from the field. Parting Shot offers another method for Pokémon ensnared by Spider Web to switch out.

In Pokémon Contests, Spider Web can be used as the second move in a combination, yielding 2 bonus appeal points if String Shot was used during the previous turn. For Contest Spectaculars, using Spider Web in combination with String Shot from the prior turn provides an extra three appeal points. It can also initiate a combination in Contest Spectaculars, granting three additional appeal points to certain moves (Explosion, Memento, Perish Song, and Self-Destruct) if they are performed in the subsequent turn.

When empowered by a Buginium Z to become Z-Spider Web, the user's Defense stat is boosted by one stage. Spider Web targets the enemy directly in front and inflicts the 'stuck' status condition, which was formerly known as Leg Hold and Shadow Hold. This condition prevents the Pokémon from moving (though it may still attack) and from swapping places with its team leader. Starting from Super Mystery Dungeon, Ghost-type Pokémon are immune to becoming stuck. Magic Coat can affect this move. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Spider Web, were exclusively used by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Ariados was notably seen using Spider Web in this game.

During Generation III, the status screen mistakenly displayed this move as having 100% accuracy, despite its mechanic of ignoring accuracy and evasion calculations. Since Generation VIII, Spider Web is the sole variant of Mean Look that cannot be utilized in a battle.

Title: Mind Reader (move)

The Normal-type technique known as Mind Reader (Japanese: こころのめ, translated as Mind's Eye) first appeared in Generation II and deals no damage. Once initiated by the user, and persisting until its duration concludes (as detailed below), every accuracy check performed by that user against the designated opponent will invariably connect. This ensures offensive actions will bypass misses, even when facing a foe within the protective phase of moves like Dig or Fly. While this guarantees hits, attacks are still susceptible to failure from other causes, such as the target being Protected or Fake Out being improperly executed beyond the initial turn, or they may be drawn away by specific moves and Abilities. Notably, the turn when Future Sight and Doom Desire inflict damage is considered an accuracy check. However, Mind Reader itself will not activate if the adversary is shielded by a substitute.

Specifically in Generation II, this technique allowed attacks to disregard accuracy checks and connect unfailingly, with the sole exception of foes within the semi-invulnerable phase of maneuvers like Dig or Fly. Uniquely for this generation, the effect was applied as a status directly to the target, meaning *any* subsequent attacker, not just the original user, would benefit from guaranteed hits against that target. Furthermore, during move execution, Mind Reader's effect was evaluated *after* several conditions: if Dream Eater was aimed at a waking foe; if Protect or Detect were active; or if Dream Eater, Absorb, Mega Drain, Leech Life, or Giga Drain were directed at a Pokémon protected by a Substitute. Consequently, if an attack failed due to any of these specific conditions, Mind Reader's influence would nonetheless remain active. However, this generation presented an exception: Mind Reader would *not* enable Earthquake, Fissure, or Magnitude to strike a Pokémon in the airborne phase of Fly; such moves would still miss, and Mind Reader's effect would terminate. (It's important to note that type immunities were assessed prior to Mind Reader's effect, primarily impacting Pokémon like Mew or non-Flying types that gained Fly through means such as Mimic or Sketch.)

Conventionally, a single target can only be affected by one instance of Mind Reader or Lock-On concurrently. Should a different Pokémon employ either of these moves on the identical target, the initial effect will be supplanted, associating the aim-assisting benefit with the new Pokémon. In Pokémon Contests, Mind Reader serves as an initiating move in a combination, leading to DynamicPunch, Hi Jump Kick, Jump Kick, Mega Kick, Mega Punch, and Submission earning twice their usual appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn.

Mirroring the behavior observed in Generation III, only one Pokémon's Mind Reader or Lock-On effect can be active on a particular target at any given moment. However, exclusively within this generation (implying Generation IV), a specific glitch manifested: if a move gained the benefits of Mind Reader while its target was protected, the protective effect would be disregarded, yet the attack would proceed with inverted accuracy. This particular anomaly did not extend to one-hit knockout moves. For Contest Spectaculars, Mind Reader can form part of a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to a one-hit knockout move when executed on the subsequent turn.

Beginning with Generation V, the restriction loosened, permitting multiple Pokémon to apply the effects of Mind Reader or Lock-On simultaneously to the same adversary. Consequently, Mind Reader will no longer fail if another Pokémon has already applied such an effect to the target; its failure condition is now limited to instances where the user itself has already established the effect on its current target. When enhanced by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Mind Reader, the user's Special Attack stat experiences a one-stage increase. The utilization of Mind Reader confers the Sure Shot status condition upon its user, thereby causing all of their subsequent moves to bypass both accuracy and evasion calculations. This beneficial status persists for durations of 11-12 / 3 turns.

Title: Nightmare (move)

For information concerning the Mystery Dungeon level, see The Nightmare. To learn about the Ability, consult Bad Dreams. Nightmare (Japanese: あくむ Nightmare), a non-damaging Ghost-type move, made its debut in Generation II. During Generation II, it held the TM designation TM50.

If a target is already asleep, Nightmare afflicts it with a status effect that causes it to suffer damage equivalent to one-quarter of its maximum HP at the conclusion of each turn. This damage is applied after the afflicted Pokémon or its Trainer has completed their action, unless the opponent faints (in which case Nightmare will not deal damage that turn). Should the Pokémon awaken, the Nightmare effect ceases and will not reactivate even if the Pokémon subsequently falls asleep again.

Nightmare typically bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring a hit unless the target is within the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. In Generation II exclusively, an oversight meant that if an opposing Trainer used a Full Heal or Full Restore to cure their Pokémon of sleep, the Nightmare status would persist despite the Pokémon waking up; notably, this specific issue did not occur if the player cured their own Pokémon's sleep. Regardless, a Pokémon under the influence of Nightmare incurs damage at the end of every turn, even if its adversary has fainted. In Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire only, another oversight caused Pokémon that self-cured sleep via Shed Skin to remain afflicted by Nightmare, even after waking up.

Nightmare can function as the second component of a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Hypnosis was performed in the preceding turn. It also contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding an extra three appeal points when a sleep-inducing status move (excluding Psycho Shift and Rest) was utilized in the prior turn.

When empowered by a Ghostium Z, Nightmare transforms into Z-Nightmare, which subsequently increases the user's Special Attack stat by one stage. In the Mystery Dungeon series, Nightmare inflicts a unique status condition that causes the target to fall asleep and deals 8 / 10 HP damage upon awakening. This status persists for 4-7 / 10 turns. The move can be reflected by Magic Coat in the Explorers series. In Generation III, the status screen erroneously displays the move as having 100% accuracy, despite it inherently ignoring accuracy and evasion checks.

Title: Flame Wheel (move)

Originating in Generation II, Flame Wheel (Japanese: かえんぐるま Flame Wheel) is a damaging Fire-type attack. When executed, this move delivers damage to the target while simultaneously thawing out the user if they are currently frozen. Moreover, it carries a 10% chance of inflicting a burn on the adversary. In Pokémon Contests, Flame Wheel serves as an effective second move in a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Sunny Day was performed in the preceding turn. Essentially, this technique inflicts damage and possesses the potential to leave the target burned. The in-game description for Flame Wheel, prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), stated: "Unfreezes the user. Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned." Interestingly, Platinum's Ponyta was revealed on the official website to know Flame Wheel, but the Pokémon was never depicted using the move. It is also notable that before Generation IX, the Korean name for this move was an error in translation, as the Japanese term くるま encompasses both "wheel" and "car."

Title: Snore (move)

Debuting in Generation II, Snore (Japanese: いびき) is a Normal-type attack designed to inflict damage. It functioned as TM13 during Generation II and later as TM24 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Snore's successful execution is strictly conditional on the user being asleep; otherwise, the attempt will fail. This move deals damage and carries a 30% likelihood of causing the target to flinch. Pokémon endowed with the Comatose Ability are regarded as asleep for practical purposes, permitting them to successfully deploy Snore (or Sleep Talk) even without truly having the sleep status condition. If aimed at a Pokémon shielded by a substitute, Snore will strike the substitute directly. Should Sleep Talk or Snore be used consecutively at least once before a Pokémon switches out, that Pokémon will re-enter battle sleeping for the exact number of turns that remained prior to using those moves. In Pokémon Contests, Snore functions as the second move in a combination, granting 4 bonus appeal points if Rest was employed in the preceding turn. Turns where Snore is activated invariably contribute towards the Pokémon's awakening. In current mechanics, Snore is capable of hitting Pokémon even when they are protected by a substitute. For Contest Spectacular combinations, Snore yields an additional three appeal points if Rest was utilized in the turn immediately before. As a general effect, Snore inflicts harm and may cause the opponent to flinch. Manual attempts to use Snore will result in no action, as the move only functions when the user is asleep. This move is not linkable. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series, Snore automatically triggers if a sleeping Pokémon is targeted by an attack. Despite its specified range of a single tile, it will nonetheless activate even if the attacker is positioned beyond this range. Snore remains unaffected by cowering and flinching (or cringing) status conditions, but it is impacted by the Muzzled status. Being a sound-based move, Soundproof completely blocks its effect. Snore consistently takes precedence over Sleep Talk when both are an option. In the Super Mystery Dungeon title, Snore's automatic activation occurs when the user is asleep and an adjacent enemy is present. If both Sleep Talk and Snore are part of a Pokémon's moveset, the one listed first is selected. Pokémon possessing the Abilities Insomnia or Vital Spirit, previously able to acquire Snore via Move Tutor in Pokémon Emerald, found themselves unable to do so from Pokémon Platinum until Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire.

Title: Curse (move)
Known in Japanese as のろい (Curse/Slow), Curse is a non-damaging Ghost-type technique that was first introduced in Generation II. Before Generation V, this move was categorized as a ???-type, holding the distinction of being the sole move of that particular typing.

Across all game generations, Curse's effect varies based on whether the Pokémon employing it possesses the Ghost typing. The move's behavior is dictated solely by the Pokémon's current type, disregarding its original typing. Consequently, if the user's type undergoes alteration—for instance, acquiring the Ghost type via Trick-or-Treat, shedding it to become a pure Water-type through Soak, shifting species with Transform, or Terastallizing—Curse will evaluate the Pokémon's current type rather than any former one.

If the user is a Ghost-type, it will sacrifice half of its maximum HP (rounded down) to inflict a curse upon the target. Should this HP cost reduce the user's health to zero, the move will fully resolve before the user faints. A Pokémon afflicted by this curse will endure damage equivalent to ¼ of its maximum HP at the conclusion of every turn. This cursed status persists until the affected Pokémon is switched out or the battle concludes. An exception occurs if Baton Pass is used; in this scenario, the curse transfers to the incoming Pokémon.

When executed by a non-Ghost-type, the user's Speed stat is lowered by one stage, and both its Attack stat and Defense stat are boosted by one stage. Even though this effect targets the user, the move will still fail if no opponents are present in battle. A non-Ghost-type using Curse will fail if both its Attack and Defense stats have already reached +6 stages, irrespective of its Speed stat not being at -6 stages. Subsequently, a non-Ghost-type Pokémon can have its Speed stat lowered by Curse even if its Attack and Defense stats are already at +6 stages. Nevertheless, the move will still fail if its Attack and Defense stats are at +6 stages and its Speed stat is at -6 stages.

Curse cannot be impeded by Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, but Crafty Shield will successfully block it. The user avoids losing HP if Curse misses (for example, against a Pokémon in a semi-invulnerable state), fails (such as when the target is already cursed), or is successfully blocked by Crafty Shield.

When converted into Z-Curse by a Ghostium Z, a Ghost-type user regains all of its HP. For a non-Ghost-type user, Z-Curse provides an additional increase of one stage to its Attack stat, beyond the standard stat changes.

When a cursed Pokémon defeats its opponent, it will not take curse damage during that turn. Subsequently, if a cursed Pokémon defeats its opponent, it *does* now take curse damage on that turn.

Should the user's type change prior to using Curse, the move will observe the Pokémon's type at the exact moment of its execution. In a Single Battle, if a non-Ghost-type user transforms into a Ghost-type before executing Curse (e.g., via Mega Evolution, Color Change, or Trick-or-Treat), the move will behave as it would for any Ghost-type, damaging the user and applying the curse.

During a Double Battle, if the user acquires the Ghost type before Curse executes, the move will prioritize targeting the opponent positioned directly opposite the user. Specifically in Double Battles, if a non-Ghost-type user turns Ghost-type before Curse executes (owing to Mega Evolution, Color Change, or Trick-or-Treat), Curse's target may vary based on the user's position. From the Trainer's viewpoint, if the user occupies the right-side slot, Curse will direct itself at the user's ally (or fail if no ally is present). If the user is positioned on the left side (from its Trainer's perspective), Curse will target a random opposing Pokémon. Later, in Double Battles, if a user not initially Ghost-type transforms into one before executing Curse (via Mega Evolution, Color Change, or Trick-or-Treat), Curse will now always target a random opponent. Ghost-type Pokémon can no longer select specific targets for Curse in Double Battles; instead, they will consistently endeavor to inflict the curse on a randomly chosen opponent.

In a Triple Battle, if the user gains the Ghost type before Curse executes, Curse will prioritize the opponent directly opposite the user as its target. When in a Triple Battle or Horde Encounter, if a user that isn't initially Ghost-type acquires the Ghost type before Curse execution (through Mega Evolution, Color Change, or Trick-or-Treat), Curse will subsequently target a random adjacent opponent.

An identified glitch can cause Curse to exhibit atypical behavior under specific circumstances when the user's type is altered immediately before deploying the move. Follow Me and Rage Powder retain their normal ability to redirect Curse. Should a non-Ghost-type user transform into a Ghost-type via Protean upon executing Curse, it will apply the Ghost-type effect of Curse to itself, ignoring the influence of Follow Me or Rage Powder. This specific scenario cannot be blocked by Crafty Shield.

Dynamax Pokémon are susceptible to Curse; nonetheless, they incur damage equivalent to 1/4 of their maximum HP *before* Dynamaxing. This translates to between 1/6 and 1/8 of their actual Dynamax HP, or as low as 1/12 for Max Raid Battle bosses.

Within Pokémon Contests, Curse serves as an initial move in a combination, enabling Destiny Bond, Grudge, Mean Look, and Spite to yield double their usual appeal when performed on the subsequent turn. Exclusively within this generation, an oversight meant that a Pokémon under Curse's effect, having previously used Rage, would be liberated from the curse upon selecting a different move, yet its Rage build-up would persist.

During Generation II, Curse was available via TM to every Pokémon that could learn Technical Machines.

In the context of *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon*, when a non-Ghost-type Pokémon employs Curse, its Attack and Defense stats increase by one stage, while its Travel Speed decreases by one stage. If a Ghost-type uses Curse, it imposes the Cursed status condition on the target by sacrificing half of the user's *current* HP. This particular status effect causes the afflicted Pokémon to lose 1/4 of its maximum HP at the end of the subsequent turn and then every 10 turns thereafter, or alternatively, at the end of *every* turn. It persists for a duration of 4-5 / 2 turns. Curse is categorized as a Ghost-type move starting with *Gates to Infinity*, having been classified as a Normal-type before this title. Exclusively in *Gates to Infinity*, the reduction in Travel Speed from Curse lasts for merely one turn. For non-Ghost-type Pokémon, Curse's effects mirror its behavior from Generation V. If a Ghost-type Pokémon employs Curse, it inflicts immediate damage and subsequently drains the opponent's health over time.

In *Pokémon Rumble Rush*, all status moves, including Curse, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired on any captured Pokémon. Boss Haunter was observed to use Curse in *Pokémon Rumble Rush*.

Within *Pokémon UNITE*, Curse is the initial move learned by Trevenant. Trevenant acquires Curse upon evolving at level 5, by upgrading Will-O-Wisp rather than Wood Hammer. The user establishes a cursed zone, sacrificing 4% of its maximum HP per second for 6 seconds. This zone inflicts continuous damage to enemies within its area of effect every .5 seconds for the 6-second duration, concurrently diminishing their movement speed. The reduction in movement speed intensifies as the user's HP decreases. During the execution of this move, the user accumulates 'curse power' whenever it deals or sustains damage. Unleashing this stored power causes extra damage to foes within the affected area. Should the curse power reach its maximum upon release, the cooldown timers for Branch Poke, Horn Leech, and Pain Split are reset. Upon reaching level 11, the move's effective radius expands.

Curse, along with its Z-Move variant Z-Curse, stands alone as moves whose effects are determined by the user's type. The divergent effects of Curse are a linguistic homage to its Japanese name, のろい (Noroi), which can signify "curse" (呪い) or "dull/slow" (鈍い), based on the specific kanji employed. This linguistic duality explains why Ghost-type Pokémon inflict a literal curse, while non-Ghost-type Pokémon experience a Speed reduction.

In Generation II, three Pokémon—Porygon, Porygon2, and Smeargle—were legitimately capable of utilizing both effects of Curse by employing Conversion or Conversion 2 to switch their type to and from Ghost. Any Pokémon capable of in-battle type alteration (for example, through Color Change, Reflect Type, or Trick-or-Treat) can, by technicality, access both of Curse's distinct effects. Likewise, if a Ghost-type Pokémon uses Curse while influenced by Soak or Magic Powder, it will execute the non-Ghost version of the move. Furthermore, in Generation IX, any Pokémon capable of using Curse can access both effects through Terastallization.

Curse is among four moves introduced in Generation II whose type underwent modification in a subsequent generation; the others include Charm, Moonlight, and Sweet Kiss. Among these four, Curse is unique as the only one that did not change in Generation VI, thereby making it the sole move among them that did not transition into a Fairy-type move. Considering all eight moves that have undergone a type change (including Gust, Sand Attack, Karate Chop, and Bite), Curse stands out as the sole move that was not initially a Normal-type.

Title: Flail (move)

The Normal-type, damage-dealing attack known as Flail (じたばた in Japanese) made its debut in Generation II. This move delivers damage without any secondary effects. The potency of Flail scales inversely with the user's remaining HP percentage; a lower health status results in greater power. From Generation II through V, Flail was incapable of landing critical hits, and its damage output lacked the typical random variance in calculations. However, starting with Generation VI, Flail became capable of scoring critical hits, and its damage now incorporates a random factor in its calculation. In Pokémon Contests, Flail functions as the second step in a combination, awarding 1 extra appeal point if Endure preceded it. Furthermore, within Contest Spectaculars, Flail forms a combination that grants an additional three appeal points when paired with Endure in the preceding turn. Fundamentally, Flail deals damage, which intensifies as the user's health diminishes.

Within the Rescue Team series, Flail's ultimate damage output undergoes multiplication: by 2x at 51-75% HP, by 4x at 26-50% HP, and by 8x when the user's HP falls to 25% or below. The Explorers series adjusts this by multiplying damage by 1.5x for HP between 26-50% and by 2x when HP is 25% or less. Super Mystery Dungeon employs a damage calculation where the power multiplier roughly correlates inversely with current HP, spanning from approximately 0.1x at 100% HP, 0.5x at 83%, 1.0x at 62%, 1.3x at 50%, 2.0x at 20%, to 2.5x when only 1 HP remains.

For Pokémon UNITE, Flail serves as Snorlax's initial designated move. Players acquire it at level 5 by evolving Tackle into Flail, as an alternative to Heavy Slam. Upon activation, the user gains a 4-second buff that amplifies the damage of their basic attacks. This damage boost becomes more potent for every 20% increment of missing HP. Furthermore, a brief surge of enhanced movement speed is bestowed upon the user whenever Flail is initiated. At level 11, the move's damage output further increases, and the user's basic attacks additionally restore health when their HP drops below 40% of maximum.

Within the unique Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Flail functions as the Power Card specifically assigned to Magikarp. A player controlling Magikarp can, once per game, bypass rent payment on a property and instead collect Pokémon Dollars equivalent to half the owed amount from the property's owner. In the chapter "Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming," Emerald's Pokédex identified that a Pinsir he had obtained from the Battle Factory possessed the move Flail.

Title: Conversion 2 (move)

Known in Japanese as テクスチャー２ (Texture 2) and formatted as Conversion2 before Pokémon Stadium 2, Conversion 2 is a non-damaging Normal-type move introduced in Generation II. Until Generation IX, it was considered a signature move of the Porygon evolutionary line. Its primary effect causes the user's type to randomly transform into any type that either resists or is immune to the type of the last damaging move it was struck by. For instance, if an Electric-type attack was the last move to hit the user, its type could become Ground, Electric, Grass, or Dragon. If the opponent's preceding action was a move that calls another move (such as Metronome), the type of the called move is the one considered. Likewise, if the opponent's previous move was one that undergoes a type change (like Hidden Power), its resulting type is taken into account. Even though Struggle inflicts typeless damage, it is classified as a Normal-type move for Conversion 2's purposes. Conversion 2 is unaffected by Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, or Baneful Bunker. It will no longer convert the user to a type it already possesses. The move fails if the user already has all possible types that resist the type of the last damaging move. If the last move that connected with the user was influenced by Normalize, it is treated as a Normal-type move.

Later, Conversion 2 was updated to target an adjacent Pokémon, bypassing accuracy checks to ensure a hit, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. In this updated form, it randomly changes the user's current type to one that either resists or is immune to the type of the last move used by the target, encompassing status moves. This iteration of Conversion 2 will not change the user to any of its current types. In an Inverse Battle, Conversion 2 determines resistances based on inverse type matchups. For example, if the target's last move was an Electric-type attack, the user may become Ground-type, Electric-type, Grass-type, or Dragon-type. If the target's most recent action was a move that invokes another (e.g., Metronome), the invoked move's type is considered. Should the target's previous move be type-altering (such as Hidden Power), its final type is the one factored in. Furthermore, if the type of the target's last move was modified, as with Electrify or Normalize, the altered type is the one accounted for. Conversion 2 will fail if the target's last move was Struggle, a typeless Revelation Dance, or if no type exists that resists that move (e.g., a Normal-type move in an Inverse Battle). Substitute does not affect Conversion 2, but Crafty Shield can block it.

When powered up by a Normalium Z into Z-Conversion 2, the user fully restores its HP. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 to 1.1.1, Conversion 2 was unselectable in battle. Starting with Version 1.2.0, it became selectable and functioned as it did in prior generations. Conversion 2 will fail if the last move the target used was Stellar-type.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Conversion 2 applies the "Conversion 2 status" to the user for 11 turns. If the user is struck by any move (even a non-damaging one), it will transform into a type possessing the most favorable matchup against that incoming move. This effect triggers mid-sequence for multi-hit moves. It takes into account the actual types of Hidden Power, Natural Gift, and Weather Ball (except in the Rescue Team series, where Weather Ball is treated as Normal-type). In Super Mystery Dungeon, Conversion 2 no longer bestows a separate status condition but directly alters the user's type to a random one advantageous against the target's last move.

Excluding Z-Moves, Conversion 2 is unique as the sole move to feature a number in its name. In Generation III, the status screen erroneously displayed the move as having 100% accuracy, despite it targeting the user and thus inherently ignoring accuracy and evasion checks.

Title: Aeroblast (move)

Known in Japanese as エアロブラスト (Aeroblast), this Flying-type offense, which inflicts damage, first appeared in Generation II. It serves as Lugia's distinctive signature technique. Beyond its damage output, Aeroblast boasts an elevated chance of landing a critical hit. In the specific context of Triple Battles, Aeroblast possesses the unique property of striking foes not positioned immediately next to the user. Furthermore, it functions within Contest Spectacular combinations; a user earns an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was employed during the preceding turn.

Across versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Aeroblast was unavailable for selection during combat. However, from Version 1.3.0 onward, it became fully accessible, operating identically to its mechanics in earlier generational releases. Similarly, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, from initial release 1.0 up to version 2.0.2, this move was unselectable in battle scenarios. As of Version 3.0.0, it became available for use, maintaining its established functionality from prior generations.

Aeroblast not only inflicts harm but also features an enhanced critical-hit probability, specifically 50% in the Rescue Team series and 30% in the Explorers series. Its execution in Gates to Infinity necessitates a preparatory turn for charging. In Pokémon GO, Aeroblast is classified as a Charged Attack, having been accessible to players since November 6, 2020. Two distinct variants of Aeroblast exist: Aeroblast+ and Aeroblast++. Aeroblast+ functions as the inherent Charged Attack for Apex Shadow Lugia. Subsequent to purification, Aeroblast+ undergoes a transformation, becoming Aeroblast++.

Notably, Aeroblast stands as the sole Flying-type technique introduced within Generation II. Certain Japanese literary works refer to the beam attack employed by Lugia in the second Pokémon film as 'Lugia Beam,' drawing a distinction from the canonical Aeroblast. Across Generation II, the animated television series, and the second Pokémon movie, Aeroblast's visual representation involves one or multiple beams of formidable energy. Conversely, from Generation III onwards, including its portrayal in the Super Smash Bros. series, Aeroblast is illustrated as a focused, vortex-like current resembling a hurricane. The earlier visual interpretation persists in titles such as Super Mystery Dungeon and Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX. As per The Power of One illustrated novelization, Aeroblast held the power to cleave oceans, fracture continents, and obliterate Lawrence III's airship. Within the Pokémon Adventures manga series, Lieutenant Surge is credited with bestowing this move's name.

Title: Cotton Spore (move)

Known in Japanese as わたほうし (Cotton Spore), this Grass-type status move made its debut in Generation II. Its primary effect is to decrease the opponent's Speed stat by two stages, with an accuracy of 85. Within the Generation II core series titles, if employed by an in-game adversary outside the confines of the Battle Tower, the move carried an inherent 25% failure probability, in addition to its standard miss chance. In earlier Double Battles, Cotton Spore could target any single combatant, whether a foe or an ally.

Presently, its impact is negated for Grass-type Pokémon, those possessing the Overcoat Ability, and any holding Safety Goggles. Modern application in both Double and Triple Battle formats sees it affecting all adjacent opposing Pokémon. Should it be amplified by a Grassium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Cotton Spore, the user's previously lowered stats will all be restored to their original levels.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, it reduces an enemy's Travel Speed by a single stage. As of Super Mystery Dungeon, it ceases to affect Grass-type Pokémon in those games. Any statistical diminutions inflicted by this move are individually applied to every Pokémon it successfully strikes.

An earlier description, preceding Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021), concisely read: "Sharply lowers the target's Speed."

For Pokémon UNITE players, Cotton Spore serves as Eldegoss's secondary ability. Acquisition occurs upon reaching level 6, by evolving Synthesis into Cotton Spore rather than Cotton Guard. During its execution, the user initiates a channeling phase, collecting spores and simultaneously boosting its movement speed. Once the channel concludes, the gathered spores burst forth, inflicting damage and launching proximate adversaries airborne, subsequently imposing a slowing effect upon their descent. A further enhancement at level 13 grants the user a 40% increase to both Defense and Sp. Defense for the duration of its channeling.

Title: Reversal (move)

This entry details the move Reversal; for the geographical feature in Unova, please consult Reversal Mountain. Known in Japanese as きしかいせい, also transcribed as Reversal, this damage-dealing Fighting-type move debuted in Generation II. It was accessible as TR21 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM134 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Reversal delivers direct damage and possesses no supplementary effects. Its potency is derived from the user's current HP as a fraction of its maximum HP, with the power escalating as the user's health depletes. Originally, Reversal was unable to land critical hits, and its damage calculation did not incorporate a random variable. However, contemporary game mechanics permit Reversal to achieve critical hits, and its damage output is now subject to a random factor during calculation. In Pokémon Contests, Reversal functions as a second-stage combination move, awarding the user 2 bonus appeal points if Endure was performed on the immediately preceding turn. Similarly, in Contest Spectacular combinations, employing Reversal after Endure yields an additional three appeal points. Fundamentally, this attack inflicts damage. Its strength is inversely proportional to the user's remaining HP. Within the Rescue Team series, Reversal's final damage is multiplied by 2 when at 75-51% HP, by 4 at 50-26% HP, and by 8 when the user's HP is 25% or less. The Explorers series implemented a revised damage multiplication for the move: 1.5x at 50-26% HP and 2x at 25% or lower. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's damage calculation is approximately proportional to the user's current HP, featuring a power multiplier roughly spanning from 0.1x at 100% HP, 0.5x at 83%, 1.0x at 62%, 1.3x at 50%, 2.0x at 20%, and a peak of 2.5x at 1 HP.

Title: Spite (move)

This Ghost-type technique, known as うらみ (Spite) in Japanese, first appeared in Generation II and deals no damage. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, trainers can find it as Technical Machine 177. Its effect involves reducing the Power Points (PP) of the last move performed by the opposing Pokémon. The move will not work if the target's most recent attack already has zero PP. Initially, Spite randomly depletes 2 to 5 PP from the designated move. Should the randomly determined reduction exceed the remaining PP, the move instead loses all its current PP. In earlier iterations, if the foe's last action was Metronome, Spite would target the move Metronome transformed into; however, because Metronome in this generation cannot select moves the user already possesses, Spite invariably failed against it. Furthermore, it used to fail if the targeted move had precisely 1 PP left. Later changes ensure that if the target's preceding action was Metronome or another move that invokes others, Spite now correctly siphons PP from the original calling move rather than simply failing. In Pokémon Contests, Spite serves as the second component of a combination, granting the performer a single bonus appeal point if Curse was executed during the preceding turn. Its PP deduction mechanism was later standardized to exactly 4 (unless fewer than 4 PP remained, in which case it depletes the existing amount). The issue of Spite failing against moves with exactly 1 PP remaining has also been rectified. For Contest Spectaculars, utilizing Spite in a combination earns the user three additional appeal points if Encore, Taunt, or Torment was employed in the immediate prior turn. When enhanced by a Ghostium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Spite, the user fully restores its HP. This move specifically targets the opposing Pokémon directly ahead, reducing the PP of their last executed ability. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers game series, Spite causes the opponent's last used move to lose all its PP, reducing it to 0. In Gates to Infinity and Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Spite applies a flat deduction of 20 PP from the foe's most recent attack. Notably, its Generation II animation depicts the identical demon-like figure also seen in Lovely Kiss.

Title: Powder Snow (move)

Introduced in Generation II, Powder Snow (Japanese: こなゆき Powder Snow) is an Ice-type offensive technique that deals damage. This attack has a 10% chance of leaving its target frozen. When used in a Double Battle, Powder Snow affects both opposing Pokémon. However, in a Triple Battle, it will only strike enemy Pokémon that are adjacent.

Powder Snow can initiate a Pokémon Contest combination, allowing Blizzard to achieve double its usual appeal score if performed on the subsequent turn. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Hail was utilized in the preceding turn. For Contest Spectacular combinations, using Powder Snow awards an extra three appeal points if Hail was employed in the prior turn.

The move inflicts damage and carries the potential to freeze the target. From the game Gates to Infinity onward, Powder Snow exclusively hits the enemy directly in front; previously, it would impact all adversaries within the room.

In Pokémon GO, Powder Snow functions as a Fast Attack and has been available since February 16, 2017. Its description before Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) indicated a very small chance of leaving the target frozen.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Powder Snow is Alolan Ninetales's initial move when it is still an Alolan Vulpix. The user unleashes a chilly gust of powdery snow, which both slows and damages enemies caught in it. Upon evolving at level 4, this move is replaced by either Avalanche or Dazzling Gleam. Thanks to the Snow Warning ability, Powder Snow disperses snow and aids Alolan Ninetales in freezing opposing Pokémon.

Powder Snow stands as the sole variant of Acid to have been introduced after Generation I. Although its description consistently refers to it as a "chilling gust of powdery snow" and it appears in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Powder Snow is not categorized as a wind move.

Title: Protect (move)

The technique known as Protect (まもる in Japanese) emerged in Generation II as a non-damaging Normal-type maneuver. It was assigned as TM17 across a range of titles, from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and is also slated for Pokémon Legends: Z-A. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was designated as TM07. When activated, Protect renders the user impervious to all effects, including damage, from moves that target it during that specific turn. However, this defense does not extend to damage accumulated at the turn's conclusion, such as that caused by weather phenomena or status conditions. Protect benefits from increased priority, typically allowing it to execute before most other attacks in a turn. Nevertheless, if the user happens to be the last to act in the turn, the move will inevitably fail. Its success rate is further reduced with each consecutive successful deployment of Endure, any self-targeting protective move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard.

Certain attacks can bypass this defense. Most damaging Z-Moves and Max Moves will only inflict 25% of their original damage, though G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow are exceptions that deliver full damage. Moves designed to break through protection, such as Feint, will deal their complete damage and simultaneously nullify Protect's effects. The Ability Unseen Fist permits contact moves to completely bypass Protect. While a comprehensive list of unaffected status moves is provided elsewhere, it generally holds that moves targeting only the user, those affecting the user and all allies, moves targeting all Pokémon (with the exceptions of Shadow Shed and Shadow Half), and entry hazard moves cannot be blocked by Protect.

In one specific generation, Protect will unfailingly fail if its user is positioned behind a Substitute. For the initial eight uses, Protect's success rate is calculated as x/255, where x starts at 255 and is halved (rounded down) with each subsequent, successful use. After eight consecutive successful uses, any further attempts to use Protect will invariably fail. In this particular generation, the success rate was intended to have a minimum threshold of 1/8, but a programming error results in erratic behavior thereafter.

Protect can be utilized as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point if Harden was successfully performed during the preceding turn. Exclusively in Diamond and Pearl, a bug caused attacks granted perfect accuracy via a move's effect (like Lock-On or Mind Reader), specific weather conditions (Thunder during rain or Blizzard during hail), or an Ability (such as No Guard) to ignore protection with inverted accuracy. One-Hit Knockout (OHKO) moves, however, were unaffected and could not penetrate this defense. This specific defect was rectified in Platinum.

When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Protect, the user benefits from a complete reset of all their lowered stats. Protect shields the user from incoming moves for a duration of 45 frames, which translates to 1.5 seconds. Damaging Plus Moves are capable of inflicting 25% of their damage through Protect. Upon activation, the user acquires the "Protect" status condition, which renders them invulnerable to the majority of assaults. Nevertheless, the user remains susceptible to damage from items or explosions. The duration of this status was 3-6 turns before Gates to Infinity, but was shortened to a single turn from Gates to Infinity onward. The likelihood of Protect failing increases if the user's immediately preceding action was either Protect itself or an enemy-evading move like Detect or Endure.

When Liza (Summer 2023) & Celesteela employ Protect, it becomes a Flying-type move courtesy of their passive skill, Flying Shift. This specific buddy move was formerly designated as B Protect until version 2.38.0. Asuka referenced Houndoom utilizing Protect in "The Legendary Pokémon and the Village of the Gods (Part Three)"; however, the move itself was not visually depicted.

During Generation II, following the display of the "[Pokémon]'s PROTECTING itself!" message, a subsequent notification appeared, indicating the opposing Pokémon's move had missed. This behavior stemmed from Protect's internal mechanics causing attacks subject to an accuracy check to register as misses, a detail that was revised in Generation III to remove the redundant second message. Protect stands as the most frequently observed move within the winning teams' movesets at the Video Game World Championships.

While Reflect, Light Screen, Barrier, Protect, and Safeguard each possess unique functionalities within the video games, their portrayals in the animated series frequently depict them as sharing the same defensive outcome as Protect in the games—shielding the user from the majority of move effects. Although Protect itself does not exist as a playable move in Pokémon GO, it seemingly served as the conceptual basis for the Protect Shield employed in Trainer Battles. Notably, the Protect Shield features a hexagonal tiling design, mirroring the animation of Protect in Generation VII, the same generation during which Trainer Battles were first implemented.

Title: Scary Face (move)

Hailing from Generation II, Scary Face (Japanese: こわいかお Scary Face) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. It was listed as TM26 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the designation TM006 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. In combat, its primary effect is to decrease the opponent's Speed stat by two stages. Scary Face initially possessed an accuracy of 90%; a notable alteration later saw this move's accuracy modified, increasing to a perfect 100%. Specifically within the Generation II core series titles, if an in-game adversary (outside the Battle Tower) employs Scary Face, it faced an additional 25% inherent failure probability, separate from its regular chance to miss.

As a strategic opener in Pokémon Contest combinations, Scary Face enables a subsequent use of Bite, Crunch, Leer, or Super Fang in the following turn to yield twice the standard appeal points. Conversely, it serves as a finisher, awarding the performer an extra 2 appeal points if Leer or Rage preceded it. When boosted by a Normalium Z to become Z-Scary Face, the Pokémon's own Speed stat receives a one-stage increase.

Should a Kyurem possessing Scary Face undergo fusion with either Reshiram or Zekrom through the use of the DNA Splicers, the move will transform into Fusion Flare or Fusion Bolt, respectively. Undoing this fusion process will reverse these specific move alterations. From Generation VIII onward, because Scary Face can be taught via TM, a fused Kyurem is now capable of acquiring the move. However, if a fused Kyurem that knows Scary Face is subsequently separated, it will first lose Scary Face from its moveset prior to Fusion Flare or Fusion Bolt being replaced. Beyond combat, Scary Face also has the effect of reducing the target's Travel Speed by one stage. It is susceptible to the effects of Magic Coat.

Within the realm of Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status techniques, Scary Face among them, were exclusively wielded by enemy Pokémon and were unavailable for acquisition by captured Pokémon. Notably, Boss Silvally was observed employing Scary Face in this game.

Title: Feint Attack (move)

For the separate move named 'Feint,' consult Feint (move). This damage-dealing Dark-type move, which first appeared in Generation II, is known in Japanese as だましうち (Sneak Attack). Prior to Pokémon X and Y, its name was spelled 'Faint Attack.' When executed, Feint Attack deals damage and possesses perfect accuracy, ensuring it always connects, unless the target Pokémon is currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Within a Pokémon Contest combination, Feint Attack serves as a valid second move; the user receives 2 additional appeal points if Fake Out, Leer, or Pound preceded it in the previous turn. The move avoids triggering abilities or items that specifically activate upon contact. Despite this, it is presently categorized as a physical move that makes contact. Across various media, including Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, and both the English Trading Card Game and Trading Figure Game, the name 'Feint Attack' has been used without variation. During Pokémon Festa 2002, an early animated depiction of this attack revealed that its background remained static. Within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, despite its general unavailability, Ace Trainer Meagan, encountered on Route 228, initially possesses a Delcatty that knows Feint Attack. However, starting with version 1.1.3 of these titles, that specific Delcatty's move was altered to Covet.

Title: Sweet Kiss (move)

Sweet Kiss, known in Japanese as てんしのキッス (Angel's Kiss), is a non-damaging Fairy-type technique that first appeared in Generation II. Before Generation VI, this move was classified as a Normal-type. It functions as the confusion-inducing counterpart to Lovely Kiss. The primary effect of Sweet Kiss is to bewilder the target; however, it has no impact on Pokémon possessing the Own Tempo Ability.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Sweet Kiss can serve as the second move, granting the user 2 additional appeal points if Charm was executed in the preceding turn. When empowered by a Fairium Z crystal, it transforms into Z-Sweet Kiss, which results in the user's Special Attack stat increasing by one stage. This move is susceptible to Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Sweet Kiss, were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Specifically, Boss Togepi and Togetic were observed employing Sweet Kiss. Prior to Generation VII, the in-game animation for Sweet Kiss mirrored its Japanese name, depicting an angel descending to bestow a kiss upon the opponent. A similar visual characteristic was present for Lovely Kiss, though only during Generation II and Generation III.

Sweet Kiss is one of only four Generation II moves to have its type reclassified in a later generation; the others are Charm, Curse, and Moonlight. With the exception of Curse, all these moves were originally Normal-type techniques that transitioned to Fairy-type in Generation VI.

Title: Belly Drum (move)

Debuting in Generation II, Belly Drum (Japanese: はらだいこ Belly Drum) is a Normal-type move that inflicts no damage. When executed, Belly Drum causes the user to forfeit half of its maximum HP (rounded down) from its present HP pool. In exchange for this sacrifice, the user's Attack stat is immediately boosted to its maximum of +6 stages, irrespective of whether its current Attack modifiers were negative before activation. This move will not succeed if the Pokémon's current HP falls below half of its maximum value, or if its Attack stat has already reached its +6 maximum, a condition that holds true even for Pokémon with the Contrary ability. Should the user possess the Contrary ability, its Attack will instead be reduced to -6 stages (despite the in-game message stating that its Attack was maximized); remarkably, even if the user's Attack is already at -6 stages, the HP deduction component of Belly Drum will still occur. Exclusively within Generation II handheld titles, a programming error allows a Pokémon to receive a 2-stage Attack boost when using Belly Drum, even if its HP is below the 50% threshold that would normally cause the move to fail.

In Pokémon Contests, Belly Drum serves as an initial component of a combination, enabling Rest to achieve double its usual appeal score if performed on the subsequent turn. When transformed into Z-Belly Drum by a Normalium Z, the user completely restores its HP prior to the standard HP cost of Belly Drum being applied. Ultimately, Belly Drum's primary effect is to maximize the user's Attack power.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad iterations of the Mystery Dungeon series, this technique reduces the user's Belly stat to 1, rendering it ineffective if the user's Belly is already at 1 or lower. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, Belly Drum's effect was altered to instead decrease the user's Belly by 50 points, consequently failing if the user's Belly is at 50 or less when attempted. In the game Pokémon UNITE, Azumarill's Unite Move, Belly Bash, functions in a comparable manner to Belly Drum.

Unique among moves, Belly Drum is the sole ability capable of elevating a single stat by more than three stages without relying on the Simple ability, an exclusion that does not apply to moves like Haze, Psych Up, Power Swap, Guard Swap, Heart Swap, and Spectral Thief. Although Shell Smash generates a total of eight net stat modifications, Belly Drum can induce a greater number of stat changes if the user's Attack stat has been lowered by at least three stages, potentially reaching twelve total changes if the user's Attack is at a -6 modifier. Owing to the interaction between Belly Drum and Darmanitan's Zen Mode Ability, the move has the potential to trigger a form change for a Standard Mode Darmanitan, unless the Pokémon is at full HP with an odd maximum HP value.

Title: Sludge Bomb (move)
