Known in Japan as ヘドロばくだん (Sludge Bomb), this damage-dealing Poison-type attack first appeared in Generation II. It served as Technical Machine TM36 across various titles, from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. However, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned to TM52. When executed, Sludge Bomb deals damage and carries a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon poisoned. Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof ability are immune to its effects. In Pokémon Contests, Sludge Bomb can initiate a combination; if Sludge is subsequently used on the following turn, its appeal score will be doubled. Alternatively, it can conclude a combination, granting the user 2 additional appeal points if Sludge was deployed on the preceding turn. Overall, Sludge Bomb delivers direct harm and carries the potential to inflict poison upon its adversary. For its application within Pokkén Tournament, consult Gengar's moveset in that particular game. Before Version 2.10.0 (implemented June 28, 2021), the move's description noted a moderate likelihood of poisoning the opponent. Similarly, an older description, preceding Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022), stated that the move's power would escalate against targets already affected by poison or bad poison.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Sludge Bomb functions as the initial signature move for both Venusaur and Gengar. Venusaur acquires this move upon reaching the Ivysaur stage at level 5, choosing to evolve Seed Bomb into Sludge Bomb rather than Giga Drain. In this context, the Pokémon discharges noxious sludge toward a chosen area, which, upon impact, harms and poisons all foes within its blast zone. The lingering sludge continues to damage adversaries over time within its radius, simultaneously slowing them and diminishing their Sp. Def by 30% after each damage tick (this Sp. Def reduction stacks only once) for a brief duration. Its area of effect expands once the Pokémon reaches level 11. Gengar, conversely, gains access to Sludge Bomb as Haunter at level 7 by upgrading Will-O-Wisp into it instead of Shadow Ball. When Gengar uses it, the Pokémon flings unhygienic sludge at a specified location, causing ongoing poison damage to enemies within its proximity. The duration of this poison effect lengthens at level 13.

An interesting note from the English dub of 'A Crasher Course in Power!' reveals Crasher Wake instructing his Quagsire to use Sludge Bomb; however, this represents a translation inaccuracy, as the original Japanese script specified Mud Bomb. In GDZ60, Sludge Bomb was both referenced and visually depicted, though the identity of the Pokémon employing it remained undisclosed. Notably, Sludge Bomb stands alone as the sole Poison-type attack introduced during Generation II. From Generation V onwards, its in-game description text mirrors that of the move Sludge. Starting with Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, likely due to an oversight, the Polish dub of the animated series has utilized the identical translated name for both Sludge Bomb and Mud Bomb. In Generation II, Oddish and Gloom were capable of learning Sludge Bomb, a capability that Bellossom lacked, though this inconsistency was rectified in Generation III.

Title: Octazooka (move)

Known in Japanese as オクタンほう (Okutank Cannon), Octazooka is a Water-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation II. Until Generation VIII, it was Octillery's unique signature move, though other Pokémon could still gain it through breeding. Octazooka causes damage and holds a 50% probability of reducing the target's accuracy by one stage. Pokémon with the Bulletproof Ability are entirely immune to Octazooka. In Pokémon Contests, Octazooka can be used as the second move of a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Lock-On or Rain Dance was performed during the previous turn. The attack causes damage and results in the target being less likely to land hits on the user. Damage is inflicted by Octazooka. Prior to Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Octazooka might lower the foe's accuracy by one stage. Conversely, from Super Mystery Dungeon and subsequent titles, Octazooka may instead induce the Blinker status condition. This affliction prevents the affected Pokémon from observing other Pokémon, items, or dungeon walls, and compels it to view allies as adversaries. Octazooka is a Charged Attack within Pokémon GO, available since December 11, 2019. Its in-game description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) read: "Has a good chance of lowering the target's accuracy." An event-exclusive Sableye has the capability to learn Octazooka, despite its lack of any connection to Water-type Pokémon or firearms. Chief Golgo Matsumoto chose its moves, believing it would contribute to a more enjoyable and surprising event experience.

Title: Spikes (move)

Known in Japanese as まきびし (Caltrop), Spikes debuted in Generation II as a Ground-type status move, inflicting no direct damage. This move deploys an entry hazard across the opposing team's side of the battlefield. Any Pokémon entering the battle onto this hazard-laden terrain suffers damage equivalent to 12.5% of its maximum HP, always rounded down. However, creatures of the Flying type are unaffected by this damage. The effect of Spikes can be cleared from the user's side of the field by utilizing the move Rapid Spin.

A specific scenario in Pokémon Stadium 2 dictates that if both combatants are compelled to deploy their final remaining Pokémon, and these Pokémon possess 12.5% or less of their HP, they will take Spikes damage, subsequently faint, leading to a declared draw. Uniquely within this generation, Pokémon knocked out by Spikes do not undergo standard faint checks immediately. This anomaly can result in a Pokémon with 0 HP remaining on the field that has not yet fainted, provided no additional unrelated faint checks occur within the game loop. This phenomenon is most easily replicated by having a Pokémon succumb to Perish Song, then switching in another Pokémon that instantly faints from Spikes.

Spikes can be deployed a maximum of three times, with each subsequent layer escalating the damage inflicted on incoming opponents. A single layer of Spikes causes a Pokémon to lose 1/8 (12.5%) of its maximum HP; two layers inflict 1/6 (16.67%) damage; and three layers result in a loss of 1/4 (25%) of maximum HP. Furthermore, any Pokémon possessing the Ability Levitate is also immune to this damage. The move Defog serves to eliminate Spikes from the target's side of the field. Pokémon affected by Magnet Rise (if received via Baton Pass) or those with the Magic Guard Ability remain impervious to the damage. Nevertheless, Flying-type and Levitating Pokémon become vulnerable if under the influence of Gravity, while holding an Iron Ball, or after being Baton Passed an Ingrain effect. Spikes can also be sent back to the user through the use of Magic Coat or the Magic Bounce Ability. A Pokémon holding an Air Balloon will also experience immunity to the Spikes damage. Defog has been updated to clear all Spikes from both sides of the field, irrespective of their initial placement.

In Contest Spectaculars, Spikes contributes to a combination, granting Circle Throw, Dragon Tail, Roar, and Whirlwind an additional three appeal points when performed on the subsequent turn. When Spikes is empowered by a Groundium Z to become Z-Spikes, the user's Defense statistic increases by one stage. Pokémon equipped with Heavy-Duty Boots are protected from Spikes damage upon entering combat.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Spikes functions as a physical attack, delivering direct damage with a base power of 40. It additionally inflicts splinters on the target for 4 turns when used with Strong Style, 2 turns with Agile Style, or 3 turns under normal circumstances. The move Ceaseless Edge will establish a single layer of spikes on the opponent's side of the terrain. Similar to other entry hazard moves reenvisioned for Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Spikes scatters its projectiles in a fan-shaped zone directly ahead of the user. Adversaries sustain damage upon initial contact with these caltrops and continue to take damage if they remain within the hazardous area for a prolonged duration. Even with visible contact, Flying-type Pokémon are immune to Spikes' damage. Given its damage-dealing nature in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Gliscor benefits from STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus) when employing Spikes.

In Mystery Dungeon games, Spikes prompts the user to generate a Spikes trap (referred to as a Spiked Tile before Super Mystery Dungeon) directly beneath itself, provided the tile is a land tile, not stairs, and not Kecleon Shop carpet. This trap is further restricted from being set in hallways or at room entrances. Any Pokémon that triggers a Spikes trap will lose 15 / 20 / 30 HP.

Title: Zap Cannon (move)

The Electric-type attack known as Zap Cannon (Japanese: でんじほう Electromagnetic Cannon) made its debut in Generation II as a damage-dealing technique. During Generation II, trainers could acquire it as TM07. This move possesses a base power of 100. Initially, it carried a 255/256 (~99.6%) likelihood of inflicting paralysis upon its target, but currently, any successful hit from Zap Cannon guarantees paralysis for the affected Pokémon. In Pokémon Contests, if Lock-On was utilized in the preceding turn, employing Zap Cannon as the second move in a combination grants the user 4 additional appeal points. Creatures possessing the Bulletproof Ability remain unaffected by Zap Cannon. Furthermore, within Contest Spectaculars, Zap Cannon forms part of a combination where using Hex or Smelling Salts on the subsequent turn results in an extra three appeal points. Fundamentally, this attack inflicts damage and ensures the foe becomes paralyzed. Its presence in Pokémon GO dates back to February 16, 2017, where it functions as a Charged Attack.

Title: Foresight (move)

A non-damaging Normal-type move, Foresight (Japanese: みやぶる, meaning 'See Through') debuted in Generation II. When activated, if the user's accuracy stat stage is lower than the target's evasion stat stage, both stages are treated as zero during subsequent accuracy checks against that target. This effect persists until the target switches out, but it can be passed via Baton Pass. Foresight also negates the Ghost type's immunity to Fighting-type and Normal-type moves for any attacks directed at the target. This move boasts a perfect 100% accuracy. Initially, Foresight would fail if used on a Pokémon already under its influence.

In Pokémon Stadium 2, Foresight's accuracy could be diminished by Bright Powder, Flash, Kinesis, Mud-Slap, Sand Attack, and Smokescreen, yet it was immune to Double Team and Minimize. Later updates established that Foresight causes all accuracy checks against the target to ignore any changes to the target's evasion stat stages. This particular effect, however, was no longer transferable via Baton Pass. Concurrently, Foresight became capable of being successfully reapplied to Pokémon already under its spell. Further refinements dictated that Foresight only causes accuracy checks against the target to ignore evasion stat stage changes if the target's evasion stat stage is greater than 0. Foresight itself now inherently bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring it always connects, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly. Eventually, the move reverted, once again failing if deployed against a Pokémon already under its effect.

When amplified by a Normalium Z into Z-Foresight, the user gains a two-stage boost to their critical hit ratio. Foresight also resets any positive evasion stat changes on the target back to 0. Should the target be a Ghost-type, Foresight additionally inflicts the 'Exposed' status condition, thereby stripping away their immunity to Normal and Fighting-type moves. Professor Elm's Sentret was revealed on the official website to know Foresight, though it has never been depicted using the move.

Title: Destiny Bond (move)

This Ghost-type technique, known in Japan as みちづれ (Take Along With), causes no direct damage and first appeared in Generation II. Upon activation, Destiny Bond's effect persists until the user's next action. Should the user be directly knocked out by an attack during this period, the Pokémon responsible for the attack will also faint. The effect dissipates if the Pokémon using Destiny Bond takes another turn, even if that turn is "inactive" due to conditions like sleep or flinching. This move will not trigger in response to indirect damage sources, including weather effects, Leech Seed's HP drain, or Future Sight. Notably, Destiny Bond ignores the effects of Focus Sash, Sturdy, and Magic Guard. If an ally's actions lead to the user's fainting, Destiny Bond remains inactive. Originally, a Pokémon brought down by this move would faint prior to the Destiny Bond user.

Should a Pokémon using Destiny Bond be knocked out by a multi-hit attack that failed to land its full intended number of strikes, Destiny Bond will not activate. Similarly, if the Destiny Bond user faints from a move that *would* alter its stats, but no stat modification actually takes place, a message indicating the attacker was taken down with the user will display, yet the attacking Pokémon itself will not faint. Nevertheless, Destiny Bond *does* function as intended in situations where the user faints from a multistrike move that didn't hit its full intended number of times, or from moves designed to modify stats when no modification is ultimately applied.

In Pokémon Contests, Destiny Bond serves as a valid second move in a combination, granting the performer an additional 8 appeal points if Curse, Endure, or Mean Look was performed on the preceding turn. In a change from prior mechanics, a Pokémon felled by Destiny Bond will now faint *after* the user of Destiny Bond. This implies a critical strategic consequence: if a player's last Pokémon employs Destiny Bond and is then knocked out by the opponent's final Pokémon, the opposing player secures the victory. For Contest Spectaculars, Destiny Bond also contributes to a combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Encore, Taunt, or Torment was performed during the previous turn. Consecutive successful uses of Destiny Bond are not possible; it will invariably fail if attempted on the turn immediately following a successful activation.

When enhanced by a Ghostium Z, transforming into Z-Destiny Bond, the user gains a spotlight effect for that turn, drawing all possible opposing attacks towards itself. During a Max Raid Battle, attempting to use Destiny Bond results in failure, accompanied by the message: "The move was blocked by the power of Dynamax!" Prior to version 1.2.0 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, if a Pokémon Terastallized on the turn immediately following its use of Destiny Bond, the attacker would not faint.

Furthermore, a 'Destiny Bond' status condition is inflicted upon the enemy. Any damage the user suffers, including recoil, will also be applied to the target affected by this status condition. Should the move be reflected, the user becomes linked to itself. Consequently, Destiny Bond will activate if the user takes damage from recoil, crash damage, or from another move that was bounced back. This status condition persists for 11 or 6 turns. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, status moves, Destiny Bond among them, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and unobtainable by captured Pokémon. Opposing Wobbuffet could notably be seen utilizing Destiny Bond.

Title: Perish Song (move)

Introduced in Generation II, Perish Song (known as ほろびのうた, or 'Song of Perishing,' in Japanese) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no direct damage. The Ability Perish Body operates in a manner akin to this move. Upon its activation, all combatants present on the battlefield acquire a 'perish count,' with the exceptions of those possessing the Soundproof Ability or those already under its effect. This count initiates at 4 during the turn Perish Song is unleashed, decrementing by 1 at the conclusion of every subsequent turn, including the turn of its initial use. A Pokémon's 'perish count' reaching zero results in its fainting. Perish Song invariably connects, circumventing standard accuracy evaluations. Its effects extend to Pokémon positioned behind a Substitute or those under the influence of protective measures, even those afforded by Crafty Shield. As Perish Song induces fainting without dealing damage, it remains immune to mitigating effects like Focus Sash, Magic Guard, and Sturdy. Should a Pokémon be withdrawn from battle, its associated perish count is nullified. Nonetheless, employing Baton Pass during a switch transfers the perish count to the incoming Pokémon, even if the latter possesses the Soundproof Ability. Within Rotation Battles, if a Pokémon rotates out, its perish count persists but halts its decrement until the Pokémon is rotated back into active participation.

Should Perish Song's type be altered to Electric (e.g., via Ion Deluge, Plasma Fists, or Galvanize), combatants equipped with Volt Absorb, Lightning Rod, or Motor Drive will be impervious to it, and their respective Abilities will trigger as expected. Similarly, if it transforms into a Water-type move (as with Liquid Voice), Pokémon having Water Absorb, Storm Drain, or Dry Skin will resist its effects, and these Abilities will activate accordingly.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Perish Song serves as a viable second move, bestowing 2 additional appeal points if Mean Look or Sing preceded it. Furthermore, it forms part of a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding an extra three appeal points if Block, Mean Look, or Spider Web were employed in the turn before. When enhanced by a Normalium Z into Z-Perish Song, all of the user's stat reductions are cleared. Throat Chop fails to impede a Pokémon from unleashing Z-Perish Song. Dynamax Pokémon in Max Raid Battles are immune to Perish Song, though it affects them in all other battle formats. Attempting to use Perish Song in a Tera Raid Battle will result in its failure.

Should several Pokémon's perish counts drop to 0 concurrently at a turn's end, they will faint at the same moment (despite their messages and animations appearing sequentially). Currently, if multiple Pokémon's perish counts hit 0 at the turn's conclusion, their fainting order aligns with their turn order. Originally, Soundproof-wielding Pokémon, including the move's user, were impervious to Perish Song. However, Pokémon possessing Soundproof no longer gain immunity to their *own* techniques; thus, a Pokémon using Perish Song will now acquire a perish count itself, even if it has Soundproof. Conversely, other Pokémon with Soundproof retain their immunity to Perish Song.

In Pokémon Legends: Z-A's real-time combat system, any Pokémon vulnerable to Perish Song will faint if not swapped out within a thirty-second window. This ability targets all opposing Pokémon on the floor, applying the Perish Song status condition, which causes them to faint three turns thereafter (unless the status is alleviated). The Muzzled status condition impacts this move. As a sound-based attack, it is nullified by Soundproof. In the Explorers series, Magic Coat can reflect this move.

Perish Song possibly alludes to the tritone, a musical interval famously dubbed Diabolus in musica, or 'Devil in music,' due to an ancient belief that its use invited malevolent forces into the melody. This historical context illuminates the move's three-turn fainting effect, its ominous name, and its animations depicting musical notes turning black. Within Pokémon Battle Revolution, Perish Song visually represents the counter's display and its final descent to zero with distinct animations.

Title: Icy Wind (move)

Known in Japanese as こごえるかぜ (Freezing Wind), Icy Wind made its debut in Generation II as an Ice-type attack capable of inflicting damage. Beyond simply causing harm, this move possesses a 255/256 (approximately 99.6%) probability of decreasing the recipient's Speed stat by a single stage. When unleashed, it strikes all adjacent opposing Pokémon, not only damaging them but also reducing the Speed of each affected creature by one stage. In Contest Spectaculars, Icy Wind can form a combination, granting its user an additional three appeal points if the move Hail was successfully employed during the preceding turn. It additionally inflicts damage while decreasing the target's Travel Speed by one stage. Notably, any stat reductions inflicted by this maneuver are applied individually to every Pokémon it affects. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description simply read: 'Lowers the target's Speed.'

Within the world of Pokémon UNITE, Icy Wind serves as Alolan Ninetales's second designated move. Here, the Pokémon unleashes a blast of icy air that not only deals damage but also shoves and slows any foes caught in its path. Upon reaching level 6, this move is superseded by a choice between Blizzard or Aurora Veil. Furthermore, thanks to the inherent ability Snow Warning, Icy Wind actively generates snow, assisting Alolan Ninetales in freezing rival Pokémon.

Title: Detect (move)

Hailing from Generation II, Detect (Japanese: みきり All-Seeing) is a Fighting-type maneuver that inflicts no direct damage. It was designated as TM43 exclusively in Generation II. When successfully executed, Detect erects a barrier, completely shielding its user from all effects, including incoming damage, of moves that target them during that turn. This defense, however, does not extend to end-of-turn damage originating from sources like weather conditions or status ailments. Detect holds increased priority, meaning it typically resolves before most other attacks within the same turn. Nevertheless, the move will fail if the user's action sequence places them last in the turn. The probability of Detect succeeding diminishes with each consecutive successful deployment of Endure, any single-target protection move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard by the user.

A select few moves can still penetrate Detect's guard. The majority of damaging Z-Moves and Max Moves will only inflict 25% of their standard damage, though G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow are exceptions, striking for their full power. Moves explicitly designed to break through protection, such as Feint, will deliver full damage and simultaneously dismantle Detect's effects. Furthermore, the Ability Unseen Fist permits contact moves to bypass Detect entirely. Status moves impervious to Detect are detailed separately. Generally speaking, moves aimed solely at the user, those targeting the user and all allies, moves affecting all Pokémon on the field (excluding Shadow Shed and Shadow Half), and entry hazard moves cannot be blocked by Detect.

Solely within a specific generation, Detect will fail if the user is behind a Substitute. Also in that generation, while its success rate was intended to have a floor of 1/8, a programming flaw rendered subsequent attempts erratic instead.

In Pokémon Contests, Detect can be utilized as the second component of a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Taunt was employed during the preceding turn. Exclusively in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, a known bug allowed attacks that achieved perfect accuracy—whether through a move (like Lock-On or Mind Reader), specific weather (Thunder in rain or Blizzard in hail), or an Ability (such as No Guard)—to disregard protection using an inverted accuracy calculation. One-Hit Knockout (OHKO) moves were unaffected by this and could not strike through protection. This particular bug was rectified in Platinum.

When powered up by a Fightinium Z to become Z-Detect, the user's evasion stat rises by one stage. Functionally, Detect is identical to Protect, differing only in its slightly quicker cooldown and shorter duration. For 18 frames (0.6 seconds), the user temporarily shields itself from all damage and the effects of moves directed at and around it. Damaging Plus Moves will inflict 25% of their normal damage through Detect. The user is granted the Protect status condition for a duration of 3-6 turns or, in some cases, 1 turn. Despite its protective effects, the user can still be harmed by items, traps, or explosions. The chance of Detect failing increases if the user's most recently performed move was Detect itself or any other move designed to evade enemy attacks (such as Protect or Endure).

In the film Lucario and the Mystery of Mew, Lucario was mentioned to possess knowledge of Detect, though he was never depicted utilizing the move. During Generation II, following the '[Pokémon's name] Protecting itself!' message, a secondary message indicating that the opponent's Pokémon's move had missed would appear. This additional message was removed in Generation III.

Title: Bone Rush (move)

Known in Japanese as ボーンラッシュ Bone Rush, this Ground-type, damage-inflicting multistrike move made its debut in Generation II. Until Generation IV, it served as a signature technique for both Cubone and Marowak. Each activation of Bone Rush causes damage by striking the opposing Pokémon between 2 and 5 times. Its strike distribution is as follows: a 37.5% probability for 2 hits, an identical 37.5% chance for 3 hits, a 12.5% likelihood for 4 hits, and also a 12.5% likelihood for 5 hits. Every individual strike possesses an identical probability of landing as a critical hit. For the purposes of Bide and Counter, only the move's ultimate strike is taken into account. Should the user be equipped with a King's Rock, its concluding strike gains a chance to induce flinching in the target. Furthermore, when the user holds either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every sequential hit possesses an equal opportunity to make the opponent flinch. In Pokémon Contests, Bone Rush serves as an initiating move in a combination; employing it causes Bone Club and Bonemerang to achieve double their standard appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. Conversely, it can conclude a combination, granting the user an additional 4 appeal points if Bone Club, Bonemerang, or Focus Energy was performed during the preceding turn.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Bone Rush features altered characteristics: its accuracy is increased from 80% to 90%, and its hit distribution changes to a 35% chance for 2 hits, 35% for 3 hits, 15% for 4 hits, and 15% for 5 hits. The average overall base power, accounting for an intrinsic 10% miss chance, is 69.8. When a Focus Sash activates before the final strike, the very next hit will still result in the defending Pokémon fainting. A Focus Band, however, can activate multiple times to prevent fainting, with each instance being independent. Sturdy will trigger with every hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. Furthermore, if the target possesses Weak Armor or Stamina, each strike will activate these abilities. Each of Bone Rush's 2-5 damage-dealing hits undergoes a separate accuracy check, though the move itself continues even if a strike fails. Its critical-hit rate is notably lowered (set at 2% within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad iterations). From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, the activation of a Reviver Seed will cause the move to cease.

In Pokémon UNITE, Bone Rush stands as one of Lucario's potential secondary moves. Players acquire it by reaching level 7 and opting to evolve Meteor Mash into Bone Rush rather than Close Combat. Upon activation, Bone Rush causes the user to execute a bone-wielding combo attack that damages and shoves opposing Pokémon, simultaneously applying an Extreme Speed mark. The concluding strike of this comoo launches the bone into the air. Should Bone Rush be activated a second time, Lucario swiftly dashes to the bone's position, inflicting damage upon nearby opposing Pokémon. Upon attaining level 13, Bone Rush transforms into Bone Rush+, which, when reactivated, resets the cooldown for either Extreme Speed or Power-Up Punch. It holds the distinction of being the sole bone-themed move learnable by Pokémon beyond Cubone and Marowak.

Title: Outrage (move)

Known in Japanese as げきりん, or "Imperial Wrath," Outrage is a Dragon-type attack capable of inflicting damage, first appearing in Generation II. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it functions as TM39. Before Generation III, this move was exclusive to Dratini's evolutionary family, though other species could acquire it through breeding.

Outrage delivers damage over 2 to 3 turns, possesses a base power of 90, and has 15 PP. Its PP is expended solely when the move is initially activated. During its execution, the Pokémon is unable to select any other action. Upon completion, the user experiences confusion, attributed to exhaustion. Should Outrage's multi-turn effect be interrupted (e.g., by full paralysis or self-inflicted damage from confusion), it concludes instantly. Confusion afflicts the user exclusively if Outrage runs its full course without interruption. Conditions like sleep, freeze, binding, or flinching will momentarily halt the move's duration but not terminate it. A notification now appears when the user is confused from fatigue. In encounters involving multiple adversaries, the user targets itself, yet strikes a randomly chosen adjacent opponent with each subsequent attack.

Subsequently, Outrage's base power was elevated to 120, while its PP was decreased to 10. The move is now disrupted if it fails due to inaccuracies, the user being asleep, paralyzed, or frozen, flinching, or if the target is Protected or immune. Furthermore, if a disruption happens on what would have been the final, confusion-triggering turn, the user invariably becomes confused. In a further adjustment, Outrage's base power was reverted to 90, and its accuracy set to 85%. It ceased its multi-turn rampage and no longer automatically induced confusion; rather, the user became fixated on it. This version of Outrage dealt damage a single time and caused confusion if successfully executed. If the user flinched during its preparation, Outrage would enter a cooldown state without confusing the user.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Outrage attacks the target 2-5 times (before Gates to Infinity) or 2-3 times (since Gates to Infinity) per use. Confusion still affects the user once the move concludes. For gigantic Pokémon in Super Mystery Dungeon, Outrage strikes only once but reaches up to 10 tiles, penetrating foes and environmental barriers.

Within Pokémon GO, Outrage functions as a Charged Attack, accessible since February 16, 2017. In Pokémon UNITE, Outrage serves as Dragonite's secondary move, unlocked upon evolving from Dragonair at level 7 by enhancing Dragon Breath instead of Hyper Beam. Dragonite then stomps the ground, dealing area damage to nearby enemies, incorporating the bonus effect of its most recent Boosted Attack. Furthermore, the user receives a 5-second buff that boosts basic attack speed, transforms all subsequent attacks into melee Boosted Attacks, and cycles through Water, Electric, and Fire Boosted Attack types sequentially. Following the buff's expiration, the user is briefly stunned. At level 13, the move further lessens the duration of hindrances imposed on the user during its rampage. Garchomp's Unite Move, Livid Outrage, shares conceptual similarities with Outrage.

In the episode The Gates of Warp!, Cynthia's Garchomp was instructed to perform Outrage but proved unable to execute the attack.

The Japanese designation, げきりん (逆鱗) gekirin, translating to "wrath of one's superior" or literally "reversed/inverted scale," possibly alludes to the idiom 逆鱗に触れる gekirin ni fureru, meaning "to infuriate one's superior" (literally, "to touch the reversed/inverted scale"). This concept stems from philosophy documented in the Han Feizi. Within East Asian mythology, dragons are typically depicted as benevolent and docile beings, yet they possess a unique scale (sometimes cited as being on their chin) which, if disturbed by stroking in the wrong direction, will provoke an uncontrollable rage, leading to immediate destructive consequences according to various tales. Although this philosophical tenet can extend to other mythical creatures, its primary intention is to draw a parallel between a subordinate inciting the wrath of their ruler, regardless of the monarch's usual temperament.

The Lake of Outrage bears the same name as this move in the majority of languages. However, in French, the lake is instead named after the move Twister (Ouragan), probably because the Lake of Rage already uses the French equivalent for Outrage. Similarly, in Italian, the lake's name corresponds to the move Dragon Rush (Dragofuria).

Title: Sandstorm (move)

Sandstorm (Japanese: すなあらし Sandstorm) is a non-damaging Rock-type move that made its debut in Generation II. It was accessible as TM37 from Generation II through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is again TM37 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When this move is activated, it summons a sandstorm that endures for 5 turns. This weather effect clears any pre-existing weather, but will fail to initiate if a sandstorm is already present.

Initially, while Sandstorm persisted, all Pokémon not belonging to the Steel, Rock, or Ground types would take damage equal to ⅛ of their maximum HP (rounded down) at the conclusion of the first four turns of the effect. Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable phase of Dig were also exempt from Sandstorm's damage, regardless of their type. Furthermore, the recovery moves Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight would restore only half the HP they normally would under clear weather. In Pokémon Stadium 2, Sandstorm consistently hit the Pokémon on Player 1's side first, if not immune, regardless of which side summoned the weather. A unique rule in Stadium 2 declared a Draw if Sandstorm caused both players' last remaining Pokémon to faint, assuming both had ⅛ HP or less at the turn's end.

Subsequently, the damage dealt by Sandstorm at the end of each turn was decreased to 1/16 of a Pokémon's maximum HP. Pokémon with the Sand Veil Ability benefit from a 4/5 accuracy modification for incoming moves and are immune to Sandstorm's damaging effects. Moonlight, Synthesis, and Morning Sun now only replenish ¼ of the user's maximum HP. The semi-invulnerable turn of Dive also prevents damage from Sandstorm.

Rock-type Pokémon now receive a 50% boost to their Special Defense for the entire duration of the sandstorm. If a Smooth Rock is held when Sandstorm is used, its duration is extended from 5 to 8 turns. Pokémon with the Sand Rush Ability have their Speed stat doubled, while those with Sand Force gain a 30% power increase for their Rock-, Ground-, and Steel-type moves. Both of these Abilities also provide immunity to Sandstorm's damaging effect. Sandstorms increase the HP recovery from Shore Up from ½ to ⅔ of the user's maximum HP. Sandstorm will fail to activate if heavy rain, extremely harsh sunlight, or strong winds are already in play.

As the first move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Sandstorm causes Mud-Slap, Sand Tomb, Sand-Attack, and Weather Ball to score double the normal appeal if used in the subsequent turn. For Contest Spectaculars, using Sand Attack, Sand Tomb, or Weather Ball after Sandstorm yields an extra three base appeal points. When powered up by a Rockium Z, it transforms into Z-Sandstorm, which also raises the user's Speed stat by one stage.

In Mystery Dungeon games, Sandstorm changes the current floor's weather to a sandstorm for 18 turns, or the entire floor, or for 66 turns, depending on the context. Sandstorm can no longer override overworld weather. Despite being categorized as a wind move, Sandstorm does not trigger either the Wind Power or Wind Rider Abilities if a Pokémon possessing one of them is affected by it.

In Pokémon Ranger and Kidnapped Riolu (Part 2), a henchman's Rhyhorn was ordered to use Sandstorm but was unable to execute the attack after being struck by Dawn's Piplup's Bubble Beam. Zirc attempted to command his Rhydon to use Sandstorm in Rivals in the Dark of Night!, but was interrupted before the move could be performed. Lucy's Shuckle was revealed to know Sandstorm by Emerald's Pokédex in Just My Luck...Shuckle. The Generation III animation for Sandstorm bore a strong resemblance to Heat Wave's, with differences mainly in color and the effect on targets' sprites. Ash's Roggenrola utilized Sandstorm in a distinct manner in every episode it appeared in Pokémon the Series: Black & White prior to its evolution.

Title: Giga Drain (move)

Giga Drain, known in Japanese as ギガドレイン (Giga Drain), is a Grass-type damaging attack that first appeared in Generation II. It was designated TM19 from Generation II through Generation IV, a role it reprised in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This move stands as the most potent among three Grass-type HP-siphoning abilities, alongside Absorb and Mega Drain.

Initially, Giga Drain dealt damage and returned up to 50% of that damage to the user as HP, always restoring a minimum of 1 HP and capping at the user's maximum HP. Its foundational statistics included a base power of 60 and 5 PP. Over time, its capabilities were enhanced; its base power was later boosted to 75, and its PP was doubled to 10. The healing mechanism was also clarified to include excess damage, meaning 50% of the total damage dealt is returned to the user.

Regarding target interactions, Giga Drain originally failed if the foe had a substitute; however, it can now successfully strike substitutes and restore the user's HP accordingly. A unique interaction occurs with the Liquid Ooze Ability: instead of gaining HP, the user of Giga Drain will lose the equivalent amount. Furthermore, Giga Drain is rendered unusable if the attacker is under the effects of Heal Block.

When the user holds a Big Root, the HP recovered from Giga Drain sees a 30% increase, resulting in 65% of the damage dealt being restored; this item does not, however, augment the move's damage output. In Pokémon Contests, Giga Drain serves as the second move in a combination, granting 2 additional appeal points if Growth was performed on the preceding turn.

Notable variations exist in specific titles: in Adventure Squad, the move's healing is set at 20% of the damage inflicted. In Gates to Infinity, the user's HP recovery is solely based on the target's actual HP loss, excluding any excess damage dealt. The move's description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) stated: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Giga Drain becomes available to Venusaur at level 5, when it evolves into Ivysaur and upgrades Seed Bomb instead of Sludge Bomb. This ability projects a cone-shaped energy blast, harming opponents and simultaneously restoring Venusaur's health upon impact. For a brief period after use, Venusaur also benefits from a 35% reduction in incoming damage. It's important to note that the healing from this move is not amplified by Venusaur's passive ability. This healing effect is further enhanced at level 13.

Title: Endure (move)

Endure (Japanese: こらえる Endure) is a non-damaging Normal-type move that debuted in Generation II. It was designated as TM58 in Generation IV and also in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For the remainder of the current turn, Endure enables the user to withstand any attack that would otherwise cause it to be knocked out, leaving the Pokémon with precisely 1 HP. This technique can be employed even if the user is already at 1 HP, and it offers protection against fainting from multiple attacks within a single turn.

The success rate of Endure diminishes if it or other protective moves are used consecutively. Its formula for calculating success, including which defensive maneuvers affect it, specific exceptions, and any associated glitches, operates identically to that of Protect across all generations. Initially, Endure offered no defense against turn-end effects from moves like Future Sight. Endure now carries a +3 priority and invariably fails if the user is the last Pokémon to act in the turn.

In earlier games, if a Pokémon survived an attack using Endure while already at 1 HP, effects contingent on damage being dealt to the Pokémon (such as contact Abilities) would not activate. However, in Generation IV, if the user was holding a Berry that mitigates attack damage (like an Occa Berry), they would be left with half of their previous HP instead of 1. This behavior resulted from Endure reducing damage to the current HP value minus one, with the Berry then halving that outcome. Subsequently, if a Pokémon endures a hit while already at 1 HP, effects requiring damage to be done (e.g., contact Abilities) *will* now trigger.

In Pokémon Contests, Endure can initiate a combination, causing Destiny Bond, Endeavor, Eruption, Flail, Pain Split, and Reversal to achieve double their usual appeal if performed on the subsequent turn. As part of a Contest Spectacular combination, Endure as the first move grants an extra three appeal points for Endeavor, Flail, Pain Split, and Reversal when used next.

When powered up by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Endure, all of the user's lowered stats are reset. Endure can now provide protection against the end-of-turn effects of both Future Sight and Doom Desire. The move also imparts the "Enduring" status condition, which causes the user to survive any attack that would otherwise lead to fainting, retaining 1 HP. This specific status persists for 4-5 / 1 turn(s).

In the "Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming" manga, Emerald's Pokédex identified Endure as a known move for a Pinsir he was renting from the Battle Factory. Endure is one of only two moves to twice lose its TM status before the TM numbering system became inconsistent in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the other being Sleep Talk. Both moves coincidentally share the Normal type and were introduced in Generation II. Furthermore, both can be taught to any Pokémon capable of learning TMs. While both were initially introduced as Move Tutors in Pokémon Emerald before regaining TM status in Generation IV, only Sleep Talk later resumed its Move Tutor role in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, a distinction Endure did not share.

Title: Charm (move)

Charm (Japanese: あまえる Fawn) is a non-damaging Fairy-type move that debuted in Generation II. Before Generation VI, its classification was Normal-type. It held the TM29 slot in Pokémon Sword and Shield and is designated as TM002 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Charm is a Normal-type move. Its primary effect is to decrease the target's Attack stat by two stages. In Generation II's core series games, when an in-game opponent outside of the Battle Tower employed Charm, the move had a 25% chance of failing.

This technique can initiate a Pokémon Contest combination, allowing Flatter, Growl, Rest, Sweet Kiss, and Tail Whip to achieve double their standard appeal if performed on the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Fairium Z into Z-Charm, the user's Defense stat increases by one stage. Charm also reduces the enemy's Attack through a separate mechanic: it halves the Attack stat, an effect that stacks up to 1/128 and is distinct from stat stage modifications. Only Aurora Beam and Memento share this unique characteristic.

In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Charm retains its Normal move classification, despite its alteration to Fairy-type in the core series games. As a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO, Charm has been available since July 9, 2019. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Charm, like all status moves, was exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any caught Pokémon. Specifically, opposing Togepi and Wynaut were observed using Charm.

The move's description prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) read: "Sharply lowers the target's Attack." In the exclusive Monopoly: Pokémon Kanto Edition, Charm serves as Eevee's Power Card. Once per game, during their turn, the Eevee player may force a trade: their lowest-valued property for an opponent's highest-valued property. Should the opponent complain about this exchange, the Eevee player may collect an additional $100 from them, besides the property. If either property involved in the trade has Poké Marts or a Pokémon Center built upon it, the original owner sells these structures back to the bank, recouping half their construction cost.

Gold's Togepi, Togebo, was revealed to possess Charm by his Pokédex entry in Gligar Glide. Charm is one of only four Generation II moves whose type was reclassified in a later generation, the others being Curse, Moonlight, and Sweet Kiss. Excluding Curse, these moves were originally Normal-type and transformed into Fairy-type in Generation VI.

In Gold and Silver, Bulbasaur and Oddish were programmed to learn Charm as an Egg Move, but no legitimate father Pokémon existed to pass it down. In Generations III and IV, both could inherit the move through chain breeding from the Shroomish evolutionary line; from Generation V onward, Cottonee became a direct source. A comparable situation occurred for Snorlax in Gold and Silver. During Generations III and IV, Snorlax could acquire the move via chain breeding from the Bulbasaur line; from Generation VI onward, the Tyrunt line provided a direct inheritance route.

Title: Rollout (move)

Rollout (Japanese: ころがる Roll), a Rock-type damaging attack, debuted in Generation II, where it was designated as TM04. This consecutively executed move inflicts damage across multiple turns, with its power doubling for each successful hit. The escalating power sequence concludes and resets either after five hits or if its execution is interrupted. Furthermore, Rollout's offensive strength receives an additional boost, doubling if the user had previously employed Defense Curl.

A notable game glitch, the "Rollout storage glitch," can be triggered in Generation VII, or in Generation VIII prior to version 1.3.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, by striking a Mimikyu in its Disguised Form or an Eiscue in its Ice Face form with either Rollout or Ice Ball.

In Pokémon Contests, Rollout functions as a second move in a combination, awarding the user 3 bonus appeal points if Defense Curl or Harden was used in the preceding turn. For Contest Spectaculars, it also forms a combination, granting an additional three appeal points if Defense Curl was performed in the prior turn.

Beyond simply dealing damage, Rollout compels the user to become locked into performing the move repeatedly. From Generation IV onwards, a Lickitung that knows Rollout will evolve into Lickilicky upon leveling up.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Rollout serves as Jigglypuff's neutral special, starting with Melee. Its speed and power increase proportionally to how long the special button is held before release, though this comes at the cost of reduced control.

When used in battles, Rollout delivers damage by hitting the target up to 5 times per use. The attack sequence will terminate prematurely if even a single hit misses.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, the mechanics of Rollout vary significantly:
*   In the Rescue Team series, subsequent hits receive damage multipliers of 1.5×, 2×, 2.5×, and finally 3×.
*   The Explorers series sees a more modest damage increase of 0.1× for each successive hit.
*   Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Rollout possesses a below-average base critical hit rate of 2%.
*   From Gates to Infinity onward, the move no longer gains power from successive hits; instead, its power doubles upon the first successful use if the user had activated Defense Curl on the same floor.
*   In Super Mystery Dungeon, the move sequence will end if a Reviver Seed is consumed.

In Pokémon GO, Rollout functions as a Fast Attack, introduced on May 21, 2022.

Pokémon UNITE features Rollout as Wigglytuff's second available move, acquired upon evolving from Jigglypuff at level 4 by choosing to upgrade Defense Curl instead of Sing. Activating it grants the user a shield equivalent to 25% of its maximum HP and propels them forward, damaging and launching hit enemies into the air for 0.5 seconds. Should the user collide with an obstacle while rolling, their movement direction will change up to 5 times, and the cooldowns of Pound, Double Slap, or Dazzling Gleam will be reset. At level 12, the aerial incapacitation duration for enemies increases by 0.3 seconds.

In Pokémon Pokopia, Rollout is an ability Ditto can acquire after being taught by a Graveler in Rocky Ridges. This enables Ditto to assume Graveler form and continuously roll, building momentum. While holding ZR, Ditto can also destroy objects by crashing into them. During this transformation, Ditto's PP steadily depletes.

Rollout was among the initial moves introduced in Generation II, notably showcased alongside Donphan, the third Pokémon from that generation, when Raymond employed it in the franchise's first movie. A unique battle quirk allows a Pokémon to continue using Rollout even after forgetting it mid-battle due to leveling up and learning a new move in its slot, provided Rollout was still active.

Title: False Swipe (move)

Debuting in Generation II, False Swipe (Japanese: みねうち Strike with the Back of the Sword) is a damaging Normal-type technique. It was distributed as TM54 across numerous titles, from Generation IV up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. While delivering damage, False Swipe uniquely guarantees that the opponent will be left with 1 HP, rather than fainting. Should the foe already possess only 1 HP, this move will still strike, maintaining their HP at that value. Its damage output is not diminished when hitting a substitute. In Pokémon Contests, it serves as the second move in a combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Swords Dance was executed on the prior turn.

Consistent with its main series effect, in the Rescue Team and Explorers series, False Swipe leaves the foe with 1 HP. Though a damaging move following typical damage calculations, it was notable that Ginseng could not boost it within the Rescue Team series. This specific limitation was corrected for the Explorers series. A significant departure occurred in Gates to Infinity and Rescue Team DX, where False Swipe *could* cause an enemy to faint, thereby increasing the odds of that Pokémon enlisting in the player's team. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's behavior reverted to its earlier iteration, as the game lacked a recruitment feature.

Although Blue's Scyther was stated on the official website to know False Swipe, its usage was never actually depicted. In the episode "Let's Get all the Badges!", Bugsy ordered his Scyther to use False Swipe, but Red's Tyrogue managed to defeat it before the move could be executed. The Japanese name of False Swipe alludes to katanas, swords characteristically featuring a sharp cutting edge on one side and a largely flat surface on the other. Therefore, engaging an opponent with the flat part of such a blade would not result in a fatal blow.

Title: Swagger (move)

The Normal-type attack known as Swagger (いばる Swagger in Japanese) made its debut in Generation II and inflicts no damage. Its mechanics bear a resemblance to the move Flatter. From Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and also present in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it was available as TM87. Upon successful execution, Swagger elevates the target's Attack stat by two levels and simultaneously inflicts confusion. The target's Attack boost still occurs even if the Pokémon is presently confused, or if it is immune to confusion from effects like Safeguard. Should the target's Attack stat already be at its maximum of +6 stages, the confusion effect will not take hold. However, even if its Attack is already at +6 stages, the target can still be afflicted with confusion. Furthermore, a target possessing the Ability Contrary, even if its Attack is already at -6 stages, will nonetheless experience confusion. The move Swagger will ultimately fail if the target is immune to confusion and simultaneously either its Attack stat is at +6 stages, or it possesses the Contrary ability with its Attack already at -6 stages. When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Swagger, any of the user's diminished stats are restored to their base levels. It specifically targets the opposing Pokémon directly ahead, inducing confusion while boosting its Attack stat by two tiers. Magic Coat can reflect this particular technique. The move's in-game description, before version 2.10.0 (which was implemented on June 28, 2021), stated: "Sharply raises the opponent's Attack. Leaves the target confused." Within the manga chapter "Just My Luck...Shuckle," it was disclosed via Emerald's Pokédex that Lucy's Seviper possessed the move Swagger.

Title: Milk Drink (move)

This Normal-type support move, known in Japanese as ミルクのみ (Milk Drink), made its debut in Generation II. It does not inflict damage. For generations preceding Generation VI, and again in Generation VIII, Miltank was its exclusive user, making it Miltank's signature move. However, starting in Generation IX, Skiddo and Gogoat now share the distinction of having Milk Drink as their signature move. Initially, this ability would replenish up to 50% of the user's total HP, with the amount rounded down. Later, its effect was standardized to restore exactly 50% of the user's maximum HP, rounded up. When enhanced by a Normalium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Milk Drink, it gains the additional effect of clearing all reductions to the user's stats.

Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 through 1.1.1, Milk Drink was unavailable for selection during battles. Nevertheless, from Version 1.2.0 onwards, it became fully functional and operated identically to its behavior in earlier game installments. Before Generation VII, and specifically within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Milk Drink possessed an out-of-battle utility: allowing the user to transfer a portion of its own HP to another Pokémon in the party. Each instance of this transfer would consume 20% of the user's HP, healing the target up to their full HP capacity. This specific function is disabled if the user's remaining HP falls to 20% or lower.

A known glitch existed in Generation IV concerning this move's out-of-battle use: if a player simultaneously pressed "A" on a chosen Pokémon for healing and a D-pad direction, the game would mistakenly heal a *different* Pokémon. This could result in a Pokémon exceeding its maximum HP, or even the recovery of fainted Pokémon or Eggs. Furthermore, selecting the "Cancel" option under these glitch conditions would cause the game to crash.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team and Explorers series, Milk Drink functions differently, restoring ¼ of the maximum HP for both the user and all allies present on the same floor. The Muzzled status condition impacts this move's usability in these titles. For the Mystery Dungeon (WiiWare) games, its effect was altered to restore 1/10 of the maximum HP to the user and their same-floor teammates. Super Mystery Dungeon further refined its effect, allowing it to recover 40% of maximum HP for the user and any teammates within the same room.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Milk Drink, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Specifically, it was observed as a move utilized by Boss Miltank. The Pokémon Gold and Silver: Official Nintendo Player's Guide mistakenly identified this move as "Milk Berry." This error was rectified in the Pokémon Crystal: Official Nintendo Player's Guide. The likely cause of this misnomer stems from the fact that Japanese Berry names also conclude with 「のみ」, despite carrying a distinct semantic context.

Title: Spark (move)

The name "Spark" refers to this move. For information regarding the leader of Team Instinct in Pokémon GO, please consult Spark (GO). This particular damage-dealing Electric-type ability, known in Japanese as スパーク (Spark), first appeared in Generation II. Spark not only inflicts damage but also carries a 30% chance of leaving the opponent paralyzed. In Pokémon Contests, Spark can be utilized as the second component of a combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Charge was performed in the preceding turn. It also forms part of a Contest Spectacular combination, where three additional appeal points are awarded if Charge was executed in the prior turn. Spark deals damage and may inflict paralysis on the target. A description from before Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed."

In Pokémon UNITE, Spark is Zeraora's inaugural move. Players unlock it upon reaching level 6, at which point Agility is upgraded into Spark instead of Volt Switch. The user executes a leap in a chosen direction, harming all enemies along its path. If a basic attack is performed before the leap concludes, the user will automatically jump to the closest enemy, dealing extra damage to that target. The move holds 3 charges in reserve; after the initial use, the subsequent use must be activated within 4 seconds, or the move will enter cooldown. Activating the second use resets this timer. At level 12, if a basic attack is performed before reaching the enemy, 15% of the user's missing HP is restored. Spark is the sole variation of Psybeam that was introduced after Generation I.

Title: Fury Cutter (move)

The damaging Bug-type attack known as Fury Cutter (Japanese: れんぞくぎり Consecutive Cut) made its debut in Generation II. During Generation II, it held the TM49 designation. Initially, Fury Cutter's base power is set at 10. Its power doubles each time it successfully connects, and this effect persists even if other actions are taken by the user between uses. This power increase can accumulate to a cap of 160, achievable in a minimum of 5 turns. Should Fury Cutter fail to hit, the Pokémon be swapped out, or an item be consumed by the player, the move's power returns to its initial value.

In Pokémon Contests, Fury Cutter functions as a suitable second move in a combination, awarding 3 additional appeal points if preceded by Swords Dance. Its base power was later elevated from 10 to 20, consequently requiring four successive uses to reach the 160 power maximum. Selecting any different move will cause the accumulated power to reset. Subsequently, its base power saw another boost, going from 20 to 40, which reduced the number of consecutive turns needed to achieve the 160 power cap to three. When executed by a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness Ability, Fury Cutter's power receives a 50% amplification. Its primary function is to inflict damage.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad iterations of the game series, Fury Cutter strikes opponents twice per use. Starting with Gates to Infinity, the move hits a single time but gains a 50% power increase if it was the last move performed by that Pokémon on the current floor. The description for Fury Cutter before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) stated: "Its power increases when used in succession." An updated description, active before Version 2.20.0 (as of April 27, 2022), read: "This move's power increases when used in succession."

For sync pairs, this move employs a multiplier starting at ×1; upon a successful hit, this multiplier doubles for that specific pair, maxing out at ×8 after four or more successive applications. Nevertheless, should the same sync pair execute any other action mid-succession (such as another move by the user's Trainer or Pokémon, or even a sync move) or if the move fails to connect, the multiplier reverts to ×1.

In Pokémon UNITE, Crustle begins with Fury Cutter as its second initial move option. The user performs a single claw attack. Foes struck by Fury Cutter incur a debuff, leading to subsequent Fury Cutter strikes inflicting an extra 20% damage, stackable up to twice. This bonus damage from the debuff is consistently rounded upwards. While Fury Cutter is capable of critical hits, the user's inherent critical hit probability is 0%. Upon reaching level 6, this move is superseded by a choice between Stealth Rock or X-Scissor.

During the episode "Vanity Affair," a Trainer battling Ash commanded their Scizor to employ Fury Cutter, yet Scizor was compelled to evade Torkoal's Flamethrower, preventing the move's execution. Excluding Savage Spin-Out, Fury Cutter holds the potential for the highest power among Bug-type moves, achieving 160 on its third consecutive use. The Trainer Bugsy asserts individual discovery of this technique. In Generation II, it stood as the sole Bug-type move obtainable via TM. Before Generation V, Shedinja could acquire Fury Cutter through leveling up, specifically if Nincada's evolution was delayed until level 20, the point at which Ninjask learns the move.

Title: Steel Wing (move)

Steel Wing, known in Japanese as はがねのつばさ (Steel Wing), is a Steel-type offensive maneuver first appearing in Generation II that deals damage. It was designated as TM47 across Generations II through IV, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Subsequently, from Generation VI up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it held the TM51 slot. In Generation V, Steel Wing served as Skarmory's signature move, though other species could still acquire it through the breeding mechanic. This technique delivers damage and possesses a 10% probability of augmenting the user's Defense by one stage. Notably, Pokémon such as Doduo, Dodrio, Leavanny, Elgyem, Beheeyem, Drakloak, and Dragapult are capable of learning Steel Wing, even though they lack any discernible wings. Mew also has access to Steel Wing, again without visible wings; however, this is attributed to Mew's unique ability to learn every Technical Machine move.

Title: Mean Look (move)

Originating in Generation II, Mean Look (known in Japanese as くろいまなざし, or Dark Look) is a Normal-type technique that deals no damage. This move immobilizes the opponent, preventing them from escaping battle or switching with another Pokémon (even through abilities like Teleport). Generally, Mean Look strikes its intended foe without fail, ignoring standard accuracy checks; however, it will miss if the target is mid-animation for semi-invulnerable moves like Dig or Fly. The trapping effect persists only while the Pokémon that initiated Mean Look stays on the field. It successfully lands even against targets protected by moves such as Protect or Detect. An afflicted Pokémon can still utilize Baton Pass to switch, but the incoming Pokémon will then inherit the trapping status. Should the Pokémon that utilized Mean Look exit the battle via Baton Pass, the trapping effects will nonetheless persist. Furthermore, an affected Pokémon will still be forced out of battle if struck by Roar or Whirlwind. When a wild Pokémon employs Mean Look against a player's Pokémon, escape from the encounter becomes impossible for the player. Pokémon under the influence of Mean Look can flee (though not switch) if they possess the Run Away Ability or are equipped with a Smoke Ball. Targets caught by Mean Look are also able to switch out using U-turn. In Pokémon Contests, Mean Look can initiate a combination, granting Destiny Bond and Perish Song double their usual appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. It also serves as a finisher, awarding the user 2 extra appeal points when Curse was executed in the preceding turn. However, if a Pokémon ensnares a foe with Mean Look and subsequently switches out using Baton Pass, the target will cease to be trapped. Additionally, Pokémon affected by Mean Look can escape via Volt Switch. Moves like Dragon Tail and Circle Throw are also capable of forcibly switching out a trapped Pokémon. Ghost-type Pokémon are immune to Mean Look's effects in current mechanics. While it now penetrates defensive moves such as Protect, Detect, and Spiky Shield, it is rendered ineffective if the target is safeguarded by Crafty Shield. Affected Pokémon can also utilize Parting Shot and Flip Turn to swap out. In Contest Spectaculars, Mean Look contributes to combinations, causing specific moves (Explosion, Memento, Perish Song, and Self-Destruct) to yield an additional three appeal points when used consecutively. When empowered by a Normalium Z into Z-Mean Look, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its Special Defense stat. In Super Mystery Dungeon, Mean Look imposes the "stuck" status condition (formerly "Leg Hold" or "Shadow Hold"), which prevents the target from changing its position (though it can still attack) or swapping with the team leader. This "stuck" status lasts for 2-5 or 7 turns. Within Super Mystery Dungeon, Ghost-type Pokémon are immune to becoming stuck. Magic Coat can reflect this move. In the game Pokémon UNITE, Umbreon's inaugural move is Mean Look. It becomes accessible upon Umbreon's evolution at level 4, chosen as an upgrade for Swift over Foul Play. The user effectively immobilizes an opposing Pokémon. For a brief duration, the opponent is enveloped by a black zone from which it cannot exit. While the user remains within this black zone, its standard attacks transform into boosted attacks, delivering amplified damage. Upon reaching level 10, the duration of this black zone extends. In Generation III, the in-game status display incorrectly indicated 100% accuracy for the move, despite its actual mechanism of bypassing both accuracy and evasion checks. From Generation VII forward, the visual asset employed in Mean Look's battle animation appears to draw inspiration from Espurr's eyes, even though Espurr itself is unable to learn this specific move. This particular visual characteristic is also observable in Tearful Look and Baby-Doll Eyes (from Generation VII onwards).

Title: Attract (move)

Attract, known in Japanese as メロメロ (Mad Love), is a Normal-type status move that originated in Generation II. Its Technical Machine designation has varied: it was TM45 from Generation II through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, while in Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was TM31. This move causes the target to become infatuated with the user, provided they are of opposite genders. Attract functions regardless of the target's type immunities and, unusually for a status move, can bypass a Substitute. However, it will have no effect if the user and target share the same gender, or if either Pokémon is gender-unknown (an example being Magnemite). Furthermore, it fails if the target is already under the effect of infatuation.

Certain Pokémon are impervious to Attract's effects: those possessing the Oblivious Ability, or those shielded by an ally's Aroma Veil. Should a Pokémon become infatuated by Attract while holding a Mental Herb or an Eggant Berry, that item will be consumed, and the infatuation will immediately cease. This consumption occurs even if the Pokémon possesses the Oblivious Ability. If the target becomes infatuated while holding a Destiny Knot, the user will also experience infatuation with the target. Exclusively in Generation IV, an oversight allowed an infatuated Pokémon that had previously used Rage to select another move successfully, which would prematurely end its infatuation while its Rage effect continued to build.

When boosted by a Normalium Z, Attract transforms into Z-Attract, which provides the additional benefit of resetting all of the user's lowered stats. In contrast to Max Raid Battles where it can affect opponents, Attract is ineffective against foes in Tera Raid Battles, regardless of their gender, as Terastallized raid bosses are immune to infatuation. Attract primarily inflicts the Infatuated status condition upon the target. While gender is a specific gameplay mechanic influencing infatuation only within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, in all other games, infatuation generally affects all Pokémon. The Infatuated status typically persists for 5-6 / 6 turns and prevents the affected Pokémon from attacking 100% / 50% of the time. Before the release of Gates to Infinity, Infatuation also restricted movement and item usage.

Despite its inherent failure when used by them, several gender-unknown Pokémon can learn Attract in particular games, including the Staryu evolutionary line (specifically in Generation II), Mew, Cryogonal, Minior, and Dhelmise. Conversely, some gendered Pokémon, such as Nincada, Ninjask, the Ferroseed line, the Larvesta line, and Oranguru, are unable to learn Attract in certain games. Nincada's inability to learn Attract is likely a design choice to prevent the gender-unknown Shedinja from acquiring the move. Although Attract is depicted as working exclusively between opposite-gendered Pokémon, numerous animal species in real life have been observed exhibiting homosexual behaviors.

Title: Sleep Talk (move)

Introduced in Generation II, Sleep Talk (Japanese: ねごと Sleep Talk) is a non-damaging Normal-type move. It was assigned as TM82 in Generation IV and in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, then as TM88 from Generation VI up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When the user is currently asleep, this move causes them to automatically execute a randomly chosen ability from their known moves. Sleep Talk cannot select itself or any of the unselectable moves detailed below. This move will fail if its user is not asleep, or if the user's moveset consists solely of Sleep Talk and other unselectable abilities. A Pokémon endowed with the Comatose Ability is also considered an asleep Pokémon, enabling it to successfully employ Sleep Talk (or Snore) without possessing the actual status condition. In Double and Triple Battles, due to the inability to manually select a target, the chosen move will consistently strike a random opponent.

Sleep Talk possesses the capability to call a move that would otherwise be blocked by Disable, Gravity, Heal Block, Imprison, Taunt, Throat Chop, or Torment, by the Ability Cursed Body, or by the user's held Assault Vest. As Sleep Talk carries normal move priority, a move with higher or lower priority may not be performed at its customary timing. For instance, Sleep Talk might cause Quick Attack to be used after a normal priority move, or Vital Throw to be used before one. Only Sleep Talk's PP will be deducted, which means it can be utilized to execute moves that have exhausted their own PP.

If Sleep Talk calls Rest, and the user's HP is not full, the user will proceed to use Rest, regaining HP and resetting its sleep counter. If Sleep Talk selects Metronome or Mirror Move (moves that are only selectable by Sleep Talk in this specific generation) and this consequently triggers a two-turn move, that two-turn move will fail. Sleep Talk can call a move with no remaining PP, but will ultimately fail to execute it due to that lack of PP.

When Sleep Talk is employed while holding a choice item or under the influence of Encore, the initial use functions as normal, but all subsequent uses will invariably fail, even if the user has only one move besides Sleep Talk itself. Should Sleep Talk activate Rest, it will automatically fail. If Sleep Talk or Snore is used at least once consecutively in the turns immediately preceding a switch-out, the Pokémon will retain the same number of sleep turns it had remaining prior to using those moves when it is switched back into battle.

Sleep Talk can function as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, granting the user 3 bonus appeal points if Rest was performed in the previous turn. A move with no Power Points remaining can be successfully called and executed by Sleep Talk. Turns during which Sleep Talk is used are always tallied towards awakening the Pokémon. Sleep Talk now functions multiple times if employed while holding a choice item. Sleep Talk can also be integrated into a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding an extra three appeal points for the user if Rest was performed in the preceding turn.

When empowered by a Normalium Z into Z-Sleep Talk, the user's critical hit rate experiences a two-stage increase. Additionally, the copied move will transform into its corresponding Z-Move. However, if the copied move is a status move, it will not receive its Z-Power effect. Moves marked with a checkmark (✔) are unable to be selected by Sleep Talk.

When the user is asleep, Sleep Talk will activate a randomly chosen move from its known abilities. In the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the move will automatically trigger if the Pokémon is asleep and attacked. Upon activation, the user will orient itself towards the attacker and use one of its moves (should it select itself, it will fail). Any links associated with the selected move are disregarded. Sleep Talk remains unaffected by the cowering and cringing status conditions, but is impacted by the Muzzled status condition. Sleep Talk will only be chosen if Snore is not present in the user's moveset. This move is only counted as the Pokémon's most recent action if it was manually initiated. In Super Mystery Dungeon, Sleep Talk now automatically triggers when the user is asleep and an enemy is adjacent. It is no longer capable of selecting itself. If both Sleep Talk and Snore are available in the Pokémon's moveset, the move appearing earlier in the list will be chosen.

Prior to Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! (the juncture at which TM numbers ceased to be consistent), Sleep Talk was unique as the sole move to have regained its TM status twice, and the only move to have ever been assigned three distinct TM numbers. In Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, Sleep Talk's description mistakenly read "Uses an own move" instead of the correct "Use its own move." This textual inaccuracy was corrected in Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness.

Title: Heal Bell (move)

Originating in Generation II, Heal Bell (いやしのすず Heal Bell in Japanese) functions as a Normal-type status move, incapable of inflicting damage. Its primary effect is to cleanse the user, their entire team, and any allies from every prevailing status affliction, such as sleep, poison, paralysis, freeze, and burn. This move's restorative properties extend to Pokémon even when protected by a Substitute. Pokémon with the Soundproof Ability, even when inactive, remain untouched by Heal Bell's effects. Conversely, there are circumstances where Heal Bell *is* capable of influencing Pokémon that possess the Soundproof Ability. Presently, Heal Bell will not impact actively battling Pokémon that carry the Soundproof Ability. Nonetheless, Pokémon that are not currently in battle will receive healing from this move, regardless of their individual Abilities. When enhanced by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Heal Bell, the move gains an additional effect: its user's HP is fully restored. Notably, even under the effects of Throat Chop, a Pokémon remains able to execute Z-Heal Bell. In its current implementation, Heal Bell invariably impacts the user, even if they possess Soundproof; however, other active Pokémon holding the Soundproof Ability will remain immune to its benefits. Furthermore, within certain contexts, Heal Bell removes all detrimental status effects (sustaining examples like sleep or sealed) from both its user and nearby allies within the same area. As a characteristic sound-based attack, its general rule dictates it is obstructed by the Soundproof Ability.

Title: Return (move)

Beyond its identity as a combat technique, "Return" also denotes a common exclamation used in the animated series when recalling a Pokémon. The offensive Normal-type move, Return (Japanese: おんがえし, meaning "Return Favor"), made its debut in Generation II. It functioned as TM27 across all main series games from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Return's sole purpose is to inflict damage; it possesses no additional effects. Its base power scales directly with the user's friendship level: higher friendship translates to greater base power. The precise base power can be calculated with a specific formula. Notably, if the user's friendship stat is 127 or lower, Frustration will prove more potent than Return. Return's base power varies from 0 (at the minimum friendship of 0) to a maximum of 102 (at the maximum friendship of 255). Presently, should the calculation yield a base power of 0, it is automatically adjusted to 1.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team and Explorers series, Return's destructive potential hinges on the user's IQ. An IQ of 0 results in 5 damage. Damage subsequently increases by 5 upon reaching 50 and 100 IQ, and continues to rise by 5 for every subsequent 100 IQ, culminating in a peak damage of 45 at 700 IQ. Under circumstances not typically achievable through standard gameplay, an IQ between 1000 and 9999 causes Return to function as a one-hit knockout attack. Conversely, an IQ of 10000 or greater reduces its output to just 1 damage due to a numerical overflow. Should a negative IQ be present, it reverts to dealing the default 5 damage.

Starting with Gates to Infinity and in subsequent Mystery Dungeon installments, the IQ system was discontinued. Instead, Return's damage now scales with the number of dungeons the user has explored. Damage is no longer a static value; rather, it is determined by the user's friendship, which gains 2 points each time a dungeon is entered. These friendship values are categorized into four distinct tiers: 0-10, 11-20, 21-30, and 31 or higher. The resulting power enhancements are quite modest.

In Pokémon GO, Return is classified as a Charged Attack, made accessible from July 22, 2019. This move is uniquely available to Purified Pokémon, serving as their default technique. When a Shadow Pokémon undergoes purification, Return automatically takes the place of the Charged Attack in the first slot, irrespective of whether Frustration was previously occupying that position. Once forgotten, it cannot be relearned using either a Charged TM or an Elite Charged TM.

In Spanish localizations of the games preceding Generation VI, Return bore the name "Retroceso," a probable consequence of a literal translation of "return." A more precise translation was introduced in Pokémon X and Y, where the move was appropriately renamed "Retribución." Curiously, a Pokémon Presents trailer promoting Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl featured a Pikachu performing in a Super Contest Show that appeared to know Return, despite the move being unavailable for use in those specific titles.

Title: Present (move)

The Normal-type attack known as Present (Japanese: プレゼント Present) made its debut in Generation II, capable of inflicting damage. This move is exclusively associated with Delibird and its Paradox Pokémon variant, Iron Bundle. Present possesses a unique dual effect: it can either inflict harm upon the chosen Pokémon or restore 1/4 of its total HP. Should Present result in damage, its base power is randomly determined, selecting from values of 40, 80, or 120. The specific outcome of Present, whether damage or healing, is randomly selected.

When a user performs a healing Present while holding a Normal Gem, the item is still expended, despite the fact that the amount of HP restored remains unchanged.
