Correspondingly, if Present's type is altered (e.g., by Electrify, Ion Deluge, or Pixilate), any held Gem of that new type will also be consumed. A Pokémon with the Parental Bond ability will only hit once with Present if it heals the target. Conversely, if Present inflicts damage, a Pokémon with Parental Bond will cause it to hit twice. Should Present be directed at an ally possessing Telepathy, the move will invariably be blocked, irrespective of whether its intended effect was to heal. When a target is under the influence of Heal Block, Present will fail to restore its HP, though any damage-dealing effect will proceed as usual.

During this specific generation, Present possesses a 20% probability of attempting to heal a Pokémon that already has full HP, with the subsequent in-game message varying slightly by title. Specifically in Pokémon Gold and Silver, if Present attempts to heal a full-HP target, the game will display the message: "Enemy <Pokémon> can't receive the gift!" For Pokémon Stadium 2, the move's animation still plays, accompanied by the text "It couldn't receive the gift!" English versions of Pokémon Crystal, however, will not show any such message.

A programming error in Gold and Silver leads to the replacement of the level, Attack, and Defense variables within Present's damage calculation formula. The Attack stat is substituted with a value of 5 if the target is Rock- or Steel-type, or 10 for all other types. Furthermore, the level is derived from the defending Pokémon's type index number, while the Defense stat is based on the attacking Pokémon's type index number (using the secondary type if the Pokémon has two). This specific glitch was resolved in Crystal (excluding link battles) and Pokémon Stadium 2, allowing Present's damage to be calculated accurately. After this fix, Present will no longer have any effect if its healing component targets a Pokémon with full HP.

Present is also viable for use in Contest Spectacular combinations; its user earns an additional three appeal points if Celebrate, Covet, Happy Hour, or Wish was performed in the preceding turn.

Before the Super Mystery Dungeon installment, Present's targeting was limited to foes directly ahead, and it would either inflict damage or potentially restore 25% of an enemy's maximum HP. Within the Rescue Team series, if Present causes damage, it will deal a base of 40, 80, or 120, which is then multiplied by STAB and type matchup bonuses. However, in the Explorers series and the Adventure series, these damage values are adjusted to 25, 50, or 75. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, Present's targeting expanded to include allies in addition to adversaries. When directed at a teammate, the move consistently restores 25% of their maximum HP. If a teammate's HP falls below 50% of their maximum, the user will instantly teleport beside an ally anywhere on the floor to administer healing. From Generation VI onward, the healing variant of Present no longer features a unique animation.

Title: Frustration (move)

Frustration (Japanese: やつあたり, meaning 'Angry Outburst') stands as a Normal-type damage-dealing attack that first appeared in Generation II. Throughout Generations II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it held the designation of TM21.

This Normal-type attack deals damage without any secondary effects. Its base power is intrinsically linked to the user's friendship level; lower friendship values result in a more potent attack. If the user's friendship stands at 128 or higher, Return ultimately surpasses Frustration in strength. The move's power fluctuates between 0 (achieved at the maximum friendship value of 255) and 102 (attained at the minimum friendship value of 0). However, if the underlying calculation would produce a power of 0, Frustration's base power is instead fixed at 1.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team and Explorers series, Frustration's damage output is determined by the user's IQ. It begins at 45 damage with 0 IQ. The damage then diminishes by 5 upon reaching 50 IQ, then again at 100 IQ, and continues to decrease by 5 for every subsequent 100 IQ milestone, eventually hitting a low of 5 damage at 700 IQ. While not achievable through standard gameplay, an IQ of 1000 or greater causes Frustration to deal a mere 1 damage, whereas a negative IQ value transforms it into a one-hit knockout attack.

Conversely, in the Adventure Squad series, the move's damage initiates at 35 for IQs ranging from 0 to 199. It subsequently decreases by 5 for every 100 IQ increment thereafter, reaching its minimum of 5 damage at 700 IQ.

From Gates to Infinity and later titles, the IQ system was removed. Consequently, Frustration's damage now diminishes in proportion to the number of dungeons the user has explored. Damage is no longer constant; instead, it relies on the user's friendship, which advances by 2 points each time a dungeon is entered. This system incorporates four distinct friendship tiers: 0-10, 11-20, 21-30, and 31+, with any power enhancements being very minor.

As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Frustration first became obtainable on July 22, 2019. It is exclusively learned by, and serves as the default Charged Attack for, Shadow Pokémon. When a Shadow Pokémon undergoes purification, Frustration is superseded by Return. Notably, Frustration cannot be replaced by a Charged TM or an Elite Charged TM, save for specific event periods. Similarly, it cannot be relearned through either a Charged TM or an Elite Charged TM. Across both Gyms and Trainer Battles, Frustration holds the distinction of being the weakest Charged Attack available in Pokémon GO.

In Crystal's Battle Tower, alongside all core series Pokémon titles from Generation III through Generation VII, any Pokémon owned by a non-playable Trainer that possesses the move Frustration is invariably given 0 friendship.

Title: Safeguard (move)

Known in Japanese as しんぴのまもり (Mysterious Protection), Safeguard is a Normal-type technique introduced in Generation II that deals no direct damage. This move served as TM20 across various titles, specifically from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

For a duration of five turns, Safeguard shields the user and its teammates from incurring non-volatile status ailments and the confused status. However, it does not alleviate pre-existing status conditions, which continue to affect Pokémon normally. While Safeguard doesn't prevent self-inflicted sleep from Rest, it does stop a Pokémon from becoming drowsy due to Yawn, though it won't prevent sleep if the Pokémon is already drowsy. It provides no defense against confusion caused by a held Berserk Gene. The Safeguard effect is dispelled from a Pokémon's side of the field if struck by the move Defog, even when Defog is employed by an ally. Furthermore, the Ability Infiltrator can circumvent Safeguard's protection.

Regarding evolving game mechanics: Safeguard *did previously prevent* confusion resulting from fatigue after using moves such as Outrage, Petal Dance, or Thrash, but this particular effect was later removed. Similarly, Safeguard *used to* protect allies from confusion triggered by Figy, Wiki, Mago, Aguav, or Iapapa Berries (irrespective of their consumption method via held item, Fling, Bug Bite, or Pluck), and also shielded against burns or poison from held Flame Orbs or Toxic Orbs; these allied protections have also since been discontinued. Conversely, Safeguard *now* provides protection to the user against status conditions received from making contact with Pokémon that have the Abilities Effect Spore, Static, Flame Body, or Poison Point.

When transformed into Z-Safeguard via a Normalium Z, the move grants an additional benefit, boosting the user's Speed stat by one stage.

Within the *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon* series, Safeguard targets all teammates in the same room, bestowing upon them the "Safeguard" status. This status grants immunity to detrimental status conditions (including Drowsy-induced sleep) and move seals. Similar to the main series, it does not remove pre-existing conditions. Its duration spans 16-20 turns, extending to 21 turns in some instances. This move is susceptible to Snatch. An older description, preceding version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated its function as: "Blocks status conditions from affecting the allied field of play."

In *Pokémon UNITE*, Safeguard serves as Blissey's secondary move. It becomes available upon evolution at level 4, chosen as an upgrade path from Heal Pulse, distinct from Soft-Boiled. This ability provides a 3-second shield to both the targeted ally and Blissey herself, simultaneously cleansing hindrances and granting immunity to them for the same duration. At level 10, the potency of this shield is enhanced.

In *Pokémon Battle Revolution*, a specific animation signals the conclusion of Safeguard's effect. Curiously, despite their varied in-game mechanics, moves such as Reflect, Light Screen, Barrier, Protect, and Safeguard are frequently portrayed in the animated series as having a unified effect, akin to Protect's game mechanic of shielding the user from the majority of move effects.

Title: Pain Split (move)

Known in Japanese as いたみわけ, which translates to Pain Split, this Normal-type technique made its debut in Generation II. It is characterized as a non-damaging action. Before the advent of Generation IV, Misdreavus held Pain Split as its exclusive signature move; however, other species of Pokémon were capable of acquiring it through the process of breeding. In the games Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it functions as Technical Machine TM202.

This maneuver sums the current Hit Points (HP) of both the initiating Pokémon and its chosen target. Subsequently, this combined total is halved, with any fractional result being rounded downwards. The HP of each participant is then adjusted — either raised or lowered — to match this calculated figure, always respecting their individual maximum HP limits. The resulting HP modification remains unaffected by retaliatory moves like Counter or Bide. Furthermore, Pain Split disregards typical type effectiveness, permitting it to impact even Ghost-type Pokémon. Should the target be shielded by a substitute, Pain Split will prove unsuccessful. Presently, this move circumvents standard accuracy assessments, guaranteeing a hit unless the opposing Pokémon is within the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly.

Exclusively within Generation III, any HP reduction incurred from Pain Split would add to the damage tallied by Bide; however, it continued to ignore other effects such as Counter. In a Pokémon Contest combination, Pain Split serves as a viable second move, bestowing an additional 1 appeal point upon the user if Endure was performed during the preceding turn. Currently, Pain Split no longer factors into Bide's damage accumulation. Furthermore, it participates in a Contest Spectacular combination, granting an extra three appeal points if Endure was deployed just prior. When enhanced by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Pain Split, the user experiences a one-stage increase in its Defense stat. The move also functions by averaging the non-Dynamax HP of both combatants. It then determines the signed difference between this calculated average and each Pokémon's respective non-Dynamax HP, subsequently altering each Pokémon's HP by that precise signed difference. Pain Split focuses on a front-facing Pokémon, ensuring an equitable distribution of HP between itself and the user.

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Pain Split serves as Trevenant's inaugural move. Players acquire it upon reaching level 7 by evolving Branch Poke into Pain Split rather than Horn Leech. The user establishes a connection lasting 6 seconds with a chosen enemy. For the duration of this bond, a fraction of the damage sustained by the user is diverted to the connected foe. This redirection scales with the user's remaining HP: a lower health total translates to a larger percentage of damage being rerouted. Furthermore, should the user possess a lower percentage of HP than the linked adversary, Pain Split will consistently inflict damage and simultaneously restore the user's HP by an amount equivalent to the damage dealt, occurring every 0.5 seconds. The established link will dissipate if the distance between the user and the linked enemy becomes too great. Activating this move also grants a temporary increase to the user's movement speed. Upon reaching level 13, the link's operational duration extends to 8.5 seconds.

The Japanese appellation for Pain Split originates from a sumo wrestling phrase, previously part of the sport's rules, signifying the declaration of a draw due to one participant being too injured to proceed. During Generation III, the in-game status display incorrectly indicated the move possessed 100% accuracy, despite it inherently bypassing all accuracy and evasion calculations. When Shedinja employs Pain Split (for instance, through Mimic), it uniquely reduces the target's HP by half, mirroring the effect of Super Fang.

Title: Sacred Fire (move)

A Fire-type attack, Sacred Fire (Japanese: せいなるほのお, translated as Sacred Flame), made its debut in Generation II as a move that inflicts damage. Before Generation VI, Ho-Oh was exclusively known for its signature use of this move. Beyond simply causing damage, it carries a 50% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon afflicted with a burn. Should the user be in a frozen state, activating Sacred Fire simultaneously defrosts the user and delivers damage to the foe during that very turn. In Pokémon Contests, Sacred Fire serves as a viable second move in a combination, awarding the user an additional 4 appeal points if Sunny Day preceded it in the prior turn. Between Versions 1.0 and 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Sacred Fire was unavailable for selection during combat. However, from Version 1.3.0 onwards, its functionality was restored, mirroring its behavior in earlier generations. This attack both inflicts harm and possesses the potential to inflict a burn upon the target. Exclusively within Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Sacred Fire notably fails to connect with frozen adversaries, a distinction from other Fire-type attacks. Its deployment in Gates to Infinity necessitates a one-turn charging period. As a move in Pokémon GO, Sacred Fire became obtainable starting February 26, 2022. Furthermore, Sacred Fire exists in two distinct variations: Sacred Fire+ and Sacred Fire++. The former, Sacred Fire+, serves as the standard Charged Attack for Apex Shadow Ho-Oh. Upon the purification process, Sacred Fire+ undergoes a transformation into Sacred Fire++. Its description before Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated: "Removes the frozen condition from the user. Has a good chance of leaving the target burned." During the episode 'All Things Bright and Beautifly!', Jessie issued the command for her Seviper to use Sacred Fire; however, as Seviper is incapable of learning this particular move, the instruction could not be carried out.

Title: Magnitude (move)

Magnitude (Japanese: マグニチュード Magnitude) is a Ground-type, damage-dealing attack first introduced in Generation II. Its destructive force fluctuates, determined by a randomly selected "magnitude" value spanning from 4 to 10, with 4 representing the minimum power and 10 delivering the maximum. When Magnitude strikes a Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable stage of Dig, its power against that specific target is doubled. Although sometimes described as the damage itself being doubled rather than the move's power, the ultimate effect is identical. If Grassy Terrain is active, Magnitude will inflict only half its usual damage.

In the *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team* and *Explorers* series, Magnitude deals one of seven fixed damage values: 5, 10, 15, 25, 30, 35, or 40, which are subsequently modified by STAB and type matchups. For *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity*, the damage outputs are fixed at 10, 20, 30, 35, 40, 50, and 60, without any influence from STAB or type matchups. Beginning with *Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon*, Magnitude operates with standard damage calculations, where its power multiplier (0.1, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, or 2.0) depends on the Magnitude level. Across these titles, the move impacts all other Pokémon within the room, with damage scaling according to the Magnitude level. Magnitude consistently inflicts double damage on Pokémon utilizing Dig.

Interestingly, despite its debut in Generation II, no Pokémon introduced in that generation gained the ability to learn Magnitude until Generation IV. Magnitude 10, possessing a base power of 150, holds the distinction of having the highest base power among all Ground-type moves. Conversely, Magnitude 4 registers a base power of 10, making it the weakest Ground-type move and tying it with Constrict for the lowest fixed base power of any move. Following the 2004 Chuetsu earthquake, this move, along with Fissure and Earthquake, has not appeared in the animated series since the unaired episode AG101.

Title: Dynamic Punch (move)

Originating in Generation II, Dynamic Punch (known in Japan as ばくれつパンチ, or Exploding Punch, and previously formatted as DynamicPunch before the release of Pokémon X and Y) is a potent Fighting-type attack that inflicts damage. This move notably served as TM01 during Generation II. Its primary effect is a high 255/256 (~99.6%) chance to confuse the opponent upon impact, in addition to dealing damage. When a Pokémon with the Iron Fist ability utilizes Dynamic Punch, its power output benefits from a 20% increase. In Pokémon Contest events, Dynamic Punch functions as a second-stage combination move, awarding an extra 2 appeal points if either Focus Energy or Mind Reader was employed by the user on the preceding turn.

Shifting to other game iterations: In Pokémon GO, Dynamic Punch operates as a Charged Attack and has been available to players since February 16, 2017. Within Pokémon UNITE, it serves as Machamp's initial move, becoming accessible upon evolving into Machoke at level 5 as an upgrade to Bulk Up, rather than Submission. This attack involves the user leaping to a chosen destination, momentarily stunning any hit adversaries and delivering damage four times consecutively within the landing zone. Furthermore, using Dynamic Punch temporarily elevates the user’s Attack stat by 15% and grants increased movement speed for 6 seconds. These attack and movement speed boosts are further enhanced once the user reaches level 11.

Beyond gameplay mechanics, Dynamic Punch also features in specific lore and quirks: Red's Poliwrath, Poli, was stated to know Dynamic Punch in PSs1, although its actual use of the move was never depicted. Intriguingly, Wooper, despite its lack of visible hands, is capable of learning this move. A peculiar visual anomaly in Generation IV caused the opposing Pokémon's sprite to shift one pixel to the left whenever Dynamic Punch's animation played.

Title: Megahorn (move)

Megahorn (Japanese: メガホーン Megahorn) is a Bug-type attack introduced in Generation II that deals direct damage. This technique functioned as Technical Machine 60 (TM60) in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, and as Technical Record 28 (TR28) within Pokémon Sword and Shield. Until Generation III, it served as Heracross's exclusive signature move. Megahorn delivers damage without any additional side effects. While Giovanni's Nidoking was revealed on the official website to possess knowledge of Megahorn, its use of the move was never depicted. Among all Bug-type attacks, Megahorn boasts the greatest base power, with the sole exceptions of Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. However, a Savage Spin-Out derived from Megahorn achieves the highest power of all Bug-type moves.

During Pokémon Stadium 2, the Trainer Bugsy mistakenly designates this technique as "Megaphone" when deployed by his Heracross, which is featured exclusively in Round 2. This discrepancy likely stems from a translation inaccuracy, given that the Japanese character sequence ホーン (hōn) can translate to either 'horn' or 'phone' (the latter being an alternative representation of フォーン, fōn). The same linguistic ambiguity explains why Korean localizations rendered this move as 'Megaphone' until Generation IX. Intriguingly, an early prototype build of Pokémon Gold and Silver actually featured a move named 'Megaphone,' whose Japanese script, 「メガホーン」, was identical to Megahorn's. This prototype move even had a distinct megaphone visual asset that would display during battle execution. This particular sprite, however, persists as unused data within the final code of Pokémon Gold and Silver. Ultimately, this 'Megaphone' technique was supplanted by Megahorn in the final Generation II releases. The shared Japanese writing (「メガホーン」) for both Megahorn and the original 'Megaphone' implies that Game Freak, at some stage of development, opted to implement the Bug-type Megahorn instead of the 'Megaphone' ability, which had been slated for inclusion in the move repertoires of Pokémon like Hoothoot and Noctowl. This further suggests that the designation "Megahorn" itself emerged from a reinterpretation of the Japanese term 「メガホーン」 [megahōn], initially understood as 'megaphone.'

Title: Dragon Breath (move)

Known in Japanese as りゅうのいぶき (Dragon Breath), this Dragon-type offensive move, introduced during Generation II, was styled as "DragonBreath" before the release of Pokémon X and Y. During Generation II, it held the Technical Machine designation TM24. Beyond its primary function of dealing damage, Dragon Breath possesses a 30% likelihood of afflicting the opposing Pokémon with paralysis. In Pokémon Contests, utilizing Dragon Breath as the initial step in a combination allows Dragon Claw, Dragon Dance, and Dragon Rage to achieve twice their usual appeal points when performed immediately afterward. Conversely, it can conclude a combination, granting the user an additional 1 appeal point if either Dragon Dance or Dragon Rage was executed on the preceding turn. The Muzzled status condition impacts this particular move. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, when a Gigantic Pokémon employs Dragon Breath, it boasts a base PP of 20 and necessitates a turn for charging before execution. Altered Forme Giratina, when summoned from a Poké Ball in both the 3DS and Wii U iterations of Super Smash Bros., dispatches foes from the stage using Dragon Breath. This behavior persists into Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, where Giratina again emerges from a Poké Ball with the same stage-clearing technique. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated it had "a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed." Within Pokémon UNITE, Dragon Breath functions as Dratini's second initial move, propelling a potent gust in a chosen direction. Should this attack strike an adversary, the user's subsequent offensive action transforms into a boosted attack. Upon Dratini's evolution into Dragonair at level 8, this capability is subsequently superseded by either Hyper Beam or Outrage. As highlighted in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer, Dragon Breath holds the distinction of being the sole Dragon-type move capable of inducing paralysis. Notably, its in-game description across Generation II and the Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald titles omits any reference to its secondary paralysis effect. Only Dragon Breath and Tri Attack share this characteristic of undisclosed secondary effects in their descriptions.

Title: Baton Pass (move)

Baton Pass (Japanese: バトンタッチ Baton Touch), a Normal-type move that causes no direct damage, was introduced in Generation II. It was accessible as TR29 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently functions as TM132 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This technique allows the user to switch out, transferring all existing temporary stat stage alterations, both increases and decreases, to the Pokémon that takes its place in battle.

Certain volatile status conditions are also conveyed by Baton Pass. These encompass confusion, being pumped, escape prevention, Ability suppression, seeding, Curse, Substitute, Ingrain, Power Trick, Heal Block, Embargo, Perish Song, Magnet Rise, and Aqua Ring. However, Ability suppression vanishes if the replacement Pokémon's Ability is inherently immune to such an effect. The influence of Telekinesis is also passed on, unless the Pokémon entering the field is Mega Gengar.

Prior to Generation V, if a Pokémon prevented a target from escaping and subsequently used Baton Pass, the target would remain trapped. Beginning with Generation V, this specific trapping effect can no longer be passed by the user. Nevertheless, in all generations, if the Pokémon that *cannot* escape utilizes Baton Pass, the trapping effect *is* successfully transferred.

Baton Pass does not transfer non-volatile status conditions, infatuation, drowsiness (whether from Yawn or G-Max Snooze), the 'center of attention' status (from Spotlight), any changes to type or Ability, a move learned via Mimic, or a transformation (from Transform or Imposter). Furthermore, it fails to convey the effects of Disable (even if the incoming Pokémon knows the disabled move), Imprison, Defense Curl's unique property of doubling the power of Rollout or Ice Ball, or the critical hit rate boosts from G-Max Chi Strike. It also omits passing Minimize's distinctive tiny sprite, or its associated effect of making the user more susceptible to specific moves (such as Stomp, Steamroller, and Heat Crash). Despite the Baton Pass user departing the battle, Pursuit does not gain additional power or priority when targeting it. The move will fail if the user's party lacks any other Pokémon capable of substituting into battle.

From Generation II through IV, the animation consistently displayed a generic Poké Ball. Starting in Generation V, the animation accurately portrays the specific type of Poké Ball that was used to capture the Pokémon. Baton Pass can also contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination; it awards an extra three appeal points if one of the following moves was performed in the preceding turn: Agility, Amnesia, Autotomize, Calm Mind, Cotton Guard, Hone Claws, Nasty Plot, Rock Polish, or Shell Smash. When empowered by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Baton Pass, all of the user's negatively altered stats are reset to normal.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Baton Pass targets all other Pokémon in the immediate room, sequentially swapping places with them (assuming no misses) in a largely random order that prioritizes allies over enemies. Any Pokémon that ends up on a tile they cannot traverse will be automatically warped away. Within Gates to Infinity, the user exchanges positions with a teammate directly in front, and that switched ally receives a random stat boost; when used by an enemy, they will swap spots with another enemy on the current floor. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the user now trades places with a teammate located anywhere on the floor, specifically the one listed first in the team order (or second, if the user itself holds the first position). Prior to Generation V, Shedinja could learn Baton Pass by leveling up only if Nincada's evolution was delayed until level 45, which is the level at which Ninjask acquires this particular move.

Title: Encore (move)

This entry discusses the move Encore. For its counterpart, the negative status condition in the Mystery Dungeon series, consult the Status condition (Mystery Dungeon) page. Known in Japanese as アンコール Encore, this non-damaging Normal-type attack debuted in Generation II. It was designated as TR30 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the identifier TM122 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Encore's primary effect is to temporarily restrict the target to using only the move it last executed.

Throughout all game generations, Encore will not succeed if the target's preceding action was Transform, Mimic, Sketch, Mirror Move, Encore itself, Struggle, or any move with exhausted PP. Similarly, it will fail if the opposing Pokémon has not yet performed any move. Should a Pokémon afflicted by Encore deplete all PP for the move it is locked into, the Encore effect immediately ceases. If the move a Pokémon is forced to use by Encore is simultaneously blocked by Disable, Gravity, Heal Block, Imprison, Taunt, Throat Chop, or Torment, the Pokémon will be compelled to use Struggle. Specifically, a Pokémon under both Encore and Torment's influence will resort to Struggle every other turn. Aroma Veil grants immunity, preventing Encore from affecting Pokémon with this Ability. The move also fails when employed against a Pokémon that has an active Shell Trap awaiting activation. If a Pokémon is directed to use Beak Blast on a turn it originally selected a different move, Beak Blast will be unsuccessful.

Encore can successfully be applied any turn after an opponent used Rest to fall asleep, provided they do not wake up or use Sleep Talk; furthermore, the duration of Encore's effect includes turns the target spends asleep without taking action. It also functions correctly if the opponent employed Rest while already at maximum HP. Even against a target with Truant, Encore remains effective, compelling it to repeat its last used move. Initially, Encore would fail if the target's last move was one that calls other moves (i.e., Sleep Talk and Metronome). If a Pokémon is affected by Encore before it can execute its chosen move for that turn, it will be forced to perform the move Encore has dictated.

In Pokémon Stadium 2, if Roar or Whirlwind were used, and the Pokémon forcibly switched in subsequently used Encore, the move would fail. However, if Roar or Whirlwind were used when only a single opposing Pokémon remained, and that Pokémon used Encore on the following turn, the move would succeed. Later, Encore no longer failed against moves that call other moves, with Mirror Move being the sole exception.

Previously, a Pokémon under Encore's influence could not manually select its move's target; if the chosen move could target a foe, a random opponent would be selected. If a Pokémon becomes affected by Encore before its turn and is subsequently locked into a move different from its initial selection, it will be compelled to use the Encore-imposed move. Even if this forced move has a different priority, the Pokémon will still act according to the priority of the move it initially selected. If the forced move is identical to the one chosen, it will execute on its designated target; otherwise, if the locked-in move can target a single foe, it will target one randomly. When a Pokémon was forced to use Focus Punch on a turn it had chosen another move, the charging message was absent, but the user would still lose focus if it sustained damage that turn.

Encore's duration was originally between 3 and 7 turns. The move's description in Diamond and Pearl erroneously listed the turn count from the prior generation. If a Pokémon was compelled to use Acupressure on a turn it chose another move, it would randomly target either itself or an ally.

More recently, Encore's duration was standardized to exactly 3 turns. It gained the ability to be reflected by Magic Coat, though it would then fail. A Pokémon under Encore's effect can now choose the targets of its moves. When forced to use Focus Punch on a turn it selected a different move, the charging message no longer appears, and the move executes irrespective of whether the Pokémon took damage that turn. If a Pokémon is forced to use Acupressure on a turn it chose another move, it will always target the user, not an ally.

Encore is recognized as part of a Contest Spectacular combination, granting an additional three appeal points if Counter, Destiny Bond, Grudge, King's Shield, Metal Burst, Mirror Coat, or Spite are used on the subsequent turn. Encore's failure conditions were expanded once more, causing it to fail if the target's last move was a move that calls other moves, Dynamax Cannon, or a Z-Move. Nevertheless, a Pokémon affected by Encore can still unleash a Z-Move, even when locked into another move (including on the turn Encore was inflicted).

In scenarios where a Pokémon is forced into a move with increased priority (due to selecting a priority move before a faster Encore activation), abilities such as Psychic Terrain, Queenly Majesty, Dazzling, or Armor Tail will block that move, even if the forced move typically lacks priority. Dark-type Pokémon will also intercept such moves if the priority boost stemmed from Prankster, regardless of whether the replacement move is a status move or not. When transformed into Z-Encore by a Normalium Z, the user's Speed stat receives a one-stage increase.

Encore fails if it targets a Dynamax Pokémon. However, Dynamax Pokémon that were already under Encore's effect (before Dynamaxing) can use moves freely despite the condition. If a Pokémon's Dynamax state has just concluded, and Encore is then used against it before it takes another action, it will be compelled to use the base move that powered up its most recent Max Move.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, Encore inflicts a status condition on the foe, restricting them to only their last used move. This status persists for 6-11 turns or 11 turns depending on the specific game. Beginning with Explorers of Time, Darkness and Sky, this move's effect can be reflected by Magic Coat. In Pokémon Battle Revolution, Encore features an animation when its effect concludes.

Title: Pursuit (move)

Pursuit (Japanese: おいうち, meaning 'Pursuing Attack') made its debut as a damage-dealing Dark-type move in Generation II. It possesses a power of 40.

Should the target Pokémon attempt to switch out during the turn Pursuit is executed, the move's power doubles, and it will immediately be used before it is switched out (including an accuracy check). However, this special effect does not trigger if the target is an ally swapping out, a Pokémon attempting to flee, a Pokémon utilizing Teleport or Baton Pass, or a Pokémon that is being forced to switch.

When the target successfully switches out, Pursuit will no longer perform an accuracy check, instead always striking the target with double power before its departure. Pursuit will then hit any adjacent opponent attempting to switch out (but not more than one per turn), irrespective of its original target. This mechanism bypasses redirection effects like Follow Me.

Should the target be employing U-turn, Pursuit's special effect (its doubled power) will only activate if the user of Pursuit would typically move after the target.

A glitch in the Japanese release of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl allowed a Pokémon holding a Choice item to change its move on the following turn if it used Pursuit and the opponent switched out. This oversight was corrected in localized versions and Pokémon Platinum.

In Pokémon Platinum, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, Pursuit possesses the capability to trigger the "acid rain glitch" by knocking out a target that was in the process of switching out, provided Trick Room, Gravity, Uproar, or any weather type *other than* rain is active.

Similarly, if the target employs Volt Switch or Parting Shot, Pursuit's special effect—specifically its doubled power—will activate solely if the user of Pursuit would normally act slower than the target.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Pursuit operates as a non-damaging move that grants the user the "Counter" status condition. While under 'Counter,' the user reflects all damage incurred from physical moves (including standard A button attacks) back to any adjacent opponents, in an amount equivalent to the damage received. Peculiarly, in Explorers of Time and Darkness, even though moves are designated as Physical or Special in-game, the Counter status will retaliate against damage from attacking moves belonging to types that were categorized as physical *before* the Physical/Special split. This status persists for a duration of 7-12 turns.

Beginning with Gates to Infinity and in subsequent titles, Pursuit functions as a damage-dealing attack, inflicting 50% extra damage when utilized against a target already suffering from a bad status condition.

In Pokémon UNITE, Pursuit stands as Absol's initial move, gained at level 5 by evolving Feint rather than Night Slash. The user dashes in an arc, harming and slowing the first Pokémon struck. If this initial hit connects, the move can be reactivated for a short duration, enabling a second dash that damages all Pokémon it impacts. At level 11, the move provides healing to the user upon a successful hit, with the healing amount doubling if the damage dealt is a critical hit.

Despite being the sole move capable of being executed before a Pokémon switches out, Pursuit retains a priority of 0.

Title: Rapid Spin (move)

Known in Japanese as こうそくスピン, or Rapid Spin, this Normal-type damaging attack first appeared in Generation II. Beyond its direct damage, the move serves to clear various detrimental effects: it removes Leech Seed and any binding effects from the user, in addition to eliminating entry hazards from the user's side of the field. This hazard removal is ineffective, however, if the user faints from taking damage during the move, such as from the ability Rough Skin. Currently, Rapid Spin boasts a power of 50 and, upon inflicting damage, it now also grants the user a one-stage boost to their Speed stat. Furthermore, it nullifies status conditions that impede movement, listing examples like 'bound,' Ingrain, and Fire Spin.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, from *Gates to Infinity* onwards, Rapid Spin also clears any traps situated on the tile directly ahead of the user. Within *Pokémon UNITE*, Rapid Spin functions as the second move for both Blastoise and Tsareena. For Blastoise, it becomes available at level 7 while still a Wartortle, evolving from Skull Bash as an alternative to Surf. When Blastoise executes Rapid Spin, it retreats into its shell to spin vigorously, which concurrently resets the cooldown of its other ability. During this state, the user becomes unstoppable, capable of moving unimpeded across the arena, simultaneously harming and slowing nearby adversaries. Following the move's conclusion, Blastoise's subsequent basic attack receives a boost. The move also alters the operational mechanics of its Basic attacks, Hydro Pump, and Water Spout for its duration. Upon reaching level 13 or higher, Blastoise benefits from a substantial increase in both Defense and Sp. Defense for the entire Rapid Spin period.

As for Tsareena, it learns Rapid Spin from its base form, Bounsweet. Tsareena's execution involves spinning in a chosen direction, damaging any foes it collides with. A successful hit grants a temporary boost to the user's movement speed. However, once Bounsweet evolves into Steenee and then into Tsareena at level 6, Rapid Spin is replaced by a choice between Trop Kick or Grassy Glide. Historically, Rapid Spin stands out as one of the initial Generation II moves to be both conceptualized and named, making an early appearance in *Mewtwo Strikes Back* where Mewtwo's Blastoisetwo utilized it, predating the launch of *Gold and Silver*. The visual representation of Blastoise performing Rapid Spin in *Pokémon UNITE* and *Pokkén Tournament DX* draws a parallel to Ash's Squirtle employing Hydro Pump in *Pokémon the Series*, itself seemingly an homage to Gamera's distinctive spinning flight.

Title: Sweet Scent (move)

For information regarding the e-Reader application, refer to Sweet Scent (e-Reader). Known as あまいかおり (Sweet Scent) in Japanese, Sweet Scent is a Normal-type status move, first appearing in Generation II. During Generation II, it held the TM12 designation. This move decreases the evasion stat of all neighboring adversaries by a single stage. Within Generation II's main series titles, when an NPC opponent (excluding those in the Battle Tower) employs Sweet Scent, there exists a 25% likelihood of the move failing. In Pokémon Contests, Sweet Scent can initiate a combination, granting PoisonPowder, Sleep Powder, and Stun Spore double appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. Subsequently, Sweet Scent's effect was upgraded to decrease the targets' evasion by two stages. When empowered by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Sweet Scent, it concurrently boosts the user's accuracy by one stage.

Employed as an overworld move, Sweet Scent will summon wild Pokémon, provided the player occupies an area where such encounters are naturally possible. This move instigates a wild encounter even if a Repel item's effect is active. Exclusively in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, deploying Sweet Scent (or Honey) within a shop or Poké Mart causes the descriptions of items for sale to disappear until the player navigates to the "SELL" menu or exits the establishment. This phenomenon is recognized as the Poké Mart text glitch. Should Horde Encounters be available in the current location, Sweet Scent will trigger one; otherwise, a standard wild encounter will commence. Specifically in Pokémon X and Y, Sweet Scent's field usage is restricted to clear weather conditions. However, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, weather conditions do not impact Sweet Scent's usability. Its utility as an outside-of-battle move has been removed in many titles. For instance, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Sweet Scent cannot be employed outside of combat, though it is once again available for field use in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, it reduces the evasiveness of all adversaries within the same chamber by one stage. Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, Sweet Scent additionally possesses a chance to deplete the Belly stat of affected Pokémon by 5, alongside its primary effect. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-category moves, including Sweet Scent, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and unobtainable for player-caught Pokémon. Opponent Spritzee were notably observed utilizing Sweet Scent.

Upon emerging from a Poké Ball, Bellossom executes Sweet Scent, commencing a dance and releasing a powdery cloud. Adversaries making contact with this cloud are afflicted with sleep. These affected targets remain immobilized until they sustain damage or the cloud dissipates. Bellossom's stage presence endures for an extended duration compared to other Pokémon. Notwithstanding its name, this move remains effective in attracting Pokémon that do not favor sweet tastes. Every Pokémon capable of possessing the Honey Gather ability is able to learn Sweet Scent. In the animated series, Sweet Scent was the inaugural Generation II move to make an appearance. The Pokémon Adventures manga featured Sweet Scent before its introduction in the video games. While both the animated series and the Pokémon Adventures manga consistently illustrate Sweet Scent as functioning via a powder, it has never been categorized as a powder move within the game mechanics.

Title: Iron Tail (move)

Known in Japanese as アイアンテール (Iron Tail), this Steel-type attack, which inflicts damage, first appeared in Generation II. It was available as Technical Machine TM23 across Generations II, III, and IV, and also in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was designated as TM19. Beyond its primary function of dealing damage, Iron Tail possesses a 30% probability of decreasing the opponent's Defense stat by a single stage. The Power Points (PP) for Iron Tail have been reduced to 5. Furthermore, it carries the potential to diminish an adversary's defensive capabilities. This attack causes damage. Specifically, it can reduce the target's Defense by one stage.

In PokéPark Wii: Pikachu's Adventure, Pikachu has the capability to learn Iron Tail. Initially, Pikachu lacks this move and must acquire it from Primeape, a process that costs 350 Berries. Unlike abilities such as Dash and Thunderbolt, this particular move is restricted to in-battle use. Execution involves shaking the Wii Remote during combat. Its power can be enhanced by revisiting Primeape and providing additional Berries.

Within Pokémon GO, Iron Tail functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible to players since February 16, 2017. Sygna Suit Giovanni & Nidoking possess the passive skill "Giovanni's Cunning," which guarantees Iron Tail will always connect when they use it. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its description stated: "Has a moderate chance lowering the target's Defense." The description for its default effects, preceding Version 2.20.0 (dated April 27, 2022), read: "Become Mega Steelix until the end of battle. The more the user's Defense is raised, the greater the power of this move." For its 6★ EX effect, before Version 2.18.5 (effective March 15, 2022), the description was: "The stat increase after a sync move is doubled once per battle." Contrary to some other sync moves, Iron Tail did not support 6★ EX upgrades from its initial release on May 21, 2020, for a period of 195 days, finally gaining this capability on December 2, 2020.

In the manga "Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~," specifically in the chapter "Mashing Makuhita," Sapphire instructed her Lairon, Rono, to employ Iron Tail; however, the attack's execution was not depicted. Although the portrayal of Iron Tail often suggests a requirement for a tail, Pokémon that shed their tails through evolution (e.g., Primeape) or those without a tail initially who develop one upon evolving (e.g., Nidoran♀) are nonetheless capable of learning and utilizing this move. This concept is underscored in PokéPark Wii: Pikachu's Adventure, where a Primeape, acting as the Iron Tail tutor, explicitly states its ability to execute the move despite lacking a physical tail.

Title: Metal Claw (move)

Debuting in Generation II, Metal Claw (Japanese: メタルクロー) is a damage-dealing Steel-type move. It carries the designation TM031 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This attack inflicts damage and provides a 10% chance of elevating the user's Attack stat by one stage. As the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, Metal Claw awards 4 bonus appeal points if Metal Sound was executed in the preceding turn. It delivers an offensive strike and may also raise the user's Attack by one stage. Prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its description read: "Has a very small chance of raising the user's Attack."

In Pokémon UNITE, Metal Claw serves as Duraludon's second move. The user projects sharp claws forward, damaging hit enemies and temporarily reducing their movement speed. After performing this move, the user's next basic attack becomes a boosted attack. Upon reaching level 7, this move is superseded by either Dragon Tail or Stealth Rock.

During Getting the Drop on Gallade I, a Zangoose participating in the Battle Castle was revealed to know Metal Claw, but was never seen using the move. In FireRed and LeafGreen, Charmander's evolutionary line could learn Metal Claw by leveling up. However, this method became unavailable in later generations, except through breeding. This change might have been implemented to give players who select Charmander as their first partner an advantage over the initial Gym. Furthermore, in Generation IX, Charmander can learn Metal Claw via TM, whereas Charmeleon and Charizard cannot. Metal Claw holds the distinction of being the sole move with a base PP of 35 that was not introduced in Generation I.

Title: Vital Throw (move)

A Fighting-type move that inflicts damage, Vital Throw (Japanese: あてみなげ 'Strike Throw') first appeared in Generation II. Until Generation III, this technique was exclusive to the evolutionary lineage of Machop. Although Vital Throw delivers damage, its inherently reduced priority means the Pokémon employing it will act subsequent to foes utilizing moves of standard priority. Crucially, it still precedes other moves categorized as decreased priority, including examples like Focus Punch and Avalanche. It boasts perfect accuracy, inherently bypassing typical hit rate computations, unless the target is in a temporarily invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly.

In Pokémon Contests, Vital Throw functions as the latter part of a combination; if Fake Out was performed in the preceding turn, the user accrues an additional 3 appeal points. Additionally, in a Contest Spectacular combination, three bonus appeal points are awarded if Entrainment, Hold Hands, or Play Nice preceded Vital Throw.

Within the Rescue Team series, Vital Throw operates as a status-altering technique. For a duration of 18 turns, the user becomes afflicted with the 'Vital Throw' status condition. Should an adjacent Pokémon directly inflict damage upon a Pokémon with the Vital Throw status, the attacker is flung, commonly to the opposite side of the current area. This forced movement occurs regardless of whether the attacker used a multi-target move (e.g., Night Shade) or if the Vital Throw user is paralyzed. Conversely, within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, paralysis or being hit by a multi-target move prevents the user from throwing the aggressor. Beginning with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Vital Throw reverts to a standard damaging attack.

A Generation III oversight showed 100% accuracy on the status screen, though the move fundamentally bypasses all accuracy and evasion checks. Its Japanese name, Atemi-nage, draws inspiration from judo's atemi waza (striking techniques) and nage waza (throwing techniques). The animated depiction mirrors an ura nage, a maneuver mistakenly identified as a 'side slam' in American wrestling.

Title: Morning Sun (move)

Debuting in Generation II, Morning Sun (Japanese: あさのひざし, meaning "Morning Sunlight") is a Normal-type move that inflicts no damage. For Generation II, it served as Espeon's exclusive signature move. The amount of HP Morning Sun restores to its user is determined by the current weather and the time of day. Specifically, in clear weather, both day and night, it recovers ¼ of the user's total HP. Harsh sunlight causes it to restore ½ of total HP, whereas other weather types yield ⅛ of total HP recovery, with all results rounded down. A unique property of Morning Sun is that, outside of link battles, its HP restoration is doubled when executed during the morning hours.

Under different circumstances, Morning Sun's HP restoration to the user is determined by the weather conditions present in the battle. Specifically, in conditions of no weather or strong winds, it grants ½ total HP recovery. Harsh sunlight leads to ⅔ total HP being restored, and all other weather types result in ¼ total HP recovery, each instance rounded down.

As part of a Pokémon Contest combination, Morning Sun functions as a valid second move, granting the user an additional 1 appeal point if Sunny Day preceded it. Similarly, within a Contest Spectacular combination, performing Sunny Day in the prior turn will result in an extra three appeal points for Morning Sun.

Empowering Morning Sun with a Normalium Z to become Z-Morning Sun causes all the user's debuffed stats to be restored to their original levels. In certain contexts, Morning Sun provides HP recovery to both the user and their allied Pokémon. Its healing mechanism was previously a set HP value, not a percentage, up until the release of Gates to Infinity. Subsequently, from Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, Morning Sun's healing reach is restricted to the user and teammates located in the same room, no longer impacting an entire floor.

Title: Synthesis (move)

First appearing in Generation II, Synthesis (Japanese: こうごうせい Photosynthesis) is a Grass-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move regenerates the user's HP, with the amount varying based on prevailing weather conditions and the time of day. During the morning and night, it recovers ¼ of the user's total HP in the absence of weather effects, ½ in harsh sunlight, and ⅛ under other weather conditions, always rounded down. Notably, during the daytime, and outside of link battles, Synthesis doubles the amount of HP recovered. Furthermore, Synthesis again recovers the user's current HP based on the weather in the battle. Without any active weather or during strong winds, it restores ½ of total HP; during harsh sunlight, it recovers ⅔; and in any other weather scenario, it provides ¼ of total HP, with all calculations rounded down.

In Pokémon Contests, Synthesis functions as the second step in a combination, granting the user a single bonus appeal point if Sunny Day was executed in the preceding turn. It also contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, awarding an additional three appeal points if Sunny Day had been performed in the turn before. When amplified by a Grassium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Synthesis, it completely clears all of the user's decreased stats.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Synthesis also restores HP to its user. Before the release of Gates to Infinity, this move offered a fixed quantity of HP recovery. However, starting with Gates to Infinity and in subsequent titles, Synthesis heals HP based on a percentage of the user's max HP. For Pokémon Rumble Rush, Synthesis, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and was not acquirable by any captured Pokémon. Boss Lilligant notably employed Synthesis as one of its abilities. In Pokémon UNITE, Synthesis serves as Eldegoss's secondary move, where it harnesses sunlight to restore HP to both itself and nearby teammates. Upon reaching level 6, this particular move is swapped out for either Cotton Guard or Cotton Spore.

Title: Moonlight (move)

For the Pokémon: Twilight Wings episode sharing this name, refer to TW06. Known in Japanese as つきのひかり (Moonlight), this Generation II introduction is a non-damaging move of the Fairy type. Before Generation VI, it functioned as a Normal-type attack. The HP replenished by Moonlight for its user depends on prevailing weather conditions and the time of day. During morning and daytime, with no active weather, it recovers one-quarter of the user's total HP; in harsh sunlight, it restores one-half; and under other weather effects, it heals one-eighth, always rounded down. When not in link battles, Moonlight's HP recovery doubles if used at night. In another context, Moonlight's HP recovery is determined by the in-battle weather: it restores one-half of total HP during no weather, two-thirds during harsh sunlight, and one-quarter under other weather conditions, always rounded down. As a Pokémon Contest combination move, Moonlight serves as the second component, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point if Sunny Day preceded it. Currently, Moonlight is categorized as a Fairy-type move. When utilized amidst strong winds, it recovers one-half of the user's total HP, rounded down. For Contest Spectacular combinations, Moonlight grants three additional appeal points if Sunny Day was executed in the preceding turn. Activating its Z-Move variant, Z-Moonlight, via a Fairium Z crystal, causes all of the user's diminished stats to be reset. The move replenishes HP for both the user and its allies. Before the game Gates to Infinity, Moonlight provided a fixed amount of healing instead of a percentage-based restoration. Starting with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Moonlight's effect is confined to the user and teammates within the same room, no longer spanning the entire floor. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Moonlight, alongside all other status moves, was exclusive to opposing Pokémon and unobtainable for player-caught Pokémon. Boss versions of Munna, Musharna, and Lunala have been observed utilizing Moonlight. Among the four Generation II moves whose types were altered in a subsequent generation, Moonlight stands with Charm, Curse, and Sweet Kiss. With the exception of Curse, the remaining moves transitioned from Normal-type to Fairy-type in Generation VI.

Title: Hidden Power (move)

Known in Japanese as めざめるパワー (Awakening Power), Hidden Power is a damaging attack of the Normal type, first appearing in Generation II. Despite its visual classification as Normal-type, the actual elemental typing of Hidden Power is variable. This move was designated as TM10 from Generation II up through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Beginning with Generation VIII, Unown exclusively possesses Hidden Power as its signature technique. Its type can encompass any element except Normal or Fairy, with this determination stemming from the user's individual values (IVs). However, within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, its type dynamically shifts to become super effective against the opponent. A same-type attack bonus is granted to Hidden Power if its determined type aligns with the user's inherent type. Between Generation III and V, the move's power level was also dictated by the user's IVs. As of Generation VI, its base power became standardized at 60. During Generations II and III, its classification as physical or special damage corresponded to its assigned type. Because it is consistently regarded as a Normal-type move for certain interactions, it exclusively triggers Counter and is never capable of activating Mirror Coat. From Generation IV onward, Hidden Power is invariably a special attack. Since its actual type is never Normal, Hidden Power remains immune to the effects of abilities such as Pixilate, Refrigerate, Aerilate, and the move Ion Deluge. Should the user be affected by Electrify, Hidden Power's type will instantly shift to Electric. For Z-Move mechanics, Hidden Power is consistently recognized as Normal-type, allowing it to be upgraded to Breakneck Blitz when utilizing a Normalium Z. Its type can also adjust to gain super effectiveness against the opposing Pokémon, with priority given to any double weaknesses of foes with dual types. When choosing an attack, Hidden Power's intended type for the current turn against the chosen opponent is indicated. In scenarios where several types prove equally effective, Hidden Power's type is randomly selected from among those options. This calculation process occurs every turn, meaning that if multiple valid types exist, Hidden Power's type might fluctuate between them during successive turns. In certain dungeon exploration games, Hidden Power inflicts damage, and both its type and base power will be re-determined each time the Pokémon enters a new dungeon. Within the Rescue Team series, its type is chosen with approximate equal probability from 17 available types (the game initially attempts to select from all 18 types, including 'typeless', re-attempting if 'typeless' is chosen; should this re-attempt occur 100 consecutive times, Hidden Power will default to Fire-type). Its base power in this series is randomly selected with equal likelihood from the following specific values: 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 13, 15, and 17. When viewed in the description menu, Hidden Power appears as a typeless move. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, Hidden Power's power value becomes fixed.

Within Pokémon GO, Hidden Power functions as a Fast Attack, introduced and accessible since February 16, 2017. Though classified as Normal-type in the game's underlying data, its true elemental type differs for each individual Pokémon and can be observed on that Pokémon's status screen. Similar to the core series, Hidden Power cannot manifest as Normal or Fairy types; nevertheless, its type in Pokémon GO is not influenced by IVs, but rather assigned randomly. Should two players capture the identical wild Pokémon possessing Hidden Power, the specific Hidden Power type assigned to it might still vary for each player. Upon a Pokémon's evolution, its Hidden Power type undergoes a random re-roll. The type of Hidden Power cannot be altered via a Fast TM or an Elite Fast TM. When a player employs an Elite Fast TM on a Pokémon capable of learning Hidden Power, the move selection screen will reveal its specific Hidden Power type. When confronting a Pokémon with Hidden Power in a Raid Battle or a Team GO Rocket Battle, the move's type will invariably be Fighting. Yet, if the player successfully captures that same Pokémon, its Hidden Power type will be randomly re-rolled. Dynamax Pokémon utilizing Hidden Power as their Fast Attack consistently unleash Max Strike as their offensive Max Move, irrespective of Hidden Power's specific type. Distinct from other moves, Hidden Power's type effectiveness is not verbally indicated during Trainer Battles.

In the game Pokémon Masters EX, Hidden Power's type is universally set to Psychic. For Toy Pokémon, Hidden Power is randomly allocated any of the 18 available types whenever it appears among their moves. This assigned type remains constant for the duration that the Pokémon retains the move. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Hidden Power is exclusively usable by opposing Unown and is unobtainable by the player. The type of Hidden Power in this game varies for each different opposing Unown encountered.

In the English dub episode "A Three Team Scheme!", Keanu instructed his Nincada to deploy Hidden Power; however, in the original Japanese version, Nincada was commanded to use Secret Power. A preview for the unreleased episode AG101 featured Chōta's Barboach employing Hidden Power. Although the official website indicated that several wild Unown possessed Hidden Power, they were never depicted using the attack. Unown's sole learnable move is Hidden Power, which it acquires at level 1; remarkably, it could not obtain this move via TM until Generation VI. Nonetheless, this distinction has no practical impact, apart from scenarios involving illegally adding other moves or when filtering by TM/HM compatibility, as Unown intrinsically knows Hidden Power and therefore cannot learn it through a TM.

Before Generation IV, Hidden Power and Weather Ball stood as the unique moves capable of fluctuating between physical and special categories, a consequence of damage classification being type-dependent. In Generation II, owing to gender being an IV-derived characteristic, the Hidden Power of a female Pokémon from a species having a seven male to one female ratio is restricted to a physical type. The specific individual value distributions necessary for Shininess in Generation II meant that Shiny Pokémon were limited to deploying a Grass- or Dragon-type Hidden Power, with a base power of either 49 or 69. Because of the Roaming Pokémon IV bug, a wandering Pokémon captured in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, FireRed, or LeafGreen will invariably possess a Fighting-type Hidden Power, with a power of 30 or 31. Pokémon belonging to the No Eggs Discovered Egg Group, if acquired through capture or as a gift in Pokémon X and Y, cannot possibly have a Fighting-type Hidden Power. This specific limitation applies exclusively to Legendary Pokémon, Mythical Pokémon, and Ultra Beasts within the No Eggs Discovered Egg Group in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire and the Generation VII titles. The reason for this is that these Pokémon are guaranteed to possess a minimum of three IVs at a value of 31, while a Fighting-type Hidden Power necessitates at least four IVs that are even numbers.

Even though Hidden Power is unusable in battles within Sword and Shield, the mechanism for determining its type remains intact. Evidence of this is visible when engaging in combat with a Pokémon that has the move, then accessing the move selection screen, where Hidden Power's type effectiveness will be shown if the opponent has a Pokédex entry. Pokémon Masters EX stands as the sole game where Hidden Power is exclusively of a single type, Psychic.

Title: Cross Chop (move)

This damage-dealing Fighting-type move, known in Japanese as クロスチョップ (Cross Chop), was introduced in Generation II. Cross Chop inflicts damage and inherently features an elevated critical hit ratio. In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, its critical hit rate is 50% in the Rescue Team series, dropping to 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series.

In Pokémon Contests, Cross Chop functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the user 3 bonus appeal points if Focus Energy was performed during the previous turn.

Cross Chop is also featured as one of Machamp's specialized moves, accessible when Machamp is under the influence of a Synergy Burst or during the burst itself, where it substitutes Bulk Up. When activated, Machamp executes a powerful forward lunge, its arms crossed.

For Incineroar, Cross Chop serves as its up special move. During its execution, Incineroar leaps upward before descending diagonally, engulfed in flames, with its arms crossed. This ability functions as a recovery tool for returning to the stage; however, if the attack fails to connect, it can inadvertently cause Incineroar to fall off the stage.

In Pokémon UNITE, Cross Chop is Machamp's initial move, becoming available upon reaching level 7 as Machoke by upgrading Karate Chop instead of Close Combat. This ability causes the user to dash forward, damaging any enemies hit. Cross Chop in UNITE possesses an increased critical hit rate and is capable of dealing critical strikes. At level 13, the move further enhances basic attacks by boosting their Attack stat by 2, a bonus that can accumulate up to 40 times.

Title: Twister (move)

The Dragon-type attack known as Twister (たつまき in Japanese) made its debut in Generation II, serving as a damage-dealing technique. When used, this move deals damage and possesses a 20% probability of making every affected Pokémon flinch. It is capable of striking a Pokémon even during the semi-invulnerable phases of moves like Fly and Bounce, applying twice the usual damage in such instances. In contests involving multiple Pokémon, Twister will impact all neighboring opposing combatants. If Twister connects with a Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable state of Fly or Bounce, its power rating is effectively doubled instead of the final damage calculation, yielding an almost identical outcome. Furthermore, this move now also targets Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable phases of Fly, Bounce, and Sky Drop. When it successfully strikes such a Pokémon, it inflicts twice the usual damage, contrasting with earlier mechanics that doubled the move's power. In battles where Berry trees are present in the background, Twister has the effect of dislodging their Berries. Unlike some other techniques, Twister focuses on a single Pokémon, similar to all other moves within this particular game. A consequence of this attack is a reduction in the target's action speed. This attack delivers damage and carries the possibility of causing its recipient to flinch. Opponents airborne from moves like Fly or Bounce are susceptible to this technique, which then applies double the standard damage. Any potential status alterations originating from Twister are determined separately for every Pokémon it affects. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, the move's description mentioned it had a minor possibility of causing the target to flinch. Within the game Pokémon UNITE, Twister functions as Dratini's initial move, preceding its evolution into Dragonite. It manifests as a swirling vortex, unleashed in a chosen direction, inflicting harm upon adversaries it connects with and temporarily hindering their movement pace. Upon Dratini's evolution into Dragonair at level 5, Twister is superseded by a choice of either Dragon Dance or Extreme Speed. In the narrative of The True Road to Becoming the Strongest!!, Blue commanded his Pidgeot to execute Twister, though the Pokémon was bested in battle before the move could be performed. The Japanese appellation for Twister, たつまき (竜巻) Tatsumaki, directly translates to "dragon spiral," reflecting a traditional belief that dragons coil themselves into a spiraling motion to generate actual twisters. With a base power of 40, Twister possesses the second lowest power among all Dragon-type moves, trailing only Scale Shot. The Lake of Outrage in the French localization derives its name from this specific attack.

Title: Rain Dance (move)

This entry details the Pokémon move Rain Dance. For information regarding the Field Move, please refer to Field Move (Ranger). Designated あまごい (Rain Prayer) in Japanese, Rain Dance is a non-offensive Water-type technique that first appeared in Generation II. It was assigned TM18 across a range of titles, from Generation II through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

When activated, this move initiates a downpour across the battle arena, persisting for a duration of five turns. This weather event supersedes any pre-existing weather conditions, and notably, it is permissible to use Rain Dance even if it is already raining. Under the influence of Rain Dance, Water-type attacks inflict 50% more base damage, while Solar Beam and Fire-type attacks have their base damage reduced by 50%. Furthermore, the HP restoration from Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight is halved compared to clear weather. Thunder's accuracy and evasion checks are bypassed when Rain Dance is active. Should the user hold a Damp Rock upon deploying Rain Dance, the precipitation will persist for an extended duration of eight turns, instead of the standard five.

However, Rain Dance will not succeed if rain is already present. Similarly, Hurricane disregards accuracy and evasion parameters during Rain Dance's effect. Exclusively in Diamond and Pearl, Thunder possesses a 30% likelihood of penetrating Protect and Detect while Rain Dance is active. Various Abilities interact with rainfall: a Pokémon possessing Rain Dish recovers 1/16 of its maximum HP each turn; Swift Swim doubles a Pokémon's Speed in the rain; the Dry Skin Ability restores ⅛ of a Pokémon's maximum HP at the end of each turn; and Hydration eliminates a Pokémon's status conditions at the close of each turn. Moonlight, Synthesis, and Morning Sun will only restore ¼ of the user's total HP. Moreover, a Pokémon with the Forecast Ability will assume the Water type, and Weather Ball transforms into a Water-type attack.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Rain Dance can initiate a sequence, resulting in double the standard appeal for subsequent use of Bubble, BubbleBeam, Clamp, Crabhammer, Dive, Hydro Cannon, Hydro Pump, Muddy Water, Octazooka, Surf, Thunder, Water Gun, Water Pulse, Water Sport, Water Spout, Waterfall, Weather Ball, Whirlpool, and Withdraw on the following turn. It additionally features in Contest Spectacular combinations, granting an additional three appeal points to Hurricane, Soak, Thunder, Water Sport, and Weather Ball when used immediately afterward.

The rain summoned by Rain Dance does not trigger Sliggoo's evolution into Goodra. Should extreme weather conditions such as heavy rain, extremely harsh sunlight, or strong winds already exist, Rain Dance will be unsuccessful. When enhanced by a Waterium Z into Z-Rain Dance, the user's Speed stat receives a one-stage boost. Its ability to alter overworld weather is no longer present. In specific contexts, Rain Dance transforms the current floor's weather to rain for durations of 18 turns, the entire floor, or 66 turns. During TA14, Soro directed Bronzong to execute Rain Dance, though only the latter portion of the move was depicted.

Before its introduction to the main series games in Generation II, this technique existed as Blastoise's Pokémon Power (あまごい / Rain Prayer in Japanese, Rain Dance in English) within the TCG's Base Set. During Generation II, Rain Dance stood as the sole Water-type move available through TM instruction. In Generation III, it initiated a Contest combination with 19 potential follow-up moves, marking it as the most versatile combination starter.

Until Generation VIII, Bronzong was unique among Pokémon that learned Rain Dance via leveling up, in that it lacked any moves or Abilities that gained advantage from rain. This peculiarity might stem from its Pokédex entries, many of which highlight Bronzong's inherent capability to conjure rain clouds. However, with Weather Ball's inclusion in its movepool in Generation VIII, this observation is no longer valid. The move's original Japanese designation bears no connection to dancing, confirming it is not classified as a dance move. Consequently, Dancer does not influence Rain Dance, and Tierno does not ask to see a Pokémon knowing this move in Pokémon X and Y. Even if it were categorized as a dance move, an attempt to activate it through Dancer would result in failure if rain was already present, given Rain Dance's inherent inability to function under existing rainfall. Rain Dance draws its inspiration from amagoi, Shinto prayers and ceremonies intended to invoke rain. Its English nomenclature is derived from certain amagoi variations that incorporate dance rituals.

Title: Sunny Day (move)

Known in Japanese as にほんばれ (Clear Sky), Sunny Day is a non-damaging Fire-type move that debuted in Generation II. It was designated as TM11 from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When this move is employed, bright sunlight illuminates the battlefield. This environmental alteration endures for 5 turns, a period during which Pokémon cannot be frozen (though this effect does not influence the thawing time of already-frozen Pokémon). It dispels any other prevailing weather condition and can even be activated if the field is already sunny.

While Sunny Day is active, the base power of Fire-type moves receives a 50% boost, whereas the base power of Water-type moves is diminished by 50%. Additionally, the healing moves Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight recover twice the HP compared to clear conditions, Solar Beam will execute instantly without a charge turn, and Thunder's accuracy is reduced to 50%. A subsequent modification dictates that Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight will instead recover ⅔ of the user's maximum HP under harsh sunlight. Furthermore, Castform (due to its Forecast Ability) and the move Weather Ball transform into Fire-type. If the user holds a Heat Rock when Sunny Day is used, the duration of the sunlight extends from 5 to 8 turns.

Moreover, the following Abilities manifest specific effects while the sun is shining:
*   Pokémon with the Chlorophyll Ability experience a doubling of their Speed.
*   Those with the Dry Skin Ability forfeit ⅛ of their maximum HP at the end of each turn.
*   Pokémon possessing the Flower Gift Ability enhance their team's Attack and Special Defense by 50%. Cherrim will adopt its Sunshine Form, a transformation not inherently linked to Flower Gift in Generation IV.
*   Pokémon featuring the Solar Power Ability gain a 50% increase in their Special Attack but simultaneously lose ⅛ of their HP each turn.
*   Individuals with the Leaf Guard Ability become immune to non-volatile status conditions.

Sunny Day can serve as the initiating move in a Pokémon Contest combination, causing Blast Burn, Blaze Kick, Ember, Eruption, Fire Blast, Fire Punch, Fire Spin, Flame Wheel, Flamethrower, Heat Wave, Moonlight, Morning Sun, Overheat, Sacred Fire, SolarBeam, Synthesis, Weather Ball, and Will-O-Wisp to earn double their standard appeal if performed on the subsequent turn. Growth now elevates the user's Attack and Special Attack by two stages instead of just one. Hurricane's accuracy also diminishes, similar to Thunder. The Ability Harvest gains a guaranteed success rate in restoring a Berry. Sunny Day will fail to activate if the conditions are heavy rain, extremely harsh sunlight, or strong winds. During Sunny Day's effect, Solar Blade also requires no charge turn.

Sunny Day can also be incorporated into a Contest Spectacular combination, enabling certain moves (Growth, Moonlight, Morning Sun, Solar Beam, Synthesis, and Weather Ball) to provide an extra three appeal points when used on the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Firium Z into Z-Sunny Day, the user's Speed stat is raised by one stage. In the overworld, Sunny Day is no longer capable of overriding existing weather. Within specific contexts, Sunny Day converts the current floor's weather to sunny for 18 turns / the entire floor / 66 turns. Courtney's Ninetales was officially revealed online to know Sunny Day, although she was never shown employing the move. In Pokémon Sun and Moon, Morelull could learn Sunny Day via TM, but Shiinotic could not; this inconsistency was rectified in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Crunch (move)

Known in Japanese as かみくだく (Crunch), this Dark-type attack delivers damage and first appeared in Generation II. Beyond inflicting harm, Crunch possesses a one-in-five probability (20%) of decreasing the opponent's Special Defense stat by a single stage. In Pokémon Contests, Crunch serves as a viable second move in a combination, awarding its user an extra appeal point if Scary Face was executed immediately beforehand. Currently categorized as a physical attack, Crunch consequently carries a 20% likelihood of reducing the foe's Defense stat by one stage. The move's PP count has been lowered to 10, and it maintains a 20% probability of diminishing the target's defensive stats. It causes damage and carries the potential to reduce an adversary's Defense by a single stage (an effect that previously targeted Sp. Defense in the Rescue Team series). The Muzzled status condition impacts this particular move.

Within Pokémon GO, Crunch functions as a Charged Attack, accessible to players since February 16, 2017. Before Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated: "Has a small chance of lowering the target's Defense." The 6★ EX effect description, before Version 2.18.5 (issued on March 15, 2022), read: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents."

During the events of Trial on a Golden Scale!, Nito commanded his Krokorok, along with his subordinates' Zweilous and Eelektross, to employ Crunch; however, Danika's Urshifu vanquished them all before the attack could be executed. Nessa's Drednaw was noted to possess knowledge of Crunch in Early-Evening Waves, though its actual usage occurred off-screen. Emerald's Pokédex disclosed that a Mawile, rented by Noland from the Battle Factory in Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming, was capable of using Crunch.

Title: Mirror Coat (move)

This Psychic-type attack, known in Japan as ミラーコート (Mirror Coat), deals damage and first appeared in Generation II. Should the user have sustained damage (exceeding zero) from a special move immediately preceding Mirror Coat's activation, it retaliates against the foe, inflicting double the damage originally received. Conversely, if the adversary employs a physical technique or performs no action, Mirror Coat will be unsuccessful. While Dark-type Pokémon are impervious to its effects, Mirror Coat's intrinsic type otherwise has no bearing on the damage inflicted upon its target.

Mirror Coat inherently possesses a reduced priority. In a turn where the opponent does not also utilize a decreased priority move, Mirror Coat executes last, irrespective of either Pokémon's speed stat. However, if the adversary similarly employs a diminished priority move in that same turn, standard speed-based calculations dictate the action order.
