For multi-hit attacks, Mirror Coat only factors in the final strike's damage. It fails to counter moves absorbed by a Substitute. Furthermore, Beat Up cannot trigger Mirror Coat, despite Dark-type moves being categorized as special in Generations II and III.

During Double Battles, Mirror Coat strikes the last opposing Pokémon that inflicted special damage upon its user. It is unable to impact allied Pokémon. In Pokémon Contests, Mirror Coat functions as a secondary move in a combination, awarding the user an additional 2 appeal points if Taunt was previously employed. Mirror Move is no longer capable of replicating Mirror Coat. Given that Hidden Power is classified as a special move irrespective of its actual type, it can now successfully activate Mirror Coat. Within Triple Battles, Mirror Coat can target non-adjacent foes, provided such a foe was the most recent attacker to inflict damage on the user.

Mirror Coat proves effective even against Psystrike, Psyshock, and Secret Sword, despite these moves using the target's Defense stat for damage calculation. Should a special attack inflict precisely 0 damage upon the Pokémon activating Mirror Coat, Mirror Coat itself transforms into a special move possessing 1 base power. For Contest Spectaculars, Mirror Coat contributes to a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if Encore, Taunt, or Torment was utilized on the preceding turn.

The use of Mirror Coat confers the Mirror Coat status condition upon its user. This status enables the user to reflect any special move damage received back to all adjacent opponents, in an amount equivalent to the damage sustained. Curiously, in Explorers of Time and Darkness, despite moves having explicit Physical or Special classifications, Mirror Coat specifically returns damage from damaging moves of types historically considered special before the Physical/Special split. This status persists for 7-12 / 6 turns.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Mirror Coat was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. It was notably employed by Boss Wobbuffet and Wynaut.

An older description, preceding Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated that the user would attack upon taking a special hit while it was in this counterattacking posture, subsequently leaving it. The move's power increased proportionally to the damage taken from the opponent's attack. A subsequent description, before Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022), clarified that the move's power scaled with HP loss in the counterattacking posture, with greater HP loss leading to a higher power multiplier, which would reset upon exiting the posture.

In the episode "Mixing It Up with Magcargo," Mirror Coat was utilized by an unidentified Pokémon under the ownership of Team Aqua. During Generations II and III, Mirror Coat was unable to reflect Hidden Power, irrespective of Hidden Power's actual type. This was due to Hidden Power being hard-coded by the game as Normal-type, causing Mirror Coat to invariably fail against it (even for Special Hidden Power types like Ice or Grass), whereas Counter would consistently reflect it.

Title: Psych Up (move)

Hailing from Generation II, the Normal-type maneuver known as Psych Up (じこあんじ Self-suggest in Japanese) delivers no direct damage. This move serves as TM77 across a range of titles, specifically from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappearing in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When executed, Psych Up first nullifies any existing stat modifications on the user, then duplicates all of the target's current stat stages – covering Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, Speed, accuracy, and evasion – applying them to the user. An attempt to use Psych Up proves unsuccessful if the target possesses no altered stat stages (i.e., all are at 0). This technique circumvents accuracy checks, ensuring it always connects, unless the chosen opponent is currently within the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Both Protect and Detect offer no defense against its effects. The condition for Psych Up to fail when all of the target's stat stages are 0 has since been removed. Should an opponent employ Snatch, Psych Up can be appropriated, subsequently redirecting its effect back to the Pokémon from which it was stolen (specifically, the most recent such instance). Furthermore, the move now replicates the target's critical hit ratio. For instance, if the target benefits from Focus Energy, the user will acquire this advantage; conversely, if the user possesses Focus Energy's effect but the target does not, the user will then forfeit that effect. While Spiky Shield and Baneful Bunker do not hinder Psych Up, it will nonetheless fail if the target is safeguarded by Crafty Shield. Utilizing a Normalium Z to enhance it into Z-Psych Up bestows a powerful secondary effect: the user's HP is fully restored. The move targets the Pokémon directly opposite, regardless of whether it's an ally or an adversary, and copies changes to their Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, evasion, and accuracy stats. This replication even encompasses the distinct stat multipliers from moves such as Charm, Aurora Beam, Memento, and Screech, specifically those existing before the introduction of Gates to Infinity.

Title: Extreme Speed (move)

This Normal-type damage-dealing technique, initially spelled ExtremeSpeed until Pokémon X and Y, originated in Generation II and is known in Japan as しんそく (Extreme Speed). Its primary function is to inflict harm. Boasting a priority level of +1, it activates ahead of any move lacking an elevated priority. Consistent with other attacks that historically held priority status, Extreme Speed propels the user in a forward dash, leading to damaging physical contact with the adversary (though environmental factors can impede this action). While in motion, the user gains intangibility, allowing them to bypass both incoming projectiles and entry hazards encountered along their path.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Lucario utilizes Extreme Speed as its up special maneuver. Upon activation, Lucario executes a rapid lunge. Varying directional inputs modifies Lucario's trajectory. Initially, in Super Smash Bros. Brawl, this particular move dealt no damage and covered a fixed distance. However, in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, it not only causes damage but also, as Lucario accumulates greater aura, its travel distance and damage output both increase.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Extreme Speed functions as the initial move for both Dragonite and Lucario. Dragonite acquires this ability by reaching level 5, evolving into Dragonair, and then upgrading Twister into Extreme Speed rather than Dragon Dance. The user surges towards a chosen foe, dealing damage and applying a slow effect to all adversaries within a small area surrounding the impact point, while simultaneously launching the primary target and pushing away others. Activating Extreme Speed immediately resets the cooldown timer for Multiscale. Upon reaching level 11, this move additionally nullifies any prior movement speed reductions affecting the user before its execution.

Lucario gains access to Extreme Speed at level 5 by opting to upgrade Quick Attack to it, as an alternative to Power-Up Punch. The user charges ahead, inflicting damage on all struck enemies and restoring their own HP for every enemy impacted. Hitting the initial enemy with this move will reset Extreme Speed's cooldown. However, this specific effect cannot be triggered on the same opponent more frequently than once every 10 seconds. Executing an attack immediately following the dash initiates a distinctive area-of-effect strike, which deals 75% of the user's Attack stat as damage and possesses the potential to land a critical hit. Once level 11 is achieved, the move temporarily boosts the user's Attack stat by 15% for a brief duration subsequent to its deployment.

In-game descriptions for Extreme Speed contain an inaccuracy, claiming it precedes all other moves. This particular characteristic is also common among Baby-Doll Eyes, Bullet Punch, Ice Shard, Mach Punch, Quick Attack, Shadow Sneak, Vacuum Wave, and Water Shuriken. Despite its Normal typing, only a single Normal-type Pokémon can naturally learn Extreme Speed through leveling up, if one excludes Togekiss, which held the Normal typing before Generation VI.

Title: Ancient Power (move)

Ancient Power (Japanese: げんしのちから Primeval Power), which was styled as AncientPower before Pokémon X and Y, is a Rock-type damage-dealing attack first introduced in Generation II. For Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it holds the Technical Machine designation TM120. This offensive move inflicts damage and possesses a 10% probability of boosting the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats by one stage each. Ancient Power initiates physical contact solely within Generation III. Its Power Points (PP) have since been increased from 5 to 15. The move potentially raises the user's offensive and defensive attributes. From Generation IV onward, three Pokémon can undergo evolution by leveling up while knowing Ancient Power: Tangela transforms into Tangrowth, Piloswine into Mamoswine, and Yanma into Yanmega. As a damaging technique, it also carries a small chance to enhance the user's Attack, Defense, Sp. Attack, Sp. Defense, and Travel Speed by one stage. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, when employed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Ancient Power features a base of 20 PP and strikes enemies within a 1-tile radius around the user. Its secondary effect was altered from "2 Attack and Defense" to "1 Attack and Defense." Prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, the additional effect was described as having a very minor chance of elevating the user's Attack, Defense, Speed, Sp. Atk, Sp. Def, and Speed. Every Fossil Pokémon, along with a considerable number of Legendary Pokémon, are capable of learning Ancient Power. With three Pokémon reliant on it for evolution, Ancient Power triggers more evolutions than any other evolution-inducing move. It stands as the only move featuring an additional effect of raising all stats that was not removed in Generation VIII, a fact possibly attributable to its role as an evolution prerequisite. Piloswine, which evolves into Mamoswine upon leveling up while knowing Ancient Power, has been learnable by breeding since the move's original introduction in Generation II.

Title: Shadow Ball (move)

Shadow Ball (Japanese: シャドーボール Shadow Ball) is a damaging Ghost-type technique that first appeared in the second generation of games. It held the TM30 designation from Generation II up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and once again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned as TM43. This attack inflicts damage and carries a 20% probability of reducing the target's Special Defense by one stage, a characteristic that remained constant even in Generations II and III when the move was physically categorized. Pokémon with the Bulletproof ability are entirely impervious to Shadow Ball. The Power Points (PP) for Shadow Ball have been set at 10, and it still possesses a 20% chance of lowering the adversary's defensive stats. Shadow Ball delivers harm and potentially diminishes the opponent's Special Defense by a single level. Curiously, in the Japanese versions of Explorers of Time and Darkness, this move mistakenly targeted the foe's Defense stat.

In the Super Smash Bros. series, Shadow Ball functions as Mewtwo's signature special maneuver. It is an attack that can be charged and then propelled in a distinct zigzag pattern. When Mewtwo forms a Shadow Ball, its hand position mimics its portrayal in *Mewtwo Strikes Back*, though the sphere itself showcases an individualistic design. The blue hue of the orb is reminiscent of its appearance in Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal. Within Super Smash Bros. Melee, any enemy touching Mewtwo during the Shadow Ball's charging phase would incur damage. This mechanism was altered in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, where it no longer harms opponents while being charged. Mewtwo also deploys Shadow Ball against foes when performing a forward throw. Lucario, who debuted in Super Smash Bros. Brawl, employs Aura Sphere, a move with similar operational characteristics.

The description preceding Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def." Unlike certain sync moves, this particular attack could not be elevated to 6★ EX status from its launch date (March 19, 2020) until 1011 days later (December 25, 2022).

Within Pokémon UNITE, Shadow Ball serves as the initial ability for both Gengar and Hoopa. Gengar can acquire this move by reaching level 7 as Haunter, choosing to evolve Will-O-Wisp into it instead of Sludge Bomb. The user hurls a dark orb straight forward, damaging, slowing, and decreasing the Special Defense of enemies it strikes. This projectile passes through wild Pokémon. At level 13, its damage output is enhanced. Hoopa gains access to Shadow Ball by upgrading Astonish at level 6, as an alternative to Phantom Force. This move causes the user to unleash a shadowy blob directly in front, dealing damage to opposing Pokémon hit and temporarily reducing their movement speed and Sp. Def. If the user subsequently lands a move or boosted attack on the identical opposing Pokémon within a specified timeframe, it inflicts additional damage and briefly renders them immobile. When executing this ability, if a nearby ally Pokémon is linked by Trick, the move will also discharge a shadowy blob at the same target from the ally’s location. At level 12, the shadowy blob will detonate before vanishing. Opposing Pokémon affected by this explosion suffer the same consequences as if hit by the shadowy blob itself. In Hoopa's Unbound form, this move is superseded by Hyperspace Fury.

In *Cunning Kirlia*, Ruby's Kirlia, Rara, demonstrated knowledge of Shadow Ball when she utilized Imprison to prevent Emerald's Dusclops from employing the move against her. Bruno's Hitmonlee was listed on the official website as knowing Shadow Ball, but it has never been depicted using the move. Furthermore, Hitmonlee is actually unable to learn Shadow Ball. Because Shadow Ball was classified as a physical move before Generation IV, it holds the distinction as the sole damaging attack (excluding Max Moves) that has ever dealt damage from a category different from the target's lowered defensive stat.

Shadow Ball was introduced in the animated series before its appearance in the video games. In Pokémon Colosseum, specifically in Mt. Battle Area 10 in Battle Mode Singles and in Battle Now, Cooltrainer Cruik's Muk is shown possessing Shadow Ball, even though Muk cannot naturally learn this move until Generation IV. In Generation III, as well as in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Shadow Ball was the only Ghost-type move that could be taught via a Technical Machine (TM). In Generation VIII, Shadow Ball holds the unique position of being the only Ghost-type move teachable via a Technical Record (TR). Shadow Ball stands alone among Ghost-type moves for having its damage category altered during the transition from Generation III to Generation IV. While Night Shade also became a special attack, its nature as a fixed-damage ability meant it remained unaffected by the change in damage category.

Title: Future Sight (move)

Future Sight (Japanese: みらいよち Future Sight) is a damaging Psychic-type maneuver that debuted in Generation II. When chosen, this attack initially performs no action beyond signaling that the user has anticipated an impending strike. After a delay of two turns, Future Sight unleashes its damage upon the target, occurring after both Pokémon have executed their moves. The damage calculation for this move relies on the user's Special Attack stat and the target's Special Defense stat as they stood at the moment the move was initially selected, not when the damage actually lands. If the original target is swapped out, the subsequent Pokémon that replaces it will be hit, but the damage remains based on the stats of the Pokémon originally targeted. The damage inflicted by Future Sight is typeless; it neither benefits from nor is hindered by type effectiveness (allowing it to strike Dark-type Pokémon), it does not receive the Same-Type Attack Bonus (STAB), and it can bypass the Wonder Guard ability. Future Sight is incapable of landing a critical hit, and its damage output is not augmented by a Life Orb. Protection moves or Endure will not prevent Future Sight's damage. However, a Pokémon holding a Focus Band or Focus Sash can still endure the hit. The move will fail if activated against a target that is already slated to be hit by either Future Sight or Doom Desire. Notably, Future Sight has the potential to strike the very Pokémon that employed it. Should Future Sight miss, the miss condition does not manifest until the third turn, and the game will declare the move "failed" rather than "missed." In the context of Double Battles, if the designated target has already fainted by the time the attack is foreseen, leaving no active target, Future Sight will redirect its effect, much like other moves would (e.g., to another opposing Pokémon). Conversely, if no Pokémon occupies the targeted slot when the attack is due to hit, Future Sight will simply fail without any explicit notification. Initially, Future Sight possessed a base power of 80 and an accuracy of 90. In Pokémon Stadium 2, a unique outcome occurs if both players are down to their last Pokémon and both are simultaneously knocked out by Future Sight: the battle is ruled a Draw. In Pokémon Contests, Future Sight serves as a potent second move in a combination, awarding the user 3 bonus appeal points if Calm Mind, Confusion, Kinesis, or Psychic was performed on the preceding turn.

Future Sight later underwent substantial revisions. Its base power was elevated from 80 to 100, its accuracy was perfected from 90% to 100%, and its PP count was reduced from 15 to 10. The move's damage calculation was fundamentally altered to occur at the moment it strikes, rather than at its initial selection. Consequently, it now utilizes the user's Special Attack and the hit Pokémon's Special Defense stats at the exact time damage is dealt. It now inflicts damage as a true Psychic-type attack, making it susceptible to type effectiveness (thus unable to harm Dark-type Pokémon), allowing it to benefit from STAB, and preventing it from inherently bypassing Wonder Guard. Future Sight gained the capability to land critical hits. If the user is on the field and has the Normalize ability or was affected by Electrify when Future Sight hits, the move's type is converted to Normal or Electric, respectively (irrespective of the user's state when the move was initially employed); however, it cannot be redirected by Lightning Rod, even if it becomes an Electric-type move. If the user is no longer active on the field when Future Sight lands, its Ability and held item will not influence the attack. In battles involving multiple opponents, Future Sight may now target a vacant slot. If a Pokémon subsequently occupies that chosen slot by the time the attack is due, Future Sight will execute as intended. Nevertheless, if the targeted slot remains empty when the attack is scheduled, Future Sight will still fail silently. Pokémon can now endure Future Sight through Endure or Sturdy, in addition to Focus Sash and Focus Band. If Future Sight is destined to hit the Pokémon that originally used it, the move will dissipate without any message. Future Sight's power received another boost, increasing from 100 to 120. In later Contests, the move now yields six appeal points instead of two if the move used by the immediately preceding Pokémon in that specific round was also Psychic-type. This bonus, however, cannot be triggered if Future Sight is used first in a round, even if the last Pokémon in the previous round used a Psychic-type move.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Future Sight initially functions as a time-delayed variant of Rock Slide; upon its activation, the area centered on the target's position visibly glows before fading. After a brief interval, damaging projectiles descend upon the designated area. This allows Future Sight to strike multiple enemies simultaneously, though it will not inflict additional damage if a single target is hit more than once. Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Future Sight is a status move that bestows the user with the "Set Damage" status condition for 3-4 turns. This status forces all of the user's subsequent attacks to deal a fixed 35 / 30 damage, regardless of other multipliers. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Future Sight transitioned back to being a damage-dealing move, granting the user the "Future Sight" status condition, which causes the user to charge the move for one turn before unleashing it on the subsequent turn.

In Pokémon GO, Future Sight is categorized as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since February 16, 2017.

In Pokémon UNITE, Future Sight is the second move for both Espeon and Gardevoir. Espeon gains access to Future Sight at level 6 as an upgrade from Growl, replacing Psybeam. The user locks onto an enemy, then, after a brief delay, deals damage to foes within an area of effect centered on that locked-on target. Greater damage is inflicted when the locked-on target's remaining HP is lower. While Espeon is locked onto a target, all its other moves will prioritize that enemy whenever feasible. Each time one of these moves successfully deals damage, Future Sight's cooldown is reduced. If the locked-on opposing enemy is knocked out before Future Sight's area-of-effect damage is delivered, a single empowered use of either Psyshock or Stored Power becomes available for a short duration. When Psyshock is powered up, it hurls stronger psychic projectiles at a chosen location, which then explode, damaging enemies in the vicinity. When Stored Power is powered up, it unleashes an increased number of psychic blasts and restores the user's HP upon hitting. At level 12, Espeon deals an additional 10% damage to the enemy locked onto by Future Sight. Gardevoir obtains Future Sight by evolving into Kirlia at level 5 and upgrading Teleport instead of Psyshock. Gardevoir's Future Sight creates a delayed explosion at a targeted area, harming enemies. If this explosion connects with an enemy, Future Sight's cooldown is reduced. At level 11, successful hits will completely reset the move's cooldown.

Title: Rock Smash (move)

Should you be searching for the Pokémon Shuffle skill, refer to Skill (Shuffle) → Rock Break. Known in Japanese as いわくだき (Rock Smash), this damage-inflicting Fighting-type technique debuted in Generation II. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it is designated as TM98. Beyond its direct damage, the move carries a 50% probability of reducing the opponent's Defense stat by a single stage. To utilize Rock Smash outside of combat in some titles, players must possess a particular gym badge, specifically the Marsh, Zephyr, Dynamo, or Coal Badge. Its utility extends to shattering destructible rocks, thereby opening new routes. Sometimes, the act of breaking these rocks may lead to an encounter with a wild Pokémon. In other instances, it can destroy breakable rocks without summoning any wild Pokémon. Furthermore, using Rock Smash on breakable rocks can yield either wild Pokémon or hidden items. These discoverable items comprise Heart Scales, various shards, and Fossils. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, possessing a Pokémon that knows Rock Smash allows players to assist Workers in Clay Tunnel with clearing boulder sections. The move also serves to demolish both breakable rocks and certain cracked walls. While wild Pokémon and items can be unearthed from rocks, cracked walls do not yield such discoveries. In many contemporary games, Rock Smash has lost its out-of-battle utility. However, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it functions as a field move accessible through the Pokétch's Hidden Moves application, which deploys a wild Bidoof to execute the technique. Generally, Rock Smash is no longer usable outside of battle. Within Pokémon Emerald, employing Rock Smash in the Desert Ruins opens a passageway to a chamber housing Regirock.

Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Rock Smash operates as a non-damaging maneuver, converting a wall tile directly ahead of the user into a floor tile, regardless of whether a Pokémon occupies that space. However, it lacks the capacity to demolish walls associated with locked chambers or those situated at the perimeter of a floor, and diagonal usage is impossible. Before the Explorers series, this move was impervious to the effects of Taunt. Starting with Gates to Infinity onwards, Rock Smash transitioned into a damaging attack with a chance to decrease the target's Defense. It preserved its wall-demolishing function in Super Mystery Dungeon.

In Super Smash Bros. Brawl, Charizard employs Rock Smash as its Side Special move. Diverging from its Pokémon game mechanics, Charizard generates a boulder and shatters it with its head, causing multiple debris fragments to scatter and inflict additional damage. This technique boasts substantial damage output and knockback, capable of potentially knocking out an opponent even at low damage percentages. For Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, Rock Smash shifted to Charizard's Down Special, while Flare Blitz occupied its previous slot. Though significantly weakened, it now provides Charizard with armor, shielding it from incoming assaults. However, in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, Rock Smash was excised from Charizard's repertoire, replaced by Pokémon Change, due to Charizard's reintegration into the Pokémon Trainer character.

A description predating Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021) stated: "Possesses a strong likelihood of reducing the target's Defense." Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto gains access to Rock Smash after acquiring it from a Hitmonchan in the Withered Wasteland. This enables Ditto to dismantle obstacles directly before it. Consuming any variety of hamburger can temporarily enhance Rock Smash, empowering Ditto to shatter even more robust materials. Carl's Piloswine was officially noted to be capable of Rock Smash on the website, though it was never depicted performing the move. Rock Smash holds the unique distinction of being the sole move to originate as a Technical Machine (TM), transition to a Hidden Machine (HM), then revert to a TM, and subsequently become an HM once more. Both Rock Smash and Rock Climb are the only techniques featuring "rock" in their nomenclature without being Rock-type. Incidentally, each has functioned as an HM at some stage. During Generation V games, Rock Smash erroneously displayed the Rock-type TM icon instead of its proper Fighting-type representation. Despite its description as a "punch," Rock Smash does not receive a power boost from the Iron Fist ability.

Title: Whirlpool (move)

Known in Japanese as うずしお (Whirl Tide), Whirlpool is a damage-dealing Water-type move that debuted in Generation II. This attack delivers initial damage and subsequently traps the target for a specific number of turns, also inflicting damage at the conclusion of each turn. If the user equips a Binding Band, the trapping damage is amplified. Should the user hold a Grip Claw, the duration of the trap is affected. A Pokémon caught in Whirlpool is also prevented from being switched out or escaping the current battle. Whirlpool possesses the ability to strike Pokémon during Dive's semi-invulnerable turn, dealing twice the damage across all generations, with the exception of Generation IV. In Generation IV, the move's power is instead doubled, yielding virtually the same overall effect. This enhancement to damage or power applies solely to the move's initial use, not to the subsequent trapping damage. A trapped Pokémon holding a Shed Shell, however, can still be switched out. Furthermore, a Pokémon with the Run Away ability or holding a Smoke Ball (except in Generation II) can escape a battle against a wild Pokémon while trapped, but remains unable to switch. Freedom from the trap occurs if the affected Pokémon uses Rapid Spin or if the Whirlpool user withdraws from combat. The message displayed when this move previously trapped a target was: "<Pokémon> was trapped!" Currently, the text that appears when the target is ensnared reads: "<Pokémon> was trapped in the vortex!" In Pokémon Contests, Whirlpool serves as the second move in a combination, granting the user 3 bonus appeal points if Rain Dance was utilized in the preceding turn. Ghost-type Pokémon are immune to being trapped by Whirlpool. Outside of battle, this move can be employed either from the party menu or by simply pressing the A button when facing a whirlpool. In Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, the Glacier Badge is a prerequisite for using Whirlpool outside of battle. While surfing, Whirlpool clears whirlpools present in the field. However, in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Whirlpool instead permits the user to traverse whirlpools on the field, a departure from its Generation II function of clearing them.

In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Whirlpool inflicts damage and has a 10% chance to bestow the Constriction status condition upon the enemy. This condition renders the enemy unable to move (though attacking is still possible) and causes a loss of 5 HP every two turns, persisting for 4-6 turns. From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, the move's effect shifts; it may instead inflict the Whirlpool status condition for 6 turns, which immobilizes the enemy (though attacks remain possible) and results in a loss of 3 HP every two turns. Across all Mystery Dungeon series, Whirlpool can strike a Pokémon in the middle of Dive for double damage.

In Pokémon UNITE, Whirlpool is the first move available to both Azumarill and Cramorant. Azumarill obtains the move by evolving from Marill at level 5, where it upgrades Tackle into Whirlpool instead of Aqua Tail. For Azumarill, the move generates four small whirlpools that swirl around the user for a brief duration as it moves. If one of these whirlpools strikes an opposing Pokémon, it deals damage to them and restores the user’s HP. An increased amount of HP is restored to the user if a whirlpool hits a Pokémon on the opposing team. At level 11, the move’s cooldown is shortened, and the amount of HP it restores to the user is amplified. Cramorant begins with the move. Cramorant's Whirlpool creates a whirlpool at a designated location, damaging all enemies within its radius. It also applies a 50% slow to enemies within the area at the end of its duration. The user can enter this whirlpool to capture up to 2 prey for Gulp Missile. At level 6, Cramorant's Whirlpool is replaced by either Surf or Dive.

Notably, Whirlpool is the first of three moves to lose its HM status, yet it is also the first former HM to regain that designation. This made it the initial HM move teachable by two separate HMs: HM06 in Generation II, and HM05 in HeartGold and SoulSilver. If a player instructs a Pokémon to learn Whirlpool via HM in HeartGold and SoulSilver and then trades it to Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum, the Pokémon can be freely imported to Black and White using the Poké Transfer feature. This is possible because Whirlpool is not required for overworld use in Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum. An identical transfer mechanism applies to Defog, by moving a Pokémon that learned it via HM in Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum to HeartGold or SoulSilver. In Latin Spanish, Whirlpool is named Remolino, which is the European Spanish equivalent for Whirlwind. Conversely, Torbellino is the European Spanish term for Whirlpool and the Latin Spanish term for Whirlwind.

Title: Beat Up (move)

For details concerning Pokémon Trading Card Game deck archetypes, consult Beat Up (TCG). Beat Up, known as ふくろだたき (Gang Up) in Japanese, is a Dark-type, damage-inflicting technique featuring multiple strikes that debuted in Generation II. Before the advent of Generation IV, this attack was Sneasel's exclusive signature move; however, other Pokémon had the capability to pass it down through breeding. In the games Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was cataloged as Technical Machine 37 (TM37). The move Beat Up deals harm to its chosen target, with contributions from the user and every other conscious, non-afflicted Pokémon residing in the user's party. Every individual hit is typeless and possesses a base power of 10. Damage calculation takes into account each assaulting Pokémon's base Attack and current level, contrasted with the target's base Defense. Fluctuations in stat stages, for instance, those caused by Swords Dance, are disregarded by Beat Up. The same-type attack bonus (STAB) is excluded from damage computations. Before each strike, a message appears: '<name>'s Attack!', where <name> refers to the name of the specific Pokémon from the player's party that is attacking. The likelihood of each individual strike scoring a critical hit is independently determined. The activation probability for King's Rock's secondary effect is solely dependent on the final strike of the attack. Although Dark-type moves were categorized as special attacks before Generation IV, Beat Up provokes Counter but fails to trigger Mirror Coat. In the context of a Double Battle, if a Pokémon employs Beat Up on its ally, the ally will strike itself, provided it is not currently suffering from a status condition. Should Beat Up cause a target's Focus Sash to activate, any following hits from that same Beat Up sequence will be incapable of achieving a knockout. Wonder Guard is circumvented by Beat Up because the move is classified as a typeless assault. The Sniper Ability offers no modification to its critical hits.

Presently, each strike carries its own independent opportunity to activate a King's Rock or Razor Fang. Boosts from the Technician Ability are not applied to Beat Up. Rather than utilizing the Attack stat of each party member, the damage for every strike is now solely determined by the user's Attack stat. Consequently, Beat Up now benefits from Attack boosts, such as those from Swords Dance, and from Attack-enhancing items like the Choice Band. Moreover, the base power for each individual strike is no longer a fixed 10, but is instead individually calculated based on the Attack base stats of the party Pokémon: Beat Up now benefits from STAB when used by Dark-type Pokémon, ceases to inflict typeless damage, and gains effectiveness from Technician. The move no longer displays the names of the individual Pokémon contributing to the attack. Every strike of Beat Up will trigger the Abilities Justified, Weak Armor, and Stamina. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike being delivered, the subsequent strike will inevitably lead to the defending Pokémon's fainting. A Focus Band retains its capability to activate multiple times to avert fainting, though each activation attempt holds independent odds. If the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1, Sturdy will activate upon every single hit received. An independent chance exists for each strike to activate Stench; however, this effect does not compound with those of the Razor Fang or King's Rock.

In Mystery Dungeon, Beat Up functions as a status move, causing all teammates on the dungeon floor to warp to the user's location. Before Super Mystery Dungeon titles, allies would be positioned within a maximum range of 2 tiles from the user. This move exclusively affects teammates and has no impact on clients who are temporarily part of the player's team. Warping a Pokémon will fail if insufficient space is available in the designated vicinity; the move considers only ground tiles, even for Pokémon capable of traversing non-standard terrain. Starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move targets an opposing Pokémon directly ahead, subsequently warping all teammates on the floor to encamp the identified target. The user inflicts damage upon all Pokémon situated immediately in its frontal arc.

Beat Up delivers a single hit for every team member, including the user themselves. By index number order, Beat Up holds the distinction of being the final Generation II move. It stands as the sole move whose base power is derived from base stats.

Title: Fake Out (move)

Hailing from Generation III, Fake Out (known in Japanese as ねこだまし, or Slap Hands) is a Normal-type move that inflicts damage. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is designated as TM141. This move not only deals damage but invariably causes the opponent to flinch, unless they possess the Inner Focus or Shield Dust Ability. With a priority of +1, Fake Out activates ahead of any move lacking elevated priority. Its execution requires it to be used exclusively on the first turn the Pokémon enters the battle. Notably, in wild encounters, if the player's Pokémon attempts to flee but fails due to a Speed differential, Fake Out will not be unsuccessful. This move is non-contact.

In Pokémon Contests, initiating a combination with Fake Out will double the appeal of Arm Thrust, Faint Attack, Knock Off, Seismic Toss, and Vital Throw when these moves are performed on the subsequent turn. Fake Out currently possesses a +3 priority level. During a Rotation Battle, a Pokémon can unleash Fake Out on its first turn after being rotated into play; simply being outside its Poké Ball but rotated out of active combat does not impede its usage. Instructing a Pokémon to use Fake Out will result in its failure, even if that Pokémon has just entered the field. Successfully employing Fake Out against a Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon inflicts damage, yet it will not trigger the flinching effect. A Pokémon equipped with a Covert Cloak is immune to Fake Out's flinching effect, given that the item negates moves' secondary effects, which for Fake Out is indeed flinching. The move causes damage and has the potential to make the foe flinch.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Fake Out serves as Mr. Mime's initial starting move. It involves the user clapping, which damages, knocks back, and stuns opponents within a cone-shaped area for 0.4 seconds. Upon reaching level 4, this move is superseded by either Confusion or Psychic. The episode "Noibat Stands in the Way" depicted Shauna instructing her Skitty, Neko, to use Fake Out, but the Pokémon, unable to hear its Trainer, failed to execute the technique. By index number order, this move holds the distinction of being the first Generation III move. Its Japanese designation, ねこだまし (Nekodamashi, or Slap Hands), derives from a sumo maneuver where a wrestler claps their hands before an opponent's face at the outset of a match, prompting the opponent to shut their eyes. The move's prevalence among feline-like Pokémon is likely a clever reference to this name, which literally means "fool the cat." Among damage-dealing moves, Fake Out shares the highest priority with Upper Hand, with exceptions for Pursuit when targeting a switching-out Pokémon and for HP-draining moves executed by a Pokémon with Triage. Aside from Fling when combined with a King's Rock or Razor Fang, Fake Out and Upper Hand stand as the sole moves guaranteed to make the target flinch.

Title: Uproar (move)

Uproar (Japanese: さわぐ, Clamor) is a Normal-type damage-dealing move that originated in Generation III. It was available as TR35 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and now serves as TM191 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

The move Uproar inflicts damage and causes the user to commence an uproar for several turns. While the Pokémon is in this state, it automatically uses Uproar each turn, though PP is deducted solely during its initial activation.

In earlier generations, Uproar's base power was 50, and it compelled the user to continue the uproar for 2 to 5 turns. During an ongoing uproar, no Pokémon could fall asleep, although certain game-dependent exceptions existed. Specifically, Pokémon without the Ability Soundproof were prevented from falling asleep due to moves that inflict sleep (including Rest, which would fail if the user lacked Soundproof), Effect Spore, or disobedience; they also couldn't become drowsy from Yawn. Nevertheless, a Pokémon already affected by Yawn-induced drowsiness *could* fall asleep during an uproar, regardless of its Soundproof status. A sleeping Pokémon without Soundproof would awaken immediately before attempting a move. At the close of each turn (even when the user calmed down), any sleeping Pokémon on the field lacking Soundproof would be roused. Even if the Uproar user possessed the Ability Mold Breaker, Pokémon with Soundproof remained unaffected by the ongoing racket. An uproar would not commence if Uproar's initial execution failed entirely—for instance, against a Ghost-type (which is immune), a protected Pokémon, or one with Soundproof. However, once an uproar was ongoing, it would persist even if subsequent attempts failed under these conditions. In multi-opponent battles, the user targets itself but randomly strikes an adjacent foe with each use. If an opponent was positioned behind a Substitute, Uproar would strike the Substitute.

Beginning with Generation V, Uproar's mechanics underwent significant changes. Its base power increased to 90, and the user now consistently maintains the uproar for exactly 3 turns. When Uproar is initially used (meaning the user is not already making an uproar), *all* sleeping Pokémon on the field are woken up, regardless of their Soundproof Ability or adjacency to the user. This initial use is now the *only* time Uproar awakens sleeping Pokémon. While an uproar is active, Pokémon cannot fall asleep, even if they have Soundproof or are not adjacent to the Pokémon making the uproar. Yawn can still cause drowsiness, but a drowsy Pokémon cannot fall asleep during an uproar, even with Soundproof. If Uproar fails to fully execute on *any* turn—for example, against a Ghost-type, a protected target, or a Pokémon with Soundproof—the user immediately calms down and is no longer forced to use the move. Uproar can now hit Pokémon even if they are behind a Substitute. If affected by Throat Chop, the user will calm down at the end of the turn.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Uproar is a status move. It targets all team members in the same room, granting them the Sleepless status condition for the rest of the floor. This status prevents sleep and wakes any already-asleep teammates. Uproar is affected by the Muzzled status condition. While a sound-based move in the main series, it could affect Pokémon with Soundproof in these games. From Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity onwards, Uproar inflicts damage to all enemies in the room. It also applies the Sleepless status condition to all Pokémon in the room (including teammates). Ghost-type Pokémon will not become Sleepless unless immunity is bypassed, and Uproar will not affect Pokémon with the Ability Soundproof. In Gates to Infinity, Sleepless is not granted if no enemies are targeted.

The official Pokémon Adventures website states Wanda's Loudred knows Uproar, though its use of the move has never been depicted. Uproar displays an animation during the end-of-turn message in Pokémon Battle Revolution.

Title: Stockpile (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Stockpile (Japanese: たくわえる Stockpile) functions as a Normal-type status move that deals no direct damage. Most Pokémon capable of learning Stockpile can also learn its associated moves, Spit Up and Swallow. Notable exceptions include Snorlax, Munchlax, Trubbish, Garbodor, and Guzzlord, none of whom can learn Spit Up. Additionally, Qwilfish, Dewpider, and Overqwil are unable to learn Swallow. Typically, a Pokémon acquiring Stockpile by leveling up will also immediately gain access to Spit Up and/or Swallow. This move allows the user to store energy. Initially, it had no additional effects. A user can accumulate up to three 'stockpiles'; attempting to use the move when already at three stockpiles will result in its failure. Spit Up inflicts damage, and Swallow restores HP, with the potency of both being directly proportional to the current stockpile count. Using either move, however, resets the stockpile counter. Furthermore, both Spit Up and Swallow will fail if no stockpiles have been accumulated.

Within Pokémon Contests, Stockpile serves as an initiating move in a combination, enabling Spit Up and Swallow to achieve double the standard appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. From Generation V onward, Stockpile acquired a new secondary effect: each successful use now boosts the user's Defense and Special Defense stats by one stage apiece. However, these accrued Defense and Special Defense boosts are negated upon the user's execution of either Spit Up or Swallow. In Contest Spectaculars, employing Stockpile sets up a combination where Spit Up and Swallow yield an additional three appeal points when used immediately afterward. When enhanced by a Normalium Z into Z-Stockpile, the user experiences a full HP recovery. Activation of Stockpile confers the "Stockpiling" status condition, signifying the storage of energy (up to 3 times) which, in turn, influences the effectiveness of Spit Up and Swallow. Snatch and the Muzzled status condition are known to affect this move.

In the Explorers series of Mystery Dungeon games, this move mirrors its main series counterpart by increasing the user's Defense and Special Defense by one stage per successful Stockpile. Notably, these Defense and Special Defense enhancements persist even after using Spit Up or Swallow and can continue to accumulate. The passive skill "Giovanni's Greed," belonging to Sygna Suit Giovanni (Alt.) & Guzzlord, causes Stockpile to elevate the Defense and Sp. Def of all allied sync pairs by three stat ranks with every use. Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto naturally possesses the ability to use Stockpile. This unique application permits Ditto to consume and stow items in its bag that would ordinarily be too cumbersome or weighty. Furthermore, Stockpile directly interfaces with the move Spit Out. Interestingly, Ditto in Pokémon Pokopia can employ Stockpile in its human form, despite never having the capacity to learn the move through conventional means.

Title: Spit Up (move)

Spit Up (Japanese: はきだす Spit Up) is a damage-dealing Normal-type attack that debuted in Generation III. Pokémon capable of learning Spit Up are generally also able to learn the associated moves Stockpile and Swallow; however, Qwilfish, Dewpider, and Overqwil are exceptions, as they cannot learn Swallow. This move will fail outright if the user has not first employed Stockpile. If activated after stockpiling, Spit Up inflicts damage on the target without any additional effects.

In its initial implementation, during Generations III and IV, Spit Up possessed a base power of 100, and its total damage output was determined by multiplying this value by the number of times Stockpile had been used. Regardless of whether the opponent guarded against the attack, the user's stockpile count would invariably reset to zero after Spit Up's execution. During this period, Spit Up was incapable of scoring critical hits, nor was its damage subject to the standard random factor in damage calculation. In Pokémon Contests, it served as the second move in a combination, granting 4 bonus appeal points if Stockpile had been performed in the preceding turn. A distinct glitch in Generation IV caused the Pokémon's sprite to translate two pixels to the right during the move's animation.

Beginning with Generation V, Spit Up's power was directly set to 100 multiplied by the number of Stockpile uses. Since Stockpile now also boosted the user's Defense and Special Defense, Spit Up consequently gained the effect of nullifying any such defensive stat increases accumulated via Stockpile. Initially, this stat removal was indicated by a simple text message ("the effect wore off") without a visible stat drop animation, contrasting with Stockpile's own visual effects. Critical hits became a possibility for Spit Up from this generation onwards, though its damage remained unaffected by random variation.

Further changes in Generation VI made the removal of Stockpile-generated stat boosts visually apparent. The magnitude of the stat reduction now depended on the user's Ability: a two-stage drop for each successful Stockpile if the Pokémon possessed Simple, a one-stage *increase* per successful Stockpile if it had Contrary, or a one-stage drop per successful Stockpile for any other Ability. This calculation specifically accounts for Stockpile uses that successfully altered a defensive stat, even if those boosts were later negated by moves like Haze or Clear Smog, but it disregards Stockpiles that failed to produce a stat change (e.g., due to stats already being maximized). Furthermore, Spit Up's damage began to incorporate a random factor during damage calculation. In Contest Spectaculars, using Spit Up after Stockpile in the previous turn yielded an additional three appeal points.

Spit Up is affected by the Muzzled status condition. When Stockpile has been used three times, Spit Up achieves the highest base power among all Normal-type moves, surpassing even Explosion. In Pokémon Pokopia, a move called Spit Out, which draws clear inspiration from Spit Up due to its direct interaction with Stockpile, enables Ditto to retrieve items it had previously stored in its bag using Stockpile.

Title: Swallow (move)

Appearing first in Generation III, Swallow (Japanese: のみこむ Swallow) is a Normal-type technique that deals no direct damage. Most Pokémon capable of learning Swallow can also learn its associated moves, Stockpile and Spit Up; however, Snorlax, Munchlax, Trubbish, Garbodor, and Guzzlord are exceptions, as they cannot learn Spit Up. This move will fail if the user has not first accumulated Stockpile boosts.

When executed after stockpiling, Swallow restores the user's HP based on the number of times Stockpile was used. Following its use, the stockpile count will invariably be reset to 0.

Swallow can serve as the second move in a Pokémon Contest combination, awarding 1 bonus appeal point if Stockpile was performed in the preceding turn.

Swallow also clears any Defense and Special Defense boosts acquired via Stockpile. Initially, this removal was conveyed by a generic "effect wore off" message, lacking the explicit visual stat change animations associated with Stockpile itself. The Stockpile removal is now depicted as an actual stat change. The precise effect on defensive stats varies based on the user's Ability at the moment Swallow is employed: for each Stockpile that successfully altered a defensive stat, that specific stat will drop by two stages for a Pokémon with Simple, rise by one stage for a Pokémon with Contrary, or drop by one stage with any other Ability. Stockpiles that did not result in stat modifications (most often because the stat was already maxed out) are not factored into this adjustment. However, Stockpiles that did cause stat changes, even if those changes were subsequently nullified by moves such as Haze or Clear Smog, are included.

In Contest Spectaculars, Swallow contributes to a combination, granting an additional three appeal points if Stockpile was executed in the prior turn. Empowering Swallow with a Normalium Z transforms it into Z-Swallow, which resets all of the user's negative stat changes.

The user's HP regeneration from Swallow is directly linked to the current number of Stockpile boosts, which are invariably cleared to zero post-execution. This move is susceptible to Snatch.

Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, Swallow provides a fixed HP restoration: 20 HP after one Stockpile, 40 HP after two Stockpile boosts, and 80 HP after three Stockpile boosts. Beginning with Gates to Infinity, Swallow's HP restoration becomes percentage-based: 25% of the user's maximum HP after one Stockpile, 50% after two Stockpile boosts, and 75% after three Stockpile boosts.

Red's Victreebel was officially stated to know Swallow on the official website, despite never being depicted utilizing it.

Title: Heat Wave (move)

Originating in Generation III, Heat Wave is a damage-dealing Fire-type attack. It is known in Japanese as ねっぷう, translating to Hot Wind. Beyond its primary damage, there's a ten percent likelihood of inflicting a burn on the target. In Double Battles, this move strikes every opposing Pokémon. However, its reach is more limited in a Triple Battle, affecting only adjacent adversaries. Within Pokémon Contests, it functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the user an extra 4 appeal points if Sunny Day was executed during the preceding turn. Additionally, in battle environments, Heat Wave can obliterate round rocks present in the background. In dungeons, it delivers damage to all enemies within the same room and carries the potential to inflict a burn status. When utilized by Sygna Suit Red & Charizard or Blue (Classic) & Charizard, the attack gains perfect accuracy, a benefit derived from their respective passive abilities, Piercing Gaze and Kanto Journey Legacy. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description stated: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned." Despite being a Fire-type attack, its strong connection to the Flying type — observable through the numerous Flying-type Pokémon able to learn it — is emphasized in its Japanese designation, 「ねっぷう」, which means "Hot Wind." Before Generation VI, its in-game animation was identical to Ominous Wind's, save for the distinct color palette. Likewise, in Generation III, it shared significant animation similarities with Sandstorm, differing only in color and its visual impact on the targets' sprites. Early internal coding within Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire reveals a planned mechanic for Heat Wave: it could have functioned as a combo attack in Double Battles when Gust and Ember were deployed in the same turn. This particular feature was ultimately discarded at some point during the game's development phase.

Title: Hail (move)

Making its debut in Generation III, Hail (Japanese: あられ Hail) is an Ice-type move that does not inflict direct damage. It was designated as Technical Machine 07 (TM07) from its introduction through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon Sword and Shield, its TM slot was TM35. Upon its activation, a hailstorm commences across the battleground, an environmental effect that persists for a duration of 5 turns. This effect supersedes and removes any pre-existing weather conditions. While the hailstorm is active, at the conclusion of every turn, all Pokémon except those with the Ice type suffer damage equivalent to 1⁄16 of their total HP. Additionally, recovery moves such as Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight will only restore ¼ of the user's maximum HP. Castform and the move Weather Ball also transform into Ice-type under these conditions.

In Pokémon Contests, Hail can serve as the initial move in a combination, causing Blizzard, Powder Snow, and Weather Ball to accrue double their usual appeal if performed on the subsequent turn. Within a hailstorm, Blizzard gains perfect accuracy, bypassing both the target's evasion and the user's accuracy stat. Exclusively within Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, Blizzard notably possessed a 30% chance to penetrate the defensive moves Protect and Detect. Pokémon possessing the Ice Body Ability regenerate 1⁄16 of their total HP at the conclusion of each round, while those with the Snow Cloak Ability experience a 20% boost to their evasiveness. Pokémon endowed with the Slush Rush Ability see their Speed stat doubled.

Should the Pokémon deploying Hail be holding an Icy Rock, the hailstorm's duration extends from 5 turns to 8 turns. However, Hail will be unsuccessful if the prevailing weather consists of heavy rain, extremely harsh sunlight, or strong winds. Moreover, in Contest Spectaculars, Hail can initiate a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to subsequent uses of Blizzard, Glaciate, Icicle Crash, Icy Wind, Powder Snow, and Weather Ball. When empowered by an Icium Z into its Z-Move form, Z-Hail, the user's Speed attribute is elevated by one stage. Currently, Hail cannot be chosen in battle, its function having been superseded by Snowscape, which generates a snowstorm. In specific contexts, Hail alters the current floor's weather to hail for either 18 turns, the entire floor, or 66 turns.

Title: Torment (move)

Known in Japanese as いちゃもん (Falsely Accuse), Torment is a Dark-type status move that debuted in Generation III. This move held the Technical Machine designation TM41 across several generations, specifically from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappeared as TM41 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is identified as TM121. Its primary effect is to hinder the opponent, preventing them from consecutively utilizing the same attack. This restrictive state persists until the affected Pokémon is withdrawn from battle. Should a Pokémon already have chosen its previous turn's move before being afflicted by Torment, it is still permitted to execute that move during the current turn, even if the user of Torment acts later in the turn order. When the target is restricted to only a single available move—for instance, due to holding a Choice item, having insufficient PP for all other attacks, or being under the influence of Encore—it will be compelled to resort to Struggle every other turn. Torment does not, however, impose its restriction on Struggle; thus, a Pokémon that has depleted the PP for all its other moves may continue to use Struggle without impedance. Furthermore, multi-turn moves like Rollout will proceed to full completion despite the Torment status. Pokémon shielded by the Aroma Veil ability are immune to Torment's effects. The move also fails to stop an opponent from reiterating its last move if commanded by Instruct. A specific quirk in Pokémon Emerald dictates that if a confused target harms itself, Torment will not influence its subsequent move selection. Notably, in Generation IV, an unintended glitch allowed a tormented Pokémon that had previously used Rage to escape Torment's hold by selecting a different move, though its Rage boost would persist. Any Pokémon capable of using Torment is barred from featuring in films at Pokéstar Studios. Beyond battle, Torment serves as a component in Contest Spectacular combinations; employing it grants an additional three appeal points to specific moves—Counter, Destiny Bond, Grudge, King's Shield, Metal Burst, Mirror Coat, and Spite—when those moves are used during the subsequent turn. A Pokémon under Torment's influence retains the ability to convert a status move into a Z-Move, even if that particular move was executed on the preceding turn. When amplified by a Darkinium Z crystal, Torment transforms into Z-Torment, which bestows a one-stage increase to the user's Defense stat. This move is ineffective against a Dynamax Pokémon, and a Pokémon already afflicted by Torment can shed its effects by Dynamaxing itself. Essentially, Torment places a seal on the target's most recently employed move, rendering that move unusable. The Muzzled status condition impacts this move's functionality. In games predating Gates to Infinity, a sealed move was not classified as a status condition, yet it could still be alleviated by restorative moves like Refresh. However, beginning with Gates to Infinity, the act of sealing a move became formally recognized as a status condition. Uniquely among Disable-like effects, this particular move's duration is not fixed to a set number of turns; instead, its influence only ceases when the affected Pokémon is recalled.

Title: Flatter (move)

A Dark-type technique originating in Generation III, Flatter (おだてる in Japanese) inflicts no damage. Its effect bears a resemblance to Swagger. This maneuver elevates the opponent's Special Attack attribute by a single stage while simultaneously causing confusion. The Special Attack increase occurs regardless of whether the adversary is already bewildered, or if abilities like Own Tempo or the effects of Safeguard prevent them from being confused. Conversely, should the target's Special Attack already be maximized at +6 stages, the confusion status will still be applied. Even a foe with the Contrary ability, whose Special Attack is at its minimum of -6 stages, will still be afflicted with confusion. In Pokémon Contests, Flatter functions as the latter part of a combination, granting the user an additional 2 appeal points if Charm was executed on the preceding turn. When enhanced into Z-Flatter via a Darkinium Z, the user's Special Defense attribute sees a one-stage boost. Additionally, Z-Flatter confuses the target and enhances their Special Attack by two stages, an increase from the normal one-stage boost. Magic Coat can reflect this move, and its usage is impacted by the Muzzled status condition. Uniquely among Dark-type moves, Flatter possesses the ability to inflict confusion.

Title: Memento (move)

The Dark-type, non-damaging move Memento, known in Japanese as おきみやげ, debuted in Generation III. Its primary effect is causing the user to faint, subsequently reducing the target's Attack and Special Attack stats by two stages apiece. This move consistently lands, disregarding standard accuracy checks, even against foes in a semi-invulnerable state. The user's fainting is guaranteed even if a protection move obstructs Memento. Furthermore, the user faints even if Memento fails (e.g., due to a Substitute) or if it cannot alter the target's stats (e.g., from Clear Body or Mist). However, no fainting occurs if Memento's stat reduction is prevented solely because both target stats are already at -6.

Later on, Memento ceased to ignore accuracy checks, now possessing a 100 accuracy rating. Under current mechanics, the user will faint even when Memento fails to lower stats because both are at -6, or if the move misses. Conversely, the user does not faint if Memento fails completely (e.g., no target, a miss, or blocked by Substitute), if the target is immune as a Dark-type and Memento's priority is amplified by the user's Prankster ability (from Generation VII onwards), or if a protection move blocks it. Nevertheless, the user still faints if Memento lands but cannot affect the target's stats (e.g., due to Clear Body or if both stats are already at -6).

In Contest Spectaculars, Memento contributes to combinations, granting an additional three appeal points if Block, Mean Look, or Spider Web was performed on the preceding turn. When transformed into Z-Memento by a Darkinium Z, any Pokémon that switches in to replace the user on the same turn will have its HP completely restored, even if Z-Memento itself didn't successfully execute after its Z-Power effect was gained. A bug in version 1.0 of Sun and Moon prevented this Z-Move effect from activating. Memento will fail and the user will not faint if employed in a Max Raid Battle or Tera Raid Battle.

Within Mystery Dungeon games, Memento targets every enemy in the room, diminishing their Attack and Special Attack. Subsequently, the move reduces the user's HP to 1 and, if feasible, randomly teleports the user to another location on the current floor. The Attack and Special Attack stats are reduced to ¼ of their initial value, a reduction distinct from standard stat stages. Aurora Beam and Charm share this particular characteristic. This reduction can be applied repeatedly, down to a minimum of 1/128. Starting with Gates to Infinity, Memento's stat reduction became ½ of the original value, paralleling Aurora Beam and Charm.

In Generation III, the status screen mistakenly displayed Memento as having 100% accuracy, despite its actual mechanic of bypassing accuracy and evasion checks. Notably, Memento stands as the sole stat-lowering status move impervious to being reflected by the Magic Bounce ability. The move's name, especially its German translation, alludes to the Latin phrase "memento mori," which signifies "remember that you will die." During Generation VII, a specific glitch in the Battle Spot led to game crashes when Memento was employed as a Z-Move. Parting Shot experienced an identical issue. Consequently, both moves faced a temporary ban from Battle Spot battles, effective November 2016. The aforementioned glitch was rectified in the version 1.1 update.

Title: Facade (move)

The move Facade (known in Japanese as からげんき, or Bravado) is a Normal-type offensive attack that made its debut in Generation III. It was designated as TM42 from Generation III up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the designation of TM12. Facade deals direct damage. Its base power, typically 70, becomes 140 – effectively doubling – if the Pokémon using it is afflicted by poison, paralysis, or a burn. Ordinarily, when Facade is used, the burn condition's damage reduction for physical moves is still applied, unless the user possesses the Guts Ability. Conversely, the halving of physical damage caused by a burn is now bypassed when Facade is executed. This move delivers damage. Its potency is amplified if the user is afflicted with a detrimental status condition that doesn't hinder move usage; however, before the introduction of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, only burn and poison would boost Facade's power. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series, its power sees a twofold increase, while in Super Mystery Dungeon, it receives a 50% boost. Moreover, in Super Mystery Dungeon titles, Facade bypasses the Attack stat reduction typically incurred by the Burned status. According to its description before Version 2.10.0 (as of June 28, 2021), the move's power is doubled when the user is poisoned, badly poisoned, paralyzed, or burned, and it ignores lowered Attack from burns. A subsequent description, active prior to Version 2.20.0 (from April 27, 2022), stated that this move's power doubles under the same conditions (poisoned, badly poisoned, paralyzed, or burned) and also disregards the Attack reduction caused by burns. A known glitch in the Generation IV core series games causes the Pokémon's sprite to shift one pixel upward each time it executes Facade's animation. While it is conceivable to employ Facade through Sleep Talk even when the user is asleep, the move's damage or power will not receive its usual doubling effect under such circumstances.

Title: Focus Punch (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Focus Punch (Japanese: きあいパンチ, meaning Fighting Spirit Punch) is a Fighting-type attack that inflicts damage. Throughout Generation III, Generation IV, and Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it held the Technical Machine designation TM01. Presently, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, its TM number is TM192. This move is characterized by a lowered priority. When chosen, the Pokémon using Focus Punch commences focusing at the turn's outset. It then unleashes the attack at a priority of -3, unless a damaging move from another Pokémon strikes it prior to execution. If struck, the Pokémon's concentration breaks, preventing it from attacking during that turn. However, if the user's Substitute is attacked or if a status move impacts it, its focus remains unbroken. The message indicating its charging phase appears before any other move's execution. Historically, Power Points (PP) were consumed even if the user lost focus and failed to perform the move. Furthermore, if focus was lost, it would still register as the last move utilized for effects like Encore. The act of beginning to focus is distinct from executing the move itself, meaning order-dependent effects like Zoom Lens do not activate based on this initial action. Should a Pokémon be locked into Focus Punch by Encore after selecting an alternative move for the current turn, and if it hasn't yet acted, it will unleash Focus Punch using the priority of its originally chosen move. In this scenario, the charging message is absent, and the Pokémon still loses focus if it sustains damage from an attack during that turn.

In Pokémon Contests, Focus Punch serves as a valid second move in a combination, granting its performer 3 additional appeal points if Focus Energy was performed on the preceding turn. If a wild Pokémon possesses Focus Punch (acquired through Transform, Mimic, Sketch, or challenging the Battle Frontier), and the player's attempt to flee the battle fails while the opponent has chosen Focus Punch for that turn, the move will be instantly executed, bypassing its charging animation and message. Previously, if the user was struck before attacking, the game would display '<Pokémon> used Focus Punch!' prior to the message '<Pokémon> lost its focus and couldn't move!'. Contrarily, wild Pokémon performing this move while the player's escape attempt fails will consistently show the charge-up animation before attacking. The game no longer displays '<Pokémon> used Focus Punch!' before '<Pokémon> lost its focus and couldn't move!'. Currently, if the user loses its focus and is unable to execute the move, Power Points are not expended. Should the user lose focus, this action is disregarded when identifying the last move performed for mechanics such as Encore. In a more recent change, if a Pokémon becomes locked into Focus Punch by Encore after selecting a different move that turn and not yet having moved, it will now utilize Focus Punch at the priority of its chosen move, irrespective of whether it sustained damage that turn, and the charging message will not manifest. Attacks capable of a one-hit knockout do not cause Focus Punch's concentration to waver.

Within other game series, distinct from the core series, Focus Punch functions as a two-turn attack that proceeds to execute even if the user is struck during its charging phase. During its first turn, the user acquires the Focus Punch status condition, then launches its attack on the subsequent turn. Specifically in the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, this move's damage output is doubled after all other damage calculations are complete. Its nature as a two-turn move prevents it from being linked. The Adventure Squad series also restricts Focus Punch's use within a Pokémon Tower.

Among all Fighting-type moves, excluding Z-Moves and Reversal at its peak power, Focus Punch shares the distinction with Meteor Assault of possessing the highest base power. An All-Out Pummeling derived from Focus Punch matches Reversal at its maximum power for the title of highest base power among Fighting-type moves. Given that Focus Punch holds a higher priority than Avalanche, Revenge, Counter, Dragon Tail, or Circle Throw, these moves cannot disrupt the concentration of a Pokémon utilizing Focus Punch. Consequently, these moves, along with Focus Punch itself, are the sole attacks capable of hitting a Pokémon during the same turn it successfully executes Focus Punch, barring the use of Quash or After You.

In Generation III, a known glitch exists where a Pokémon preparing Focus Punch will fail to execute the attack. This specific glitch is activated in a Double Battle by choosing Focus Punch for the first Pokémon, subsequently pressing the 'B' button, and then switching out that initial Pokémon. During the actual battle, the first Pokémon will begin focusing in anticipation of Focus Punch before it proceeds to switch out. This behavior stems from the charging turn possessing a priority of +8, causing it to execute prior to the switch-out action.

Remarkably, Poliwag and Togekiss can acquire Focus Punch despite their lack of discernible hands required for the move. Likewise, Shroomish was capable of learning Focus Punch through a Move Tutor and breeding until prior to Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Smelling Salts (move)

Originating in Generation III, the Normal-type attack known as Smelling Salts (きつけ Resuscitation in Japanese) was previously called SmellingSalt before the release of Pokémon X and Y. While it serves as the exclusive signature move for Makuhita and Hariyama, other Pokémon species are able to learn it through the process of breeding. This move inflicts damage, possessing a base power of 60. When employed against a foe suffering from paralysis, its power is doubled; however, this effect simultaneously removes the target's paralysis. Should the target be protected by a substitute, SmellingSalt will only deliver standard damage, meaning the paralysis remains uncured and the power boost is not applied.

The move's nomenclature was later updated from SmellingSalt to Smelling Salts, concurrently with an increase in its base power to 70. Within Contest Spectaculars, Smelling Salts facilitates a combination, awarding an additional three appeal points if the user previously executed Force Palm, Glare, Nuzzle, Stun Spore, Thunder Wave, or Zap Cannon on the preceding turn. This attack delivers damage. Should the opponent be affected by paralysis, the move's damage output is doubled, and the paralysis condition is subsequently removed from the target. Unlike its real-world counterpart, which is used to restore consciousness, Smelling Salts specifically alleviates paralysis. The move Wake-Up Slap also performs this function. This peculiar effect of curing paralysis likely alludes to its Japanese designation, Resuscitation, which refers to the application of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to aid patients with paralysis who might otherwise rely on mechanical ventilators.

Title: Follow Me (move)

The Normal-type technique known as Follow Me (Japanese: このゆびとまれ, meaning "Stay With This Finger") debuted in Generation III and inflicts no direct damage. With a priority bracket of +3, Follow Me compels the user to become the primary focus for the turn's duration. This means all foe Pokémon are directed to aim their attacks at the user instead of their original recipient, even if that recipient was an ally. This redirection does not apply to moves incapable of targeting an opponent, such as Acupressure or Helping Hand. Should a specific move be unable to target the Pokémon drawing attention, it will proceed to strike its initial designated recipient. Allies remain unaffected by this diversionary tactic. The effect of drawing attacks ceases if the Pokémon acting as the focal point either faints or is withdrawn from battle. Even moves requiring a charging phase, like Fly and Solar Beam, can be drawn by Follow Me, provided the move is executed on the turn they would deliver damage. However, Future Sight and Doom Desire are only diverted by Follow Me if the latter is activated during the very turn those delayed-strike moves are initially chosen. This move also overrides the effects of abilities like Lightning Rod and Storm Drain, drawing Electric- and Water-type attacks respectively, even when Pokémon possessing these abilities are present. In instances where multiple allied Pokémon are attempting to draw attention simultaneously, the first Pokémon to use such a move establishes priority. If this primary user is beyond an attacker's range or stops being the focus, then subsequent attacks are directed toward the next available center of attention.

Moves designed to strike multiple Pokémon in a single battle are not affected by Follow Me. Although usable in Single Battles, it yields no practical effect in such encounters. Within Triple Battles, Follow Me diverts the assaults of all adversaries present on the field. Nevertheless, it can only redirect attacks from non-adjacent foes if those foes employ a long-range move. Sky Drop is immune to redirection by Follow Me. Should the Pokémon acting as the center of attention be lifted airborne by Sky Drop, its ability to draw attacks ceases. Furthermore, it does not redirect the actual impact of Future Sight or Doom Desire. The move Snipe Shot, alongside the Abilities Stalwart and Propeller Tail, entirely bypass Follow Me's effect, always striking their intended target. When empowered by a Normalium Z item to become Z-Follow Me, this technique additionally resets all of the user's previously lowered stat stages. Starting with Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Follow Me invariably fails when used in Single Battles.

Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, Follow Me bestows the Decoy status condition upon the user, causing enemies to prioritize attacking that Pokémon. This particular status persists for a duration of 21 to 50 turns. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's function changes: it teleports a randomly selected foe from the same room to the tile directly in front of the user, while simultaneously warping nearby allies to encircle that enemy. A musical piece shares this move's Japanese name as its title, incorporating both Pokémon and move-related wordplay. Curiously, in Generation III, the move's status screen incorrectly displays "100% accuracy," despite the fact that it targets only the user and therefore inherently bypasses all accuracy and evasion calculations. Paradoxically, despite its introduction in Generation III, no Pokémon originally from that generation can naturally learn Follow Me. The sole exception to this rule is an event-exclusive Jirachi, which became available during Generation V. In the Pokémon UNITE game, specifically within the Panic Parade mode, Tinkaton is observed employing this move at the beginning of every boss-containing wave, despite its evolutionary line being incapable of learning Follow Me in the core series games.

Title: Nature Power (move)

Nature Power, known in Japanese as しぜんのちから, is a Normal-type attack that made its debut in Generation III. From Generation VI up until Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it was designated as TM96. This move's effect is to transform into a distinct damaging attack, contingent upon the specific environment where the battle takes place. Notably, Nature Power benefits from priority granted by the Prankster Ability, even though it consistently invokes a damaging move in all games where Prankster exists. Any effects that are reliant on a move's type, such as Pixilate or those triggered by Gems, will activate according to the type of the move that Nature Power ultimately becomes.

Nature Power is unique in that it targets the user rather than an adversary. Consequently, in Double and Triple Battles, where direct target selection is not an option for this move, the resulting attack invariably strikes the opponent positioned directly opposite the user. An exception to this occurs in Pokémon Emerald, where the target is chosen at random. Should the opponent directly opposite the user have already fainted, the move will then randomly target one of the other remaining opponents. If Nature Power's transformation leads to a move that typically affects multiple Pokémon, it will target the Pokémon that such a move would normally hit.
