In the Generation III core series, the battle environment is determined through a sequence of internal checks, which take into account the player's current map and tile. This generally aligns the move's outcome with the player's actual location, though certain peculiarities are observed: for instance, water encountered within caves, despite its appearance resembling seawater, is categorized as pond water (though Camouflage yields the same effect in both). Battles against legendary Pokémon like Moltres and Ho-Oh occur in a cave setting, despite their outdoor locations. Deoxys is fought in either a mountainous or building environment. Furthermore, since fishing is performed from an adjacent tile, it can lead to various environments being registered, but never an actual water environment. During a Pokémon Contest, Nature Power utilizes AncientPower's animation, but without its associated effects.

For the Generation IV core series, the battle environment is broadly determined as follows: specific environments are applied in areas with tall grass, sand, snow, ice, water, and marsh. The marsh environment, however, is exclusively found in the Safari Zone, rendering its effects effectively unused. Unlike Generation III, fishing consistently triggers a water environment. Encounters with Honey Trees always result in a plain environment. In all other scenarios, the game examines the current map, leading to environments classified as cave, plain, rocky, tall grass, or urban. This system can sometimes create an apparent discrepancy with the map's visual representation; for example, Burned Tower, Embedded Tower, and Ilex Forest are treated as caves (while Lost Tower is not), and Cherrygrove City and Azalea Town are not considered urban (though Route 17 is). Additionally, in Pokémon Platinum, Pokémon HeartGold, and SoulSilver, distinct environments are present for battles against members of the Elite Four and the Champion, as well as within the Distortion World and each of the Battle Frontier facilities. Two unused environments exist: "puddles," which revert to the map's default terrain and share effects with tall grass, and an incomplete, glitched environment possessing a unique, specific effect. In a Pokémon Super Contest, Nature Power employs Tri Attack's animation, but none of its actual effects.

In Pokémon Battle Revolution, the particular move Nature Power calls depends on the Colosseum where the battle is underway. Here, Nature Power targets an adjacent Pokémon instead of the user (even if the resulting move affects multiple Pokémon), thereby enabling single-target moves generated by Nature Power to target an ally, a capability not previously possible. Within a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, Nature Power showcases Encore's animation without its actual functionality.

If Nature Power is enhanced by a Normalium Z to become Z-Nature Power, the chosen move will transform into its corresponding Z-Move. In the Mystery Dungeon series, Nature Power changes into a different damaging move based on the dungeon's terrain, which can shift during exploration. Despite being categorized as a status move in the Rescue Team series, it remains unaffected by Taunt, a mechanic that was altered from the Explorers series. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, it is also influenced by moves that modify the terrain. Furthermore, in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the Pokémon executing Nature Power uses various moves according to the type of stage it is deployed in.

In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, Nature Power's accuracy is displayed as 95%. However, this numerical value holds no practical consequence in battle, as Nature Power is guaranteed to call another move, which then uses its own accuracy statistic. From Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen onward, as well as throughout Pokémon Diamond and Pearl and subsequent titles, Nature Power's accuracy is represented by a dash ("—").

The Generation III games represent the sole instance where Nature Power can manifest as a status move, specifically Stun Spore. From Generation VI onward, all moves called by Nature Power are classified as special moves. In Pokémon X and Y, every move called by Nature Power is a single-target attack. However, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire and Generation VII, when Nature Power is activated in a volcanic area, it transforms into Lava Plume, marking the only occasion in Generation VI or VII where it can become a multi-target move. An observation from the official X and Y website depicts Diancie using Power Gem, despite not being able to learn it in Generation VI; given that the battle takes place in a cave, it is plausible that the move Diancie used was, in fact, Nature Power.

Title: Charge (move)

For the Japanese equivalent of the move named Charge, consult Take Down. Known in Japanese as じゅうでん (Charge), this Electric-type technique is non-damaging and made its debut in Generation III. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is designated as TM173. If the user's subsequent move is Electric-type, it will inflict double the standard damage. Should the next move not be Electric-type or be a non-damaging action, the effect of Charge will dissipate without being utilized.

Charge can initiate a Pokémon Contest combination, causing Shock Wave, Spark, Thunder, Thunder Wave, ThunderPunch, ThunderShock, Thunderbolt, and Volt Tackle to achieve twice their usual appeal if performed on the following turn. When the user's next move is an Electric-type Attack, its base power is doubled instead of the damage dealt (resulting in a virtually identical outcome). However, if the subsequent move is a Status move, a non-Electric-type Attack, or impacts a Pokémon using Protect/an immunity, the Charge enhancement is lost. Charge now also provides a one-stage increase to the user's Special Defense stat.

Additionally, Charge serves as a component in a Super Contest combination, granting Thunder Fang extra appeal points if used on the subsequent turn. It also forms part of a Contest Spectacular combination, causing any damaging Electric-type move (with the exceptions of Electroweb, Wild Charge, and Zap Cannon) to yield an extra three appeal points when executed on the next turn. When amplified by an Electrium Z, transforming it into Z-Charge, the user's Special Defense receives a further stage boost.

If the user's next move is not Electric-type, Charge's effect no longer fades, instead remaining active for the next move that *is* Electric-type. Nevertheless, if the user *attempts* to perform an Electric-type move, Charge will still be consumed even if external conditions, such as being asleep or flinching, prevent the move's execution. Multiple instances of the Charge effect – such as repeated uses of Charge while the user's Ability is Normalize, or using Charge followed by an activation of Electromorphosis or Wind Power – are not cumulative; the next Electric-type move will still only deal twice as much damage, and no residual effects will persist for subsequent moves. When a Pokémon with the Electromorphosis Ability takes damage from an attack, the effects of Charge are automatically duplicated. The user gains the "Charging" status condition, which lasts until the end of the next turn, causing their subsequent Electric-type move to have its damage doubled. This status will expire immediately after any move is used, regardless of its type.

Starting from Explorers of Time and Darkness, Charge also boosts the user's Sp. Defense by one stage. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Charge, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any captured Pokémon. Charge was observed as a move used by Boss Blitzle. Although an official website indicated that a wild Shinx knew Charge, it was never depicted using the move. In Generation III, the status screen erroneously listed the move as having 100% accuracy, despite it targeting the user (thereby bypassing accuracy and evasion checks).

Title: Taunt (move)

The Dark-type, non-damaging technique known as Taunt (ちょうはつ in Japanese) first appeared in the third generation of games. It functioned as TM12 from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned as TM02. A Pokémon affected by Taunt becomes unable to execute status moves, an effect that dissipates if the Pokémon is withdrawn from battle. Notably, in contrast to the majority of status moves, Taunt successfully impacts a target even if it has a substitute. Should a Pokémon that exclusively knows status moves be subjected to Taunt, or be taunted after utilizing a status move while holding a Choice item, it is compelled to resort to Struggle. During the turn Taunt is employed, if the user acts prior to the target and the target had chosen a status move for that same turn, the target's intended move will consequently fail.

In a Pokémon Contest, Taunt serves as an opening move in a combination, enabling Counter, Detect, and Mirror Coat to earn twice their standard appeal points if executed on the subsequent turn. Its duration currently ranges from three to five turns. Specifically, the effect lasts for three turns if the user moves before the target, or four turns if the target moved first. The move is now capable of being bounced back by Magic Coat and ceases to impact Me First. Pokémon possessing the Ability Oblivious, or those shielded by Aroma Veil, are immune to being taunted. Furthermore, within a Contest Spectacular combination, Taunt can grant an additional three appeal points to specific moves (Counter, Destiny Bond, Grudge, King's Shield, Metal Burst, Mirror Coat, and Spite) if they are performed on the turn immediately following. While it does not hinder a target from executing a status Z-Move, Taunt will incapacitate a Dynamaxed opponent from employing Max Guard. When amplified into Z-Taunt by a Darkinium Z, the user's Attack stat is boosted by one stage. A Clobbopus that has learned this particular move will evolve into Grapploct upon gaining a level.

In its execution, Taunt targets the immediate opponent and bestows upon them the "Taunted" status condition. A Pokémon afflicted with this status cannot employ status moves. Before Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, such Pokémon were also prohibited from using Seeds or Wonder Orbs. Starting with Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time and Darkness, this move became reflectable via Magic Coat. Although the list of moves it impacts generally aligns with the core series, some attacking moves (detailed further on) are categorized as status moves within these specific titles. Additionally, even though Assist, Fling, Metronome, Nature Power, and Rock Smash* are designated as status moves, they remained unaffected by Taunt in earlier iterations of the series; these discrepancies were later rectified in subsequent games. Moves indicated by a checkmark (✓) are unusable while a Pokémon is under the Taunted condition. Within the narrative of Stick This in Your Craw, Crawdaunt! II, Ruby's Pokédex disclosed that a wild Crawdaunt possessed knowledge of Taunt.

Title: Helping Hand (move)

First appearing in Generation III, Helping Hand (known in Japan as てだすけ, meaning 'Help') is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it holds the designation of TM03. As a high-priority move, Helping Hand serves to augment the damage inflicted by the user's ally during the current turn, specifically by a margin of 50%. However, its execution is unsuccessful if no ally is nearby, or if the targeted ally has already completed its action for the round. Nevertheless, it remains effective on an ally protected by a Substitute, or one in the semi-invulnerable phase of maneuvers such as Fly or Dig. Its current mechanic involves targeting an adjacent partner, thereby increasing the power of their executed move by 50% during the turn (which results in an effect largely identical to the original). Should a player's Pokémon attempt to use Helping Hand in a Horde Encounter, the move will inevitably fail. During a Battle Royal, however, Helping Hand gains the ability to target *any* opponent, successfully augmenting the power of that foe's move. When executed multiple times within a single turn, provided all instances focus on the same Pokémon, the power enhancements from Helping Hand are cumulative. Two applications of Helping Hand will escalate move power by 125%, while three will lead to a significant 237.5% increase. When amplified by a Normalium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Helping Hand, the user experiences a complete restoration of all their reduced stats. Conversely, within Tera Raid Battles, repeated uses of Helping Hand on the same target do not accumulate; only the initial 50% power boost is applied. This move can target all fellow teammates within the same room, excluding the user themselves, leading to a one-stage increase in their Attack and Special Attack attributes.

Within the context of Pokémon UNITE, Helping Hand functions as Blissey's initial offensive maneuver. Players acquire it at Level 6 by evolving Pound into Helping Hand rather than Egg Bomb. The user, alongside its allies, experiences a significant boost of 35% to both movement speed and basic attack speed, lasting for 3 seconds. Throughout this period, every basic attack performed by the user receives an enhancement. Upon the expiration of this bonus, any previously granted boosted attack is rescinded from the user. Reaching Level 12 further augments the move, causing all affected targets to deal 15% more basic attack damage.

In *Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion* and *Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom*, and indeed ever since its debut, Helping Hand has maintained the highest consistent priority among all moves, ensuring that a Pokémon executing it will always act first, save for specific instances: when another Pokémon employs Pursuit on a Pokémon being recalled, or when a Pokémon possessing the Prankster Ability utilizes a move with +4 priority. Within Generation III, the move's status display incorrectly indicated 100% accuracy, despite the fact that Helping Hand bypasses both accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Trick (move)

Known in Japan as トリック, the Psychic-type technique Trick originated in Generation III and deals no direct damage. It was obtainable as TR38 during Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently functions as TM109 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move's primary function involves the user exchanging held items with its opponent. Should only one of the participating Pokémon be clutching an item, that item is then transferred to the other. The target is considered to possess the Sticky Hold Ability for this interaction, even if it isn't holding an item.

Certain items are exempt from being swapped: Mail (except within the Japanese versions of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire); a Mega Stone, provided either Pokémon could utilize it for Mega Evolution; a colored orb, if either Pokémon could employ it for Primal Reversion; a Booster Energy, if either Pokémon is a Paradox Pokémon (with the specific exceptions of Koraidon, Miraidon, Gouging Fire, Raging Bolt, Iron Boulder, and Iron Crown); and a Wellspring Mask, Hearthflame Mask, or Cornerstone Mask, should either Pokémon be Ogerpon. Furthermore, Trick's execution may fail under other conditions depending on the game generation.

In specific competitive and challenge environments like Link Battles, battle facilities, Secret Bases, and the Trainer House, any items acquired via Trick are automatically returned post-battle. Uniquely in the Japanese versions of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, Mail could be exchanged, though this carried a risk of corruption. However, in international versions of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire and all subsequent game releases, Trick will entirely fail if either Pokémon is holding Mail. During these early generations, the move also failed if a Pokémon possessed the Multitype Ability or was holding a Griseous Orb.

While Trick no longer permanently alters the items held by a Trainer's Pokémon, items swapped during encounters with wild Pokémon remain permanently exchanged. Subsequent updates allow Trick to exchange the Griseous Orb, though it still fails if the Griseous Orb is held by a Giratina. Similarly, item exchanges are now possible with Pokémon that have the Multitype Ability, yet the move will fail if a Plate is held by an Arceus.

When enhanced by a Psychium Z to become Z-Trick, the user gains a two-stage increase in its Speed statistic. However, the move's item-swapping effect itself then fails immediately after gaining this Z-Power, as Z-Crystals are not eligible for exchange. Trick invariably fails when deployed in either a Max Raid Battle or a Tera Raid Battle, irrespective of any held items or the chosen target. (Nevertheless, it functions as usual against Dynamax Pokémon encountered in other battle formats.)

Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, a specific glitch—likely related to the handling of removed colored orbs—caused Trick to fail in versions 1.2.0 through 1.3.1. This occurred if the move was used either by or against Groudon or Kyogre, and additionally, if either the user or the target was not holding any item. This erroneous behavior was rectified in version 1.3.2.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Trick exchanges the user's and the target's held items. Before the release of Super Mystery Dungeon, the move would invariably fail if at least one of the Pokémon involved was not holding an item. Starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, however, the technique now successfully executes even if either the user or the target lacks an item.

Within the context of Pokémon UNITE, Trick serves as Hoopa's initial move, acquired at level 4 by evolving Confusion instead of Hyperspace Hole. This ability allows the user to forge a ring-based link between itself and an allied Pokémon, bestowing both with heightened movement speed and a temporary shield. When the user deploys Shadow Ball or executes a basic attack, the linked ally Pokémon simultaneously unleashes the identical move or attack through its ring. Hoopa can also target itself with Trick, resulting in its own movement speed increase and a temporary shield, alongside an enhancement to Shadow Ball's damage output. Upon reaching level 10, the move further gains the effect of restoring the linked ally Pokémon's HP whenever it inflicts damage upon an opposing Pokémon. Notably, when Hoopa assumes its Unbound form, Trick is substituted by Psybeam.

Despite Arceus being able to learn Trick through a Move Tutor in Generation IV, its usage by this Pokémon would consistently fail because of its Multitype Ability. In subsequent generations, however, Arceus can successfully employ the move, provided neither participant is holding a Plate.

Title: Role Play (move)

Originating in Generation III, Role Play (known in Japanese as なりきり, or "Change Appearance") is a Psychic-type technique that inflicts no damage. This maneuver causes the Pokémon using it to assume the Ability of its chosen target. It circumvents accuracy calculations, guaranteeing a hit unless the adversary is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. While unaffected by defensive measures such as Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, Role Play will nonetheless fail against a foe shielded by Crafty Shield.

This technique cannot be executed successfully if the opponent's Ability is Wonder Guard, Multitype, Illusion, Zen Mode, Imposter, Stance Change, Power of Alchemy, Receiver, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Power Construct, Battle Bond, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, Neutralizing Gas, Zero to Hero, Commander, Quark Drive, or Protosynthesis. Furthermore, it will also fail against targets with Trace, Forecast, or Flower Gift when used in Generation V or later games. Similarly, Role Play will not work if the user itself possesses Multitype, Zen Mode, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Power Construct, Battle Bond, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, Zero to Hero, or Commander.

Should the user be protected by Illusion, employing Role Play will cause that disguise to dissipate. A Pokémon that has altered its form because of Forecast will revert to its initial state upon successfully executing this move. If the target's Ability matches the user's, Role Play would historically succeed, but without triggering Abilities like Intimidate that activate upon being gained. However, under current mechanics, Role Play fails if the target possesses the same Ability as its initiator. This move now reveals the Ability the user possessed prior to its execution.

When augmented by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Role Play, this move bestows a one-stage increase to the user's Speed stat. In scenarios where a Pokémon might possess multiple Abilities, Role Play will swap the user's full set of Abilities for the target's. In a mechanic present before Gates to Infinity, back when Pokémon could hold two distinct Abilities, if the target possessed only one, the user's secondary Ability would be erased. Moreover, an attempt to copy either Wonder Guard or Stance Change will cause the move to fail. During Generation III, the status display inaccurately reported Role Play as having 100% accuracy, despite the fact that it inherently disregards all accuracy and evasion evaluations.

Title: Wish (move)

For information on the ending theme featured in the Korean dub of M22, refer to Wish (song). The move Wish, known in Japanese as ねがいごと (Wish), is a Normal-type status move that causes no direct damage, first appearing in Generation III. When Wish is executed, the Pokémon using it makes a wish. By the conclusion of the *following* turn, the Pokémon currently occupying the user's battlefield slot receives health restoration. While Wish can be activated even if the user's HP is at maximum, it will have no effect if the intended recipient Pokémon already has full HP. A Pokémon under the influence of Heal Block is prevented from using Wish. Should Wish already be in effect, but the Pokémon slated for healing is subsequently affected by Heal Block, the move's healing outcome will be nullified. Initially, Wish restored half of the affected Pokémon's maximum HP. As of more recent mechanics, Wish's healing effect activates prior to a Pokémon switching in to replace one that was knocked out during the same turn. Furthermore, Wish now restores half of the *user's* maximum HP, rather than that of the recipient. In Contest Spectacular combinations, Wish allows Bestow, Fling, and Present to each grant an additional three appeal points if utilized on the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Normalium Z into Z-Wish, the user's Special Defense stat increases by one stage.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Wish functions as Umbreon's second move. Players can acquire it by evolving Fake Tears at Level 6, offering an alternative to Snarl. This ability targets a nearby allied Pokémon, providing damage reduction until it triggers to restore their HP. If an ally benefiting from Wish is knocked out, the wish's effect transfers to another nearby ally. In scenarios where no allies are within range, Wish will instead apply its benefits to Umbreon itself. Upon reaching level 12, Wish evolves into Wish+, enhancing both its damage reduction and healing properties. Clefable's Unite Move, Wonder Wish, shares a similar characteristic of healing allies with Wish, though its overall mechanics bear a stronger resemblance to Metronome.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, encompassing Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad, using Wish confers the Wish status for a duration of 11 to 12 turns, which boosts natural HP regeneration to 3.3% of the Pokémon's maximum HP per turn. Beginning with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the user obtains the Wish status on the initial turn, then recovers 75% of its maximum HP to itself and all adjacent allies on the subsequent turn. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Wish, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any caught Pokémon. Wish was notably observed as a move used by Boss Slurpuff. In Generation III, an oversight in the status screen erroneously displayed Wish as having 100% accuracy, despite the fact that it targets the user and therefore bypasses accuracy and evasion calculations entirely.

Title: Assist (move)

Making its debut in Generation III, Assist (known in Japanese as ねこのて, or Cat's Paw) is a Normal-type move that deals no damage. Before Generation IV, it was exclusively associated with Skitty as its signature technique. The Pokémon employing Assist will perform a move chosen at random from the pool of eligible moves possessed by its teammates within the same party. Should identical moves be known by several Pokémon, each instance contributes to its likelihood of being selected.

Assist can notably utilize moves with no remaining PP, or those belonging to fainted Pokémon. It also possesses the ability to bypass temporary restrictions, such as those imposed by Disable, Heal Block, Taunt, or Torment, allowing access to moves otherwise unavailable to their original users. Should no eligible moves be present among the user's party members, Assist will invariably fail.

Historically, if a Pokémon's moves were temporarily altered in battle (e.g., via Transform or Mimic), Assist would only access its initial move set. In Multi Battles, Assist was once capable of drawing a move from an allied Pokémon even if it belonged to a different Trainer. A unique exception existed in Pokémon Colosseum, where Assist could not utilize moves from fainted Pokémon.

Subsequently, Assist's behavior evolved. It can no longer call moves from allied Pokémon owned by separate Trainers in Multi Battles. Initially, Assist was unable to select moves that would fail due to the effects of Gravity or Heal Block; however, this restriction has since been lifted, allowing such moves to be chosen. While it once had the capacity to utilize moves known by unhatched Eggs within the party, this is no longer the case.

In a wild Double Battle, a wild Pokémon employing Assist may access a move known by the other wild Pokémon, regardless of whether that ally is fainted. Conversely, when a Pokémon's moves are temporarily altered in battle, Assist now exclusively accesses its *currently* available moves, rather than its original set. Additional restrictions prevent Assist from calling moves that force the target to switch out, or those that grant a semi-invulnerable turn.

When enhanced by a Normalium Z to become Z-Assist, the outcome varies: if the randomly chosen move is a damaging one, it transforms into its corresponding Z-Move; however, if a status move is called, Z-Assist simply executes it with no added effects. Assist is unable to call itself or any moves designated by a checkmark (✓) within related documentation. In the context of the Mystery Dungeon series, Assist selects a move known by any Pokémon on the floor (before Gates to Infinity) or any teammate on the floor (from Gates to Infinity onwards).

In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Blue Rescue Team, unlike most non-damaging moves, Assist can benefit from Ginseng's boost, though this is not the case in Explorers of Time and Darkness. Regarding Taunt, Assist remains unaffected in Red Blue and Rescue Team, as well as Explorers of Time and Darkness, yet it *is* affected in Explorers of Sky.

A notable error in Generation III's status screen incorrectly displayed Assist as having 100% accuracy; however, since the move targets the user, it bypasses standard accuracy and evasion computations. Nevertheless, the move *executed* by Assist is still subject to the usual accuracy and evasion checks.

The Japanese designation for this move, ねこのて (Neko no Te), literally 'Cat's Paw,' draws its origin from the idiom 猫の手も借りたい (neko no te mo karitai). This phrase, meaning 'I would even like to borrow a cat's hands' or 'I would even like a cat to assist me,' conveys a sense of needing 'all the help I can get.' This theme is mirrored both in Assist's operational mechanics and its prevalence among various feline Pokémon.

The limitations imposed on Assist starting in Generation VI were likely implemented to address an exploit involving Pokémon with the Prankster Ability in Generation V. This tactic involved ensuring the rest of the party only knew a single eligible move callable by Assist, thereby guaranteeing that Assist would execute that specific move with boosted priority every turn. When this move was a target-switching move, it could repeatedly force the opponent to swap out without ever making a move, a strategy frequently paired with entry hazards for continuous damage. Alternatively, if the called move offered a semi-invulnerable turn, a user equipped with a Lagging Tail or Full Incense could exploit Assist to maintain a perpetual state of semi-invulnerability.

Title: Ingrain (move)

Ingrain, known in Japanese as ねをはる (Extend Roots), is a non-damaging Grass-type technique that made its debut in Generation III. Upon activation, the user plants roots firmly into the ground, enabling it to recover 1/16th of its maximum HP at the conclusion of every turn. This action anchors the user in place, rendering it incapable of escaping or switching out, even if it attempts to use moves like Baton Pass or U-turn. Rooted Pokémon are also immunized against forced switches, meaning items such as the Red Card and displacement moves like Roar or Whirlwind cannot compel them to leave the battlefield. A Pokémon under the effect of Heal Block can still successfully initiate Ingrain, establishing its roots as usual; however, it will not receive any healing benefits from the move while Heal Block remains active. The status effects of Ingrain are transferable via Baton Pass.

Historically, Ghost-type Pokémon affected by Ingrain were treated like any other type, unable to switch out. Similarly, a rooted Pokémon could not switch when the opponent's Pokémon fainted if the battle style was set to “shift”.

Ingrain causes the affected Pokémon to become grounded. This means the Pokémon becomes susceptible to Ground-type attacks, entry hazards (Spikes, Toxic Spikes, and Sticky Web – the latter being relevant only if Ingrain is acquired upon switching in via Baton Pass), and terrain effects. This grounding occurs irrespective of the Pokémon's Flying-type, if it possesses the Levitate Ability, is holding an Air Balloon, or is under the effects of Magnet Rise or Telekinesis. Consequently, Magnet Rise will fail if attempted by a rooted Pokémon, and Telekinesis cannot affect a Pokémon under Ingrain's influence.

In current mechanics, a rooted Pokémon is now able to switch out following the defeat of an opponent's Pokémon when the battle style is set to “shift”. Furthermore, Ghost-type Pokémon under Ingrain’s effects are now capable of switching out, though they maintain their immunity to being forced out by opposing target-switching moves.

When powered up by a Grassium Z, Ingrain transforms into Z-Ingrain, which additionally boosts the user's Special Defense stat by one stage.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Ingrain confers a status condition that makes the Pokémon immobile (though it can still attack) and prevents it from switching places with the team leader. In the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the Pokémon regains 10 HP at the end of the second turn, and then every five turns thereafter. This status typically lasts for 16-30 turns. For Gates to Infinity, the Ingrain status persists for 7 turns, and the Pokémon receives 15 HP at the end of every second turn. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the Ingrain status lasts 3 turns, providing 15 HP recovery at the conclusion of each turn. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Ingrain and all other status moves were exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any captured Pokémon; Boss Tangela was notably observed using Ingrain.

In Generation III, the move's status screen erroneously indicated 100% accuracy, despite Ingrain being a self-targeting move that bypasses all accuracy and evasion checks.

Title: Superpower (move)

The Fighting-type attack known as Superpower (ばかぢから, or Brute Force, in Japanese) first appeared in Generation III, dealing damage to its target. Trainers in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! could teach this move using TM49, while in Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was available via TR39. When deployed, Superpower delivers damage to its target, after which it reduces the user's Attack and Defense attributes by one stage apiece. In Pokémon Contests, Superpower serves as an effective follow-up, granting the user an additional 6 appeal points if Lock-On was performed during the turn immediately prior. The attack, Superpower, deals damage and causes the user's Attack and Defense to drop by one stage each. For players in Pokémon GO, Superpower functions as a Charged Attack, having been implemented and available for use since December 11, 2019. Notably, the German translation for Superpower shares its name with the German designation for the Ability, Huge Power. Although its Japanese name, ばかぢから, typically translates as "Great Power," it also carries the connotation of "Animal Power." This dual meaning might elucidate its learnset, as numerous Pokémon resembling beasts, like Luxray, are capable of acquiring this move.

Title: Magic Coat (move)

The Psychic-type technique known as Magic Coat (マジックコート in Japanese) debuted in Generation III and inflicts no damage. Before Generation IV, Magic Coat was exclusive to Spoink and Grumpig, though other Pokémon could acquire it through inheritance via breeding. For the duration of the current turn, Magic Coat intercepts and sends certain non-damaging moves directed at the user back to their origin. When a move aims at an entire team's side (such as entry hazard abilities), the entire side remains untouched if any Pokémon on it successfully bounces the move with Magic Coat; in such cases, only the *first* Pokémon possessing Magic Coat or the Magic Bounce Ability will execute the reflection. A move that has already been reflected cannot be reflected again by the new target's Magic Coat or Magic Bounce. Abilities that attract moves will redirect them prior to Magic Coat's activation. The effect of Magic Coat precedes that of Soundproof. As a move with increased priority, Magic Coat executes before the majority of other moves. Should the user be the final Pokémon to act in a turn, Magic Coat will not succeed. Specifically within this generation, during a Double Battle scenario where a reflectable move targets both Pokémon on one side: if the leftmost Pokémon benefits from Magic Coat, neither it nor its partner will suffer harm, and the move will be redirected to both opposing Pokémon. Conversely, if only the rightmost Pokémon is shielded by Magic Coat, its ally will sustain damage initially, before the move is reflected back towards the attacker. Also in this specific generation, Soundproof's effect activates before Magic Coat. In skirmishes involving multiple foes, when a reflectable move is directed at several Pokémon on a single side, the move will be bounced back *only* at the original attacker, irrespective of the position of the Pokémon protected by Magic Coat. Magic Coat's effect no longer dissipates upon reflecting a move, thus enabling it to deflect several moves within a single turn. When facing multiple adversaries, if a deflectable move is aimed at multiple Pokémon on one side, it becomes possible for the move to be reflected at *multiple* opponents. When augmented by a Psychium Z into Z-Magic Coat, the user's Special Defense stat increases by two stages. The subsequent roster of moves is susceptible to reflection by Magic Coat; this same list also applies to the Magic Bounce Ability. Magic Coat grants the user (and in Super Mystery Dungeon, its teammates) the "Magic Coat status," an effect that causes specific incoming moves to be sent back to the assailant rather than affecting the recipient. This ability only deflects the impact a move would have on the individual user, not the move's overall execution; for instance, a multi-target move will still impact other targets (provided they aren't under the Magic Coat status effect), even if the Pokémon that bounced the move becomes incapacitated. Magic Coat will not repel attacks if the attacking entity is not adjacent, if the move has previously been reflected, or if it originated from "cutting a corner." The status persists for either 11-12 turns or 6 turns. In Generation III, the status display inaccurately indicates that Magic Coat possesses 100% accuracy, despite it targeting the user and therefore not requiring an accuracy check. Nevertheless, any move that Magic Coat deflects must still pass standard accuracy checks. In Generation VII, Blacephalon gains the ability to learn this move through leveling up, but cannot acquire it from a Move Tutor.

Title: Recycle (move)

While this entry pertains to the move, for the Item card or Trainer card in the Pokémon TCG, refer to Recycle (Fossil 61). Known in Japanese as リサイクル (Recycle), this Normal-type move, which deals no damage, debuted in Generation III. It holds the designation of TM67 in both Generation IV and Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Recycle grants its user the ability to retrieve a held item, such as a Berry, that was previously consumed during the course of a battle. Items that have been merely transferred to another Pokémon (for instance, through a move like Thief) are not considered consumed in that process; thus, Recycle cannot restore them simply because they were transferred. Once an item is recovered, whether by Recycle or other effects like Harvest or Pickup, it ceases to be recognized as a consumed item. Consequently, it cannot be restored again until it is consumed once more. Recycle is ineffective at recovering items that were knocked off. Furthermore, it cannot restore a burst Air Balloon or an item that Incinerate destroyed.

In certain games, Recycle empowers the user to reclaim a held item that any Pokémon occupying its current field position had consumed earlier in the battle. The move fails if the user is already holding an item. Consumed items are tracked according to their position on the field. A Pokémon can recycle an item initially consumed by a different Pokémon, provided that item was consumed while the other Pokémon was in the user's current position. Each field position maintains a record of only one consumed item at a time; should a Pokémon consume an item, and then another Pokémon switches into that same position and consumes a different item, the original consumed item is no longer tracked and cannot be restored, even if the second item is successfully recovered. Items consumed by Fling, Natural Gift, Bug Bite, and Pluck are eligible for recovery. They are deemed consumed at the field position of the Pokémon that held the item immediately prior to the move's execution.

Recycle cannot retrieve items that have been removed by the move Knock Off. (In these specific generations, items knocked off remain visible on the summary screen and are restored automatically at the battle's conclusion.) If a Pokémon that has had its item knocked off attempts to use Recycle, it can successfully recover a consumed item (though not the item that was knocked off), but the exact mechanism of this interaction differs across generations. In Generation III, when a Pokémon with a knocked-off item uses Recycle, it recovers the consumed item, and its original knocked-off item is permanently lost (it will not be restored at the end of the battle). The newly recovered item then functions as its held item. If the Pokémon's original held item was Mail, this particular scenario has the potential to cause game corruption. In Generation IV, if a Pokémon with a knocked-off item utilizes Recycle, it regains the consumed item as its current held item. However, the summary screen will still list it as holding the knocked-off item. At the conclusion of the battle, its knocked-off item is restored, irrespective of whatever item it was holding at that time. If a Pokémon replaces its knocked-off item in this manner, it can indeed use the newly held item. Nevertheless, Trick and Switcheroo will fail if used by or directed at that Pokémon, and its held item cannot be stolen via Thief or Covet (nor can it steal items using those moves). Conversely, Bug Bite, Pluck, and Fling operate normally when interacting with the recovered item.

More recent mechanics dictate that Recycle allows the user to recover a held item that the user itself had previously consumed during the battle. This move will fail if the user is already equipped with a held item. Consumed items are tracked individually per Pokémon that consumed them and are not forgotten, even if the Pokémon is switched out. A Pokémon can only track one consumed item at any given time; if a Pokémon consumes an item, then later acquires and consumes another, the initial consumed item cannot be restored, even if the subsequent item is recovered and transferred elsewhere. Recycle no longer possesses the capability to restore items that were lost through Bug Bite and Pluck. Conversely, it can now restore the user's Berry if it was consumed as a result of Teatime.

When boosted by a Normalium Z to become Z-Recycle, the user's Speed stat experiences a two-stage increase. However, the move itself will subsequently fail after gaining this Z-Power, as it becomes impossible to restore its own Z-Crystal.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Recycle transforms a Used TM back into the specific TM it contained. Notably, in the Explorers series, it cannot reverse Used TMs that originally held the Recycle TM itself. From the Gates to Infinity installment onward, Recycle instead converts a Plain Seed into either a random Seed or an Oran Berry, with the exclusion of Life Seeds and Golden Seeds. Exclusive to Gates to Infinity, Recycle can yield Blinker Seeds, Joy Seeds, Doom Seeds, and status-curing berries. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, it can also produce Training Seeds, Encourage Seeds, and Energy Seeds.

In Generation III, the status screen inaccurately displays the move as having 100% accuracy, despite the fact that it targets the user (thereby bypassing accuracy and evasion checks entirely).

Title: Revenge (move)

Originating in Generation III, the Fighting-type attack known as Revenge (リベンジ in Japanese) inflicts damage upon its target. Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was assigned as Technical Machine TM42. While delivering an offensive blow, Revenge operates with reduced priority, meaning its user will typically act subsequent to Pokémon executing moves with standard priority. Should the opposing Pokémon have inflicted harm upon the user earlier in the current turn, Revenge's base power will be amplified, doubling its potency to 120. Upon activation, Revenge bestows the Bide status effect upon its user. During this state, which persists for 4 turns / 3 turns, the Pokémon is rendered incapable of moving, initiating attacks, or utilizing any items. Crucially, all damage sustained during this Bide period will be returned to the frontmost adversary at a multiplier of 2× / 1.5×. Notably, within the Adventure Squad series, Revenge is unavailable for use inside a Pokémon Tower. Furthermore, Incineroar employs Revenge as its down special ability, allowing it to absorb incoming damage and subsequently boost the strength of its subsequent attacks for a limited duration.

Title: Brick Break (move)

Known in Japanese as かわらわり (Tile Break), Brick Break is a Fighting-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in the third generation of games. It functioned as Technical Machine 31 (TM31) from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, its TM designation was TM13. This move initially removes protective barriers like Light Screen and Reflect from the adversary's field side (even if an ally is the target), prior to dealing its damage. The removal of Light Screen and Reflect occurs even if the Pokémon hit is immune to Brick Break itself, such as a Ghost-type. However, these screens are not dispelled if Brick Break fails to connect or if the target is safeguarded from the attack. A programming error causes the trainer AI to neglect checking for Light Screen when deciding whether to utilize Brick Break. In Pokémon Contests, Brick Break serves as a follow-up move in a combination, granting the performer 1 extra appeal point if Focus Energy was executed during the preceding turn. More recently, Brick Break has been established to clear Light Screen and Reflect from the specific target's side of the field, including when an ally is targeted. However, it subsequently ceased removing these screens if the target happens to be immune to Brick Break. The attack ultimately deals damage and dispels any Reflect or Light Screen conditions active on the Pokémon it hits. A distinct aspect of its mechanics specifies that Brick Break ignores, rather than removes, Reflect and Light Screen. Its damage rating is designated as ☆☆☆. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated: "Removes physical and special damage-reducing effects from the opponents' field of play." Professor Burnet noted in "No Stone Unturned!" that the Masked Royal's Incineroar previously knew Brick Break prior to acquiring Cross Chop. In "Blinding!! The End of the Battle," it was stated that Henry's Sirfetch'd, Lancelot, knew Brick Break, though he has not yet been observed employing it. Cynthia's Garchomp was publicly stated on the official website to possess knowledge of Brick Break, despite never being depicted using the technique. Within a Pokémon Super Contest, where no target Pokémon exists, Brick Break instead shatters a minor glass partition. Bonsly and Pupitar are capable of learning Brick Break even though they lack hands. Nevertheless, their subsequent evolutions are equipped with hands, and for Pupitar, its earlier form, Larvitar, also possesses them.

Title: Yawn (move)
Originating in Generation III, Yawn (Japanese: あくび) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move causes the target to become drowsy. By the conclusion of the subsequent turn, this drowsiness leads the Pokémon to fall asleep, unless it is already burdened by a persistent status ailment. Should a Pokémon switch out while drowsy, the effect is removed. It's impossible to relay drowsiness through Baton Pass. Yawn consistently strikes its foe, bypassing accuracy calculations, unless the opponent is currently using the semi-invulnerable phase of an action like Dig or Fly. The move will not succeed if the target is already under a major status condition or protected by a Substitute. Targets with the Abilities Insomnia or Vital Spirit, or those with Leaf Guard when harsh sunlight is active, are immune to Yawn; a Pokémon already drowsy but with Insomnia, Vital Spirit, or Leaf Guard (in harsh sunlight) will resist falling asleep. Using Yawn on a Pokémon that lacks Soundproof will fail if an uproar is occurring. Safeguard prevents Yawn from taking effect on its initial use, but a Pokémon already drowsy prior to Safeguard's activation can still fall asleep. Should a Pokémon fall asleep from Yawn during the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly, it will persist in that state until it tries to re-use the move, regardless of whether it wakes up. In Pokémon Contests, Yawn serves as an initiating move in a combination, leading Rest and Slack Off to earn twice their usual appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. While Yawn itself no longer fails during an uproar, the drowsiness it inflicts will not result in sleep if an uproar is active. It will also fail against targets with the Ability Comatose or those that are Meteor Form Minior; similarly, a Pokémon already drowsy but possessing Comatose or being Meteor Form Minior will not fall asleep. The move fails when aimed at a target under the influence of Sweet Veil, Electric Terrain, or Flower Veil. Furthermore, Sweet Veil, Electric Terrain, and Misty Terrain all similarly prevent Pokémon already made drowsy by Yawn from eventually falling asleep. In Contest Spectaculars, Yawn can be integrated into a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to specific moves (Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap) if executed on the subsequent turn. When Yawn is enhanced by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Yawn, the user's Speed attribute increases by one stage. It applies the "Yawning" status condition to the foe, leading them to fall asleep after three turns; if the target is engaged in a two-turn move such as Dig, falling asleep will immediately cancel that move. If the target is immune to sleep, this move will not succeed. Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition both influence Yawn. Within Pokémon GO, Yawn functions as a Fast Attack and has been accessible since December 8, 2017. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, status moves, Yawn among them, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Snorlax and opposing Slakoth were observed using Yawn. For Pokémon UNITE, Yawn serves as Snorlax's secondary move, unlocked at level 7 by evolving Rest into it, rather than Block. The user projects a sluggish, short-distance projectile that induces 2 seconds of Sleep in all struck targets. Upon reaching level 13, affected adversaries also experience a 3-second slow effect once they awaken. While the official website disclosed that several wild Slowpoke possessed Yawn, there was no visual evidence of them employing the move. Notably, Yawn holds the distinction of being the sole move with an English name starting with the letter Y. The move's conceptual origin lies in the contagious nature of yawns spreading between individuals. In Generation III, the status screen incorrectly indicated Yawn had 100% accuracy, despite the fact it inherently bypasses both accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Knock Off (move)

Knock Off, known in Japanese as はたきおとす, is a damage-dealing attack of the Dark type, originally introduced in Generation III. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it functions as TM181, while in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is TM045. This move primarily inflicts damage and has the distinctive effect of dislodging any item the target Pokémon may be holding.

Should an Exp. Share or Lucky Egg be removed, the Pokémon will nevertheless continue to gain experience as appropriate. Similarly, if an Amulet Coin is dislodged, the Pokémon still receives its corresponding prize money bonus. Notably, Knock Off possesses the unique ability to remove Mail, a feat often impossible for other item-manipulating effects.

Once a Pokémon's item has been affected by Knock Off, that item becomes unusable for the remainder of the current battle, even if the Pokémon is switched out, though it remains visible on the status screen. A Pokémon with a disabled item is prevented from acquiring or losing any further items; Trick and Switcheroo will always fail if either combatant possesses a knocked-off item, Thief and Covet cannot pilfer an item from such a Pokémon, and Recycle attempts by the affected Pokémon will invariably fail.

If Knock Off's damage triggers an item (such as a Focus Band, Focus Sash, or Colbur Berry) after the attack, that item's effect will activate prior to Knock Off removing it. In the specific case of a Focus Band, which is not consumed, it will be removed only after its protective effect has taken place. Even if the user faints while executing Knock Off (e.g., due to the target's Rough Skin or the user's Life Orb), the target's held item will still be successfully removed. Historically, Knock Off could not dislodge an item from Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability or if it struck a Pokémon's substitute. It also could not remove any item from a Pokémon with Multitype, nor a Griseous Orb from any Pokémon.

In Pokémon Contests, Knock Off can be employed as the second move in a combination, granting the user a single bonus appeal point if Fake Out was performed in the preceding turn.

A significant mechanical update dictates that Knock Off now physically removes the held item, rather than merely rendering it unusable. This change means the Pokémon can subsequently obtain another item if its held item was removed by Knock Off. Despite this, Recycle still cannot recover an item that has been removed by Knock Off. Wild Pokémon are unable to remove held items from the player's Pokémon.

Under current mechanics, if the user of Knock Off faints due to the target's Ability (Rough Skin or Iron Barbs) or a held Rocky Helmet, it will not successfully remove the target's held item. However, if the user faints from the recoil of its own held Life Orb, Knock Off will still dislodge the target's item. Furthermore, if Knock Off causes a Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability to faint, it can now successfully remove that Pokémon's held item. The move is also now capable of removing a held Griseous Orb from any Pokémon, with the sole exception of Giratina.

Knock Off cannot remove a Plate if the target is Arceus (Multitype itself does not prevent Knock Off), nor can it remove a Drive if the target is Genesect. The base power of Knock Off has been substantially increased from 20 to 65.

A wide array of specific items are entirely immune to Knock Off's effects: Z-Crystals from any Pokémon; Mega Stones or Orbs if the target could use them to Mega Evolve or undergo Primal Reversion; a Memory held by Silvally; Zacian's Rusted Sword; Zamazenta's Rusted Shield; any of Ogerpon's Hearthflame, Wellspring, or Cornerstone Masks; and Booster Energy from any Paradox Pokémon (excluding Koraidon and Miraidon), even if the item remains unconsumed because the Pokémon's Ability triggered from the appropriate weather or terrain condition instead.

If Knock Off is used on a Pokémon that is currently holding a removable item, its base power receives a 50% boost. This bonus damage will still apply even if the target possesses Sticky Hold or the attack hits a substitute, preventing the item from being removed. When used against a Dark-weak Pokémon holding a Colbur Berry, the berry will be consumed, but Knock Off will still gain the damage boost regardless. Conversely, if used against a Pokémon whose held item cannot be removed—such as one clutching its corresponding Mega Stone—this move will not receive the power increase. This boost is also not applied if the user of Knock Off targets a Pokémon holding an item that is inherently non-removable.

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, when used in a Max Raid Battle, Knock Off fails completely, dealing no damage. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet's Tera Raid Battles, if used on an ally, Knock Off deals damage but does not remove their item. However, if directed at a raid boss holding an item, that item will be removed as normal.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Knock Off dislodges the target's held item. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, it also inflicts damage, which is increased by 50% if the adversary is clutching an item.

Ruby's Pokédex confirmed a wild Crawdaunt knew Knock Off in the episode "Stick This in Your Craw, Crawdaunt! II". An official website indicated a Team Magma Grunt's Shuppet knew the move, though it was never depicted in actual use. In Generation III, a visual animation of a bag disappearing was displayed when Knock Off successfully removed an opponent's item.

Title: Endeavor (move)

Known in Japanese as がむしゃら (Daredevil), Endeavor stands as a Normal-type, damage-dealing maneuver that first appeared in Generation III. In the Pokémon Scarlet and Violet titles, it holds the Technical Machine designation TM205. This move compels the foe's HP to match the user's current HP. The HP disparity is then subtracted from the target's remaining HP. Should the user's HP be equivalent to or surpass the target's, this technique yields no effect. As type immunity remains unbypassed, Endeavor typically fails to impact Ghost-type Pokémon, irrespective of their HP levels. In Pokémon Contest combinations, Endeavor serves effectively as a second-stage action, granting the user an additional 2 appeal points if Endure preceded it in the prior turn. Furthermore, within a Contest Spectacular combination, its application awards an extra three appeal points when Endure was performed on the preceding turn. Essentially, Endeavor inflicts damage upon an adversary, reducing their HP until they match the user's own. No impact occurs, however, if the user's HP exceed those of the opponent.

Title: Eruption (move)

Known in Japanese as ふんか (Eruption), this damage-dealing Fire-type move was introduced in Generation III. Eruption's primary purpose is to inflict damage. It commands a base power of 150 when the user is at maximum HP, but this power decreases linearly corresponding to the user's remaining HP, resulting in 0 base power if the user has 0 HP. Specifically, its base power is determined by the formula ⌊150×HPcurrentHPmax⌋. Nevertheless, should this calculation result in a value less than 1, the move's base power will instead be set to 1. In battles involving multiple opponents, Eruption strikes all adjacent adversaries. Additionally, Eruption can destroy round rocks observed in the background scenery of battles. In Pokémon Contests, Eruption functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point if Earthquake, Endure, or Sunny Day was previously executed.

Beyond the main series games, Eruption's damage mechanics differ. In the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the final damage output is multiplied by ½ when the user's HP is between 75-51%, by 51/256 (approximately 0.2×) when HP is between 50-26%, and by 25/256 (roughly 0.1×) when HP is 25% or lower. For Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's power scales down proportionally to the user's HP. Furthermore, when a Gigantic Pokémon utilizes Eruption in Super Mystery Dungeon, it impacts all enemies present in the room.

Prior to Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), the description stated: "The lower the user's HP is, the lower the power of this move becomes." A later description, in effect before Version 2.20.0 (as of April 27, 2022), read: "The smaller the user's percentage of remaining HP, the lower the power of this move becomes." Information derived from the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer details that this offensive move's power is reduced to 80% if its user's remaining HP is at or above ½ but less than full; to 65% if HP is at or above ⅓ but less than ½; or to 55% if HP is less than ⅓. Its power remains constant when the user's HP is at its maximum.

Title: Skill Swap (move)

This document focuses on the core series move. For details on the Skill Swapper enhancement in Pokémon Shuffle, consult List of items in Pokémon Shuffle → Enhancements. Known in Japanese as スキルスワップ (Skill Swap), this non-damaging Psychic-type technique first appeared in the third generation of games. It was designated as TM48 during Generation III, Generation IV, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Skill Swap's primary effect is to cause the Pokémon employing it and its chosen target to swap their respective Abilities. Should a Pokémon whose Ability has been altered by Skill Swap be withdrawn from battle, its original Ability is reinstated. This move is unsuccessful if either the user or the target possesses any of the following Abilities: Wonder Guard, Multitype, Illusion, Zen Mode, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, Neutralizing Gas, Hunger Switch, As One, Zero to Hero, Commander, Protosynthesis, Quark Drive, Orichalcum Pulse, Hadron Engine, Tera Shell, Tera Shift, Teraform Zero, or Poison Puppeteer. It always connects, bypassing conventional accuracy checks, unless the opposing Pokémon is within the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. Certain Abilities, for example Intimidate, which trigger upon acquisition, will not activate a second time via Skill Swap if the receiving Pokémon has already possessed that specific Ability at any point since entering the current battle. Should both participants in the Ability exchange possess Abilities that activate with an effect or message upon being gained, their activations will occur in the standard sequence as if they were just sent into combat. Upon successful execution against an adversary, the game displays the newly acquired Abilities for both Pokémon. In scenarios where both Abilities involved in the swap have a gain-triggered effect or message (like Intimidate and Unnerve), the Pokémon that initiated Skill Swap will invariably have its new Ability activate before the target's. Skill Swap fails if the target Pokémon's Ability is identical to the user's. However, if both Pokémon possess identical Abilities, Skill Swap will nonetheless succeed. In cases where an Ability like Intimidate, which activates upon being gained, is swapped between two Pokémon already sharing that Ability, it will trigger for both creatures post-exchange. When transformed into Z-Skill Swap by a Psychium Z, this technique grants the user a one-stage increase in its Speed stat. It is ineffective against Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon. The move targets the Pokémon directly opposing the user, initiating an Ability exchange. Should a Pokémon possess a pair of Abilities, Skill Swap will exchange both, potentially resulting in the Pokémon retaining only a single Ability afterward. Furthermore, it will fail if the target holds Abilities such as Wonder Guard, Void Shadows, Dark Matter, or Stance Change. During Generation III, the move's status screen incorrectly displayed an accuracy of 100%, despite the fact that Skill Swap inherently bypasses all accuracy and evasion calculations. Even though Iron Valiant is capable of learning Skill Swap via TM, the move will invariably fail when used by this Pokémon, as its Quark Drive Ability is among those that cannot be exchanged.

Title: Imprison (move)

Known in Japanese as ふういん (Seal), Imprison is a Psychic-type status move that first appeared in Generation III. It served as TM44 during the Pokémon Sword and Shield era and is currently TM092 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. For the duration the user remains on the battlefield, opposing Pokémon are blocked from executing any move that the user also possesses. This restriction applies even to foes that enter combat subsequent to Imprison's activation, as well as to non-adjacent adversaries in Triple Battles. The effect of Imprison is tied solely to the user's present set of known moves, encompassing any new techniques acquired through leveling up during battle, or those learned mid-fight via Mimic, Sketch, or Transform. Should a Pokémon find itself with no other usable moves possessing PP, apart from those prohibited by Imprison, it will resort to using Struggle. Likewise, if compelled into using a specific move by Encore or a held Choice item, and that particular move is subject to Imprison's lockout, the Pokémon will then employ Struggle.

During the turn Imprison is unleashed, if an opposing Pokémon's action would follow Imprison's execution and it had chosen a move also known by the user, that opponent will be rendered inactive for the turn, failing to use the move (and thus not consuming its PP). Importantly, in this specific scenario, the affected Pokémon will not substitute its action with Struggle. Despite being blocked, moves otherwise affected by Imprison can still be invoked by abilities such as Assist, Me First, Metronome, or Sleep Talk. Conversely, Instruct will fail if it attempts to compel a Pokémon to use a move that Imprison has suppressed. Imprison holds no power to prevent adversaries from deploying the move Struggle. While Imprison is usable in both Max Raid Battles and Tera Raid Battles, the raid boss itself remains unaffected by its ability-suppressing properties. The effects of Imprison are not transferable via Baton Pass. When empowered by a Psychium Z to become Z-Imprison, the user receives a two-stage boost to its Special Defense.

Should no opponent share any move with the user at the moment Imprison is initiated, the move itself will fail. Nevertheless, Imprison *can* still function effectively even if, at its activation, no opponent currently possesses any of the user's moves. While Imprison may not have an immediate impact in such a scenario, its effect will nonetheless manifest if the user or opposing Pokémon subsequently alter their known moves (e.g., via Mimic, Sketch, Transform, or in-battle leveling up) or if a new opposing Pokémon enters the fray, thereby preventing them from utilizing the user's techniques.

Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series of games, Imprison targets the directly facing adversary, applying the Paused status condition for a duration of 5 to 12 turns, which renders the target incapable of any actions. However, starting with Gates to Infinity and continuing thereafter, Imprison instead affects all enemy Pokémon present on the dungeon floor, stopping them from employing any move also known by the user. The kanji displayed in Imprison's Japanese animations is 封, which constitutes the initial character of 封印 (ふういん, Fūin, meaning Seal), its Japanese designation. During Generation III, the move's status screen incorrectly indicated 100% accuracy, despite the fact that it targets the user itself (thereby bypassing all accuracy and evasion calculations).

Title: Refresh (move)

Should your search concern the in-game mechanic "Pokémon Refresh", please consult that specific resource. Identified in Japanese as リフレッシュ (Refresh), this Normal-type technique emerged in Generation III as a non-damaging action. It remedies the user of burns, poison, or paralysis. However, it proves ineffective against freeze or sleep; consequently, an attempt to invoke it via Sleep Talk will result in failure. In Pokémon Contest combinations, Refresh is eligible as a second move, granting the user an additional 1 appeal point if Sing or Water Sport was executed in the prior round. Amplified by a Normalium Z into Z-Refresh, this technique restores the user's entire HP. For allies located within the same room as the user, Refresh eradicates all negative status conditions and also eliminates move seals; it's worth noting these are not classed as status conditions. An error in Generation III's status display incorrectly presented the move as possessing 100% accuracy. This was inaccurate, given Refresh's self-targeting characteristic means it inherently disregards all accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Grudge (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Grudge (Japanese: おんねん Grudge) is a non-damaging move of the Ghost type. When Grudge is employed, if the user is knocked out as a direct consequence of an attack, the specific move that caused the user to faint will have all of its PP depleted. This particular effect only takes hold if the user faints before it can execute another action. Grudge notably does not activate against Struggle or against damage that occurs at the conclusion of a turn, encompassing harm from Leech Seed, poison, burn, Sandstorm, Hail, and Future Sight.

If Grudge is powered up by a Ghostium Z into Z-Grudge, the user commands attention, compelling all of the opponent's moves to target the user for the turn, whenever possible. Should the Grudge user faint due to a Z-Move, Grudge will produce no effect. However, if the user faints as a result of a Max Move, Grudge *will* activate, displaying the name of the base move that was powered up, and subsequently draining all PP from that move.

In Pokémon Contests, Grudge can be utilized as the second move in a combination, granting the user 1 bonus appeal point if Curse was executed in the preceding turn. It also participates in a Contest Spectacular combination, where the user gains an additional three appeal points if any of the moves Encore, Taunt, or Torment was used in the prior turn.

Prior to Super Mystery Dungeon, using Grudge would inflict the Grudge status condition on the user. If the Pokémon affected by this status is knocked out, the move that delivered the finishing blow will have all its PP depleted. Specifically, if the Pokémon is knocked out by a standard attack, the PP of the attacker's last used move will be depleted. This particular effect is nullified by a Reviver Seed, making it ineffective when applied to the team leader. This Grudge status condition endures for the entirety of the current floor.

From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, the user cuts its HP in half to acquire the Grudge status condition. The PP of the attacking Pokémon's move will be reduced to 0 when the user is attacked (and the user does not need to faint for this effect to trigger). This updated status condition lasts for 3 turns.

In Generation III, the status screen mistakenly reported that Grudge had 100% accuracy, despite it being a move that targets the user (thereby inherently bypassing accuracy and evasion checks). Despite its category matching this move in both English and Japanese, Runerigus is unable to learn Grudge.

Title: Snatch (move)

This entry details the move Snatch. For its status condition counterpart in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, consult Status condition (Mystery Dungeon). The Dark-type, non-damaging move Snatch (Japanese: よこどり Snatch) made its debut in Generation III. It served as TM49 throughout Generation III and Generation IV. Snatch operates with increased priority. Upon its successful activation, if any other Pokémon—including an ally—attempts to use a beneficial status move, Snatch's user will commandeer that move, executing it personally instead of the move's intended user. Snatch can only intercept the very first susceptible move performed by another Pokémon within the same turn.

If multiple Pokémon attempt Snatch during a single turn, the Snatch from the fastest Pokémon activates initially. Traditionally, the pilfered move would then be re-stolen by the next fastest Pokémon's Snatch, leading to only the slowest Snatch user ultimately receiving the stolen effect. However, Snatch has been altered and can no longer intercept moves that have already been stolen by another Pokémon's Snatch within the same turn. The order in which moves are successfully stolen aligns with the execution order of Snatch itself.

In prior game mechanics, if Acupressure was stolen, its benefits would invariably be applied to the Snatch user, irrespective of its original target. Similarly, a stolen Psych Up would target the Pokémon from which it was most recently taken. Currently, however, both Acupressure and Psych Up are immune to Snatch. All moves that are susceptible to Snatch either affect solely the user or benefit the user's entire team. Rest and Swallow can now only be appropriated by Snatch if they would produce an effect when used by their original user (regardless of whether they would have an effect on the Snatch user). Should Swallow be stolen when the Snatch user is not under the influence of Stockpile, they will nonetheless restore 25% of their maximum HP. Snatch cannot intercept Z-Moves, even if it would typically be able to steal their base move. When empowered by a Darkinium Z into Z-Snatch, the user's Speed stat is boosted by two stages.

According to their internal data, the Generation VIII moves Clangorous Soul, No Retreat, Stuff Cheeks, Life Dew, Victory Dance, Lunar Blessing, and Take Heart are marked as being affected by Snatch. Due to Snatch being unavailable for use in any core series games from Generation VIII or IX, these specific moves are not included in relevant game tables.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, employing Snatch bestows a temporary "Snatch" status condition upon the user, enabling them to steal certain moves from any Pokémon on the floor, be it an ally or an enemy. Only one Pokémon on the floor can possess this status at any given time; another Pokémon using Snatch will remove the status from its current holder. The Snatch status endures for 4-5 turns, or 6 turns depending on the specific game. Moves susceptible to this status largely correspond to those in the main series. Unlike in the main series games, Imprison cannot be snatched in Mystery Dungeon. Intriguingly, in the Explorers series, the list of affected moves closely mirrors the mechanics of the Generation V games; for instance, Psych Up cannot be snatched, while Aqua Ring, Healing Wish, Lucky Chant, Lunar Dance, and Magnet Rise can. Moves that would ordinarily affect other teammates (e.g., Mist and Safeguard) will exclusively affect the Pokémon bearing the Snatch status, even if that Pokémon is a teammate. The user will lose the Snatch status after appropriating Rest, but will regain it upon waking up.

The move's description prior to version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) erroneously stated: "Returns the target's raised stats to normal and raises the user's stats by that amount." In Generation III, the status screen incorrectly indicated Snatch possessed 100% accuracy, despite it being a self-targeting move and thus ignoring accuracy and evasion checks. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, despite Snatch being unusable, the Gym Leader Fantina's Banette inexplicably knew Snatch during a rematch. This oversight was rectified in the 1.1.3 update, which replaced Snatch with Payback.

Title: Secret Power (move)

The Normal-type, damage-dealing attack known as Secret Power (Japanese: ひみつのちから Secret Power) made its debut in Generation III. It held the TM43 designation throughout Generations III and IV, then became TM94 specifically for Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire.

Audino claimed Secret Power as its exclusive move in Generations V and VII, though other species were able to acquire it through breeding. This attack deals damage and carries a 30% probability of triggering a secondary effect. Both this effect and the move's visual animation depend on the immediate surroundings. The specific effect and animation linked to each environment vary across different games, as does the set of environments the game identifies.

Within the Generation III core series, environmental factors are determined through a sequence of checks, incorporating the player's present tile and map. This generally aligns the move's behavior with the player's physical location, though certain anomalies exist: cave water, despite appearing as seawater, is categorized as pond water. Battles against Moltres and Ho-Oh occur in a cave setting, even though these encounters take place outdoors. Deoxys encounters are set in either a mountainous or building environment. Furthermore, since fishing is performed from a tile adjacent to water, it can lead to diverse environments, yet the actual water environment is never triggered.

During a Pokémon Contest, Secret Power utilizes Needle Arm's animation, but not its associated effects. The ability Serene Grace causes Secret Power's secondary effect probability to double. Secret Power consistently displays Needle Arm's animation, irrespective of the current location. Specific environments are imposed by tall grass, sand, snow, ice, water, and marsh (the latter exclusively appearing in the Safari Zone, rendering its effects unutilized). Unlike Generation III, fishing consistently triggers a water environment. Encounters at honey trees invariably result in a plain environment.

In all other scenarios, the game scrutinizes the current map. This analysis can lead to a cave, plain, rocky, tall grass, or urban environment. Occasionally, this produces an apparent discrepancy with the map's visual representation; for instance, Burned Tower, Embedded Tower, and Ilex Forest are classified as caves (whereas Lost Tower is not), while Cherrygrove City and Azalea Town are not considered urban (though Route 17 is).

Pokémon Platinum, alongside Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, introduced additional specialized environments for confrontations with each Elite Four member and the Champion, as well as within the Distortion World and various Battle Frontier facilities. These platforms utilize the identical secondary effect for Secret Power as an urban setting, but, excluding Aaron/Will's room, they feature a distinct animation and are categorized further down as "most special battle platforms." Two environments remain unutilized: puddles (since these tiles instead default to the map's primary terrain), which share the same effects as tall grass, and a unique, unfinished, and glitchy environment whose specific effect is detailed later.

For a Pokémon Super Contest, Secret Power employs Body Slam's animation (but not its effects). In Pokémon Battle Revolution, Secret Power's animation remains consistent across all Colosseums; however, the move's secondary effects vary based on the specific Colosseum of battle. In this game, Secret Power's secondary-effect chance is not augmented by Serene Grace. (Later versions clarified: Secret Power's secondary-effect chance *is now* doubled by Serene Grace, though it remains unaffected by the rainbow effect from combining Water Pledge and Fire Pledge.) During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, Secret Power uses Last Resort's animation (but not its effects).

Secret Power enables the user to establish a personal Secret Base. Activating it on specific trees causes a vine to descend. Employing it against indented walls reveals a cave entrance. Using it on particular bushes opens an access point. This move later lost its out-of-battle utility for generating a Secret Base. However, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Secret Power regained its functionality for creating Secret Bases at designated Secret Spots. Its field usage is restricted until the player acquires its Technical Machine, TM94.

In Mystery Dungeon titles, Secret Power deals damage and has the potential to inflict a secondary effect determined by the dungeon's prevailing terrain. Starting with Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, it additionally reacts to moves that alter terrain. Prior to Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, its animation was unvarying, employing visual elements akin to Needle Arm's. Subsequently, Secret Power received a distinct animation that consistently appears regardless of the dungeon's terrain. The secondary effect is contingent on the type of stage where it's employed. All other characteristics remain uniform across different areas.

A Zangoose in the Battle Castle was shown to know Secret Power during "Getting the Drop on Gallade I," though it never performed the move. This move holds the distinction of being one that can possess a freeze chance exceeding 10%, with the exceptions of Blizzard in Japanese Generation I handheld games and Sheer Cold in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Secret Power exclusively adopts animations from other damage-dealing moves. Specifically, in Generations IV and V, as well as Pokémon X and Y, Secret Power only borrows animations from single-target moves.

Within Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Secret Power displays Incinerate's animation in volcanic regions and Swift's animation when used in space. These are the sole occasions in Generation VI where it adopts a multi-target move's animation.
