Consequently, it exhibits a distinct animation during Horde Encounters in volcanic areas (given that space lacks multi-opponent battles). While Incinerate launches fireballs at all foes, Secret Power directs only one fireball at its target. This marks the singular instance of Secret Power having a unique animation in the core series. For Generation VII, Secret Power again employs Incinerate's animation when utilized in volcanic environments; this stands as the generation's only instance of it mimicking a multi-target move. Differing from Generation VI, it adopts Incinerate's exact animation during multi-opponent battles, which leads to both adversaries reacting to the impact even though the move targets only one.

Title: Dive (move)

Known in Japanese as ダイビング (Diving), Dive is a damage-dealing Water-type attack first introduced in Generation III. Its mechanics involve: the user submerges underwater, gaining protection from most moves during the first turn, which is characterized as a semi-invulnerable state. On the second turn, the user emerges to unleash its attack. However, submerged Pokémon are vulnerable to Surf and Whirlpool, which inflict double the standard damage across all generations, with the exception of Generation IV where the *move's power* is doubled instead (resulting in a virtually identical outcome). A Pokémon using Dive can also be struck underwater if it was previously targeted by Lock-On or Mind Reader, or if either the attacker or the diving Pokémon possesses the No Guard ability. Environmental effects such as Sandstorm, hail, and Shadow Sky will not cause damage during the semi-invulnerable turn. If the user holds a Power Herb, this item is consumed, and Dive is executed in a single turn. Should a Cramorant with Gulp Missile use Dive (or Surf), it will capture prey and change forms, unless it has already caught prey or is Dynamaxed. Pokémon are capturable during Dive's semi-invulnerable stage. Dive can serve as the inaugural move in a Pokémon Contest combination, causing Surf to achieve double its usual appeal if employed on the subsequent turn. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Rain Dance or Surf was used in the preceding turn. However, Pokémon can no longer be caught during Dive's semi-invulnerable stage in current games.

Beyond battle, Dive allows the player to submerge in dark patches of water, requiring the player to be traveling on water using Surf. Conversely, if the player is already underwater, Dive enables them to return to the surface in areas illuminated by light from above. This move can be activated either directly from the party, or by pressing A while in an appropriate location (either surfing over a dark patch or diving beneath a lit area). The only regions where these spots (and the HM itself) have appeared are Hoenn and Unova. Consequently, Dive has only functioned as an HM in Generations III, V, and VI. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Pokémon Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, the Mind Badge is a prerequisite for using Dive outside of battle.

In its first turn, Dive bestows the user with a "diving" status. While underwater, the Pokémon cannot be struck by most moves, Surf and Whirlpool being the exceptions. Projectiles will pass through the user as if their tile were empty. On the second turn, the user assaults the enemy in front. In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, the move's damage is doubled at the conclusion of damage calculation. This move is usable only on a water tile (including shallow water in water dungeons); it will fail if attempted on any other tile type. If water is drained by a Drought Orb mid-Dive, the user will still attempt to attack but fail due to no longer being on a water tile. Being a two-turn move, Dive cannot be linked. Dive is unusable in a Pokémon Tower within the Adventure Squad series.

In Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, Dive functions as a TM. However, only Mew is technically compatible with it, as it was not a TM in the Generation IV core series games. The only other Pokémon capable of using it are those that already learn Dive via level-up, owing to the game's logic allowing Pokémon to learn TM moves if they naturally acquire them through leveling, even without direct TM compatibility.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Dive is Cramorant's initial move, obtained by reaching level 6 and upgrading Whirlpool into it instead of Surf. The user transforms a designated area into a puddle, then plunges into it, shoving and damaging enemies within the area. As they emerge from the puddle, the user catches prey for Gulp Missile. This ability holds 3 uses in reserve, with a 1.5-second cooldown between charges, which can be reduced with cooldown reduction. At level 13, the move's damage is increased. Gyarados's Unite Move, Dragon Current, shares similarities with Dive.

In 'The Beginning of the End with Kyogre & Groudon XII', Sapphire's Wailord, Lorry, was mentioned to know Dive, though its execution occurred off-panel. In Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom, unlike Whirlpool and Defog—which Pokémon can freely retain when transferred to Generation V from a game where they aren't HMs—a Pokémon knowing Dive is prevented from being migrated to Generation IV, even from Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, games where Dive is neither usable, required, nor available to the player without trading.

Dive holds the distinction of being the only HM move not yet featured in the animated series. It also boasts the most individual numbers of any HM move, having appeared as HM06, HM07, and HM08 in different generations. Curiously, the European Spanish dub has occasionally depicted Trainers instructing airborne Pokémon to use Dive, despite their inability to learn it. This is an error stemming from translating the English script and confusing a general command to "dive" with the specific move name.

Title: Arm Thrust (move)

A Fighting-type attack that deals damage and strikes multiple times, Arm Thrust (つっぱり Thrust in Japanese) made its debut in Generation III. Prior to Generation V, it was recognized as a unique move for Makuhita and Hariyama. When used, Arm Thrust assaults the opponent, connecting 2 to 5 times. The specific probabilities dictate the number of hits: 37.5% for two strikes, 37.5% for three, 12.5% for four, and 12.5% for five. Every individual strike carries an identical probability of scoring a critical hit. Even if a substitute is broken, Arm Thrust can persist with its assault. Moves like Bide and Counter will only register the final strike of Arm Thrust. For targets possessing a contact-activated Ability, each hit from Arm Thrust is treated separately, allowing the Ability to trigger repeatedly. With the Skill Link Ability, a Pokémon will consistently land all five hits of Arm Thrust, assuming the move doesn't miss entirely. In Pokémon Contests, Arm Thrust serves as the second move in a combination, awarding the user an additional 2 appeal points if Fake Out or Focus Energy was executed on the preceding turn.

When the user holds a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every successive hit has an independent chance to make the target flinch. A revised probability distribution for hits shows 35% for two strikes, 35% for three, 15% for four, and 15% for five. Consequently, the average total base power becomes 46.5. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, has the potential to trigger multiple times to avert fainting, though each activation attempt is separate. If the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1, Sturdy will activate with every single hit. Should the target possess Weak Armor or Stamina, each incoming strike will trigger the Ability. When the target equips a Rocky Helmet, the user suffers damage for every individual strike landed.

In its damaging effect, Arm Thrust impacts the target 2-5 times during each activation. Each individual hit undergoes its own accuracy check; however, the move proceeds even if one of these checks fails. The critical-hit rate for this move is lowered (specifically to 2% within the Rescue Team and Explorers series). From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, the activation of a Reviver Seed will cause the move to cease.

Title: Camouflage (move)

First appearing in Generation III, the Normal-type technique known as Camouflage (Japanese: ほごしょく, or 'Camouflage') is a non-offensive maneuver. It served as Staryu's exclusive signature move until Generation IV. This move allows the user to alter its elemental type, adapting it to match the prevailing battlefield environment. Both the specific type granted by each environment and the environments themselves that the move acknowledges exhibit variation across different game titles.

Within the core series titles of Generation III, the environment for Camouflage is determined via a sequence of checks, primarily factoring in the player's current map and specific tile. This generally results in the Pokémon's type aligning with the player's visible location, though certain peculiarities exist. For instance, water encountered inside caves, despite its resemblance to seawater, is categorized as pond water; however, this distinction doesn't alter Camouflage's effect. Battles against Moltres and Ho-Oh, despite their outdoor settings, occur within a cave environment. Deoxys encounters are classified as either mountainous or building environments. Furthermore, when fishing, which is performed from an adjacent tile, various environments can be triggered, but a 'water' environment itself is never the result.

The core Generation IV games establish the battle environment by a different approximate logic: distinct environments are applied for tall grass, sand, snow, ice, water, and marsh. Notably, the marsh environment is exclusive to the Safari Zone, rendering its specific effects for Camouflage unobtainable in standard play. Contrary to Generation III, fishing in Generation IV consistently yields a water environment. Battles initiated via Honey Trees invariably present a plain environment. Should none of these specific conditions be met, the game evaluates the current map, potentially resulting in a cave, plain, rocky, tall grass, or urban environment. This system can sometimes lead to discrepancies between the visual map and the determined environment; for example, Burned Tower, Embedded Tower, and Ilex Forest are designated as cave environments (while Lost Tower is not), and Cherrygrove City and Azalea Town are not classified as urban (though Route 17 is).

In *Pokémon Platinum*, *HeartGold*, and *SoulSilver*, additional specialized environments are introduced for encounters with each member of the Elite Four and the Champion, in addition to unique settings within the Distortion World and various Battle Frontier facilities. The game data contains two environments that remain unused: 'puddles,' where tiles instead revert to the map's default terrain, despite having effects identical to tall grass; and an incomplete, glitchy environment possessing a distinct effect.

In *Pokémon Battle Revolution*, the resultant type is contingent upon the specific Colosseum hosting the battle. When Camouflage is enhanced into Z-Camouflage by a Normalium Z, the Pokémon's evasiveness stat receives a one-stage boost.

In dungeon environments, Camouflage modifies the user's type based on the dungeon's prevailing terrain or any moves that alter the terrain. This transformation persists until the Pokémon advances to the subsequent floor.

In the game *Pokémon Pokopia*, Ditto gains the ability to use Camouflage after being taught the move by a Zorua in the Bleak Beach area. This enables Ditto to assume the form of an object immediately ahead of it. While Ditto maintains this transformation, its PP continuously diminishes.

During Generation III, the move's status screen incorrectly displays an accuracy of 100%. This is an oversight, as Camouflage targets the user directly and therefore bypasses any accuracy or evasion calculations.

Beginning with Generation IV, Camouflage's effect causes the user to adopt the same type that the move Nature Power would call forth in the respective environment. Curiously, within *Pokémon Pokopia*, Zorua is depicted using Camouflage and teaching it to Ditto, despite Zorua itself never having been able to legitimately learn this particular move.

Title: Tail Glow (move)

Tail Glow, identified in Japanese as ほたるび (Firefly Glow), is a Bug-type status move that first appeared in Generation III. This move causes no damage. Until Generation IV, it served as the signature move for Volbeat.

Its effect on stats has evolved: initially, Tail Glow boosted the user's Special Attack by two stages, but starting from Generation IV, this increment increased to three stages. Powering it up with a Buginium Z transforms it into Z-Tail Glow, which also gains the property of resetting all of the user's lowered stats. Snatch is capable of affecting this move.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Tail Glow, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained by any caught Pokémon. Boss Volbeat notably utilized Tail Glow.

An erroneous detail appeared on the move's status screen in Generation III, claiming 100% accuracy. This was incorrect as Tail Glow targets the user and therefore inherently ignores accuracy and evasion assessments.

Manaphy, despite not being a firefly or possessing a tail, can learn Tail Glow, presenting a contradiction with the move's Japanese and English names. This learning capability might stem from its head antennae or allude to the bioluminescence found in numerous deep-sea creatures. Furthermore, Xurkitree, also not a firefly, is another Pokémon capable of learning this move; while it features a "tail," this appendage does not emit light.

Title: Luster Purge (move)

Luster Purge (Japanese: ラスターパージ Luster Purge) made its debut as a damaging Psychic-type attack in Generation III. This particular move is exclusive to Latios. Beyond dealing damage, Luster Purge possesses a 50% probability of diminishing the opponent's Special Defense statistic by a single stage. Within Pokémon Contests, Luster Purge serves as a potential second move in a combination, bestowing 2 additional appeal points upon the user if Calm Mind was executed during the preceding turn. From Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, players were unable to choose Luster Purge during combat. However, commencing with Version 1.3.0, it became fully operational and maintained its prior generational functionality. Similarly, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Luster Purge was unselectable for battle across Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2. As of Version 3.0.0, its use is permitted, and its effects mirror those from earlier generations. Moreover, Luster Purge's base power has been boosted to 95. It continues to deliver damage and possesses the chance to decrease the foe's Special Defense by a single stage. Within Pokémon GO, Luster Purge functions as a Charged Attack, becoming accessible on May 3, 2022. During the episode "All Things Bright and Beautifly!", Jessie commanded her Seviper to employ Luster Purge; however, as Seviper is incapable of learning this technique, the command could not be fulfilled. While Luster Purge's actual usage in the animated series didn't occur until near the conclusion of Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, its initial verbal reference was made by Jessie in "All Things Bright and Beautifly!", early in Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire.

Title: Mist Ball (move)

Mist Ball (Japanese: ミストボール Mist Ball) is a Psychic-type damaging technique that made its debut in Generation III. It stands as Latias's exclusive signature move. This attack deals direct damage and carries a 50% probability of decreasing the target's Special Attack stat by a single stage. Notably, opponents possessing the Bulletproof ability are immune to its effects. In Pokémon Contest combinations, Mist Ball can be employed as the second move, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point if Calm Mind was executed during the preceding turn.

During Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Mist Ball was unavailable for selection in battles. Its usability was reinstated with Version 1.3.0, performing as it did in previous generations. Similarly, from Versions 1.0 to 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Mist Ball could not be chosen in combat. However, starting with Version 3.0.0, its functionality returned, operating consistently with prior games, and its base power was concurrently elevated to 95. In Pokémon GO, Mist Ball operates as a Charged Attack, having become available on May 3, 2022.

Title: Feather Dance (move)

Making its debut in Generation III, Feather Dance (Japanese: フェザーダンス Feather Dance) is a Flying-type move that inflicts no damage. Before Pokémon X and Y, its name was stylized as FeatherDance. Until Generation IV, this move was exclusive to Pidgey's evolutionary family, though other Pokémon had the potential to learn it through breeding. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Feather Dance is designated as TM216. When used, Feather Dance causes the target's Attack stat to decrease by two stages. If boosted by a Flyinium Z, transforming it into Z-Feather Dance, the user's Defense stat receives a one-stage increase. The in-game description for Feather Dance, prior to Version 2.10.0 (issued on June 28, 2021), stated: "Sharply lowers the target's Attack," which corresponds to the target's Attack being reduced by two stages. Within the mobile game Pokémon GO, Feather Dance functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible to players since May 31, 2021. As part of Pokémon UNITE, Feather Dance is Cramorant's second move. It involves the user casting a circular area of feathers at a chosen location. Within this zone, adversaries experience a 20% reduction in movement speed and a 30% decrease in their attack speed. Upon reaching level 4, this ability is superseded by either Hurricane or Air Slash.

Title: Teeter Dance (move)

This Normal-type technique, known in Japan as フラフラダンス (Dizzy Dance), made its debut in the third generation of Pokémon games, providing no direct damage output. Until Generation V, Spinda uniquely claimed this move as its own, although other species were capable of learning it through the breeding mechanic. It has the effect of rendering all Pokémon immediately surrounding the user confused. When transformed into Z-Teeter Dance by a Normalium Z, the user benefits from a one-stage increase in their Special Attack stat. Within the Mystery Dungeon series, this maneuver inflicts confusion upon every Pokémon situated in the user's current room, encompassing the entire floor in the *Rescue Team* titles. As of *Gates to Infinity* and subsequent iterations, the user themselves is immune to the move's confusing effect. Beginning with the *Explorers* series, Magic Coat gained the ability to reflect Teeter Dance. Notably, in *Pokémon Rumble Rush*, Teeter Dance belonged to a category of status moves exclusively wielded by hostile Pokémon, preventing any captured Pokémon from acquiring it. It was, in fact, observed as a technique employed by Boss Spinda in that particular game. The status alterations generated by this move are applied individually to each Pokémon it affects. However, Teeter Dance adopts a Fighting typing when utilized by Rosa (Champion) & Meloetta, a unique characteristic granted by their 'Fighting Performance' passive skill.

Title: Blaze Kick (move)

Known in Japanese as ブレイズキック (Blaze Kick), this damage-dealing Fire-type move made its debut in Generation III. Before the fourth generation, it was exclusively Blaziken's signature technique. This move is listed as TR41 in *Pokémon Sword and Shield* and TM133 in *Pokémon Legends: Z-A*. Blaze Kick delivers damage and possesses an enhanced critical hit ratio. It also holds a 10% likelihood of inflicting a burn on its target.

In Pokémon Contests, Blaze Kick can be utilized as a secondary move in a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if Sunny Day was performed in the preceding turn. It also contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, earning an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was used in the prior turn. Within the *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon* series, Blaze Kick causes damage and boasts a heightened critical-hit ratio, specifically 50% in the *Rescue Team* titles and 30% in the *Explorers* and *Adventure Squad* entries. This attack has the potential to leave the foe burned.

As a Charged Attack in *Pokémon GO*, Blaze Kick became available from May 19, 2019. For *Pokémon UNITE* players, Blaze Kick functions as Cinderace's initial move. It is acquired by evolving Raboot at level 7 and opting to upgrade Ember into Blaze Kick rather than Pyro Ball. The user executes a fiery flying kick that shoves, burns, and lands critical hits on hit enemies. Additionally, the user gains immunity to hindrances while performing this move. At level 11, successfully landing this move temporarily increases the user's basic attack speed.

In the episode "All Things Bright and Beautifly!", Jessie instructed her Seviper to use Blaze Kick, but the Pokémon was unable to execute it as it cannot learn the move. The *Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire* Animated Trailer featured Blaze Kick as the first Generation III move to be seen in the animated series. Harrison's Blaziken demonstrated the move in "Johto Photo Finish," which aired before the release of *Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire*. Interestingly, despite being physically offensive Fire-type Pokémon that can naturally learn other kicking moves, neither Flareon nor the Ponyta evolutionary line are capable of learning Blaze Kick.

Title: Mud Sport (move)

This non-damaging Ground-type maneuver, known in Japanese as どろあそび (Mud Game), made its debut in Generation III. Initially, Mud Sport's effect was to diminish the base power of all Electric-type attacks by half (50%) for every combatant on the field. This effect would persist until the Pokémon that initiated it was withdrawn from battle. Within Pokémon Contests, it functions as an opening move for a combination; if Mud-Slap, Water Gun, or Water Sport are subsequently used on the following turn, their appeal score is doubled. Alternatively, it serves as a finishing move, granting the performer an extra 4 appeal points should Mud-Slap or Water Sport have been performed immediately before it. As of later updates, Mud Sport now weakens the base power of Electric-type attacks by approximately 67% (specifically, a factor of 1352/4096) for all Pokémon in the encounter. Its duration has also been extended to five turns, and crucially, its effects now remain active even if the user departs the field. When augmented by a Groundium Z to become Z-Mud Sport, it bestows a one-stage boost to the user's Special Defense stat. It establishes a floor status of "Mud Sport," which inherently diminishes the potency of Electric-type attacks. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers game series, Mud Sport persists for 10-11 turns and causes Electric-type moves to inflict half their normal damage. Subsequently, starting with Gates to Infinity, the status duration increased to 20 turns, and Electric-type moves are weakened by 30%. A significant number of Ground-type Pokémon acquire Mud Sport through level-up, despite already possessing an immunity to Electric-type attacks. Consequently, for these Pokémon, the move's benefits were non-existent in Single Battles before Generation VI, unless their elemental typing was altered. Nevertheless, its utility extends to Double Battles, where it can aid allies. Furthermore, since Generation VI, it gained viability in Single Battles as well, owing to its persistent effect even after the user exits combat. It's worth noting that in Generation III, the status screen mistakenly displayed this move as having 100% accuracy, despite it targeting the entire field and thereby bypassing all accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Ice Ball (move)

The Ice-type offensive technique, Ice Ball (アイスボール in Japanese), made its debut in Generation III, functioning as a damage-dealing attack. Until Generation VI, it served as the unique move of the Spheal evolutionary line, although other Pokémon could still acquire it through inheritance via breeding. Ice Ball delivers damage over a maximum of five turns. Its power multiplies, doubling with every successful hit in sequence, but this amplification resets after the fifth strike or if the move's execution is interrupted. It is a consecutively executed move that also causes the user to become fixated on its continuous use. Furthermore, its power is doubled if the user has previously performed Defense Curl. Each instance of Ice Ball can strike the target and deal damage a maximum of 5 times; should even one strike fail to land, the move's sequence terminates early.

Creatures possessing the Bulletproof Ability are entirely unaffected by Ice Ball. Striking Mimikyu while it is in its Disguised Form activates the Rollout storage glitch. This move also contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Defense Curl was performed during the previous turn. In Pokémon Sword, Shield, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, Ice Ball's fundamental power was augmented to 40.

Within the Rescue Team series, the damage output of Ice Ball is progressively magnified for each subsequent strike: 1.5×, then 2×, 2.5×, and ultimately 3×. The Explorers series, in contrast, sees damage increase by only 0.1× for every consecutive hit. Its inherent critical hit probability is below average, standing at 2% in the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series. Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, Ice Ball ceases to gain power with successive hits; instead, its power doubles upon its first successful use if Defense Curl was employed by the user on that particular floor. Activation of a Reviver Seed causes the move to terminate.

The episode A Timeless Encounter! portrays a Sealeo performing Ice Ball as an image found in Professor Kukui's "Book of Pokémon Super Moves". Aside from signature moves and Z-Moves, Ice Ball stands as the singular Ice-type attack unavailable for use in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Its visual representation varies across different titles: in mainline entries and certain spin-off games, it appears as a projectile ice sphere. However, in Pokémon Battle Revolution, it takes the form of an ice-encrusted sphere that rolls.

Title: Needle Arm (move)

First appearing in Generation III, the Grass-type attack known as Needle Arm (Japanese: ニードルアーム Needle Arm) deals direct damage to its target. Before Generation V, it served as the unique signature technique for both Cacnea and Cacturne. Beyond its damaging effect, Needle Arm possesses a 30% likelihood of making the opposing Pokémon flinch. Exclusively within Generation III, this move's power doubles against a foe that has previously employed Minimize. From Generations III through V, when Secret Power is executed within tall grass, it adopts the visual effects of Needle Arm. However, in the Orre region, Secret Power consistently displays Needle Arm's animation, irrespective of the surrounding terrain. Notably, Needle Arm stands alone as the sole Grass-type technique that can induce flinching in an opponent. Even though its visual representation involves a fist, Needle Arm does not receive a power increase from the Iron Fist Ability.

Title: Slack Off (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Slack Off (Japanese: なまける Slack Off) is a non-damaging move of the Normal type. Prior to Generation IV, this maneuver was the signature move exclusive to Slakoth and Slaking. The amount of the user's maximum HP restored by Slack Off has varied throughout different game installments. Initially, it would recover up to 50% of the user's maximum HP, rounded down. Currently, it rejuvenates precisely 50% of the user's maximum HP, with the healing value rounded upwards.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Slack Off can be utilized as the second move. If Yawn was successfully executed on the preceding turn, the user gains 1 bonus appeal point. When empowered by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Slack Off, all of the user's diminished stats are instantly reset.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Slack Off functions as Slowbro's second move. The user initiates a nap, regenerating HP over a period of time. However, sustaining damage during this channel will decrease the total HP restored. Upon reaching level 6, this move is subsequently replaced by either Amnesia or Telekinesis.

In the episode "The Good, The Bad, and The Lucky!", Jessie and James ordered the Slakoth and Slaking they obtained from the Rocket Prize Master to use Slack Off, but the two Pokémon were too lethargic to comply. Notably, in Generation III, the move's status screen erroneously stated a 100% accuracy, despite being a self-targeting move that bypasses all accuracy and evasion checks.

Title: Hyper Voice (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Hyper Voice (Japanese: ハイパーボイス Hyper Voice) is a Normal-type move that inflicts damage. It strikes all foes located adjacent to the user. When employed against a Pokémon protected by a substitute, the attack connects with that substitute. Furthermore, Hyper Voice is currently able to affect Pokémon even when they are positioned behind a substitute. This move causes damage and will destroy certain natural objects found in the background of battles. Within the Rescue Team and Explorers series, the Muzzled status condition impacts this move. As a sound-based attack, it is nullified by the Soundproof Ability. For Jigglypuff, Hyper Voice serves as a special Customization move, offering an alternative to Sing. Its visual effect resembles Sing, though it emits shock waves capable of hitting the opponent three times.

Turning to Pokémon UNITE, Hyper Voice functions as Sylveon's initial move. Sylveon acquires this ability upon evolving from Eevee at level 4, specifically by upgrading Swift rather than Mystical Fire. The user releases several sound waves which inflict damage upon impact. Its damage output scales with the distance between the target and the user, increasing as the target moves further away. Each individual sound wave, after damaging an enemy, decreases that enemy's Sp. Defense by 20%, a reduction that can stack up to four times, totaling an 80% decrease. Upon reaching level 10, the user generates an additional sound wave.

Title: Poison Fang (move)

Originating in Generation III, Poison Fang (Japanese: どくどくのキバ, translated as "Excessive Poison Fang") is classified as a Poison-type maneuver that deals damage. It appears as Technical Machine 123 (TM123) within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. This attack not only harms its target but also carries a 30% probability of inflicting the badly poisoned status. Beyond its primary function of causing damage, the move holds the potential to leave the foe badly poisoned. Prior to Version 2.10.0, updated on June 28, 2021, its in-game description read: "Has a good chance of leaving the target badly poisoned." An earlier description, valid until Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022), stated: "This move's power increases when the target is poisoned or badly poisoned." This particular description was found in Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom. Until Generation IX, Poison Fang stood as the unique move capable of inflicting bad poison as a secondary effect.

Title: Crush Claw (move)

The combat technique named Crush Claw (Japanese: ブレイククロー Break Claw), a damage-dealing Normal-type maneuver, made its debut in Generation III. Prior to Generation IV, it functioned as Zangoose's signature move, although other Pokémon were still able to obtain it through breeding. When executed, Crush Claw inflicts damage and carries a fifty percent chance of reducing the target's Defense stat by a single stage. As part of a Pokémon Contest combination, Crush Claw can serve as the second action, awarding the user 1 additional appeal point if Swords Dance was performed in the preceding turn. Fundamentally, Crush Claw delivers an attack and may lower the opponent's Defense by one stage. The descriptive text for the move, active before Version 2.10.0 (which was issued on June 28, 2021), noted: "Has a good chance of lowering the target's Defense."

Title: Blast Burn (move)

Known as ブラストバーン (Blast Burn) in Japanese, this Fire-type attack first appeared in Generation III as a damage-inflicting technique. It is cataloged as TM153 in *Pokémon Scarlet* and *Violet*, and as TM158 in *Pokémon Legends: Z-A*. Alongside Hydro Cannon and Frenzy Plant, Blast Burn stands as one of the ultimate moves within its elemental category. This attack is accessible to all fully evolved Fire-type first partner Pokémon. After delivering damage, Blast Burn mandates a recharge period for the user on the subsequent turn. This recharge is unavoidable unless the attack fails to connect. In Pokémon Contests, Blast Burn functions as the second move in a combination, granting the performer 4 additional appeal points if Sunny Day was utilized on the preceding turn.

Numerous Move Tutors are capable of instructing fully evolved Fire-type first partner Pokémon in this technique. Between Generation III and VII, a Pokémon also needed to possess a maximum friendship rating. These tutors include: in *HeartGold* and *SoulSilver*, an old man residing in Blackthorn City's Move Deleter/Reminder house; in *Black 2* and *White 2*, an individual positioned outside the Pokémon World Tournament facility; in *X* and *Y*, a man found in Snowbelle City's southwestern residence; in *Omega Ruby* and *Alpha Sapphire*, a person on Mauville City's first floor, in the southeast; and in *Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon*, a man on the second story of Hau'oli City's mall.

Beyond its direct damage, Blast Burn also thaws any frozen opponents it hits. Upon successfully striking at least one target, the user is afflicted with the recoil status condition (known as Paused before *Gates to Infinity*) for a single turn, rendering them unable to act. Prior to *Gates to Infinity*, this move was influenced by the Muzzled status condition.

Given its exclusive tutor-move status in the main series, Blast Burn's acquisition method diverges in the *Mystery Dungeon* games: *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team* and *Blue Rescue Team* see Charizard attempt to master Blast Burn at level 99, provided it possesses a minimum of 333 IQ points. Should this condition not be met before level 99, the move can be acquired later at the Gulpin Link Shop. *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky* allow an eligible Pokémon to learn Blast Burn at level 72 with at least 300 IQ points. If these prerequisites are not fulfilled by level 72, the Electivire Link Shop offers a way to relearn it. For *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity*, Emboar can be taught Blast Burn through a specialized TM, which becomes available once Pokémon Paradise achieves Perfect Rank. In *Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon*, Hawlucha's Slam School provides training for this move for a fee of 11,000. And in *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX*, Blast Burn can be taught at the Gulpin Link Shop for 11,000 upon finishing the main narrative.

Within *Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U*, Blast Burn serves as a personalized version of Charizard's Flare Blitz. Upon activation, Charizard undergoes a brief charge before surging towards the adversary. A successful impact triggers an explosion that propels the opponent, while Charizard itself incurs recoil damage. This custom move boasts significantly greater power than Flare Blitz, but it also inflicts increased recoil damage and covers a short distance at a slower pace.

In *Pokémon GO*, Blast Burn manifests as a Charged Attack, accessible since May 19, 2018. Sygna Suit Red & Charizard's passive skill, Piercing Gaze, guarantees that their deployment of this move will never miss. Similarly, Blue (Classic) & Charizard's passive skill, Kanto Journey Legacy, ensures the move always connects. This sync move's capability to reach 6★ EX was not enabled until August 28, 2020, occurring 182 days subsequent to its initial release on February 28, 2020. Its description preceding Version 2.10.0 (as of June 28, 2021) stated: "Become Mega Charizard X until the end of battle. Raises the user’s critical-hit rate." The 6★-EX effect description before Version 2.18.5 (effective March 15, 2022) read: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents."

Title: Hydro Cannon (move)

Hydro Cannon, known in Japanese as ハイドロカノン (Hydro Cannon), is an offensive Water-type technique first introduced in Generation III. It holds the designation TM154 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM159 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Alongside Blast Burn and Frenzy Plant, Hydro Cannon ranks among the ultimate attacks of its type. All Water-type first partner Pokémon, once fully evolved, are capable of learning this potent move. When deployed, Hydro Cannon inflicts damage upon its target, subsequently obliging the user to recharge during the following turn. This recharge period is an unavoidable consequence of a successful strike unless the attack misses.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Hydro Cannon can be utilized as a second move, yielding a bonus of 4 appeal points if Rain Dance was performed in the preceding turn.

Various Move Tutors are available to teach Hydro Cannon to a fully evolved Water-type first partner Pokémon. From Generation III up to Generation VII, the Pokémon also needed a maximized friendship rating to learn it this way. These specialized tutors include: an elderly man found in Blackthorn City's Move Deleter/Move Reminder's house in HeartGold and SoulSilver; a man positioned outside the Pokémon World Tournament building in Black 2 and White 2; a man within the southwestern house in Snowbelle City in X and Y; a man located in the southeast sector of Mauville City's first floor in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire; and a man on the second level of Hau'oli City's mall in Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon.

Due to Hydro Cannon being exclusively a tutor-taught ability in the main series games, its acquisition methods differ in the Mystery Dungeon spin-off titles. In these games, a successful strike inflicts the user with the 'recoil' status condition for one turn, thereby preventing them from acting. (Before Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, this specific effect was termed the 'Paused' status condition). Specifically: in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Blastoise will attempt to learn Hydro Cannon at level 99 if it possesses at least 333 IQ points; if this prerequisite isn't met by level 99, it can be relearned at the Gulpin Link Shop. For Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, an eligible Pokémon will try to learn it at level 72, provided they have a minimum of 300 IQ points; failure to meet this by level 72 allows relearning at the Electivire Link Shop. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, Samurott can be taught Hydro Cannon through a special TM, which is acquired upon Pokémon Paradise achieving Perfect Rank. Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon allows the move to be taught at Hawlucha's Slam School for a fee of 11,000. Finally, in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, instruction for Hydro Cannon is available at the Gulpin Link Shop subsequent to the completion of the main story.

Hydro Cannon is noteworthy for sharing the highest base power among all Water-type attacks with Water Spout, disregarding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. A Hydro Vortex move derived from either of these two techniques holds the highest base power of all Water-type moves overall.

Excluding the movie 'The Power of Us', Hydro Cannon has, in the animated series, been exclusively used by Pokémon belonging to rivals.

Title: Meteor Mash (move)

Introduced during Generation III, Meteor Mash (Japanese: コメットパンチ Comet Punch) is a Steel-type attack that deals damage. This move has a 20% chance of escalating the user's Attack stat by one stage, in addition to its damaging effect. Meteor Mash underwent modifications where its power was reduced from 100 to 90, and its accuracy saw an increase from 85% to 90%. Its primary function continues to be inflicting damage, carrying the potential to boost the user's Attack by a single stage.

In Pokémon GO, Meteor Mash operates as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since October 21, 2018. The description for this move, prior to Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), stated: "Has a small chance of raising the user's Attack."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Meteor Mash is Lucario's second designated starting move. Here, Lucario launches a punch with meteoric force, harming and knocking back adversaries. Once level 7 is attained, it is subsequently substituted by either Bone Rush or Close Combat.

Meteor Mash is also among the upgrade selections available to Metagross. This version sees the user channeling energy into its fist, dealing damage to opposing Pokémon within an area of effect while simultaneously activating a shield for itself. Upon reaching level 5, it becomes one of the options to replace Iron Defense, with Gyro Ball being the alternative choice.

The Ability Iron Fist, which grants a 20% power boost to punching moves, does affect Meteor Mash. The characteristic of this move as a punching maneuver is more explicitly highlighted by its Japanese appellation, コメットパンチ Comet Punch, a name that was already in use by a Generation I move in English.

Title: Astonish (move)

For information on the skill known as Astonish in Pokémon Shuffle, refer to Skill (Shuffle) → Astonish. Astonish (Japanese: おどろかす Astonish) is a damage-inflicting Ghost-type technique that made its debut in Generation III. This attack deals damage and carries a 30% chance of causing the opposing Pokémon to flinch. Exclusively within Generation III, Astonish's damage output doubled if its target had previously activated Minimize. Its Power Points (PP) have since been increased to 25, and it also lowers the target's action speed. As a damaging maneuver, Astonish retains the potential to induce flinching in the target. In Pokémon GO, Astonish functions as a Fast Attack, having been available since February 16, 2017. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch." Within Pokémon UNITE, Astonish serves as Hoopa's initial move and Decidueye's secondary move. Hoopa commences with this ability; the user inflicts damage upon rival Pokémon within a designated area of effect, temporarily rendering them unable to act. At level 6, this move is supplanted by either Phantom Force or Shadow Ball. Decidueye also starts with this move, dealing damage to adversaries and reducing their movement speed by 80% for a brief duration. By level 8, this move is replaced by either Leaf Storm or Shadow Sneak. In Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, Fantina's Drifblim was confirmed to know Astonish on the official website, despite never being depicted using the move. Astonish holds the unique distinction among Ghost-type moves of being able to cause a target to flinch.

Title: Weather Ball (move)

The move known as Weather Ball (Japanese: ウェザーボール Weather Ball) debuted in Generation III as a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage. Before Generation IV, it was exclusively associated with Castform as its signature technique. It featured as Technical Machine TM46 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the designation of TM193 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Weather Ball's elemental typing shifts according to the prevailing weather conditions when it is executed. When the atmosphere is clear, it retains its Normal typing. During any active weather type, excluding strong winds, Weather Ball's power doubles to 100. (This power augmentation is in addition to any standard type-based power bonuses from weather effects, which still apply.) Its type defaults to Normal if no weather is present, during fog, or amidst strong winds.

In Pokémon Contests, Weather Ball can serve as the second move in a combination, granting its user 4 bonus appeal points if Hail, Rain Dance, Sandstorm, or Sunny Day was performed on the preceding turn. It also forms part of a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding three extra appeal points if any weather-summoning move was utilized in the prior turn.

Due to the mechanic in Generation III where a move's damage category was determined by its type, Weather Ball's category also varied accordingly. Unlike Hidden Power, which invariably triggers Counter but never Mirror Coat, Weather Ball can activate Mirror Coat if it functions as a special move.

Specifically within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, if Weather Ball is employed during a shadowy aura, its power escalates to 100, and it transforms into a typeless physical attack. As a typeless move, it circumvents Wonder Guard, inflicts consistent damage regardless of the target's types, and prevents the user from receiving Same-Type Attack Bonus (STAB). Should a Pokémon with the Color Change ability be struck by a typeless Weather Ball, its type will subsequently become Normal.

Weather Ball was affected by Normalize. However, it is now unaffected by Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, Aerilate, and Galvanize. Presently, it consistently functions as a special move, making it susceptible to Mirror Coat but impervious to Counter. Nevertheless, it is responsive to Ion Deluge (provided its type would be Normal) and Electrify. Targets protected by the Bulletproof ability are immune to Weather Ball.

When used as a Z-Move via Normalium Z, Weather Ball initially converts into Breakneck Blitz; however, Breakneck Blitz then transforms into the specific Z-Move that corresponds to Weather Ball's elemental type under the prevailing weather. This specific transformation sequence does not transpire if Weather Ball becomes a Z-Move through alternative means, such as Sleep Talk. An analogous phenomenon unfolds when Weather Ball is employed as a Max Move: it manifests as Max Strike but then morphs into the suitable Max Move or G-Max Move aligned with Weather Ball's type in the current weather. Weather Ball delivers damage, with its type and power dynamically adjusting based on the atmospheric state.

In the Rescue Team series, Weather Ball assumes a Normal typing in Clear and Cloudy conditions; it becomes Fire-type under Sun; Water-type during Rain and Fog; Ice-type in Hail and Snow; and Rock-type within a Sandstorm. Its power sees a 100% increase in all weather types apart from Clear and Cloudy. Subsequently, in the Explorers series, Weather Ball ceased to alter its type or power when Fog was present. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, the weather conditions Cloudy, Fog, and Snow were removed from the game. The power enhancement observed in weather conditions in these later games is 25%.

In Pokémon GO, Fire-type, Ice-type, and Water-type variations of Weather Ball function as Charged Attacks, accessible since March 30, 2019. The Rock-type Weather Ball, also a Charged Attack, supplanted the Normal-type Weather Ball on May 31, 2021. However, on August 30, 2021, the Normal-type Weather Ball was reinstated into the game, featuring revised statistics. Diverging from the core series, Weather Ball's type in Pokémon GO is static and independent of atmospheric conditions; instead, the game's data registers its various type forms as distinct move entries. Even though Weather Ball adopts a Rock typing during a sandstorm, Castform itself does not undergo a form change in such circumstances.

Before Generation IV, Weather Ball stood as one of only two moves (the other being Hidden Power) that possessed the unique characteristic of being either physical or special, owing to the damage category being dictated by the move's elemental type.

Title: Aromatherapy (move)

First appearing in Generation III, Aromatherapy (Japanese: アロマセラピー Aromatherapy) is a Grass-type technique that inflicts no damage. Its primary function is to remove every detrimental status ailment, such as sleep, poison, paralysis, freeze, and burn, from the user, all other Pokémon within their party, and any present allies. This restorative action can notably affect the user even if they are behind a Substitute. However, while the user can be cured in this state, allies protected by a Substitute will not benefit from Aromatherapy. Furthermore, employing this move on an ally will activate the Sap Sipper ability. When amplified by a Grassium Z crystal, the move transforms into Z-Aromatherapy, which completely replenishes the user's HP. In specific contexts, such as a shared room, it remedies all negative status conditions (like sleep or sealed) for both the user and their allies present. Prior to the 1.2.0 update, despite its general unavailability in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Ice Rider Calyrex's level-up moveset inadvertently retained Aromatherapy. The subsequent 1.2.0 patch rectified this by substituting it with Psychic Terrain, thereby aligning its learnset with Calyrex's other forms at that particular level.

Title: Fake Tears (move)

Known in Japan as うそなき (Fake Crying), Fake Tears is a non-damaging Dark-type maneuver that debuted in Generation III. It held the TM47 designation in Pokémon Sword and Shield and is presently TM003 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This technique's principal effect is to reduce the target's Special Defense stat by two stages. Should it be enhanced by a Darkinium Z, becoming Z-Fake Tears, the user concurrently receives a one-stage boost to their Special Attack. Critically, Fake Tears results in a two-stage decrement to the adversary's Sp. Defense. The move itself can be influenced by Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Fake Tears, along with all other status moves, was solely available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired on any caught Pokémon. It was also a move notably employed by Boss Bonsly. When utilized by Marnie & Liepard, their passive skill, Piercing Gaze, ensures this move achieves perfect accuracy. Before Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), its in-game description read: "Sharply lowers the target's Sp. Def." Intriguingly, no Dark-type Pokémon possessed the ability to learn this move until Generation V.

Title: Air Cutter (move)

Air Cutter (Japanese: エアカッター Air Cutter) is a damaging Flying-type technique that first appeared in Generation III. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is designated as TM040. This move inflicts damage and inherently carries an increased critical hit ratio. It strikes both opposing Pokémon in a Double Battle, and its effect extends to all adjacent adversaries in Triple Battles. The probability of Air Cutter landing a critical hit is individually calculated for each target it connects with. The move's base power was adjusted from 55 to 60. When utilized in Horde Encounters, it is capable of targeting every opponent. Beyond combat, Air Cutter can also dislodge Berries from Berry trees present in the background of battle scenes. If a Pokémon with the Sharpness ability employs this move, its power is boosted by 50%. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Air Cutter's PP was reduced to 15, and consistent with all other moves in that game, it can only affect a single target. It delivers damage with an elevated critical hit rate, specifically 50% in the Rescue Team series and 30% in the Explorers series. An additional effect described prior to Version 2.20.0 indicated that the move's power would increase if the target was flinching. In the episode The Shape of Love to Come!, Rapp commanded her Zubat to use Air Cutter, but Mina's Ribombee defeated it before the move could be executed. Air Cutter holds the distinction of being the sole damage-dealing Flying-type move that cannot strike non-adjacent foes in Triple Battles. It is one of three moves, along with Twister and Blizzard, that can be used to gather Berries from Berry trees appearing behind opponents in wild battles.

Title: Overheat (move)

Known in Japanese as オーバーヒート (Overheat), this Fire-type damage-dealing technique debuted in Generation III. It served as TM50 across various generations, specifically from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, appearing again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and is also slated for inclusion in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon successful execution, Overheat inflicts harm while simultaneously decreasing the attacker's Special Attack by two stages. The attack is specified as making contact with the target in this generation. During Pokémon Contests, Overheat functions effectively as the second step in a combination, granting the user an additional 6 appeal points should Sunny Day have been executed on the preceding turn. Its base power has been adjusted down to 110, and it imposes a reduction on the user's offensive capabilities for a duration of three turns. When unleashed, it deals damage to all opposing Pokémon positioned adjacently to the attacker, subsequently diminishing the user's Special Attack by two stages. A descriptive error exists in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, where Overheat is mistakenly said to reduce the user's Special Defense rather than its Special Attack. However, this particular mistake is absent from the TM's accompanying description for Overheat. Regarding Pokémon GO, Overheat functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible within the game since February 16, 2017. Prior to the update to Version 2.10.0 on June 28, 2021, its in-game description read: "Sharply lowers the user's Sp. Atk." The move Overheat is also featured among Chandelure's available Pokémon moves. Chandelure's execution involves a red glow and charge-up phase, during which it acquires defensive armor against various assaults before unleashing a scorching tornado. Subsequent to the assault, Chandelure topples, experiencing a decrease in both its Attack and Speed stats, a mechanic that mirrors the Special Attack reduction inherent to the move's original design. Repeated use while Chandelure's stats are debuffed results in substantially diminished damage output; conversely, when employed from a positive state, it inflicts considerably greater damage, albeit at the cost of negating Chandelure's active buffs. Notably, Overheat shares identical descriptive texts with the move Psycho Boost.

Title: Odor Sleuth (move)

Hailing from Generation III, Odor Sleuth (known in Japanese as かぎわける, or Find Scent) is a Normal-type status move that deals no damage. This move ensures that subsequent accuracy checks made against the affected Pokémon disregard any modifications to its evasion stat stages. Furthermore, should a Ghost-type Pokémon be the recipient of Odor Sleuth, their immunities to both Fighting-type and Normal-type attacks are nullified. The effects of Odor Sleuth persist until the affected Pokémon withdraws from battle. A more recent change dictates that Odor Sleuth's ability to make accuracy checks bypass evasion stat stage changes only applies if the target's evasion stat stage is currently above 0. Moreover, Odor Sleuth itself is now guaranteed to land, circumventing accuracy checks, unless the opponent is presently in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. It will also fail if an attempt is made to use it on a Pokémon already experiencing its influence. When transformed into Z-Odor Sleuth via a Normalium Z, the user's Attack stat receives a one-stage boost. In certain contexts, Odor Sleuth affects all opposing Pokémon present, restoring any positive evasion stat modifications they might have to zero. Should any of these targets be Ghost-types, they are additionally afflicted with the Exposed status, thereby stripping them of their immunity to Normal-type and Fighting-type moves.

Title: Rock Tomb (move)

Known in Japanese as がんせきふうじ, or Rock Seal, Rock Tomb is a Rock-type damaging move that first debuted in Generation III. This attack was available as TM39 from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is also found as TM39 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Its primary effect involves inflicting damage while simultaneously decreasing the target's Speed by one stage. Initially, it possessed a base power of 50, a PP count of 10, and an accuracy rating of 80%.

Later revisions to Rock Tomb adjusted its power to 60, increased its PP to 15, and improved its accuracy to 95%.

Beyond standard battles, Rock Tomb holds a role in Pokémon Contest combinations. When utilized as the second move in a sequence, it grants the user 3 bonus appeal points, provided Rock Throw was performed in the preceding turn.

A unique implementation is seen in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, where Rock Tomb causes a rock to be dropped at a fixed distance ahead of the user. Should this rock strike an enemy, it deals damage and diminishes their Travel Speed (previously termed Movement Speed) by one stage. If the rock misses its intended target, it persists on the field as a static obstacle, capable of impeding both movement paths and incoming projectile attacks. This environmental rock itself can be targeted and will be obliterated upon sustaining damage from any attacking move.

In the Explorers series of games, a mistranslation led to the move's description erroneously suggesting only a chance to lower Movement Speed. The official description before Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021) simply stated: "Lowers the target's Speed."

In Pokémon UNITE, Rock Tomb serves as Crustle's initial ability. It becomes accessible after evolving from Dwebble at level 4, specifically by upgrading Rock Slide rather than Shell Smash. The user first emits a beam, delivering 100% ability damage. Upon reaching its maximum range or striking a foe, a curved wall emerges from the ground, inflicting 50% ability damage to any enemies positioned atop it. A further 50% ability damage is dealt to all nearby enemies when this barrier despawns. Although Rock Tomb is capable of landing critical hits, Crustle's base critical hit chance for this move stands at 0%. Furthermore, once Crustle reaches level 11, every hit from Rock Tomb also applies a 2-second slow effect.

The move has also appeared in other media; in All About Arceus VIII, Gold commanded his Sudowoodo, named Sudobo, to use Rock Tomb, though its execution occurred off-panel.

Noteworthy errors exist in older game descriptions. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, alongside Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD, the TM description inaccurately implies that Rock Tomb does not consistently lower the target's Speed. The same TM description in these titles also incorrectly states that the move can cause the target to flinch. Finally, when deployed in a Double Battle, Rock Tomb visually targets the Pokémon opposite the player's selection, yet the damage is actually applied to the Pokémon initially chosen by the player.

Title: Silver Wind (move)

The Bug-type, damage-dealing attack known as Silver Wind (ぎんいろのかぜ in Japanese) made its debut in Generation III. This move is found as Technical Machine (TM) 62 within Generation IV titles, and it will be TM117 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used, Silver Wind deals damage and provides a 10% probability of simultaneously increasing the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats, each by a single stage. The Power Points (PP) for Silver Wind have been adjusted upwards to 15.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, including the Red and Blue Rescue Team and Explorers games, Silver Wind affects all adversaries present in the same room. It can increase the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Travel Speed by one stage, with these stat enhancements potentially lasting for 3 turns. Crucially, the move's secondary effect triggers for *each* foe struck, with individual boosts being applied instantly upon activation.

For Pokémon GO players, Silver Wind functions as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since February 16, 2017. In Pokémon GO, its secondary effect was altered from boosting 2 Attack and Defense to 1 Attack and Defense. The in-game description for this effect, prior to Version 2.10.0 (which went live on June 28, 2021), stated: "Has a very small chance of raising the user's Attack, Defense, Speed, Sp. Atk, and Sp. Def, and Speed."

Among the variations of Ancient Power, Silver Wind stands out as the sole instance that cannot be acquired through a Move Tutor, instead relying solely on Technical Machine instruction. During the Brazilian Portuguese localization of the eleventh season of Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, the move Silver Wind was frequently misidentified as "Asa de Prata." This translates to "Silver Wing" and is presumed to be an error arising from the lexical resemblance between "Wing" and "Wind."

Title: Metal Sound (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Metal Sound (Japanese: きんぞくおん) is a non-damaging Steel-type technique. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it holds the designation of TM223. This move's core effect is to decrease the target's Special Defense stat by two stages.

As an opening action in Pokémon Contest combinations, Metal Sound can set up Metal Claw to achieve double its standard appeal if performed on the subsequent turn. When augmented by a Steelium Z, transforming into Z-Metal Sound, the user's Special Attack stat is boosted by one stage. Notably, Throat Chop does not prevent a Pokémon from executing Z-Metal Sound.

Regarding barriers, Metal Sound previously could not affect Pokémon behind a Substitute; however, it is now capable of hitting Pokémon even when they are protected by one. The extent of its Special Defense reduction has varied: before *Gates to Infinity*, it lowered the target's Sp. Def by three stages, but from *Gates to Infinity* onward, this reduction was changed to two stages. Magic Coat can deflect this move.

In the spin-off title Pokémon Rumble Rush, status moves like Metal Sound were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any caught Pokémon. Boss Magnemite was observed utilizing Metal Sound in this game. Metal Sound stands as the sole Steel-type move that is classified as sound-based.

Furthermore, the resources needed to craft Metal Sound's TM, TM223, were altered in update 3.0.1 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This modification occurred because the original crafting recipe required materials from Shieldon, a Pokémon exclusive to the *Violet* version.

Title: Grass Whistle (move)

This Grass-type technique, known in Japanese as くさぶえ (Grass Flute), made its debut in Generation III. Before Pokémon X and Y, its name appeared as GrassWhistle, and it inflicts no direct damage. Up until Generation IV, Roselia alone possessed this move as its signature attack, though other species could still acquire it through the breeding mechanic. Several Abilities grant immunity to the sleep status condition, namely Insomnia, Vital Spirit, Comatose, and Shields Down when the shield is active. Grass Whistle specifically fails against Pokémon possessing the Abilities Soundproof (because it's a sound-based move) or Sap Sipper (due to its Grass typing). When transformed into Z-Grass Whistle via a Grassium Z, this move grants its user a one-stage boost to their Speed stat. The effects of Throat Chop do not hinder a Pokémon's ability to utilize Z-Grass Whistle. In Contest Spectaculars, Grass Whistle can initiate a combination, leading to an additional three appeal points for specific moves (Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap) if performed on the subsequent turn. Historically, Grass Whistle was unable to affect targets shielded by a substitute; however, it is now capable of hitting Pokémon even when they are behind one. The primary effect of Grass Whistle is to induce sleep in its target. Both Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition influence this move's outcome. Its classification as a sound-based technique also means Soundproof Pokémon are unaffected. Notably, Grass Whistle stood alone as the sole sleep-inducing move rendered unusable in Generation VIII. Curiously, even though multiple Pokédex entries for Nuzleaf describe it utilizing the leaf on its head as a flute, this Pokémon is unable to learn Grass Whistle. It holds the unique distinction of being the sole Grass-type move categorized as sound-based. Furthermore, it is the only Grass-type sleep move that Grass-type Pokémon themselves do not possess immunity against.

Title: Tickle (move)

Hailing from Generation III, Tickle (Japanese: くすぐる Tickle) is a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. Its effect is to diminish the opposing Pokémon's Attack and Defense parameters, each by a single stage. Uniquely in Generation III, Substitute offers no protection against Tickle's influence. When enhanced into Z-Tickle via a Normalium Z, the move grants a one-stage boost to the user's own Defense. Fundamentally, this technique's consequence is a decrement of one stage to both the opponent's Attack and Defense attributes. Its effects can be reflected by Magic Coat. Regarding Pokémon Rumble Rush, Tickle was among the array of status maneuvers solely available to adversaries, remaining unobtainable for any Pokémon captured by players. Boss Mime Jr., Minccino, and Gothita are recognized as Pokémon capable of employing Tickle.

Title: Cosmic Power (move)

Originating in Generation III, Cosmic Power (Japanese: コスモパワー Cosmo Power) is a Psychic-type maneuver that does not deal damage. Its Technical Record designation in Pokémon Sword and Shield was TR44. Upon deployment, this technique elevates the user's Defense and Special Defense stats by one stage apiece. Should it be amplified by a Psychium Z into Z-Cosmic Power, an extra stage is added to the user's Special Defense. Furthermore, the user's Defense and Sp. Defense are each boosted by a single stage. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, every status-modifying move, Cosmic Power among them, could only be employed by enemy Pokémon and could not be acquired on any captured Pokémon. Opposing Cosmoem were noted for their use of this particular move.

Title: Water Spout (move)

Making its debut in Generation III, Water Spout (Japanese: しおふき Water Spout) is a damaging Water-type move. Its primary function is to inflict harm. While possessing a maximum base power of 150 when the user's HP is full, this potency diminishes in proportion to the user's current hit points. More precisely, its base power is determined by the formula ⌊150×HPcurrentHPmax⌋. Should this calculation result in a value less than 1, the move's base power defaults to 1.

During encounters involving multiple adversaries, Water Spout strikes all foes situated adjacently. Additionally, it possesses the capability to demolish stalagmites present in battle backgrounds. As a Pokémon Contest move, it functions as the second step in a combination, awarding the user 1 extra appeal point if Rain Dance was executed on the preceding turn.

Though its damage output generally diminishes as the user's health wanes, the *Rescue Team* and *Explorers* titles implement specific damage multipliers: the ultimate damage is halved (½) when HP is between 75-51%, reduced to 51/256 (approximately 0.2×) for 50-26% HP, and further to 25/256 (roughly 0.1×) when HP falls to 25% or less. *Super Mystery Dungeon*, however, applies a proportional reduction in power based on the user's current HP. Should a gigantic Pokémon deploy Water Spout in *Super Mystery Dungeon*, it will target all adversaries in the current chamber.

Within *Pokémon UNITE*, Water Spout serves as Blastoise's initial move. It is acquired upon evolving to the Wartortle stage at level 5, by opting to upgrade Water Gun over Hydro Pump. The user summons a torrent of water within a specified area, hitting foes caught in the radius 6 times over its active period, both inflicting damage and decreasing their speed. Its damage output sees an increase once the user reaches level 11.

Sharing the distinction with Hydro Cannon, Water Spout possesses the highest base power among all Water-type offensive moves, with the exception of Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. Furthermore, a Hydro Vortex derived from either Water Spout or Hydro Cannon claims the absolute highest base power across all Water-type moves.

In Generation III, the animation depicting the opponent's shaking when hit by Water Spout, specifically its intensity and length, is influenced by the move's calculated base power. Notably, this phenomenon isn't limited by maximum power; instances where the user's HP surpasses its maximum, like via the Pomeg glitch, will cause opponents to shake with greater intensity and for an extended period compared to normal.

Title: Signal Beam (move)
First appearing in Generation III, Signal Beam (Japanese: シグナルビーム Signal Beam) is a Bug-type attack that deals damage. In addition to its damaging effect, it possesses a 10% chance of confusing the target. It holds the unique distinction among Platinum Move Tutor moves of requiring every type of shard for acquisition. Conversely, other available moves from these tutors need only one, two, or three shard types. Notably, it is the sole Bug-type move capable of inducing confusion.

Title: Shadow Punch (move)

A damage-dealing Ghost-type move, Shadow Punch (Japanese: シャドーパンチ Shadow Punch) debuted in Generation III. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is designated as TM118. This attack inflicts damage and characteristically bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring it always hits. The only exception is if the target is in a semi-invulnerable turn from moves like Dig or Fly. For Pokémon GO, Shadow Punch was present from the game's release on July 6, 2016, though it wasn't assigned to any Pokémon until February 16, 2017, when Haunter received it.

Title: Extrasensory (move)

Extrasensory, known in Japanese as じんつうりき (Supernatural Power), is a damage-dealing Psychic-type move that debuted in Generation III. Up until Generation IV, this attack served as Nuzleaf's signature technique, though other Pokémon were capable of inheriting it through breeding. Extrasensory delivers damage and possesses a 10% probability of causing the target to flinch. Solely within Generation III, Extrasensory would inflict double damage if the opposing Pokémon had previously used Minimize. The move's power has since been lowered to 70 and its PP reduced to 15. Additionally, it causes a decrease in the opponent's action speed. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, when employed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Extrasensory impacts all adversaries within the same chamber and has a base accuracy of 90%. Within Pokémon GO, Extrasensory functions as a Fast Attack, having been made available on February 16, 2017. In the manga *Shock!! Chairman Rose's Plan*, Shielbert directed his Bronzong to unleash Extrasensory, but its execution occurred off-panel. Before its PP was reduced in Generation VI, Extrasensory boasted the most favorable PP to power ratio among all moves.

Title: Sky Uppercut (move)

A Fighting-type offensive move introduced in Generation III, Sky Uppercut (スカイアッパー, or Sky Uppercut in Japanese) inflicts damage upon its target. This damaging technique holds a unique advantage, as it can successfully land hits on Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable periods of Fly, Bounce, and Sky Drop. Furthermore, in Pokémon Contest combinations, Sky Uppercut functions as a viable second move, earning the user an extra 2 appeal points if Focus Energy was utilized in the turn prior. The attack causes injury, even extending its reach to opponents suspended in the air by moves such as Fly or Bounce.

Title: Sand Tomb (move)

Known in Japanese as すなじごく (Sand Hell), Sand Tomb is a damage-dealing Ground-type attack that debuted in Generation III. It was assigned TM49 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM176 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Upon use, Sand Tomb not only deals initial damage but also inflicts residual harm, causing the target to lose 1/16 of its maximum HP for an additional two to five turns. If successful, the battle text "<Pokémon> was trapped by SAND TOMB!" appears. The move also ensnares the target, thereby preventing them from switching out or escaping. Should the user switch out, the trapped Pokémon is immediately freed. A Pokémon caught by Sand Tomb can also break free by using Rapid Spin. When a wild Pokémon employs Sand Tomb against the player's Pokémon, an escape is possible if the affected Pokémon possesses the Run Away Ability or is holding a Smoke Ball; however, these exceptions do not permit the player to switch out the Pokémon. Sand Tomb possesses a base Power of 15 and an Accuracy of 70.

In Pokémon Contests, Sand Tomb functions as a valid second move in a combination, granting the user 3 bonus appeal points if Sandstorm was performed in the preceding turn. During Generation IV, if the user was holding a Grip Claw, Sand Tomb's trapping duration was consistently 5 turns. Furthermore, a Pokémon affected by Sand Tomb could switch out if it was holding a Shed Shell.

Later updates saw Sand Tomb's Power increased to 35 and its Accuracy to 85, with its trapping effect then lasting four to five turns. If the user holds a Binding Band, the end-of-turn damage inflicted would increase from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. Subsequent changes also altered Grip Claw's effect, making the trap last for 7 turns when held by the user. The battle text was also modified to "<Pokémon> became trapped by Sand Tomb!". A general enhancement to Sand Tomb then raised its passive, end-of-turn damage from 1/16 to 1/8 of the target's maximum HP. With a Binding Band held by the user, this end-turn damage is further amplified to 1/6 of the target's total HP. Ghost-type Pokémon are immune to Sand Tomb's trapping effect. For Contest Spectaculars, Sand Tomb also serves as part of a combination, granting an additional three appeal points if Sandstorm was previously executed.

Across the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Sand Tomb not only inflicts damage but also has the potential to impose the Constriction status on foes. This condition prevents the Pokémon from moving (though attacking remains possible), restricts it from swapping positions with the team leader, and causes it to lose 5 HP every two turns, persisting for 4 to 6 turns. Starting with Gates to Infinity, the move instead applies a distinct "Sand Tomb" status condition, compelling the enemy to shed 3 HP at the conclusion of every 2 turns, with this effect lasting for a total of 6 turns. In Pokémon GO, Sand Tomb operates as a Charged Attack, having been accessible to players since February 16, 2017. The Japanese designation, すなじごく (Suna Jigoku, or Sand Hell), is widely believed to be a homage to the natural sand pit traps crafted by antlions.

Title: Sheer Cold (move)

Debuting in Generation III, Sheer Cold (ぜったいれいど, or Absolute Zero in Japanese) is an Ice-type attack notorious for its one-hit knockout capability. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, its Technical Machine designation is TM155. A successful strike from Sheer Cold results in damage equivalent to the target's entire current HP.

Although its accuracy is listed as 30%, Sheer Cold's actual probability of hitting a Pokémon is determined by a specific formula: it begins at 30% when both Pokémon share the same level, then increases by 1% for each level the user surpasses the target. When the user's level advantage over the target reaches 70 or more, Sheer Cold is guaranteed to hit. Conversely, if the user's level is inferior to the target's, the attack invariably misses. This hit probability is distinct from standard accuracy; it remains impervious to accuracy and evasion stat modifications, as well as external accuracy-altering effects like Gravity, Wide Lens, and Compound Eyes. Furthermore, Sheer Cold is incapable of striking Pokémon currently in a semi-invulnerable state. Nevertheless, certain effects that ensure move connection, including No Guard and Lock-On, will still influence Sheer Cold, provided the user's level is not below the target's.

Within Contest Spectaculars, Sheer Cold forms part of a combination and yields an additional three appeal points if Lock-On, Mind Reader, or Miracle Eye were executed in the turn immediately preceding it. An important distinction is that the move's fundamental hit chance decreases to 20% when used by a non-Ice-type Pokémon; under this particular calculation, if the user's level is at least 80 higher than the target's, Sheer Cold achieves a perfect hit rate.

In a Max Raid Battle, when targeting a Dynamax Pokémon protected by a mysterious barrier, a successful Sheer Cold strike will diminish two segments of that barrier's strength. Otherwise, Sheer Cold will consistently miss when aimed at Dynamax Pokémon. Tera Raid bosses, however, are impervious to Sheer Cold.

Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Sheer Cold does not instantly fell opponents, but rather inflicts standard damage. A successful hit from Sheer Cold consistently inflicts the freeze status on targets. As Sheer Cold operates differently in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, no longer being a one-hit knockout move, Cryogonal and all other Ice-type Pokémon gain STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus) when employing it.

Generally, this attack eliminates the target in a single blow. Before the release of Gates to Infinity, Sheer Cold would defeat foes by inflicting "calamitous damage" (とてつもない タメージ, or tremendous damage in Japanese), internally recorded as 9999 damage. Distinctively, Sheer Cold holds the unique position of being the sole one-hit knockout move introduced after Generation I, as well as the only special OHKO move. Prior to Generation VII, it stood alone as the only OHKO move lacking any Pokémon with a type-based immunity against it. Uniquely, Sheer Cold is the sole damaging move where type immunity arises from the move's inherent properties, rather than conventional type matchups or specific Ability immunities. This unique immunity applies to Ice-type Pokémon, who became impervious to the attack starting from Generation VII. Furthermore, in its Pokémon Legends: Z-A iteration, Sheer Cold stands as the only move possessing a guaranteed 100% chance to apply the freeze status condition to its target.

Title: Muddy Water (move)

Known in Japan as だくりゅう (Muddy Stream), this offensive Water-type technique debuted in Generation III. Until Generation IV, Marshtomp and Swampert exclusively employed it as their signature attack. Muddy Water inflicts damage and also carries a 30% probability of diminishing the accuracy of each affected foe by a single stage. Its effect reaches all adversaries positioned nearby. As a secondary move in Pokémon Contest combinations, its user garners 2 additional appeal points if Rain Dance was executed on the preceding turn. The move's offensive strength was reduced to 90. It is also capable of obliterating stalagmites present in the background scenery of battles. This attack causes damage and has the potential to reduce a target's accuracy by one stage.

Starting with *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity*, its area of effect expanded to encompass all Pokémon within the same room as the user, excluding the user itself (impacting both allies and adversaries). Within *Pokémon GO*, Muddy Water functions as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since July 21, 2019.
