Any stat reductions triggered by this technique are determined individually for every Pokémon it strikes. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated it possessed "a moderate chance of lowering the target's accuracy." Since Muddy Water's debut, its in-game animation has mirrored that of Surf, with the sole distinguishing factor being the hue.

Title: Bullet Seed (move)

Bullet Seed (タネマシンガン, or Seed Machine Gun in Japanese) is a damage-dealing Grass-type multistrike move that made its debut in Generation III. It held the TM09 designation from Generation III through Generation IV, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This move causes damage by striking the target 2 to 5 times per use. Bullet Seed possesses the ability to continue attacking even after breaking a substitute. Bide will only register the final strike of this move. Pokémon equipped with the Skill Link ability will invariably land five hits when utilizing Bullet Seed, provided it does not miss. Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof ability are entirely unaffected by Bullet Seed. Should a defending Pokémon have a maximum HP of 1, Sturdy will activate with each individual hit. Similarly, if the target has Weak Armor or Stamina, each distinct strike will trigger these abilities. When the user is holding either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every successive hit has an equal likelihood of causing the opponent to flinch.

Initially, Bullet Seed's base power was 10 per strike. The probability of hitting was as follows: a 37.5% chance for 2 hits, an identical 37.5% chance for 3 hits, a 12.5% chance for 4 hits, and a 12.5% chance for 5 hits. Each strike had an independent chance of becoming a critical hit. At this stage, Bullet Seed was classified as a special move. Mirror Coat, like Bide, would only acknowledge the last strike of the attack. In Pokémon Contests, Bullet Seed could be used as the second move in a combination, awarding the user 2 bonus appeal points if Growth was employed in the preceding turn.

Subsequently, Bullet Seed underwent a change, becoming a physical move. Counter, similar to Bide and Mirror Coat, would only register the final blow. Its base power per strike was increased from 10 to 25. The revised hit probabilities were set at a 35% chance for 2 hits, a 35% chance for 3 hits, a 15% chance for 4 hits, and a 15% chance for 5 hits. This adjustment led to an average total base power of 77.5. If a Focus Sash activates before the last strike is delivered, the immediate subsequent strike will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band retains its capacity to activate repeatedly to prevent fainting, but each chance is independent. For Contest Spectaculars, Bullet Seed can also form part of a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if the move Rototiller was utilized in the prior turn.

Later, Bullet Seed was modified to consistently hit 5 times. Furthermore, when employed as a Plus Move, it will always strike 6 times. Additionally, it is noted that Bullet Seed delivers damage by striking its target 2-5 times per use, where each individual hit undergoes a separate accuracy check, yet the move persists even if a particular hit fails. The move's critical-hit rate is reduced, specifically to 2% within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series. Prior to the release of Gates to Infinity, if a targeted Pokémon fainted mid-attack, the remaining projectiles could then strike a new target, and the remaining hits would still be executed even if a Reviver Seed was activated.

In other media, Bullet Seed serves as Ivysaur's Neutral Special move. Its use causes seeds to erupt from Ivysaur's bulb, inflicting repeated damage on those caught in the flare. In Pokémon GO, Bullet Seed is a Fast Attack that has been available since February 16, 2017. Greedent's initial move in Pokémon UNITE is Bullet Seed; it is acquired upon reaching level 7 by upgrading Tackle into it instead of Belch. This version sees the user spitting Berry seeds in a designated direction, damaging enemies within the area of effect and temporarily reducing their movement speed. The damage output of this move increases with the quantity of Oran Berries the user has stashed. Moreover, at level 13, this move's damage receives an further increase. In the episode No Stone Unturned!, Ash attempted to teach his Rowlet Bullet Seed, despite Rowlet being unable to learn the move until Generation IX. Bullet Seed holds the distinction of being the sole Grass-type multistrike move.

Title: Aerial Ace (move)

Known in Japanese as つばめがえし (Swallow Return), Aerial Ace is a damage-dealing Flying-type attack that first appeared in Generation III. This move was made available as TM40 across various main series games, specifically from Generation III up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Aerial Ace deals damage to its target and inherently bypasses all accuracy checks, ensuring it always lands unless the foe is within the semi-invulnerable turn of certain moves, such as Dig or Fly. In Triple Battles, it possesses the distinct characteristic of being able to strike opponents not directly adjacent to the user. A Pokémon utilizing Aerial Ace while possessing the Sharpness Ability will find its power boosted by 50%.

Regarding its 6★ EX effect in specific gameplay versions, prior to Version 2.18.5, Aerial Ace offered an "Additional Effect" where "The stat increase after a sync move is doubled once per battle."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Aerial Ace serves as Talonflame's inaugural move. It is acquired upon Fletchinder's evolution at level 5, chosen as an upgrade to Peck in place of Flame Charge. When activated, the user dashes through a designated enemy, inflicting damage. Furthermore, the user's subsequent basic attack becomes boosted. At level 11, the damage output of this move is enhanced.

In the manga series' Magnificent Meditite & Really Riolu II arc, Platinum instructed Pearl's Chatot, Chatler, to execute Aerial Ace, though the actual performance of the move occurred outside the displayed panels. The move's inspiration and nomenclature stem from Sasaki Kojirō's celebrated "Turning Swallow Cut" sword technique. This technique earned its name due to its resemblance to the nimble motion of a swallow's tail during flight and is traditionally interpreted as a rapid upward slash immediately succeeded by another downward cut along the identical trajectory, a sequence reflected in the move's visual animation.

Title: Icicle Spear (move)

A multi-hit offensive technique of the Ice type, Icicle Spear (known in Japan as つららばり, or Icicle Needle) debuted in the third generation of games. Before Generation V, Shellder exclusively possessed this move, although it was possible for other Pokémon to acquire it through genetic inheritance. When utilized, Icicle Spear causes harm by striking the opponent between two and five times. Its strike distribution involves a 37.5% probability for two hits, another 37.5% for three hits, and a 12.5% likelihood for either four or five strikes. Every individual strike carries the same potential for landing a critical hit. This attack possesses the capability to persist with its assault even subsequent to demolishing a substitute. Both Bide and Mirror Coat will register solely the final strike from Icicle Spear. Should the user be equipped with a King's Rock, every successive hit has an equivalent probability of inducing a flinch in the opposing Pokémon. A Pokémon possessing the Ability Skill Link will invariably achieve five strikes when deploying Icicle Spear, provided the move does not miss. Having been reclassified as a physical technique, Icicle Spear now falls under the effect of Counter rather than Mirror Coat. Similarly, if the user holds a Razor Fang, each subsequent connection holds an identical chance of causing the target to recoil. The base power of Icicle Spear has seen an increment to 25. The current probabilities for hits are a 35% chance for two strikes, a 35% chance for three strikes, a 15% chance for four strikes, and a 15% chance for five strikes. Consequently, the calculated average cumulative base power now stands at 77.5. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final blow being delivered, the subsequent strike will result in the defending Pokémon's immediate fainting. Conversely, a Focus Band is capable of triggering multiple times to avert a KO, though each activation attempt remains statistically independent. Sturdy will be invoked by every hit if the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1. Should the adversary possess Weak Armor or Stamina, each individual strike will trigger its effect. This technique projects a fan of five icicles forward from the user, with the central projectile oriented towards the designated target. It is possible for several of these icicles to impact the same opponent. Given Icicle Spear's classification as a long-range maneuver, the Pokémon executing it will first relocate to its trainer's proximity before initiating the attack's preparation; consequently, its efficacy is enhanced when deployed with the trainer near the target, facilitating impacts from numerous icicles. Each deployment of Icicle Spear delivers damage through 2 to 5 distinct blows to the adversary. An independent accuracy check is performed for every hit, yet the sequence of strikes persists even if one particular hit misses. In the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, its critical-hit rate is diminished to 2%. Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, should a Reviver Seed become active, the execution of the move will cease. Within Pokémon GO, Icicle Spear functions as a Charged Attack, which became accessible on January 16, 2022. Players cannot obtain this move in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Colosseum, or XD without external trading, given that no Pokémon can acquire it through means other than breeding, except in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen.

Title: Iron Defense (move)

This entry details the move known as Iron Defense. For information regarding the Pokémon Battle e card pack, please consult Iron Defense (Battle e). Known in Japanese as てっぺき (Iron Wall), Iron Defense is a non-damaging Steel-type technique that was first introduced in Generation III. This move significantly enhances the user's Defense stat by two stages. Its Power Points (PP) have been increased to 20, and it is also noted to raise the user's defensive capabilities for a duration of five turns. When powered up by a Steelium Z into Z-Iron Defense, the move gains the additional effect of resetting all of the user's previously lowered stats.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Iron Defense, along with all other status moves, was exclusively usable by opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired for any captured Pokémon. Boss Pokémon observed using Iron Defense included Blastoise, Shieldon, Bastiodon, and Bronzor. Within Pokémon UNITE, Iron Defense is Aegislash's second initial move. It provides the user with a powerful protective force, lasting for 3 seconds or until it successfully blocks one incoming attack. If an attack is blocked by this move, the user's boost count increases by 1. Upon reaching level 8, this move is then superseded by the option to learn either Wide Guard or Iron Head.

Title: Block (move)

For information regarding Bulbapedia's policy on blocking users, refer to Bulbapedia:Blocking policy. Known in Japanese as とおせんぼう (Block), this non-damaging move of the Normal type debuted in Generation III. Block functions to impede the target's ability to switch out of battle or escape, which includes attempts made with Teleport. This move circumvents conventional accuracy checks, ensuring it always connects unless the target is within the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Its effect persists solely while the Pokémon that initiated it stays on the battlefield.

Despite being affected, a Pokémon can still depart through Baton Pass; however, the incoming Pokémon will then become subject to Block's effect. Additionally, Pokémon under Block's influence are able to exit battle using U-turn. Conversely, if the Pokémon that deployed Block utilizes Baton Pass to switch, the blockade's effects will nonetheless continue. Furthermore, should a Pokémon ensnared by Block be targeted by Roar or Whirlwind, it will still be forced to switch. When a wild Pokémon employs Block on the player's team, the player will find escape impossible, unless their Pokémon possesses the Run Away Ability or is equipped with a Smoke Ball. Should a Pokémon immobilize a foe with Block and subsequently switch out via Baton Pass, that foe will cease to be trapped. Electrified departures are also possible, as Pokémon under Block's effect can exit using Volt Switch. Moreover, offensive moves like Dragon Tail and Circle Throw will compel the trapped Pokémon to switch places.

As of a certain point, Block is ineffective against Ghost-type Pokémon. It now strikes its intended victim even when they are shielded by Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, yet it will be thwarted if the target is safeguarded by Crafty Shield. Furthermore, Pokémon held by Block can still make an exit using Parting Shot and Flip Turn. When incorporated into a Contest Spectacular combination, Block allows specific moves—namely Explosion, Memento, Perish Song, and Self-Destruct—to accrue an additional three appeal points if deployed during the subsequent turn. Boosting Block with Normalium Z transforms it into Z-Block, which elevates the user's Defense stat by a single stage.

In a different context, Block imposes the "stuck" status condition (formerly known as Leg Hold/Shadow Hold), which inhibits the target's movement (though attacking remains possible) and prevents them from swapping positions with their team leader. This impediment persists for 2-5 or 7 turns. Within Super Mystery Dungeon, spectral Pokémon are immune to becoming stuck.

Shifting to Pokémon UNITE, Block serves as Snorlax's secondary maneuver. Players acquire it upon reaching level 7, by evolving Rest into Block instead of Yawn. The user manifests and maintains a barrier in a specified direction for a duration of 4 seconds. Adversaries encountering this barrier are repelled and incapacitated. While actively Blocking, the user is incapable of attacking or utilizing abilities, though slow movement is still possible. During the act of Blocking, the user experiences a 25% augmentation to both their Defense and Sp. Defense stats. Reaching level 13 grants the user reduced incoming damage for the duration of this move's effect. Intriguingly, in Generation III, the status display inaccurately indicates this move possesses 100% accuracy, despite it inherently disregarding all accuracy and evasion calculations.

Title: Howl (move)

First appearing in Generation III, Howl (とおぼえ in Japanese) is a Normal-type status move that deals no damage. When upgraded to Z-Howl using a Normalium Z, it bestows an additional Attack stat stage increase upon the Pokémon using it. Notably, Throat Chop is ineffective at stopping a Pokémon from executing Z-Howl. The primary effect of Howl is to boost the Attack stats of both the user and any allied Pokémon by a single stage. It is currently categorized as a sound-based move. Consequently, it fails to impact allies possessing the Soundproof Ability. Nevertheless, Howl invariably influences the user, even if that Pokémon has Soundproof. Allies protected by a Substitute are immune to Howl's effects, unless the user's Ability is Infiltrator. Despite this, protection moves, such as Max Guard and Crafty Shield, do not impede its use. The user itself receives a one-stage boost to its Attack. Furthermore, this move is subject to the Muzzled status condition. Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, Howl, alongside all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. It was observed as a move employed by Boss Poochyena and Rockruff. Uniquely, Howl stands as the sole variant of Defense Curl that has not yet appeared in the animated series. It is also the sole sound-based move incapable of affecting Pokémon positioned behind a Substitute.

Title: Dragon Claw (move)

Dragon Claw (Japanese: ドラゴンクロー Dragon Claw) is a damaging Dragon-type technique that made its debut in Generation III. It held the designation of TM02 from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappears as TM02 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, as well as in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Dragon Claw delivers damage and possesses no supplementary effects. Within the context of Pokémon Contests, Dragon Claw qualifies as the second action in a combination, bestowing 2 bonus appeal points upon the user if Dragon Dance, Dragon Rage, or DragonBreath was previously employed in the preceding turn. For Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Dragon Claw's Power Points (PP) were adjusted from 15 down to 10, and it gained an enhanced critical hit ratio. The move fundamentally inflicts damage. When wielded by a Gigantic Pokémon in Gates to Infinity, Dragon Claw strikes adjacent enemies, featuring a base PP of 20 and a base accuracy of 90%.

In Pokémon UNITE, Dragon Claw serves as Garchomp's alternative second move. It becomes available when Gabite attains level 7, allowing players to upgrade Bulldoze into Dragon Claw instead of Earthquake. The user viciously slashes all targets within the designated area twice. The initial strike deals damage and pushes opponents. The subsequent strike inflicts increased damage within a range slightly exceeding the initial designated area. Incorporating this move into a Dragon Rush combo heightens the slow effect applied to enemies. Upon reaching level 13, the move's damage output is further amplified. An instance in the anime episode "All Things Bright and Beautifly!" showed Jessie instructing her Seviper to use Dragon Claw, though Seviper was unable to comply as it cannot learn the move. Dragon Claw is also referenced in the media titles Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, and Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder.

Title: Frenzy Plant (move)

Known in Japanese as ハードプラント (Hard Plant), Frenzy Plant is a Grass-type damage-dealing attack first appearing in the third generation of Pokémon games. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it functions as TM155, and will be TM160 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. It stands alongside Blast Burn and Hydro Cannon as one of the quintessential elemental attacks of its respective type. Every Grass-type first partner Pokémon, once fully evolved, is capable of learning this particular technique.

After inflicting damage, Frenzy Plant obligates the user to spend the subsequent turn recharging. This recharge requirement is constant unless the attack fails to hit its target. Within Pokémon Contests, Frenzy Plant serves as the second move in a combination, awarding an additional 4 appeal points if preceded by Growth.

Numerous Move Tutors are available to instruct fully evolved Grass-type first partner Pokémon in Frenzy Plant. Between Generation III and VII, Pokémon also required a maximized friendship rating to be taught the move. Specific tutors include: in HeartGold and SoulSilver, an elderly man residing in Blackthorn City's Move Deleter/Move Reminder's house; in Black 2 and White 2, a man positioned outside the Pokémon World Tournament building; in X and Y, a man located in the southwestern residence of Snowbelle City; in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, a man situated in the southeastern part of Mauville City's first floor; and in Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, a man found on the second level of Hau'oli City's shopping mall.

This attack deals damage, and upon a successful hit, imposes a 1-turn recoil status condition on the user, rendering them unable to act the following turn. Before Gates to Infinity, this effect was known as the Paused status condition. As Frenzy Plant is exclusively obtained via Move Tutors in the main series, its acquisition method differs significantly within the Mystery Dungeon games. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Venusaur gains an opportunity to learn Frenzy Plant at level 99, provided it possesses a minimum of 333 IQ points. Should this condition not be met by level 99, the Gulpin Link Shop allows for its relearning. For Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, a qualifying Pokémon endeavors to learn Frenzy Plant at level 72 if its IQ points total at least 300. If this prerequisite isn't fulfilled by level 72, the Electivire Link Shop offers relearning options.

Serperior can be instructed in Frenzy Plant through a unique TM within Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity. This TM becomes available once Pokémon Paradise achieves a Perfect Rank. Hawlucha's Slam School in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon provides instruction for this move upon payment of 11,000. In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, players can have the move taught at the Gulpin Link Shop subsequent to completing the main narrative.

During its American and European region releases in Pokémon Colosseum, the move was mistakenly titled Solid Plant, a name more closely reflecting its initial Japanese designation. It shares the highest base power among all Grass-type attacks with Chloroblast, when excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. When utilized as the basis for Bloom Doom, Frenzy Plant yields a Z-Move with the highest base power among all Grass-type moves.

Title: Bulk Up (move)

Known as ビルドアップ (Build Up) in Japanese, Bulk Up is a non-damaging Fighting-type maneuver that debuted in Generation III. It served as Technical Machine TM08 from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, its TM designation shifted to TM30. When executed, Bulk Up causes the Pokémon employing it to enhance both its Attack and Defense stats, each ascending by a single stage. Harnessing the power of a Fightinium Z item provides an additional boost, elevating the user's Attack stat by yet another stage. In certain contexts, Bulk Up's PP stands at a maximum of 10, and it confers an increase to the user's offensive and defensive capabilities for a duration of four turns. As a core effect, this move enhances the user's Attack and Defense by one stage apiece.

Within the world of Pokémon Rumble Rush, every status-modifying move, including Bulk Up, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon; none could be acquired by Pokémon captured by players. Specifically, Boss Gurdurr was observed utilizing Bulk Up. For Machop, before its evolution into Machamp, Bulk Up functions as its secondary move. Activating it causes the Pokémon to self-buff for three seconds, boosting its attack power by 15%. Throughout this period, the user becomes unstoppable, simultaneously receiving a modest increase to both attack speed and movement speed. Furthermore, the subsequent basic attack initiated by the user becomes empowered. Upon reaching level 5 and evolving into Machoke, this particular move is substituted by a choice between Dynamic Punch or Submission. Intriguingly, the designation for this maneuver shares its name with Grimmsnarl's species category.

Title: Bounce (move)

First introduced in Generation III, Bounce (Japanese: とびはねる Bounce) is a Flying-type attack capable of inflicting damage. This move was designated as TM52 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. On the initial turn, the user springs high into the air, thereby evading the majority of incoming attacks. On the subsequent turn, the user plummets onto the adversary, dealing damage and possessing a 30% chance of inducing paralysis.

During Bounce's semi-invulnerable ascent, the user remains susceptible to Gust, Twister, Thunder, and Sky Uppercut; attacks from Gust and Twister will inflict double damage. The user can also be struck during this semi-invulnerable phase if it was previously marked by Lock-On or Mind Reader. Historically, wild Pokémon could be captured during Bounce's semi-invulnerable stage. A Pokémon in this elevated state can also be hit if attacked by a Pokémon possessing the No Guard ability, or if the user itself has No Guard. If Gust or Twister connects while the user is semi-invulnerable, the move's power will be doubled instead of the damage dealt, resulting in an almost identical outcome. Note that Pokémon are no longer capturable during Bounce's semi-invulnerable stage.

Should the user be holding a Power Herb, Bounce can be executed entirely within one turn. In Triple Battles, Bounce is capable of hitting opponents that are not adjacent. If Gust or Twister lands a hit during the semi-invulnerable turn, the damage dealt will be doubled rather than the move's power, producing virtually the same effect. Hurricane, Smack Down, and Thousand Arrows can all strike the user during Bounce's semi-invulnerable stage. A successful Smack Down will ground the user. Thousand Arrows specifically will knock the user down in the same turn it connects.

The user cannot be targeted while airborne, granting full immunity to attacks until its descent. Hurricane, Gust, Twister, and Thunder are unable to hit an airborne target. Bounce confers a "bouncing" status upon the user during the first turn. While airborne, the Pokémon is immune to most moves, with specified exceptions including Gust, Thunder, Twister, and Sky Uppercut. Projectiles will also pass through the user as if its tile were unoccupied. On the second turn, the user assaults the enemy directly in front, potentially inflicting paralysis.

Within the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Bounce's damage is doubled at the conclusion of damage calculation. Given its nature as a two-turn move, it cannot be linked. Bounce is also unusable in Pokémon Towers across the Adventure Squad series.

While the user is bouncing, specific situations will cancel the move's use: an opponent's sync move targeting the user's team, the user's team performing a unity attack in a co-op battle, or the user being hit by an opponent's max move (an ally being attacked by a max move does not count). Even if Bounce is not fully completed, MP will still be consumed.

As in the core series, the full execution of this move spans approximately two actions. Only the initial bouncing portion of this move reduces the user's sync-move countdown by one, not the attacking half. If Bounce's user would take damage from weather effects or gain healing from their skill or status change, these triggers occur only after the bouncing begins, not after the move's attack. These events unfold during the predetermined duration of the user's bouncing. If the user's bouncing constitutes the final action before their team's sync moves are ready, and any of these triggers activate, the sync moves of the user's allies will only become available after the trigger(s) have concluded. This can sometimes result in the user's team's sync move becoming ready only after the user has ceased bouncing and executed its attack.

Initial wording described the user beginning a bounce, then, after a set duration, stopping the bounce to attack, with a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), the description stated: "The user begins bouncing. After a set time, the user stops bouncing and attacks. Has a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed. No other actions can be taken while bouncing. Most moves used by opponents will not hit the user while it is bouncing. If the user is hit by a sync move while bouncing, it will stop bouncing." A subsequent description change before Version 2.11.0 (from July 28, 2021) merely reiterated: "...If the user is hit by a sync move while bouncing, it will stop bouncing." Further refining the text prior to Version 2.23.5 (from August 8, 2022) updated this to include max moves: "...If the user is hit by a sync move or max move while bouncing, it will stop bouncing."

In "With a Spoink in Your Step II", Tate and Liza's two Spoink were stated to know Bounce, but they were never observed employing the move. Similarly, in "All About Arceus VIII", Gold commanded his Politoed, Polibo, to use Bounce, yet the move's execution transpired off-panel.

Bounce holds the distinction of being the sole Flying-type move capable of inducing paralysis. Despite its Flying typing, no Flying-type Pokémon were able to learn Bounce in its debut generation.

Title: Mud Shot (move)

A Ground-type attack that deals damage, Mud Shot (マッドショット in Japanese) made its debut in Generation III. This move inflicts damage and subsequently reduces the target's Speed stat by one stage. In Pokémon Contests, Mud Shot can contribute to a combination, granting an additional appeal point if Sandstorm was used during the prior turn. Mud Shot also causes damage and lowers the enemy's Travel Speed by one stage. The description for this move, prior to Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), was simply: "Lowers the target's Speed." In the comic Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder In Shock!! Chairman Rose's Plan, Sordward commanded his Golisopod to use Mud Shot, but the action itself took place off-panel. During Generation III, Secret Power adopted the same animation as Mud Shot when activated on sandy terrain. From Generation V onward, Secret Power similarly mirrors Mud Shot's animation when executed in puddles. Distinctively, Mud Shot is the only move among Icy Wind variations that does not strike both opponents in a Double Battle.

Title: Poison Tail (move)

Originating in Generation III, Poison Tail (Japanese: ポイズンテール Poison Tail) is a damaging Poison-type attack. Prior to Generation V, it functioned as the exclusive signature technique of Seviper, although other Pokémon were still able to learn it through breeding. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is identified as TM026. This move inflicts damage and inherently possesses an increased critical hit ratio. It carries a 10% chance of poisoning the opposing Pokémon. Fundamentally, Poison Tail delivers damage and may leave the target poisoned. The elevated critical-hit rate for this move differs in spin-off series, being 50% in the Rescue Team series and 30% in the Explorers series. Furthermore, Poison Tail can contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was utilized in the preceding turn. A description prevalent before version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) indicated: "Critical hits land more easily. Has a very small chance of leaving the target poisoned." Interestingly, Goomy and Sliggoo cannot acquire Poison Tail via TM in Generation IX, despite having been capable of inheriting it through breeding in Generations VI and VII.

Title: Covet (move)

The Normal-type damaging attack known as Covet (Japanese: ほしがる, Covet) debuted in Generation III. This move inflicts damage and simultaneously seizes the target's held item, provided one is present. Item theft is prevented if the target holds no item or if the user is already holding one.

Initially, even if the user of Covet fainted during the attack (e.g., from the target's Rough Skin), the held item would still be stolen. At first, Covet could not pilfer items from Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability, nor from those protected by a substitute. Held e-Reader Berries and Mail were also immune to theft. Historically, Covet facilitated permanent item acquisition from both wild Pokémon and those belonging to Trainers; however, wild Pokémon were unable to steal items from the player's Pokémon. In specific environments like Link Battles, most battle facilities (with the exception of the Battle Pyramid), and Secret Bases, any stolen items were returned post-battle (or after the challenge, as with the Battle Pike). In Pokémon Colosseum and XD, opponents using Covet did not permanently remove items.

Over time, mechanics evolved. Covet now registers as a contact move. Should an opposing Trainer's Pokémon use Covet on the player, the item is returned at the battle's conclusion. Pokémon with the Multitype Ability are immune to item theft. Previously, a held Griseous Orb could not be stolen. When Covet now triggers a consumable held item on the target (such as a Chilan Berry or Focus Sash), that item is consumed before it can be stolen. A notable change dictates that if the user faints while executing Covet (e.g., due to Rough Skin or Rocky Helmet), it can no longer steal the target's held item. Conversely, if Covet causes a Pokémon with Sticky Hold to faint, its item *can* now be stolen. The mechanic of permanent item theft from Trainer's Pokémon has been removed; however, items from wild Pokémon can still be permanently acquired by the player. A held Griseous Orb is now vulnerable to Covet, unless either the user or the target is Giratina. Plates cannot be stolen if either the user or target is Arceus (Multitype's effect on Covet is irrelevant here), and Drives are safe if either the user or target is Genesect. Further protections exist: Mega Stones or Orbs cannot be stolen if usable for Mega Evolution or Primal Reversion by either Pokémon; Z-Crystals are universally safe; Memories are protected if Silvally is involved; the Rusted Sword if Zacian is present; the Rusted Shield if Zamazenta is present; and Booster Energy if a Paradox Pokémon is involved.

If Covet's type is altered to Electric or Ice and the target holds a Cell Battery or Snowball, respectively, that item will activate and be consumed after Covet deals damage. In Contest Spectaculars, Covet can initiate a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to Bestow, Fling, or Present if used in the subsequent turn. In Max Raid Battles, Covet completely fails, dealing no damage, regardless of whether it targets the raid boss or an ally.

The core function persists: Covet inflicts damage and acquires the enemy's held item if the user is unburdened. However, if the target faints, Covet will not secure the held item, though it will fall to the ground.

In Super Mystery Dungeon, if an enemy successfully steals an item from the player, it will warp, become Terrified, and flee with the stolen item.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Covet is Greedent's second move. It's unlocked when Skwovet evolves at level 5, upgrading Defense Curl into Covet instead of Stuff Cheeks. In UNITE, the user charges forcefully, damaging and throwing hit opposing Pokémon. The user gains temporary hindrance resistance. While running, Berries are dropped; eating one temporarily increases movement speed, with greater boosts for more Berries eaten. Tackle, Belch, or Bullet Seed can also be used during this run. Using Covet resets the cooldown of Tackle or Belch. At level 11, both Oran Berry drop frequency and Covet's damage are enhanced.

Title: Volt Tackle (move)

This entry details the move Volt Tackle; for information regarding the song of the same name, please refer to Volt Tackle (song). Known as ボルテッカー (Volteccer) in Japanese, Volt Tackle is an Electric-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation III. It serves as the exclusive move for Pikachu's evolutionary family. Pikachu can transform it into the unique Z-Move Catastropika when equipped with Pikanium Z. While delivering damage, the user suffers recoil damage equal to ⅓ of the harm dealt to the target.

In Pokémon Contest combinations, Volt Tackle functions as a secondary move, awarding the user 6 additional appeal points if Charge was executed during the prior turn. Currently, Volt Tackle is classified as a physical move and has a 10% likelihood of paralyzing its target. As part of a Contest Spectacular combination, this move grants an extra three appeal points if Charge was utilized in the preceding turn. The move's Power Points (PP) have been lowered to 5. Furthermore, it carries a 30% chance of paralyzing the target for three turns and simultaneously frees the user from drowsiness. Should Mega Raichu X deploy Volt Tackle, its Attack stat experiences a rise.

Commencing with Pokémon Emerald, a Pichu hatching from an Egg will naturally possess Volt Tackle if one of its parents held a Light Ball at the point of the Egg's creation. Nonetheless, Volt Tackle is not categorized as a standard Egg Move; consequently, it cannot be inherited merely by breeding with a parent already familiar with the technique. The singular method for a Pichu to hatch knowing Volt Tackle involves one of its parents holding a Light Ball. Additionally, Volt Tackle cannot be conveyed to another Pokémon via a Mirror Herb during a picnic, nor through the Nursery system.

In Pokémon Sun and Moon, a woman in Konikoni City, notable for her three Pikachu, is capable of teaching Volt Tackle to the player's Pikachu after providing them with Pikanium Z. For Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, a gentleman located at Hano Grand Resort can instruct Pikachu in this move subsequent to the player's acquisition of Pikanium Z, which is bestowed as a prize by the renowned Pikachu, Chuuster (Japanese: チュウジロウ Chujirō), for triumphing over Reporter Rita. An Alolan Raichu that learned Volt Tackle in Generation VII and was then transferred through Pokémon Bank and Pokémon HOME is eligible to have the move recalled via the Pokémon HOME move reminder feature. This constitutes the exclusive means for Alolan Raichu to possess Volt Tackle in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Furthermore, an Alolan Raichu that knows this move is prohibited from online trades and cannot be utilized in the Battle Stadium.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Volt Tackle deals damage, and the user experiences recoil. Specifically, in the Rescue Team and Explorers titles, the recoil damage taken is equivalent to ⅛ of the user's maximum HP. From Gates to Infinity onwards, Volt Tackle gained the potential to inflict paralysis, and the recoil damage calculation shifted to 20% of the user's maximum HP. As Volt Tackle is exclusively acquired as an Egg Move in the core series, its acquisition methods vary in the Mystery Dungeon games: In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Pichu can master Volt Tackle at level 50, provided it also attains a minimum of 333 IQ points (7.5 stars). If this prerequisite isn't satisfied until after level 50, Volt Tackle remains available for re-teaching at the Gulpin Link Shop. For Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, where Egg Moves are a common feature, Pichu originating from Eggs might inherently know Volt Tackle. Within Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, Pichu, Pikachu, and Raichu are able to acquire Volt Tackle through a TM, which is rewarded upon Paradise achieving Perfect Rank. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Pichu, Pikachu, and Raichu can learn Volt Tackle at Hawlucha's Slam School for a cost of 10,000. Finally, in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, Pichu, Pikachu, and Raichu can be taught Volt Tackle at the Gulpin Link Shop for 11,000, once the main story is concluded.

Turning to Pokémon GO, Volt Tackle is a Charged Attack that became available starting March 5, 2024.

From Super Smash Bros. Brawl onwards, Volt Tackle has been recognized as Pikachu's Final Smash. When activated, rather than surging forward cloaked in electricity, Pikachu becomes encased in a blue orb of electrical energy and maneuvers through the air. This electrified sphere delivers significant damage and is capable of traversing platforms, although its control proves quite difficult. In contrast to its self-damaging effect in the core series, Pikachu sustains no harm from Volt Tackle when it is deployed in the Super Smash Bros. franchise. For Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS and Wii U, Volt Tackle's path is directed by a smaller, player-controlled orb of light, thereby simplifying its tracking. In Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, the re-appearing combatant Pichu also acquires Volt Tackle as its Final Smash; akin to Pichu's other electric techniques, it inflicts self-damage after execution. This self-inflicted damage on Pichu renders this iteration of Volt Tackle more faithful to the Pokémon games. Similar to numerous Final Smashes in Ultimate, Volt Tackle has been rendered completely automatic and swifter, which eases its execution and facilitates players' quicker return to battle. A description related to the move states: "Pikachu, transformed into a ball of light that can slam into foes. It can also fly to chase down those who try to jump out of range. Sparks get stronger when you press the attack button. However, its increased inertia makes midair movement tough. If you get carried away flying, the effect will end, and you'll destroy yourself. Be careful it doesn't happen to you." This particular "buddy move" was referred to as B Volt Tackle before version 2.38.0.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Volt Tackle is Pikachu's secondary ability, acquired at level 6 by evolving Electroweb into it, as an alternative to Thunderbolt. The Pokémon executing this move dashes towards a chosen foe, gaining unstoppable status, inflicting damage, and launching the target skyward for 1.4s. Upon reaching level 13, its cooldown period is lessened by 2s.

The Japanese designation, ボルテッカー (Volteccer), for Volt Tackle stems from an attack featured in the Game Freak title Pulseman, utilized by its titular protagonist. Both techniques draw their inspiration from Tekkaman, renowned for his signature attack named Voltekka. In the Pokémon Adventures manga, Volt Tackle initially appeared in The Last Battle XIV, where Gold gave it the moniker "Super Rising Thunder," though its depiction significantly diverged from most other canonical portrayals. Nevertheless, in a subsequent chapter, the move is referred to by its core series name and, contrary to its earlier depiction, is illustrated as more of a physical technique than a special one.

Title: Magical Leaf (move)

A Grass-type offensive technique, Magical Leaf (Japanese: マジカルリーフ Magical Leaf), was first introduced in Generation III and is capable of inflicting damage. It was designated TM10 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the TM033 slot in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Magical Leaf deals damage and inherently bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring it always connects with the target. The only exception to this guaranteed hit occurs if the target is in the semi-invulnerable state of a move such as Dig or Fly. In Pokémon Contests, Magical Leaf functions as the second move in a combination, awarding the user 2 extra appeal points provided Growth was performed on the preceding turn. Within Pokémon GO, Magical Leaf is classified as a Fast Attack and has been available since February 26, 2022. A unique detail from Generation III is that Secret Power utilizes Magical Leaf's animation when used in very long grass.

Title: Water Sport (move)

Water Sport (Japanese: みずあそび, meaning Water Game) is a non-damaging move of the Water type, first appearing in Generation III. This field-effect move initially reduced the base power of Fire-type attacks by 50% for all combatants, with its influence persisting until the user was switched out.

In subsequent generations, the base power reduction for Fire-type moves on both sides in battle was updated to 67%, precisely approximated as a factor of 1352/4096, and this effect now endures for a fixed five turns, regardless of whether the user remains in battle.

Beyond standard battles, Water Sport has roles in Pokémon Contests and Contest Spectaculars. As a starter move in a Pokémon Contest combination, it enables Mud Sport, Refresh, and Water Gun to earn double their usual appeal if employed on the subsequent turn. It can also conclude a combination, granting the user 4 bonus appeal points if either Mud Sport or Rain Dance was performed on the preceding turn. For Contest Spectaculars, using Water Sport after Rain Dance awards an additional three appeal points.

When enhanced by a Waterium Z, Water Sport transforms into Z-Water Sport, which boosts the user's Special Defense by one stage upon activation. The move also establishes a "Water Sport" floor status, which generally lowers the damage inflicted by Fire-type moves.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Water Sport exhibits specific mechanics. In the Rescue Team and Explorers games, it lasts for 10-11 turns and halves incoming damage from Fire-type attacks. From Gates to Infinity onward, this status extends for 20 turns and weakens Fire-type moves by 30%. Notably, in Generation III, the in-game status screen incorrectly listed Water Sport as having 100% accuracy, despite it targeting the entire field and thus bypassing accuracy and evasion checks.

Title: Calm Mind (move)

Known in Japanese as めいそう (Meditation), Calm Mind debuted in Generation III as a non-damaging Psychic-type move. It was assigned as TM04 from Generation III through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappeared as TM04 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was designated as TM33. The move boosts the user's Special Attack and Special Defense stats by one stage each.

Within Pokémon Contests, Calm Mind serves as an initiating move in a combination. When used first, it causes Confusion, Dream Eater, Future Sight, Light Screen, Luster Purge, Meditate, Mist Ball, Psybeam, Psychic, Psycho Boost, Psywave, or Reflect to score double the standard appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. Furthermore, in Contest Spectaculars, Calm Mind contributes to a combination where using Baton Pass or Stored Power next grants the user an additional three appeal points.

When empowered by Psychium Z, Calm Mind uniquely resets all of the user's lowered stats. Although its PP has been reduced to 10, Calm Mind also enhances the user's offensive and defensive capabilities for a duration of four turns, specifically raising the user's Sp. Attack and Sp. Defense by one stage each.

In the context of Gardevoir's specific move set, Calm Mind is featured. When Gardevoir utilizes it, the Pokémon meditates to charge energy, subsequently empowering Stored Power based on the number of prior Calm Mind uses, culminating in a maximum of four shockwaves. Additionally, Calm Mind can be seamlessly transitioned, or 'cancelled,' into Energy Ball, Dazzling Gleam, Psychic, or Teleport.

Within Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Calm Mind, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any caught Pokémon. Specifically, Boss Lugia was observed employing Calm Mind.

Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) simply read: "Raises the user's Sp. Atk and Sp. Def."

In Pokémon UNITE, Calm Mind functions as Sylveon's secondary move. Players acquire it at level 6 by choosing to upgrade Baby-Doll Eyes to Calm Mind instead of Draining Kiss. Upon activation, the user concentrates its mind and spirit, gaining increased movement speed, a 40% boost to Sp. Attack, and a 10% increase to Sp. Defense for a brief duration. Reaching level 12 further enhances this move: for up to 3 seconds following its use, any incoming enemy attack that hits the user has its damage entirely nullified, and the user receives a protective shield.

The episode "Leaping Past Lopunny" depicted Buck instructing his Claydol to perform Calm Mind; however, the execution of the move occurred off-panel.

Title: Leaf Blade (move)

Known in Japanese as リーフブレード, Leaf Blade is a Grass-type damage-dealing move first introduced in Generation III. Before Generation IV, it served as the unique signature move for Grovyle and Sceptile. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was available as TR50. This attack inflicts damage and inherently possesses an increased critical hit ratio. Its base power started at 70, later escalating to 90. If executed by a Pokémon with the Sharpness Ability, its power receives a 50% boost. Leaf Blade can also contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if the move Focus Energy was utilized in the preceding turn. More recently, its Power Points (PP) were reduced to 10, and its power was lowered to 85. Beyond main series games, this move also features an elevated critical hit chance in spin-off titles: 50% in the Rescue Team series and 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. As a sync move, prior to June 22, 2023, it offered no secondary effects. Although Virizion was publicly announced on the official website as knowing Leaf Blade, it was never actually depicted using the move. In the Brazilian Portuguese dub of Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire, spanning from "Clamperl of Wisdom" to "From Cradle to Save," Leaf Blade was rendered as "Folha Navalha" or a minor variation. This was a translation previously used since "Enter the Dragonite" for the move Razor Leaf. Intriguingly, from its animated series debut through forty-seven subsequent episodes, Leaf Blade was accurately translated as "Lâmina de Folha," now its official and most fitting designation. The reason for the subsequent disappearance of "Lâmina de Folha" from the dub until "Odd Pokémon Out!" is currently unknown.

Title: Dragon Dance (move)

Should you be searching for its equivalent skill in Pokémon Shuffle, consult Skill (Shuffle) → Dancing Dragons. Dragon Dance (Japanese: りゅうのまい Dragon Dance) is a Dragon-type move that inflicts no damage, having been introduced in Generation III. It featured as TR51 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM100 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. The move itself boosts the user's Attack and Speed stats by one stage apiece. When augmented by a Dragonium Z into Z-Dragon Dance, any of the user's stat reductions are entirely nullified. Within Pokémon Contests, Dragon Dance functions as an opening move for a combination, resulting in Dragon Claw, Dragon Rage, and DragonBreath gaining double the standard appeal if executed on the following turn. Alternatively, it can conclude a combination, awarding the user 1 bonus appeal point provided Dragon Rage or DragonBreath was performed in the turn prior. In certain contexts, Dragon Dance also boosts the user's Attack and Travel Speed by one stage apiece. Pertaining to Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status moves, including Dragon Dance, were exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and unobtainable by any caught Pokémon. It was particularly observed as a move utilized by Boss Dragonite. Transitioning to Pokémon UNITE, Dragon Dance is assigned as Dragonite's primary move. It becomes available after evolving into Dragonair at level 5, when Twister can be upgraded into it as an alternative to Extreme Speed. The user executes a dash towards a specified direction, cycling through a left arc, a right arc, and a direct straight dash with each subsequent activation. Upon activation, the move bestows a temporary enhancement, boosting the attack speed of the subsequent 3 basic attacks, elevating movement speed, increasing Attack by 8% (with a cap at 24%), and advancing the stage of Hyper Beam. This cumulative effect can stack up to 3 times, and its duration is fully replenished with each reuse. All accumulated stacks dissipate after a short duration. Successful basic attacks decrease this ability's cooldown by 1s. Reaching level 11 grants the move an additional increase to the user's movement speed, provided enemies are in close proximity when the dance concludes. Notably, before Clangorous Soul's introduction in Generation VIII, Dragon Dance stood as the only Dragon-type status move.

Title: Rock Blast (move)

Originating in Generation III, Rock Blast (Japanese: ロックブラスト) is a damage-dealing Rock-type move characterized by its multistrike nature. It was designated as TM54 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM076 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This attack inflicts damage by striking the target between two and five times per activation. Initially, the probability distribution for its hits was: a 37.5% chance for two hits, another 37.5% for three hits, a 12.5% chance for four hits, and a final 12.5% chance for five hits. Each individual strike carries an independent and equal chance of becoming a critical hit. Rock Blast can persist in its attack sequence even after successfully shattering a substitute. However, moves like Bide and Counter will only register and respond to the final strike executed by Rock Blast. Should the user be equipped with a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every successive hit has an equivalent opportunity to induce flinching in the opposing Pokémon. For Pokémon possessing the Skill Link Ability, Rock Blast will invariably connect five times, provided it doesn't miss.

A revision saw Rock Blast's accuracy improve, escalating from 80% to 90%. Concurrently, its hit distribution was adjusted to: 35% chance for two hits, 35% chance for three hits, 15% chance for four hits, and 15% chance for five hits. With this change, the averaged total base power, accounting for a 10% inherent miss chance, now stands at 69.8. When a Focus Sash activates prior to the delivery of the final strike, the subsequent strike will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its capacity for repeated activation to avert fainting, though each chance operates independently. Sturdy will trigger with every hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. Should the target possess Weak Armor, each strike from Rock Blast will activate its effect. Pokémon endowed with the Bulletproof Ability are entirely immune to this attack. Additionally, each strike will now also trigger the Stamina Ability. Rock Blast will always hit 5 times. When utilized as a Plus Move, its strikes extend to always hit 6 times.

Rock Blast again inflicts damage, hitting the target 2-5 times per use. Every individual hit undergoes a separate accuracy check, yet the move will continue its sequence even if one of these checks fails. Within the Mystery Dungeon series, specifically Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad, the move's critical-hit rate is reduced to 1%. From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, the activation of a Reviver Seed will cause the move to conclude. In Pokémon GO, Rock Blast functions as a Charged Attack and has been available since February 16, 2017. Notably, Rock Blast holds the distinction of being the sole Rock-type multistrike move.

Title: Shock Wave (move)

Introduced in Generation III, Shock Wave (Japanese: でんげきは Electrical Shock Wave) is an Electric-type attack capable of inflicting damage. This particular move served as TM34 across Generation III, Generation IV, and within the games Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Shock Wave boasts the unique characteristic of always hitting its target by bypassing typical accuracy checks, unless the foe is momentarily immune during the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. In Pokémon Contests, Shock Wave functions as the subsequent move in a combination, granting the user 2 bonus appeal points if Charge was previously utilized. Moreover, it contributes to a Contest Spectacular combination, awarding an extra three appeal points when Charge has been performed in the turn prior. In the narrative of Rayquaza Redemption I, Ruby's Kirlia, known as Rara, was revealed to possess Shock Wave via Imprison, though she has never actually been depicted employing the move.

Title: Water Pulse (move)

This offensive Water-type technique, known in Japanese as みずのはどう (Water Pulse), first appeared in Generation III. It was designated as TM03 across Generations III and IV, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Beyond dealing damage, Water Pulse carries a 20% probability of leaving the affected Pokémon confused. This move possesses the unique property of striking foes not directly next to the user in Triple Battles. A Pokémon possessing the Mega Launcher Ability will see Water Pulse's power boosted by 50% when employed. For Pokémon Contest combinations, Water Pulse serves as an effective second move; using it immediately after Rain Dance grants the performer 3 additional appeal points. There are specific instances where Water Pulse is unable to inflict confusion, instead bypassing accuracy and evasion checks to guarantee a hit. Overall, it deals damage and holds the potential to confuse its target. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a small chance of leaving the target confused."

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Water Pulse functions as Azumarill's initial signature move. Trainers acquire it at level 7 by evolving Bubble, an alternative to selecting Play Rough. When activated, this ability discharges a pulsating aquatic projectile directly ahead of the user. Upon striking an adversary, the water blast reduces their movement speed for 2 seconds and inflicts damage upon all surrounding foes within an area of effect centered on the primary target. Following the initial impact, the aquatic projectile automatically ricochets to another available opponent. This allows the water blast to consecutively rebound between a maximum of three targets, counting the first, with every subsequent hit replicating the effects of the initial strike. During the water blast's trajectory, the Pokémon can execute a brief rolling dash toward a specified direction. Reaching level 13 enhances this capability, increasing the total potential bounce targets by 2.

During the episode "All Things Bright and Beautifly!", Jessie commanded her Seviper to unleash Water Pulse; however, as Seviper is unable to learn this move, the command could not be fulfilled. A preview for the unbroadcasted episode AG101 featured a Whiscash employing Water Pulse. In "Analysis Versus Passion!", Sawyer instructed his Clawitzer to utilize Water Pulse, but the attempt was cut short when Ash's Pikachu intervened with an Electro Ball attack. The official website confirmed Wallace's Seaking, named Charles, as knowing Water Pulse, though its use of the move has never been depicted onscreen. Beginning with Generation III, Secret Power adopts the visual animation of Water Pulse when activated on a water-based surface. Notably, Water Pulse stands as the singular Water-type move that possesses the ability to inflict the confusion status condition.

Title: Doom Desire (move)

はめつのねがい (Doom Desire) is the Japanese designation for this damage-dealing Steel-type move. First appearing in Generation III, it serves as Jirachi's unique signature attack. Upon selection, Doom Desire initially yields no immediate effect beyond signaling the user's chosen destiny. The actual damage infliction against the target then materializes two turns subsequently. Damage computation for this delayed strike relies on the user's Attack stat and the target's Defense stat as they exist at the moment of the move's initial selection, rather than when the strike lands. Should the initial target be swapped out, the impending blow will connect with its substitute, but the damage remains pegged to the stats of the Pokémon originally targeted.

Distinctively, the damage dealt by Doom Desire possesses no intrinsic type. This renders it impervious to type effectiveness modifiers, precludes it from receiving a Same-Type Attack Bonus (STAB), and permits it to successfully bypass the protective effects of abilities like Wonder Guard. This attack is incapable of scoring a critical hit and entirely disregards the effects of protective maneuvers such as Protect, Detect, or Endure. However, a Pokémon equipped with a Focus Band retains its ability to survive an otherwise fatal Doom Desire. The move will fail if initiated against a target already marked to be struck by a pending Future Sight or another previously selected Doom Desire. Notably, Doom Desire retains the capacity to strike the Pokémon that originally launched it. Should Doom Desire miss its mark, this outcome isn't processed until the third turn, at which point the game interface will indicate the move "failed" instead of reporting a "miss."

Within Double Battles, if the intended recipient has already fainted by the time Doom Desire is scheduled to hit, the attack will redirect to another available opponent, mirroring the behavior of most other moves. However, if the specific slot targeted by Doom Desire is vacant when the attack is set to land, the move will simply fail without any explicit notification. It boasts a base power of 120 and an accuracy rating of 85.

Subsequent adjustments shifted its damage calculation to utilize the user's Special Attack stat and the target's Special Defense stat instead. Interestingly, this attack does not receive a damage boost from the Life Orb item. Similar to Focus Band, a Pokémon carrying a Focus Sash can also endure a hit from Doom Desire that would otherwise be a knockout. Later iterations saw Doom Desire's base power increased from 120 to 140, concurrently with its accuracy becoming a perfect 100%. Furthermore, its damage calculation methodology was revised to occur at the moment of impact, rather than at the initial selection of the move. This means the user's Special Attack and the struck Pokémon's Special Defense stats are now factored in at the precise instant damage is delivered.

Crucially, Doom Desire now functions as a true Steel-type attack. Consequently, its damage output is subject to type effectiveness, it can benefit from STAB, and it no longer bypasses the protective effects of Wonder Guard automatically. Should the user possess the Normalize ability or be under the influence of Electrify at the moment Doom Desire strikes, the move's type will dynamically become Normal or Electric, respectively, irrespective of the user's status when the move was initially chosen; despite potentially becoming an Electric-type attack under these circumstances, it remains immune to redirection by the Lightning Rod ability. Unlike its prior mechanics, Doom Desire is now capable of landing critical hits. In multi-opponent encounters, Doom Desire gained the ability to target an empty slot. If a Pokémon subsequently occupies that chosen slot by the time the attack is due, the move will proceed as planned. Nevertheless, if the designated slot remains vacant when Doom Desire is scheduled to hit, the attack will still silently fail. Survival against Doom Desire was further expanded; a Pokémon can now withstand it through Endure or the Sturdy ability, alongside the previously mentioned Focus Sash and Focus Band. Conversely, if the attack would otherwise strike the Pokémon that originally used it, Doom Desire now simply vanishes without any notification.

During Versions 1.0 through 1.3.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Doom Desire was notably unavailable for selection in battles. However, from Version 2.0.1 onward, its usability was restored, functioning identically to its mechanics in prior generations.

Within the Mystery Dungeon titles, specifically the Rescue Team, Explorers, and Adventure Squad series, Doom Desire is reimagined as a status-inducing move. It grants the user a "Set Damage" status condition lasting 3-4 turns, during which all of the user's subsequent attacks inflict a fixed 35 or 30 damage, overriding any other potential multipliers. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, it reverted to a damage-dealing move, bestowing a "Doom Desire" status upon the user. This status requires a one-turn charge, with the actual attack being unleashed on the subsequent turn.

In Pokémon Masters EX, no sync pair can directly select Doom Desire. However, Tate (Summer 2023) & Jirachi possess the unique ability "2 P-Moves: Doom Desire," enabling them to trigger it following any other move. Here, it is classified as a Steel-type special attack.

When considering Z-Moves, the Corkscrew Crash derived from Doom Desire shares the distinction with Searing Sunraze Smash of having the highest base power among all Steel-type moves.

Title: Psycho Boost (move)

A Psychic-type attack dealing damage, Psycho Boost (Japanese: サイコブースト) was first introduced in Generation III. This move serves as Deoxys's signature technique. When unleashed, Psycho Boost inflicts damage, but it also causes the user's Special Attack stat to drop by two stages. In Pokémon Contests, Psycho Boost can be employed as the second move of a combination, granting the user an additional 6 appeal points if Calm Mind was performed on the preceding turn.

In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, from Versions 1.0 up to 2.0.2, Psycho Boost could not be selected during battle. Its usability was restored with Version 3.0.0, functioning identically to its operation in prior generations. In Pokémon GO, this move is classified as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since October 1, 2018. Sygna Suit Steven & Deoxys possess the passive skill Downside Up, which reverses the usual effect of Psycho Boost, causing their Sp. Atk to increase by two stat ranks after use, rather than decrease. Additionally, Sygna Suit Steven & Deoxys in their Attack Forme benefit from the Expanded Sync passive skill, allowing their sync move to target all opposing Pokémon.

The descriptive text for Psycho Boost is identical to that of Overheat. This particular move holds the distinction of being the final Generation III move in terms of index number order. Unlike several other similar attacks, Psycho Boost's power was not reduced to 130 in Generation VI. Due to Deoxys being unobtainable in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the only Pokémon that could legitimately learn this move in those games was a purified Lugia, transferred from Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. However, the move remained unselectable in battle, making it unusable throughout Generation VIII until the launch of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In these titles, thanks to moveset alterations facilitated by Pokémon HOME, Deoxys became the sole Pokémon capable of legally knowing Psycho Boost. A Shattered Psyche that draws its power from either Psycho Boost or Prismatic Laser boasts the highest base power among all Psychic-type moves.

Title: Roost (move)

Known in Japanese as はねやすめ (Feather Rest), Roost is a Flying-type maneuver that deals no damage, first appearing in Generation IV. Its technical machine assignment has varied: it was TM51 during Generation IV and within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, then TM19 from Generation VI up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and finally TM50 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Initially, Roost recovered a maximum of 50% of the user's total HP, with the value rounded down. For a Flying-type Pokémon that successfully executes Roost, its Flying type is disregarded when struck by attacks until the current turn concludes. Should the Pokémon possess an additional type, only that remaining type influences damage calculations; if the Pokémon is exclusively Flying-type, every incoming attack will deal normal effectiveness. In subsequent generations, Roost's healing effect was adjusted to restore precisely 50% of the user's maximum HP, with the amount rounded upwards. A successful application of Roost by a Flying-type Pokémon now causes it to shed its Flying type until the end of that turn, with specific outcomes varying: For a dual-type Pokémon where Flying is one of its classifications, utilizing Roost removes its Flying attribute until the turn's conclusion, without substituting it with the Normal type. A pure Flying-type Pokémon that successfully performs Roost will temporarily adopt the Normal type until the turn concludes. Should a Fire/Flying-type Pokémon have already lost its Fire type because of Burn Up, subsequently employing Roost renders it typeless for the remainder of that turn. For a pure Flying-type Pokémon under the effects of Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat, executing Roost results in its Flying type being substituted by Normal, while still preserving the added Grass or Ghost typing. If a Pokémon is airborne solely due to its Flying type, successfully performing Roost will cause it to become grounded until the turn concludes, thereby rendering it vulnerable to effects like terrain and Rototiller. When boosted by a Flyinium Z crystal to become Z-Roost, this move causes all of the user's decreased stats to be restored to their original levels. The precise amount of HP Roost recovers, expressed as a percentage of the user's maximum HP, has not been constant across all game iterations. Generally, if the user possesses a partial Flying type, that specific typing is temporarily nullified for the remainder of the turn. The move Roost is subject to the effects of Snatch. Usage of this move is not possible on air tiles. In terms of index number ordering, Roost holds the distinction of being the inaugural Generation IV move.

Title: Gravity (move)

The Psychic-type status move, Gravity (Japanese: じゅうりょく), debuted in Generation IV and is designated as TM178 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move inflicts intense gravity upon the battlefield, an effect that persists for five turns. Subsequent attempts to use Gravity while its influence remains active will fail. While under intense gravity, the precision of all moves, excluding one-hit knockout attacks, is significantly boosted: by a factor of 5/3 in Generation IV, and by 6840/4096 (approximately 1.67) from Generation V onwards.

This powerful gravitational pull forces all Pokémon present on the field to become grounded. Consequently, grounded Pokémon forfeit their immunity to Ground-type attacks, irrespective of whether that immunity originated from their Flying type, the Levitate Ability, the effects of Magnet Rise or Telekinesis, or a held Air Balloon. These grounded combatants also become susceptible to the Arena Trap Ability and various entry hazards; notably, airborne Poison-type Pokémon will clear Toxic Spikes upon entering the battlefield while Gravity is active. Furthermore, terrains now impact grounded Pokémon.

Intense gravity also prohibits Pokémon from executing certain moves: Bounce, Fly, Flying Press, High Jump Kick, Jump Kick, Magnet Rise, Sky Drop, Splash, and Telekinesis. Should a Pokémon be midway through the semi-invulnerable turn of Fly or Bounce when Gravity is activated, that specific move is instantly nullified. A Pokémon left with no viable options other than moves restricted by Gravity will resort to using Struggle. Grav Apple benefits from the increased gravitational force, having its power amplified by 50% when used under these conditions.

A unique glitch in Generation V allowed for a specific interaction: if Gravity was employed while two Pokémon were caught in Sky Drop's semi-invulnerable phase, the Sky Drop user would descend, but its target would remain immobilized in the semi-invulnerable state. This paralysis would persist until Gravity's effect concluded, the Sky Drop user was swapped out or fainted, or the affected Pokémon itself fainted. This particular bug exclusively manifested in Double and Triple Battles. In subsequent generations, this issue was resolved, and both the Sky Drop user and its target are correctly brought down.

When transformed into Z-Gravity by a Psychium Z, the move grants the user a one-stage boost to its Special Attack.

In Mystery Dungeon titles, Gravity heightens the dungeon's gravitational pull, causing Flying-type Pokémon and those with the Levitate Ability to become grounded and thereby lose their immunity to Ground-type attacks. This condition also prohibits the usage of Bounce, Fly, Splash, Jump Kick, High Jump Kick, and Magnet Rise; it forces Pokémon out of the semi-invulnerable states of Fly and Bounce, and prematurely terminates Magnet Rise's effect. Commencing with Super Mystery Dungeon, this floor condition additionally boosts the accuracy of moves, mirroring the core series games, and further disables Telekinesis, Sky Drop, and Flying Press, alongside the previously mentioned moves. Moreover, Pokémon possessing flying mobility capabilities will lose them. It's worth noting that while Orbeetle's G-Max Move, G-Max Gravitas, imitates the effects of Gravity, Orbeetle itself cannot learn the move Gravity.

Title: Miracle Eye (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, Miracle Eye (Japanese: ミラクルアイ) is a non-damaging Psychic-type attack. When active, it causes subsequent accuracy checks against the target to disregard any positive alterations to its evasion stat stages, provided its evasion stat stage is greater than 0. If Miracle Eye's target is a Dark-type Pokémon, the move also removes that Pokémon's inherent immunity to Psychic-type moves. The influence of Miracle Eye ceases when the affected Pokémon switches out. This move inherently bypasses accuracy checks to always connect with its intended target, unless the target is within the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. Miracle Eye will now fail if attempted on a Pokémon already experiencing its effects. In Contest Spectaculars, Miracle Eye can be used as part of a combination, causing a one-hit knockout move to grant an additional three appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Psychium Z into Z-Miracle Eye, the user's Special Attack stat receives a single-stage boost. It focuses on the directly opposing Pokémon, effectively resetting any positive evasion stat stage modifications to 0. Should the target be a Dark-type, it additionally inflicts the Miracle Eye status condition upon them, thereby eliminating their immunity to Psychic-type moves. This specific status effect persists throughout the current floor's duration. In Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, the move's description erroneously states that using Miracle Eye allows the user to hit a Ghost type with any type of move, most likely due to being copied from Foresight and Odor Sleuth, which actually possess this effect. Miracle Eye may reference nazar charms, with their eye-pattern motifs, or the Hamsa, both used to ward off the "evil eye." The move neutralizes the Dark-type's (known as the "Evil-type" in Japanese) immunity to Psychic-type moves, potentially alluding to spiritual talismans used to counteract the type's evil associations. Miracle Eye is the exclusive move capable of removing the Dark-type's immunity to Psychic-type moves.

Title: Gyro Ball (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Gyro Ball (Japanese: ジャイロボール) is a Steel-type offensive technique. It was designated as TM74 starting from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

This attack deals greater damage the slower the user is in relation to its adversary. Consequently, a more substantial difference between the user's and target's Speed stats translates into increased power. The base power is determined by the following calculation: BasePower=min(150,25×CurrentSpeedtargetCurrentSpeeduser+1). All factors influencing Speed are incorporated into this computation, including stat stages, paralysis, held items like the Iron Ball, and Abilities such as Slush Rush. However, effects that solely modify turn order without altering the underlying Speed value, such as Trick Room and Stall, do not impact Gyro Ball's effectiveness. If the user's Speed stat rounds down to 0, it will be considered as 1 within the power formula to prevent division by zero.

The highest possible base power for Gyro Ball is 150, achieved if the target's Speed is at least 5.96 times that of the user. Should the user's Speed stat round down to 0, the move's power is instead fixed at 1, completely bypassing the aforementioned formula.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Gyro Ball's mechanics have varied. In the Explorers titles, the final damage is doubled if the user is under the Half Speed status condition. For Gates to Infinity, the move inflicts 50% more damage if the user's Travel Speed is less than the target's, though the move's in-game description misleadingly suggests a progressive increase in intensity. From Super Mystery Dungeon onward, damage calculation exclusively utilizes the standard Speed stat.

In Pokémon GO, Gyro Ball is a Charged Attack that became available on February 16, 2017. Its descriptions underwent revisions: prior to Version 2.10.0 (issued June 28, 2021), it read: "Its power is doubled if the target's Speed has risen." Later, before Version 2.20.0 (from April 27, 2022), the text stated: "This move's power is doubled when the target's Speed is raised."

The move's name is likely a homage to the Gyroball, an uncommon baseball pitch developed in Japan known for its distinctive spinning motion that minimizes horizontal and vertical break. Its Steel typing probably alludes to the gyroscope, a metallic apparatus that uses the rotation of its internal rings to maintain stability in space.

In Pokémon Conquest, Gyro Ball invariably deals 1 damage point if the user is faster than the target. This occurs due to the game's damage calculation method: the target's Speed stat is divided by the user's, and the resulting quotient is rounded down to generate a damage multiplier. A user that is quicker than the target will yield a multiplier of 0, initially causing the move to deal 0 damage, which is then adjusted to a minimum of 1.

Title: Healing Wish (move)

The technique known as Healing Wish (Japanese: いやしのねがい Healing Wish) debuted as a non-damaging Psychic-type move in Generation IV. This move compels the user to faint, subsequently sending a new Pokémon onto the battlefield immediately. The incoming Pokémon receives a complete restoration of its HP and is simultaneously cured of any status ailments upon its deployment. Healing Wish is rendered ineffective if the Trainer possesses no other conscious Pokémon in their party, or if a wild Pokémon attempts its use. Currently, the beneficiary Pokémon is dispatched at the conclusion of the turn rather than being instantly sent out. Should Healing Wish be powered up with a Psychium Z to become Z-Healing Wish, no additional effects are gained. If the Pokémon intended to replace the user already has full health and is free of status conditions, the effect of Healing Wish will not become active until a Pokémon is placed into that specific slot (either by switching in or through the use of Ally Switch). A Healing Wish that is pending will not be triggered if a Pokémon already in that position loses HP or gains a status condition; its activation is exclusively tied to a *new* Pokémon entering that position. Each position can only hold a single pending instance of Healing Wish, meaning multiple uses do not accumulate. However, Healing Wish can stack its effects with Lunar Dance. In the context of Max Raid Battles or Tera Raid Battles, Healing Wish fails entirely, and the user does not faint. Within an alternative gameplay scenario, Healing Wish targets allied Pokémon present in the same area, fully restoring their HP and removing all negative status conditions. Following this, the user's own HP is diminished to 1.

Title: Brine (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Brine (Japanese: しおみず Brine) is a damage-dealing Water-type technique. This particular move holds the TM55 designation in Generation IV and Generation VIII, a status it also maintains within the games Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Brine typically inflicts damage, but its power doubles to 130 when the target's health has dropped to 50% or less. It subsequently delivers double damage if the target's HP is at half or below. An earlier description of the move, prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), stated: "The power is doubled if the target's HP is at half or below."
