Following this, the textual account found before Version 2.20.0 (effective April 27, 2022) read: "This move's power is doubled when the target's remaining HP is at half or below." Brine's characteristic of inflicting increased harm on weakened opponents might be conceptually linked to the idiom "rubbing salt into the wound," which signifies making an already undesirable situation worse. Notably, Brine stands as the sole Water-type attack introduced in Generation IV that does not feature the word "aqua" in its name.

Title: Natural Gift (move)

Known in Japanese as しぜんのめぐみ (Nature's Blessing), Natural Gift is a Normal-type attack first appearing in Generation IV. During Generation IV, it occupied TM slot 83. This move deals direct damage and lacks any additional effects. Its offensive power and elemental typing are determined by the specific Berry held by the Pokémon utilizing it. Initiating Natural Gift causes the held Berry to be consumed, even should the attack miss its target due to an accuracy check. This move will not execute if the user lacks a held Berry, or if circumstances prevent it from using its held Berry (such as the Abilities Klutz, Magic Room's effect, or the status condition Embargo). Effects like Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, and Aerilate do not influence Natural Gift. Conversely, it is susceptible to Ion Deluge (provided the move's determined type would be Normal) and also Electrify. Should a Red Card compel the user to exit the battlefield, the Berry remains unconsumed. Similarly, if Powder prevents the move's execution, the held Berry will not be used up. Furthermore, if Natural Gift's full execution is hindered by heavy rain (when attempting Fire-type damage) or extremely harsh sunlight (when attempting Water-type damage), the Berry is likewise preserved. The consumption of a Berry through Natural Gift does not trigger interactions with Abilities such as Unnerve, Cheek Pouch, or Belch.

The attack's elemental type and base power are determined in the following manner: This move inflicts damage. In the context of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, the move's type and power shift when a Seed or Berry is held; however, diverging from the core series titles, this move is usable even without an item, and the held item is not expended. Within Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, a held food item will both boost the move's power and alter its type. Peculiarly, the Oran Berry transforms the move into the Fairy type, a behavior distinct from prior Mystery Dungeon installments and the core series.

Regarding the Cram-o-matic, each Berry contributes a type identical to the type Natural Gift adopts when used with that specific Berry, and its point value corresponds to Natural Gift's base power in Generation VII:
2 Cram-o-matic points: 80 power, with the exception of Berries that mitigate damage or have the potential to induce confusion.
4 Cram-o-matic points: 80 power, exclusively for Berries that reduce incoming damage or are capable of causing confusion.

Title: Feint (move)

For the alternate feinting maneuver, refer to Feint Attack (move). Known as フェイント in Japanese, Feint is a Normal-type damage-dealing attack first introduced in Generation IV. This move originally dealt damage only to a target that had already used Protect or Detect during the current turn, simultaneously nullifying the effects of Protect or Detect for the remainder of that turn. Feint could not remove these protective effects if it missed or failed to impact the target, such as against a Ghost-type Pokémon. Possessing an elevated priority of +2, Feint is executed after Protect or Detect but before other moves that also have increased priority.

Subsequently, Feint gained the ability to inflict damage even if the target had not employed Protect or Detect, and its base power was adjusted from 50 down to 30. Despite its increased priority, Feint would initially only remove the effects of Wide Guard and Quick Guard for the duration of the turn if these moves were utilized by an opposing team. Feint was not blocked by an opponent's Quick Guard, but it *was* blocked by an ally's Quick Guard if targeting an ally. In a later update, Feint now removes the effects of Quick Guard and Wide Guard even when targeting allies, and it is no longer hindered by an ally's Quick Guard.

Feint is capable of striking a target even when protected by Mat Block, Spiky Shield, King's Shield, Baneful Bunker, Obstruct, Silk Trap, or Burning Bulwark, and it dispels these specific protections for the remainder of the turn. Moreover, the Pokémon using Feint does not suffer any negative secondary effects associated with these protective moves. While it can hit a target protected by Max Guard, Feint does not remove this particular protection. Feint also causes both Crafty Shield and Mat Block to be removed from the target's entire team. Ultimately, Feint inflicts damage by piercing the Protect status condition and prematurely ending its duration.

In Pokémon UNITE, Feint is assigned as Absol's first move and Cinderace's second move. Absol begins the match with this move, executing an arcing dash that damages any Pokémon it hits. This version of the move calculates damage as if all struck Pokémon have 0 Defense, and it entirely bypasses shields. At level 5, Absol's Feint is replaced by either Night Slash or Pursuit. Cinderace acquires Feint by reaching level 8 and upgrading Low Sweep into it, as an alternative to Flame Charge. Cinderace's Feint causes the user to dodge, boosting their movement speed and granting brief invincibility. Upon reaching level 13, the subsequent three basic attacks after Cinderace's dodge will restore its HP.

Title: Pluck (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Pluck (Japanese: ついばむ Pick) is a Flying-type attack that deals damage. It served as TM88 throughout Generation IV and Generation V, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Beyond simply inflicting damage, Pluck possesses a secondary effect: if the opposing Pokémon holds a Berry, the user consumes it and gains its benefits. However, Pluck will not eat a target's Berry if it strikes a substitute, nor will it remove a Berry from a Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability. Berries like Liechi or Sitrus Berries are consumed by the attacker via Pluck, regardless of whether their typical damage-based activation requirements upon taking damage have been met. In Triple Battles, Pluck can reach non-adjacent opponents. Initially, if the user possessed the Klutz Ability or was affected by Embargo, it would consume the target's Berry without acquiring its effect. Pluck would also preemptively eat Coba and Jaboca Berries from the target, preventing their activation. Subsequently, the user now receives the effects of the target's held Berry even if burdened by Klutz or Embargo. Similarly, Coba or Jaboca Berries will now activate their effects before being eaten. This move also delivers damage and can consume various other edible items held by the target, including a Seed, Apple, Max Elixir, or Gummi. Should Pluck be directed at a Pokémon on the player's own team, the opposing combatant will instead select an item from the player's Bag. This move's operation is also affected by the Muzzled status condition.

Title: Tailwind (move)

Known as おいかぜ (Tailwind) in Japanese, this Flying-type technique, which causes no direct damage, made its debut in Generation IV. In the games Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, trainers can acquire it as Technical Machine 113. Upon activation, Tailwind causes the Speed stat of the Pokémon that used it, along with all other allies in that trainer's party, to become twice its usual value for a duration of three turns. When enhanced by a Flyinium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Tailwind, the Pokémon executing the move experiences a two-stage boost to its critical-hit ratio. Additionally, it grants a one-stage increment to the Travel Speed of both the user and its teammates who are present in the same room. The effects of this technique can be intercepted by Snatch. Notably, Togekiss has been observed utilizing Tailwind to augment its own speed and simultaneously regain a portion of its HP. Within the game Pokémon Rumble Rush, all non-damaging moves, Tailwind among them, were exclusively wielded by enemy Pokémon and were unavailable for acquisition by any Pokémon captured by the player. Furthermore, Boss Tornadus has been identified employing Tailwind.

Title: Acupressure (move)

The move Acupressure, known in Japanese as つぼをつく (Pressure Point Poke), debuted in Generation IV as a Normal-type technique that inflicts no damage. It randomly selects one of the designated target's stats and boosts it by two stages. The possible stats for enhancement include Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, Speed, accuracy, or evasion. However, Acupressure will not attempt to elevate a stat that has already reached its maximum level; consequently, if all applicable stats are already maximized, the move will fail. Additionally, an active substitute on the target will cause Acupressure to fail.

Exclusively in Generation IV, Acupressure was susceptible to being intercepted by Snatch. Should Snatch successfully appropriate it, the effect of Acupressure would then apply to the Snatch user, irrespective of whom it was originally intended for. In subsequent generations, if the user is both the target and protected by a substitute, Acupressure will not fail. Nevertheless, it will still be ineffective if an ally is the target and protected by a Substitute. Furthermore, Acupressure can no longer be seized by Snatch.

When Acupressure is selected but the Pokémon's position changes before execution (e.g., via a Triple Battle shift or Ally Switch), the move will instead affect the Pokémon presently occupying the designated target slot, even if the user initially intended to target itself. Conversely, if the user now occupies the target slot but was not the initial target, Acupressure will fail. Should Crafty Shield be active on the user's side of the field, Acupressure will fail if directed at an ally, but it will successfully execute if used on the user.

When enhanced by a Normalium Z into Z-Acupressure, the user gains an additional two stages to its critical hit ratio. Within the context of Mystery Dungeon, Acupressure confers a random two-stage boost to either Attack, Defense, Special Attack, or Special Defense for the user and every teammate present in the same room, with a distinct random selection for each individual. It will not elevate a stat that has already reached the maximum of +10, and should all four of these stats be maximized for a given Pokémon, the move will fail for that particular Pokémon. Acupressure boasts a unique targeting range, affecting only the user or an adjacent ally; no other known move shares this distinctive range.

Title: Metal Burst (move)

Known in Japanese as メタルバースト (Metal Burst), this Steel-type offensive technique first appeared in Generation IV. Metal Burst retaliates by inflicting 1.5 times the damage sustained from the opponent's most recent assault. It specifically accounts for only the *last* damage taken; if the user endures a multi-hit attack, solely the damage from the final strike contributes. Distinct from Counter or Mirror Coat, Metal Burst is not restricted to either physical or special damage types, nor does it possess reduced priority. Should the user execute the move prior to being struck by a foe's damaging attack, Metal Burst will unfortunately be unsuccessful. Furthermore, the move fails if a substitute takes the hit instead of the user itself.

Within multi-Pokémon engagements, Metal Burst targets the specific adversary that most recently inflicted damage upon the user, irrespective of its proximity. Nevertheless, should the Pokémon responsible for the last damaging blow be knocked out before the user's turn commences, the attack will instead strike one of the remaining hostile Pokémon, mirroring the behavior of a standard single-target damaging move. In such a scenario, the damage calculation still utilizes the amount absorbed from the fainted Pokémon's prior attack. This maneuver is incapable of harming allied Pokémon.

This move also finds utility within Contest Spectacular combinations, where its performance awards the user an additional three appeal points if Encore, Taunt, or Torment was successfully employed during the preceding turn. In the Mystery Dungeon series, before Gates to Infinity, Metal Burst imposed the Metal Burst status condition upon its user for 8-11 turns, causing half of any damage from adjacent attacks to be mirrored back to adjacent foes. Subsequently, from Gates to Infinity and later titles, the user now reflects 3/4 of the incoming move's damage directly back at the attacker, with this enhanced status effect persisting for 6 turns.

Before Version 2.20.0 (released April 27, 2022), the move's in-game description stated: "The more damage the user has taken, the greater the power of this move. This move's power scales based on how much HP the user loses while in their counterattacking posture. The more HP lost, the higher this move's power multiplier. The power multiplier resets when the user leaves this posture." This description was historically identical to that of Comeuppance. Zamazenta, specifically in its Hero of Many Battles form, stands as the sole Pokémon capable of learning Metal Burst without being classified as Steel-type or having a connection to geological materials like rocks (e.g., Rhyhorn) or gemstones (e.g., Sableye). It is noteworthy that Zamazenta's Crowned Shield form is indeed Steel-type, yet it is not a prerequisite for the Legendary Pokémon to utilize Metal Burst.

Title: Close Combat (move)

Known in Japanese as インファイト (Infight), Close Combat is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move that made its debut in Generation IV. After inflicting damage, this attack then lowers the user's Defense and Special Defense stats by a single stage each. Its base power was reduced to 100, and it causes the user's defensive stats to drop for three turns. Close Combat aggressively tracks targets, setting it apart from most other close-range moves. Should an adversary move outside of Close Combat's attack range once the wind-up phase begins, the user will "teleport" into range to ensure damage is dealt, with the exception of Ghost-type targets. The move inflicts damage and reduces the user's Defense and Special Defense by one stage each.

In Pokémon GO, Close Combat functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since February 16, 2017. A description valid prior to Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) indicated: "Lowers the user's Defense and Sp. Def." Another description from before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021) stated: "Become Mega Gallade until the end of battle. Raises the user’s critical-hit rate." This sync move was not able to be raised to 6★ EX until October 22, 2020, which occurred 161 days after its initial release date on May 14, 2020. The 6★-EX effect's description, before Version 2.18.5 (from March 15, 2022), read: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Close Combat is recognized as Machamp's first move and Lucario's second move. Machamp can acquire this move at level 7 as Machoke by upgrading Karate Chop into it instead of Cross Chop. The user unleashes a series of blows in a chosen direction, striking multiple times and concluding with a powerful singular strike. For the move's duration, the user is unstoppable and retains the ability to move while performing Close Combat. At level 13, the attack deals supplementary damage if the target is afflicted by a status condition. Lucario gains Close Combat at level 7 by upgrading Meteor Mash into it, offering an alternative to Bone Rush. The user rapidly punches, damaging enemies in the frontal area 11 times over the course of the move. When Lucario reaches level 13, the move's damage is amplified. In the story "Thieving and Boss Crabominable," Hala instructed his Hariyama to utilize Close Combat, though its execution took place off-panel.

Title: Payback (move)

Known in Japanese as しっぺがえし (Retribution), Payback is a Dark-type offensive technique first appearing in Generation IV. This attack held the technical machine designation TM66 across its presence from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, as well as in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl; however, it was assigned as TM57 within Pokémon Sword and Shield. The move naturally delivers damage. Its power amplifies to 100, double its normal strength, should its wielder strike after the adversary, if the opposing Pokémon withdraws from battle, or if the foe utilizes an item. A subsequent modification dictates that Payback's strength no longer increases when the target voluntarily swaps places.

Utilizing Payback bestows the Counter status effect upon the attacker. While under this condition, the Pokémon will retaliate against any adjacent adversaries by mirroring any damage sustained from physical attacks (including standard 'A button' attacks), matching the exact amount of harm received. Intriguingly, within the titles Explorers of Time and Darkness, despite an in-game distinction between Physical and Special moves, Payback's Counter status reflects damage from offensive abilities belonging to types that were historically categorized as physical before the broader Physical/Special division. This Counter condition remains active for 7 to 12 turns, or alternatively, 6 turns. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Payback functions as a Charged Attack and has been implementable within the game since January 16, 2021.

Title: Assurance (move)

First appearing in Generation IV, Assurance (known in Japanese as ダメおし, or Make Sure) is a Dark-type attack that inflicts damage. Trainers could teach this technique using TM58 during their journeys through Pokémon Sword and Shield. This move's damage output doubles if the recipient has already sustained any form of harm earlier in the same turn it's employed. This condition encompasses both direct and indirect sources, including self-inflicted harm like Life Orb damage, recoil, crash damage, or even the target hurting itself due to confusion. Additionally, Assurance delivers twice its normal damage when striking a foe whose HP is at 50% or less of its maximum. Every Pokémon situated directly in front of the attacker will be affected by this move. Furthermore, Assurance inflicts increased damage when it connects with an opponent that has been blown back.

Within the manga series Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~ Going Ashore and Neighboring Akala Island, the character Sun noted that his Alolan Meowth, Cent, possessed knowledge of Assurance; however, the ongoing battle concluded prematurely, preventing Sun from issuing the command to execute the move. The Japanese designation for Assurance, ダメおし (Dameoshi, meaning 'Make Sure'), when rendered verbatim, translates to 'useless push.' This interpretation arises from 'dameoshi' being a compound of ダメ (dame), signifying 'no good,' 'useless,' or 'hopeless,' and おし (oshi), denoting 'pushing.' Such a literal meaning potentially accounts for its in-game animations, which frequently depict hands applying force to the adversary. In the context of sumo wrestling, 'dameoshi' describes the action of continuing to push an opponent even after they have already been vanquished. This particular practice is generally frowned upon, as it carries the risk of inflicting severe harm upon an unprepared combatant once the outcome of the contest has become definitive.

Title: Embargo (move)

Embargo (Japanese: さしおさえ, rendered as Seize) is a Dark-type status move, initially featured in Generation IV, that deals no direct damage. This technique was designated as TM63 from its introduction in Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. For five consecutive turns, the afflicted Pokémon's held item becomes functionally inert, and its Trainer is barred from applying any items from their Bag to it. This effect is immediately lifted if the Pokémon is withdrawn from battle.

The move Embargo does not nullify item benefits related to prize money, experience accumulation, effort value gains, or friendship levels; examples include the Amulet Coin, Lucky Egg, Power Weight, or Soothe Bell. Historically, the Speed penalty imposed by items such as the Iron Ball, Macho Brace, and various Power items (for instance, the Power Bracer) remained unaffected by Embargo. Conversely, the Iron Ball's grounding property *was* negated. A Pokémon operating under Embargo's influence was also unable to execute the moves Fling or Natural Gift. If the intended recipient of Fling was affected by Embargo, items propelled at it would not trigger their effects. Furthermore, if a Pokémon subjected to Embargo used Bug Bite or Pluck, it would consume the target's held Berry without acquiring its benefits. Should a Pokémon under Embargo perform Judgment, the attack would register as a Normal-type move. Embargo would also fail if directed at a Pokémon with the Multitype ability or one equipped with a Griseous Orb.

In more recent changes, Embargo now *does* neutralize the Speed reductions from the Iron Ball, Macho Brace, and Power items (including the Power Bracer). When the target of Fling is affected by Embargo, thrown items will now activate as they ordinarily would. If a Pokémon under Embargo employs Bug Bite or Pluck, it will now properly receive the effects of any consumed held Berry. Additionally, if a Pokémon suffering from Embargo utilizes Techno Blast or Multi-Attack, these moves will become Normal-type. Embargo can now be successfully applied to Pokémon with Multitype, including Origin Forme Giratina. However, Embargo is still incapable of overriding items' form-changing properties. It will not hinder a Pokémon from undergoing Mega Evolution with a Mega Stone, initiating Primal Reversion through a Red Orb or Blue Orb, or performing a Z-Move with a Z-Crystal. When transformed into Z-Embargo by a Darkinium Z, the user's Special Attack stat is boosted by one stage.

Embargo establishes the "Embargo" status condition, which prohibits the target from utilizing any items, encompassing both throwing and eating them. In the Explorers series of Mystery Dungeon games, however, this status does not prevent the passive effects of held items; for example, a Mobile Scarf will still allow movement across all terrain, and Seeds will still influence Natural Gift. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the affected user is additionally prevented from utilizing held items such as Looplets. In Pokémon Battle Revolution, a visual animation marks the conclusion of Embargo's effect.

Title: Fling (move)

Fling (Japanese: なげつける Fling) is an offensive Dark-type move that debuted in Generation IV. It was designated as TM56 from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Fling causes damage, with its strength determined by the item the user is currently holding. The move will fail if the Pokémon has no held item, or if the item is a TM, an Apricorn, a Gem, any type of Poké Ball, Mail, a Z-Crystal, an Ability Capsule, an Ability Patch, a Festival Ticket, the Red Orb, the Blue Orb, the Rusted Sword, or the Rusted Shield. Fling is also unsuccessful if executed by Giratina when holding the Griseous Orb, Arceus with a plate, Genesect with a drive, Silvally holding a memory, a Pokémon clutching a Mega Stone enabling Mega Evolution, a Pokémon possessing the Ability Protosynthesis or Quark Drive while holding Booster Energy, or Ogerpon holding the Cornerstone Mask, the Wellspring Mask, or the Hearthflame Mask.

Once Fling is used, the item is expended, even if the target successfully employed a protection move. The precise power of Fling, which varies based on the held item, is detailed in a subsequent table. If the user possesses a TR, Fling's power will correspond to the power of the move contained within that TR, unless it's a status move or a variable power move without a default, in which case it imparts 10 power. Consequently, TR71 (Leaf Storm) and TR43 (Overheat) achieve the maximum Fling power of 130, matching items like the Iron Ball and Big Nugget. Should the user's held item typically enhance move power (such as Black Glasses or Life Orb), Fling's power will be boosted accordingly. Since the user no longer holds the Life Orb after the move, it notably does not incur the usual HP reduction.

When a Pokémon uses Fling while holding a Berry, that Berry's effect will activate for the target, irrespective of its typical trigger conditions. Certain other items and game states also interact specially with Fling: Embargo, Klutz (from Generation V onward), and Magic Room all prevent Fling's execution. Exclusively in Generation IV, if Fling targets a Pokémon under the influence of Embargo, and the user's item normally has a special effect, that effect will not manifest. If a Pokémon with Cud Chew is struck by Fling with a Berry, the Berry will reactivate at the conclusion of the subsequent turn. Fling can also contribute to Contest Spectacular combinations, awarding an additional three appeal points if Celebrate, Covet, Happy Hour, or Wish was performed in the preceding turn.

Within the Explorers series of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon, Fling functions as a status move that propels the enemy in front across the room. If another enemy Pokémon is within range, the target will collide with it, causing 10 HP damage to both Pokémon. Despite its classification as a status move, it is only susceptible to Taunt in Explorers of Sky. From Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity onward, using Fling brings up the inventory, allowing the leader to select an item to throw (excluding currently held items). It is effective only when an enemy is within a fan-shaped zone up to 10 tiles ahead; in hallways, this range is reduced to 2 tiles, expanding to 5 from Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon. Akin to Gravelerocks, the thrown item travels in an arc, but the move cannot hit Pokémon through walls, even when using rock items. Fling also bypasses allies and remains unaffected by the Power-Pitching and Slip-Through Team Skills. Other Pokémon in these games will automatically fling their held item when using this move. The move is not influenced by the Prankster Ability. In Gates to Infinity, Fling has the unique property of waking up sleeping-spawned Pokémon even if the move's trajectory is obstructed.

Fling stands as one of only two moves guaranteeing a 100% chance of causing flinching when holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, the other being Fake Out. Furthermore, Fling is the sole Dark-type move capable of inducing paralysis (with a Light Ball), a burn (with a Flame Orb), or poisoning (with a Poison Barb or Toxic Orb). In the animated series, Fling can additionally be employed to hurl the remnants of attacks, as demonstrated when Bianca's Emboar flung the fragments of Trip's Conkeldurr's Stone Edge back at it. It has also been depicted as capable of throwing the opponent directly.

Title: Psycho Shift (move)

In Generation IV, the Psychic-type, non-damaging technique known as Psycho Shift (サイコシフト in Japanese) made its debut. This move removes any persistent status ailment from the Pokémon employing it and subsequently inflicts that same condition upon the opponent. However, temporary afflictions like confusion are not transferred. The move will not succeed if the foe is immune to the status effect, whether due to its type, an active Ability, the Safeguard effect, or if it already possesses a status condition. When a Pokémon suffering from bad poison utilizes Psycho Shift, its adversary will also become badly poisoned. Should it be activated through Sleep Talk, Psycho Shift is capable of passing on the sleep status. Since a frozen Pokémon cannot execute Psycho Shift, transferring the frozen status is impossible. Originally, Psycho Shift would first cure the user and then apply the status condition to the opponent. As a result, if targeting a Synchronize-holding Pokémon, Synchronize would activate, re-afflicting the Psycho Shift user (if vulnerable) with the same status. Currently, the move first afflicts the target with the status condition before curing the user. Therefore, when facing a Synchronize user, the Psycho Shift user will already possess the status condition by the time Synchronize triggers. When augmented by a Psychium Z to become Z-Psycho Shift, this move bestows a two-stage increase to the user's Special Attack. It works by shifting the user's detrimental status conditions onto the immediate adversary. Within Super Mystery Dungeon, Psycho Shift additionally remedies sealed moves from the user, though it does not pass these onto the target. Notably, Psycho Shift stands alone as the sole Psychic-type technique able to inflict opponents with burn, paralysis, or poison. A majority of the Pokémon capable of mastering this move are either inspired by or linked to avian species.

Title: Trump Card (move)

This entry pertains to the move Trump Card; for the TCG Supporter card, refer to Lysandre's Trump Card (Phantom Forces 99). Introduced in Generation IV, Trump Card (Japanese: きりふだ Trump Card) is a Normal-type offensive maneuver that deals damage. This attack reliably connects, bypassing standard accuracy checks, and deals damage to the foe. Its success is only thwarted if the adversary is currently engaged in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Trump Card's effectiveness is directly linked to the amount of PP remaining after its use, with its power intensifying as its PP count decreases.

The calculation of its power incorporates PP lost due to effects such as Spite and Pressure. Conversely, it remains unaffected by Eerie Spell and G-Max Depletion, a distinction attributed to their unavailability within the same game titles. Upon execution, the move consistently factors in its immediate PP count, irrespective of whether PP has been replenished by effects like Lunar Dance or held items such as a Leppa Berry. If invoked by a different move, for example, Metronome or Assist, its power determination will instead refer to the PP of the *calling* move, applying the identical scaling chart.

This move delivers damage, its intensity directly correlating with its current PP count. Within the Explorers series, Trump Card's damage output receives a multiplier: 1.5× at 70% PP remaining, 2× at 50% PP remaining, and a potent 2.5× when only 20% PP is left. Conversely, in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's effectiveness sees an approximate increase of 30%, 60%, 90%, and 120% when it possesses 4, 3, 2, or 1 PP remaining, respectively. Visually, the majority of Trump Card's animations portray it as a slicing attack against the adversary. This graphical representation likely stems from its Japanese name, which, when read with its kanji, incorporates 切り (kiri), translating to "cut."

Title: Heal Block (move)

Known in Japanese as かいふくふうじ (Recovery Block), Heal Block is a Psychic-type status move that first appeared in Generation IV, inflicting no damage. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, its Technical Machine designation is TM097. For five turns, this move restricts affected targets from recovering HP through specific methods. Pokémon under Heal Block's influence are prevented from utilizing immediate self-healing status moves, the HP-restoring aspect of HP-draining moves, Wish, Lunar Dance, Healing Wish, Heal Pulse, or Floral Healing. They also cannot target an ally with Pollen Puff.

Furthermore, these Pokémon cannot be the recipient of Heal Pulse, Floral Healing, or an ally's Pollen Puff. Healing from Wish is also blocked, even if the Wish effect was successfully applied before Heal Block's activation. Moves like Present, Ingrain, Aqua Ring, and Grassy Terrain remain usable, yet their healing properties will not restore HP to the affected Pokémon. Should a Pokémon have already chosen a forbidden move before falling under Heal Block's influence, its use will be prevented, and no Power Points (PP) will be consumed. If Heal Block renders a Pokémon incapable of selecting any other available move (for instance, having no other moves with PP, or holding a Choice Band), it will resort to using Struggle. Moves that call other moves, like Metronome, can still call restricted moves, but the called move's execution will be prevented.

Heal Block does not stop a Pokémon from using Leech Seed, thereby still causing the target to become seeded. It's noteworthy that Sappy Seed and Heal Block do not interact, as they are not present in the same core series games. The seeded Pokémon continues to take damage each turn normally, even if the Pokémon that used Leech Seed is under Heal Block's influence. While the Leech Seed user (or its replacement in the same position) is affected by Heal Block, no HP will be restored to them, though they will still suffer damage if the seeded Pokémon possesses the Liquid Ooze ability.

Heal Block's effect is transferable via Baton Pass. All Abilities and items designed to restore HP during battle generally continue to function as normal. Uniquely in this specific generation, Magic Bounce is unable to reflect Heal Block. When an affected Pokémon uses an HP-draining move, it will still inflict damage, yet no HP will be restored to the user. Conversely, if the target possesses Liquid Ooze, the user will still incur damage.

Affected Pokémon can no longer be healed by Black Sludge, Leftovers, or Shell Bell. Held HP-restoring Berries or Berry Juice will not be consumed by affected Pokémon. Even if an HP-restoring Berry is consumed through moves like Fling, Bug Bite, or Pluck, an affected Pokémon's HP will not be restored. For Pokémon with the abilities Dry Skin or Water Absorb, Water-type moves will inflict no damage, but also provide no healing. Similarly, Pokémon possessing Volt Absorb will avoid damage from Electric-type moves, yet gain no HP restoration from them. During rainy weather, Pokémon with Dry Skin or Rain Dish abilities are prevented from healing. Pokémon having the Ice Body ability cannot heal during hail, although they also do not take damage from it. Poisoned Pokémon with the Poison Heal ability will neither be healed nor damaged. After consuming a Berry, Pokémon with the Cheek Pouch ability will not receive healing. The Regenerator ability's HP restoration upon switching out is unaffected by Heal Block. The healing component of HP-draining moves remains unusable by affected Pokémon.

HP restoration from the Z-Power effects of status Z-Moves remains accessible to affected Pokémon. Heal Block is ineffective at stopping a Pokémon from using any Z-Move or from receiving its healing, encompassing moves such as Z-Soft-Boiled and Z-Heal Pulse. When powered up by a Psychium Z to become Z-Heal Block, the user's Special Attack stat receives a two-stage increase.

Heal Block targets all enemies in the same room and applies the Heal Block status condition, thereby preventing their HP restoration, including natural recovery. This status effect endures for a duration of 5-7 turns, or 6 turns. Magic Coat can reflect this move. In Pokémon Battle Revolution, Heal Block displays an animation upon its expiration. While Heal Block is not usable in Generation VIII or Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the expansion The Indigo Disk introduced Psychic Noise, a move that both inflicts damage and prevents healing.

Title: Wring Out (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, Wring Out (Japanese: しぼりとる, literally "Wring Out") is a damage-dealing Normal-type move. Its effectiveness is directly tied to the target's remaining health, becoming more potent when wielded against foes possessing higher HP. Historically, its power has been recorded to range from a low of 1 to a high of 121. More recently, its calculated power is understood to vary between 1 and 120.

In the Explorers and Adventure Squad series of games, Wring Out's ultimate damage output is subjected to specific multipliers depending on the opponent's HP: a factor of ½ is applied if the enemy's HP rests between 75% and 51%; this multiplier then drops to 51/256 (roughly 0.2×) when HP is in the 50-26% bracket; and it diminishes further to 25/256 (approximately 0.1×) once the target's HP falls to 25% or lower. In contrast, for Super Mystery Dungeon, the damage multiplier dynamically ranges from 1.0x down to 0.0x, adjusting based on the target's current HP percentage, though it invariably inflicts a minimum of 1 damage.

Although Red's Weepinbell were officially listed on the web site as knowing Wring Out, there were no depictions of them using the move.

Title: Power Trick (move)

A Psychic-type move from Generation IV, Power Trick (Japanese: パワートリック Power Trick) causes no direct damage. It performs an exchange between the Pokémon's base Attack and base Defense stats. However, any current stat modifiers are unaffected by this swap. The alteration in stats resulting from Power Trick can be conveyed via Baton Pass, affecting the recipient's stats. Re-using Power Trick will revert the user's stats to their previous configuration, yet this does not impede Baton Pass from transferring the effect. Since Power Trick modifies the raw stats, a Pokémon employing Transform on a target whose stats have been switched will acquire those altered stats rather than the target's original ones. Should a Pokémon utilize Power Trick and subsequently Transform, the effect is nullified, overwriting the swapped stats, and Power Trick's effect becomes untransferable via Baton Pass. Analogous to the Transform scenario, if a Pokémon activates Power Trick and then either gains a level or changes its form, the switched stats are reset, even if the new form shares identical base stats with the preceding one. Despite this, unlike with Transform, the ability to Baton Pass Power Trick's effect persists. Presently, Power Trick is susceptible to being stolen by Snatch. A Pokémon that employs Power Trick and subsequently undergoes Mega Evolution will also find its swapped stats overwritten. Nevertheless, the effect of Power Trick remains conveyable via Baton Pass. When enhanced by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Power Trick, the user's Attack stat receives a one-stage boost. In addition to base stats, Power Trick also exchanges the user's Attack and Defense stat modifications (including the distinct multipliers from Aurora Beam, Memento, Charm, and Screech specifically within the Explorers series).

Title: Gastro Acid (move)

Originating in Generation IV, the Poison-type technique known as Gastro Acid (Japanese: いえき, Gastric Juice) does not inflict direct damage. Its primary function is to neutralize the target's Ability for the duration they remain in battle. However, this effect fails against Pokémon possessing specific Abilities: Multitype, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, As One, Zero to Hero, or Commander. Gastro Acid can be deflected by Magic Coat and the Ability Magic Bounce; nevertheless, if Magic Bounce does not reflect it (for example, when used by a Pokémon with Mold Breaker), Gastro Acid will still suppress Magic Bounce itself. Importantly, even when suppressed, Abilities such as Pickup and Honey Gather retain their capacity to find items after a battle. When empowered by a Poisonium Z, transforming it into Z-Gastro Acid, the user's Speed stat receives a one-stage boost. This move inflicts the Gastro Acid status condition upon the target for 6-8/ 21 turns, which suppresses the effects of their Abilities. Gastro Acid's functionality is influenced by both Magic Coat and the Muzzled status condition. In Super Mystery Dungeon, Gastro Acid gains an additional property, destroying a random Emera from the target's equipped Looplet.

Title: Lucky Chant (move)
Debuting in Generation IV, Lucky Chant (Japanese: おまじない Good Luck Charm) is classified as a Normal-type move. For a period of five turns, Lucky Chant prevents critical hits from connecting with the user's party, a protection that extends even to moves that unfailingly land critical strikes (for instance, Frost Breath). Should Normalium Z power it up into Z-Lucky Chant, the user's evasiveness receives an enhancement of one stage. The move targets all allies in the battle area, bestowing upon them the Lucky Chant status condition, which subsequently prevents critical strikes from landing. The duration of this protective status varies, lasting for 8-15 / 21 turns. A specific animation marks the conclusion of Lucky Chant's effect in Pokémon Battle Revolution.

Title: Me First (move)

Me First, known in Japanese as さきどり (Take in Advance), made its debut in Generation IV as a Normal-type status move. This technique allows the user to preemptively replicate a damage-dealing attack chosen by the target, provided the target has not yet acted during the current turn, and subsequently amplifies its power by 50%. Me First possesses perfect accuracy, consistently connecting unless the adversary is in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly. However, it will fail to execute if the target opted for a non-damaging move or has already performed its action this turn. Notably, if the target is in the process of recharging after using a move such as Frenzy Plant, Me First will successfully copy that particular move. When copying a multi-turn attack like Outrage, Me First compels its user to continue performing the copied move for multiple turns; however, the 50% power boost is exclusively applied to the initial turn. In a unique deviation from other moves, if Me First targets an empty space or a Pokémon that has already fainted prior to the user's turn, the move will simply fail, rather than being re-directed to an adjacent, unfainted foe.

Exclusively within Generation V, a Pokémon holding a Choice item that employs Me First will not become locked into any specific move, thereby retaining the freedom to choose a different action on subsequent turns. Conversely, across all other generations, the Pokémon will become restricted to using Me First itself, not the move it replicated (even if it originally knew that copied move in one of its own slots). The Assault Vest item, uniquely, does not inhibit a Pokémon from choosing Me First, making it the sole status move unaffected by this restriction. Currently, if a Pokémon equipped with a Choice item utilizes Me First to copy a consecutively executed move or Bide, the copied move will cease after its initial turn, as the Pokémon becomes locked into Me First and effectively attempts to use a different move.

When enhanced by a Normalium Z, transforming into Z-Me First, the user experiences a two-stage boost to its Speed stat. Furthermore, the attack that is copied will also manifest as its own Z-Move counterpart. Me First is ineffective if the target has opted for a Z-Move or any of the specific moves listed below. Attacks that invoke other moves are categorized as their original status move, not the move they summon, ensuring Me First invariably fails if the target has chosen such an action.

Within the context of the Mystery Dungeon series, Me First directs its effect at a front-facing opponent, randomly employing one of the damaging moves known by that foe. If the target lacks any damaging moves, the attempt will fail. The power of this invoked move is then enhanced by 50% in the Explorers series, a boost also observed in the Adventure Squad series. During player-versus-player link battles, the Pokémon employing Me First will turn towards its own Trainer during a distinctive "stealing" animation before proceeding to execute the copied move as usual.

Title: Copycat (move)

For information pertaining to the non-player character inhabiting Saffron City, consult Copycat (character). For the Pokémon TCG card depicting this character, refer to Copycat (Expedition 138). This entry describes the technique known as Copycat (Japanese: まねっこ Imitate), a non-damaging Normal-type move that debuted in Generation IV.

Copycat enables its user to perform the last move executed in the current battle, even if the user themselves was the one to perform it. Should no move have been made yet in the encounter, Copycat's activation will fail. If the move being replicated was originally directed at a target other than the user, a suitable new target is chosen randomly.

The record of the most recently used move is updated whenever any Pokémon attempts an action. A move becomes the new "last-used move" upon its successful execution (typically indicated by the message "<Pokémon> used <move>!"). This occurs irrespective of the move's outcome, whether it connected or missed, or if its effect was successful or failed (for example, a Light Screen that fails to activate because a preceding Light Screen has not yet expired).

Historically, certain multi-turn moves were not recognized as valid actions on all of their turns. If Copycat was performed immediately following such an invalid turn, it would fail. Specifically: the initial turn of charging moves, the recharge turn of recharging moves, and the final turn of Bide were all considered invalid for replication. When a move called another move, the calling move, rather than the one it triggered, was recorded as the last action. This rule applied exclusively to the turn where the calling move was initially selected. However, if a called move itself necessitated multiple turns for full execution (such as Bide, Dig, or Thrash), it could legitimately become the last-used move in subsequent turns. Furthermore, if a Pokémon attempted to use a move but was prevented by a status condition or inhibiting effect (like sleep, paralysis, or flinching), the previously recorded last-used move would become invalid, causing Copycat to fail if used next.

More recently, these mechanics have been adjusted. If any move successfully calls another, the *called* move is now the one counted as the last move used, rather than the calling move. The last move used is also now unaffected if a Pokémon attempts an action but is hindered by a status condition or other effect; in essence, the last-used move persists as it was prior to that Pokémon's turn. Additionally, charging moves, recharging moves, and Bide no longer have specific invalid turns that would cause Copycat to fail. Instead, both charging moves and Bide can be replicated on any of their turns, while recharging moves are simply ignored on their recharge turn (the last move used remains what it was before the recharge message appeared).

When boosted by a Normalium Z into Z-Copycat, the user's accuracy increases by one stage. Moreover, if the move intended for replication is not a status move, it will transform into its corresponding Z-Move. Copycat fails if the most recent move executed was a Z-Move, regardless of whether Copycat itself was powered up into Z-Copycat. In the event that a Max Move was the last action, the user will replicate the base move from which it originated. For example, if a Dynamax Pokémon used Max Guard by selecting a move slot that initially held Trick Room, a subsequent Copycat would replicate Trick Room, rather than Max Guard or resulting in a complete failure.

Copycat cannot replicate every technique. It will specifically fail if the last move used was any of the following: Encore, Sketch, Assist, Metronome, Mirror Move, or Mimic. Furthermore, if the most recent action was a Z-Move, Copycat will also fail. Copycat copies the target's last used move.

Title: Power Swap (move)

Known in Japanese as パワースワップ (Power Swap), this non-damaging Psychic-type technique made its debut in Generation IV. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was designated as Technical Machine (TM) 60. This move's primary function involves exchanging the user's Attack and Special Attack stat stages with those of its target. Because it modifies stat stages, these alterations are transferable through the move Baton Pass. Power Swap possesses perfect accuracy, circumventing typical accuracy checks, and will always connect unless the opposing Pokémon is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly. When augmented by a Psychium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Power Swap, it bestows a one-stage increase to the user's Speed stat. Fundamentally, Power Swap executes an exchange of the user's Attack and Special Attack stat stages for the target's respective Attack and Special Attack stat stages. Additionally, it can interchange the distinct multipliers applied by Aurora Beam, Memento, and Charm.

Shifting focus to Pokémon UNITE, Power Swap functions as one of Mr. Mime's potential secondary abilities. Players acquire it at level 6 by opting to evolve Light Screen into Power Swap rather than Barrier. Prior to this, Guard Swap occupied its slot as a potential second move. In UNITE, Power Swap establishes an active link between its user and the Pokémon it strikes. During its active duration, this connection grants the user enhanced movement speed and continuous HP regeneration. Should the linked Pokémon be an ally, the beneficial effects—increased movement speed and HP restoration—are mirrored onto them, alongside an amplification of their damage output. Conversely, if an opponent is targeted and linked, they suffer inverse effects: reduced movement speed, diminished damage dealt, and continuous damage over time. The connection severs if the user and the linked Pokémon exceed a certain distance. At level 13, Power Swap evolves into Power Swap+, which further enhances the damage dealt by allied Pokémon and amplifies the reduction in damage dealt by opposing Pokémon.

Title: Guard Swap (move)

This Psychic-type technique, known in Japanese as ガードスワップ (Guard Swap), debuted in Generation IV and inflicts no direct damage. In the games Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was assigned Technical Machine number 61 (TM61). The core effect of Guard Swap is to interchange the user's current Defense and Special Defense stat modifications (stages) with those of the chosen target. As this manipulation directly alters stat stages, these changes are transferable through the move Baton Pass. This move always connects, ignoring accuracy checks, unless the opposing Pokémon is currently semi-invulnerable from executing a move like Dig or Fly. When enhanced into Z-Guard Swap using a Psychium Z, an additional effect activates, boosting the user's Speed stat by a single stage. Beyond the core stage swapping, it additionally transfers any unique multiplier derived from a move like Screech.

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Guard Swap formerly served as Mr. Mime's secondary offensive option. However, it has since been supplanted by Power Swap. Players acquired it upon reaching level 6, by evolving Light Screen into Guard Swap rather than Barrier. In this context, the user projects a beam that exchanges base Defense and Special Defense stats with another Pokémon; this effect persists as long as the target remains within proximity or until a new target is chosen for Guard Swap. When used on an opponent, this swap inflicts gradual damage and reduces their movement speed, while concurrently boosting the user's own movement speed. Conversely, applying it to an ally enhances the movement speed for both the ally and the user. Damage accumulation occurs every 0.5 seconds. Upon reaching level 13, the move gains an additional healing effect, restoring the user's HP, along with any tethered ally's HP, every 0.5 seconds.

Title: Punishment (move)

Named おしおき in Japanese, Punishment is a Dark-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation IV. While its standard base power is 60, Punishment's strength escalates by 20 points for each positive stat stage the opposing Pokémon possesses, reaching a peak base power of 200 (equivalent to 7 stat increases). Reductions in the target's stats, falling below their base levels, have no impact on this move's calculated power. To illustrate, if a target has executed Shell Smash once (boosting Attack, Special Attack, and Speed by two stages each, while concurrently lowering Defense and Special Defense by one stage each), it accumulates 6 net positive stat stages, resulting in Punishment achieving a base power of 180. The concurrent drops in Defense and Special Defense do not influence this power calculation. In the Explorers series of games, Punishment's power is similarly enhanced by the target's stat boosts, up to a maximum of 10 boosts, though any Defense increases the target possesses are disregarded during damage computation. Within Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Murkrow is uniquely coded to acquire Punishment via breeding; however, no other Pokémon within the Flying Egg Group are capable of transmitting Punishment as an Egg Move in these titles. While Vullaby and Mandibuzz are the sole members of the Flying Egg Group that can learn Punishment, their exclusively female gender prevents them from being able to pass the move on.

Title: Last Resort (move)

The term "The Best" refers to this move; for the Korean opening song, please see The Best (song). Last Resort (Japanese: とっておき The Best) is a Normal-type damage-dealing attack first appearing in Generation IV. When Eevee holds an Eevium Z, Last Resort transforms into the unique Z-Move Extreme Evoboost. This move inflicts damage without any additional effects. For a Pokémon to successfully employ Last Resort, it must have previously utilized each of its other known moves at least once during its current stint on the battlefield. Using a move, then switching out and returning, will nullify any progress towards meeting this prerequisite. Last Resort cannot be executed if it is the user's sole known move, or if the user does not genuinely know the move (for instance, if it's called via an indirect method). Moreover, it will fail if the user has not depleted any of its other moves to 0 PP. However, its power is amplified, dealing 1.5× damage when two other moves are out of PP, and 2× damage if three other moves have reached 0 PP. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Last Resort is classified as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since August 11, 2018.

Title: Worry Seed (move)

Originating in Generation IV, the Grass-type move known as Worry Seed (Japanese: なやみのタネ Worry Seed) does not inflict damage. Its primary effect is to alter the target Pokémon's Ability, transforming it into Insomnia. Should a Pokémon under this effect be withdrawn from battle, its previous Ability is restored. Historically, this move could still succeed even if the target already possessed the Insomnia Ability. However, it is rendered ineffective against targets with specific Abilities: Truant, Multitype, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, As One, or Commander. Furthermore, if the opposing Pokémon holds a Griseous Orb, Worry Seed will also fail. The consequence of having Insomnia is that the affected Pokémon becomes immune to sleep and will awaken if it is currently asleep. Modern iterations of the move now display the Pokémon's original Ability before its alteration. A subsequent update caused Worry Seed to fail if the target's Ability is already Insomnia. Conversely, targets holding the Griseous Orb can now be successfully impacted by Worry Seed. Within Contest Spectaculars, Worry Seed facilitates a combination: the user earns an additional three appeal points if Rototiller was performed on the preceding turn. When boosted by a Grassium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Worry Seed, the user experiences a one-stage increase in its Speed stat. This move is capable of influencing Hunger Switch, preventing Morpeko from changing forms while under its effect. In the Explorers and Adventure Squad game series, Worry Seed applies the Sleepless status condition to the target, lasting for the remainder of the current floor. Specifically, in these spin-off titles, the move also fails if the target possesses the Ability Truant. Starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, its effect was standardized to change the target's Ability to Insomnia. The move's nomenclature, Worry Seed, is a clever pun derived from the Japanese phrase 悩みの種 (Nayami no Tane). While typically meaning 'cause of trouble', a strictly literal translation results in 'Worry Seed'. The use of katakana for 種 (Tane), meaning 'seed', serves to underscore the literal involvement of a seed in the move's execution.

Title: Sucker Punch (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Sucker Punch is a Dark-type offensive maneuver (Japanese: ふいうち Surprise Attack). This technique boasts a power rating of 80. With a priority of +1, it executes ahead of any move lacking elevated priority. Damage is inflicted by Sucker Punch solely if its intended recipient has chosen a physical or special attack and has not yet acted; failing under any other circumstance. Its success is exclusively contingent on the target's selected move, remaining unaffected by factors that might prevent attack execution, such as status conditions like sleep or freeze, or Abilities like Truant. Notably, Sucker Punch now lands successfully even if the opponent employs Me First, despite Me First being categorized as a status move.

Initially, a programming anomaly caused Sucker Punch to consistently miss the opponent positioned to the right in a Double Battle, specifically when the non-initiating player was reduced to their last Pokémon, irrespective of the target's chosen action for that turn. However, this issue was rectified with the Sword and Shield version 1.1 update, allowing Sucker Punch to correctly strike Pokémon in that specific position.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Sucker Punch functions as Absol's secondary ability. Absol obtains this ability at level 7 by upgrading Slash, offering it as an alternative to Psycho Cut. The user strikes a chosen Pokémon, resulting in a speed reduction for both the attacker and the target. Should the user be attacked, or the target initiate any form of offense, the move inflicts additional damage. At level 13, the user's fundamental attack speed is boosted following a successful counter.

Notwithstanding its nomenclature, Sucker Punch is not categorized as a punching technique. It stands alone in this unique classification. Reflecting its Japanese name ふいうち ('surprise attack'), the term 'sucker punch' signifies an unforeseen or unfair strike against an opponent. Consequently, numerous armless Pokémon are capable of learning it, and its power remains unaffected by the Iron Fist Ability.

Title: Toxic Spikes (move)

Known in Japanese as どくびし (Toxic Spikes), this Poison-type maneuver, introduced in Generation IV, inflicts no direct damage. It functions as an entry hazard, deploying around the opponent's Pokémon. When a single layer is present, incoming opposing Pokémon become afflicted with the poison status; with two or more layers, they are instead rendered badly poisoned. This hazard proves ineffective against creatures of the Flying, Poison, or Steel types, as well as those possessing the Abilities Immunity, Levitate, Leaf Guard (specifically when intense sunlight is active), or Magic Guard. A Pokémon benefiting from Magnet Rise also remains untouched by Toxic Spikes. Nevertheless, Flying-type or Levitating Pokémon *will* be susceptible if they enter battle while equipped with an Iron Ball, during the active effect of Gravity, or after receiving Ingrain via Baton Pass. Should a grounded Poison-type Pokémon enter the field, it will absorb the Toxic Spikes, thereby nullifying their effect. The hazards can be cleared by the moves Rapid Spin and Defog; Rapid Spin clears those around its user, whereas Defog removes those around its target. Notably, Toxic Spikes never triggers the Ability Synchronize, even if another Pokémon on the field forces a Synchronizing Pokémon to switch in, for example, through moves like Roar or Dragon Tail. A Pokémon clutching an Air Balloon also gains immunity to Toxic Spikes, unless Gravity is active. Furthermore, a Poison-type Pokémon holding an Air Balloon will not absorb these spikes, again, unless Gravity is in effect. Magic Coat is capable of reflecting Toxic Spikes. While Magic Guard doesn't prevent the poisoning status itself, it does negate any damage received from poison. Pokémon with the Comatose Ability are immune to being poisoned by Toxic Spikes. In Contest Spectaculars, Toxic Spikes contributes to a combination, granting an additional three appeal points if followed on the subsequent turn by Circle Throw, Dragon Tail, Hex, Roar, Venom Drench, Venoshock, or Whirlwind. When amplified by a Poisonium Z into Z-Toxic Spikes, the user's Defense stat receives a boost of one stage. Equipping Heavy-Duty Boots renders a Pokémon immune to Toxic Spikes upon its entry into combat. However, a grounded Poison-type Pokémon holding Heavy-Duty Boots can still clear the hazard upon switching in.

In other game contexts, Toxic Spikes allows the user to deploy a Toxic Spikes trap directly beneath them, provided the tile is land, not stairs, and not Kecleon Shop carpet. This trap cannot be placed in hallways or at a room's entrance. Any Pokémon activating this trap will suffer poisoning and take damage (10 HP in the Explorers series, 20 HP in Super Mystery Dungeon). Even Poison-type and Steel-type Pokémon will still incur damage from the trap, though they will not be poisoned. Furthermore, Flying-type and levitating Pokémon are also susceptible to the Toxic Spikes trap. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Toxic Spikes, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Boss Mareanie was observed using Toxic Spikes. Finally, within Pokémon Battle Revolution, the visual placement of the spikes varies with each distinct animation sequence.

Title: Heart Swap (move)

The Psychic-type technique known as Heart Swap (Japanese: ハートスワップ Heart Swap) debuted in Generation IV and inflicts no damage. With the sole exception of Generation VII, this move is considered one of Manaphy's signature attacks. When activated, Heart Swap causes the user's current stat stage modifiers to be exchanged with those of the chosen target. Because it alters these stat stages directly, the modifications can subsequently be transferred through the move Baton Pass. This move is guaranteed to connect, disregarding standard accuracy checks, unless the opposing Pokémon is in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Should it be amplified by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Heart Swap, the attacking Pokémon's critical hit ratio receives a boost of two stages. This critical hit rate enhancement is applied *after* the primary exchange of stat stages has occurred. The categories of stat changes affected by Heart Swap include Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, accuracy, and evasion (along with specific multipliers from moves like Screech, Charm, Memento, and Aurora Beam, as observed in the Explorers and Adventures game series). Within the Super Smash Bros. Brawl game, Manaphy employs Heart Swap upon its release from a Poké Ball. This action causes the controls for the players operating the two characters nearest to Manaphy at the moment it initiates the attack to be interchanged. Furthermore, the damage meters are exchanged, and in the event of a character's knockout, the player *currently* in control of that character forfeits a stock, rather than the player who initially selected them. The standard control scheme is reinstated after an approximate duration of 15 to 30 seconds. Manaphy holds the possibility of engaging four brawlers in a heart swap if they are all within sufficient proximity.

Title: Aqua Ring (move)

Known in Japanese as アクアリング (Aqua Ring), this Water-type, non-damaging technique debuted in Generation IV. Upon activation, the Pokémon envelops itself in a watery veil, subsequently regaining 1/16 of its maximum HP (rounded down) at the close of every turn. Its effect applies universally, irrespective of the user's elemental typing. Should the Pokémon under its effect be equipped with a Big Root, the healing potency escalates by 30%. While a Pokémon under the influence of Heal Block can still *perform* Aqua Ring, the restorative properties of the water veil are suppressed for the duration of Heal Block. Activating Z-Aqua Ring with a Waterium Z causes the user's Defense stat to ascend by a single stage. Exclusively within Generation IV, Snatch is incapable of seizing Aqua Ring; however, from Generation V onward, it becomes vulnerable to appropriation by Snatch. The Pokémon employing it acquires the Aqua Ring status, a condition that provides gradual HP recovery over time. Specifically in Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, the status endures for 9 to 16 turns, with the user recovering 8 HP every four turns. Starting with Gates to Infinity, the duration reduces to 6 turns, and the user receives 6 HP every two turns. The Pokémon pauses for approximately two seconds, after which it restores HP in 2s and 3s. In the context of Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-altering moves, Aqua Ring among them, were exclusive to opposing Pokémon and unattainable for any captured Pokémon. Notably, Boss Dewgong and Manaphy utilized Aqua Ring as one of their techniques. The animated series, as depicted in episodes Cilan Takes Flight! and An Amazing Aerial Battle!, portrays Aqua Ring with expanded effects, such as healing the user's burns and acting as a barrier against Electric-type attacks.

Title: Magnet Rise (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, the Electric-type move known as Magnet Rise (Japanese: でんじふゆう, or Electromagnetic Levitation) inflicts no damage. For a duration of five turns, its user gains immunity to Ground-type attacks. Similar to Flying-type Pokémon or those possessing the Levitate ability, the Pokémon using Magnet Rise becomes ungrounded, thereby preventing it from being affected by numerous ground-based phenomena. Should the user be under the influence of Ingrain, Magnet Rise will fail; furthermore, it cannot be chosen if Gravity is active. Even if an Iron Ball is held, Magnet Rise remains usable, though the user will not leave the ground until that item is no longer equipped. The move Snatch is capable of stealing Magnet Rise. Its effects cease if the user is struck by either Smack Down or Thousand Arrows. Subsequent attempts to use Magnet Rise will not succeed. When amplified by an Electrium Z into Z-Magnet Rise, the user's evasiveness stat increases by a single stage. The user gains the Magnet Rise status condition, which provides immunity to Ground-type moves and a flying movement type. This condition endures for 3-5 / 10 turns.

Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can acquire Magnet Rise from a Magnemite located in Withered Wasteland after the credits roll. This enables Ditto to shapeshift into a Magnemite, gaining the ability to hover freely. During this transformation, Ditto's PP steadily diminishes. Uniquely among Ditto's transformations, Magnet Rise permits the picking up and placement of objects, offering significantly greater flexibility in execution compared to Ditto's human form. Placement of objects occurs via Spit Out, either singly or in arrangements forming squares of four or nine, which can be oriented vertically or horizontally, or even replace items already present. Even shattered objects, including those typically requiring Rock Smash or Cut for destruction, are retrieved in their current state and immediately deposited into the bag. In Pokémon Battle Revolution, an animation is shown when Magnet Rise concludes. For certain Pokémon, such as Electrode, which are solely vulnerable to the Ground type, Magnet Rise can eliminate all weaknesses. During Generation IV, the Porygon family of Pokémon was able to learn Magnet Rise by leveling up, but not through the Move Tutor mechanism.

Title: Flare Blitz (move)

Flare Blitz (Japanese: フレアドライブ Flare Drive), a damage-dealing Fire-type move, first appeared in Generation IV. This attack inflicts damage, with the user simultaneously receiving recoil damage equivalent to one-third (⅓) of the damage dealt to the target. It also carries a 10% probability of burning the opponent. If the user is currently frozen, Flare Blitz will first remove this condition before proceeding to inflict damage. The move's Power Points (PP) have been reduced to 5, and its base power has been lowered to 100. Additionally, it offers a cure for the user's frostbite status.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Flare Blitz's recoil mechanics differ. Throughout the Explorers series, the user takes recoil damage equal to one-eighth (⅛) of their maximum HP. From Gates to Infinity onwards, this recoil calculation changed to 20% of the user's maximum HP. Blue (Classic) & Charizard, notably, do not take any recoil damage from using this move, an effect attributed to their passive skill, Recoil Removal 9.

A description of Flare Blitz in the main series, valid prior to Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), noted that it removed the frozen condition from the user, caused the user to take 25% of the damage dealt to the target, and had a very small chance of leaving the target burned.

As Charizard's side special move in Super Smash Bros., Flare Blitz replaced Rock Smash, which subsequently became its down special after Charizard was established as an independent fighter. When performed, Charizard becomes shrouded in flames and propels itself forward in a corkscrew motion, delivering substantial damage upon impact. Mirroring its behavior in the core Pokémon games, Charizard incurs recoil damage when utilizing this move. Flare Blitz remains Charizard's side special, exhibiting significantly greater power than its iteration in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U.

In Pokémon UNITE, Flare Blitz is Charizard's second move. It becomes available when Charmeleon reaches level 7, upgrading from Fire Spin rather than Fire Blast. The user charges forward, gaining a shield while simultaneously damaging and knocking back the targeted enemy. At level 13 or higher, the move acquires an extra effect, briefly slowing down enemies after they are thrown.

Manga appearances include *Pocket Monsters Diamond and Pearl: Regigigas Major Capture Operation!!*, where Marley instructed her Arcanine to use Flare Blitz in the chapter "Leaping Past Lopunny," though the action itself was off-panel. It also featured in *Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder*.

For Pokémon Contests in Generation IV, Flare Blitz was distinctive as the only Fire-type Smart move. By Generation VI, this classification changed, positioning it as a Cool move instead. Flare Blitz holds the unique distinction of being the sole Fire-type move that inflicts recoil damage.

Title: Force Palm (move)

Known in Japanese as はっけい (Force Discharge), Force Palm stands as a Fighting-type attack capable of dealing damage, first appearing in Generation IV. Beyond its damaging effect, this technique carries a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon afflicted with paralysis. Within Contest Spectaculars, Force Palm initiates a combination wherein subsequent use of Hex or Smelling Salts by the performer will grant an additional three appeal points. In the context of other games, it functions as Lucario's signature side special move. Its versatility allows it to serve as either a long-range offensive maneuver or a command grab, adapting based on the adversary's proximity. For Pokémon GO players, Force Palm operates as a Fast Attack, having become accessible from July 27, 2024 onwards. The move's Japanese appellation draws its origins from a Chinese martial arts concept, particularly one concerning internal energies and the application of explosive power. In Japanese, the term 'fajin/hakkei' typically translates to 'release internal power'. Notably, Force Palm holds the distinction as the sole Fighting-type move capable of inducing paralysis that is available for player use, with its only counterpart, Combat Torque, being solely wielded by the Revavroom encountered within Team Star's Caph Squad's Starmobile.

Title: Aura Sphere (move)

Unveiled in Generation IV, Aura Sphere (Japanese: はどうだん Wave Bomb) is a Fighting-type attack that deals damage. It was obtainable as TR56 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM112 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move consistently connects with its target by circumventing accuracy checks, except when the foe is within the semi-invulnerable period of an action like Dig or Fly. In Triple Battles, it possesses the capability to strike targets that are not immediately adjacent. Its base power experienced a reduction, decreasing from 90 to 80. Furthermore, the attack's potency is amplified by 50% when wielded by a Pokémon with the Mega Launcher ability, whereas creatures boasting Bulletproof are impervious to its effects.

For Lucario, Aura Sphere functions as its neutral special. Consistent with many of Lucario's other abilities, this move gains strength when Lucario has endured more harm. Should an adversary collide with Lucario during the charging phase of the sphere, that foe will also incur damage.

In Pokémon GO, Aura Sphere is a Charged Attack that became available on December 11, 2019. Within Pokémon Rumble Blast, the user conjures a blue orb that actively tracks the closest Pokémon; this buddy move was formerly designated as B Aura Sphere before version 2.38.0. The manga "Exit Empoleon" noted that Dahlia's Togekiss possessed Aura Sphere, though its actual usage of the move was never depicted. Both its visual representation and its Japanese name (はどうだん Hadōdan) draw parallels to the Hadouken technique from the Street Fighter video game series. A specific camera clipping glitch manifests in Pokémon X and Y when players utilize Aura Sphere against certain Pokémon belonging to Drasna.

Title: Rock Polish (move)

Debuting in Generation IV, Rock Polish (known in Japanese as ロックカット, or Rock Cut) stands as a non-damaging move of the Rock type. This move was available as TM69, spanning from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappearing in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When employed, Rock Polish boosts the user's Speed stat by a significant two stages. Within Contest Spectaculars, Rock Polish serves as an element of a combination; its usage guarantees an additional three appeal points for Baton Pass and Electro Ball if either move is performed on the subsequent turn. Should it be enhanced by a Rockium Z, transforming into Z-Rock Polish, this move gains the powerful effect of resetting all of the user's lowered stats. Beyond battle, Rock Polish also elevates the user's Travel Speed by two stages. Furthermore, Snatch can interfere with this move's execution.

Title: Poison Jab (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Poison Jab (Japanese: どくづき, or Poison Stab) stands as a damage-dealing Poison-type technique. It was designated as TM84 across all core series titles from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and also served as TM84 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned TM26. Beyond simply inflicting damage, this move carries a 30% chance of leaving the opposing Pokémon poisoned. Additionally, Sygna Suit Giovanni & Nidoking's passive skill, Giovanni's Cunning, ensures that their uses of Poison Jab will never miss. Prior to the Version 2.10.0 update on June 28, 2021, its in-game description read: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target poisoned." In the animated episode "Trial on a Golden Scale!", one of Nito's henchmen commanded his Conkeldurr to execute Poison Jab, but the Pokémon was swiftly defeated by Quillon's Urshifu before it could perform the move. Significantly, Poison Jab was the very first Generation IV move to be depicted in the animated series.

Title: Dark Pulse (move)

The Dark-type attack, Dark Pulse (known as あくのはどう, or Evil Pulse, in Japanese), made its debut as a damage-dealing technique in Generation IV. It was accessible as TM79 throughout Generation IV and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. From Generation VI through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it was designated as TM97. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it could be acquired as TM20. This move inflicts damage and carries a 20% probability of causing the opposing Pokémon to flinch. Within Triple Battles, Dark Pulse is capable of striking opponents not positioned directly adjacent to the user. A Pokémon possessing the Mega Launcher Ability will experience a 50% power boost for Dark Pulse when executing it. Its mechanics underwent changes in Pokémon Legends: Arceus; its Power Points (PP) were diminished from 15 to 10, and the move ceased to cause flinching. Instead, it gained the effect of bypassing accuracy and evasion checks, thereby guaranteeing a hit. The attack deals damage and has a chance to make the foe flinch. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), its description stated: "Has a small chance of making the target flinch." This particular sync move was unable to be raised to 6★ EX until July 5, 2022, which marked 1041 days following its initial release on August 29, 2019. The 6★ EX effect's description, before Version 2.18.5 (from March 15, 2022), read: "Additional Effects: Power of sync move increases by 50%." Earlier, preceding Version 2.20.0 (as of April 27, 2022), the description noted: "This move's power increases when the zone is Dark Zone." Furthermore, an older description, prior to Version 2.23.0 (from July 27, 2022), indicated: "This attack's power increases when the zone is Dark Zone."

Title: Night Slash (move)

A Dark-type offensive technique, Night Slash (known in Japan as つじぎり, or Crossroad Killing) made its debut in Generation IV. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it holds the technical machine designation TM034. This offensive technique inflicts damage and carries an improved critical hit chance. In the Explorers and Adventure Squad game series, this critical hit ratio is set at 30%. When executed by a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness ability, its potency sees a 50% increase. Furthermore, Night Slash contributes to Contest Spectacular combinations; should Focus Energy have been performed in the preceding turn, the user accrues an additional three appeal points. Before version 2.38.0, this particular buddy move bore the designation B Night Slash.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Night Slash serves as Absol's initial combat technique. Absol acquires it upon reaching level 5, by evolving Feint into Night Slash rather than Pursuit. The user ashes in an arc, harming and impeding the first Pokémon struck. Should a target be successfully struck, the move can be re-activated for a short duration, enabling the user to dash and inflict damage upon all Pokémon encountered. Upon reaching level 11, the technique provides the user heals heals on hit, with the healing effect doubling if the inflicted damage is a critical hit. Absol's signature Unite Move, Midnight Slash, bears resemblance to Night Slash.

During the episode Jumping Rocket Ship!, Meowth asserted that he had mastered Night Slash, yet he has never been observed employing the attack, stating its terrifying nature prevents his usage. Within Classroom Training!, Jeremiah instructed his Skuntank to perform Night Slash; however, the Pokémon's incapacitation by James's Carnivine's Vine Whip attack rendered it unable to execute the technique.
