In Stunning Staravia & Stinky Skuntank II, a Skuntank owned by a Team Galactic Grunt prepared to unleash Night Slash on Paka's Burmy, but Pearl's timely warning enabled Burmy to halt and retaliate against the assault before its execution.

The Japanese designation, 「つじぎり」 Tsujigiri, which translates literally to "Crossroad Killing," alludes to a historical practice by which certain samurai in feudal Japan would evaluate newly forged blades. This involved concealing themselves beside a thoroughfare (frequently a crossroads, hence the literal interpretation of the name) and anticipating the arrival of an unsuspecting commoner (meaning an individual of lower social standing, possessing significantly fewer rights than the samurai stratum). The samurai would then strike with lethal intent, thereby assessing the efficacy of their new sword's cutting prowess.

Title: Aqua Tail (move)

Known in Japanese as アクアテール Aqua Tail, this Water-type attack made its debut in Generation IV and is designed to inflict damage. Its primary function is to cause harm, lacking any additional effects or conditions. Prior to the Version 2.20.0 patch released on April 27, 2022, its official description stated that its power escalated in proportion to the user's Defense stat boosts. As a sync move, it was unavailable for promotion to 6★ EX status until June 6, 2022, a milestone reached 1012 days following its initial launch on August 29, 2019. Within the world of Pokémon UNITE, Aqua Tail functions as Azumarill's initial special move. Players acquire it by evolving Marill at level 5, subsequently upgrading the Tackle move to Aqua Tail rather than Whirlpool. Upon activation, this move enhances the speed and geographical reach of the subsequent three basic attacks for a duration of 5 seconds. Should these specific basic attacks successfully strike foes from afar, they additionally inflict amplified damage, replenish the user's HP, and possess the capability to bypass the Defense statistic and protective shield effects of affected adversaries. Activating Aqua Tail will immediately supersede any currently active alterations to basic attacks granted by Play Rough. Once the Pokémon reaches level 11, after deploying Aqua Tail, the ensuing three basic attacks, if they connect with an opponent from a range, will further decrease the cooldown timer of all abilities by .5 seconds.

Title: Seed Bomb (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Seed Bomb (Japanese: タネばくだん Seed Bomb) is a Grass-type attack that deals direct damage. This move's only effect is to inflict harm, as it possesses no secondary properties. Furthermore, targets with the Bulletproof ability are immune to its effects. For Contest Spectaculars, Seed Bomb contributes to a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if Rototiller was performed in the preceding turn. As of its current iteration, Seed Bomb functions as a multistrike move with 35 power, launching four seeds in front of the user. These seeds sweep from left to right, detonating upon contact with the ground or an opposing target. In Pokémon UNITE, Seed Bomb is Venusaur's first available move while it is in its Bulbasaur form. The Pokémon utilizing it hurls a substantial seed to a designated location, harming all enemies within the impact radius. However, upon evolving into Ivysaur at level 5, Seed Bomb is superseded by a choice between Sludge Bomb or Giga Drain.

Title: Air Slash (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Air Slash (Japanese: エアスラッシュ Air Slash) is a Flying-type attack that inflicts damage. This move held the TM95 slot in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and now serves as TM065 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Beyond its damaging effect, it possesses a 30% probability of making the opposing Pokémon flinch. Notably, the King's Rock item no longer influences Air Slash's effects. During Triple Battles, it has the unique property of striking foes not directly next to the user. Its Power Points (PP) saw a reduction, decreasing from an initial 20 to 15. When executed by a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness ability, its power escalates by 50%. Subsequently, Air Slash's maximum PP was further lowered to 10. Furthermore, it has the effect of diminishing the target's action speed. This attack both inflicts harm and retains the capacity to make the target flinch.

Within the realm of Pokémon GO, Air Slash functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible to players since February 16, 2017. As for Pokémon UNITE, Cramorant utilizes Air Slash as its secondary move. Trainers acquire this move for Cramorant upon reaching level 4, choosing to evolve Feather Dance into Air Slash rather than Hurricane. The Pokémon employing it fires numerous air blades while retreating, damaging all adversaries caught within the conical range. Each successful blade hit contributes to a reduction in the move's cooldown time. Upon reaching level 11, individual blades additionally restore the user's HP when they strike an opponent.

Dahlia's Togekiss was noted to possess Air Slash in the narrative Exit Empoleon, yet its actual deployment of the attack was never depicted. Contrary to what its name and textual description might suggest, Air Slash is not classified as a wind-based attack.

Title: Bug Buzz (move)

Originating in Generation IV, the Bug-type attack known as Bug Buzz (Japanese: むしのさざめき, or Insect Noise) functions as a damage-dealing maneuver. Trainers can teach this move using TM62 within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Typically, when a move is used against a Pokémon behind a substitute, it hits the substitute. However, Bug Buzz has the ability to bypass substitutes, hitting the Pokémon directly. Beyond its primary damage output, Bug Buzz possesses a 10% probability of reducing the opponent's Special Defense stat by a single stage. Its base power has seen a reduction, settling at 80. Additionally, there is a 20% likelihood that it will decrease the target's overall defensive stats for a duration of three turns. When employed, Bug Buzz inflicts harm upon all foes positioned adjacently, with a possibility of reducing their Sp. Defense by one stage. As an attack rooted in sound, it is consequently nullified by the Soundproof Ability. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description noted only a "very small chance" of diminishing the target's Sp. Def. For its 6★ EX effect, the description preceding Version 2.18.5 (dated March 15, 2022) indicated that the associated Sync move would target every opponent. Though various descriptions for Bug Buzz imply the necessity of wings for its execution, Pokémon not possessing wings are nevertheless capable of mastering this technique. Contrastingly, upon its debut in Generation IV, every Pokémon initially able to learn Bug Buzz was indeed characterized by having wings.

Title: Dragon Pulse (move)

This entry details the move known as Dragon Pulse. For the energy type appearing in the Japanese localizations of White—Victini and Zekrom and Black—Victini and Reshiram, which shares the name "Dragon Pulse," please consult Dragon Force.

Dragon Pulse (Japanese: りゅうのはどう Dragon Pulse) is a Dragon-type attack that inflicts damage, first introduced in Generation IV. It was designated as Technical Machine TM59 during Generation IV and in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was assigned as TM34.

Beyond its damage-dealing capabilities, Dragon Pulse possesses the unique property of being able to strike opponents not directly adjacent to the user when employed in Triple Battles. The move's base power currently stands at 85, a reduction from its previous value of 90. Furthermore, a Pokémon utilizing Dragon Pulse will see its power boosted by an additional 50% if it possesses the Ability Mega Launcher.

In the world of Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this attack exhibits perfect accuracy, consistently connecting with its target by bypassing standard accuracy checks. A Poipole that has learned this particular move will undergo evolution into Naganadel upon gaining a level.

Its presence extends to Super Smash Bros. Brawl, where Dragon Pulse manifests as one of the attacks available to Rayquaza when it appears as a combatant, projected as spherical bursts of red and white electrical energy.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Dragon Pulse serves as Duraludon's initial offensive option. It becomes available at level 7 by upgrading Laser Focus, offering an alternative to Flash Cannon. The user initiates a power charge-up sequence before unleashing damage upon enemies within a specified area of effect. While charging, the user's movement speed is decreased; however, a more prolonged charge duration results in both an expanded area of effect for the attack and increased damage output. Upon reaching level 11, should Dragon Pulse secure a knockout against an opposing player, it will rejuvenate 8% of the user's maximum HP.

Title: Dragon Rush (move)

Dragon Rush (Japanese: ドラゴンダイブ Dragon Dive) is an offensive Dragon-type attack first introduced in Generation IV. This technique deals damage and carries a 20% chance of causing the target to flinch. If the opposing Pokémon has used Minimize, Dragon Rush will inflict double its standard damage and completely ignore accuracy checks, thus always hitting, unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable state of moves such as Dig or Fly. Dragon Rush's execution is comparable to Heat Crash and Heavy Slam: the user leaps high into the air (briefly evading attacks that pass through their position) before descending onto the foe. It also bears a resemblance to Fly and Bounce, though the duration the user remains airborne is significantly shorter. This move possesses a base Power Points (PP) of 20 and a base accuracy of 90%.

In *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity*, when performed by a Gigantic Pokémon, Dragon Rush's range is extended, allowing it to target enemies within the user's line of sight. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a small chance of making the target flinch." For Iris (Alt.) & Hydreigon, their passive skill, Piercing Gaze, guarantees their usage of this move will never miss.

Within *Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U*, Dragon Rush serves as a custom move for Charizard, replacing Flare Blitz. When activated, Charizard dashes at the opponent while spinning. Upon making contact, it carries the adversary, inflicting repeated damage. Distinct from Flare Blitz and Blast Burn, Dragon Rush does not cause Charizard to take self-damage. Mega Charizard X employs the identical move by pressing the A button, with the added capability of being directed in various directions.

In *Pokémon UNITE*, Dragon Rush is Garchomp's initial combat ability. It is acquired when the user evolves into Gabite at level 5, by upgrading Sand Attack into Dragon Rush instead of Dig. The user first jumps backward, briefly channels, then dives forward, pulling all enemies within a small radius to a designated area and reducing incoming damage by 35%. Following this, it deals damage in a radius. Should Dragon Claw be used during the first half of the channel, it provides two additional hits and replaces the final radial damage with a damaging stun and slow effect. At level 11, the user becomes unstoppable while executing this move. Finally, the Lake of Outrage is named after Dragon Rush in the Italian localization.

Title: Drain Punch (move)

Drain Punch (Japanese: ドレインパンチ Drain Punch) is a Fighting-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation IV. It was designated as TM60 during Generation IV and reappears as such in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. The core effect of Drain Punch is to inflict harm upon the opponent, while simultaneously restoring the user's HP by an amount equivalent to up to 50% of the damage inflicted. Even if only 1 HP of damage is caused by this strike, 1 HP will be given back to the attacker. Should the user's current HP surpass their maximum HP after restoration, their HP total will be capped at the maximum.

A notable exception occurs if the target possesses the Ability Liquid Ooze, in which case the user suffers HP loss equal to the amount they would have typically recovered. Furthermore, holding a Big Root amplifies the HP regeneration by 30%, resulting in 65% of the damage dealt being restored; however, the Big Root item does not augment the damage output itself.

In its initial introduction, Drain Punch featured 5 PP and a base power of 60. Later, its base PP was increased to 10, and its power was boosted from 60 to 75. Use of Drain Punch is prohibited if the user is affected by either Heal Block or Psychic Noise. The move's healing effect factors in all damage dealt to the target, including any excess damage beyond their remaining HP, restoring 50% of that total.

Variations exist across different game titles: In Adventure Squad specifically, the move instead recovers 20% of the damage dealt. For Gates to Infinity, the user's HP recovery is solely based on the HP lost by the target, with excess damage not contributing to the heal. In Pokémon GO, Drain Punch functions as a Charged Attack, becoming available on April 23, 2022. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021), its description noted that it restored the user's HP based on damage, specifically 10% of damage dealt, rounded down.

Interestingly, Shroomish and Togekiss can learn Drain Punch despite lacking hands, a capability that their respective evolutionary lines do possess. Drain Punch holds the unique distinction of being the sole Fighting-type move capable of draining HP.

Title: Vacuum Wave (move)

Vacuum Wave, known in Japanese as しんくうは (Shinkūha), is a Fighting-type attack introduced in Generation IV that inflicts damage. It functions as TM184 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet and TM150 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. This move features a priority of +1, ensuring its execution before any moves without elevated priority. The Japanese moniker しんくうは (Shinkūha) draws inspiration from a prevalent ninja technique commonly depicted across manga, anime, and other fiction, often referred to as a "vacuum wave" in English. Exemplary instances include the Naruto series or the Megami Tensei franchise. Curiously, despite Scyther's ability to learn this attack by leveling up in specific titles, it cannot be taught by a Move Tutor in Pokémon Platinum, HeartGold, or SoulSilver. Vacuum Wave is distinct as the sole special attack among all moves considered variations of Quick Attack.

Title: Focus Blast (move)

Known in Japanese as きあいだま (Fighting Spirit Bullet), Focus Blast is a damage-dealing Fighting-type attack that debuted in Generation IV. It functioned as TM52 from Generation IV up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This move deals damage and possesses a 10% likelihood of decreasing the target's Special Defense by one stage. Pokémon with the Bulletproof ability are immune to its effects. In Pokémon GO, Focus Blast is classified as a Charged Attack, having been available since February 16, 2017. Its in-game description, prior to the Version 2.10.0 update on June 28, 2021, stated: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def." Focus Blast is notable as the sole special Fighting-type move to have an accuracy stat below 100%. Pre-release materials for the Japanese versions of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl showcased a different animation for Focus Blast, where it disintegrated upon impact with an opponent instead of spreading. Uniquely among special moves considered similar to Blizzard, Focus Blast was the only one that did not have its power reduced to 110 in Generation VI. An early trailer for Pokémon X and Y depicted Focus Blast's animation against a red background, a stark contrast to the blue background seen in the game's final version.

Title: Energy Ball (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, Energy Ball (エナジーボール in Japanese) is a damage-dealing Grass-type move. It functioned as TM53 in various titles, from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. The move deals damage and carries a 10% chance of lowering the target's Special Defense by one stage. Its power was initially increased from 80 to 90. Targets with the Bulletproof Ability are immune to its effects. Later, its base power was reduced back to 80. Additionally, it has a 20% chance to reduce the target's defensive stats for three turns. It inherently causes damage and may decrease the foe's Sp. Defense by a single stage. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), its description read: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def." In the animated series, Energy Ball's visual appearance strongly resembles Zap Cannon, Hidden Power, and Electro Ball.

Title: Brave Bird (move)

Known in Japanese as ブレイブバード (Brave Bird), this damaging Flying-type technique debuted in the fourth generation of Pokémon games. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was cataloged as TR66, while its current designation in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet is TM164. Upon striking, Brave Bird delivers damage, but the Pokémon employing it also suffers self-inflicted recoil damage amounting to one-third of the damage dealt to the adversary. However, if no damage is ultimately inflicted (for example, if an ability like Disguise absorbs the hit), the user avoids this recoil. During Triple Battles, Brave Bird possesses the unique property of being able to target foes that are not immediately adjacent to the user. Its Power Points (PP) have been adjusted downwards to 5, and its base power has also seen a reduction, now standing at 100.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Brave Bird inflicts damage and also causes the user to take recoil. This recoil is set at one-eighth of the user's maximum HP in the Explorers and Adventure installments, and was subsequently increased to 20% of maximum HP from Gates to Infinity and subsequent titles. Within Gates to Infinity, gigantic Pokémon employing Brave Bird benefit from an elevated base PP of 20, though its base accuracy is simultaneously diminished to 90%.

As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Brave Bird has been accessible to players since its introduction on February 16, 2017. The sync pairs Skyla & Unfezant and Grimsley & Honchkrow are immune to Brave Bird's recoil effect, thanks to their specialized passive skill, Recoil Removal 9. Before Version 2.18.5 (effective March 15, 2022), a 6★ EX effect for this move described "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents." Another 6★ EX effect, active prior to Version 2.18.5 (as of March 15, 2022), stated "Additional Effects: Power of sync move increases by 50%." A description pre-dating Version 2.20.0 (from April 27, 2022) indicated: "The more the target's Attack is lowered, the greater the power of this move."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Brave Bird serves as Talonflame's initial move. Players acquire it upon Fletchinder's evolution at level 7, by choosing to upgrade Acrobatics into Brave Bird instead of Fly. The user executes a dive towards a specified area, incurring recoil damage equivalent to 5% of its current HP while simultaneously harming all adversaries within that zone. Should the user follow up with a boosted basic attack on an opponent struck by Brave Bird within a 4-second window, the cooldown for either Flame Charge or Aerial Ace will be instantly reset. This cooldown reset for Flame Charge and Aerial Ace is limited to once per target affected by Brave Bird. Upon reaching level 13, successful use of Brave Bird grants the user a 15% damage reduction for 5 seconds.

In Generations IV and V, Sky Attack and Brave Bird exhibited comparable in-game animations; their primary visual distinction was Sky Attack's blue aura contrasting with Brave Bird's red aura. Interestingly, this color scheme was inverted in the animated series, a trend that persisted until Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon. Further parallels exist in the Japanese version, where Sky Attack bears the name 'God Bird'. While the animated series and Pokémon Adventures depict Pokémon with outstretched wings when executing Brave Bird, the official in-game descriptions contradict this by stating the user tucks its wings in. Notably, Brave Bird stands alone as the sole Flying-type move capable of inflicting recoil damage. Excluding Mew, which possesses the unique ability to learn virtually any move through TMs or TRs, the Zubat evolutionary line represents the only Pokémon family capable of mastering Brave Bird, even though its members are not avian-based.

Title: Earth Power (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Earth Power (Japanese: だいちのちから Earth Power) is a Ground-type attack designed to inflict damage. This offensive maneuver has a 10% probability of reducing the target's Special Defense stat by one stage. Its base power has been established at 80, a value to which it was previously adjusted downwards.

Beyond its primary damage, it also possesses a 20% likelihood of diminishing the opponent's defensive stats for a duration of three turns. The move itself manifests as an explosion erupting from beneath the target, capable of striking additional foes positioned sufficiently close to the initial impact zone. However, due to its subterranean origin, Pokémon located too far above the ground will evade its effects and sustain no damage. In certain battle environments, Earth Power assaults all hostile entities within the chamber. It also carries the potential to lower a target's Sp. Defense by a single stage.

Regarding its presence in Pokémon GO, Earth Power functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible to players since May 17, 2019. The in-game description for this move, as it appeared before Version 2.10.0 (with that version having been introduced on June 28, 2021), stated that it "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def."

Notably, Earth Power holds a unique distinction: it is the sole Move Tutor-taught ability whose Pokémon compatibility shifted within the same generation. Specifically, Sunkern and Sunflora gained the capacity to learn Earth Power through a Move Tutor in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, a learning option not present for them in Pokémon Platinum.

Title: Switcheroo (move)

Beyond this move, "Switcheroo" additionally serves as the designation for a facility in Festival Plaza tied to the Battle Agency. Known in Japan as すりかえ (Secret Switch), Switcheroo is a Dark-type status move, first appearing in Generation IV. Its core function is to cause the user and the chosen target to swap their held items. Should only one participant possess an item, that item is transferred to the other Pokémon.

Certain conditions cause Switcheroo to fail: if the target has the Sticky Hold Ability (even without an item), or if either Pokémon holds a Mega Stone usable for Mega Evolution, or a colored orb for Primal Reversion. It also fails if a Booster Energy is held by a Paradox Pokémon (excluding Koraidon, Miraidon, Gouging Fire, Raging Bolt, Iron Boulder, and Iron Crown) or if Ogerpon holds a Wellspring Mask, Hearthflame Mask, or Cornerstone Mask.

Within specific battle environments, such as Link Battles, various battle facilities, Secret Bases, and the Trainer House, any items exchanged via this move are automatically restored to their original owners once the encounter concludes. During earlier generations, Switcheroo would not succeed if either Pokémon had the Multitype Ability or if a Griseous Orb was held by either participant.

In a later shift, items swapped between a Trainer's Pokémon are no longer permanently exchanged; conversely, items switched in wild Pokémon encounters remain permanently transferred. A Griseous Orb can now be exchanged by Switcheroo; however, the move will still fail if a Griseous Orb is held and either of the Pokémon involved is Giratina. The move can also now swap items with a Pokémon possessing Multitype, yet it remains ineffective if a Plate is held and either Pokémon is Arceus.

When enhanced by a Darkinium Z to become Z-Switcheroo, the user gains a two-stage increase to its Speed stat. Subsequently, the item-swapping effect of the move itself fails, as Z-Crystals cannot be exchanged. In Max Raid Battles and Tera Raid Battles, Switcheroo invariably fails, irrespective of the items held or the target Pokémon. Nevertheless, it functions as usual against Dynamax Pokémon encountered in other battle formats.

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, a specific issue existed between versions 1.2.0 and 1.3.1: Switcheroo would erroneously fail if used by or against Groudon or Kyogre, provided either the user or the target lacked a held item. This was thought to be an oversight related to the removal of colored orbs. Version 1.3.2 resolved this anomaly.

In the Mystery Dungeon series, prior to Super Mystery Dungeon, the move would fail if one of the Pokémon involved did not possess an item. However, starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's execution remains successful even if either the user or the target lacks a held item. As of Generation IX, Switcheroo shares its animation sequence with the move Trick.

Title: Giga Impact (move)

Giga Impact (Japanese: ギガインパクト, Giga Impact), a damage-dealing Normal-type attack, was first introduced in Generation IV. It was available as Technical Machine 68 (TM68) from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is also found as TM68 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When used by Snorlax while holding Snorlium Z, this move transforms into the unique Z-Move, Pulverizing Pancake.

In the core series games, Giga Impact delivers substantial damage, but subsequently forces the user to spend the following turn recharging. This compulsory recharge occurs invariably, unless the attack itself misses. For Pokémon with the Truant ability, both the recharge period and the "loafing around" effect are processed within the same turn. The move's base power was adjusted to 120.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Giga Impact's effect diverges from a simple recharge; instead, it diminishes the user's action speed. Additionally, if the move connects successfully, the user is afflicted with a recoil status condition for one turn, preventing any actions. This condition was known as "Paused" in titles preceding Gates to Infinity.

As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Giga Impact became available starting November 1, 2019. In Pokémon Masters EX, Giga Impact's typing and other attributes can change based on the specific sync pair. For instance, it becomes a Fairy-type attack when employed by Lisia (New Year's 2023) & Rapidash (due to Fairy Shift), Volo & Togepi (Piqued Curiosity), and Adaman (Palentine's 2026) & Sylveon (Change It Up!), respectively. Liza (Summer 2023) & Celesteela utilize it as a Flying-type move, courtesy of their Flying Shift passive skill. Furthermore, Lance & Charizard's Dragon's Ascent skill not only makes it Flying-type but also guarantees it will never miss. Arc Suit Lance & Dragonite's passive, The Caped Dragon Master, transforms Giga Impact into a Dragon-type move that consumes only two move gauge slots and invariably hits its target. Lastly, Hilda (Champion) & Iron Leaves' Future-Honed Strike skill redefines it as a Psychic-type attack that requires only three move gauge slots to use.

Outside of gameplay, Oluolu's Snorlax was confirmed to know Giga Impact in "Pulling Out the Pokémon Base Pepper!" by employing Pulverizing Pancake, although Giga Impact itself was never visibly used. An official website also disclosed Cynthia's Garchomp as knowing Giga Impact, yet its usage has never been depicted. In the manga, specifically Omega Alpha Adventure 21, Sapphire commanded her Donphan, Phado, to use Giga Impact, but the action occurred off-panel.

A commonality exists between Giga Impact and Hyper Beam: the majority of Pokémon capable of learning one via TM can also learn the other. Notably, several Pokémon that had reached their final evolution in previous generations, but later received an additional evolution by Giga Impact's Generation IV introduction, such as Porygon or Seadra, are also able to acquire this move via TM.

Title: Nasty Plot (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Nasty Plot (known in Japanese as わるだくみ, or Wiles) is a non-damaging Dark-type technique. It is obtainable as TM63 within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. The primary effect of Nasty Plot is to significantly boost the user's Special Attack stat by two stages. In Contest Spectaculars, Nasty Plot contributes to a specific combination, awarding an extra three appeal points if either Baton Pass or Stored Power is performed on the following turn. When enhanced by a Darkinium Z into Z-Nasty Plot, it gains the ability to reset all of the user's decreased stats. Additionally, Nasty Plot elevates the user's offensive stats for a duration of five turns and improves their action speed.

Title: Bullet Punch (move)

Known in Japanese as バレットパンチ (Bullet Punch), this Steel-type attack was first introduced in Generation IV and is capable of inflicting damage. Bearing a priority value of +1, its execution occurs ahead of any moves that do not possess a boosted priority. Its maximum Power Points (PP) have been lowered to 20. Moreover, it serves to enhance the user's action speed. In congruence with other moves that formerly held priority status, the user performs a swift dash to make damaging physical contact with the adversary, though environmental terrain might hinder this action. Throughout this dash, the user becomes intangible, allowing them to disregard and pass through any projectiles and hazards without effect.

Title: Avalanche (move)

This entry refers to the move named Avalanche. For the location featured in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, please see Mt. Avalanche. The move Avalanche (Japanese: ゆきなだれ Avalanche) is an Ice-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation IV. It was assigned TM72 in Generation IV and Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, then TM64 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. It is currently designated as TM046 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Avalanche deals damage and is characterized as a decreased priority move, which means the user typically acts after other Pokémon that employ standard priority moves. Should the target have already wounded the user within the same turn, Avalanche's power doubles, escalating to 120. Using Avalanche confers the Bide status upon the user, preventing movement, attacking, or item usage. This status persists for 4 or 3 turns, and any damage sustained during this period is returned to the front enemy at a 2× or 1.5× multiplier. Avalanche is unavailable for use in a Pokémon Tower within the Adventure Squad series.

In Pokémon GO, Avalanche serves as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since February 16, 2017. A description of the move, present before Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), stated: The user readies themselves to counterattack. Activating this move again will cause the user to strike the target. While poised for counterattack, no other actions can be performed. If the user is hit by a move while prepared to counterattack, the user then retaliates against the target with double that power. This sync move could not be upgraded to 6★ EX status until December 25, 2021, a span of 849 days following its release date on August 29, 2019. The description for the 6★ EX effect prior to Version 2.18.5 read: "Additional Effects: Power of sync move increases by 50%." An earlier description, preceding Version 2.20.0, noted that this move's power would increase during a hailstorm.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Avalanche is Alolan Ninetales's initial move. It becomes available after evolving from Alolan Vulpix at level 4 by upgrading Powder Snow instead of Dazzling Gleam. The user discharges a gust of frigid ice toward a chosen direction. This ice both injures foes and establishes an impassable wall. After a brief period, this wall thaws and collapses, inflicting further damage upon enemies within its radius. At level 11 or above, the move briefly reduces the movement speed of affected enemies upon impact. Due to the influence of Snow Warning, Avalanche releases snow, aiding Alolan Ninetales in freezing opposing Pokémon. Owing to their staggered introduction times, Avalanche (Alud) and Rock Slide (Avalancha) possess Spanish game names that would be more accurate if reversed. Despite being an attack launched from a distance, Avalanche is categorized as a contact move. In Generations IV and V, Secret Power adopted Avalanche's animation when utilized on snow.

Title: Ice Shard (move)

Ice Shard (Japanese: こおりのつぶて, which translates to Ice Pebble) is an Ice-type offensive technique introduced in Generation IV, designed to inflict damage. This damaging maneuver holds a priority rating of +1, guaranteeing its activation before any abilities without elevated priority. Its Power Points (PP) are established at 20.

In Pokémon UNITE, Ice Shard functions as Swinub's inaugural move and additionally boosts the user's action speed. The Pokémon discharges three individual chunks of ice towards a specified location, dealing harm to opponents within the impact zone and momentarily reducing their movement velocity. If an enemy is hit by all three ice fragments, they become temporarily frozen. After Swinub evolves into Piloswine at level 5, this move is then replaced by either Icicle Crash or Ice Fang.

Title: Shadow Claw (move)

Known in Japanese as シャドークロー (Shadow Claw), this Ghost-type offensive technique debuted in Generation IV, inflicting damage upon foes. Its TM designation was TM65, spanning from Generation IV's introduction up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and reappearing as such in Generation VIII. Subsequently, in Generation IX, it acquired the TM061 identifier. Shadow Claw inflicts damage and possesses an enhanced critical hit ratio. Additionally, Shadow Claw participates in Contest Spectacular combinations; its execution awards an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was performed during the preceding turn. Regarding its offensive capabilities, the technique provides an elevated chance for a critical strike, specifically set at 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad titles.

Within the Pokémon UNITE arena, Shadow Claw serves as Aegislash's initial ability. Players acquire it upon evolving into Doublade at level 5, choosing to upgrade Shadow Sneak into Shadow Claw rather than Sacred Sword. The wielder executes two slashes with its blade in a specified direction, harming any adversaries struck. This is then followed by a concluding upward slash, which also deals damage to affected opponents and throws them. Should at least one of these three slashes connect, the boost count increments by one. Upon reaching level 11, Shadow Claw further augments the user's critical hit rate by 20% for a brief duration. Notably, Shadow Claw stands alone as the sole move to have kept its TM number consistent from Generation VII to Generation VIII.

Title: Thunder Fang (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Thunder Fang (Japanese: かみなりのキバ Thunder Fang) is an Electric-type attack that deals damage. It is considered a counterpart to Ice Fang and Fire Fang, sharing similar names and effects. Initially, this move carries a 10% chance of paralyzing the opponent. Furthermore, if the user acts before the target, there is an independent 10% chance for Thunder Fang to cause the target to flinch.

As a component of Contest Spectacular combinations, Thunder Fang allows the user to earn an extra three appeal points if the move Charge was utilized in the preceding turn. A notable change is that Thunder Fang is no longer affected by the item King's Rock.

Beyond these effects, Thunder Fang is also noted to have a 20% chance of paralyzing its target. It additionally lowers the target's action speed. In summary, this move delivers damage and holds the potential to paralyze or flinch an adversary. The Muzzled status condition impacts Thunder Fang.

Regarding Pokémon GO, Thunder Fang functions as a Fast Attack and has been available since September 27, 2019. Its description before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021) stated: "Has a very small chance of making the target flinch or leaving the target paralyzed."

Title: Ice Fang (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Ice Fang (Japanese: こおりのキバ Ice Fang) is an Ice-type attack designed to inflict damage. It is considered a direct counterpart to Fire Fang and Thunder Fang, sharing analogous names and mechanical effects. When employed, Ice Fang deals damage to the target and carries a 10% probability of leaving them frozen. Independently, if the user strikes before the opponent, there is an additional 10% chance for this move to cause the target to flinch. Furthermore, Ice Fang has a 20% chance to inflict the frostbite status condition and also reduces the target's action speed. In essence, the move delivers damage and possesses the potential to either freeze or flinch the enemy, or both. Its effects are subject to alteration by the Muzzled status condition.

Within Pokémon GO, Ice Fang functions as a Fast Attack, having been made available to players starting September 27, 2019. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), its accompanying in-game description read: "Has a very small chance of making the target flinch or leaving the target frozen."

In Pokémon UNITE, Ice Fang is Mamoswine's primary ability. It is acquired upon evolving into Piloswine at level 5, by choosing to upgrade Ice Shard into Ice Fang rather than Icicle Crash. The user utilizes fangs imbued with cold to grab and hurl the closest opposing Pokémon, dealing damage and momentarily freezing them. Subsequently, the user can specify a direction to slam the tossed Pokémon either forward or backward, damaging and briefly freezing enemies within the affected area. At level 11, the area of effect in which enemies are left frozen after this slam attack is expanded. Notably, Ice Fang holds the distinction of being the sole Ice-type move that is not classified as Beautiful.

Title: Fire Fang (move)

For information on the EX Dragon theme deck, please refer to FireFang (TCG). Fire Fang, known in Japanese as ほのおのキバ (Flame Fang), constitutes a damage-dealing Fire-type attack first introduced in Generation IV. It is often regarded as a counterpart to Ice Fang and Thunder Fang, sharing an affinity in both nomenclature and combat effects. This attack inflicts damage and possesses a 10% probability of leaving the target burned. Furthermore, it independently offers a 10% chance to make the opponent flinch, provided the user executes the move prior to the target's action.

A programming anomaly permits Fire Fang to invariably bypass Wonder Guard, irrespective of whether the target possesses a weakness to Fire-type attacks. The Muzzled status condition influences this technique's execution. Within Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, Fire Fang serves as a custom move exclusively for Charizard, taking the place of Flamethrower. Its application sees Charizard initially project a modest stream of fire from its maw. Subsequently, as the maneuver concludes, Charizard snaps its jaws shut, generating a minor explosion directly ahead.

A previous in-game description, valid before the release of Version 2.10.0 on June 28, 2021, noted: "Has a very small chance of making the target flinch. Has a very small chance of leaving the target burned." Notably, Fire Fang stands as the singular Fire-type move capable of inducing flinching in an opponent.

Title: Shadow Sneak (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, Shadow Sneak (known in Japan as かげうち, or Shadow Strike) is a Ghost-type attack that deals damage. This move possesses a +1 priority, meaning it is executed before any abilities lacking heightened priority. Its Power Points (PP) have been adjusted downwards to 20. Additionally, employing Shadow Sneak enhances the user's action speed.

As Greninja's side special in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, Shadow Sneak can be charged to cover a greater distance, with Greninja maintaining mobility while preparing the attack.

In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Shadow Sneak functions as a Charged Attack and has been part of the game since February 16, 2017.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Shadow Sneak is Aegislash's initial move and Decidueye's secondary move. Aegislash begins with this ability as Honedge, its base form. The Pokémon projects a shadow in a chosen direction, harming any foes it intercepts. Should this shadow strike an opponent or traverse its maximum range, the user instantly warps to that position, inflicting damage upon proximate adversaries and incrementing their boost count by 1. Upon Aegislash's evolution into Doublade at level 5, Shadow Sneak is exchanged for a choice between Sacred Sword or Shadow Claw.

Decidueye gains access to Shadow Sneak at level 8, opting to upgrade Astonish into it instead of Leaf Storm. Decidueye's version of the move manifests a shadow that seizes a single enemy within its operational zone, dealing damage. It also reduces the target's movement speed by 30% and their Defense by 60%, while simultaneously boosting the user's movement speed by 70% for 3 seconds. At level 13, the Defense reduction applied to the foe deepens to 80%.

Title: Psycho Cut (move)

Known in Japanese as サイコカッター (Psycho Cutter), Psycho Cut is a Psychic-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation IV. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it held the designation TM69. This move not only causes damage but also possesses an elevated chance of landing a critical hit. Should a Pokémon utilize Psycho Cut while possessing the Sharpness ability, the move's power receives a 50% boost. Furthermore, Psycho Cut serves as an element in Contest Spectacular combinations; its user will earn an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was performed during the preceding turn. Beyond its standard damage output, Psycho Cut carries an enhanced likelihood of striking critically, specifically boasting a 30% critical hit rate within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series.

Within Super Smash Bros. Brawl, Cresselia makes appearances on the Spear Pillar stage, frequently unleashing multiple Psycho Cuts across the area, and on occasion, launching a singular, large Psycho Cut that functions like a boomerang, damaging any combatant it contacts. This specific buddy move was formerly identified as B Psycho Cut before version 2.38.0. As part of Pokémon UNITE, Psycho Cut functions as Absol's secondary move. Players acquire it upon reaching level 7 by evolving Slash into this ability, rather than Sucker Punch. The user executes a broad frontal slash, which both harms and applies a debuff, along with a slowing effect, to affected Pokémon. This applied debuff causes additional damage for the subsequent 3 instances the user strikes the afflicted Pokémon with either an auto attack or another Psycho Cut. Upon reaching level 13, the user experiences an increase in movement speed when in proximity to debuffed adversaries.

During the events of Chasing to the Finish!, Gary instructed his Hatterene to perform Psycho Cut, though the execution of the move itself occurred off-screen. In Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, Psycho Cut stands alone as the sole physical Psychic-type move that does not require direct contact, excluding Z-Moves and Max Moves.

Title: Zen Headbutt (move)

Debuting in Generation IV, Zen Headbutt (known in Japanese as しねんのずつき, or Thought Headbutt) is a Psychic-type attack that deals damage. This particular technique carried the identification TR69 within Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the designation TM059 in Generation IX. Beyond its primary function of inflicting harm, it possesses a 20% probability of making the opposing Pokémon flinch. Its Power Points (PP) have been reduced to 10, and it is capable of decreasing the foe's action speed. This move delivers offensive power and carries the potential to cause the adversary to flinch. A previous description, in place before Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), stated its effect as: "Has a small chance of making the target flinch."

Title: Flash Cannon (move)

Flash Cannon (Japanese: ラスターカノン Luster Cannon) is a Steel-type damaging attack that first appeared in Generation IV. It was available as TM91 from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and also in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it was designated TM54. This move inflicts damage and carries a 10% chance of lowering the target's Special Defense by one stage. Additionally, it possesses a 20% probability of reducing the target's general defensive stats for three turns. It has also been described as inflicting damage and potentially reducing the target's Sp. Defense by one stage. An earlier description, valid until Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated it "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Sp. Def."

In Pokémon UNITE, Flash Cannon serves as Duraludon's initial move, obtained upon reaching level 7 by upgrading Laser Focus instead of Dragon Pulse. The user ruptures the ground in the designated direction, dealing damage to enemies within the area of effect and decreasing their movement speed for a short time. Following this, the user enters "Cannon Mode," becoming rooted for 6 seconds, or until a basic attack is not performed for 2.5 seconds. While in Cannon Mode, basic attacks gain extended range and inflict increased damage. Other moves such as Stealth Rock and Dragon Tail, along with boosted attacks, can be utilized in this state. Boosted attacks, when used in Cannon Mode, also decrease the movement speed of struck enemies for a brief duration. At level 11, enemies caught in the ruptured ground's area of effect are additionally rendered unable to act for 1 second.

Title: Rock Climb (move)

The attack known as Rock Climb (ロッククライム in Japanese) is a Normal-type damage-dealing maneuver that originated in Generation IV. During Generation IV, it served as HM08, and in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it holds the designation of TM100. When used in battle, Rock Climb deals damage and carries a 20% likelihood of leaving the opposing Pokémon confused. Though its battle functionality remains consistent across generations, Rock Climb previously required specific badges (the Earth Badge or Icicle Badge) for out-of-battle use in some titles. However, it can generally no longer be employed as a field move. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, its field utility is managed through the Pokétch's Hidden Moves app, which causes a wild Bibarel to appear and execute the action. Notably, Rock Climb shares with Rock Smash the unique distinction of being the only moves containing "rock" in their name without being classified as Rock-type. Both moves have also, at different times, held the status of an HM. A specific change in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl prevents the Gligar evolutionary line, the Zigzagoon evolutionary line, and Bidoof from learning Rock Climb, a move they could previously acquire through breeding in earlier installments.

Title: Defog (move)

This entry focuses on the move Defog. For information on the field move Demist, please refer to the Field Move article. Known in Japanese as きりばらい (Fog Wipe), Defog is a Flying-type move that does not inflict damage, first appearing in Generation IV. This move served as HM05 across the Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum versions, and later as TM97 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. To utilize Defog outside of combat, players require the Fen Badge in Diamond and Pearl, or the Relic Badge in Platinum. During battle, Defog decreases a target's evasion by one stage, a reduction that applies even when the opponent is protected by a substitute. Its strikes are guaranteed to connect, bypassing standard accuracy checks, except when the foe is executing a semi-invulnerable move like Dig or Fly. Beyond its evasion-lowering effect, Defog dissipates fog present on the battlefield. It also removes various effects from the opponent's side, including Light Screen, Reflect, Safeguard, Mist, Spikes, Toxic Spikes, and Stealth Rock. Subsequently, Defog gained the ability to be reflected by Magic Coat, and it ceased lowering the evasion of Pokémon shielded by a substitute. Its utility expanded to clear Spikes, Toxic Spikes, Stealth Rock, and Sticky Web from both the user's and the target's field sides. Furthermore, in addition to Light Screen, Reflect, Safeguard, and Mist, Defog additionally clears Aurora Veil, but exclusively from the opposing side of the field. When augmented by a Flyinium Z into Z-Defog, the move confers a one-stage boost to the user's accuracy. The move's functionality also encompasses clearing terrains and the lingering effects of G-Max Steelsurge. Certain game iterations mandate a specific gym badge—either the Fen Badge or the Relic Badge—for Defog's deployment outside of battle. Out of combat, Defog disperses fog within the immediate area, an effect that persists until the player exits that location. In titles spanning from HeartGold and SoulSilver up to Sword and Shield, and throughout Generation IX, Defog reduces the evasiveness of all adversaries present in the current area by a single stage. Within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, it additionally eradicates Mist, Safeguard, Reflect, or Light Screen status conditions from foes, alongside any existing foggy weather on the dungeon floor. The description preceding Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) stated: 'Clears the entire field of terrain effects. Clears all field effects from the opponents' field of play. Lowers the target's evasiveness.' Before Version 2.16.1 (effective December 27, 2021), the description was: 'Clears the entire field of terrain effects. Clears all field effects from the opponents' field of play. Lowers the target's evasiveness by one stat rank.' The description before Version 2.23.5 (dating August 8, 2022) read: 'Clears the entire field of terrain and zone effects. Clears all field effects from the opponents' field of play. Lowers the target's evasiveness by one stat rank.' A notable error in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum involved the Bag sprite for the HM housing Defog, which incorrectly depicted a Water-type HM instead of the correct Flying-type icon. Much like Flash in Generations I, II, and III, Defog is not essential for game completion; players can navigate foggy zones without employing the move. Nonetheless, contrasting with dark caverns that necessitated Flash, foggy environments uniquely impact battles occurring within them. The animated series has occasionally depicted Gust and Whirlwind performing functions akin to Defog. Should a Pokémon learn Defog via HM in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum and subsequently be traded to Pokémon HeartGold or SoulSilver, it can then be freely transferred to Pokémon Black or White using the Poké Transfer system. This is permissible because Defog's out-of-battle utility is not required in HeartGold and SoulSilver. A similar process applies to Whirlpool: a Pokémon taught this move by HM in HeartGold or SoulSilver can be moved to Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum. Although having the capacity to learn Defog in earlier generations, several Pokémon—specifically Charizard, Ledian, the entire Torchic evolutionary line, Seedot, Nuzleaf, Volbeat, Illumise, the Swablu evolutionary line, Castform, Chimecho, Rayquaza, Carnivine, and Rotom—are unable to acquire Defog in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Kartana's proficiency with Defog potentially alludes to origami pinwheels, aligning with Kartana's design inspiration from origami.

Title: Trick Room (move)

This entry focuses on the move Trick Room; for information regarding the Festival Plaza facility, consult Festival Plaza → Haunted houses. Known in Japanese as トリックルーム (Trick Room), this Psychic-type move debuted in Generation IV and inflicts no damage. It served as TM92 from Generation IV up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Trick Room alters the turn order *within* individual priority brackets, allowing Pokémon possessing a lower Speed stat to act before those with a higher Speed stat. Crucially, distinct priority brackets remain intact; moves belonging to a higher bracket will consistently execute prior to those in lower ones, irrespective of Trick Room's effect. The effect persists for five turns, with the turn it is used being counted as the first. Much like Magic Room and Wonder Room, activating Trick Room when it is already active will cause it to conclude immediately. Certain effects that modify the sequence within particular priority brackets take precedence over Trick Room. Consequently, a Pokémon equipped with a Full Incense or Lagging Tail, or one possessing the Stall Ability, will move last within its priority bracket, irrespective of its Speed stat. Conversely, Pokémon whose Quick Claw activates will proceed first in their priority bracket. With a priority of -7, Trick Room is executed after all moves having normal priority.

Beginning with Generation V, a specific anomaly arises in Speed calculation under Trick Room's influence, affecting Pokémon with an effective Speed value between 1809 and 8192. Specifically, the Speed is calculated such that a Pokémon with an effective Speed of 1808 will see it reduced to 0, whereas one with an effective Speed of 1 will have it boosted to 1807. Intriguingly, at an effective Speed of 1809, the modulo component of the function yields 8191, resulting in the Pokémon acting first even within the Trick Room environment. Subsequent values then diminish as anticipated, making a Pokémon with an effective Speed of 1809 quicker than those both below and above this threshold. Furthermore, a Pokémon at an effective Speed of 8193 will exhibit the same Speed as one at an effective Speed of 1 (though attaining such extreme Speed statistics is highly improbable in standard gameplay scenarios). When amplified by a Psychium Z into Z-Trick Room, the move grants the user a one-stage accuracy boost.

Within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, Trick Room affects all Pokémon present on the floor, randomly either increasing or decreasing their Movement Speed by a single stage, regardless of whether they have already reached maximum or minimum limits. Beginning with Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, Trick Room inverts any modifications to Travel Speed. This dungeon status persists until the player's team advances to the subsequent floor. However, it does not influence the permanent Travel Speed enhancement of opposing Kecleon. In contrast to the core series titles, re-using Trick Room does not terminate its effect.

Pokémon Battle Revolution features a distinct animation for Trick Room, wherein a sphere composed of blinking and fading purple squares encircles the battle area. Will's room in HeartGold and SoulSilver displays a comparable visual effect. An animation also occurs upon its expiration or cancellation. Within HeartGold, SoulSilver, and Platinum, a roulette selection in the Battle Arcade can occasionally impose the Trick Room effect on a battle. Among all moves, Trick Room possesses the absolute lowest priority, a characteristic it shared with Magic Room and Wonder Room during Generation V. In the episode "Fairy-Type Trickery!", Trick Room is depicted as destructible through direct attack, a mechanic absent from the games.

Title: Draco Meteor (move)

Known in Japanese as りゅうせいぐん (Meteor Shower), Draco Meteor is a Dragon-type attack capable of dealing damage, first introduced in Generation IV. It holds the designation of TM169 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM054 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used, Draco Meteor strikes its target but simultaneously causes the user's Special Attack to drop by two stages. The power of Draco Meteor has been adjusted to 110, and this reduction in the user's offensive stats now persists for three turns.

A variety of Move Tutors are capable of instructing a Dragon-type Pokémon in Draco Meteor. For Generations IV through VII, a Pokémon also needed to possess a maximum friendship rating. Certain Pokémon, such as Arceus when holding the Draco Plate, or Silvally equipped with the Dragon Memory, can also learn this move. However, in Pokémon Sword and Shield and Pokémon Legends: Arceus, any iteration of Arceus or Silvally is eligible to be taught the technique. Pokémon that only achieve Dragon typing through temporary transformations like Mega Evolution, Ultra Burst, Terastallization, or Abilities such as Protean and Libero, are ineligible, as their Dragon typing cannot be accessed outside of battle.

Specific Move Tutors include:
- In DPPt and BDSP: Grandma Wilma, found within her residence on Route 210.
- In SMUSUM: A female Ace Trainer located inside the Pokémon Center in Seafolk Village.
- In SwSh: An elderly gentleman situated in the Hero's Bath in Circhester.
Draco Meteor deals damage to all opposing entities within the room, subsequently decreasing the user's Special Attack by two ranks.

While Draco Meteor was exclusively a tutor move in the main series games prior to Generation IX, its acquisition methods differ within the Mystery Dungeon series:
- In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, all eligible Dragon-type Pokémon can acquire Draco Meteor upon reaching level 72, provided they have accumulated at least 300 IQ points. If this condition is not met by level 72, the move can still be relearned at the Electivire Link Shop.
- For Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, Draco Meteor is learnable by all Dragon-type Pokémon via TM. This TM becomes available once the player's Paradise achieves a Perfect Rank.
- In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, following the completion of the epilogue's narrative arc, any Dragon-type Pokémon can be taught Draco Meteor at Hawlucha's Slam School for a fee of 11,000.
- In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, the move is available for instruction at the Gulpin Link Shop, priced at 11,000, after the main story has been finished.

Within Pokémon GO, Draco Meteor functions as a Charged Attack, having been made available since February 9, 2018. Its in-game description before Version 2.10.0 (which launched on June 28, 2021) stated that it "Sharply lowers the user's Sp. Atk."

In Pokémon Masters EX:
- Steven (Anniversary 2021) & Rayquaza's passive skill, Downside Up, allows this move to *increase* their Sp. Atk by two stat ranks after execution, instead of reducing it.
- Drasna & Dragalge's passive skill Team Sp. Atk ↓ Immunity, and Ryuki & Turtonator's passive skill Dauntless, both prevent the move from lowering their Sp. Atk post-use.
- A specific sync move associated with Draco Meteor could not be raised to 6★ EX status until July 29, 2022, marking 911 days since its release on January 30, 2020.
- Zinnia & Rayquaza's power enhancement from this attack is solely attainable via the villain event "Team Aqua's Stormy Seas." Notably, the Dragon Zone, which typically amplifies Dragon-type attacks, must *not* be active for this specific attack's condition to be satisfied.
- The main description for this move prior to Version 2.16.1 (issued on December 27, 2021) read: "Become Mega Altaria until the end of battle. This move's power increases if there are no field effects on the opponents' field of play and also no effects on the entire field of play, including weather or terrain effects."
- The description for the 6★ EX effect before Version 2.18.5 (released on March 15, 2022) stated: "Additional Effects: Power of sync move increases by 50%."
- From Version 2.16.1 to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022), the main description was: "Become Mega Altaria until the end of battle. This move's power increases when there are no field effects on the opponents' field of play and also no effects on the entire field of play, including weather, terrain, or zone effects."
- Intriguingly, Miracle Idol Draco Meteor stands as the sole Dragon-type attack in Pokémon Masters EX that is weaker within a Dragon Zone (dealing 1.5× damage) than outside it (where it inflicts 2× damage if no field effects are present).

In November 2005, preceding the launch of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, it was announced that the January issue of the Japanese magazine CoroCoro Comics the following year would host a competition. Fans were invited to submit name proposals for a new Dragon-type move. By May of that same year, りゅうせいぐん Ryūseigun, which was later localized as Draco Meteor, was declared the winning submission. The "りゅう" (ryū) component of its Japanese name translates to "dragon," likely inspiring the move's Dragon typing. The English name, Draco Meteor, is thought to allude to the Draconids, a meteor shower named after the constellation Draco (Latin for dragon), from which they appear to originate.

Title: Discharge (move)

Discharge (Japanese: ほうでん, which translates to Electrical Discharge) is an Electric-type attack, first appearing in Generation IV, that inflicts damage. This move holds the TM039 designation within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used in Double Battles, Discharge strikes all combatants on the field apart from the user, with each hit carrying a 30% probability of causing paralysis. In Triple Battles, its effect extends to all Pokémon positioned adjacently to the user. Furthermore, Discharge can form part of a Contest Spectacular combination; its user earns an additional three appeal points if the move Charge preceded it in the previous turn. It inflicts harm upon foes located within the same room. Paralysis may result from its use. When employed by Iono & Kilowattrel, this technique possesses perfect accuracy, never missing thanks to their passive skill, Piercing Gaze. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), its in-game description stated: "Has a moderate chance of leaving the target paralyzed." Within Pokémon UNITE, Discharge functions as Zeraora's secondary offensive option. Players acquire it at level 8 by evolving Slash into Discharge, as an alternative to Wild Charge. The user unleashes an electrical aura, harming all opposing Pokémon caught within its radius while simultaneously providing a shield to the user upon making contact. At the move's conclusion, any paralyzed adversaries within its effective range are drawn toward the user, suffering damage and becoming stunned. Upon reaching level 14, this move also imparts a slowing effect on opponents. Although Ghetsis's Eelektross was disclosed on the official website to possess knowledge of Discharge, it has never been depicted actually employing the attack. Intriguingly, the designation of this move shares its name with Manectric's Pokémon category.

Title: Lava Plume (move)

First appearing in Generation IV, Lava Plume (Japanese: ふんえん Volcanic Plume) is an offensive Fire-type attack. In Double and Triple Battles, it harms all adjacent Pokémon, with each target also facing a 30% chance of being burned. This move can also demolish spherical rocks observed in the battle's backdrop. Broadly, Lava Plume inflicts damage on proximate opponents and might impose a burn. In PASV16, Mela's Torkoal attempted to unleash Lava Plume, but Violet's Clodsire's Mud Shot disrupted the action. Commencing with Generation VI, Lava Plume shares its in-game description text with Searing Shot.

Title: Leaf Storm (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, the Grass-type attack known as Leaf Storm (リーフストーム in Japanese) inflicts damage. This move also causes the user's Special Attack to drop by two stages immediately after use. Furthermore, it imposes a reduction on the user's offensive capabilities for a duration of three turns. Its power, which has been lowered, now stands at 110, consistently delivering damage and reducing the user's Special Attack by two stages. Trainers could acquire it as TR71 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and it currently serves as TM159 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Transitioning to Pokémon GO, Leaf Storm operates as a Charged Attack, having been introduced into the game on April 12, 2021. Its in-game description before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, stated that it "Sharply lowers the user's Sp. Atk."

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Leaf Storm is designated as Decidueye's inaugural move. Players unlock it upon reaching level 8, choosing to evolve Astonish into Leaf Storm instead of Shadow Sneak. Decidueye unleashes a violent gust of sharp foliage, injuring foes caught in its path and reducing their movement speed by 30% for a brief period. If used at extremely close quarters, the move additionally shoves and stuns any affected adversaries. Upon achieving level 13, the duration of this movement speed impediment is extended.

Before the advent of Generation VI, it held the unique distinction of being the sole Grass-type move classified as "Cute." A notable anomaly occurs in Pokémon Black and White: School Kid Ann, encountered on Route 12, possesses a Sandy Cloak Wormadam that utilizes Leaf Storm. This is peculiar, as Sandy Cloak Wormadam is incapable of learning this attack through any method; it is, in fact, a move exclusive to Plant Cloak Wormadam. The Sandy Cloak Wormadam in question ought to properly know Fissure instead of Leaf Storm.

Title: Rock Wrecker (move)

Rock Wrecker, known in Japanese as がんせきほう (Rock Cannon), is an offensive Rock-type move that inflicts damage, first introduced in Generation IV. This attack serves as Rhyperior's signature move during Generation IV and Generation IX. When unleashed, Rock Wrecker deals damage but subsequently forces the user to spend the following turn recharging. This compulsory recharge will always occur, provided the attack successfully connects. Pokémon endowed with the Bulletproof Ability are completely immune to the effects of Rock Wrecker. If Rock Wrecker lands successfully, the executing Pokémon will be afflicted with the 'recoil' status condition for one turn; prior to Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, this state was referred to as 'Paused.' This specific status completely prevents the user from taking any actions. In Pokémon GO, Rock Wrecker functions as a Charged Attack and has been available since February 22, 2020. Excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves, Rock Wrecker shares the distinction with Head Smash for possessing the highest base power among all Rock-type moves. A Continental Crush, when based on either Rock Wrecker or Head Smash, holds the absolute highest base power across all Rock-type moves.

Title: Cross Poison (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Cross Poison (Japanese: クロスポイズン Cross Poison) is a damaging Poison-type technique. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it is designated as Technical Machine 73 (TM73). This attack delivers damage and carries an elevated chance of landing a critical hit, which is specified as 30% in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. Furthermore, it has a 10% probability of inflicting the poisoned status condition on the target.

Beyond battles, Cross Poison can contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, granting the user an extra three appeal points if Focus Energy was previously employed during the prior turn.

Earlier descriptions include: prior to Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), it was stated: "Critical hits land more easily. Has a very small chance of leaving the target poisoned." As a buddy move, its former designation was B Cross Poison before version 2.38.0. An additional prior description, preceding Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022), noted: "This move's power increases when the target is poisoned or badly poisoned."

When Emma & Crobat utilize this move, their passive skill Critical Sync ensures it will always be a critical hit, save for specific scenarios. Cross Poison is unique in its variation, being the only one to possess a secondary effect.

Title: Gunk Shot (move)

Introduced in Generation IV, Gunk Shot (Japanese: ダストシュート Dust Shoot) is a Poison-type attack capable of inflicting damage. This technique also possesses a 30% probability of poisoning its target. In the episode "Camping It Up!", Dawn's Pokédex revealed that the Grimer she used during the Pokémon Summer Academy knew Gunk Shot; however, the move was never actually seen being performed. Excluding Venoshock when it hits a poisoned foe and all Z-Moves, Gunk Shot shares with Belch the highest base power among all Poison-type moves. Nevertheless, an Acid Downpour Z-Move, when derived from either Gunk Shot or Belch, holds the ultimate base power across all Poison-type attacks.

Title: Iron Head (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Iron Head (Japanese: アイアンヘッド, romanized as Iron Head) is a Steel-type damaging attack. This move delivers direct harm and carries a 30% probability of causing the opposing Pokémon to flinch. Its PP capacity has been adjusted to 10. Beyond its direct damage, it also lowers the target's action speed. In summary, Iron Head causes damage and possesses the potential to make the foe flinch.

A unique interaction occurs with the Legendary Pokémon Zacian and Zamazenta: For these Pokémon to transform into their Crowned Sword and Crowned Shield forms, they are required to possess the move Iron Head. Should either Zacian or Zamazenta know Iron Head upon entering a battle while in their respective Crowned forms, the move will transform into Behemoth Blade (for Zacian) or Behemoth Bash (for Zamazenta), remaining as such throughout the battle's duration. This transformation means the original Iron Head is not directly used but becomes its signature counterpart.

An earlier description, prior to Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), noted that the move had a "moderate chance of making the target flinch."

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Iron Head serves as Aegislash's secondary combat option. It becomes available when Aegislash reaches level 8, evolving from Iron Defense instead of Wide Guard. Activating this move causes the user to lunge in a chosen direction, dealing damage to any foes encountered. It also grants a boost count increment of 1 and temporarily bestows a protective shield upon the user. While this shield offers no protection against hindrances, it possesses the ability to nullify incoming damage. If this shield successfully blocks an incoming attack, it dissipates, and the user's boost count increases by 1 once more. Furthermore, upon reaching level 13, blocking an attack with this move will temporarily raise the user's Attack stat by 10%.

The anime series Sword and Shield: The Legends Awaken! confirmed Zacian and Zamazenta's knowledge of Iron Head through their respective demonstrations of Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash; however, they have never been depicted using Iron Head itself. Similarly, in Fwoom!! Goodbye, Galar, Zacian and Zamazenta's proficiency with Iron Head was also established via their use of Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash, yet direct usage of Iron Head by them has not been observed.

Title: Stone Edge (move)

The Rock-type attack known as Stone Edge (ストーンエッジ in Japanese) made its debut in Generation IV, serving as a damage-dealing technique. It was available as TM71 across several titles, specifically from Generation IV through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again featured as TM71 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. For players in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, equipping a Lycanium Z to Lycanroc enables Stone Edge to transform into the unique Z-Move, Splintered Stormshards. This move inflicts damage upon its target and boasts an elevated chance of landing a critical hit. Furthermore, Stone Edge functions within Contest Spectacular combinations; should Focus Energy have been performed on the preceding turn, the user accrues an additional three appeal points. Visually, Stone Edge manifests as projectiles that erupt from the terrain. However, it will fail to connect if the chosen target is situated excessively high above the ground. Beyond its damaging effect, Stone Edge inherently possesses an improved critical hit rate, specifically a 30% chance in the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. Post-Mega Evolution, Hilda (Special Costume) & Diancie benefit from their passive skill, Fuel Economy 1, which reduces Stone Edge's usage cost to merely two move gauge slots.

Title: Captivate (move)

A Normal-type technique without offensive capabilities, Captivate (ゆうわく, or Allure in Japanese) made its debut in Generation IV. During its introductory generation, it was designated as Technical Machine 78. This move reduces the target's Special Attack by two stages, contingent on the target being of the opposite gender to the Pokémon using the move. The move's effect extends to all nearby adversaries. Pokémon lacking a defined gender, exemplified by Magnemite, alongside those possessing the Oblivious Ability, are immune to its effects. Furthermore, an attempt to use Captivate by a gender-unknown Pokémon will consistently result in failure. Should Captivate be upgraded to Z-Captivate via a Normalium Z, the user's Special Defense stat will ascend by two stages. Ultimately, Captivate diminishes the foe's Sp. Attack by 2 stages. The prerequisite for the user to be the opposite gender of the target was a condition for Captivate's functionality until Gates to Infinity, after which this specific requirement was later eliminated. Mew stands alone among gender-unknown Pokémon in its capacity to learn Captivate; its proficiency in learning all TMs, specifically TM78 in Generation IV, accounts for this. Nincada's inability to acquire Captivate is likely attributed to the game's mechanics: any move known by Nincada is passed on to Shedinja upon evolution into Ninjask, which would equip the gender-unknown Shedinja with a futile move. An identical situation exists with Attract.

Title: Stealth Rock (move)

Referred to in Japanese as ステルスロック (Stealth Rock), this Rock-type technique was introduced in Generation IV and causes no direct damage. It was designated as TM76 in Generation IV and in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, then as TM56 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Stealth Rock establishes an entry hazard on the opposing side of the battlefield. Any Pokémon entering the battle on that side will sustain damage. The damage taken upon switching in is contingent on the Rock type's effectiveness against the target, beginning at 1/8 of the target's maximum HP under normal effectiveness. Stealth Rock effects do not stack. Pokémon with the Magic Guard Ability are unaffected by its damage. Should Rapid Spin successfully connect with its target, it will clear Stealth Rock from the user's side of the field; conversely, Defog, when successfully applied to its target, removes Stealth Rock's influence from the opponent's side. The damage calculation is based on the victim's maximum HP, and the result is rounded down, though a minimum of 1 HP damage is always dealt if the calculation yields zero. Given that Castform's transformation occurs after entry hazards apply their effects, it consistently receives 12.5% damage from Stealth Rock, regardless of the active weather. Strong winds do not affect Stealth Rock. In an Inverse Battle, Stealth Rock calculates damage by reversing the Rock type's weaknesses and resistances.

This move can contribute to a Contest Spectacular combination, providing an extra three appeal points if followed by Circle Throw, Dragon Tail, Roar, or Whirlwind on the subsequent turn. Any Pokémon summoned for help during SOS Battles is also susceptible to Stealth Rock. When powered up by a Rockium Z to become Z-Stealth Rock, the user's Defense stat will increase by one stage. Both G-Max Stonesurge and Stone Axe have the secondary effect of setting Stealth Rock on the opponent's side of the field.

Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Stealth Rock operates as a physical attack, delivering direct damage with a base power of 40. It also afflicts the target with splinters for 4 turns (Strong Style), 2 turns (Agile Style), or 3 turns (otherwise). Here, Stealth Rock manifests as floating stones appearing in front of the user. Opponent Pokémon touching these stones incur damage equivalent to ⌊RockEffectiveness×MaxHP8⌋, with a guaranteed minimum of 1 point. Because it is a damage-dealing move in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Rock-type Pokémon benefit from STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus) when utilizing Stealth Rock.

In other game contexts, Stealth Rock permits the user to create a Stealth Rock trap directly beneath them, provided the tile is a land tile and neither stairs nor Kecleon Shop carpet. This trap cannot be placed in hallways or at a room's entrance. In the Explorers series, any Pokémon activating a Stealth Rock trap sustains damage equal to 12.5% of their maximum HP, irrespective of the type matchup, and Rock-type Pokémon are immune. However, in Gates to Infinity, damage is now affected by type, and Rock-type Pokémon are no longer immune. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the damage inflicted ranges: 7.4% of the user's max HP if both of its types resist Rock, 8.4% if one type resists Rock, 9.6% for neutral Rock-type damage, 11.4% if weak against Rock, and 13.6% if both types are weak against Rock.

In Pokémon UNITE, Stealth Rock serves as the second move option for both Crustle and Duraludon. Crustle gains access to this ability at level 6 by evolving Fury Cutter into Stealth Rock rather than X-Scissor. Crustle's version involves the user aiming and creating a radius where rocks strike enemies 8 times. Each hit applies a debuff stack, causing subsequent hits to deal an additional 15% damage, which is always rounded upwards. While Stealth Rock can critically hit, the user's inherent critical hit chance is 0%. Upon reaching level 13, this assault strikes two additional times. Duraludon acquires Stealth Rock at level 7 by upgrading Metal Claw into it, as an alternative to Dragon Tail. Duraludon's version entails the user launching a stone at a designated area, establishing an unseen field of stones that persists for 60 seconds or until triggered. This field inflicts continuous damage on foes within its area of effect and reduces their movement speed by 15% for 2 seconds. Should the thrown stone impact an opponent prior to reaching the chosen area, the field will instead originate from the point of impact on that Pokémon. As soon as an enemy sustains damage from this field, its presence becomes visible to all opponents. An enemy struck by this move 4 times will be rendered immobile for 1 second. Following the use of this move, the user's subsequent basic attack gains a boost. Up to 2 uses of this move can be stored in reserve. A 1.1-second cooldown period exists between each activation. While a maximum of 3 Stealth Rocks can be active on the battlefield simultaneously, an additional 3 may still be held in reserve. At level 13, this move gains the capacity to store an additional reserved use.

Title: Grass Knot (move)

Referred to in Japanese as くさむすび (Grass Knot), this damage-dealing Grass-type move made its debut in Generation IV and is characterized by its variable damage output. It served as TM86 across multiple titles, specifically from Generation IV all the way to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is also present as TM86 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Grass Knot does not possess a fixed base power; rather, its damage value is directly computed based on the target's weight.
