The game exclusively employs kilograms, not pounds, for all the weight cutoffs used in this power calculation. When targeting species that exhibit varying heights and weights, Grass Knot disregards these individual differences, considering only the base weight assigned to that species within the Pokédex. Furthermore, Grass Knot will invariably fail if it attempts to strike a Dynamaxed opponent. The heavier the target, the more significant the damage dealt by Grass Knot. A distinct damage calculation method applies to this move within the Explorers and Adventure Squad game series. In the context of Pokémon GO, Grass Knot is categorized as a Charged Attack, having become available as of February 16, 2017.

Title: Chatter (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Chatter (Japanese: おしゃべり Chatter) is a damage-dealing Flying-type move. As Chatot's signature attack, it inflicts damage and possesses the potential to confuse its target. Across Generations IV and V, players could leverage Chatter as a field move to record audio, a mechanic directly influencing its confusion chance. When Chatter contained recorded audio via this field functionality, that specific sound recording would substitute Chatot's standard cry as the move's sound effect. In Triple Battles, Chatter is unique in its ability to strike opponents that are not directly adjacent.

In Generations IV and V, if Chatter had no recorded audio data, its confusion chance stood at 1%. With recorded audio, the probability of inflicting confusion varied between 1%, 11%, or 31%, contingent on the specific recording. Should any Pokémon other than Chatot attempt to use Chatter, the confusion chance invariably became 0%. For these generations, Serene Grace did not impact the confusion odds, but Shield Dust did.

Subsequent game modifications altered these mechanics: a Chatter without audio data now carried a 0% confusion chance. If recorded audio was present, the chance to confuse became either 0% or 10%, depending on the recording. The likelihood of confusion was then influenced by Shield Dust, Serene Grace, Sheer Force, and the combined effect of Water Pledge and Fire Pledge. Furthermore, Chatter was later revised to consistently confuse the target in addition to dealing damage. The move's field functionality for recording audio outside of battle was removed. Despite this removal, Chatter remains immune to Sketch, though it can still be copied by Mirror Move.

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, Chatter cannot be selected for use in battle. During Generations IV and V, Chatter served as a field move, allowing audio recording via the Nintendo DS's microphone. This captured audio would subsequently play as Chatot's cry, both generally and specifically during Chatter's execution. Recorded audio for Chatter underwent distortion, presumably as a safeguard against the playback of inappropriate language. Should all Chatots be removed from the player's party—for instance, by depositing them into the PC or trading them—any recorded audio would revert to Chatot's default cry. Exclusively in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, an oversight meant depositing Chatot into the PC would not reset its cry. The reset would only occur if the player initiated a trade, placed a Pokémon in the daycare, or offered a Pokémon on the GTS, and subsequently no longer had a Chatot in their party.

Throughout Generation IV and V, the probability of inflicting confusion correlated with the recording's volume; higher volumes generally equated to a greater chance of confusion. The recording was stored within the game as a 1000-byte, 4-bit PCM (pulse-code modulation) structure. When audio was captured, 1984 8-bit samples of microphone input were collected at approximately 2000 samples per second. Sixteen zero bytes were then appended to form a 2000-byte 8-bit PCM recording, which was subsequently downsized to a 4-bit PCM recording using a translation table where the first 4-bit sample occupied the lower four bits of a byte, and the second occupied the upper four. Depending on the value of the 16th byte in the 1000-byte 4-bit PCM recording (which corresponded to the 31st and 32nd samples of the original input), the chance of confusion was determined as follows, with corresponding approximate volume levels provided in a table. Notably, a sustained high volume throughout the recording was not necessary, as this critical sample was located near its beginning. If Chatter has no audio data, the chance of confusion is 0%.

Generation V games adopted a different recording method, capturing 8000 8-bit samples at 8000 samples per second. From these, every fourth sample, starting with the first, was converted into the 1000-byte 4-bit PCM recording, mirroring the Generation IV conversion process. However, instead of a single byte dictating the confusion odds, these were determined by the exclusive OR (XOR) of three bytes: the 100th, 500th, and 700th bytes (corresponding to the 397th, 401st, 1997th, 2001st, 2797th, and 2801st samples of the initial recording). The odds of confusion were calculated as outlined. Consequently, maintaining a medium or high volume for approximately the first quarter-second of the recording was crucial for achieving maximum confusion probability.

Chatter deals damage and retains the potential to confuse the target. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, however, it consistently inflicts confusion on any targets it strikes. The Muzzled status condition affects Chatter, and as a sound-based move, it is nullified by the Soundproof Ability. Chatter is one of the rare core series moves that cannot be Sketched. This is likely because, in its original field move capacity, if Chatter was used by a Pokémon other than Chatot, that Pokémon's cry would not temporarily adopt the recorded version even if audio was captured. This is due to Chatter's audio recording being programmed to switch the user to an alternative cry, and only Chatot is coded to access its alternative cry from the recorded audio data. Despite these complexities, Chatter remains un-sketchable even across Generations VI, VII, and VIII, where vocal recording capabilities were removed.

Prior to the discontinuation of Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection, Chatot was banned from the Pokémon Global Battle Union, presumably because Chatter was exploited to record vulgar language. This misuse is also believed to be the reason for the move's audio recording ability being removed, starting with Generation VI. In Pokémon X and Y, Chatter's description inaccurately mentioned its field move functionality, despite sound recording no longer being possible; this textual inconsistency was rectified beginning with Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. Uniquely, Chatter stands as the sole non-HM field move from the original Pokémon Diamond and Pearl that does not retain its field move status in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Judgment (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Judgment (known in Japanese as さばきのつぶて, or Judgment Pebble) is a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage. This attack is Arceus's signature move, and its type changes based on the Plate the user holds. Judgment delivers damage without any additional effects. The move's type correlates directly with the held Plate's type; if no Plate is equipped, it defaults to Normal-type. Should Magic Room be active, Judgment's type invariably becomes Normal, irrespective of any held Plate. Effects from Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, and Aerilate do not influence Judgment. Conversely, Ion Deluge (when Judgment would otherwise be Normal-type) and Electrify can affect it. Even when the user holds a Z-Crystal, Judgment's type remains Normal and is not altered. Though programmed into Pokémon Sword and Shield, Judgment is unusable in battle because no learnable Pokémon exist to select it. Nevertheless, Metronome can summon it. Lacking an animation, its usage with battle effects active will cause the game to crash. Its functionality mirrors that of previous generations.

In *Pokémon Legends: Arceus*, Judgment's Power Points (PP) were decreased to 5. Its type in this game is now dictated by the last Plate Arceus interacted with, causing the move to align with Arceus's current type. When a different Pokémon employs Judgment via Mimic, it will consistently be Normal-type, irrespective of the type it possessed when Mimic copied it. Distinct from other titles, Judgment features a subtly varied animation depending on which of the eighteen types it is currently manifesting. The move's operational mechanics undergo a significant alteration if the Legend Plate was the last Plate Arceus interacted with. During combat, Arceus acquires the capacity to modify its own type when executing Judgment, adapting to the selected foe's type. Upon move selection, Judgment indicates the type it will assume when targeting the chosen opponent during that turn. This mechanic bears resemblance to Unown's unique move, Hidden Power. Judgment transforms into a type that is super effective against the target, with priority given to any double weaknesses present in dual-type adversaries. Should several types prove equally effective, Judgment will convert to the type offering the strongest resistance to the target's primary type, prioritizing immunities, regardless of the target's secondary typing. For instance, against Palkia, whose primary type is Water, Judgment invariably becomes Dragon. Similarly, when facing Giratina, with its primary Ghost type, Judgment consistently becomes Dark. While Fairy is immune to their secondary Dragon typing, it offers no resistance to Water or Ghost. If multiple valid types remain, Judgment will then shift to the type that best resists the target's secondary type, if one exists. Failing that, a type is chosen randomly. This calculation occurs every turn, meaning Judgment's type might fluctuate among valid options each turn. Arceus initiates battle as a Normal type. When Judgment is employed, Arceus first morphs into the chosen type for the move, then executes the attack. Consequently, Judgment invariably benefits from a same-type attack bonus. Arceus will undergo this transformation even if Judgment fails to hit the opponent. However, Arceus will not transform if the move's execution is prevented by paralysis or the drowsy status condition. Arceus additionally reverts to its Normal type upon the battle's conclusion. The game includes a mechanism (often represented as a table) outlining Judgment's resulting type for every possible type combination. It's worth noting that certain type combinations allow for multiple potential outcomes, from which Judgment randomly selects one. As Judgment's type is solely contingent on the held Plate, Terastallizing into a type distinct from the Plate's will not alter its type.

Although neither Arceus nor Judgment are legitimately acquireable before the WiiWare titles, the move's data is present in *Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky*—Japanese releases retained its original name, さばきのつぶて, whereas English versions utilized the placeholder "$$$". Both linguistic variants share the same placeholder for its description. Due to the absence of Plates in these games, Judgment instead adopts the user's primary type. Within the WiiWare games, Judgment's type varies according to the specific version being played. Specifically, it functions as a Fire-type move in *Keep Going! Blazing Adventure Squad*, a Water-type move in *Let's Go! Stormy Adventure Squad*, and an Electric-type move in *Go For It! Light Adventure Squad*. This distinction arises from the lack of Plates and Arceus's inherent adoption of the type associated with each game version. In *Gates to Infinity*, the move is unobtainable and consists solely of dummy data, given Arceus's absence from that game. *Super Mystery Dungeon* sees Judgment inflicting damage as though it were the same type as its user. Once more, owing to the lack of Plates, this typically results in Normal-type damage. Nonetheless, Judgment transforms into Water-type if the user's type is altered by Soak (though not if a type is merely added via Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat). In *Pokémon Rumble Rush*, the type of Judgment differs for every individual Arceus acquired. The English dub of *Arceus and the Jewel of Life* depicts Arceus consistently uttering "Justice" during the execution of this move. Beyond a glitch impacting Present in *Gold and Silver*, Judgment and Revelation Dance are the sole battle mechanics influenced by the specific order of a Pokémon's types. During the boss encounter with Arceus in *Pokémon Legends: Arceus*, it possesses the ability to employ Judgment consecutively multiple times.

Title: Bug Bite (move)

Known in Japanese as むしくい (Bug Bite), this Bug-type damaging attack was first introduced in Generation IV. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, trainers can find it as TM182. Bug Bite inflicts damage. If the target holds a Berry, the user will consume it and acquire its effect. This move, however, will not eat the target's Berry if it strikes a substitute. Targets possessing the Sticky Hold Ability are immune to having their Berries consumed. Certain Berries, such as Liechi or Sitrus Berries, are eaten by the attacker regardless of whether their typical consumption requirements are met by the damage received from Bug Bite. A Pokémon with the Klutz Ability or affected by Embargo will consume the target's held Berry and, contrary to prior mechanics, now *will* gain its effect. While Bug Bite previously ate Jaboca and Tanga Berries before they could activate, their effects now trigger prior to consumption. This technique fails when utilized in a Max Raid Battle. Damage is dealt by Bug Bite, and it will also consume other food items held by the target, like a Seed or an Apple, if present. Should Bug Bite be directed at one of the player's own Pokémon, the opposing trainer will then select an item from their Bag. Its functionality is impacted by the Muzzled status condition. Despite its name, Bug Bite is not classified as a biting move and therefore does not receive a power boost when used by a Pokémon with the Strong Jaw Ability.

Title: Charge Beam (move)

Known in Japanese as チャージビーム (Charge Beam), this Electric-type attack was first introduced in Generation IV, dealing damage to its target. It functioned as TM57, being available from Generation IV up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and again within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. In battle, Charge Beam delivers damage and has the potential to elevate the user's Special Attack by one stage. This additional effect uniquely carries a 70% chance of activation, making it the sole move with such a specific probability for a secondary effect.

A glitch once existed wherein if the user possessed the Ability Serene Grace and was influenced by the rainbow effect from a Fire Pledge and Water Pledge combination, the calculation for this additional effect chance would reach 280%, leading to an overflow that resulted in a 24% chance instead. This particular overflow glitch impacting Charge Beam's additional effect chance has since been resolved.

Beyond standard battles, Charge Beam can be integrated into a Contest Spectacular combination; using the move Charge in the turn prior grants an extra three appeal points. Regarding Pokémon GO, Charge Beam is categorized as a Fast Attack, and it has been part of the game's move pool since February 16, 2017.

Title: Wood Hammer (move)

Wood Hammer (Japanese: ウッドハンマー Wood Hammer) is an offensive Grass-type technique introduced in Generation IV. This move inflicts damage, yet the user sustains recoil equivalent to 33% of the damage dealt to the target. Wood Hammer's PP has been lowered to 5, and its power stat adjusted to 100. Overall, Wood Hammer causes damage while also inflicting recoil upon its user. In the Explorers series of games, this self-damage amounts to ⅛ of the user's maximum HP. From Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, the recoil damage is instead calculated as 20% of the user's total HP.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Wood Hammer serves as Trevenant's inaugural move. It is acquired through evolution at level 5, specifically by upgrading Will-O-Wisp instead of Curse. The Trevenant pulls back its arms before striking, harming enemies within the area of effect and drawing them in front of itself, leaving them incapacitated for .5s. If this initial assault successfully damages at least one foe, a subsequent attack becomes available for 5s. This follow-up maneuver slams a massive wooden hammer down before the user, damaging any adversary it strikes and rendering them unable to act for .6s. A lower current HP for the user results in an extended duration of enemy immobilization. Each use of this ability consumes 10% of the user's current HP. Upon reaching level 11, enemies hit by the second hammer have their outgoing damage reduced by 20% for 3s.

Title: Aqua Jet (move)

A Water-type attacking maneuver, Aqua Jet (アクアジェット in Japanese) made its debut in Generation IV. Its purpose is to inflict harm upon opponents. With a priority of +1, this technique is executed before any abilities lacking elevated priority. Aligning with other moves historically possessing priority, Aqua Jet compels its user to surge forward and make damaging contact with the intended target, though certain terrains might impede this action. While making this dash, the user becomes intangible, allowing them to pass through projectiles and hazards without adverse interaction. The description for Crashdown Aqua Jet, as it stood before Version 2.20.0 (dated April 27, 2022), specified that its power would increase when attacking a flinching foe. In the storyline of Mine Mayhem, Black's Tirtouga, Costa, was commanded to use Aqua Jet but chose to ignore the instruction. Since its initial release in Generation IV, Aqua Jet has consistently featured the identical descriptive text as Quick Attack.

Title: Attack Order (move)

Emerging in Generation IV, Attack Order (こうげきしれい in Japanese, rendered as Attack Order) is a Bug-type technique primarily used to deal damage. It stands as one of Vespiquen's signature abilities, forming a distinctive trio with Defend Order and Heal Order. This move not only inflicts damage but also features an elevated critical hit ratio. Furthermore, in Contest Spectaculars, Attack Order contributes to a specific combination: its user receives three additional appeal points if Focus Energy was deployed in the turn immediately preceding it. While consistently causing damage and enjoying a heightened critical-hit rate, this probability reaches 30% specifically within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. Though it has yet to make a full-fledged appearance in the animated series, Attack Order was featured when a Vespiquen employed it during the Professor Oak's Big Pokémon Encyclopedia segment tied to episode DP047.

Title: Heal Order (move)

Originating in Generation IV, Heal Order (Japanese: かいふくしれい, Recovery Order) is a non-damaging Bug-type technique. It stands as one of Vespiquen's characteristic maneuvers, alongside Attack Order and Defend Order. Initially, this move would restore up to 50% of the user's maximum HP, always rounded down. However, its effect was later modified to precisely restore 50% of the user's maximum HP, with the value now rounded up. When amplified by a Buginium Z, it transforms into Z-Heal Order, an enhanced version that completely resets all of the user's diminished stats.

Within the Explorers series of games, Heal Order provides a restoration of 25% of the user's total HP. The Adventure Squad series implements a fixed HP recovery of 40 points, irrespective of the user's maximum HP. In Super Mystery Dungeon, the move's healing capability is 40% of the user's maximum HP. Regarding Pokémon Rumble Rush, all status-based moves, including Heal Order, were exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and were unavailable for acquisition on any captured Pokémon. Specifically, Heal Order was associated with Boss Vespiquen in this title. A notable instance from the manga "Vexing Vespiquen & Unmanageable Mothim I" depicts a Roughneck attempting to command his Vespiquen to use Heal Order, but the Pokémon's grubs were too bewildered to follow through. As of Generations VIII and IX, Heal Order is uniquely restricted among Vespiquen's signature moves, being unusable in standard battles.

Title: Head Smash (move)

Debuting in Generation IV, Head Smash (Japanese: もろはのずつき Double-Edged Headbutt) is an offensive Rock-type technique. This maneuver deals damage to the target, with the user taking recoil harm equivalent to ½ of the damage inflicted. In specific instances, the user suffers recoil damage equal to 25% or 30% of their maximum HP. Diantha & Tyrantrum and Perrin & Growlithe, however, incur no recoil when executing this move, thanks to their passive skills Recoil Removal 9 and Sure Hit & Recoil Removal, respectively. Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder.

Within HeartGold and SoulSilver's game data, it was discovered that Nosepass could learn Head Smash through breeding. Despite this, no Pokémon capable of teaching it at the time belonged to Nosepass's Mineral Egg Group, rendering the move untransferable without employing the Rage glitch. This particular learnset information was subsequently removed in Generation V, only to be reinstated in Generation IX. In this most recent generation, Nosepass is able to inherit the move from Sudowoodo, a Pokémon that has learned Head Smash since Generation VII.

Head Smash shares the highest base power among all Rock-type moves with Rock Wrecker, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. An A Continental Crush derived from either of these techniques possesses the supreme base power of any Rock-type move. Head Smash is distinguished as the sole Rock-type move that inflicts recoil damage. Furthermore, it holds the highest base power among all moves that cause recoil.

Title: Double Hit (move)

Double Hit, known in Japanese as ダブルアタック (Double Attack), emerged in Generation IV as a Normal-type damage-dealing multistrike move. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is designated as TM132. This attack delivers two separate strikes per use, each possessing an identical probability of scoring a critical hit. Should the initial blow shatter a substitute, Double Hit proceeds with its second strike. Notably, Bide and Counter register only the second strike of this technique. For targets possessing a contact-activated Ability, each strike separately triggers the Ability, allowing for a double activation. When the user holds a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every individual hit carries an independent chance to cause the foe to flinch. Should a Focus Sash be triggered prior to the final strike, the subsequent hit will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can still repeatedly prevent fainting, though each activation attempt functions independently. Sturdy will activate with every hit if the defending Pokémon possesses a maximum HP of 1. Opponents with Weak Armor or Stamina will have their respective Abilities activated by each individual strike. Furthermore, if the target is equipped with a Rocky Helmet, the user sustains damage for each strike landed.

In a distinct application within Pokémon Legends: Z-A, Double Hit functions as a status move, consuming 10 PP. Its effect grants the user the Primed status, resulting in a 50% damage increase for its offensive moves. Additionally, this move boosts the user's action speed. An Aipom, upon learning Double Hit and gaining a level, will evolve into Ambipom. This move consistently inflicts damage on the foe twice. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, the activation of a Reviver Seed will terminate the move's execution.

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Double Hit presents itself as an option for Scizor's second move slot. Players acquire it at level 7 by selecting to evolve Quick Attack into it, rather than Swords Dance. Utilizing Double Hit causes the user to leap in a chosen direction, harming any opposing Pokémon encountered during the jump and applying a unique mark to them. Following this initial strike against enemies, the user executes a second jump in the specified direction. Upon landing, the user's subsequent basic attack transforms into a boosted attack, triggering a charge towards a selected opposing Pokémon. Should the user successfully fell a marked opposing Pokémon, all of its move cooldowns are instantly refreshed. At level 13, Double Hit receives an upgrade to Double Hit+, which notably decreases the move's cooldown.

Title: Spacial Rend (move)

Known in Japanese as あくうせつだん (Subspace Tear), Spacial Rend is a Dragon-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation IV. This move is uniquely associated with Palkia. Beyond its damage output, Spacial Rend possesses a heightened likelihood of landing a critical hit. For Contest Spectaculars, Spacial Rend can be incorporated into a combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Focus Energy was employed during the preceding turn. Initially, from Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Spacial Rend was unavailable for selection in combat. However, with the release of Version 1.3.0, it became usable and performs identically to its behavior in earlier generations. Within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, players can select it, and it operates consistently with its performance in prior installments. The base power of Spacial Rend underwent a reduction, settling at 90. When executed by Origin Forme Palkia, Spacial Rend experiences alterations in its animation, base power, accuracy, and critical-hit ratio. In certain contexts, Spacial Rend inflicts harm upon every adversary present in the immediate vicinity. It maintains an elevated critical hit rate, specifically reaching 30% within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series. Palkia can be encountered during a particular mission. When Palkia unleashes Spacial Rend, it warps the region of Almia, leading to numerous peculiar occurrences, including individuals teleporting upon traversing doorways and anglers attempting to fish in sand or lava. During its capture sequence, Palkia deploys Spacial Rend to periodically distort the capture environment, resulting in effects like flames or ice erupting from the terrain. In Pokémon GO, Spacial Rend functions as a Charged Attack, which became accessible on February 24, 2024. Outside of combat, its Adventure Effect broadens the area within which players can detect wild Pokémon. Activating this effect requires 5,000 Stardust and 5 Palkia Candy, and it persists for 10 minutes. Upon being summoned, Palkia employs Spacial Rend to momentarily invert the stage's perspective. This assault is also capable of harming combatants who come into contact with it. If Origin Forme Palkia utilizes Spacial Rend in its strong style, the move achieves the highest base critical hit rate among all moves, registering at +3. Among the signature moves of mythical Pokémon, Spacial Rend stands alone as the only one whose base power is diminished when wielded by one of the Pokémon's Origin Formes. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Palkia's Spacial Rend displays a varying animation based on whether Palkia is in its standard or Origin Forme. During boss encounters against Origin Forme Palkia and Arceus in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, both entities can unleash a charged, unavoidable variant of Spacial Rend; however, this offensive can be interrupted by striking them with Space Balms and Mysterious Balms, respectively, leading to their temporary incapacitation.

Title: Lunar Dance (move)

This Psychic-type technique, known in Japan as みかづきのまい (Crescent Moon Dance), first appeared in Generation IV and inflicts no damage. Cresselia possesses it as one of its exclusive signature moves. Upon execution, the user faints, yet fully revitalizes the HP and PP of the Pokémon that enters the battle to replace it, simultaneously clearing all existing status conditions (with the exception of fainting itself). Originally, if the user acted prior to the opponent, the beneficiary Pokémon was deployed before the conclusion of its turn. This mechanism was later altered, and the replacement Pokémon is now introduced at the very end of the turn, subsequent to all other Pokémon's actions. When augmented by a Psychium Z to become Z-Lunar Dance, this move does not acquire any supplementary effects.

From Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Lunar Dance was unselectable during combat. However, as of Version 1.3.0, it became fully operational. Should the Pokémon entering to substitute the user already possess maximum HP, be free of any status ailments, and have full PP, Lunar Dance's effect will lie dormant until a different Pokémon occupies that slot (either via a manual switch-in or the use of Ally Switch). Furthermore, a deferred Lunar Dance will not trigger if a Pokémon currently in that spot loses HP, acquires a status condition, or expends PP; its activation is solely contingent upon a *new* Pokémon being moved into that position. Only one instance of Lunar Dance can be pending per position; successive uses do not accumulate. Notably, Lunar Dance is able to combine its effects with Healing Wish. Its use in a Max Raid Battle or Tera Raid Battle results in failure, and the user does not faint in these scenarios.

Within the Mystery Dungeon series, Lunar Dance targets all other allies present in the same chamber, fully restoring their HP and PP, and also purging any negative status conditions. In these games, the user's HP is reduced to 1 after activating this move. Specifically, in the Explorers and Adventure Squad sub-series, PP is completely refilled, whereas starting with Super Mystery Dungeon, only 5 PP are recovered.

Title: Crush Grip (move)

Debuting in Generation IV, Crush Grip (Japanese: にぎりつぶす Grip Crush) is a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage. This move is distinctive to Regigigas, serving as its signature technique. The amount of damage dealt by Crush Grip scales with the target's remaining HP, becoming more potent against healthier foes. Its base power fluctuates from a minimum of 1 to a maximum of 121. This attack's power is computed based on the target's HP, with its current power scaling from 1 to 120. During Pokémon Sword and Shield's initial release, specifically from Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1, Crush Grip was unselectable during battles. However, as of Version 1.3.0, it became fully operational, mirroring its functionality from prior generations. In a general sense, the move inflicts damage, with its strength amplified by the opponent's current HP. Within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, the damage value undergoes specific multiplication: a ½ factor applies when the enemy's HP lies between 51-75%, decreasing to 51/256 (approximately 0.2×) for HP in the 26-50% range, and further reduced to 25/256 (around 0.1×) when HP is at 25% or less. For Super Mystery Dungeon, the damage multiplier spans from 1.0x down to 0.0x, dynamically adjusting according to the target's current HP percentage, though it always inflicts a minimum of 1 damage.

Title: Magma Storm (move)

Magma Storm, known in Japan as マグマストーム, is an offensive Fire-type move that debuted in Generation IV. It serves as Heatran's exclusive signature attack. This ability inflicts damage and ensnares the opposing Pokémon for a duration ranging from two to five turns. While bound, a target is prevented from withdrawing or escaping battle, and it suffers damage equivalent to 1/16 of its total health at the close of every turn.

Should the user depart the field, the ensnared foe is immediately released. Similarly, if a constrained Pokémon successfully employs Rapid Spin, it will break free from the hold. Specific conditions offer exceptions to this immobility: a Pokémon possessing the Run Away ability or holding a Smoke Ball item can still flee despite being bound (though switching out remains impossible); conversely, a Pokémon equipped with a Shed Shell can switch out while bound (yet cannot flee). Only a single binding effect can influence a Pokémon at any given moment, and the condition dissipates entirely if the move's initiator is no longer active on the battlefield. When the Magma Storm user is equipped with a Grip Claw, the binding period is consistently extended to 5 turns.

Initially, Magma Storm possessed an accuracy of 70% and a base power of 120. Subsequently, the trapping duration was modified to between four and five turns. If the move's user holds a Grip Claw, this period expands to 7 turns. The passive damage inflicted by the bound status also saw revisions: originally, a Binding Band would cause the target to suffer 1/8 of its maximum HP per turn instead of the standard 1/16. Later, the base damage for a Pokémon ensnared by Magma Storm was increased to 1/8 of its total health each turn, with a Binding Band further amplifying this to 1/6 of its maximum HP. Ghost-type Pokémon are completely immune to Magma Storm's trapping effect.

Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, Magma Storm was unselectable in battle from versions 1.0 up to 1.2.1. However, with the release of Version 1.3.0, it became fully functional, operating identically to its previous generation mechanics. Its base power was later reduced to 90. Furthermore, the move consistently inflicts a burn on its target.

In certain contexts, aside from dealing damage, Magma Storm may potentially apply the Constriction status condition to the foe. This status prevents the enemy from moving (though attacking remains possible) and causes a depletion of 5 HP every two turns, persisting for 4 to 6 turns. Specifically in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the move might instead impose a distinct 'Magma Storm' status effect for 6 turns, which similarly incapacitates movement (while permitting attacks) and results in a loss of 10 HP biennially. As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Magma Storm has been obtainable since June 29, 2023. Among all moves capable of binding, Magma Storm holds the distinction of having the greatest power.

Title: Dark Void (move)

This entry details the move known as Dark Void. For information on the Field Move 'Dark Power', refer to the 'Field Move (Ranger)' page. Known in Japanese as ダークホール (Dark Hole), Dark Void is a Dark-type status move that first appeared in Generation IV. It serves as Darkrai's signature technique. With an 80% individual accuracy rate, Dark Void inflicts sleep upon all neighboring opponents. Pokémon possessing Abilities such as Insomnia or Vital Spirit, or those benefiting from Sweet Veil's effects, will remain immune to Dark Void's influence. Within Pokémon Battle Revolution, specifically in a Double Battle where the sleep clause is in effect, Dark Void can only successfully incapacitate one adversary, failing against any additional targets.

Beyond battles, Dark Void offers utility in Contest Spectacular combinations. When used, it empowers subsequent turns' moves like Dream Eater, Hex, Nightmare, and Wake-Up Slap, granting them an additional three appeal points. Later, Dark Void's accuracy was diminished to a 50% chance to affect each Pokémon. Furthermore, its successful execution became restricted solely to Darkrai, or Pokémon that have mimicked Darkrai's form. Nonetheless, Metronome can still select Dark Void, even though Darkrai itself is incapable of using Metronome. Should any other Pokémon attempt to employ this move (even through abilities or moves that call other moves), it will invariably fail, accompanied by the battle message, "But <Pokémon> can't use the move!". When reflected via Magic Bounce, Dark Void nonetheless operates without failure. When augmented by a Darkinium Z, transforming into Z-Dark Void, it causes all of the user's decreased stats to be reset.

Pokémon Legends: Arceus presented a unique iteration of Dark Void: its accuracy was elevated to 90%, though it decreased the user's action priority. Here, it induces drowsiness in the target and simultaneously reduces their defensive stats. Consistent with all other moves in this particular game, it affects only a single Pokémon. Between Versions 1.0 and 1.3.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Dark Void was unselectable during battles. However, from Version 2.0.1 onwards, it became fully usable once more, retaining its functionality from prior generations.

Pokémon Legends: Z-A introduces a modified Dark Void as a Special move with 60 power. It deals damage, inflicts drowsiness on targets, and heals the user for 50% of the damage inflicted. Furthermore, it inflicts double damage on already drowsy foes. As a Special move in this title, Darkrai gains STAB when employing it. In the game Pokémon Rumble Rush, Dark Void, alongside all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any captured Pokémon. Specifically, Boss Darkrai was observed to utilize Dark Void.

An important clarification: any status conditions inflicted by this move affect each successfully targeted Pokémon individually. From Generation VII onward (with the exception of Pokémon Legends: Arceus), Dark Void holds the distinction of having the lowest accuracy among all status moves, signature moves, and Dark-type moves. In the film 'The Rise of Darkrai', Darkrai verbally declares the move's name when deploying Dark Void for both the second and third instances. This vocalization is absent in the original Japanese rendition. Curiously, within the Brazilian Portuguese dub, likely an oversight, Darkrai utters "Dark Void" upon its third use of the move, rather than its localized equivalent.

Title: Seed Flare (move)

Seed Flare, known in Japanese as シードフレア (Seed Flare), is a damage-dealing Grass-type technique that first appeared in Generation IV. It serves as the exclusive move for Shaymin. When used, Seed Flare deals damage and carries a 40% chance of significantly reducing the opponent's Special Defense stat by two stages. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Seed Flare is categorized as a Charged Attack and was introduced to players on June 4, 2022. During the film Giratina and the Sky Warrior, Nurse Joy provided a description of Seed Flare as the process by which Shaymin purifies inhaled polluted air within its body, subsequently expelling it as a combination of light and water.

Title: Ominous Wind (move)

Ominous Wind (Japanese: あやしいかぜ Ominous Wind) is a Ghost-type offensive technique that debuted in Generation IV. This move is designated as TM119 in the upcoming Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Beyond its direct damage, Ominous Wind carries a 10% probability of simultaneously enhancing the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed by a single stage apiece. The move's Power Points (PP) were notably augmented, climbing from 5 to 15.

In the context of the Mystery Dungeon series, there's a 20% chance for this attack to elevate the user's overall offensive and defensive capabilities. Within these spin-off titles, Ominous Wind hits every foe located within the same room. It also possesses the potential to boost the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Travel Speed by one stage. Specifically within the Explorers series, the secondary effect activates for *each* opponent struck, with every resultant stat increase taking effect the instant it's triggered. Its secondary effect was modified, shifting from '2 Attack and Defense' to '1 Attack and Defense'.

Before Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), the move's in-game description stated: "Has a very small chance of raising the user's Attack, Defense, Sp. Atk, Sp. Def, and Speed." Up until Generation VI, Ominous Wind shared its in-game animation with the move Heat Wave, with the sole distinguishing factor being their respective colors. It holds the distinction of being the sole Ghost-type move classified as Beautiful for Contest purposes.

Title: Shadow Force (move)

Originating in Generation IV, the Ghost-type technique known as Shadow Force (シャドーダイブ, or Shadow Dive in Japanese) serves as Giratina's distinctive signature attack. When chosen, the user instantly disappears, becoming semi-invulnerable during that turn, only to resurface on the subsequent turn to inflict damage. This maneuver penetrates protection moves, striking the target regardless of their use, and subsequently dispels the defensive effects from that specific target for the remainder of the current turn. Should the attack land on an adversary shielded by a field-wide protective measure, such as Wide Guard, it will nullify that protection for their entire side of the field, even if the protection wouldn't typically block Shadow Force itself. However, Shadow Force cannot dismantle protective effects if the move itself fails to impact the target, such as when attacking a Normal-type Pokémon. Should the user succumb to sleep from Yawn while in its semi-invulnerable state, the attack will be aborted prior to striking.

Furthermore, a rival who has just employed Lock-On or Mind Reader can still land an attack on the user, as can any Pokémon if either the attacker or the user of Shadow Force possesses the No Guard Ability. It is impossible to capture wild Pokémon during Shadow Force's semi-invulnerable phase. Equipping the user with a Power Herb allows Shadow Force to be unleashed in a single turn. When facing a target that has used Minimize, Shadow Force inflicts double its normal damage and inherently bypasses accuracy checks, guaranteeing a hit, unless the target itself is in the semi-invulnerable turn of an ability like Dig or Fly. Regardless of whether the target is an opponent or an ally, if Shadow Force connects with a Pokémon shielded by a protection move spanning their entire side of the field (like Wide Guard), it eradicates that defensive effect from their whole side, even if Shadow Force would not typically be affected by it. When striking a Pokémon guarded by a protection move that carries a detrimental effect (for instance, Spiky Shield), the user of Shadow Force remains immune to that negative consequence. Conversely, in other circumstances, Shadow Force is described as no longer bypassing accuracy checks or inflicting double damage against targets that have used Minimize.

Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1, Shadow Force was unselectable in combat. However, commencing with Version 1.3.0, it became fully usable and reverted to its functionality from the preceding generation. It is unable to bypass Max Guard or negate its effects. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Shadow Force operates as a single-turn attack, which reduces the user's action speed and conceals them. When executed by Origin Forme Giratina, Shadow Force's animation, base power, and accuracy undergo alterations. Should a Shiny Origin Forme Giratina utilize this move, the game will unfortunately crash during the animation sequence.

In its typical two-turn execution, the user acquires the "Shadow Force" status on the initial turn, rendering the Pokémon invulnerable to attacks and allowing projectiles to pass through its space as if unoccupied. On the subsequent turn, the user assails the opponent directly in front, bypassing defensive moves such as Protect, Wide Guard, or Quick Guard to inflict harm. Within the Explorers and Adventure Squad series, Shadow Force's damage is doubled upon the conclusion of damage calculation. As a two-turn maneuver, it is incapable of being linked with other moves. Furthermore, its use is prohibited within a Pokémon Tower in the Adventure Squad series. During co-operative battles, the user's vanished presence will also dissipate if they execute a unity attack.

In Pokémon GO, Shadow Force functions as a Charged Attack, becoming available from October 20, 2022. Among all moves featuring a semi-invulnerable turn, Shadow Force possesses the highest power. It also holds the distinction of being the final Generation IV move according to index number order. Notably, it stands as the sole non-Dragon-type signature move belonging to a Pokémon of myth, and concurrently, the only one among them that is not categorized as a Special attack. During the rematch against Psychic Corbin in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, his Banette unexpectedly uses Shadow Force, a move it cannot legally acquire. This is presumed to be an oversight, with Phantom Force, a similar and learnable move for Banette, being the intended choice. Finally, in its climactic boss encounter within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Arceus itself is capable of employing Shadow Force.

Title: Hone Claws (move)

Known in Japanese as つめとぎ (Claw Sharpen), Hone Claws is a non-damaging Dark-type move first introduced in Generation V. For Generations V and VI, it was designated as TM01. This move boosts the user's Attack and accuracy by one stage apiece. Hone Claws also serves a role in Contest Spectacular combinations, yielding an extra three appeal points for the user if Baton Pass or Stored Power are used in the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Darkinium Z, transforming into Z-Hone Claws, the user's Attack stat receives a further stage increase. The fundamental effect of Hone Claws is to elevate both the user's Attack and accuracy by one stage. Like all other status moves, Hone Claws is exclusively utilized by opposing Pokémon and cannot be employed by any Pokémon a trainer has caught. It is recognized as a move wielded by Boss Liepard. Hone Claws is among Meowscarada's initial repertoire of moves. It functions as a Dash move, can be learned at either level 1 or 3, has a cooldown duration of 8 seconds, and upgrades into Double Team or Trailblaze upon reaching level 7. Hone Claws involves the user sharpening its claws while in motion, thereby augmenting the speed and damage of its next three basic attacks. In terms of index number order, Hone Claws holds the distinction of being the first move from Generation V.

Title: Wide Guard (move)

Wide Guard (Japanese: ワイドガード Wide Guard) is a Rock-type move, incapable of inflicting damage, that was first introduced in Generation V. For the remainder of the turn, Wide Guard safeguards all Pokémon on the user's team from attacks designed to hit multiple Pokémon. This defensive effect extends even to moves originating from an ally that strike every Pokémon on the field, such as Earthquake and Surf. Wide Guard is effective at blocking these moves even during Single Battles. The protective state granted by Wide Guard endures until the turn concludes, even if the user is knocked out within that same turn. However, if the user makes its move last in the turn, Wide Guard will fail.

Should a damaging Z-Move that targets multiple Pokémon be directed at a Pokémon under Wide Guard's protection, the Z-Move will bypass the defense but will only inflict 25% of its original damage. Moves capable of breaking protection, like Feint, will negate Wide Guard's effects. The Unseen Fist Ability allows contact moves to circumvent Wide Guard. A specific category of moves exists that are entirely impervious to protection.

This move boasts a +3 priority, meaning it is executed before the vast majority of other actions. Exclusively in Generation V, the probability of Wide Guard succeeding decreases each time the user successfully and consecutively employs Endure, any single-target protection move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Each subsequent success halves the chance of success (multiplied by 1/2). Also in Generation V, if a Pokémon uses Feint or Shadow Force to assault its own ally already protected by Wide Guard, the effect will not be dispelled. During Generations V and VI, Wide Guard was unable to intercept status moves that targeted multiple Pokémon.

Due to an oversight, in version 1.0 of Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Wide Guard incorrectly reduced the damage of all damaging Z-Moves, rather than being limited to those that target multiple Pokémon; this behavior was corrected in version 1.1 of Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. (It is worth noting that Pokémon Sun and Moon do not feature any damaging Z-Moves that target multiple Pokémon.) When powered up by a Rockium Z, transforming into Z-Wide Guard, the user's Defense stat increases by one stage.

The user confers the Wide Guard status condition upon itself and its teammates in the same room for one turn. This status shields Pokémon from wide-ranging moves, though they remain vulnerable to damage from items or explosions. The probability of this move failing rises if the user's most recently executed move was Wide Guard or another move designed to evade enemy attacks (such as Protect or Endure).

Its description prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) stated: "...Nullifies moves that target all allies when hit by such a move while in this defensive posture."

In Pokémon UNITE, Wide Guard serves as Aegislash's second move. It is acquired upon reaching level 8 by upgrading Iron Defense into it, rather than Iron Head. The user momentarily focuses in place to generate a formidable shield around itself. When this shield materializes, the boost count increments by 1, simultaneously granting a shield to the user and pushing back enemies directly in front. While this shield remains active, attacks from different enemy players each increase the boost count by 1 (with a maximum cap of 4). Upon the move's conclusion, enemies in front of the user are shoved once more and subsequently slowed for 2 seconds. At level 13, the move's cooldown period is shortened. In the Brazilian Portuguese localization of Pokémon UNITE, this move possesses two distinct names: Vasta Proteção and Defesa Aberta, the latter being observed only after Aegislash achieves level 13 and evolves the move into Wide Guard+.

Title: Guard Split (move)

Known in Japanese as ガードシェア (Guard Share), Guard Split is a Psychic-type technique that deals no direct damage, first appearing in Generation V. Its core function involves calculating the mean of the user's and the target Pokémon's Defense and Special Defense statistics. When computing this average, any active stat stage modifiers on either combatant are disregarded. This move inherently possesses perfect accuracy, circumventing typical accuracy checks, and will invariably connect, with the sole exception being if the opponent is currently invulnerable due to moves like Dig or Fly. Subsequent to the averaging, various influences such as stat alterations, the effects of items like Assault Vest and Eviolite, and other enhancements that boost Defense or Special Defense, all interact with the Pokémon's newly adjusted stats, rather than their initial values. Should the Pokémon be withdrawn from battle, its modified statistics revert to their prior state. For the purpose of calculating the new average, if either the user or the recipient Pokémon already possess altered stats—implying modifications from items or passive effects, distinct from temporary stat stage changes—Guard Split will utilize these current, adjusted values rather than their unmodified original stats. When empowered by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Guard Split, this technique confers an additional benefit, boosting the user's Speed by a single stage. Execution of Guard Split is impossible within a Tera Raid Battle, applying to both the Raid Boss and fellow combatants. Fundamentally, Guard Split operates by taking the mean of the initiator's and the foe's Defense and Special Defense stats. A narrative mention of this technique occurs in the Giant Chasm, where Gorm commanded his Cofagrigus to perform Guard Split, though its execution was unseen.

Title: Power Split (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Power Split (Japanese: パワーシェア, 'Power Share') is a Psychic-type status move. Its core mechanic involves combining the user's Attack and Special Attack stats with the target's equivalent stats, subsequently adjusting both Pokémon's Attack and Special Attack to become the computed average. Significantly, this statistical averaging completely disregards any existing stat changes affecting either the user or the target. Power Split inherently bypasses standard accuracy checks, guaranteeing a hit unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. If empowered by a Psychium Z, transforming it into Z-Power Split, an additional effect triggers, boosting the user's Speed stat by one stage. Nevertheless, Power Split is rendered ineffectual and will fail if utilized in a Tera Raid Battle, applying to both the Raid Boss and its allies.

Title: Wonder Room (move)

A Psychic-type technique first appearing in Generation V, Wonder Room (Japanese: ワンダールーム Wonder Room) causes no direct damage. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, trainers can acquire it as TM71. This move reverses the Defense and Special Defense stats for every Pokémon present, though any existing stat modifications persist on their original stat. The alteration endures for a duration of five turns. Should Wonder Room be activated while already active—a mechanic shared with Magic Room and Trick Room—its effects cease instantly. Initially, Wonder Room possessed a priority of -7, meaning its execution occurred subsequent to all standard priority actions. It currently holds a priority rating of 0. Furthermore, Wonder Room influences which stat is amplified by the Beast Boost Ability. It additionally alters the functions of both Sword of Ruin and Beads of Ruin. When empowered by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Wonder Room, the user gains a one-stage increase to their Special Defense stat. As a floor status, Wonder Room reverses the Defense and Sp. Def stats for every Pokémon present on that particular floor. This floor condition persists for 20 turns. Activating Wonder Room again while this floor status is active will similarly cause its immediate cessation. Notably, during Generation V, Wonder Room shared the distinction with Trick Room and Magic Room of having the absolute lowest priority among all moves.

Title: Psyshock (move)

Known as サイコショック (Psycho Shock) in Japanese, Psyshock is a Psychic-type damage-dealing attack that first appeared in Generation V. From Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it held the designation of TM03. While Psyshock is fundamentally a special attack, leveraging the user's Special Attack stat, its unique property lies in targeting the opponent's Defense stat for damage calculation, rather than their Special Defense.

This special categorization means Psyshock interacts with various game mechanics accordingly: it triggers Mirror Coat, not Counter; activates Maranga Berry, but not Kee Berry; and its damage is mitigated by Light Screen, not Reflect. Notably, it does not cause Toxic Debris to activate.

In Pokémon UNITE, Psyshock serves as Espeon's inaugural move and Gardevoir's secondary move. Espeon gains access to Psyshock upon evolving from Eevee at level 4, selecting it as an upgrade for Swift over Stored Power. When Espeon uses Psyshock, it manifests five psychic projectiles, propelling them towards a specified location. These projectiles damage any foes they strike and temporarily reduce their movement speed. The orbs originate in a horizontal line, perpendicular to Espeon's orientation, and continue their trajectory beyond the targeted area until reaching the move's maximum range. Enemies struck by all five projectiles are briefly stunned. However, after the initial hit on a single foe, subsequent projectiles inflict 70% less damage. At level 10, Psyshock's projectiles gain increased speed.

Gardevoir acquires Psyshock after evolving into Kirlia at level 5, choosing it as an evolution of Teleport instead of Future Sight. Gardevoir's version involves channeling three distinct attacks into the designated zone, harming enemies within. Psyshock's cooldown duration is lessened with each successful hit on an opponent. Upon reaching level 11, every strike from Psyshock also amplifies the damage of the next hit.

Historically, in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Psyshock was the English translation for ねんりき (Nenriki) on numerous cards, dating back to Generation I with Base Set Abra, long before its debut as a video game move in Generation V. In the TCG, its effect was to induce paralysis. Following the introduction of the move Psyshock, ねんりき (Nenriki) was re-translated as Psy Bolt, aligning with the translation of ちょうねんりき (Chōnenriki) as Super Psy Bolt.

Among the limited trio of special-based moves that uniquely inflict physical damage, Psyshock stands out as the sole non-signature move. Its in-game description text is identical to that of Psystrike. Within the game Pokémon Conquest, Psyshock's description mirrors its Generation V core series counterpart, including the mention of physical damage. However, Conquest lacks the physical-special damage distinction, possessing only a single attack and defense stat, rendering all damage uniformly categorized.

Title: Venoshock (move)

Venoshock, known in Japanese as ベノムショック (Venom Shock), is a Poison-type attack that inflicts damage, making its debut in Generation V. This move functioned as TM09 from Generation V up until Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Later, it became TM74 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the designation of TM045 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Its base power is doubled, resulting in an output of 130, when the target is afflicted by poison, thus dealing twice its usual damage against such enemies. Additionally, Venoshock's damage potency is also doubled if the opposing Pokémon has any status condition whatsoever. Furthermore, Venoshock offers utility in Contest Spectaculars, forming a combination that grants an additional three points when performed after Poison Gas, Poison Powder, Toxic, or Toxic Spikes on the preceding turn. Historical descriptions of Venoshock have varied: Before Version 2.10.0 (issued on June 28, 2021), its description noted that its power would double if the target was poisoned or badly poisoned. A subsequent description, prior to Version 2.20.0 (released April 27, 2022), similarly stated that the move's power doubled when the target was poisoned or badly poisoned. Within the manga series Teary!! The Voice of the Master, Allister's Gengar was shown to possess knowledge of Venoshock, though it was never depicted performing the attack. Among all Poison-type maneuvers, Venoshock boasts the highest base power, a distinction that excludes Z-Moves, yet is contingent solely on the target being poisoned.

Title: Autotomize (move)

Known in Japan as ボディパージ (Body Purge), Autotomize is a non-damaging Steel-type attack that debuted in Generation V. When executed, this move elevates the user's Speed stat by two stages; moreover, provided the Speed modification is successful, the user's weight is reduced by 220 lbs (100 kg). A successful weight alteration prompts the in-game message: "<Pokémon> became nimble!" The user's weight cannot be decreased below a minimum threshold of 0.2 lbs (0.1 kg) by Autotomize; if a reduction would result in a lower value, the weight is instead set to this minimum. This weight loss effect is cumulative, meaning repeated uses will continue to lighten the user until the minimum weight is attained. Notably, the weight reduction achieved by Autotomize cannot be passed on through Baton Pass nor cleared by Haze. However, a Pokémon's modified weight will reset upon changing form (applicable from Generation VI onwards), switching out, fainting, or at the conclusion of the battle. Beyond battles, Autotomize finds utility in Contest Spectaculars as part of a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to Baton Pass or Electro Ball if either is performed on the subsequent turn. When augmented by a Steelium Z, transforming it into Z-Autotomize, it gains an added benefit: all of the user's decreased stats are instantly restored to their base levels. Furthermore, it boosts the user's Travel Speed by two stages. It bestows the "Autotomize status," making the user lighter, which in turn impacts moves that scale with weight, such as Low Kick and Heat Crash. The move's name is derived from "autotomy," a biological process where certain animals intentionally shed body parts, often as a defense mechanism against predators. This phenomenon is frequently observed in reptiles, salamanders, and diverse invertebrate species.

Title: Rage Powder (move)

Known in Japanese as いかりのこな, or Rage Powder, this Bug-type technique was introduced in Generation V and inflicts no direct damage. Possessing a +3 priority, Rage Powder causes its user to become the focal point, compelling all hostile Pokémon to direct their attacks toward the user for the remainder of the turn. This redirection occurs even if the original target was an ally, with exceptions for maneuvers that inherently cannot target foes, such as Acupressure or Helping Hand. Should an attack be unable to strike the Pokémon drawing attention, it will proceed against its original objective. Allied Pokémon are unaffected. The effect of drawing moves ceases if the Pokémon serving as the center of attention is knocked out, swapped out, or lifted by Sky Drop.

Within Triple Battles, Rage Powder attracts the assaults of all adversaries present; however, it can only intercept attacks from non-adjacent foes if those attacks are categorized as long-range. This move can divert techniques requiring a charge turn, like Fly or Solar Beam, provided Rage Powder is executed on the turn the charged move is meant to strike. Sky Drop, however, cannot be drawn. Notably, Rage Powder will attract Electric- and Water-type maneuvers, even when a Pokémon possessing Lightning Rod or Storm Drain is present. Should another teammate already be acting as the focal point, the Pokémon that initiated the effect first maintains precedence; if that initial user moves out of range or ceases to be the center of attention, subsequent attacks will then be drawn to the next available Pokémon designated as such. It holds no sway over attacks that strike numerous Pokémon simultaneously. Despite being usable in Single Battles, its influence is nil.

Currently, Grass-type Pokémon, those with the abilities Overcoat, Stalwart, or Propeller Tail, and creatures equipped with Safety Goggles are impervious to Rage Powder; their attacks are therefore not rerouted. The move Snipe Shot bypasses Rage Powder entirely, striking its intended recipient directly. When amplified by a Buginium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Rage Powder, the user experiences a restoration of all its diminished stats. Utilizing Rage Powder bestows the decoy status onto the user, making them the preferred target for enemy assaults. Multi-target enemy moves, for example Ominous Wind (but excluding those like Magnitude that affect both allies and adversaries), will exclusively home in on the decoy if circumstances allow. A Pokémon under the decoy status retains full freedom of movement, though computer-controlled decoys exhibit erratic motion. This condition persists for six turns, or until another decoy emerges.

Title: Telekinesis (move)

Originating in Generation V, Telekinesis (Japanese: テレキネシス Telekinesis) is a non-damaging Psychic-type technique. During Generation V, it was designated as TM19. This move elevates its chosen target into the air for a duration of three turns. As a consequence, all attacks launched against this aerial target, with the sole exception of one-hit knockout moves, will bypass any accuracy checks, thereby guaranteeing a successful hit, unless the target is currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of maneuvers such as Dig or Fly. Furthermore, the affected Pokémon gains immunity to Ground-type attacks and is rendered ungrounded.

Should a Pokémon under the influence of Telekinesis become anchored by Ingrain or be equipped with an Iron Ball, it will revert to a grounded state (meaning it becomes susceptible to Ground-type moves, among others). However, the effect causing incoming attacks to ignore accuracy checks persists. If a Pokémon affected by Telekinesis is struck by Smack Down or Thousand Arrows, the levitation effect is dispelled. Similarly, the activation of Gravity will cause the Telekinesis effect to vanish from all Pokémon. The move Telekinesis itself cannot be used on a Pokémon already experiencing the effects of Ingrain, Smack Down, or Thousand Arrows. Additionally, while Gravity is active, Telekinesis cannot be chosen. Certain Pokémon, namely Mega Gengar, Diglett, Dugtrio, Sandygast, and Palossand, are inherently immune to the move Telekinesis; yet, a Pokémon transformed into one of these can still be affected. In the specific case of Mega Gengar, if it is affected by Telekinesis before Mega Evolving or receives the status via Baton Pass, the effect will cease; the other four mentioned Pokémon, however, will retain the effect if acquired through Baton Pass. Telekinesis also possesses perfect accuracy, always connecting unless its target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move such as Dig or Fly.

When boosted by a Psychium Z, transforming into Z-Telekinesis, the user's Special Attack statistic experiences a one-stage increase. Telekinesis applies the "Telekinesis status condition" to the foe directly in front. With this status, the afflicted Pokémon cannot move and its evasiveness is reduced, though it will not suffer any damage from Ground-type moves. A description from before version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated: "Makes the target unable to evade moves."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Telekinesis serves as Slowbro's second move. It becomes accessible upon reaching level 6 and choosing to upgrade Slack Off into it instead of Amnesia. The user projects a beam, causing the first enemy it hits to float aloft and restoring a portion of the user's Oblivious health. The user can reactivate the ability to pull the suspended enemy towards them, but otherwise remains unable to act for the move's duration. At level 13, the beam's range and speed are enhanced.

Title: Magic Room (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Magic Room (マジックルーム, Magic Room in Japanese) is a Psychic-type technique that inflicts no damage. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was designated as TM72. The primary function of Magic Room is to neutralize the effects of all items held by any Pokémon currently present on the field. This effect endures for a period of five turns. Should Magic Room be activated while already in effect, its ongoing influence will be immediately terminated. Items regain their functionality the moment Magic Room's duration concludes. Magic Room prohibits the use of items via Natural Gift and Fling; however, it does not suppress the benefits of Berries consumed through Pluck or Bug Bite. Effects that relocate items, such as those generated by Trick and Pickpocket, remain unaffected by Magic Room. When Judgment, Techno Blast, or Multi-Attack are utilized by a Pokémon during Magic Room's active state, these moves will register as Normal-type. Magic Room does not negate the item effects that impact prize money, experience points, effort values, or friendship, like those from the Amulet Coin, Lucky Egg, Power Weight, or Soothe Bell. Nevertheless, it does successfully nullify the Speed drop caused by the Macho Brace and various Power items (such as the Power Weight). The move is incapable of negating item-induced form changes. If a Pokémon is already locked into a move due to a held Choice item (e.g., Choice Scarf, Choice Specs, Choice Band), and that item is removed while its effect is suppressed, the Pokémon will be afflicted by the Choice item lock glitch. In this generation, Magic Room possesses a priority of -7, meaning its execution always occurs after all moves with a normal priority. Furthermore, Magic Room does not hinder a Pokémon from Mega Evolving with a Mega Stone, undergoing Primal Reversion via a Red Orb or Blue Orb, achieving Ultra Burst with Ultranecrozium Z, or deploying a Z-Move using a Z-Crystal. When empowered by a Psychium Z into Z-Magic Room, the user's Special Defense stat receives a one-stage boost. Magic Room does not cause Poltergeist or Teatime to fail. A distinct condition, the "Magic Room" floor status, prevents the use, consumption, and throwing of items. This floor status persists for 20 turns. Its effect also dissipates if Magic Room is utilized while this status is already active. In Generation V, Magic Room shared the distinction of having the lowest priority among all moves, alongside Trick Room and Wonder Room.

Title: Smack Down (move)

Originating in Generation V, Smack Down (うちおとす Knock Down in Japanese) is a Rock-type offensive maneuver that deals damage. It held the technical machine designation TM23 from its debut generation up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and currently functions as TM179 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This attack damages its target and simultaneously forces an ungrounded foe to the ground. More precisely, targets of the Flying-type, those possessing the Levitate Ability, or individuals under the influence of Magnet Rise or Telekinesis, will have this grounding effect imposed upon them. This renders the Pokémon susceptible to Ground-type attacks, affected by terrain conditions, vulnerable to Arena Trap, and restricts its ability to hover or traverse aerial obstacles such as water tiles. This grounded state endures until the Pokémon switches out. Additionally, the specific grounded status condition imparted by Smack Down is noted to last for 10 turns.

Furthermore, Smack Down neutralizes any active Magnet Rise or Telekinesis effects. However, the grounding effect does not manifest if the attack connects with a substitute, if the target is amidst the semi-invulnerable phase of Sky Drop, is holding an Iron Ball, or is already under the influence of Gravity or Ingrain. A Pokémon affected by Smack Down will find Magnet Rise attempts failing, and Telekinesis will be unable to impact it. The protective properties of an Air Balloon are also nullified while a Pokémon is grounded by Smack Down.

Uniquely, Smack Down can successfully strike foes during the semi-invulnerable stages of moves like Fly, Bounce, and Sky Drop. When connecting with a Pokémon utilizing Fly or Bounce, the target is brought down, interrupting and canceling their move. Conversely, if it hits a Pokémon engaged in or affected by Sky Drop, no such disruption occurs. Nevertheless, a Pokémon suffering from Smack Down's effect retains the ability to initiate these moves in subsequent turns.

In Contest Spectaculars, Smack Down can contribute to a combination, granting its user an additional three appeal points if Entrainment, Hold Hands, or Play Nice was performed in the preceding turn. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Smack Down functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible to players since June 16, 2018. The English designation for this maneuver potentially serves as an homage to the former professional wrestler and actor Dwayne Johnson, who is linked to popularizing the term 'smackdown.' Coincidentally, his wrestling moniker, 'The Rock,' aligns with the move's Rock typing.

Title: Storm Throw (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Storm Throw (Japanese: やまあらし Mountain Storm) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type move. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it holds the designation of TM137. This attack not only inflicts damage but is also programmed to always land as a critical hit, provided the target is not shielded by Battle Armor, Shell Armor, or the protective influence of Lucky Chant. Storm Throw's base Power was adjusted from 40 to 60, a change coinciding with critical hits subsequently dealing 1.5× damage instead of the previous 2× multiplier. During Contest Spectaculars, Storm Throw contributes to a combination, awarding its user an extra three appeal points if Entrainment, Hold Hands, or Play Nice was executed in the preceding turn. It remains usable and operates consistently with its functionality in prior generations. The move causes damage and inherently possesses an increased critical hit ratio. Prior to Generation IX, every Pokémon capable of learning Storm Throw also had the potential to possess the Ability Mold Breaker, an Ability known to negate the critical hit protection offered by Battle Armor and Shell Armor. This characteristic meant that Lucky Chant was the sole guaranteed method to prevent Storm Throw from achieving a critical hit.

Title: Flame Burst (move)

Originating in Generation V, the Fire-type attack known as Flame Burst (Japanese: はじけるほのお Flame Burst) inflicts direct damage upon its primary target. Subsequently, it deals residual damage to as many as two Pokémon positioned next to that target (this includes the move's user if the primary target is an ally), with this secondary damage amounting to 1/16 of each affected Pokémon's total HP. The recipients of this secondary damage vary depending on the battle format: outside of Battle Royals, it affects up to two allied Pokémon situated alongside the target. However, within Battle Royals, the additional damage targets the two other opposing Pokémon.

This subsequent damage is only applied if the primary target was successfully hit by Flame Burst; it will not occur if the initial attack missed or if the target avoided the effect through means such as the Flash Fire Ability or protection. Classified as effect damage, this secondary impact behaves uniquely: it bypasses abilities like Flash Fire or Color Change on the Pokémon taking the collateral damage, and remains unaffected by defensive tactics such as protection moves, Endure, held items like the Focus Sash or Focus Band, or Abilities like Wonder Guard, Substitute, Destiny Bond, or Telepathy. Nonetheless, because it is considered effect damage, it *is* entirely nullified by the Magic Guard ability. Crucially, this supplementary damage is incapable of missing. Moreover, Flame Burst is noted to inflict 10 HP damage on any foes positioned immediately next to the target.

Visually, when used by Chandelure, Flame Burst involves the Pokémon orienting away from its adversary before launching an arcing fiery projectile. This missile then detonates in mid-air, creating a vast circular explosion. Should the move be charged, the projectile travels an extended distance, culminating in an explosion upon striking the ground.

In the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Flame Burst functions as Charmander's initial offensive capability, belonging to Charizard's evolutionary line. The user unleashes a forward-bursting flame that detonates upon striking the first opposing Pokémon, thereby inflicting damage and applying a burn effect to foes within a modest area. Successfully connecting with an enemy grants the user a brief increase in movement speed. This burn condition persists for 4 seconds and diminishes the affected enemy's Attack stat by 5%. Once Charmander reaches level 5 and evolves into Charmeleon, Flame Burst is subsequently replaced by a choice between Flamethrower and Fire Punch.

Within Pokémon Conquest (full title: Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~), the artificial intelligence exhibits notable deficiencies in its application of Flame Burst, largely failing to differentiate between the move's primary damage output and its secondary effect damage.

Flame Burst holds the distinction of being the sole Fire-type move, aside from signature moves and Z-Moves, that cannot be utilized in the games Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Sludge Wave (move)

Originating in Generation V, the damage-dealing Poison-type technique known as Sludge Wave (Japanese: ヘドロウェーブ) made its debut. Trainers could acquire it as TM34, available from Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. This move, in addition to causing damage, offers a 10% likelihood of inflicting the poisoned status on its recipient. Its reach extends to every Pokémon present in Double Battles, and in Triple Battles, it targets all combatants positioned next to the user. The area of effect for Sludge Wave originates from the user, much like the spread of Bulldoze and Earthquake. While Sludge Wave delivers damage and potentially poisons, the calculation for applying such a status effect is performed uniquely for every Pokémon it hits. An earlier in-game description, current until Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), described its effect as: "Has a very small chance of leaving the target poisoned." Notably, the animated sequence for Sludge Wave, beginning in Generation VII, shares a visual similarity with the Surf animation seen in Generation VI.

Title: Quiver Dance (move)

Debuting in Generation V, Quiver Dance (Japanese: ちょうのまい, rendered as Butterfly Dance) is a non-damaging Bug-type technique. It serves as a special-oriented counterpart to Victory Dance. Activating Quiver Dance boosts the user's Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats, each by one stage. When empowered by a Buginium Z, transforming into Z-Quiver Dance, any of the user's currently lowered stats are reset. This move also specifically elevates the user's Sp. Atk, Sp. Def, and Travel Speed by one stage apiece. Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder

Title: Heavy Slam (move)

Known in Japan as ヘビーボンバー (Heavy Bomber), Heavy Slam is a Steel-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation V. This technique was designated TR79 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM121 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. The power output of Heavy Slam is directly influenced by the relative weights of both the user and its target; specifically, the greater the weight disparity in favor of the user, the more damage is dealt. Should the target have previously used Minimize, Heavy Slam's damage is doubled, and it successfully bypasses all accuracy checks to guarantee a hit. This guaranteed connection is only nullified if the target is currently undergoing the semi-invulnerable turn of another move, such as Dig or Fly. Functionally similar to Heat Crash, Fly, and Bounce, the user of Heavy Slam initially ascends high into the air, thereby momentarily avoiding moves that pass through their position, before forcefully descending upon the adversary. When deployed against the Hyperrogue Ange Floette flowers, Heavy Slam consistently possesses a base power of 120.

In Pokémon GO, Heavy Slam operates as a Charged Attack, having been made available to players since February 16, 2017. Within Pokémon UNITE, Heavy Slam is Snorlax's inaugural ability, acquired by reaching level 5 and choosing to evolve Tackle into it, rather than Flail. The user performs a leap to a designated area, dealing damage upon landing and briefly launching any opposing Pokémon within the impact radius into the air. At level 11, the damage inflicted by this move receives an increase. The episode "A Timeless Encounter!" showcases an image of an Alolan Golem utilizing Heavy Slam within Professor Kukui's distinguished "Book of Pokémon Super Moves".

Among the Pokémon capable of learning Heavy Slam, the heaviest is Copperajah, especially when equipped with the Heavy Metal Ability, which confers an effective weight of 2866 lbs. This is closely followed by Celesteela, which itself weighs 2204.4 lbs. Conversely, Flapple holds the distinction of being the lightest Pokémon capable of mastering this move, with a weight of merely 2.2 lbs.

Title: Synchronoise (move)
A Psychic-type offensive technique, Synchronoise (シンクロノイズ Synchro Noise in Japanese) made its debut in Generation V. This move inflicts harm upon all neighboring Pokémon possessing a type in common with the user. When the user is multi-typed, any Pokémon sharing *any* of its types will suffer damage. Its base power was adjusted, increasing from 70 to 120, while its PP count decreased from 15 to 10.
