Within Contest Spectaculars, Synchronoise contributes to a combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Reflect Type preceded it during the previous turn. The move fails to be effective if the user lacks any types (for instance, a pure Fire-type employing Burn Up), even when an adjacent target also possesses no types. Fundamentally, Synchronoise targets and damages adjacent foes that possess a shared type with the initiator. In Pokémon GO, Synchronoise functions as a Charged Attack, accessible since August 3, 2019. Its English designation combines "synchronize" and "noise," a linguistic blending from which its Japanese and Korean counterparts are directly transliterated. Conversely, its German, Italian, Brazilian Portuguese, and Spanish appellations are direct translations. Given the inherent properties of Synchronoise and the Dark type's immunity to Psychic-type attacks, pure Dark-type Pokémon (with Umbreon being the sole species capable of naturally learning it) are typically unable to execute this move effectively. Notwithstanding the inclusion of "noise" in its title, Synchronoise is classified as a non-sound-based maneuver.

Title: Electro Ball (move)

A damaging Electric-type attack, Electro Ball (Japanese: エレキボール Elecball) made its debut in Generation V. Trainers could teach this move via TR80 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and it serves as TM072 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Its power output is directly proportional to how much faster the move's user is than its intended target. All applicable Speed modifiers are factored into this calculation, encompassing stat stages, status conditions like paralysis, held items such as the Iron Ball, and various Abilities like Slush Rush. However, Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof Ability are entirely unaffected by Electro Ball.

In Contest Spectaculars, Electro Ball contributes to a combination, granting the user an additional three appeal points if a move like Agility, Autotomize, Calm Mind, Charge, Rock Polish, or Shell Smash was performed on the preceding turn. Its damage calculation is tied to the user's and target's Speed stats; should the user's Speed exceed the target's, the attack will deliver 50% more damage. Within Gates to Infinity, where the Speed stat is absent, Travel Speed serves as its substitute for this move's calculation. Contrary to the move's description, its effect does not escalate in intensity over time.

Moving to Pokémon UNITE, Electro Ball becomes available to Pikachu at level 4, acquired by evolving Thunder Shock rather than Thunder. The user launches an electric orb towards a selected foe, which, upon striking, damages and applies a slowing paralysis effect to all adversaries within a small blast radius surrounding the initial target. Furthermore, it inflicts additional damage proportional to the opponent's missing HP. Its damage output experiences an enhancement once level 11 is reached.

Within the episode To Catch a Pokémon Smuggler!, Officer Jenny stated her Manectric possessed knowledge of Electro Ball, though the attack was never visibly employed by the Pokémon. Excluding Z-Moves, Electro Ball possesses the potential for the highest base power among all Electric-type moves, reaching a maximum of 150.

Title: Soak (move)

This entry focuses on the Pokémon move "Soak," distinguishing it from the "Soak (Field Move)" found in Pokémon Ranger titles. "みずびたし" (Flood) in Japanese, Soak is a non-damaging, Water-type move introduced in Generation V. Its primary function is to alter the target's type to pure Water-type. This transformation persists throughout the battle, unless the affected Pokémon undergoes a form change or transformation, which will override the effect.

Soak will successfully execute even against a Pokémon that is already a pure Water-type. However, when applied to a pure Water-type, while the move's animation plays, the game will indicate that it has failed. Soak fails outright if directed at a substitute or a Pokémon possessing the Multitype or RKS System Abilities.

In Contest Spectaculars, Soak contributes to combinations; an additional three appeal points are awarded if Rain Dance was performed in the preceding turn. When upgraded into its Z-Move variant, Z-Soak, using a Waterium Z, the user's Special Attack stat increases by one stage. A unique interaction occurs with Mimikyu: if its Disguise is active when hit by Soak, the Water typing imparted by the move will be lost if the Disguise subsequently breaks.

In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Soak, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by player-caught Pokémon. Boss Pyukumuku notably utilized Soak in this game.

While Soak itself has not appeared in the animated series, the concept of a Pokémon becoming wet and thereby losing its Ground-type immunity to Electric-type moves has been depicted. Examples include Brock's Onix in "Showdown in Pewter City," Pryce's Piloswine in "Nice Pryce, Baby!," and Hapu's Mudsdale in "A Grand Debut!," where getting wet was explicitly stated to replicate Soak's effect. The Pokémon pool capable of learning Soak consists solely of those that are either Water-type themselves or belong to one of the Water Egg Groups.

Title: Flame Charge (move)

Known as ニトロチャージ (Nitro Charge) in Japanese, Flame Charge is a Fire-type attack that deals damage, first appearing in Generation V. It was available as TM43 from its introduction in Generation V up until Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. In the main series of games, this technique inflicts harm upon an opponent while also boosting the user's Speed stat by one stage.

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Flame Charge exhibits unique properties. Its current effect involves dealing damage and may enhance the user's Travel Speed by a single stage. Previously, before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, the move's description in UNITE stated: "Raises the user's Speed."

In Pokémon UNITE, Flame Charge functions as Talonflame's primary move and Cinderace's secondary move. Talonflame gains access to it by evolving into Fletchinder at level 5, replacing Peck with Flame Charge instead of Aerial Ace. When Talonflame uses it, the user dashes forward, damaging foes encountered along its trajectory, and receives a speed boost following the dash. Upon reaching level 11, for a brief period after Flame Charge successfully lands, the user's abilities and basic attacks will partially bypass enemies' Defense for the subsequent 3 seconds (with the Defense reduction capped at 0), and enemies struck will experience a 30% slow for 5 seconds.

Cinderace obtains Flame Charge upon attaining level 8, substituting Low Sweep for it rather than Feint. When Cinderace performs the move, it charges forward, causing damage to all hit enemies. At level 13, the boosted attack that follows the conclusion of this move will also temporarily slow any enemies it strikes.

In the manga series *Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder*, within the chapters "The Party Crasher" and "Guzma the Destroyer," it was noted that Gladion's Type: Null possessed knowledge of Flame Charge, although its execution was depicted off-panel.

Title: Coil (move)

This entry pertains to the move Coil. For the Pokémon referred to as Coil in Japanese, please consult Magnemite (Pokémon). Coil (Japanese: とぐろをまく Coil Up) is a non-damaging Poison-type move that debuted in Generation V. When used, Coil increases the user's Attack, Defense, and accuracy statistics by one stage each. If utilized with a Poisonium Z, becoming Z-Coil, all of the user's diminished stats are completely reset. Coil, in conjunction with Hone Claws and Acupressure, stands as one of the only moves that can raise a Pokémon's accuracy stat. Befitting its designation, most Pokémon capable of learning this move typically feature a serpentine physical form.

Title: Low Sweep (move)

For the move known in English as Low Kick, please refer to Low Kick. Low Sweep (Japanese: ローキック Low Kick) is a damage-dealing Fighting-type attack that debuted in Generation V. It was designated as TM47 from Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently became TM27 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This technique inflicts damage and simultaneously decreases the opponent's Speed stat by one level. It also causes harm and diminishes the foe's Travel Speed by one stage. A prior description, active before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), simply stated: "Lowers the target's Speed."

Within Pokémon UNITE, Low Sweep functions as Cinderace's secondary ability. It involves the user performing a rapid low sweep towards a chosen direction, harming any adversaries caught in its path. Upon attaining level 8, this particular move evolves into either Flame Charge or Feint.

The conceptualization of Low Sweep appears to draw from diverse martial arts disciplines, including Muay Thai or the broader MMA category, which incorporate leg kicks aimed at impairing an adversary's equilibrium and movement. The game's mechanic of decreasing the target's speed after a successful strike directly mirrors the real-world consequences of such blows, frequently leaving combatants visibly impaired in their gait. Conversely, the Japanese designation for the move Low Kick—けたぐり (Ketaguri), signifying "pulling inside ankle sweep"—denotes a sumo maneuver designed to fell an opponent. This technique's primary objective is to force a combatant to the ground, a decisive act for achieving victory in a sumo bout. This distinct origin explains Low Kick's unique game mechanic of inflicting damage proportional to the target's mass.

Title: Acid Spray (move)

The Poison-type move Acid Spray (Japanese: アシッドボム Acid Bomb) first appeared in Generation V as a damaging attack. It is designated as TM013 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM112 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used, Acid Spray delivers damage and subsequently lowers the target's Special Defense by two stages. However, it fails to affect Pokémon that possess the Bulletproof ability. Furthermore, this move deals damage and actively reduces the opponent's Sp. Defense by two stages. In Pokémon GO, Acid Spray functions as a Charged Attack and has been obtainable since March 19, 2019.

Title: Foul Play (move)

Foul Play (Japanese: イカサマ Foul Play) is a Dark-type damaging attack first introduced in Generation V. It was also available as TM21 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move inflicts damage by uniquely calculating its power based on the target's Attack stat rather than the user's. The target's Attack stat stage-modifiers are applied to this calculation, but all other pertinent Attack modifiers, such as those from the user's held item, Ability, or a burn status, are still accounted for from the user's side. For instance, an Ability like Huge Power or a held Choice Band on the user will increase the stat value employed in this calculation, whereas such buffs on the target will not.

In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Foul Play functions as a Charged Attack and has been available since February 16, 2017.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Foul Play serves as Umbreon's initial move, obtained when evolving into Umbreon at level 4 by upgrading Swift instead of Mean Look. The user performs a charge that damages any opposing Pokémon it connects with and reduces their movement speed. If this charge successfully hits an opponent, the move can be used a second time. Activating the move again deals damage, further decreases movement speed, and shoves hit opposing Pokémon. This second stage's damage scales up based on the Attack stat of the opposing Pokémon struck by the first stage; a higher Attack stat on the opponent results in greater damage. Additionally, upon reaching level 10, Umbreon's next basic attack after using Foul Play becomes a boosted attack.

Although Foul Play is not a usable move in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it receives a mention during Side Mission 175: "Romance avec Purrloin," where it is notably referred to as "the move that Purrloin surprisingly can't learn on its own."

Title: Simple Beam (move)

Known in Japanese as シンプルビーム (Simple Beam), this non-damaging Normal-type technique was introduced in Generation V. Its primary function is to alter the target's Ability to Simple. If a Pokémon affected by Simple Beam is subsequently switched out of battle, its original Ability is restored. The move Simple Beam will fail if the target's Ability is Simple, Truant, Multitype, Zen Mode (Generation VII onward), Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, or As One. A Pokémon with Simple as its Ability experiences double the effect when its stat stages are either increased or decreased. Should Simple Beam be powered up by a Normalium Z, becoming Z-Simple Beam, the user's Special Attack stat gains one stage.

Title: Entrainment (move)

Entrainment, known as なかまづくり (Befriend) in Japanese, is a non-damaging Normal-type status move that first appeared in Generation V. This move's core effect is to alter the target Pokémon's Ability, making it an exact copy of the user's Ability. The modification to the target's Ability is not permanent; if the Pokémon affected by Entrainment is subsequently withdrawn from battle, its original Ability will be fully restored.

Several specific situations will cause Entrainment to fail. The move proves ineffective if the user and the target Pokémon already possess identical Abilities. It also fails when directed at Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon. Furthermore, Entrainment will not succeed if the target's current Ability is any of the following: Truant, Multitype, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, Wonder Guard, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, Ice Face, Gulp Missile, As One, Zero to Hero, Commander, Orichalcum Pulse, or Hadron Engine. Similarly, Entrainment cannot be executed if the user itself has one of these Abilities: Trace, Forecast, Flower Gift, Zen Mode, Illusion, Imposter, Power of Alchemy, Receiver, Disguise, Power Construct, Ice Face, Hunger Switch, Gulp Missile, Neutralizing Gas, As One, Zero to Hero, or Commander.

When amplified by a Normalium Z, Entrainment transforms into Z-Entrainment, which bestows upon the user a one-stage increase in its Special Defense stat. Beyond battles, Entrainment functions as a component in Contest Spectacular combinations. If any of the moves—Circle Throw, Seismic Toss, Sky Drop, Smack Down, Storm Throw, or Vital Throw—are performed in the turn immediately following Entrainment, they will earn an additional three appeal points.

Title: After You (move)

This move, known in Japanese as おさきにどうぞ (After You), made its debut in Generation V and inflicts no damage, being categorized as a Normal-type technique. Its primary effect is to compel the designated target to execute its action immediately, irrespective of typical move priority rules, during the present turn. This move possesses perfect accuracy, circumventing standard accuracy checks, ensuring it connects unless the opposing Pokémon is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. A critical condition for its failure is if the selected Pokémon has already acted within that very turn. Furthermore, it disregards any effects imposed by the move Quash. When enhanced by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-After You, the Pokémon using it experiences a one-stage increase in its Speed stat. The move is subject to failure if, following its execution, the turn sequence remains unaltered. Conversely, a subsequent development indicates that After You will no longer fail even if the turn order persists as before. Intriguingly, After You possesses an identical effect to Baton Pass, wherein the user exchanges places with a fellow team member, granting the incoming ally a randomly determined stat enhancement. Within the context of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, the user's action involves swapping positions with the teammate directly ahead, or, if initiated by an opposing entity, with a random teammate present on the dungeon floor. For Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, the user performs a switch with a teammate located anywhere on the map who holds the primary position on the team roster, or with the teammate listed second if the user itself occupies the first slot.

Title: Round (move)

Known in Japan as りんしょう Round, this Normal-type attack debuted in Generation V and delivers damage. It functioned as TM48 from its introduction up through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, then became TM76 for Pokémon Sword and Shield. Round targets and damages a single opponent positioned directly ahead. A unique mechanic of Round allows consecutive uses within a single turn to synchronize: if another Pokémon employs Round before the user, the user immediately follows with its own Round, bypassing standard Speed order. Furthermore, every subsequent Round after the initial one in a turn receives a substantial power boost, doubling its base power to 120. If a Pokémon behind a substitute is targeted, Round impacts that substitute. Separately, Round is now capable of hitting Pokémon even when they are behind a substitute. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, a different effect occurs: if a teammate in the same room also uses Round in the same turn, this move's power is increased by 50% for that turn. Additionally, within Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, utilizing Round applies a unique 'Round' status effect to the user, persisting for one turn. The move's designation draws from vocal rounds, a musical canon characterized by two voices singing harmoniously.

Title: Echoed Voice (move)

Echoed Voice, known in Japan as エコーボイス (Echo Voice), is a Generation V addition, functioning as a Normal-type damage-dealing attack. From Generation V up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, this move was assigned as Technical Machine 49 (TM49). With each successive turn it's utilized, its base power escalates by 40, capping at a maximum of 200. Once this peak is attained, subsequent continuous uses will merely sustain the amplified power level. Crucially, its power does not increment within the same battle round; it only gains power a single time per round, irrespective of the number of Pokémon that employed it. The base power still rises as long as the move is performed, even if it fails to connect or deal damage (for instance, if the target is shielded). It's not mandatory for the same Pokémon to use the move each turn for its power to grow. Within the context of Tera Raid Battles, the move's power remains constant at 40, without any cumulative increase. When used against a Pokémon protected by a Substitute, it will strike the Substitute. However, as a sound-based move, Echoed Voice can also inflict damage upon the Pokémon itself, bypassing the Substitute. All hostile Pokémon positioned next to the user will incur damage from Echoed Voice. The attack's power receives a 50% boost if a fellow Pokémon on the same floor employs it within the identical turn. Specifically in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, an additional power surge occurs if this was the user's most recent move executed on that particular floor. Among sound-based moves and those without detrimental effects on the user (excluding Z-Moves), Echoed Voice is capable of achieving the peak base power. Through consistent application over several turns, its base power will culminate at 200. This potential can be further augmented by the ability Punk Rock, enabling it to hit an impressive base power of 260.

Title: Chip Away (move)

The Normal-type attack, Chip Away (Japanese: なしくずし; "Little by Little"), was introduced to the Pokémon world in Generation V as a damaging offensive option. This particular move calculates its damage output by completely disregarding any modifications to the target's Defense and evasion stat stages, whether they are positive or negative adjustments. Despite this, it does not bypass other forms of stat alteration; for instance, it fails to ignore effects that do not use stat stages, such as the barrier created by Reflect, the impact of Abilities like Sand Veil, or the defensive boost provided by items like Eviolite. Moreover, Chip Away's effectiveness remains subject to any changes affecting the user's own Attack or accuracy statistics.

Title: Clear Smog (move)

Appearing first in Generation V, the Poison-type offensive move Clear Smog (known as クリアスモッグ in Japanese) delivers damage. Beyond its damaging effect, this attack also neutralizes all stat level modifications on its chosen target, bringing them back to 0. Unlike Haze, which influences all Pokémon on the field, Clear Smog targets just one adversary. The stat-resetting property will not activate if the target manages to avoid the attack due to a type immunity, protective measures, or if it is shielded by a substitute. Nonetheless, it successfully applies its effect to a Max Raid Battle boss, even when the boss's shield is active. This move inherently possesses perfect accuracy, always connecting unless the opposing Pokémon is in a semi-invulnerable state from using moves like Dig or Fly. Furthermore, holding a Covert Cloak offers no defense against Clear Smog's ability to remove stat changes. In essence, Clear Smog inflicts damage and reverts any positive or negative stat adjustments made to the opponent.

Title: Stored Power (move)

Stored Power (Japanese: アシストパワー Assist Power) is a damaging Psychic-type attack that debuted in Generation V. It was cataloged as TR82 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the identifier TM041 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

This ability has an intrinsic base power of 20, but it accumulates an additional 20 power for each elevated stat stage the user possesses. Stat reductions below the normal threshold do not influence its base power. Its absolute maximum possible base power reaches an impressive 860. This peak is achieved when all seven of the user's attributes—Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, Speed, evasion, and accuracy—are boosted to their highest six stages.

Stored Power can also be incorporated into a Contest Spectacular combination, where the performer is granted three extra appeal points if Amnesia, Calm Mind, Cotton Guard, Hone Claws, or Nasty Plot was utilized on the preceding turn.

(Description prior to version 2.20.0): The greater the user's stat increases, the more potent this move becomes. Notably, the user's stat boosts are consumed by this move, rather than being lowered. Consequently, preventing the removal of these stat increases through effects like Dauntless is not possible. This move operates with a power multiplier that begins at ×1; for every rise in the user's stat rank (which includes critical-hit rate), this multiplier increases by one. The multiplier can reach its apex of ×12 if the user's stats have been raised by a combined total of eleven or more ranks.

In Pokémon UNITE, Stored Power functions as Espeon's primary move. It is acquired upon evolving from Eevee at level 4, specifically by upgrading Swift into it instead of Psyshock. The user releases bolts of psychic energy at randomly chosen enemies within the designated area, inflicting damage and temporarily impeding their movement speed. One psychic blast is stockpiled every 3 seconds, or each time the user connects with an empowered basic attack. A maximum of 5 psychic blasts can be held concurrently. After activating this move, the user's movement speed sees a 1-second enhancement. Targets struck by a psychic blast will receive 70% less damage from subsequent hits. At level 10, the move inflicts additional damage on targets whose HP has fallen below half.

Within Giant Chasm, Gorm instructed his Sigilyph to use Stored Power, though the execution of the move occurred off-panel. When accounting for all achievable positive stat modifications, Stored Power claims the highest base power among all special moves.

Title: Quick Guard (move)

Hailing from Generation V, Quick Guard (known in Japanese as ファストガード Fast Guard) is a Fighting-type maneuver that inflicts no damage. This move shields all allied Pokémon from heightened priority attacks used within the current turn, including examples like Quick Attack and Aqua Jet. Should the user act last during the turn, this maneuver will not succeed. Certain attacks designed to bypass defensive measures will dispel Quick Guard's effects; notably, Feint, while possessing priority, will inflict its full damage and eliminate the safeguard. Furthermore, the Ability Unseen Fist permits contact moves to circumvent Quick Guard's defense. A separate category of status moves exists which remain unaffected by protective measures. This generally holds true when such moves gain priority through an Ability like Prankster. With a +3 priority rating, Quick Guard executes ahead of the vast majority of other actions. The subsequent descriptions pertain exclusively to this particular generation. Quick Guard's success rate diminishes with each successful, consecutive application by the user of Endure, other self-targeting protection moves, Quick Guard itself, or Wide Guard. On each such instance, the probability of success is halved (multiplied by 1/2). Quick Guard is ineffective against moves whose priority is artificially boosted by Prankster when they typically lack such an advantage. Should a Pokémon employ Feint or Shadow Force to strike its own ally under Quick Guard's protection, the defensive effect will persist. When amplified by a Fightinium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Quick Guard, the user's Defense stat receives a one-stage increase. The user bestows upon itself and its allies within the same physical space the Quick Guard status condition, lasting for a single turn. This protective status shields Pokémon from attacks originating from beyond an adjacent square. Nevertheless, Pokémon can still incur damage from items or explosive effects. The likelihood of this move failing increases if the user's immediate preceding action was either Quick Guard itself or an evasion-oriented maneuver (like Detect or Endure). During the episode "Evolution Exchange Excitement!", Bianca's Escavalier was disclosed to possess knowledge of Quick Guard, though its actual usage has never been depicted.

Title: Ally Switch (move)

A Psychic-type technique, Ally Switch (known as サイドチェンジ, or Side Change, in Japanese), made its debut in Generation V, and it causes no direct damage. In Generation V, it was assigned as TM51, and later became TR83 within Pokémon Sword and Shield. This move allows the user to swap positions with their directly opposing ally and possesses increased priority. Conditions for its failure include the user being positioned in the center of a Triple Battle arena, the absence of an ally directly across from them, or its deployment in Single, Multi, or Rotation Battles, as well as Horde Encounters. Should a Pokémon initially designate an ally as its target, but subsequently find itself targeting its own position due to Ally Switch, that move will invariably fail upon execution (this holds true even for moves like Acupressure). However, specific exceptions exist: Snipe Shot and the Abilities Stalwart and Propeller Tail bypass Ally Switch's effect, successfully striking their original intended target. When boosted by a Psychium Z to become Z-Ally Switch, the user's Speed characteristic experiences an increase of two stages. Subsequent, successive uses of Ally Switch will now diminish its probability of success with each attempt.

Ally Switch operates with the same effect as Baton Pass. In this scenario, the user trades positions with a teammate, and the ally taking their place receives a random boost to one of their stats. The user will initiate this swap with any teammate on the field who is ordered first on the team's roster (or second on the list if the user is initially ordered first). Any gendered Pokémon capable of acquiring Ally Switch via TM in Generation V can then learn it through breeding in Generations VI and VII. Within Pokémon X and Y, Tyrogue is coded to inherit Ally Switch as an Egg Move; however, due to Tyrogue being exclusively male and none of its evolutionary forms possessing alternative learning methods for Ally Switch, there are no legal methods to pass this move. Furthermore, despite this programming, Tyrogue and its evolved forms cannot obtain Ally Switch via Move Tutor services in Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, nor through TRs in Sword and Shield.

Title: Scald (move)

Known in Japanese as ねっとう (Boiling Water), Scald is a Water-type offensive technique first appearing in Generation V that inflicts damage. It was designated TM55 from Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it held the slot of TM29. For Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Scald occupied TM49. This attack deals direct damage and carries a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon with a burn. When used, Scald also has the immediate effect of thawing a frozen user, an effect that applies even if the attack doesn't connect with its target. Beyond its offensive output, Scald possesses the potential to inflict a burn on the adversary. Furthermore, deploying Scald will remove the frozen status from the Pokémon using it. Its in-game description prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, stated: "Removes the frozen condition. Has a moderate chance of leaving the target burned."

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, Scald functions as one of Slowbro's initial abilities. Players acquire this move after Slowpoke evolves into Slowbro at level 4, selecting to upgrade Water Gun into Scald rather than Surf. Upon activation, the Pokémon unleashes a jet of scalding water, inflicting damage on any foes struck. A steam cloud then forms at the point where the initial enemy was hit, subsequently slowing and burning all adversaries caught within its area. When the Pokémon reaches level 11, the dimensions of this steam cloud are enhanced.

Before the launch of Pokémon X and Y, promotional material, specifically a banner displayed at the Summer 2013 World Hobby Fair, depicted Lapras employing Scald. However, Lapras was ultimately unable to learn this move in the games' official release.

Title: Shell Smash (move)

Known in Japanese as からをやぶる (Shell Break), Shell Smash is a Normal-type status move that first appeared in Generation V. Upon activation, it reduces the user's Defense and Special Defense by a single stage, while simultaneously boosting their Attack, Special Attack, and Speed stats by two stages apiece. Within Contest Spectaculars, Shell Smash is an integral part of a combination; employing it on one turn will grant an additional three appeal points to subsequent uses of Baton Pass or Electro Ball in the following turn. When augmented by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Shell Smash, any stats previously lowered on the user are restored to their original values. Executing Shell Smash results in a one-stage reduction for the user's Defense and Sp. Def, counterbalanced by a two-stage increase for their Attack, Sp. Atk, and movement speed.

Within the Pokémon UNITE game, Shell Smash serves as Crustle's initial ability. Crustle gains access to this move upon evolving from Dwebble at level 4, choosing to upgrade Rock Slide into it rather than Rock Tomb. The Pokémon shatters its own shell, thereby reducing its Defense and Sp. Defense to a base value of 0. Subsequently, its Attack and Sp. Attack are enhanced by an amount equivalent to 40% of the Defense and Sp. Defense that were removed, respectively. Crucially, any Defense and Sp. Defense stats not derived from the Pokémon's inherent base stats – such as those provided by held items, active buffs, or the user's passive ability – remain unaffected and are exempt from this calculation. A notable increase in overall speed is also conferred upon the user. By level 11, this percentage multiplier for Attack and Sp. Attack enhancement rises to 50% of the Defense and Sp. Defense lost.

Shell Smash is unique in that it instigates the largest net change of base stats from a neutral state (no changes), resulting in a total shift of 8 stages. Nevertheless, Belly Drum possesses the potential to exceed a +8 stat change, provided the user's Attack stat is already reduced by a minimum of three stages; it can achieve a maximum +12 change if activated from an Attack stat at -6. Interestingly, Huntail is capable of utilizing Shell Smash despite no longer possessing a visible shell following its evolution from Clamperl.

Title: Heal Pulse (move)

Known as いやしのはどう (Healing Pulse) in Japanese, Heal Pulse is a Psychic-type status move that causes no direct damage and was first introduced in Generation V. Its primary function is to replenish the target's HP, restoring up to 50% of their maximum health, a figure that increases to 75% when the user possesses the Mega Launcher Ability. This move reliably connects, bypassing standard accuracy checks; the only exceptions are when the target is temporarily immune during the semi-invulnerable phase of moves like Dig or Fly. In Triple Battles, it exhibits the unusual property of being able to target Pokémon that are not adjacent to the user.

However, Heal Pulse cannot be activated if either the user or the target is under the effect of Heal Block. Should this occur, and no other usable moves are available (for instance, due to Disable, a Choice Band restriction, or insufficient PP), the Pokémon will instead resort to using Struggle. The move will also fail to execute if the target's HP is already at its maximum or if the target is protected by a substitute. When upgraded by a Psychium Z into Z-Heal Pulse, all of the user's lowered stats are reset, and the healing effect will proceed even if Heal Block is affecting either the user or the target.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Heal Pulse operates somewhat differently: in Gates to Infinity, it restores 25% of the target's maximum HP, a value that then increases to 40% in Super Mystery Dungeon. When augmented by the Mega Launcher Ability in these spin-off titles, it will heal 48% of the target's max HP. Within Pokémon UNITE, Heal Pulse is Blissey's initial move when it is still Chansey. It allows the user to continuously recover its own HP and mend the health of a designated ally for a brief period. Once Chansey evolves at level 4, Heal Pulse is replaced by either Soft-Boiled or Safeguard. Uniquely, Heal Pulse holds the distinction of being the sole move that is classified as both a status aura and a pulse move.

Title: Hex (move)

Known in Japanese as たたりめ (Evil Eye), Hex is an offensive Ghost-type technique that made its debut in Generation V. This move held the designation of TM77 during the Pokémon Sword and Shield era, and currently functions as TM029 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Initially, Hex possessed a base power of 50, which would double to 100 if the opponent was inflicted with a non-volatile status condition. This base power was subsequently amplified to 65, allowing it to double to 130 under similar circumstances. Furthermore, Hex participates in Contest Spectacular combinations, awarding its user an additional three appeal points. This bonus is gained if, in the preceding turn, either a status-inducing status move (excluding Psycho Shift and Rest) or one of the specified damaging moves (Force Palm, Inferno, Nuzzle, or Zap Cannon) had been performed. When Hex strikes, it inflicts damage, with its destructive power gaining an additional 50% if the target is already burdened by a bad status condition.

In the context of Chandelure's appearances, Hex is listed among its Pokémon moves. Chandelure employs this attack by remotely seizing and assailing the adversary, thereby diminishing their recoverable HP. Should the opponent's stats be debuffed, the move's damage output further escalates.

Within the mobile game Pokémon GO, Hex functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible to players since February 16, 2017. An earlier description, preceding Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), stated: "Its power is doubled if the target is affected by a status condition." The description found before Version 2.20.0 (dated April 27, 2022) read: "This move's power is doubled when the target is affected by a status condition."

Moving to Pokémon UNITE, Hex serves as Gengar's secondary ability. It becomes available by evolving into Haunter at level 5, offering an upgrade path from Lick as an alternative to Dream Eater. The user disappears and warps to a specified area, dealing damage to foes within the radius of their reappearance. Should this strike an adversary already under a status condition, its damage output is boosted, and its cooldown period is shortened. Further enhancing its capabilities, at level 11, its damage receives an additional increase.

Notably, Hex stands alone as the sole Ghost-type attack introduced during Generation V. It also holds the distinction of being the only move possessing a three-letter English name that is restricted to in-battle use.

Title: Sky Drop (move)

The damage-dealing, Flying-type move known as Sky Drop (フリーフォール Free Fall in Japanese) made its debut in Generation V. From Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it held the Technical Machine designation TM58. Upon selection, Sky Drop causes its target to ascend into the air. During this airborne state, the target is rendered immobile. Both the move's initiator and the airborne target evade all incoming attacks, with the exceptions of Gust, Thunder, Twister, Sky Uppercut, Hurricane, and Smack Down. The following turn, the target is dropped, sustaining damage. Although Sky Drop deals no damage to Flying-type Pokémon, it still lifts them on its initial turn, incapacitating them.

In Triple Battles, Sky Drop has the capability to select non-adjacent Pokémon as targets. While allies can be targeted by Sky Drop, the move will invariably fail if used against them. Despite being airborne, Pokémon can still be struck by moves if the Ability No Guard is active, or if Lock-On or Mind Reader were previously employed. Sky Drop will not succeed if the target is protected by a Substitute, or if it's already in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Fly, Bounce, or a preceding Sky Drop.

Sky Drop's execution is impossible under the influence of Gravity. Activation of Gravity during Sky Drop's semi-invulnerable turn will cancel the move, causing both Pokémon to descend. While in Sky Drop's semi-invulnerable state, both the user and the target are now susceptible to Thousand Arrows.

In Generation V (specifically Black, White, Black 2, and White 2), a notable glitch emerged in Double and Triple Battles: if Gravity was activated while two Pokémon were simultaneously in Sky Drop's semi-invulnerable state, the move's user would descend, but its target would become trapped and immobile in the semi-invulnerable condition. This incapacitation would persist until Gravity's effect ceased, the Sky Drop user either switched out or was knocked out, or the affected Pokémon was knocked out or struck by one of the specific counter-moves (Gust, Thunder, Twister, Sky Uppercut, Hurricane, or Smack Down). As a direct consequence of this glitch, Sky Drop faced a ban from Generation V Wi-Fi Random Matches starting November 10, 2010, and from all official tournaments thereafter.

If a Pokémon bound to a multi-turn move is lifted by Sky Drop, it will cease its locked-in action upon release. If that move typically induces confusion from fatigue upon completion, that confusion will manifest immediately. Protect and Detect offer defense against Sky Drop's initial action, but once airborne, the affected Pokémon is incapable of using any moves, including Protect. The Power Herb has no effect on Sky Drop, nor is it consumed by the move. Neither Follow Me nor Rage Powder can redirect Sky Drop. Should a Pokémon drawing attention via Follow Me or Rage Powder be lifted by Sky Drop, it ceases to attract further moves.

During a Rotation Battle, if Sky Drop's target is rotated out on the move's second turn, the Pokémon will fall into its newly rotated slot without taking damage, while the Pokémon rotated in retains its ability to attack. Sky Drop fails to lift targets weighing 440.9 lbs. (200 kg) or more. After a target is successfully lifted, its weight no longer impacts the success of that Sky Drop instance; even if the airborne target surpasses the weight threshold, the move will not fail.

An airborne Pokémon under the effect of Sky Drop can choose any of its moves at the turn's start, yet it is prevented from Mega Evolving or Ultra Bursting, even if those options would normally be available. Neither Wimp Out nor Emergency Exit Abilities will activate if a Pokémon's HP drops below half during Sky Drop's semi-invulnerable turn. For Contest Spectaculars, Sky Drop contributes to a combination, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if Entrainment, Hold Hands, or Play Nice was employed the preceding turn.

Another glitch, present in Pokémon Sun and Moon, could render a Pokémon an "undead" invulnerable entity if Sky Drop was used on it. This particular bug manifested when Sky Drop targeted a Pokémon simultaneously employing Spiky Shield. Spiky Shield would inflict damage upon the Sky Drop user, yet the game failed to register the move's action. This anomaly resulted in the attacking Pokémon reaching 0 HP, skipping its turn entirely, and caused all subsequent moves against the glitched Pokémon to fail.

Sky Drop targets the opposing Pokémon directly in front, lifting both the user (imparting the Sky Drop status) and the enemy (suspending it) into the air. While the user is airborne, it becomes immune to most attacks, with the exceptions of moves like Gust, Thunder, Twister, and Sky Uppercut. Additionally, projectiles will bypass the user as though its space were unoccupied. On the subsequent turn, the enemy is brought down and damaged.

As a two-turn maneuver, Sky Drop cannot be linked with other actions. Among moves requiring a charging turn, Sky Drop possesses the lowest power output. In Generation VI, Sky Drop stands as the sole move capable of rendering an opponent's Pokémon entirely immobile during its execution. Sky Drop is one of only two moves featuring a charging turn that are unusable in Generation VIII, the other being Razor Wind. Notably, Sky Drop holds the distinction of being the only move prohibited in an in-game tournament or Battle facility, specifically the Pokémon World Tournament.

Title: Shift Gear (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Shift Gear (Japanese: ギアチェンジ, or Gear Change) is a Steel-type maneuver that does not inflict damage. Until Generation VII, this specific technique was considered a signature move of the Klink Pokémon evolutionary line. When activated, Shift Gear grants the user a two-stage boost to their Speed stat and a one-stage increase to their Attack stat. Furthermore, it plays a role in Contest Spectacular combinations, causing Gear Grind to earn an additional three appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. Should it be augmented by a Steelium Z into Z-Shift Gear, the user's lowered stats are all completely reset. This move additionally enhances the user's Attack by one stage and their Travel Speed by two stages. In Pokémon Rumble Rush, Shift Gear, alongside all other status moves, was exclusively employed by opposing Pokémon and could not be obtained on any captured Pokémon. Boss Klinklang was notably observed to utilize Shift Gear. Despite its description stating "the user rotates its gears," both Amped Form Toxtricity and Cyclizar can naturally acquire this ability, even though they possess no visible gears on their forms.

Title: Circle Throw (move)

A Fighting-type offensive maneuver, Circle Throw (known in Japanese as ともえなげ, or Overhead Throw) made its debut in Generation V of the Pokémon series. Its Technical Machine designation is TM129 within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. This technique not only causes damage but also possesses a lowered action priority. During encounters against other Trainers, Circle Throw compels the opposing Pokémon to be swapped for the subsequent available creature in their party that has not been knocked out (provided one exists). Should the user engage a lone wild Pokémon, employing this move concludes the skirmish if the user's level is at least equivalent to the target's. Conversely, if multiple wild Pokémon are present, its effect mirrors that observed in Trainer battles. The target will not be forcibly withdrawn from battle if the user is knocked out by repercussions from the move's execution (for instance, due to the target holding a Rocky Helmet, or possessing the Abilities Rough Skin or Iron Barbs). The mechanism for ending wild encounters or displacing an opponent's Pokémon fails if the target exhibits the Suction Cups Ability, is affected by Ingrain, or has a Substitute active. Furthermore, Circle Throw contributes to Contest Spectacular combinations, awarding the user an additional three appeal points if a preceding turn featured the application of Entrainment, Hold Hands, Play Nice, Spikes, Stealth Rock, or Toxic Spikes. Against Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon, this move delivers damage but is incapable of compelling a switch-out. Circle Throw inflicts harm and propels the adversary backward across ten tiles. Upon collision with a barrier or another Pokémon, the propelled target (along with the other Pokémon involved) sustains 5 HP in damage. Should this technique be utilized twice within a single turn, Circle Throw strikes the foe on both occasions and displaces them by a maximum of 20 tiles. Within the animated series, Circle Throw manifests as a jujutsu/judo maneuver named "Tomoe nage", wherein the practitioner seizes the adversary high, then falls backward, leveraging their legs' combined strength and momentum to fling the opponent overhead. This action also shares resemblances with a wrestling technique known as "the monkey flip".

Title: Incinerate (move)

First appearing in Generation V, Incinerate (Japanese: やきつくす, meaning 'Complete Burn') is a Fire-type attack that deals damage. For Generations V and VI, it was assigned as Technical Machine TM59. This particular maneuver strikes all foes positioned next to the user. Furthermore, it obliterates any Berries its targets are holding. Notably, an Occa Berry will still take effect prior to its destruction by Incinerate. Conversely, Berries such as the Oran Berry or Rowap Berry, which would typically respond to incoming damage, are eradicated before their effects can be triggered or they can be consumed. Items clutched by a Pokémon possessing the Sticky Hold ability remain impervious to Incinerate's destructive power. The Symbiosis ability will not activate in instances where an item is lost due to this attack. Furthermore, any items incinerated by this move are permanently lost and cannot be retrieved through the abilities Recycle, Pickup, or Harvest. In certain contexts, such as the Mystery Dungeon series, Incinerate not only deals damage but also incinerates a food item carried by an enemy. Should it be directed at the user or an ally in these scenarios, a random food item within their bag will be consumed by fire. Its incarnation as a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO debuted on November 9, 2020. Remarkably, Incinerate holds the distinction of being the sole special move to consistently fail when deployed in Max Raid Battles.

Title: Quash (move)

Known in Japan as さきおくり (Postpone), Quash is a Dark-type status move that first appeared in Generation V. From Generation V up until Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, this move was taught via TM60. A Pokémon targeted by Quash will be forced to move at the very end of the current turn, irrespective of its usual priority, so long as it has not already taken its action. The effects of Quash are disregarded if the affected Pokémon is subsequently targeted by After You. When multiple Pokémon are simultaneously affected by Quash, their actions will proceed according to the sequence in which they were initially subjected to the move. Utilizing a Darkinium Z to transform Quash into Z-Quash bestows a one-stage boost to the user's Speed stat. Should several Pokémon be afflicted by Quash, they will subsequently act from fastest to slowest. Initially, a programming error existed within Double Battles: if the player who did not issue the challenge had only their final Pokémon remaining, Quash would erroneously fail against the opposing Pokémon positioned to the right, irrespective of the move chosen for that turn. The version 1.1 update for Sword and Shield rectified this issue, allowing Quash to now accurately target Pokémon in that specific slot. In contrast to its behavior in the core series titles, Quash induces flinching in its target. This marks it as the sole non-damaging move capable of such an effect. Despite its Generation V debut, no Pokémon originating from that generation can acquire Quash. A significant number of Pokémon capable of learning Quash exhibit a regal motif, including: Nidoqueen, Nidoking, Alolan Meowth, Alolan Persian, Kingler, Slowking, Kingdra, Slaking, Empoleon, Vespiquen, and Palossand.

Title: Acrobatics (move)

This entry pertains to the move introduced in Generation V. For information regarding the Ability whose Japanese name translates as "Acrobatics", consult Unburden (Ability). Acrobatics (Japanese: アクロバット Acrobat) is a damage-dealing Flying-type move that debuted in Generation V. It was assigned as TM62 from Generations V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Acrobatics inflicts damage, and its power surges to 110, effectively doubling, if the user is not holding an item. In Triple Battles, Acrobatics is capable of striking non-adjacent opponents. If the user is equipped with a Flying Gem, the item is consumed before this move verifies whether the Pokémon possesses an item, thus ensuring the power boost still applies. This attack causes damage, and its damage output is increased by 50% / 100% when the user is not holding an item.

In Pokémon GO, Acrobatics functions as a Charged Attack and became obtainable on February 12, 2022. This attack delivers greater damage when the player possesses no Ⓟ.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Acrobatics serves as Talonflame's second initial move. The user glides towards a designated area, assaulting all Pokémon within that zone up to three times. Following the move's conclusion, the user can choose a direction for a subsequent dash. Once Fletchinder evolves into Talonflame at level 7, this move is superseded by either Fly or Brave Bird.

Title: Reflect Type (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Reflect Type (Japanese: ミラータイプ Mirror Type) is a Normal-type status move that inflicts no damage. Its core effect causes the user's elemental typing to become identical to the target's current type or types. If the target has had an additional type bestowed by Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat, Reflect Type will also adopt that Grass or Ghost type, respectively. This type-copying is successful even if the target's true type is masked by the Illusion Ability. The move bypasses standard accuracy checks, ensuring it always lands unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly.

Reflect Type will fail under certain circumstances: if the target possesses no type whatsoever (for example, a pure Fire-type Pokémon that lost its Fire type due to Burn Up) or if the user is Terastallized. However, should such a typeless target also be under the influence of Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat, Reflect Type will instead convert the user into a Normal type, augmented by the corresponding Grass or Ghost type. If the target itself is Terastallized, the user's type will transform to match the target's specific Tera Type. An exception to this rule occurs if the target Terastallized into the Stellar type, in which case the user will assume the target's original type.

As part of a Contest Spectacular combination, using Reflect Type enables Synchronoise to earn an extra three appeal points when performed on the subsequent turn. When powered up by a Normalium Z into its Z-Move form, Z-Reflect Type, the user's Special Attack stat is boosted by one stage. Fundamentally, Reflect Type achieves a direct synchronization of the user's elemental identity with that of its opponent. This move's effect is analogous to Conversion's mechanics as they operated in Generation I. From Generation II onward, however, Conversion's effect shifted to change the user's type to match one of its own moves (with the exception of Curse, during the period it was a ???-type move).

Title: Retaliate (move)

Retaliate (Japanese: かたきうち, literally "Blood Revenge") is an offensive Normal-type technique first appearing in Generation V. It held the designation of TM67 throughout Generations V and VI, and later became TM79 in the Pokémon Sword and Shield titles. This move deals damage to its target. Its standard base power of 70 escalates dramatically to 140 if an allied Pokémon from the user's party was knocked out during the turn immediately preceding its use. For Pokémon that possess knowledge of this move, their Attack stat is observed to rise significantly when a teammate faints. The Japanese name for this move, かたきうち (Katakiuchi), references a custom prevalent among samurai households during Japan's Edo period, where a samurai was legally entitled to seek retribution for the murder of an immediate family member, such as a parent or elder sibling, as a form of justice. This cultural background might account for the move's animated sequence from Generation VI onwards. It is hypothesized that Retaliate was initially conceived as a Dark-type move. A lingering vestige of this intention exists in the Japanese editions of Black and White, where TM67 was erroneously depicted as a Dark-type TM.

Title: Final Gambit (move)

Originating in Generation V, Final Gambit (Japanese: いのちがけ Life Risk) is a Fighting-type attack that deals damage. This maneuver causes the user to faint, while concurrently striking the target for damage equivalent to the user's remaining HP *prior* to the move's execution. Should the attack fail to connect with the opponent—due to a miss, type immunity, or protective measures—Final Gambit will cease its effect, and the user will not faint.

The user's subsequent fainting is not categorized as self-inflicted damage. Consequently, items such as a Focus Band or Focus Sash, or the ability Sturdy, will not activate for the user, and the user will still collapse. Conversely, as direct damage *is* dealt to the opposing Pokémon, the target's own defensive mechanisms—including Focus Band, Focus Sash, Sturdy, or Endure—*can* trigger if applicable, allowing them to persist with 1 HP. In scenarios where Final Gambit delivers a knockout blow to the adversary, the user will fall first, preceding the target's faint.

When attempted during a Max Raid Battle or Tera Raid Battle, Final Gambit is rendered inert, and the user remains active. Furthermore, Final Gambit is described as lowering the user's HP to 1 and then dealing damage to the opponent equal to the total HP deducted from the user.

Title: Bestow (move)

Originating in Generation V, Bestow (known as ギフトパス Gift Pass in Japanese) is a Normal-type maneuver that inflicts no damage. This technique involves the user passing its currently held item to the opposing Pokémon. Its execution is unsuccessful under several conditions: if the user possesses no item, if the opponent already carries an item, or if the opponent is safeguarded by a substitute. Bestow always connects, disregarding typical accuracy calculations, unless the intended recipient is in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. However, it is impeded by defensive moves such as Protect and Detect. While items exchanged during Trainer battles revert to their initial owner once the encounter concludes, transfers made in wild battles are permanent. The move also won't succeed if the user is holding Mail. Furthermore, it cannot be executed if Giratina (user or target) is involved and the user holds a Griseous Orb, if Arceus (user or target) is present and the user holds a Plate, or if Genesect (user or target) is in play and the user holds a Drive. Curiously, Bestow will still land against a foe that has employed Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, but it will be thwarted by Crafty Shield. Another failure condition is if the user possesses a Z-Crystal. Similarly, it cannot proceed if the user carries a Mega Stone or a colored orb capable of triggering Mega Evolution or Primal Reversion for either Pokémon, or if Silvally (user or target) is involved and the user holds a memory item. In Contest Spectaculars, Bestow contributes to combinations; the performer earns an additional three appeal points if Celebrate, Covet, Happy Hour, or Wish was executed on the preceding turn. When enhanced into Z-Bestow via a Normalium Z, the user's Speed stat receives a two-stage boost; however, the move then fails because the user's Z-Crystal cannot be transferred. Essentially, Bestow moves the user's currently held object to the Pokémon positioned opposite it. Should the target already be equipped with an item, the attempt will inevitably fail.

Title: Water Pledge (move)

Known in Japanese as みずのちかい, or Water Oath, Water Pledge is an offensive Water-type maneuver that first appeared in Generation V. Trainers in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet can acquire it via TM145. It forms one of three special battle-combination techniques, alongside Fire Pledge and Grass Pledge. Every Water-type first partner Pokémon, including their subsequent evolutionary forms, is capable of learning this particular attack.

Should two allied Pokémon elect to employ Water Pledge and either Fire Pledge or Grass Pledge simultaneously within a single turn, the Pokémon that acts first will forgo its individual action. Instead, the second Pokémon to move will unleash a consolidated assault, boasting a formidable power of 150, accompanied by a distinct secondary outcome directly following the attack: If Water Pledge is paired with Fire Pledge on the same turn, the second ally will execute Water Pledge, now with 150 power, while generating a rainbow on its own side of the battlefield that persists for four turns. This rainbow phenomenon effectively doubles the likelihood of secondary effects occurring from moves initiated by Pokémon on that side of the arena. This includes flinching opportunities from items like King's Rock and Razor Fang, but notably excludes Secret Power's effects. Furthermore, this bonus stacks with the Serene Grace Ability for altering stats or applying status conditions, though not for increasing flinch rates. An overflow glitch can arise from the specific processing methods for particular secondary effects. Alternatively, when Water Pledge and Grass Pledge are combined within the same turn, the second ally will execute Grass Pledge at a boosted power of 150, subsequently establishing a swamp on the opposing side of the field, which endures for four turns. The presence of this bog reduces the Speed stat of all Pokémon on that side of the field to one-quarter of their original value.

Should one Pokémon select a Pledge move while a partner has chosen a different one, yet none of the partners can successfully perform their chosen Pledge move during that turn (for instance, due to paralysis or being affected by Sky Drop), then no action will ultimately be performed. A held Water Gem does not impact Water Pledge, nor is it consumed by the move. Any combined Pledge move invariably benefits from the same-type attack bonus (STAB), irrespective of the Pokémon performing the action or the type attributed to the resulting combined technique. The base power of Water Pledge was adjusted, increasing from 50 to 80. While Water Pledge is repeatable, its unique field effects are inactive during Tera Raid battles.

Several Move Tutors are available to instruct any Water-type first partner Pokémon, regardless of its evolutionary status, in this technique. During Generation VII, the Move Tutor's services extended to teaching Water Pledge to the Water-type elemental monkeys as well. Between Generation V and VII, this move could only be imparted by Move Tutors to Pokémon exhibiting maximum friendship. Specifically, in Black and White, a man located in the house right of Driftveil City's market offered this service. In Black 2 and White 2, the tutor was found outside the Pokémon World Tournament building. For Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, he was situated on the second floor of Hau'oli City's mall. Fundamentally, Water Pledge inflicts damage.

Within Super Mystery Dungeon, when Water Pledge is executed in conjunction with Fire Pledge as part of an Alliance, the user's entire party gains the rainbow status for five turns, elevating the likelihood of secondary effects triggering. If instead combined with Grass Pledge, hostile Pokémon within the same room are afflicted with the swamp status for five turns, resulting in a reduction to their Speed attribute. Rescue Team DX, however, removed the Alliance mechanic. Here, if Water Pledge is employed while an ally is positioned to use Fire Pledge (meaning both are adjacent to an adversary), Water Pledge's attack power sees an increase, and all allies in the same chamber receive the Rainbow condition. This interaction also expends 1 PP from Fire Pledge, though the ally holding Fire Pledge retains the ability to perform an additional action during that turn. Conversely, Water Pledge's complementary effect with Grass Pledge only manifests when Grass Pledge itself is deployed.

In Pokémon Ultra Sun, Ultra Moon, Sword, and Shield, Silvally's programming includes the capacity to learn Water Pledge. Nonetheless, the relevant Move Tutor exclusively offers to teach Grass Pledge to Silvally, rendering Water Pledge and Fire Pledge legitimately unobtainable for this Pokémon.

Title: Fire Pledge (move)

Known in Japanese as ほのおのちかい (Flame Oath), Fire Pledge is a damaging Fire-type attack first appearing in Generation V. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is designated as TM144. This move, alongside Water Pledge and Grass Pledge, belongs to a category of moves designed for battle combinations. Every Fire-type first partner Pokémon, including all of its evolutionary stages, is capable of learning Fire Pledge. Should two allied Pokémon endeavor to execute Fire Pledge and either Water Pledge or Grass Pledge during the identical turn, the Pokémon acting first will forgo its turn. Instead, the second Pokémon to act will unleash a unified assault with a base power of 150, immediately followed by a unique secondary impact:

When Fire Pledge and Water Pledge are paired on the same turn, the second ally will perform Water Pledge, boosted to 150 power, and generate a rainbow on its field side, lasting four turns. This rainbow phenomenon doubles the likelihood of additional effects triggering for moves initiated by Pokémon on that side of the battle arena (this encompasses flinching opportunities from King's Rock and Razor Fang, though it excludes Secret Power's effects); its influence combines with Serene Grace when determining chances for stat alterations or status conditions, but not for flinching chances. A specific handling method for particular secondary effects can result in an overflow glitch because of this. Conversely, if Fire Pledge and Grass Pledge are employed together in the same turn, the second ally will execute Fire Pledge, also with 150 power, and produce a sea of fire on the opposing side of the field for a duration of four turns. This fiery expanse inflicts damage equivalent to 1/8 of their maximum HP upon all non-Fire-type Pokémon on that specific side of the field at the conclusion of every turn.

Should one Pokémon select a Pledge move and its partner has chosen a different Pledge move, yet no partners are actually capable of using their chosen Pledge move during that turn (for instance, due to paralysis or Sky Drop), then no move will be carried out whatsoever. Possessing a Fire Gem does not impact Fire Pledge, nor will the item be expended. When a merged attack is unleashed, it invariably benefits from the same-type attack bonus, irrespective of the Pokémon performing it or the combined move's elemental type. The base power of Fire Pledge saw an adjustment, increasing from 50 to 80. While Fire Pledge is re-usable, its signature effects are inactive within Tera Raid battles.

Diverse Move Tutors are capable of instructing any Fire-type first partner Pokémon, regardless of its evolutionary stage, in Fire Pledge. Furthermore, in Generation VII, the Move Tutor extends this instruction to the Fire-type elemental monkeys. Between Generation V and VII, Move Tutors would impart this skill exclusively to Pokémon that had achieved maximized friendship. In Black and White: a man located in the dwelling to the right of Driftveil City's market. In Black 2 and White 2: a man situated outside the Pokémon World Tournament building. In Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon: a man found on Hau'oli City's mall's upper floor. Fire Pledge inflicts direct damage.

Within Super Mystery Dungeon, if Fire Pledge is utilized concurrently with Water Pledge as part of an Alliance, the user's entire party gains the rainbow status for five turns, thereby boosting the likelihood of secondary effects occurring. Conversely, if combined with Grass Pledge, foes sharing the same room will acquire the sea of fire condition, which harms all opposing non-Fire-type Pokémon over a five-turn period. Alliances are absent from Rescue Team DX. Instead, should Fire Pledge be deployed alongside a teammate positioned to use Grass Pledge (meaning, also adjacent to the adversary), Fire Pledge's strength is enhanced, and all enemies within the room will suffer the Sea of Fire condition, damaging all non-Fire-type opposing Pokémon for five turns. Furthermore, 1 PP from Grass Pledge will be consumed (even though the teammate possessing Grass Pledge retains the ability to execute another move during that same turn). The collaborative effect of Fire Pledge with Water Pledge only materializes when Water Pledge itself is initiated. Silvally is coded to be capable of learning Fire Pledge in Pokémon Ultra Sun, Ultra Moon, and Pokémon Sword and Shield. Nonetheless, the relevant Move Tutor exclusively offers instruction for Grass Pledge, rendering Fire Pledge and Water Pledge impossible for Silvally to acquire through legitimate gameplay means.

Title: Grass Pledge (move)

Known as くさのちかい (Grass Oath) in Japan, Grass Pledge is a Grass-type offensive technique that debuted in Generation V. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is identified as TM146. It belongs to a trio of battle-combo moves, alongside Water Pledge and Fire Pledge. Every Grass-type first partner Pokémon, including all their evolutionary stages, possesses the ability to learn this attack.

Should two allied Pokémon attempt to execute Grass Pledge simultaneously with either Fire Pledge or Water Pledge within the same turn, the Pokémon acting first will forgo its own action. Instead, the second Pokémon will unleash a combined assault, boasting a power of 150, followed immediately by an extra consequence: Specifically, if Grass Pledge is paired with Fire Pledge in a single turn, the Pokémon that moves second will deploy Fire Pledge itself, now with a power of 150. This creates a scorching "sea of fire" effect on the opposing side of the battlefield for four turns. This hazardous terrain inflicts damage equivalent to 1/8 of their maximum HP upon all non-Fire-type Pokémon on that side at the conclusion of each turn. Conversely, when Grass Pledge and Water Pledge are combined within the same turn, the Pokémon moving second will execute Grass Pledge, also at a power of 150. This generates a "swamp" on the adversary's side of the field, persisting for four turns, which effectively reduces the Speed stat of all Pokémon present on that side to one-quarter.

However, if one Pokémon initiates a Pledge move while its partner has chosen a different Pledge move, but that partner is subsequently prevented from acting during that turn (for instance, due to paralysis or being affected by Sky Drop), then no attack will ultimately be performed. A held Grass Gem will not influence Grass Pledge's performance, nor will the item be consumed upon its use. Regardless of which Pokémon performs a combined Pledge move, or what its resulting type becomes, it consistently benefits from the same-type attack bonus (STAB). Grass Pledge's base power underwent an adjustment, increasing from 50 to 80. While Grass Pledge is repeatable, its specific effects are not active within Tera Raid battles.

Multiple Move Tutors are capable of instructing any Grass-type first partner Pokémon, irrespective of its evolutionary status, in how to use Grass Pledge. During Generation VII, the Move Tutor extended this service to include the Grass-type elemental monkeys, and from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon onwards, Silvally became eligible as well. Between Generation V and VII, however, these Move Tutors would only impart this technique to Pokémon that had achieved maximized friendship. Specific locations for these tutors included: a man situated in the house to the right of the market in Driftveil City during Pokémon Black and White; a man positioned outside the Pokémon World Tournament building in Black 2 and White 2; and in Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, a man found on the second floor of Hau'oli City's mall.

Beyond its direct damage, in Super Mystery Dungeon, if Grass Pledge is employed in an Alliance with Water Pledge, foes within the same room acquire the "swamp" status for five turns, leading to a reduction in their Speed stat. When allied with Fire Pledge, it inflicts the "sea of fire" status on enemies in the same room, which harms all opposing non-Fire-type Pokémon for five turns. In Rescue Team DX, however, the Alliance mechanic is absent. Instead, should Grass Pledge be utilized while a teammate is positioned to activate Water Pledge (meaning, adjacent to the enemy), Grass Pledge receives a power boost, and opponents in the same chamber are afflicted with the "Swamp" condition for five turns, which lowers their Speed stat. This also consumes 1 PP from Water Pledge, though the teammate holding Water Pledge is still free to perform another action during that turn. The effect of Grass Pledge when combined with Fire Pledge will only manifest upon Fire Pledge's actual execution.

Although Silvally was designed to be tutored Grass Pledge, Fire Pledge, and Water Pledge in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and Pokémon Sword and Shield, a programming oversight unfortunately restricts the Move Tutor to offering only Grass Pledge, thereby preventing Silvally from acquiring the latter two techniques.

Title: Volt Switch (move)

Known in Japanese as ボルトチェンジ (Volt Change), Volt Switch is a damage-dealing Electric-type attack that made its debut in Generation V. It held the designation of TM72 from its introduction through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and is TM43 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Upon inflicting damage, Volt Switch causes the user to withdraw from battle. A key restriction is that the same Pokémon cannot be sent back out immediately after utilizing Volt Switch. The user will not be switched out under several specific circumstances: if it is the only Pokémon in the party capable of battling; if Volt Switch fails to connect with its target; if the opponent remains unaffected by Volt Switch; if the move triggers an opposing Pokémon's Wimp Out or Emergency Exit Ability; or if using Volt Switch results in the conclusion of the battle. Should Volt Switch lead to the knockout of an opposing Trainer's Pokémon, the subsequent Pokémon sent out by the user will not gain additional experience, though experience multipliers from items like Amulet Coin or Luck Incense will still apply. If a foe's Pokémon employs Pursuit on the same turn Volt Switch is used, Pursuit will inflict normal damage if the foe is faster, but double damage if the foe is slower than the user. This move allows a Pokémon to switch out even when affected by Ingrain, though Ingrain's effects are not passed on. Furthermore, Volt Switch enables an escape even if the user is under the influence of trapping effects such as Arena Trap, Magnet Pull, Shadow Tag, Mean Look, Spider Web, or Block. The forced switch aspect of Volt Switch is nullified if the user is holding a Red Card or if the target is equipped with an Eject Button. In Contest Spectaculars, Volt Switch contributes to combinations, granting three bonus appeal points if Charge was performed on the preceding turn. It deals damage; additionally, if a teammate occupies the space directly behind the user, the two will exchange positions upon the move's completion. For Pokémon GO players, Volt Switch functions as a Fast Attack, having been available since February 16, 2017. In Pokémon UNITE, Volt Switch is Zeraora's first move, obtained by reaching level 6 and upgrading Agility into it as an alternative to Spark. The user charges forward, damaging enemies in their path and temporarily boosting their basic attack speed. Reactivating the move within 3s transports the user back to their initial location. At level 12, both the duration of the increased basic attack speed and the move's damage output are enhanced. Within the animated series, Volt Switch has been depicted as capable of summoning a Pokémon that does not belong to the user's Trainer.

Title: Struggle Bug (move)

The Bug-type attack known as Struggle Bug (Japanese: むしのていこう, or Insect Opposition) made its debut in Generation V, where it deals damage. Initially designated as TM76 during Generations V and VI, it now holds the Technical Machine slot TM015 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. In combat, this move deals damage to all proximate adversaries, concurrently reducing the Special Attack stat of each hit opponent by one stage. The move's base power was adjusted to 40. An additional effect is a three-turn decrease in the target's offensive stats, with all such stat reductions applying to every Pokémon struck. Within the Pokémon GO mobile game, Struggle Bug functions as a Fast Attack and has been accessible to players since February 16, 2017. Before its description was updated in Version 2.10.0 on June 28, 2021, the move was simply described as 'Lowers the target's Sp. Atk.' Burgh's Leavanny was shown to possess Struggle Bug in the Homecoming chapter, though its usage occurred off-panel. Interestingly, Staraptor, despite lacking an arthropod basis, is capable of learning this move. Mew can also acquire it, consistent with its ability to learn all TM moves. The English title of this attack may derive from a wordplay on the idiomatic phrase 'struggle bus,' commonly understood slang for a challenging or exasperating predicament.

Title: Bulldoze (move)

This entry focuses on the move Bulldoze; for the specialty in Pokémon Pokopia, please see Bulldoze (specialty). Bulldoze, known in Japanese as じならし (Ground Levelling), is an offensive Ground-type move first introduced in Generation V. It was assigned TM78 from Generation V up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and subsequently TM83 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This attack deals damage and simultaneously reduces the target's Speed stat by a single stage. In Double Battles, it affects all opposing Pokémon as well as the user's partner. When used in Triple Battles, the move strikes every adjacent Pokémon. Should Grassy Terrain be active, Bulldoze's damage output is halved. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Bulldoze, like all other moves in that title, can only hit one target. It also diminishes the target's action speed in this specific game. The damaging effect of Bulldoze radiates outwards from the user, akin to Sludge Wave and Earthquake. In another context, Bulldoze delivers damage to all adjacent enemies and lowers their Travel Speed by one stage. A prior description, valid before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated: "Lowers the target's Speed." Furthermore, the description preceding Version 2.20.0 (April 27, 2022) read: "The more the target's Speed is lowered, the greater the power of this move." In Pokémon UNITE, Bulldoze is Garchomp's second starting move. The user powerfully stomps the ground, creating a radius of damage. After Gabite achieves level 7, this move is then replaced by either Earthquake or Dragon Claw.

Title: Frost Breath (move)

The Ice-type offensive technique known as Frost Breath (Japanese: こおりのいぶき Ice Breath) first appeared in Generation V. This move functions as TM79 from Generation V up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and will later be assigned as TM138 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Frost Breath not only deals damage but is also programmed to always land as a critical hit, with the sole exceptions being targets protected by the abilities Battle Armor or Shell Armor, or the status effect Lucky Chant. A noteworthy adjustment saw Frost Breath's power boosted from 40 to 60, a modification implemented concurrently with critical hits having their damage multiplier reduced from 2× to 1.5×. The attack reliably deals damage and achieves a critical hit, unless overridden by effects such as Battle Armor, Shell Armor, or Lucky Chant. Despite lacking an apparent orifice for breathing, Regice and Snover are among the Pokémon able to acquire Frost Breath. Among all special moves, Frost Breath stands alone with its flawless critical-hit rate.

Title: Dragon Tail (move)

Debuting in Generation V, Dragon Tail (Japanese: ドラゴンテール Dragon Tail) is a Dragon-type attack that inflicts damage. It was designated as TM82 across games from Generation V through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, then became TM17 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move not only causes damage but also possesses a reduced priority in battle. During Trainer battles, the target is forcibly swapped with the next available unfainted Pokémon in their party, if any exist.

Should the user engage a single wild Pokémon, the battle concludes if the user's level is at least equivalent to the target's; if multiple wild Pokémon are present, the effect mirrors its behavior in Trainer battles. A target will resist being forced out if the user faints as a result of using Dragon Tail, which can happen if the target holds a Rocky Helmet or has the Rough Skin or Iron Barbs Ability. The move proves ineffective at concluding wild encounters or switching out opposing Trainer's Pokémon if the target possesses the Suction Cups Ability, is affected by Ingrain, or has created a Substitute.

For Contest Spectaculars, this move is part of a combination and grants three bonus appeal points if Spikes, Stealth Rock, or Toxic Spikes were used on the previous turn. It will damage Dynamax or Gigantamax Pokémon, though it won't compel them to swap out.

In specific game modes, Dragon Tail causes damage and pushes the opponent back by ten tiles. A collision with a wall or another Pokémon will result in 5 HP damage to both the target and the colliding Pokémon. Activating the move twice in one turn allows Dragon Tail to hit the enemy twice, pushing them up to 20 tiles. When a Gigantic Pokémon employs Dragon Tail in Gates to Infinity, it strikes adjacent enemies within a 1-tile radius of the user, possesses a base PP of 20, and a base accuracy of 90%.
