As a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO, Dragon Tail has been obtainable since February 16, 2017.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Dragon Tail is Duraludon's second move, acquired at level 7 by choosing to upgrade Metal Claw to it over Stealth Rock. It pushes enemies away while the user moves backward briefly. The subsequent basic attack becomes a boosted attack. Obstacles are ignored by this move. A maximum of two charges can be stored, with a 1.1-second cooldown between activations. At level 13, it applies a distinct mark that is consumed upon the user's next damaging hit, inflicting additional damage.

Despite their anatomical lack of a proper tail, Naganadel in Generation VII and Shelgon in Generation IX are both able to learn Dragon Tail. Generation IX presents a curious case where Skrelp can learn Dragon Tail via TM, but its evolved counterpart, Dragalge, cannot.

Title: Work Up (move)

Originating in Generation V, Work Up (known in Japanese as ふるいたてる, or Cheer Up) is a Normal-type technique that deals no direct damage. It served as TM01 across Pokémon Sun and Moon, as well as Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Conversely, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, it was designated as TM10. Upon activation, Work Up elevates both the user's Attack and Special Attack stats by one stage apiece. When enhanced by a Normalium Z to become Z-Work Up, the user benefits from an extra one-stage increase to their Attack stat. The move specifically functions by increasing the user's Attack and Sp. Atk by one stage each. Within the game Pokémon Rumble Rush, Work Up, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any Pokémon captured by the player. Notably, Boss Herdier was observed utilizing Work Up as one of its techniques.

Title: Electroweb (move)

Originating in Generation V, Electroweb (Japanese: エレキネット Elecnet) is an Electric-type attack that causes damage. This move deals damage and subsequently decreases the target's Speed stat by a single stage. Notably, any Speed reductions inflicted by Electroweb affect every Pokémon it hits. In Contest Spectaculars, Electroweb participates in a combination wherein its user earns an additional three appeal points if String Shot was performed on the preceding turn. For Pokémon Rumble Rush, the specific description for "Electro Web" states it inflicts damage and lowers an enemy's Travel Speed by one stage. Within Pokémon Masters EX, this buddy move was previously called B Electroweb prior to version 2.38.0. Its description, prior to Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021), simply read: "Lowers the target's Speed." In Pokémon UNITE, Electroweb serves as one of Pikachu's two initial moves, which the Electric-type acquires at level 1 or 3, contingent on whether Thunder Shock is learned first. This UNITE ability enables the user to strike and ensnare rival Pokémon with an electrical net, rendering them immobile. Upon reaching level 6, Electroweb can be upgraded into either Volt Tackle or Thunderbolt.

Title: Wild Charge (move)

An Electric-type attack that causes damage, Wild Charge (ワイルドボルト Wild Volt in Japanese) was introduced in Generation V. It functioned as TM93 from Generation V up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When executed, Wild Charge deals harm, and the user experiences recoil damage equivalent to one-quarter (¼) of the damage dealt to the target. This move's base power has been set at 85. Employing Wild Charge has a chance to paralyze the opponent and also awakens the user from drowsiness. In certain contexts, Wild Charge inflicts damage, and the user receives recoil damage equal to 10% of the user's maximum HP.

In Pokémon GO, Wild Charge is a Charged Attack that became available starting February 16, 2017. A sync move variant of this attack was referred to as Shining Spotlight Wild Bolt prior to version 2.18.0. Within Pokémon UNITE, Zeraora utilizes Wild Charge as its second ability, obtained by reaching level 8 and upgrading Slash into it instead of Discharge. The Pokémon lunges at an adversary, inflicting damage, and automatically targets the closest new enemy if the initial target's HP drops to zero. Striking foes with other moves or boosted attacks within a specific timeframe accumulates up to 3 stacks, each stack adding an extra hit to Wild Charge, for a maximum of 6 attacks. The initial hit delivers full damage, while subsequent hits deal half damage. The user is invulnerable to targeting for the duration of this move. Each strike also causes the user to suffer physical damage equal to 1% of its remaining HP. The first strike causes two instances of recoil damage, with subsequent strikes resulting in only one. At level 14, the move expands to also strike enemies near the primary target.

Elesa's Zebstrika was shown to possess Wild Charge in the Homecoming story, though its actual use took place off-panel. Both the English and Japanese names for this ability are puns, as the terms "bolt" and "charge" hold meanings related to both swift movement and electricity. This linguistic duality helps explain why numerous non-Electric-type Pokémon are able to learn this move.

Title: Drill Run (move)

Known as ドリルライナー (Drill Liner) in Japanese, Drill Run is a Ground-type offensive maneuver that made its debut in Generation V, causing damage during battles. This move is designated as TM58 in the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. When executed, Drill Run reliably inflicts damage upon its target and possesses an elevated chance of landing a critical hit. Furthermore, Drill Run can function as an element in a Contest Spectacular combination, granting the user a bonus of three extra appeal points if the move Focus Energy was performed during the preceding turn.

Title: Dual Chop (move)

This offensive technique, known as ダブルチョップ (Double Chop) in Japanese, is a damage-dealing Dragon-type multistrike attack that debuted in Generation V. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is designated as TM135. When utilized, Dual Chop consistently delivers damage in two successive strikes. Both strikes possess an identical probability of scoring a critical hit. Should the initial strike successfully shatter a substitute, Dual Chop's second strike will still proceed. Moves such as Bide and Counter will register damage solely from this attack's second hit. If the opponent possesses a contact-activated Ability or is equipped with a Rocky Helmet, each strike of Dual Chop registers separately, allowing the Ability or item's effect to trigger twice. In scenarios where a Focus Sash prevents fainting prior to Dual Chop's final strike, the subsequent hit will inevitably lead to the defending Pokémon's incapacitation. A Focus Band retains its potential for multiple activations to avert a faint, though each activation attempt functions independently. If the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1, the Sturdy Ability will trigger upon every individual strike. When the user holds a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every individual strike carries an equivalent probability of making the opposing Pokémon flinch. Should the target possess the Weak Armor or Stamina Ability, each strike of Dual Chop will cause it to activate. This move delivers two blows to the adversary. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, if a Reviver Seed restores the target, Dual Chop's execution will cease. Within Pyroar Breathes, Lysandre commanded his Mienfoo to execute Dual Chop, yet the action transpired off-panel. Despite acquiring Dual Chop via level-up starting from Generation VII, Ambipom is unable to learn this particular technique from a Move Tutor in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Horn Leech (move)

Known as ウッドホーン (Wood Horn) in Japanese, Horn Leech is a Grass-type damaging move first appearing in Generation V. Until Generation VI, it served as Sawsbuck's signature technique. This attack inflicts damage upon a target, and precisely 50% (or ½) of the damage dealt is subsequently restored to the user's HP. Should the attack deal just 1 HP of damage, exactly 1 HP will be recovered by the user. When directed at an opponent possessing the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user experiences HP loss equal to what would have been gained. Equipping the user with a Big Root boosts the HP recovery by 30%, making the restored amount 65% of the damage caused, without increasing the damage output itself. The move cannot be executed while the user is affected by Heal Block. Its description prior to Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, stated: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target."

In Pokémon UNITE, Horn Leech serves as Trevenant's inaugural move. It is unlocked at level 7 by upgrading Branch Poke instead of Pain Split. The user charges forward, becoming unstoppable, inflicting damage and stunning enemies throughout the designated dash period. Upon the charge's culmination, any enemies that were shoved are dealt supplementary damage before being thrown. Collisions with enemies during the charge phase restore the user's HP thrice, with the amount recovered increasing as the user's current HP diminishes. After hitting an enemy, the user's next three basic attacks gain increased damage and reduce this move's cooldown by 0.5s (for each enemy hit) upon connection. At level 13, utilizing this move also grants the user a temporary surge in movement speed.

Title: Sacred Sword (move)

The Fighting-type attack known as Sacred Sword (Japanese: せいなるつるぎ Sacred Sword) made its debut in Generation V as a damaging offensive option. Prior to the advent of Generation VI, this particular technique was exclusive to the legendary Pokémon collectively known as the Swords of Justice. When employed, Sacred Sword delivers damage while disregarding any alterations to the target's Defense or evasion statistics. Its base Power Points (PP) were subsequently decreased to 15. A Pokémon possessing the Sharpness Ability sees Sacred Sword's power amplified by 50%. Furthermore, if Cobalion, Terrakion, Virizion, or Keldeo executes Sacred Sword, their Attack and Special Attack stats are boosted. This strike deals direct damage, and crucially, the opponent's current changes to their defensive or evasive capabilities have no bearing on the damage output.

Within the mobile game Pokémon GO, Sacred Sword functions as a Charged Attack, having become accessible as of March 17, 2020. An earlier in-game description, preceding an unspecified update beyond version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021), stated: "Ignores the target's raised stats when attacking with this move."

Shifting to Pokémon UNITE, Sacred Sword serves as Aegislash's initial move. Aegislash acquires it upon evolving into Doublade at level 5, choosing to upgrade Shadow Sneak into Sacred Sword rather than Shadow Claw. The user performs a thrust of its blade into the terrain, manifesting a triangular area. This designated zone then explodes with concealed energy, inflicting harm upon foes within its confines and displacing them. Additionally, it increments the user's boost count by 1 for every opponent struck (with a maximum of 2 against wild Pokémon and 4 against player-controlled Pokémon). Concurrently with this eruption, the user executes a forward slash, delivering supplementary damage to any enemies caught in its path. For a brief duration following a successful hit from this slash, the user's subsequent attacks bypass 45% of the enemies' Defense. Upon reaching level 11, the move further grants a temporary increase of 50 to the user's Attack stat after the triangle attack connects with adversaries.

In the crossover title Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~, every Pokémon capable of learning Sacred Sword exhibits a sword-like characteristic, with the solitary exception of Oshawott. It should be observed, however, that Oshawott's evolution, Samurott, brandishes its seamitars as if they were swords.

Title: Razor Shell (move)

Razor Shell, known as シェルブレード (Shell Blade) in Japanese, debuted as a damage-dealing Water-type technique in Generation V. For Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was assigned TM83. When utilized, Razor Shell delivers damage and possesses a 50% likelihood of decreasing the opponent's Defense stat by a single stage. Furthermore, if a Pokémon employs this move while possessing the Sharpness Ability, its power sees a 50% amplification. Fundamentally, Razor Shell deals damage and carries the possibility of weakening the target's Defense. In the realm of Pokémon GO, Razor Shell functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since September 19, 2021. An earlier description, present before Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021), stated: "Has a good chance of lowering the target's Defense."

Title: Heat Crash (move)

Heat Crash, known in Japanese as ヒートスタンプ (Heat Stamp), is a Fire-type offensive maneuver introduced in the fifth generation of Pokémon games. Before Generation VIII, this attack was exclusively associated with the evolutionary line of Tepig. It was accessible as TR88 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and it now appears as TM189 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

This technique inflicts damage, with its strength contingent upon the comparative weights of both the attacker and the defender. A greater disparity, specifically when the target is considerably lighter than the user, results in enhanced power output. Should the opponent have employed Minimize, Heat Crash's damage output is doubled, and it guarantees a hit by circumventing accuracy checks, unless the foe is within the semi-invulnerable state of a move like Dig or Fly.

The move's characteristics are likened to those of Heavy Slam, Dragon Rush, Fly, and Bounce, causing the user to ascend high into the air (temporarily evading attacks that cross their path) before plummeting back down to strike the adversary. Notably, when Heavy Slam is used against the Hyperrogue Ange Floette flowers, its base power consistently registers at 120.

Regarding Heat Crash, the text "Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder" mentions that Sizzlipede, with its modest weight of 2.2 lbs., holds the distinction of being the lightest Pokémon currently capable of learning this particular move.

Title: Leaf Tornado (move)

Known in Japanese as グラスミキサー (Grass Mixer), Leaf Tornado is a Grass-type damage-dealing technique that was first introduced in Generation V. This move inflicts harm and presents a 50% likelihood of decreasing the target's accuracy by a single stage.

Within Pokémon GO, Leaf Tornado functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since March 19, 2019. Its additional effect specifies: 1 Opponent Attack → 2 Opponent Attack.

In Pokémon UNITE, Leaf Tornado serves as Eldegoss's initial combat technique. It becomes available upon Gossifleur's evolution at level 4, specifically by enhancing Leafage instead of Pollen Puff. The user manifests a swirling vortex of leaves that harms opponents and generates a wind current, boosting the movement speed of any allies who come into contact with it. Once this vortex reaches its maximum range, it inflicts further damage on nearby foes. Upon reaching level 11, targets struck by the tornado will find all their basic attacks missing for a duration of 2.5 seconds.

Leaf Tornado marked the first Generation V move to be showcased in the animated series, wielded by Trip's Snivy in the episode "In the Shadow of Zekrom!".

Title: Steamroller (move)

A Bug-type attack categorized as damage-dealing, Steamroller (known in Japanese as ハードローラー, or Hard Roller) made its debut in Generation V. This move deals harm and possesses a 30% probability of causing the opposing Pokémon to recoil, unable to act. However, it is unable to induce flinching in a foe protected by a substitute. Should the adversary have employed Minimize, Steamroller's base damage output is doubled. Furthermore, against a Pokémon that has used Minimize, this attack will disregard typical accuracy calculations, ensuring a hit unless the foe is within the semi-invulnerable turn of moves such as Dig or Fly. Notably, it stands as the sole Bug-type maneuver that can inflict this flinching status upon an opponent.

Title: Cotton Guard (move)

Introduced in Generation V, the Grass-type technique known as Cotton Guard (コットンガード in Japanese) is a status move that inflicts no direct damage. Its primary effect is to significantly boost the user's Defense statistic, increasing it by a total of three stages. When performed in a Contest Spectacular, Cotton Guard can initiate a combination: if Baton Pass or Stored Power is subsequently used on the following turn, the performer gains an additional three appeal points. Employing a Grassium Z crystal transforms the move into Z-Cotton Guard, which additionally causes all of the user's decreased stats to be restored to their original values.

Within the game Pokémon UNITE, Cotton Guard functions as Eldegoss's secondary ability. Players acquire it upon reaching level 6, at which point Synthesis can be evolved into Cotton Guard rather than Cotton Spore. This action causes the user to manifest cotton barriers, bestowing a protective shield upon itself and any adjacent allies for a duration of three seconds. Upon the shield's dissolution, it regenerates health points equivalent to 30% of its residual value. An enhancement at level 13 also grants a momentary boost to the user's movement speed.

Title: Night Daze (move)
Originating in Generation V, the Dark-type attack known as Night Daze (ナイトバースト in Japanese, or Night Burst) inflicts damage upon its target. Before Generation VII, this technique was a signature ability exclusive to Zorua and Zoroark from the Unova region. Beyond its primary function of dealing damage, Night Daze also carries a 40% probability of reducing the opponent's accuracy by a single stage. A particular glitch discovered in Pokémon Sword and Shield affects Night Daze: should the move's animation conclude and damage the foe without causing a KO, the target's HP bar remains visually unchanged until either the turn concludes or another action modifies its HP. Additionally, Arc Suit N & Zoroark's passive skill, 'The Bridge Between Pokémon and Humans,' causes Night Daze to have a 100% chance of lowering the target's accuracy by six stat ranks with every strike. This same passive skill also dictates that their Sync Move, 'Almighty Obsidian Night Daze,' will similarly decrease the target's accuracy by six stat ranks on each hit. Finally, a related move, Nightfall Daze, functions as Zoroark's Unite Move in the game Pokémon UNITE, bearing resemblances to Night Daze.

Title: Psystrike (move)

The Psychic-type attack known as Psystrike (Japanese: サイコブレイク Psycho Break) was first introduced in Generation V and is a damaging offensive maneuver, serving as Mewtwo's signature technique. Uniquely, its damage calculation targets the foe's Defense stat rather than their Special Defense. This means that while its category is special, its impact relies on the physical defensive capabilities of the target. Beyond this specific interaction, it otherwise functions entirely as a special attack: it draws power from the user's Special Attack, triggers effects like Mirror Coat and Maranga Berry (but not Counter or Kee Berry), and its destructive power is mitigated by Light Screen (not Reflect), consistent with other special attacks. In its Final Smash appearance, Mewtwo undergoes a transformation into Mega Mewtwo Y before unleashing Psystrike. As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Psystrike became accessible starting September 16, 2019. Interestingly, despite its status as Mewtwo's exclusive technique, Psystrike debuted in Generation V, a time when Mewtwo itself was not naturally catchable within the game, being obtainable solely via Poké Transfer or official Nintendo events. The in-game description for Psystrike mirrors that of Psyshock. Within the Pokémon Conquest title, the move's descriptive text aligns with its Generation V core series counterpart, notably mentioning its physical damage property. This is noteworthy because Conquest lacks the distinct physical and special damage categories; instead, it operates with a singular attack stat and a singular defense stat, placing all damage within one unified classification.

Title: Tail Slap (move)

Known in Japanese as スイープビンタ (Sweep Slap), Tail Slap is a Normal-type offensive maneuver that strikes multiple times, first appearing in Generation V. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it held the technical machine designation TM84. Prior to Generation VIII, it was the exclusive move of Minccino and Cinccino, though other Pokémon could still inherit it through breeding. Tail Slap delivers damage by striking the target 2 to 5 times per use. Every individual strike carries an equal and independent chance to be a critical hit. The hit count is distributed with a 35% probability for 2 hits, a 35% probability for 3 hits, a 15% probability for 4 hits, and a 15% probability for 5 hits. Its average total base power, accounting for the 15% base miss chance, is 65.9. Tail Slap can continue its assault even after a Substitute is broken. Moves such as Bide and Counter will only consider the final strike of Tail Slap. If the target possesses Weak Armor, Stamina, or any Ability that triggers upon contact, each individual strike registers separately, potentially activating the Ability multiple times. Pokémon with the Skill Link Ability will always land five hits when utilizing Tail Slap, provided the move does not miss. Should a Focus Sash activate before the last strike is dealt, the subsequent strike will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, can still repeatedly prevent fainting, but each opportunity is independent. Sturdy will activate for each hit if the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1. When the target is holding a Rocky Helmet, the user takes damage for every strike. If the user is equipped with a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each successive hit has an equal chance to make the opponent flinch. This attack deals damage, hitting the target 2-5 times with each execution. Each hit is subject to a separate accuracy check, yet the move will persist even if a hit fails. Beginning with Super Mystery Dungeon, if a Reviver Seed activates, the move's sequence will terminate.

Title: Hurricane (move)

Known in Japanese as ぼうふう (Windstorm), Hurricane is an offensive Flying-type attack that made its debut in Generation V. This move inflicts damage and carries a 30% probability of leaving the target confused. In Triple Battles, Hurricane is capable of striking opponents not directly adjacent to the user. It can connect with Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable phases of moves like Fly, Bounce, and Sky Drop. When unleashed during rainy conditions, Hurricane is guaranteed to hit, bypassing typical accuracy checks, unless the foe is untargetable due to moves such as Dig or Dive. Under harsh sunlight, its accuracy rating drops to 50%. Hurricane serves as an element in Contest Spectacular combinations; if Rain Dance was performed in the preceding turn, the user accrues an additional three appeal points.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Hurricane's power was reduced to 100, and it no longer inflicts confusion, as that status condition is absent from the game. Its function in PLA mirrors that of Thunder and Blizzard, coincidentally all moves possessing a 70% accuracy in the main series turn-based games. The animation involves a small whirlwind appearing beneath the target's position upon the move's wind-up, which then expands into a damaging tempest after a brief delay. Given the mechanics of Bounce and Fly in Z-A, Hurricane is ineffective against targets using these moves when they are off-screen.

Within Pokémon GO, Hurricane delivers damage and may induce confusion in the opposing Pokémon. It successfully strikes aerial foes employing moves like Fly and will never miss during rain or heavy rain. Conversely, its accuracy is diminished in sunny weather or harsh sunlight. Leon & Charizard's passive ability, Piercing Gaze, ensures that their usage of Hurricane is unfailing.

Its description in Pokémon Masters EX prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) stated: "Never misses when the weather is rainy. Has a moderate chance of leaving the target confused."

In Pokémon UNITE, Hurricane is Cramorant's second available move. Players acquire it at level 4 by evolving Feather Dance into Hurricane, as an alternative to Air Slash. The user conjures a fierce gale, harming and displacing enemies caught within its area of effect. Upon reaching level 11, the move grants a brief speed boost to the user.

The episode "Homecoming" revealed Skyla's Swanna possessed Hurricane, though the move's execution was not shown on-panel. Both Hurricane and Thunder share the characteristic of never missing in rain, potentially linking them to thunderstorms. Unlike its effect on other Pokémon, Hurricane's Generation VI animation does not depict Dugtrio or Diglett being lifted from the ground.

Title: Head Charge (move)

Known as Head Charge (Japanese: アフロブレイク, or Afro Break), this Normal-type damage-dealing maneuver made its debut in Generation V. It is recognized as Bouffalant's signature move. When utilized, Head Charge delivers damage to the opponent, with the user simultaneously incurring recoil harm equivalent to one-quarter (¼) of the total damage dealt. In a peculiar bug within Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 through 1.1.1, Head Charge was unselectable during battles. However, beginning with Version 1.2.0, it was reinstated and functions exactly as it did in prior generations. In terms of its damaging effect, Head Charge causes the user to suffer recoil damage amounting to 10% of its maximum HP. Curiously, even though its Pokédex entries suggest Bouffalant's fur absorbs impacts, the Pokémon still experiences this recoil damage after unleashing the move.

Title: Gear Grind (move)

Known in Japanese as ギアソーサー (Gear Saucer), Gear Grind is a damage-dealing, Steel-type attack that strikes multiple times, making its debut in Generation V. This move is exclusive to Klink's evolutionary family. When deployed, Gear Grind delivers two distinct blows to its target, causing damage. Both individual strikes possess an identical likelihood of landing as a critical hit. Should the initial impact shatter a foe's Substitute, the move will still continue to land its subsequent strike. Abilities like Bide and Counter will only acknowledge the damage from Gear Grind's second hit. For targets possessing an Ability that activates upon contact, each of Gear Grind's strikes triggers the Ability independently, resulting in two activations. If a Focus Sash protects the defending Pokémon before the final strike is dealt, the very next strike is guaranteed to cause that Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band can potentially activate multiple times to avert a KO, yet each chance operates independently. Sturdy will be triggered by each hit if the Pokémon being attacked possesses a maximum HP of 1. Should the opposing Pokémon possess the Weak Armor or Stamina Abilities, each blow from Gear Grind will activate them. The user of Gear Grind will incur damage from a target holding a Rocky Helmet with every single strike delivered. Equipping the user with a King's Rock or Razor Fang grants an equal likelihood for each successive impact to make the foe flinch. Gear Grind can also be integrated into a Contest Spectacular combination, awarding the performer an additional three appeal points if Shift Gear was executed on the previous turn. From Super Mystery Dungeon onwards, should a Reviver Seed be consumed, the move's sequence concludes.

Title: Searing Shot (move)

Debuting in Generation V, the Fire-type attack Searing Shot (known in Japan as かえんだん, or Flame Projectile) inflicts direct damage. Victini counts this technique among its exclusive signature moves. Beyond its primary damage output, it carries a 30% probability of leaving the foe burned. Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof ability are immune to its effects. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, this move was unselectable during battles across versions 1.0 through 1.2.1. However, from Version 1.3.0 onward, it became fully functional, mirroring its behavior from prior generations. The attack strikes with power, potentially inflicting a burn upon the adversary. Its in-game description text has matched that of Lava Plume since Generation VI.

Title: Techno Blast (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Techno Blast (Japanese: テクノバスター Techno Buster) is an offensive Normal-type move. It stands as Genesect's signature attack. This technique inflicts damage and lacks any secondary effects. Its elemental typing is contingent upon the Drive item held by the user; it remains Normal-type if no Drive is equipped, but becomes Electric, Fire, Ice, or Water with the appropriate Drive. The specific Drive for Genesect is chosen when the Pokémon is selected for a team. Techno Blast is not influenced by abilities such as Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, or Aerilate. However, it can be affected by Ion Deluge (if it would be Normal-type) and Electrify. In this generation, Techno Blast's base power is 85.

From Versions 1.0 to 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Techno Blast was unselectable in battle. As of Version 1.3.0, it became fully usable, retaining its functionality from previous generations. During Dynamaxing, the move is displayed as Max Strike, but when executed, it transforms into the corresponding Max Move type based on the held Drive.

In Pokémon GO, Techno Blast functions as a Charged Attack, made available starting January 5, 2021. Unlike the core series games, its type in Pokémon GO is fixed, determined by the Drive equipped to Genesect. Pokémon Rumble Rush features Techno Blast with a type that varies for each individual Genesect obtained.

Sygna Suit Hilbert & Genesect's passive skill, Bug Shift, causes Techno Blast to always appear as a Bug-type move in menus and during combat. Nevertheless, the actual damage type inflicted by Techno Blast changes due to their other passive skill, Hilbert's Plan: if the opponent's weakness is Fire, it delivers Fire-type damage; if Water, Water-type damage; if Electric, Electric-type damage; if Ice, Ice-type damage. For any other opponent weakness, Techno Blast deals Bug-type damage.

Title: Relic Song (move)

Known in Japanese as いにしえのうた (Ancient Song), Relic Song is a damage-dealing Normal-type move that debuted in Generation V, serving as Meloetta's exclusive signature technique. This attack not only inflicts harm but also carries a ten percent chance of causing the target to fall asleep. Relic Song strikes all adjacent opponents. When successfully employed by Meloetta, provided it affects at least one Pokémon, the move triggers a transformation, cycling Meloetta between its Aria and Pirouette Formes, thereby modifying its type, base stats, and EV yield. However, if Meloetta's Ability is changed to Sheer Force, that Ability nullifies Relic Song's form-changing consequence. Should it be used on a Pokémon shielded by a Substitute, the move impacts the Substitute itself. Significantly, Relic Song is currently able to connect with Pokémon even through their Substitutes. If Meloetta has Terastallized, Relic Song's type-altering property is rendered void.

Various Move Tutors are able to instruct Meloetta in Relic Song. According to interface text presented during its Generation V scene, Meloetta had previously mastered Relic Song but subsequently lost memory of it. Specifically, in Pokémon Black, White, Black 2, and White 2, this instruction is provided by a Musician within Castelia City's Café Sonata. Relic Song both deals damage and holds the potential to put the foe to sleep. Should Meloetta know Relic Song, it gains the option to assume its Pirouette Forme upon entering a dungeon. When Meloetta is in its Aria Forme, Relic Song of Wonder and Sepia Memories Relic Song are categorized as Psychic-type moves, a direct result of Rosa (Champion) & Meloetta's passive skill, Psychic Performance. Conversely, if Meloetta is in its Pirouette Forme, those identical moves, Relic Song of Wonder and Sepia Memories Relic Song, transition to Fighting-type moves, attributed to Rosa (Champion) & Meloetta's passive skill, Fighting Performance. Despite Meloetta's absence from specific episodes, its vocal rendition of the Relic Song was utilized as background music during "Party Dancecapades!" and "That's Why the Litten is a Scamp!". Furthermore, a remastered version of the melody, originally featured in "Meloetta's Moonlight Serenade," appeared throughout "Pokémon the Series: XY" and "Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon."

Title: Secret Sword (move)

Known in Japan as しんぴのつるぎ (Secret Sword), this Fighting-type, damage-dealing attack first appeared in Generation V and serves as Keldeo's exclusive signature technique. This attack deals damage, uniquely calculating its power against the target's Defense stat rather than their Special Defense. Despite this, Secret Sword remains categorized as a special move: it leverages the user's Special Attack, triggers effects like Mirror Coat and Maranga Berry (but not Counter or Kee Berry), and its destructive output can be mitigated by Light Screen (not Reflect), among other special move interactions. A Pokémon possessing the Sharpness ability will see Secret Sword's effective power boosted by 50%.

Initially, from Versions 1.0 through 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Secret Sword was unselectable during battles. However, with the release of Version 3.0.0, it became fully functional again, operating identically to its previous generational appearances.

When Keldeo learns Secret Sword within the game versions spanning from Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, it undergoes a transformation into its Resolute Form. Conversely, if it subsequently unlearns Secret Sword, it reverts to its Ordinary Form. Notably, Keldeo that acquired Secret Sword in Pokémon Black and White will persist in their Ordinary Form when transferred to different games. Furthermore, a Resolute Form Keldeo carrying Secret Sword from Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 cannot be exchanged with Pokémon Black and White versions, given that the necessary form data is absent in the latter titles. Beginning with Pokémon Sword and Shield and in subsequent titles, an Ordinary Form Keldeo holding Secret Sword will automatically shift into its Resolute Form whenever it resides in the player's party and either a battle concludes or any Pokémon in the party learns a new move.

Numerous Move Tutors are capable of imparting Secret Sword to Keldeo. In specific instances, Keldeo can master this technique by being brought to particular locations under predefined circumstances. These specialized instructors include: in Pokémon Black and White, Cobalion, Terrakion, and Virizion found at the Moor of Icirrus, provided the player has all three along with Keldeo in their active party. In Black 2 and White 2, the tutor is a prominent rock situated in the Pledge Grove, requiring only Keldeo to be present in the party.

Within Pokémon GO, Secret Sword functions as a Charged Attack, first appearing on November 25, 2025. It supersedes Keldeo's existing Charged Attack upon its transformation from Ordinary to Resolute Form. This particular move is unique in that it cannot be substituted using either Charged TMs or Elite Charged TMs. Like its main series counterpart, this attack deals damage and, despite its special classification, uses the target's Defense stat for damage calculation rather than their Special Defense.

In the context of the Super Smash Bros. series, Resolute Form Keldeo is featured as a summonable entity from a Poké Ball. In both the 3DS and Wii U iterations of the game, it attacks adversaries using Secret Sword. This behavior is maintained in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, where Keldeo once again emerges from a Poké Ball, exhibiting the same actions as in prior titles.

Title: Glaciate (move)

Glaciate (Japanese: こごえるせかい Frozen World) is an Ice-type, damage-dealing move first introduced in Generation V. It is recognized as one of Kyurem's signature moves. This attack inflicts damage and also reduces the target's Speed stat by one stage. In Double Battles, Glaciate affects all opposing Pokémon, and in Triple Battles, it targets all adjacent adversaries. Users can incorporate Glaciate into a Contest Spectacular combination, earning an additional three appeal points if the move Hail was employed in the preceding turn. Within *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity*, Glaciate deals damage to all enemies within the room and simultaneously decreases their Travel Speed by one stage, although it does require a turn to charge up before use. Any stat reductions inflicted by this move are applied to each individual target affected. Prior to June 28, 2021, and specifically before Version 2.10.0, its in-game description read: "Lowers the target's Speed." As a Charged Attack in *Pokémon GO*, Glaciate became available on December 23, 2022. When Kyurem undergoes fusion to become Black Kyurem or White Kyurem, Glaciate transforms into Freeze Shock and Ice Burn, respectively. Glaciate holds the distinction of having the lowest base power among all Legendary Pokémon's signature moves. If a Kyurem knowing Glaciate fuses with either Reshiram or Zekrom using the DNA Splicers, the move will be replaced by Ice Burn or Freeze Shock, respectively. Conversely, upon separation of the two Pokémon, both Ice Burn and Freeze Shock revert back to Glaciate.

Title: Bolt Strike (move)

Should you be seeking the Pokémon commonly referred to as "Bolt Strike," that would be Thundurus. Bolt Strike (Japanese: らいげき, translated as "Lightning Strike") is an Electric-type offensive technique introduced in Generation V that deals damage. It stands as one of Zekrom's signature moves. Upon use, Bolt Strike deals damage and carries a 20% chance of paralyzing its target. In Contest Spectaculars, Bolt Strike can contribute to a combination, granting three extra appeal points if Charge was used on the preceding turn. Within Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Gates to Infinity, Bolt Strike is restricted to opposing Zekrom, which cannot be recruited. A description preceding Version 2.10.0 (June 28, 2021) read: "Has a small chance of leaving the target paralyzed." Similarly, the 6★-EX effect was described before Version 2.18.5 (March 15, 2022) as: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents." Excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves, Bolt Strike shares the highest base power among all Electric-type moves with Electro Shot.

Title: Blue Flare (move)
Known in Japan as あおいほのお (Blue Flame), Blue Flare is a damaging Fire-type attack that made its debut in Generation V. It serves as one of Reshiram's distinctive signature techniques. This potent attack deals damage and carries a 20% likelihood of inflicting a burn on the foe. In Gates to Infinity, its application is restricted solely to enemy Reshiram, which cannot be recruited. Prior to March 15, 2022 (pre-Version 2.18.5), the 6★-EX bonus stated: "Additional Effects: Power of sync move increases by 50%." A former description, valid until Version 2.20.0 on April 27, 2022, outlined that the move's potency amplified based on how much the target's stats had been reduced.

Title: Fiery Dance (move)

Introduced in Generation V, Fiery Dance (Japanese: ほのおのまい, translating to Flaming Dance) is a Fire-type move designed to inflict damage. This distinctive technique is a signature move for both Volcarona and its future counterpart, the Paradox Pokémon Iron Moth. When unleashed, Fiery Dance deals damage and carries a 50% probability of increasing the user's Special Attack stat by a single stage. Notably, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Fiery Dance could not be selected during battles in versions 1.0 through 1.1.1. However, with the release of Version 1.2.0, its functionality was restored, enabling its selection and operation consistent with earlier generations. As a damaging attack, Fiery Dance also possesses the potential to elevate the user's Sp. Attack by one stage.

Pokémon + Nobunaga's Ambition ~ Ranse's Color Picture Scroll ~

While only Volcarona and its Paradox form, Iron Moth, possess the capacity to learn this move, it is important to note that Volcarona's other Paradox relative, Slither Wing, is incapable of acquiring Fiery Dance.

Title: Freeze Shock (move)

Known in Japanese as フリーズボルト (Freeze Bolt), Freeze Shock is an Ice-type offensive technique that first appeared in Generation V of the Pokémon series. This move is among Black Kyurem's exclusive signature abilities. Beyond inflicting damage, Freeze Shock carries a 30% probability of leaving its target paralyzed. Upon selection, Freeze Shock initiates a charging phase, where the user is enveloped in a freezing light, and no action occurs on that turn. The item Power Herb enables the user to bypass this charge period. The subsequent turn sees the move execute, dealing its damage, consuming its PP, and registering as the most recently utilized attack. After Freeze Shock is chosen, the user becomes unable to switch out until the move is either fully performed or interrupted. Should Freeze Shock fail to execute completely, no PP will be expended, nor will it be considered the last move performed. Should an opponent employ Mirror Move while the user is charging Freeze Shock, Mirror Move will replicate the user's immediately preceding action (or fail if no such move can be copied). Conditions such as sleep, freezing, or flinching will momentarily suspend, but not cancel, Freeze Shock's execution. During its initial turn, Freeze Shock applies a specific 'Freeze Shock' status condition to the user, with the attack itself occurring on the subsequent turn. It also holds the potential to inflict paralysis on the adversary.

Within Pokémon GO, Freeze Shock functions as a Charged Attack, which became accessible on February 21, 2025. Black Kyurem can only possess this technique if the Kyurem it fused from had learned Glaciate beforehand. Outside of combat, its Adventure Effect immobilizes Pokémon during encounters, thereby increasing their catch rate. This enhanced capture probability extends to interactions with peripheral accessories like the Pokémon GO Plus. Activating this effect requires 5,000 Stardust and 5 Kyurem Candy, lasting for a duration of 10 minutes.

The move's design appears to blend elements of both Zekrom and Kyurem, being an Ice-type attack while simultaneously incorporating an Electric-type association in its name and secondary effect. Moreover, in Pokémon Black and White, the visual sequence for the damaging component of Freeze Shock strikingly resembles Bolt Strike, a signature move belonging to Zekrom. Similarly, its counterpart, Ice Burn, also alludes to Kyurem and Reshiram. Among all Ice-type techniques, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves, Freeze Shock shares the distinction with Ice Burn of having the highest base power. When transformed into a Subzero Slammer, either of these moves results in the highest base power for any Ice-type attack. It stands as the sole Ice-type move capable of inducing paralysis, with the exception of Body Slam, Tri Attack, and Secret Power when affected by the Refrigerate ability. Though Freeze Shock and Ice Burn were embedded in the game data of Pokémon Black and White, they were not legitimately accessible until Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 launched, at which point a Smeargle that had Sketched these moves could be transferred back to the original Black and White versions.

Title: Ice Burn (move)

Originating in Generation V, Ice Burn (known as コールドフレア, or Cold Flare, in Japanese) is an offensive Ice-type technique. This particular attack stands as one of White Kyurem's exclusive signature maneuvers. Beyond inflicting damage, Ice Burn possesses a 30% probability of leaving the target with a burn. Upon selection, the move does not execute immediately; instead, the user spends that turn enveloped in a chilling aura, without any direct effect. A Power Herb, however, enables the user to bypass this initial charging phase. The subsequent turn sees Ice Burn unleash its full force, dealing damage, deducting PP, and registering as the last move executed. After initiating Ice Burn, the user becomes unable to withdraw from battle until the move is either interrupted or successfully completed. Should Ice Burn fail to reach full execution, no PP will be consumed, nor will it be considered the most recent technique employed. Should an opponent employ Mirror Move during Ice Burn's charging turn, it will replicate the move the user performed directly prior to selecting Ice Burn (or simply fail if no such move is applicable). Status conditions like sleep, freeze, or flinching will momentarily suspend Ice Burn's duration without canceling it outright. In certain contexts, such as Mystery Dungeon, Ice Burn applies the "Ice Burn status condition" to the user for one turn, subsequently unleashing the attack on the following turn to harm adversaries within the same chamber. This may also inflict a burn on the target.

Within Pokémon GO, Ice Burn functions as a Charged Attack, becoming accessible from February 21, 2025. White Kyurem's ability to learn this move is contingent on Kyurem having learned Glaciate before their fusion. Its Adventure Effect, when utilized outside of combat, enhances catch rates by causing the catch circle during encounters to move at a reduced speed, thereby making Pokémon simpler to capture. This augmented catch probability extends even to supplementary devices like the Pokémon GO Plus. Activating this effect incurs a cost of 5,000 Stardust and 5 Kyurem Candy, enduring for a duration of 10 minutes.

The move's design appears to incorporate elements from both Reshiram and Kyurem, evidenced by its Ice typing combined with a Fire-type related secondary effect. Moreover, within Pokémon Black and White, the visual sequence for Ice Burn's offensive component shares a resemblance with Blue Flare, which itself is a signature move of Reshiram. Similarly, Freeze Shock, a variation of Ice Burn, also alludes to Kyurem and Zekrom. Among all Ice-type maneuvers, Ice Burn shares the distinction with Freeze Shock for possessing the greatest base power, an assessment that excludes Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. However, a Subzero Slammer derived from either Ice Burn or Freeze Shock holds the absolute highest base power across all Ice-type attacks. Uniquely, Ice Burn stands as the sole Ice-type technique capable of inflicting a burn, with the exception of Tri Attack and Secret Power when their types are altered to Ice by the Refrigerate ability. The secondary burn effect could metaphorically represent frostbite, which bears resemblances to actual burns. Alternatively, it might allude to sodium acetate, often termed "hot ice" because of its visual likeness to ice, yet it generates heat through its crystallization process, yielding high temperatures. Both Ice Burn and Freeze Shock were integrated into the code of Pokémon Black and White, yet they remained unobtainable through legitimate means until the launch of Black 2 and White 2, where a Smeargle having Sketched these moves could be transferred back to the original Black and White versions.

Title: Snarl (move)

The Dark-type, damage-dealing maneuver known as Snarl (its Japanese designation being バークアウト, or Bark Out) made its initial appearance in the fifth generation of Pokémon games. It was designated as TM95 from Generation V up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, and later served as TM78 in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. When deployed, Snarl not only inflicts damage but also diminishes the Special Attack stat of all affected targets by a single stage. In Triple Battles, this move strikes all opposing Pokémon positioned adjacently. When used against a Pokémon shielded by a Substitute, Snarl initially makes contact with that Substitute. Nevertheless, Snarl possesses the ability to strike Pokémon directly even when they are positioned behind a Substitute. In this particular game, Snarl's power has been elevated to 60, and it imposes a reduction on the target's offensive stats for a duration of three turns. It is also restricted to striking a single target, aligning with all other moves present in this game. Snarl causes damage and subsequently reduces the foe's Sp. Atk by one stage. For Pokémon GO players, Snarl functions as a Fast Attack and has been available since February 16, 2017. Any stat decreases inflicted by this move are applied individually to each Pokémon it manages to hit. Notably, when Marnie & Liepard utilize Snarl, it invariably connects with its target, a benefit stemming from their passive skill, Piercing Gaze. The description for Snarl preceding Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021) merely stated: "Lowers the target's Sp. Atk." Prior to the debut of Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, Snarl's distribution was quite exceptional; it simultaneously served as a Technical Machine (TM) and an event-exclusive move because TM95 had not yet become obtainable. Consequently, to impart Snarl to many Pokémon during that period, trainers had to acquire the aforementioned event Zoroark and then transmit the move through breeding, chain breeding, or the move Sketch, leveraging the fact that TM moves are inheritable. This stands as the singular instance, with the exception of Volt Tackle, where a move not classified as an Egg Move necessitated breeding for acquisition. Snarl is distinguished as the sole variant among moves akin to Icy Wind that does not cause a reduction in the target's Speed stat. Among all damage-dealing sound-based moves, Snarl holds the unique distinction of possessing an accuracy rating below 100%.

Title: Icicle Crash (move)

Known in Japanese as つららおとし (Icicle Drop), Icicle Crash is an offensive Ice-type technique that first appeared in Generation V. This attack delivers damage and carries a 30% likelihood of making the target flinch. Furthermore, Icicle Crash serves as a component in Contest Spectacular combinations; its performer earns an additional three appeal points if Hail was executed on the preceding turn.

The move's base power has been reduced to 80. In some contexts, it now decreases the target's action speed instead of inducing flinching. The attack still delivers damage and may cause the enemy to flinch. A description effective prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) read: "Has a moderate chance of making the target flinch."

Within the game Pokémon UNITE, Icicle Crash functions as Mamoswine's initial move. Players acquire this ability upon evolving into Piloswine at level 5, choosing to upgrade Ice Shard into Icicle Crash rather than Ice Fang. The user unleashes icicles onto a chosen spot, harming adversaries within its area of effect and temporarily diminishing their movement speed. The last icicle breaks apart either after a specific duration or if struck by the user's alternate abilities or empowered assaults, inflicting damage on foes within its radius and rendering them frozen. Should Tackle or High Horsepower be the move that causes the icicle to shatter, the terrain becomes coated in ice, granting a movement speed boost to allies traversing it. Upon reaching level 11, the concluding icicle's impact briefly incapacitates any enemies it strikes.

Title: Fusion Flare (move)

Debuting in Generation V, Fusion Flare (known as クロスフレイム Cross Flame in Japanese) is a damage-inflicting Fire-type attack. This move is recognized as one of the signature techniques belonging to Reshiram and White Kyurem. Fusion Flare's base power will double if any Pokémon successfully executed Fusion Bolt immediately beforehand in the same turn, provided no other successful moves transpired between them (failed attempts being the sole exception). When this amplified power condition is met, Fusion Flare also displays a distinct visual animation, differing from its standard appearance. Conversely, Fusion Flare itself can trigger a power doubling for a subsequent Fusion Bolt, should Fusion Bolt be the very next successful attack performed within the identical turn. If its user is afflicted by the frozen status condition, Fusion Flare has the inherent property to defrost them instantly prior to its execution. Even if a lone Pokémon employs both fusion attacks back-to-back during the same turn (for example, through the effect of Instruct), the second move will nonetheless benefit from the power amplification. In the realm of Pokémon GO, Fusion Flare functions as a Charged Attack, having become accessible to players starting December 31, 2022.

Title: Fusion Bolt (move)

Originating in Generation V, Fusion Bolt (known in Japan as クロスサンダー, or Cross Thunder) is an Electric-type attack that deals damage. It serves as a signature ability for both Zekrom and Black Kyurem, directly harming its target. This move's base power receives a significant boost, doubling, if Fusion Flare was successfully executed by any Pokémon earlier in the current turn, provided no other successful moves have intervened since then. When this power multiplier is active, Fusion Bolt will also display an altered animation. Conversely, Fusion Bolt itself can empower a subsequent Fusion Flare, causing its power to double if Fusion Flare is the very next successful move employed in the same turn. Even if a single Pokémon performs both fusion attacks sequentially within the same turn (for example, through the effect of Instruct), the second attack will still benefit from the power increase. Beyond battles, Fusion Bolt offers an additional three appeal points in Contest Spectaculars when combined with Charge, if Charge was performed in the preceding turn. In Pokémon GO, this move functions as a Charged Attack and became obtainable starting January 10, 2023. Noteworthy for Generation V, Fusion Bolt holds the final position in the index number sequence, as documented in "Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder".

Title: Flying Press (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Flying Press (Japanese: フライングプレス) is an offensive Fighting-type attack. Hawlucha is uniquely associated with this move as its signature technique. It inflicts harm upon opponents. Within Generation VI, its base power rating stands at 80. Although categorized as Fighting-type, Flying Press uniquely calculates damage as if it were both Fighting and Flying-type simultaneously. Consequently, its efficacy against various Pokémon deviates from that of typical Fighting-type attacks. Nevertheless, in all other functional aspects, it functions strictly as a Fighting-type ability: only Pokémon sharing the Fighting type benefit from the same-type attack bonus (STAB) when using it; items such as the Sky Plate or Flying Gem have no effect on it; and it will not trigger a foe's Coba Berry. Conversely, it *will* activate a Chople Berry, even in scenarios where a conventional Fighting-type move might not land a supereffective hit. Should Flying Press be executed under the influence of moves like Electrify or Normalize, its damage calculation will instead incorporate Flying-type alongside Electric-type or Normal-type modifiers, respectively. When targeting a foe that has employed Minimize, Flying Press inflicts twice its standard damage and ensures a hit by ignoring accuracy checks. This guaranteed strike is is only prevented if the opponent is within the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. In the context of Triple Battles, Flying Press possesses the capability to strike foes that are not directly adjacent. This attack is a dual Fighting and Flying-type maneuver that harms adversaries. Within Pokémon GO, Flying Press manifests as a Charged Attack, having been accessible to players since March 13, 2020. During the events of HZ131, Master Mayonnaise commanded his Hawlucha to perform Flying Press; however, the Pokémon became preoccupied with posing for its audience and failed to execute the attack. By index number order, Flying Press holds the distinction of being the very first move introduced in Generation VI. Notably, Flying Press stands as the sole move capable of inflicting damage based on two types simultaneously.

Title: Mat Block (move)

Known in Japanese as たたみがえし (Tatami Flip), Mat Block is a non-damaging Fighting-type technique that made its debut in Generation VI. Originally Greninja's signature move prior to Generation VII, it became Throh's unique skill starting in Generation VIII. Mat Block shields all allied Pokémon on the field from incoming physical or special moves for the current turn. Unlike the move Protect, Mat Block does not possess increased priority. This technique will fail if it's not employed on the very first turn after its user enters the battle; the Pokémon must be withdrawn and then sent back out to be able to utilize Mat Block again.

However, certain attacks can bypass Mat Block's protective barrier. Most damaging Z-Moves and Max Moves will inflict only 25% of their standard damage, with the sole exceptions being G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow, which deliver their full destructive power. Moves designed to penetrate protection, such as Feint, will deal their complete damage and simultaneously nullify Mat Block's effect. The Ability Unseen Fist also permits contact attacks to ignore Mat Block entirely. Furthermore, it provides no defense against the delayed damage from Future Sight or Doom Desire. When enhanced into Z-Mat Block via a Fightinium Z, the user's Defense stat receives a one-stage boost.

Mat Block confers the "Mat Block status" for one turn upon the user and teammates present in the same room. This status prevents Pokémon from taking damage from moves, though it offers no immunity to thrown items or explosions. Repeated successful deployment of Mat Block, or similar protective techniques, diminishes their likelihood of success on subsequent turns.

In the Super Smash Bros. series (Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate), Mat Block initiates Greninja's Final Smash, Secret Ninja Attack. This initial tatami flip launches adversaries skyward, enabling Greninja to perform multiple slashes before delivering a powerful finishing strike. The move itself alludes to an actual ninja technique: swiftly overturning a tatami mat – a traditional Japanese floor covering – to deflect incoming projectile attacks.

Although Mat Block is technically present and functional within Pokémon Sword and Shield, no Pokémon native to those titles can naturally acquire it. The sole method for obtaining this move involves transferring a Throh already possessing it from a previous game through Pokémon HOME, as Greninja and Smeargle are unavailable in Sword and Shield. It holds the distinction of being the only protective move without boosted priority, and it is also unique among protection moves in its strict first-turn-only activation.

Title: Belch (move)

Known in Japanese as ゲップ (Belch), this Poison-type attack first appeared in Generation VI, dealing damage upon its target. Its use is conditional, becoming available only after the Pokémon has consumed a Berry during the ongoing battle. This prerequisite can be met by the user directly eating its own Berry, or by consuming another Pokémon's held Berry through moves like Bug Bite or Pluck. It also applies if the user is hit by Fling while holding a Berry, provided that specific Berry type *can* have an effect when consumed in such a manner. However, certain methods of Berry consumption do not satisfy this condition; these include utilizing a Berry with Natural Gift, the user itself using Fling with a Berry, or a Trainer manually providing a Berry from their Bag. Once the Berry consumption requirement is met, the Pokémon gains the ability to use Belch for the remainder of that battle. This enablement persists even if the Pokémon is withdrawn and re-entered, is knocked out and subsequently revived, or if the eaten Berry is restored by abilities or moves such as Recycle, Harvest, or Pickup.

In the context of the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Belch inflicts damage. However, its execution fails if the Pokémon's Belly stat is at 20 or below.

Transitioning to Pokémon UNITE, Belch functions as Greedent's inaugural move. Greedent acquires Belch upon reaching level 7, as an upgrade option for Tackle, presented as an alternative to Bullet Seed. This ability allows the user to harm foes within a designated area, simultaneously applying a temporary reduction to their movement speed. Should Greedent have any Berries stashed, activating Belch causes the consumption of all stashed Berries, with each Berry consumed subsequently shortening Belch's cooldown duration. Further, at level 13, Belch receives an enhancement, increasing its damage output.

Regarding its base power in the main series games, Belch shares the distinction with Gunk Shot as having the greatest power among all Poison-type attacks, a comparison that excludes Z-Moves. However, an Acid Downpour executed using either Belch or Gunk Shot would then become the most potent Poison-type move overall.

Title: Rototiller (move)

Introduced in Generation VI, Rototiller is a Ground-type status move, known as たがやす (Plow) in Japan. When activated, this move bestows a one-stage boost to the Attack and Special Attack stats of all grounded Grass-type Pokémon present on the battlefield. This augmentation excludes any Pokémon currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of moves such as Fly or Dig. The move's execution fails if no qualifying Grass-type Pokémon are active. Rototiller also plays a role in Contest Spectacular combinations; utilizing it causes Bullet Seed, Leech Seed, Seed Bomb, and Worry Seed to award an additional three appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. When powered up into Z-Rototiller by a Groundium Z, the user's Attack stat receives a single stage increase. Regarding its effects on a party, Rototiller boosts the Attack and Sp. Atk of all grounded Grass-type Pokémon within the user's party and the same physical room, encompassing the user if it is a Grass-type. Within Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto can acquire Rototiller after learning it from a Drilbur encountered in the Withered Wasteland. This enables Ditto to transform dirt or grass into crop fields, as well as collect plants and flowers. Curiously, in Pokémon Pokopia, Drilbur possesses the ability to both employ Rototiller and teach it to Ditto, despite not being able to learn the move through conventional means.

Title: Sticky Web (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Sticky Web (Japanese: ねばねばネット Sticky Net) is a Bug-type technique that deals no direct damage. Its primary effect is to establish an entry hazard that encircles the foe's side of the field, causing any opposing Pokémon to have its Speed stat reduced by one stage immediately after it switches into battle. However, its effects are nullified for Flying-type Pokémon, those possessing the Levitate Ability, and any Pokémon currently holding an Air Balloon. The move's presence can be cleared by either Rapid Spin or Defog; specifically, Rapid Spin eliminates Sticky Web affecting the user's side, whereas Defog clears it from both sides of the battlefield. Beyond battle, Sticky Web has utility in Contest Spectaculars, earning the performer an additional three appeal points when utilized in a combination following String Shot on the preceding turn. When amplified by a Buginium Z, transforming it into Z-Sticky Web, the user experiences a one-stage increase in its Speed stat. In the Mystery Dungeon series, its functionality diverges from other entry hazards, as it does not manifest as a floor trap within dungeons. Rather, it targets a direct opponent, subsequently diminishing their Travel Speed. Furthermore, it renders the target's held item "sticky." Should an ally be struck by this move, a randomly selected item from their Bag or Toolbox will similarly become sticky.

Title: Fell Stinger (move)

Fell Stinger (Japanese: とどめばり Finishing Stinger) is a Bug-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation VI. When this move causes a targeted Pokémon to faint, the user's Attack stat gains two stages. During Generation VI, its base power was 30. The move's power was later augmented to 50. Currently, if it brings down the targeted Pokémon, the user's Attack stat is boosted by three stages. A notable aspect of Fell Stinger is its capacity to deal damage, and should it cause the opposing Pokémon to faint, the user's Attack stat ascends by two stages. In Pokémon GO, Fell Stinger functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible since December 11, 2019. The description for the move prior to Version 2.10.0 (dated June 28, 2021) read: "Drastically raises the user's Attack when it knocks out a target with this move."

Title: Phantom Force (move)

While "Ghost Dive" may refer to Phantom Force, it is also the title of a specific song, "Ghost Dive (song)." Phantom Force, known in Japanese as ゴーストダイブ (Ghost Dive), is an offensive Ghost-type move first introduced in Generation VI. It functioned as TM86 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently holds the designation of TM151 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Upon activation, Phantom Force causes the user to "vanish," granting a state of semi-invulnerability for that turn, before reappearing on the subsequent turn to inflict damage. This attack is capable of striking a target even if it is safeguarded by a protection move, simultaneously nullifying the effects of such moves on the target for the remainder of the turn. If it connects with a foe protected by a field-wide protection move, it removes that protection from the entire side of the field (even if the protection, like Crafty Shield, would typically not prevent Phantom Force). Should Phantom Force hit a Pokémon protected by a move that imposes a negative effect (for example, Spiky Shield), the user will not suffer from that negative effect. However, Phantom Force cannot remove the effects of protective moves if it would not affect the target itself (such as if the target is a Normal-type Pokémon).

Historically, if the target had used Minimize, Phantom Force would inflict double its standard damage and bypass accuracy checks to guarantee a hit, unless the target was already in the semi-invulnerable phase of another move like Dig or Fly. Furthermore, an opponent that has just utilized Lock-On or Mind Reader can still land an attack on the user of Phantom Force during its vanished state. This also applies if either the opponent or the user of Phantom Force possesses the No Guard Ability. If a Pokémon is asleep when it is scheduled to execute the second turn of Phantom Force, the semi-invulnerable state will end without the move being carried out. If the user is holding a Power Herb, this item will be consumed, allowing Phantom Force to be executed entirely within a single turn.

In more recent contexts, Phantom Force no longer bypasses accuracy checks nor delivers double its regular damage against a target that has used Minimize. Additionally, Phantom Force cannot pierce Max Guard or negate its protective properties.

In specific game environments, the user gains the "Phantom Force status" on its initial turn. During this phase, the Pokémon cannot be struck by moves, and projectiles will pass through the user's tile as if it were unoccupied. On the second turn, the user assaults the enemy positioned directly in front. This move breaks through the opponent's Protect, Wide Guard, or Quick Guard to inflict damage. In co-op battles, the user's phantom presence will also dissipate if a unity attack is performed. A description prior to Version 2.11.0 (released on July 28, 2021) indicated that if the user was hit by a sync move while in phantom presence, the presence would fade.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Phantom Force is Hoopa's initial combat ability, acquired at level 5 by evolving Astonish instead of Shadow Ball. The user conjures a ring beneath itself and teleports to a designated Pokémon's location, dealing damage to and shoving nearby opposing Pokémon at that point. Subsequently, the user fires two malevolent flames that damage any opposing Pokémon they strike and temporarily reduce their movement speed. This move can be activated a second time within a specific timeframe; if used again, the user can return to their original warp-in location. When this move is performed, if a nearby ally Pokémon is linked by Trick, that ally can also warp to the initial ring's position, and the ally Pokémon that warps in this manner will recover some HP. At level 12, the move grants the user a brief period of invincibility upon activation. When Hoopa is in its Unbound form, Phantom Force is replaced by Hyperspace Fury.

The Japanese name for Phantom Force was notably adopted as the title for the song "Ghost Dive." This move operates similarly to Shadow Force, but features a higher Power Points (PP) count and a lower base power.

Title: Noble Roar (move)

Noble Roar, known in Japanese as おたけび (War Cry), is a non-damaging Normal-type move that debuted in Generation VI. Before Generation VII, it served as the exclusive signature move for Litleo and Pyroar. When executed, Noble Roar decreases the target's Attack and Special Attack stats by a single stage. This technique is categorized as a sound-based move and uniquely affects Pokémon even when they are behind a substitute. Should Noble Roar be empowered by a Normalium Z, transforming it into Z-Noble Roar, the user's Defense stat will increase by one stage. It is noteworthy that Throat Chop does not inhibit a Pokémon from utilizing Z-Noble Roar. To reiterate, this move reduces the target's Attack and Sp. Atk by one stage each. The description in the game, prior to version 2.10.0 (which was effective from June 28, 2021), read: "Lowers the target's Attack and Sp. Atk. Although this move is activated as soon as the user enters an attacking posture, the move cannot be used until after the user leaves the posture." Furthermore, this buddy move was referred to as B Noble Roar before the release of version 2.38.0.

Title: Ion Deluge (move)

Known in Japanese as プラズマシャワー (Plasma Shower), Ion Deluge is an Electric-type utility move that causes no direct damage, making its debut in Generation VI. Its effect lasts for the duration of the current turn, transforming all Normal-type moves, even those that are status-oriented, into Electric-type. With a priority of +1, Ion Deluge is executed ahead of any moves lacking elevated priority. Struggle, however, remains unaffected by Ion Deluge. Conversion is also exempt from its influence; this move continues to consider a move's inherent type. Consequently, a Pokémon utilizing Conversion with a Normal-type move (including Conversion itself) positioned first in its move set will assume the Normal-type, not the Electric-type, even when Ion Deluge is active. Type-shifting attacks like Judgment or Revelation Dance fall under Ion Deluge's effect if their default damage output would be Normal-type. For moves requiring a charging phase, the type alteration only occurs if the user is under Ion Deluge's influence during the turn that damage is dealt. This type-modification takes precedence *after* any Abilities that alter move types. Thus, it cannot impact an inherently Normal-type move employed by a Pokémon possessing Pixilate, but it *can* affect a move originally of the Fairy-type used by a Pokémon with Normalize. Should Flying Press, when utilized by a Pokémon with Normalize, be caught by Ion Deluge, its damage type becomes Electric/Flying. When Freeze-Dry, employed by a Pokémon with Normalize, encounters Ion Deluge, its inherent super-effectiveness against Water-type Pokémon does not combine with the newly acquired Electric typing (resulting in only ×2 damage against Water-types). However, in Inverse Battles, Freeze-Dry under the influence of Normalize and subsequently Ion Deluge *will* inflict super-effective damage on Water-type Pokémon. A documented glitch in Version 1.0 of Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon caused Ion Deluge to invariably fail upon activation. When amplified by an Electrium Z into Z-Ion Deluge, the user receives a one-stage boost to its Special Attack stat. The floor status condition named Ion Deluge transforms all Normal-type attacks into Electric-type. This particular floor status persists for 10 turns. Ion Deluge stands as one of three moves that have appeared in a complete arc of the animated series but have never been featured in the English dub. The other two moves in this category are Conversion and Minimize. While Ion Deluge itself is inaccessible from Generation VIII onwards, its distinct effect can still be generated through the use of Plasma Fists.

Title: Parabolic Charge (move)

Debuting in Generation VI, Parabolic Charge (Japanese: パラボラチャージ Parabola Charge) is an Electric-type attack. Initially, it possessed a base power of 50 and recovered the user's HP by an amount equal to half of the damage it inflicted. If the Pokémon holding the move is equipped with a Big Root, this HP recovery is boosted by 30%, making the restored health equivalent to 65% of the damage dealt. It's important to note that Big Root does not amplify the damage output itself. Should the target Pokémon have the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user will instead suffer a loss of HP equal to the amount it would have gained. Usage of Parabolic Charge is not possible if the user is under the effects of Heal Block.

Beyond standard battles, Parabolic Charge finds use in Contest Spectaculars. It contributes to combinations, awarding an additional three appeal points if the move Charge was executed on the previous turn. Conversely, it can also initiate a combination, causing Electrify to grant an extra three appeal points if used subsequently. At a later point, Parabolic Charge's power was augmented, increasing from 50 to 65. This attack strikes all adjacent opposing Pokémon, and ½ of the total damage dealt will be restored to the user as HP.

In Pokémon GO, Parabolic Charge operates as a Charged Attack, becoming available on January 19, 2022. Here, the HP restored is 10% of the damage dealt to each target, with these individual amounts summed together. A description predating Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021) read: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target." Parabolic Charge holds two unique distinctions: it is the only HP-draining move capable of targeting all adjacent Pokémon, and it is the sole Electric-type HP-draining move.

Title: Disarming Voice (move)

Known in Japanese as チャームボイス (Charm Voice), Disarming Voice debuted as a damaging Fairy-type attack in the sixth generation of Pokémon games. This move is designated as TM019 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. It delivers damage and unfailingly connects with the target, as it circumvents accuracy calculations; the only exceptions are foes rendered semi-invulnerable during specific actions like Dig or Fly. Furthermore, Disarming Voice is capable of striking Pokémon even when they are shielded by a Substitute. As a sync move, its potential for 6★ EX elevation remained locked until July 20, 2021. This milestone occurred 354 days following its initial availability on July 31, 2020. Before Version 2.18.5, implemented on March 15, 2022, the 6★ EX effect for this particular sync move made it target all opposing Pokémon.

Title: Parting Shot (move)

The maneuver known as Parting Shot (Japanese: すてゼリフ Parting Shot), a Dark-type technique that inflicts no direct damage, made its debut in Generation VI. Before the advent of Generation VII, this particular move was exclusive to the Pokémon Pancham and Pangoro. Utilizing Parting Shot causes the opposing Pokémon's Attack and Special Attack attributes to decrease by a single stage apiece, subsequently compelling the user to withdraw from battle. It is categorized as a sound-based ability, effectively bypassing a foe's Substitute.

Should Parting Shot be directed at a Pokémon protected by Magic Coat or possessing the Magic Bounce Ability, its effects are reversed onto the originator, causing the user's own Attack and Special Attack to drop, while the targeted Pokémon is then switched out of combat. When an adversary employs Pursuit during the identical turn Parting Shot is executed, Pursuit inflicts standard damage if the opponent holds a speed advantage, but deals twice the damage if they are slower than the Parting Shot user.

The user remains in battle and does not switch out under several conditions: if Parting Shot itself fails (e.g., lacking a target), if the target is immune (e.g., via Soundproof), if a protective move intercepts it, or if no other Pokémon remains available in the party to replace the user. In previous iterations, the user would still switch out even if Parting Shot was incapable of altering the target's stats (for example, due to Abilities like Clear Body or effects like Mist). However, this behavior has since changed, and the user now remains in battle if the target's stats are unaffected by such means. Furthermore, if the adversary has already reached its minimum of -6 stages for both Attack and Special Attack, the user will not swap out. An exception exists for Pokémon with the Contrary Ability; in that specific scenario, the user will not switch if the target possesses the maximum +6 stages for both Attack and Special Attack. Interestingly, if Parting Shot's stat-lowering effect is prevented by Mirror Armor, the user still successfully switches out, irrespective of any potential stat reductions applied back to the user itself.

The Ability Liquid Voice transforms Parting Shot into a Water-type maneuver, consequently rendering it susceptible to blocking by Abilities that grant immunity to Water-type attacks. When Parting Shot is amplified into Z-Parting Shot via a Darkinium Z, any Pokémon brought into battle as a replacement for the user on that same turn will have its HP completely replenished. This HP restoration occurs even if Z-Parting Shot did not execute successfully after gaining its Z-Power enhancement. It's worth noting that Throat Chop is ineffective at impeding a Pokémon from performing Z-Parting Shot. In essence, Parting Shot diminishes an adversary's Attack and Sp. Atk by one stage, allowing the user to exchange places with an available ally.

During Generation VII, a notable bug on the Battle Spot platform led to game crashes when Parting Shot was deployed as a Z-Move. An identical issue was observed in conjunction with the move Memento. Consequently, both Parting Shot and Memento faced a temporary ban from Battle Spot competitions, effective from November 2016. This defect was ultimately rectified with the release of the version 1.1 update.

Title: Draining Kiss (move)

This Fairy-type offensive technique, known in Japanese as ドレインキッス (Drain Kiss), made its debut in Generation VI. It was assigned as TM87 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM037 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Beyond inflicting damage, Draining Kiss simultaneously restores 75% of the damage inflicted to the user's HP. An equipped Big Root amplifies this HP restoration by 30%, increasing the total healing to 97.5% of the damage dealt. Crucially, the Big Root item offers no increase to the move's damage output. However, if the target carries the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user suffers HP damage instead of healing, equal to the amount that would have been gained. Its use is also prevented if the user is under the influence of Heal Block. Earlier mechanics involved Draining Kiss inflicting damage and restoring ½ of the damage dealt to the user as HP.
