Separately, a specific description released before Version 2.10.0 (on June 28, 2021) stated: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target. HP restored is equal to 10% of the damage dealt, rounded down."

Within the distinct game of Pokémon UNITE, Draining Kiss functions as Sylveon's second move. It becomes available at level 6 by upgrading Baby-Doll Eyes, chosen over Calm Mind. This particular iteration involves the user blowing a kiss at an enemy, which then travels back and forth multiple times between the two. Upon contact with the user, HP is regained. When it strikes the enemy, damage is dealt, and their movement speed is temporarily decreased for a short time. If the user and enemy distance themselves too far from one another, the kiss's duration is shortened. At level 12, the healing effect receives an upgrade. In the special episode Stealth!! Infiltrate the Energy Plant, Opal's Alcremie was revealed to know Draining Kiss, although it was never actually seen employing the move. It maintains the distinction of being the only Fairy-type move that drains HP.

Title: Crafty Shield (move)

Known in Japan as トリックガード (Trick Guard), Crafty Shield is a non-damaging Fairy-type attack introduced in Generation VI. Before the arrival of Generation VII, this move was among Klefki's exclusive signature techniques. Crafty Shield creates a defensive barrier, safeguarding all Pokémon on the user's side of the field from status moves. However, it provides no protection against actions the user takes on itself or moves that target the user's entire side of the field (meaning an ally can still use Acupressure on themselves or deploy Aromatherapy). It also fails to defend against entry hazard moves or attacks that affect all Pokémon, such as Perish Song. Crafty Shield possesses the unique property of being usable consecutively without any chance of failure. Moves designed to penetrate defenses, like Feint, will effectively remove Crafty Shield's active effects. Should it be enhanced by a Fairium Z to become Z-Crafty Shield, the user's Special Defense stat receives a one-stage boost. This move applies the 'Crafty Shield' status condition to the user and its teammates in the same room, which provides immunity to all status moves. This protective status endures for one turn.

Title: Flower Shield (move)

Debuting in Generation VI, Flower Shield (Japanese: フラワーガード Flower Guard) is a Fairy-type maneuver that inflicts no damage. This technique is designed to elevate the Defense stat of every Grass-type Pokémon on the field by one stage. An important exception exists for Pokémon currently in the semi-invulnerable state of moves like Fly or Dig, as they will not receive the effect. Should no eligible Grass-type Pokémon be present, the move consequently fails. Furthermore, when powered up by a Fairium Z, transforming into Z-Flower Shield, it instead provides a one-stage increase to the user's Defense stat. For Grass-type Pokémon within the party in the same room, Flower Shield similarly boosts their Defense.

Title: Grassy Terrain (move)

Known in Japan as グラスフィールド (Grass Field), Grassy Terrain made its debut as a non-damaging Grass-type technique in Generation VI. This move functioned as TM88 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM137 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. When activated, Grassy Terrain establishes a specialized ground effect across the battlefield, altering the visual backdrop and superseding any existing terrain conditions. This effect persists for five turns before dissipating.

Pokémon grounded on this terrain, excluding those in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move, experience several consequences: Their Grass-type attacks gain a 50% power increase (irrespective of whether the target itself is situated on Grassy Terrain). Furthermore, at the conclusion of every turn, each affected Pokémon recovers 1/16 of its total HP. Floral Healing becomes more potent, healing up to ⅔ of a target's maximum HP instead of its typical ½. Against Pokémon benefiting from Grassy Terrain, the efficacy of Bulldoze, Earthquake, and Magnitude is halved (regardless of whether the attacker is also on the terrain). Secret Power, when used here, adopts Vine Whip's animation and has a 30% probability of inducing sleep in the opponent. While Grassy Terrain is active, natural field objects vanish and become indestructible.

A Pokémon possessing the Grassy Surge Ability automatically generates Grassy Terrain upon entering combat. Similarly, a Pokémon with the Grass Pelt Ability has its Defense stat elevated by 50% upon joining the fray. Should the user be holding a Terrain Extender, the terrain's duration extends to 8 turns instead of the standard 5. When a Pokémon holds a Grassy Seed on a Grassy Terrain field, the item is consumed, and the Pokémon's Defense stat rises by one stage (even if the Pokémon itself isn't otherwise impacted by the terrain's effects).

When Grassy Terrain is amplified by a Grassium Z into Z-Grassy Terrain, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its Defense stat. Furthermore, it boosts the power of Grass-type moves used by affected Pokémon by 30% instead of the previous 50%. This terrain also converts Terrain Pulse's type to Grass and doubles its base power from 50 to 100, and it changes a Pokémon's type to Grass if that Pokémon possesses the Mimicry Ability.

The terrain also forms automatically upon the use of Max Overgrowth, or when a Pokémon with the Seed Sower Ability takes damage from an attack. Moves such as Defog, G-Max Wind Rage, Steel Roller, and Ice Spinner can clear Grassy Terrain, along with any other terrain, when executed. Notably, Steel Roller will fail if no terrain, including Grassy Terrain, is present.

Within dungeon settings, Grassy Terrain modifies the dungeon status, leading to triple natural HP recovery for Grass-types, a 50% power increase for Grass-type moves, and a 50% reduction in damage from Earthquake, Bulldoze, and Magnitude. These dungeon effects are limited to grounded Pokémon and cannot coincide with other terrains, lasting for 10 turns.

In Pokémon Masters EX, before version 2.42.0, the sync pair Scottie/Bettie & LeafeonEgg had access to Grassy Terrain; however, with the update to version 2.42.0, this was swapped for Grass Wish, presumably due to Leafeon's inability to learn Grassy Terrain in the main series titles. The move also appeared in the anime episode "Morphing Manectric," where Trevor's Flabébé deployed Grassy Terrain to shield Élec from an Earthquake initiated by a Team Flare Grunt's Tyrunt, though its execution occurred off-panel.

Title: Misty Terrain (move)

Introduced in Generation VI, Misty Terrain (known as ミストフィールド, or Mist Field, in Japanese) is a Fairy-type technique that deals no direct damage. Trainers can acquire it as TM89 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and as TM139 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move generates a special terrain that blankets the battle arena, altering the visual background and overriding any pre-existing terrain effects. Its influence dissipates following five turns.

For Pokémon that are grounded and not currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move, this terrain imposes several specific outcomes. The potency of Dragon-type attacks directed at affected Pokémon is reduced by half, irrespective of whether the attacker itself is under the terrain's effect. Grounded Pokémon within the terrain's influence are safeguarded from incurring non-volatile status ailments; however, any Pokémon already suffering from such conditions will not be cured. Should an affected Pokémon become drowsy from the move Yawn, it will be kept from succumbing to sleep. When Secret Power is utilized under these conditions, it adopts Fairy Wind's visual effect and has a chance to decrease the target's Special Attack. While Misty Terrain is active, natural environmental objects vanish and become indestructible. It is notable that Misty Terrain does not inherently stop Pokémon from becoming confused; nevertheless, Pokémon currently under its influence are rendered immune to confusion.

Should a Pokémon possessing the Misty Surge Ability enter combat, Misty Terrain manifests automatically. A Pokémon holding a Misty Seed when Misty Terrain is active will consume the item, receiving a one-stage boost to its Special Defense stat; this occurs even if the Pokémon wouldn't typically be affected by the terrain's other properties. Holding a Terrain Extender during the activation of Misty Terrain causes the effect to persist for eight turns rather than the standard five.

When amplified by a Fairium Z into Z-Misty Terrain, the user's Special Defense stat experiences a one-stage increase. It transforms Terrain Pulse's type to Fairy and doubles its base power from 50 to 100. For a Pokémon with the Mimicry Ability, its type is altered to Fairy. An affected Pokémon made drowsy by G-Max Snooze is also prevented from falling asleep. The use of Max Starfall similarly results in the automatic generation of Misty Terrain.

Moves such as Defog, G-Max Wind Rage, Steel Roller, and Ice Spinner will eliminate Misty Terrain, along with any other active terrain, upon their execution. Notably, Steel Roller will fail to execute if Misty Terrain or any other terrain is not currently present.

Within a dungeon, Misty Terrain modifies the dungeon's status to reflect its presence, reducing damage from Dragon-type attacks by 50% and warding off detrimental status conditions. These dungeon effects exclusively impact grounded Pokémon and cannot exist simultaneously with other terrain types. In this context, Misty Terrain persists for 10 turns. Uniquely among terrains, Misty Terrain does not enhance the power of moves sharing its own type. Its specific characteristic is to instead diminish the power of Dragon-type attacks directed at grounded Pokémon.

Title: Electrify (move)

Known in Japanese as そうでん, Electrify is a non-damaging Electric-type technique first introduced in Generation VI. (For details on the field move, refer to Electrify (Field Move)). Prior to Generation VIII, it served as the signature move for Helioptile and Heliolisk. If a target has not yet acted during a turn, Electrify compels their subsequent move to become Electric-type for that specific turn, even if the move is a status-affecting one. Moves that incorporate a charging phase will only undergo this type alteration if the user is under Electrify's influence on the turn they deliver damage. Notably, Electrify does not cause Conversion to change its user to the Electric type if Conversion is performed as the user's initial move. Struggle, however, remains unaffected by Electrify.

When Flying Press is subjected to Electrify, it then inflicts Electric/Flying-type damage. If Freeze-Dry is affected by Electrify, its inherent super-effective property against Water-type Pokémon does not combine with its new Electric typing (a Water-type will still sustain only ×2 damage). However, in Inverse Battles, an Electrified Freeze-Dry will hit Water-type Pokémon with super-effective force. This move bypasses accuracy checks, ensuring a hit unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable stage of an action like Dig or Fly.

When empowered by an Electrium Z to become Z-Electrify, the user's Special Attack stat increases by one stage. Electrify can also be integrated into a Contest Spectacular combination, yielding three extra appeal points if Parabolic Charge was used in the preceding turn. If this move is directed at a Pokémon about to unleash a damage-dealing Max Move, it transforms that move into Max Lightning, regardless of the original move attempted, with the exception that Gigantamax Pikachu or Toxtricity will instead trigger their respective G-Max moves. Should Electrify modify the intended G-Max move of a Gigantamax Rillaboom, Cinderace, or Inteleon, the resulting Max Lightning will possess only its standard power. Ultimately, Electrify inflicts the Electrify status condition on the target, which causes all moves that Pokémon uses to become Electric type.

Title: Play Rough (move)

Play Rough (Japanese: じゃれつく Frolic) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type move first introduced in Generation VI. It serves as TM44 in the games Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. In Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Mimikyu, when holding Mimikium Z, can upgrade this move into the specialized Z-Move, Let's Snuggle Forever. Play Rough inflicts damage and carries a 10% chance of lowering the target's Attack stat by one stage. This offensive move causes damage and may decrease an adversary's Attack by a single level. When utilized by Hop & Zacian, thanks to their passive skill The Sword That Saved Galar, this attack targets all opponents and invariably results in a critical hit. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), its description noted: "Has a very small chance of lowering the target's Attack."

In Pokémon UNITE, Play Rough functions as Azumarill's initial move, acquired by reaching level 7 and upgrading Bubble into it rather than Water Pulse. The move provides a 35% increase in movement speed for 4 seconds and modifies the next basic attack. Upon executing the altered basic attack, the user will lunge at one enemy within range, delivering repeated blows that inflict damage and render that target unable to act for .75s. Activating Play Rough will override any active Aqua Tail attack modification. At level 13, the move further enhances movement speed when used, and its cooldown is also reduced by 1.5s. Before Generation VIII, Play Rough was the sole physical Fairy-type move, excluding Z-Moves or Max Moves. However, it remains the only one that is broadly distributed, as Spirit Break is exclusive to Grimmsnarl and Iron Valiant, while Magical Torque is not usable by the player.

Title: Fairy Wind (move)

Debuting in Generation VI, Fairy Wind (Japanese: ようせいのかぜ Fairy Wind) is an offensive maneuver of the Fairy type. It delivers direct damage without any additional effects. In Pokémon GO, Fairy Wind functions as a Fast Attack, having been accessible to players since September 1, 2022. The episode "Noibat Stands in the Way" depicted Trevor commanding his Flabébé to execute Fairy Wind; however, due to not hearing him, the Pokémon failed to carry out the attack. Fairy Wind stands among a select group of moves that saw modifications between its appearances in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon and Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX. While possessing a one-tile radius in Super Mystery Dungeon, its range expanded to a two-tile radius in Rescue Team DX. Before the launch of Pokémon X and Y, a trailer unveiled at E3 2013 showcased Gardevoir utilizing Fairy Wind. Despite this early depiction, Gardevoir is unable to learn Fairy Wind in the game's official release.

Title: Moonblast (move)

Moonblast (Japanese: ムーンフォース Moon Force) is a Fairy-type offensive technique that debuted in Generation VI. This damaging attack not only inflicts harm but also possesses a 30% probability of decreasing the target's Special Attack by one stage. Prior to Version 2.10.0, which was released on June 28, 2021, its description indicated a "moderate chance" of lowering the target's Sp. Atk.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Moonblast functions as Gardevoir's first available move. Kirlia can acquire this ability upon reaching level 7, choosing to upgrade Confusion into it rather than Psychic. When utilized, the user recoils while unleashing a blast; this projectile stuns the initial foe struck and harms all opponents within a conical area beyond them. An enhancement at level 13 additionally bestows a defensive shield upon its user.

In a press release for Pokémon X and Y issued on June 11, 2013, it was initially stated that Moonblast would diminish the target's Attack stat. However, in the game's final version, its effect was altered to instead lower their Special Attack.

Title: Boomburst (move)

Introduced in Generation VI, Boomburst (Japanese: ばくおんぱ Sound Wave Bomb) is a Normal-type move designed to inflict damage. This sound-based attack targets all nearby Pokémon, including allied combatants, and uniquely bypasses Substitutes to strike opponents. Its expansive reach means it affects every Pokémon present in the current area, friendly or otherwise, with the sole exception of the user. In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Boomburst is classified as a Charged Attack, becoming available to players on February 5th, 2023. Notably, it possesses the highest base power among all moves that do not impose negative effects on the user. It also holds the highest base power among sound-based attacks, with the specific exclusion of Echoed Voice, which can potentially reach a base power of 200. Boomburst made its animated series debut as the first Generation VI move to be showcased, utilized by Alexa's Noivern in "Celebrating the Hero's Comet!", an event that occurred prior to the release of Pokémon X and Y.

Title: Fairy Lock (move)

Debuting in Generation VI, Fairy Lock (Japanese: フェアリーロック Fairy Lock) is a status-oriented Fairy-type move. This move is uniquely associated with the Pokémon Klefki. Upon activation, Fairy Lock imposes a restriction on all active Pokémon present, barring them (with the sole exception of Ghost-type Pokémon) from either withdrawing from battle or attempting to flee during their subsequent turn. However, it does not override effects from specific moves or items designed to compel a Pokémon to switch out, including techniques like Dragon Tail and Whirlwind, or held items such as the Eject Button and Red Card. Should a Pokémon present on the battlefield when Fairy Lock is initiated be knocked out later within that identical turn, its replacement will nonetheless find itself unable to switch out during the subsequent turn. When transformed into its Z-Move variant, Z-Fairy Lock, through the application of a Fairium Z, this maneuver grants the user a one-stage increase to their Defense stat. A notable bug existed exclusively in version 1.1.0 of Pokémon Sword and Shield: activating Fairy Lock with in-game animations turned on invariably led to a software crash. This issue was particularly severe in multiplayer encounters, where animations are compulsory, resulting in both participating players' consoles freezing. The glitch was absent in prior software releases and subsequently rectified in the 1.1.1 update. Effectively, Fairy Lock applies the 'Stuck' status condition to all proximate opposing Pokémon.

Title: Play Nice (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Play Nice (なかよくする Make Friends in Japanese) is a Normal-type status move that inflicts no damage. Its primary effect is to decrease the opponent's Attack stat by a single stage. This move always connects, bypassing standard accuracy checks, except when the target is temporarily invulnerable during moves like Dig or Fly. It successfully lands even against defensive measures such as Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, or Max Guard, though it is blocked by Crafty Shield. Furthermore, it penetrates through substitutes. When enhanced by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Play Nice, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its own Defense stat. In Contest Spectaculars, Play Nice functions as part of a combination. If followed in the subsequent turn by Circle Throw, Seismic Toss, Sky Drop, Smack Down, Storm Throw, or Vital Throw, it awards an additional three appeal points. Within the game Pokémon Rumble Rush, Play Nice, like all other status moves, was exclusively available to opposing Pokémon and could not be acquired by any Pokémon captured by the player. Notably, it was observed as a move utilized by opposing Swirlix.

Title: Confide (move)

The Normal-type attack known as Confide (Japanese: ないしょばなし, or Secret Talk) made its debut in Generation VI and inflicts no damage. From Generation VI up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, this move was designated as TM100. As of Generation IX, Confide serves as the unique signature move for the Impidimp evolutionary family. Upon use, Confide decreases the opponent's Special Attack by a single stage. It inherently strikes its mark without fail, sidestepping standard accuracy calculations, unless the foe is within the semi-invulnerable phase of certain moves like Dig or Fly. It is capable of connecting with Pokémon even when they are shielded by a substitute. While it successfully affects targets that have employed Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield, Crafty Shield will nonetheless impede its effect. When boosted by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Confide, the user receives a one-stage increase to their Special Defense statistic. Notably, Throat Chop is ineffective at stopping a Pokémon from utilizing Z-Confide.

Title: Diamond Storm (move)

A Rock-type move that inflicts damage, Diamond Storm (Japanese: ダイヤストーム) first appeared in Generation VI. It serves as Diancie's exclusive signature technique. Initially, the move possessed a 50% likelihood to boost the user's Defense by one stage for each Pokémon it struck. This mechanic was subsequently updated; Diamond Storm now holds a 50% chance to raise the user's Defense by two stages if the move connects, replacing the former effect of one stage per Pokémon hit. If multiple Defense stat increases occur from a single use of the move, the stat-raising animation will play only once, but a message confirming the Defense rise will be displayed for each individual stage gain. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, from versions 1.0 to 1.2.1, Diamond Storm could not be chosen during battles. Starting with Version 1.3.0, it became usable and functioned identically to its performance in the preceding generation. This move targets and deals damage to enemies positioned adjacent to the user. Beyond its offensive capability, it also carries the potential to enhance the user's Defense. When used by Hilda (Special Costume) & Diancie, their passive skill Swag Bag 1 ensures a 100% chance of raising their Defense. Following Mega Evolution, Hilda (Special Costume) & Diancie's Fuel Economy 1 passive skill reduces the move's cost to just three move gauge slots. Any stat decreases caused by this move are applied to each target it hits. Prior to version 2.38.0, this buddy move was identified as B Diamond Storm. Diamond Storm holds the distinction of being the sole Rock-type move introduced in Generation VI. Furthermore, exclusively within Generation VI, Diamond Storm was the only move capable of increasing the same stat multiple distinct times from a single use, without the user benefiting from Parental Bond.

Title: Steam Eruption (move)

The Water-type attack known as Steam Eruption (スチームバースト, or Steam Burst, in Japanese) first appeared in Generation VI, dealing damage to opponents. This move, exclusive to Volcanion, also carries a 30% probability of inflicting a burn on the foe. When Steam Eruption is used, it will cure the user of its frozen status, even if the attack misses the target. Furthermore, any frozen opponent struck by Steam Eruption will also be thawed. From Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, this move was unavailable for selection in combat. However, since Version 1.3.0, it has been fully accessible and operates as it did in prior game generations.

Title: Hyperspace Hole (move)

Hyperspace Hole (いじげんホール Interdimensional Hole in Japanese) is a Psychic-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation VI. It serves as Hoopa Confined's characteristic technique. This move deals damage and guarantees a hit by bypassing standard accuracy checks, except when the opposing Pokémon is in the semi-invulnerable state from actions like Dig or Fly. It also strikes through protective abilities such as Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, King's Shield, Mat Block, Baneful Bunker, Obstruct, Silk Trap, or Burning Bulwark, nullifying their effects for the turn's duration. Should it connect with a target shielded by Mat Block, that Mat Block is entirely cleared from the field side. The effects of Quick Guard, Wide Guard, and Crafty Shield are additionally dispelled from the opponent's side. Hyperspace Hole is incapable of clearing defensive effects if it inherently cannot impact the foe, for instance, against a Dark-type Pokémon. It effectively penetrates through Substitute. Within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, if Hoopa Confined possesses this move and transitions into its Unbound form, Hyperspace Hole is forgotten, being superseded by Hyperspace Fury. The attack inflicts damage and pierces common defensive strategies like Protect, Wide Guard, Quick Guard, Mat Block, King's Shield, or Spiky Shield. Its power is always at its maximum potential, and it starts with 13 PP, though its underlying base PP is registered as 5.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Hyperspace Hole functions as Hoopa's initial offensive option. Players acquire it at level 4 by evolving Confusion into this move, as an alternative to Trick. The user generates a substantial ring at a chosen spot, inflicting damage upon foes within its radius. Following a 3-second delay, the user and any allied Pokémon present in the ring's area are transported back to their base, gaining a temporary boost to their movement speed. These allies retain the ability to instantly return to the ring's origin point within those same 3 seconds. An active ally forward goal zone may likewise be selected as the ring's spawn point. Should a ring be established on the top path's forward goal zone, a corresponding ring simultaneously appears on the bottom path's forward goal zone for 6.5 seconds, and vice-versa. Every ally, Hoopa included, can utilize these rings for instantaneous transit between the two goal zones, receiving a brief movement speed enhancement post-warp. After an ally uses a warp, a 15-second cooldown is imposed before that specific ally can re-use these rings. If a path's forward goal zone is obliterated, the middle goal zone on the identical path substitutes it. This ability is unusable on base goal zones. Upon reaching level 10, Hyperspace Hole additionally grants increased movement speed to nearby allied Pokémon as they approach the ring. When Hoopa enters its Unbound form, this particular move transforms into Psybeam.

Hyperspace Hole remains the unique Psychic-type attack introduced in Generation VI. Furthermore, it is the singular damaging Psychic-type move that inherently never misses, disregarding Z-Moves and Max Moves.

Title: Water Shuriken (move)

Water Shuriken (Japanese: みずしゅりけん Water Shuriken) is a Water-type offensive technique introduced in the sixth generation of Pokémon games. This multi-hit ability first appeared as a physical move within its debut generation. It serves as Accelgor's signature move in Generation VIII and Greninja's signature move in Generation IX. Functioning as a high-priority attack, Water Shuriken strikes its target between two and five times per use, with each individual strike possessing an independent chance for a critical hit.

The probability of hitting a specific number of times is distributed as follows: there's a 35% likelihood of striking twice, another 35% chance of hitting three times, and a 15% chance for either four or five strikes. Across all uses, its average cumulative base power totals 46.5.

Mechanically, Water Shuriken can continue its assault even after successfully breaking a target's Substitute. For defensive moves like Bide and Counter, only the final strike of Water Shuriken is taken into account. Abilities such as Weak Armor, Water Compaction, and Stamina will be triggered by each separate hit. Should a Focus Sash activate before the final blow, the subsequent hit will cause the defending Pokémon to faint. A Focus Band, however, can provide multiple chances to prevent fainting, with each activation opportunity being distinct. If a defending Pokémon has only 1 maximum HP, Sturdy will activate with every hit. When the user holds a King's Rock or Razor Fang, each consecutive hit has an independent chance to cause the opponent to flinch.

In a subsequent shift, Water Shuriken was reclassified as a special move. Mirror Coat, similar to Bide and Counter, will only reflect the damage of the move's last strike. The special interaction between Water Shuriken and Battle Bond, which previously boosted its power from 15 to 20 and guaranteed three hits, is no longer present.

Under specific conditions, Water Shuriken may behave differently. It is stated that the move always launches five projectiles per use. If used as a Plus Move, it will release only a single projectile but benefit from significantly increased power. When wielded by Mega Greninja, the attack features a unique animation depicting a larger, faster projectile. In both the Plus Move and Mega Greninja scenarios, the move's power is fixed at 75. Each strike also undergoes a separate accuracy check; however, the move sequence will persist even if an individual hit fails to connect. The activation of a Reviver Seed by the target will immediately conclude the move.

Beyond the main series, Water Shuriken is Greninja's neutral special attack in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate. This is a chargeable projectile that travels slowly and delivers multiple hits when fully charged, whereas an uncharged release results in a small, fast projectile. Uniquely among chargeable projectiles in this game, it cannot be stored.

In Pokémon UNITE, Water Shuriken is Greninja's initial offensive option. It becomes available at level 7 upon evolving from Frogadier, replacing Bubble as an upgrade choice over Surf. The user projects 3-5 water shurikens that detonate upon impacting an enemy, inflicting damage, slowing foes in a small area, and simultaneously restoring the user's HP. At level 11, Greninja gains an additional shuriken per use. Greninja's Unite Move, Waterburst Shuriken, bears resemblance to this move.

Water Shuriken holds several unique distinctions: it is the sole move whose category has changed since Generation IV (prior to which categories were assigned to types rather than individual moves). Furthermore, it stands as the only multi-strike move with increased priority. Before Generation IX, it was also the only multi-strike move classified as special.

Title: Mystical Fire (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Mystical Fire (Japanese: マジカルフレイム Magical Flame) is a Fire-type attack that inflicts damage. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was designated as TM92. Historically, this move initially delivered damage and decreased the target's Special Attack by one stage. Its base power later ascended from 65 to 75. A subsequent adjustment then reduced its power to 70, at which point it also began to lower the opponent's offensive stats, specifically diminishing the enemy's Sp. Atk.

Transitioning to Pokémon UNITE, Mystical Fire serves as the initial move for Sylveon, Delphox, and Chandelure. For Sylveon, it is acquired upon Eevee's evolution at level 4, taking the place of Swift as an upgrade option over Hyper Voice. The Pokémon employing this ability generates four miniature flames while making a leap in the chosen direction. These fiery projectiles then fly towards foes sequentially, inflicting harm and reducing their Special Attack by 15% for one second, with this effect accumulating up to four times. Every successful hit from a flame lessens the move's cooldown; moreover, once the initial flame strikes a target, any subsequent flames hitting that identical foe will inflict only 25% of the standard damage. Upon reaching level 10, an additional, fifth flame is incorporated into the attack.

Delphox gains this attack upon evolving from Braixen at level 6, transforming Ember into Mystical Fire as an alternative to Fire Blast. This variant involves releasing a unique, scorching fireball straight ahead. Should it connect with an adversary, it detonates, dealing damage to all within its area of effect. From level 11 onward, striking any target with the move decreases the cooldown of all other abilities by one second, with an additional 1.5-second reduction if the hit connects with an opposing team's enemy.

In the realm of Pokémon GO, Mystical Fire is categorized as a Charged Attack, having been made accessible starting May 21, 2023. During the episode "An Appetite for Battle!", Wylie's attempt to instruct his Delphox to execute Mystical Fire was thwarted when he injured his back, leading to the cancellation of the match. Notably, Mystical Fire holds the distinction of being the sole Fire-type move introduced during Generation VI.

Title: Spiky Shield (move)

Known in Japanese as ニードルガード (Needle Guard), Spiky Shield debuted as a non-damaging Grass-type technique in Generation VI. Upon its activation, Spiky Shield grants the user protection from all incoming move effects that specifically target it for the duration of that turn, encompassing any direct damage. This safeguard, however, does not extend to damage sustained at the conclusion of a turn, such as from weather conditions or persistent status ailments. Should Spiky Shield successfully impede a move requiring physical contact with the user, the initiating attacker will be harmed for 1/8 of its total HP. In cases where a contact-based multistrike attack is repelled, the recoil damage will only be inflicted a single time. Furthermore, attacks that are stopped by Spiky Shield will not trigger secondary contact-dependent effects, such as those from Rocky Helmet or the Rough Skin Ability. The move is unsuccessful if the user takes its action as the very last Pokémon in the turn order. Consecutive successful uses of Endure, any protection move exclusively affecting the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard diminish Spiky Shield's likelihood of success. With each such subsequent use, its chance of succeeding is reduced by dividing it by 3.

Certain attacks are capable of inflicting harm even through Spiky Shield's protection. The majority of damaging Z-Moves and Max Moves will strike for only 25% of their normal power; however, G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow are exceptions, dealing their full damage. Moves designed to pierce protective barriers, such as Feint, will deal their complete damage and simultaneously nullify Spiky Shield's protective effects. The Unseen Fist Ability enables contact-based moves to circumvent Spiky Shield entirely. A specific list exists detailing status moves that remain unimpeded by Spiky Shield. In general, Spiky Shield is unable to block moves that target only the user, moves that affect both the user and all allies, moves aimed at all combatants, or any entry hazard deployments. As an increased-priority move, Spiky Shield typically executes prior to the majority of other attacks within a given turn. When enhanced by a Grassium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Spiky Shield, the user's Defense stat receives a boost of one stage. This grants the user the Spiky Shield status condition for one turn, safeguarding it from the majority of incoming assaults. Nevertheless, damage can still be inflicted via items or explosion-based moves. Consecutive activations of Protect, Detect, Endure, Spiky Shield itself, King's Shield, Wide Guard, or Quick Guard will similarly decrease the move's overall chance of successful execution.

Title: Aromatic Mist (move)

Known in Japanese as アロマミスト (Aroma Mist), Aromatic Mist stands as a non-offensive Fairy-type technique that first appeared in Generation VI. Before Generation VII, it served as Aromatisse's exclusive signature move. This move increases a chosen ally Pokémon's Special Defense stat by a single stage. Within a Battle Royal format, where the concept of allies is absent, Aromatic Mist can be effectively aimed at any Pokémon on the opposing side. However, its execution will fail in a Single Battle or during a Horde Encounter, even if attempted by one of the five opposing Pokémon. The move successfully strikes its target despite the use of Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield; however, it is ineffective if the target is shielded by Crafty Shield or is currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions such as Fly or Dig. When amplified by a Fairium Z, transforming into Z-Aromatic Mist, the user's own Special Defense stat sees an increase of two stages. Beyond its primary effect, Aromatic Mist confers a one-stage boost to the Sp. Defense of all teammates located in the same room.

Title: Eerie Impulse (move)

Known in Japan as かいでんぱ (Mysterious Radio Wave), Eerie Impulse made its debut in Generation VI. This Electric-type technique deals no damage. It previously held the identifier TM93 within Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM096 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Its effect is to reduce an opposing Pokémon's Special Attack stat by two stages. When boosted by an Electrium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Eerie Impulse, it grants the user a one-stage increase in their Special Defense. Essentially, this maneuver brings down a foe's Sp. Attack by two stages. Its in-game description before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021) read: "Sharply lowers the target's Sp. Atk." Notably, among all variations of Charm, Eerie Impulse stands alone as the sole move never depicted in the Pokémon animated series.

Title: Venom Drench (move)

Introduced in Generation VI, Venom Drench (Japanese: ベノムトラップ, or Venom Trap) is a non-damaging Poison-type move. It was obtainable as TR91 in the Pokémon Sword and Shield games. The move's primary effect is to decrease the Attack, Special Attack, and Speed stats of all poisoned targets by one stage. This reduction applies to any target that is either poisoned or badly poisoned. However, if no targets are currently poisoned, Venom Drench will fail to execute. The stat drops inflicted by this move affect each poisoned Pokémon it hits.

Beyond standard battles, Venom Drench can be strategically employed in Contest Spectacular combinations. It grants an additional three points if moves such as Poison Gas, Poison Powder, Toxic, or Toxic Spikes were used during the preceding turn. Furthermore, when powered up by a Poisonium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Venom Drench, the user receives a one-stage boost to their Defense stat. A past description of the move, valid before version 2.10.0 (issued on June 28, 2021), read: "Lowers the target's Attack, Sp. Atk and Speed if the target is poisoned or badly poisoned. Fails if the target is not poisoned or badly poisoned."

Title: Powder (move)

The Bug-type, non-damaging move known as Powder (Japanese: ふんじん, meaning Dust) made its debut in Generation VI. Before Generation VII, this attack was exclusive to Vivillon. Powder blankets the chosen target in a fine dust for the current turn's duration. Should a Pokémon afflicted by Powder attempt to employ a Fire-type move, they will sustain damage equivalent to 25% of their maximum HP and be prevented from executing that move, although its PP will still be consumed.

With a priority of +1, Powder is always activated prior to any moves lacking an elevated priority status. Certain Pokémon are immune to being affected by Powder: those of the Grass type, those possessing the Overcoat Ability, or those holding Safety Goggles. However, if a Pokémon is already under Powder's effect and subsequently gains one of these properties, the effect will persist. Powder will fail if the target is already experiencing its effects.

When a Pokémon with the Magic Guard Ability, while affected by Powder, tries to use a Fire-type move, the move will be nullified, but the Pokémon will incur no damage. If a Pokémon under Powder's influence tries to use multiple Fire-type moves within a single turn (such as through the Dancer Ability), Powder's effect will trigger for each attempt. Using a move like Flame Wheel to thaw itself will not be hindered by Powder.

Should a Pokémon afflicted by Powder attempt to use Natural Gift with a Berry that would render the move Fire-type, the Pokémon will suffer damage from Powder, yet the Berry remains unconsumed. A Pokémon with Protean under Powder's effect using a Fire-type move will not undergo a type change.

If Powder affects a Pokémon that uses Fire Pledge after an ally's Water Pledge, resulting in the combination move becoming Water Pledge, Powder will not impede that move. In all other Fire Pledge scenarios—whether as the initial move in a combination, or when used as a Grass Pledge combination after an ally's Fire Pledge—Powder will prevent Fire Pledge from being executed just like any other Fire-type move.

Despite its connection to minor explosions, Powder's usage is neither prevented nor negated by the Damp Ability. Importantly, Powder will no longer inflict damage on the Pokémon during heavy rain. Powder can inhibit the use of a Fire-type Z-Move, though status Z-Moves will still activate their Z-Power effects. If a Pokémon affected by Powder attempts to use a Fire-type dance move (such as Fiery Dance), that particular move will not activate the Dancer Ability.

In the case of Shell Trap, a Pokémon affected by Powder will only take damage if Shell Trap would otherwise deal damage to its opponent, with the Powder damage replacing the Shell Trap damage. Otherwise, Shell Trap fails to execute, and the Pokémon takes no damage.

When enhanced by a Buginium Z, Powder transforms into Z-Powder, which boosts the user's Special Defense stat by two stages.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, Powder targets enemies within the same room, bestowing upon them the Powder status condition. A single Fire-type move used by a Pokémon suffering from this Powder status will fail, simultaneously inflicting damage on the Pokémon as the move is nullified. This status condition persists for 11 turns or concludes if the affected Pokémon uses a Fire-type move. As a "powder move," it holds no sway over Grass-type Pokémon.

Notably, Powder stands alone as the sole move transferable into Pokémon Sword and Shield that is unusable without an explicit generic description stating its inoperability.

Title: Geomancy (move)

ジオコントロール (Geo Control) is the Japanese name for Geomancy, a non-damaging Fairy-type maneuver first introduced in Generation VI. This technique serves as Xerneas's unique signature move. The Pokémon initiating the move dedicates the first turn to charging, then on the subsequent turn, its Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats each see an increase of two stages. Should the user be holding a Power Herb, this item permits Geomancy's execution in a single turn, after which the Power Herb is consumed.

If a Pokémon falls under the effect of Taunt during the charging phase, Geomancy will subsequently fail on the following turn. When enhanced by a Fairium Z crystal to become Z-Geomancy, the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed all receive a one-stage boost before Geomancy's charging turn begins. In the event a Pokémon is affected by Taunt, Z-Geomancy's initial stat-raising effect and its charging turn will proceed normally, yet the move itself will still fail after the charge concludes.

Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 up to 1.2.1, Geomancy was unavailable for selection in battle. However, starting with Version 1.3.0, it became fully usable and operated with the identical functionality as in preceding generations. The user also gains a temporary "Geomancy" status condition for one turn. On the next turn, the user and their allies receive boosts to their Special Attack, Special Defense, and Travel Speed. Unlike various other sync moves, this particular technique could not be upgraded to 6★ EX from its debut on February 11, 2021, until 700 days later, on January 12, 2023. This ability enhances the Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed of all friendly sync pairs by a number of stat ranks equivalent to one greater than Professor Sycamore & Xerneas's current move level, as detailed in an accompanying table.

In Super Smash Bros. for Wii U, Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS, and Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, Xerneas employs Geomancy to briefly transform fighters into golden versions of themselves, rendering them immune to knockback and more resilient to incoming attacks. These fighters are also enveloped in a rainbow aura that diminishes over time, signaling the impending end of the effect. Geomancy holds the unique distinction of being the sole status move that necessitates a charging turn. Furthermore, it is the only status move that also serves as the signature move for a game mascot. Aside from moves that were exclusive signature moves only in specific generations, it stands as the only signature move to function as a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO. When Xerneas utilizes Geomancy in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate, an audible sound effect reminiscent of a Super Mushroom or Fire Flower from Super Mario Bros. can be heard.

Title: Magnetic Flux (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Magnetic Flux (Japanese: じばそうさ, translated as Magnetic Field Manipulation) is an Electric-type support move that causes no direct damage. Its main effect is to elevate the Defense and Special Defense attributes of friendly Pokémon, including the user, possessing the Plus or Minus Ability, by one stage each. Should it be amplified by an Electrium Z, becoming Z-Magnetic Flux, the user receives an additional one-stage increase to its Special Defense, which accumulates with any previous boosts from the standard move. Magnetic Flux further provides a sharp boost to the Defense and Sp. Def of Pokémon that carry the Plus or Minus Ability, provided they are in the same party and physical location.

Title: Happy Hour (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Happy Hour (ハッピータイム Happy Time in Japanese) stands as a unique Normal-type move that deals no damage and is obtainable solely through special in-game events. Its primary effect is to double the prize money received following a battle, a bonus that applies even to earnings from moves like Pay Day and Make It Rain. This monetary benefit can be combined with either an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense, resulting in quadrupled prize money when Happy Hour and one of these items are active simultaneously. Further enhancing monetary gains, it also allows for concurrent use with Prize Money O-Powers. However, using Happy Hour multiple times within a single battle does not amplify its effect, as it notably does not stack with itself. When transformed into Z-Happy Hour by a Normalium Z, this move takes on an additional benefit: it comprehensively elevates the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats, each by a single stage. In Contest Spectaculars, Happy Hour serves as a foundational move for a combination, granting an additional three appeal points to subsequent uses of Bestow, Fling, or Present on the immediate following turn.

Title: Electric Terrain (move)

Known in Japan as エレキフィールド (Elec Field), Electric Terrain is an Electric-type status move that first appeared in Generation VI. This move held the TM90 designation in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and is currently identified as TM136 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Utilizing Electric Terrain generates a special environment, fundamentally altering the battlefield's backdrop and overriding any pre-existing terrains. This unique field persists for a duration of five turns before dissipating. Its effects apply to all grounded Pokémon, excluding those currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of certain moves.

For affected Pokémon, Electric-type moves receive a 50% power increase, irrespective of the target's own status within the terrain. Notably, this power enhancement also extends to moves that have been converted to Electric-type through abilities or moves such as Electrify. Affected Pokémon are safeguarded from falling asleep or being inflicted with Yawn's drowsy status. Should a Pokémon already be drowsy from Yawn, it will not succumb to sleep, and the drowsiness will be cured. When Secret Power is employed, it displays the animation of Thunder Shock and carries a 30% chance of inflicting paralysis. Furthermore, the move Camouflage will cause its user to transform into an Electric-type. Any natural objects present on the field vanish and become invulnerable to destruction during this terrain's presence.

A Pokémon possessing the Electric Surge Ability will automatically generate Electric Terrain upon entering battle. Pokémon equipped with the Surge Surfer Ability will find their Speed stat doubled while benefiting from Electric Terrain. Holding a Terrain Extender when Electric Terrain is initiated will extend its duration to eight turns, rather than the standard five. A Pokémon holding an Electric Seed will consume it within Electric Terrain, gaining a one-stage boost to its Defense stat; this occurs even if the Pokémon isn't otherwise susceptible to the terrain's typical effects. The use of Splintered Stormshards will dispel Electric Terrain.

When upgraded into Z-Electric Terrain with an Electrium Z, the user experiences a one-stage increase to their Speed stat. Under Z-Electric Terrain, the power of Electric-type moves used by affected Pokémon is boosted by 30%, a reduction from the regular 50% increase. It also causes Rising Voltage's base power to double from 70 to 140 if the move is directed at a target standing on Electric Terrain. Terrain Pulse's type becomes Electric and its base power increases from 50 to 100. A Pokémon with the Mimicry Ability will have its type changed to Electric. The Quark Drive Ability grants a 30% boost to a Pokémon's highest stat (or 50% if Speed is highest), even if that Pokémon isn't grounded and thus not fully affected by the terrain. Additionally, Pokémon possessing the Hadron Engine Ability receive an approximate 33% (specifically, a factor of 5461/4096) boost to their Special Attack stat, again regardless of whether they are grounded or not. Psyblade's power is increased by 50%, elevating its base power from 80 to 120. Electric Terrain is also automatically generated for five turns (or eight turns if a Terrain Extender is active) upon the execution of Max Lightning or when a Pokémon with the Hadron Engine Ability enters the arena.

Certain moves like Defog, G-Max Wind Rage, Steel Roller, and Ice Spinner will eliminate Electric Terrain, along with any other existing terrains, upon their use. It's worth noting that Steel Roller will fail if no terrains, including Electric Terrain, are currently active.

In dungeon settings, Electric Terrain alters the dungeon's status to Electric Terrain, granting a 50% power increase to Electric-type attacks and safeguarding Pokémon from sleep-inducing conditions. These dungeon effects apply exclusively to grounded Pokémon and cannot be present alongside other terrains. Within dungeons, Electric Terrain remains active for a total of ten turns. Prior to Version 2.21.0, released on May 26, 2022, its description simply stated: "Turns the field into Electric Terrain."

Title: Dazzling Gleam (move)

Known in Japan as マジカルシャイン (Magical Shine), Dazzling Gleam is a Fairy-type, damage-dealing attack first introduced in Generation VI. Its Technical Machine designation has varied: TM99 from its debut up to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, TM32 in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, and TM21 within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This move inflicts damage upon all adjacent opponents. Its base power has been decreased to 75. Additionally, it is noted to strike only one target.

Regarding its 6★ EX effect, prior to Version 2.18.5 (from March 15, 2022), the description read: "Additional Effects: The stat increase after a sync move is doubled once per battle." As a buddy move, all its effects, including any stat drops, apply to each target hit. This specific buddy move was formerly designated as B Dazzling Gleam before version 2.38.0.

In Pokémon UNITE, Dazzling Gleam serves as the inaugural move for both Alolan Ninetales and Wigglytuff. Alolan Ninetales acquires the move upon evolving from Alolan Vulpix at level 4, opting to upgrade Powder Snow into it instead of Avalanche. For Alolan Ninetales, the move manifests as a powerful, conical burst of blinding light, damaging and stunning hit enemies for 0.75 seconds. At level 11, both its damage and stun duration (to 1 second) are boosted. Owing to the Snow Warning ability, Dazzling Gleam also slings snow, assisting Alolan Ninetales in freezing opposing Pokémon. Wigglytuff gains access to Dazzling Gleam at level 6 by upgrading Pound into it instead of Double Slap. Wigglytuff's version emits a flash of fairy energy, first dealing a small amount of damage in a cone, followed by a larger burst of damage. From level 10 onward, enemies struck by the initial flash experience a slowdown.

In Omega Alpha Adventure 21, Ruby instructed Mega Diancie to use Dazzling Gleam, though the move's execution occurred off-panel. Across Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon, Dazzling Gleam is consistently portrayed as a move that makes contact.

Title: Celebrate (move)

This entry pertains to the move Celebrate. For details regarding the Pokémon Conquest Ability sharing this name, please refer to List of Abilities in Pokémon Conquest → Celebrate.

Known as おいわい (Celebration) in Japanese, Celebrate is a Normal-type move that does not inflict damage and is exclusive to special distributions, having been introduced in Generation VI. When employed in battle, Celebrate produces no direct mechanical outcome. Its animation involves the user temporarily disappearing, whereupon a present descends from above, then unfolds to reveal the Pokémon that initiated the move. Following this display, the battle log presents the message: Congratulations, <player>!

When boosted by a Normalium Z crystal, transforming it into Z-Celebrate, the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats each receive a one-stage increase. Celebrate also serves a role in Contest Spectacular combinations; if used, it grants an additional three appeal points to Bestow, Fling, and Present when any of these moves are performed on the subsequent turn. Notably, Celebrate stands as the sole event-exclusive move ever distributed for the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!.

Title: Hold Hands (move)

Known in Japanese as てをつなぐ Hold Hands, this Normal-type move deals no direct damage. Introduced in Generation VI, Hold Hands is exclusively available through special event distributions. While it provides no combat advantage, its use in a Double or Triple Battle prompts the user and its chosen ally to perform a brief, synchronized animation. The move fails if attempted in a Single Battle or a Horde Encounter, even when employed by one of the five opposing Pokémon. In a Battle Royal, though the user can target any opponent, Hold Hands will still fail. It disregards the target's activation of Protect, Detect, or Spiky Shield. However, execution will be unsuccessful if the target is shielded by Crafty Shield or is in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions such as Fly or Dig. When powered up into Z-Hold Hands by a Normalium Z, the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed all increase by one stage. As part of a Contest Spectacular combination, Hold Hands causes specific moves—Circle Throw, Seismic Toss, Sky Drop, Smack Down, Storm Throw, and Vital Throw—to grant an additional three appeal points if performed on the subsequent turn. Uniquely among event-exclusive moves, Hold Hands has been depicted outside the games, notably used by Yvette's Vivillon in the Pokémon Adventures chapter 'Xerneas Gives'.

Title: Nuzzle (move)

Originating in Generation VI, Nuzzle (Japanese: ほっぺすりすり Cheek Nuzzle) is an Electric-type attack capable of dealing damage. Upon a successful strike, Nuzzle not only inflicts harm but also guarantees paralysis for its target. In Contest Spectaculars, this move serves as a component in certain combinations: utilizing Charge in the preceding turn provides its user with an extra three appeal points. Conversely, Nuzzle can begin a combo, resulting in an additional three appeal points for Hex or Smelling Salts if either is employed immediately afterward. While the bulk of Pokémon learning Nuzzle are electric rodents (with Morpeko being the sole exception), its acquisition was restricted to them until the arrival of Pokémon Sword and Shield. Remarkably, Hatenna stands as the single non-Electric-type Pokémon able to master Nuzzle.

Title: Hold Back (move)

Debuting in Generation VI, Hold Back (Japanese: てかげん Go Easy) is an event-exclusive Normal-type technique capable of inflicting damage. Despite its damage-dealing nature, Hold Back possesses a special property: it prevents the target from fainting, leaving them instead with 1 Hit Point. Should the target already possess just 1 HP, this attack will connect and maintain their HP at that solitary point. Furthermore, when used against a substitute, Hold Back's damage output remains undiminished. This move's debut presentation took place during a Nintendo Direct episode on December 18, 2013.

Title: Infestation (move)

Infestation, known in Japanese as まとわりつく (Follow About), is a damage-dealing Bug-type attack that made its debut in Generation VI. It functioned as TM83 from Generation VI through to Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Upon activation, Infestation delivers initial damage and subsequently inflicts 1/8 of the target's maximum HP as residual damage per turn for a duration of four to five turns. It also ensnares the opposing Pokémon, preventing it from switching out or escaping. A message stating "<target> has been afflicted with an infestation by <user>!" will be displayed. The trapped status concludes if the user withdraws from battle. A Pokémon caught by Infestation can liberate itself by employing Rapid Spin. If a wild Pokémon uses Infestation on a player's Pokémon, an escape is possible only if the afflicted Pokémon possesses the Run Away Ability or is equipped with a Smoke Ball. However, these conditions do not enable the player to switch the Pokémon out. Conversely, an affected Pokémon holding a Shed Shell can switch out. When the user of Infestation is equipped with a Grip Claw, the trapping effect will invariably persist for 7 turns. Should the attacking Pokémon be holding a Binding Band, the end-of-turn damage inflicted will intensify from 1/8 of the target's max HP to 1/6.

In Pokémon GO, Infestation acts as a Fast Attack, having been available since February 16, 2017. In this game, it deals damage and can also impose an "Infestation" status condition on the enemy. This specific status causes the afflicted Pokémon to lose 3 HP at the conclusion of every 2 turns and prevents it from swapping positions with the team leader. This status condition persists for a duration of 6 turns. Notably, Infestation is the sole move in Generation VI games whose battle effect (specifically its between-turn component) still activates even when the battle effects option is turned off. Similar effects consistently played for binding moves in Generation V games; this peculiar oversight for Infestation's battle effect is perhaps linked to that Generation V characteristic, coupled with Infestation being the only newly introduced binding move in Generation VI.

Title: Oblivion Wing (move)

Known in Japanese as デスウイング (Death Wing), Oblivion Wing is a Flying-type attack that deals damage, first appearing in the sixth generation of Pokémon games. This powerful technique is exclusively wielded by the Legendary Pokémon Yveltal. Upon activation, Oblivion Wing not only strikes the target for damage but also restores the user's HP, specifically replenishing health equal to 75% of the damage dealt. In Triple Battles, it distinguishes itself by being able to target opponents not immediately adjacent to the user. Equipping the user with a Big Root enhances the HP recovery by 30%, leading to a total health restoration of 97.5% of the damage inflicted. Crucially, the Big Root item provides no increase to the move's damage output. Conversely, if the target Pokémon carries the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user will instead forfeit the HP it would have otherwise gained. Moreover, it becomes inaccessible while the user is under the effects of Heal Block. For Pokémon Sword and Shield players, Oblivion Wing was initially unselectable in battle across versions 1.0 to 1.2.1. Its functionality returned with Version 1.3.0, performing exactly as it did in preceding generations. It is additionally documented that Oblivion Wing inflicts damage, with ½ of the damage dealt being restored to the user. The in-game description for Oblivion Wing, prior to version 2.10.0 (which was effective June 28, 2021), stated: "Restores the user's HP based on the amount of damage dealt to the target. Each use restores up to half of the user's maximum HP." Later, the description was updated (before an unknown version post-2.10.0) to: "The more damage dealt to the target, the more this move restores the user's HP. Each use restores up to approximately 50% of the user's maximum HP." Boasting a base power of 80 and a 75% damage-to-HP recovery ratio, Oblivion Wing is recognized as the most efficient HP-draining move, effectively regaining health equal to 60 base power's worth of damage. It notably holds the unique distinction of being the sole Flying-type attack capable of draining an opponent's HP.

Title: Thousand Arrows (move)

Known in Japanese as サウザンアロー (Thousand Arrow), this Ground-type damage-dealing attack debuted in Generation VI. Its legitimate availability was delayed until Generation VII. Thousand Arrows is recognized as one of Zygarde's signature moves. This offensive technique inflicts direct damage.

Notably for a Ground-type attack, it has the distinct ability to strike typically ungrounded Pokémon, encompassing Flying-types, those with the Ability Levitate, Pokémon holding an Air Balloon, or targets under the effects of Magnet Rise or Telekinesis. When it connects with a Flying-type Pokémon that is not already grounded, it deals neutral damage, irrespective of its secondary typing. Furthermore, any active Magnet Rise or Telekinesis effects on the target are instantly removed.

Should Thousand Arrows hit a Flying-type Pokémon, one possessing the Levitate Ability, or a Pokémon affected by Magnet Rise or Telekinesis, it imposes a "grounded" status. This renders the Pokémon susceptible to Ground-type moves, terrain effects, and Arena Trap, with this condition lasting until the Pokémon is switched out.

However, this grounding effect does not activate if the attack connects with a target's substitute, if the Pokémon is in the semi-invulnerable phase of Sky Drop, is holding an Iron Ball, or is under the influence of Gravity or Ingrain.

Thousand Arrows can strike Pokémon even during the semi-invulnerable turns of Fly, Bounce, and Sky Drop. If it hits a Pokémon utilizing Fly or Bounce, that Pokémon is forced back to the ground, canceling their move. Conversely, a Pokémon currently using or under the influence of Sky Drop will not be knocked down. A Pokémon grounded by Thousand Arrows can still use these aforementioned aerial maneuvers on subsequent turns.

During Versions 1.0 to 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Thousand Arrows could not be selected in battle. However, starting with Version 1.3.0, it became fully usable and functions identically to its operation in the previous generation.

The move's mechanics are comparable to Rock Slide, manifesting as multiple projectiles that descend around the target. Nevertheless, a target only takes damage from a single strike, and additional hits from other projectiles do not inflict further damage.

Finally, Thousand Arrows does not compel a Flying-type Pokémon to remain vulnerable to any subsequent Ground-type moves aimed at it, which differs from its behavior in other game contexts.

Title: Thousand Waves (move)

Known in Japanese as サウザンウェーブ (Thousand Wave), Thousand Waves is an offensive Ground-type technique that deals damage, making its initial appearance in Generation VI. Despite its introduction, legitimate access to this move was not granted until Generation VII. It holds a distinction as one of Zygarde's signature abilities. When deployed, Thousand Waves not only inflicts harm but also impedes the afflicted Pokémon, preventing them from escaping or switching out of battle for as long as the user remains active. Ghost-type Pokémon, however, are immune to this trapping effect. Crucially, this trapping characteristic is an intrinsic part of the move and is not categorized as an additional effect. Initially, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, specifically from Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1, Thousand Waves could not be selected during combat. Its usability was reinstated starting with Version 1.3.0, at which point it functioned identically to its behavior in previous generations. The mechanism of Thousand Waves resembles that of attacks like Earthquake or Sludge Wave: the user closes in on a target before unleashing a damaging circular zone around it. Any alterations to status conditions triggered by this move extend to every target it successfully hits.

Title: Origin Pulse (move)

Origin Pulse (Japanese: こんげんのはどう Origin Pulse) is a damage-dealing Water-type attack introduced during Generation VI, first appearing in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This technique is uniquely associated with Kyogre. It strikes all adjacent opponents with damage. A Pokémon possessing the Mega Launcher Ability will see this move's power amplified by 50%. From Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, Origin Pulse remained unselectable during combat. Yet, as of Version 1.3.0, its use was permitted, restoring its prior functionality from earlier generations. This particular move is entirely absent from the game data of Pokémon X and Y. Consequently, a Pokémon with Origin Pulse cannot be traded from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire into Pokémon X and Y; similarly, if in Pokémon Bank, it cannot be withdrawn into Pokémon X and Y. For successful transfer into Pokémon X and Y, a Pokémon must first relinquish Origin Pulse. When unleashed by May (Champion) & Kyogre, this move is guaranteed to hit and invariably results in a critical hit, thanks to their passive skill Sure Hit | P-Moves & S-Moves Crit. The Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer showcases Origin Pulse. Notably, Origin Pulse stands as the only aura and pulse move that cannot be acquired by a Pokémon possessing the Mega Launcher Ability. Furthermore, among all aura or pulse moves present in games featuring Triple Battles, Origin Pulse is unique in its inability to strike non-adjacent Pokémon during such engagements.

Title: Precipice Blades (move)

Precipice Blades (Japanese: だんがいのつるぎ Precipice Blades) is Groudon's signature Ground-type attack, a damage-dealing maneuver that debuted in Generation VI's Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This move targets all adjacent opposing Pokémon.

Initially, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1, Precipice Blades could not be selected during battles. However, with the release of Version 1.3.0, it became fully usable, retaining its functionality from prior generations.

This move is absent from the game data of Pokémon X and Y. Consequently, Pokémon knowing Precipice Blades cannot be traded from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire to Pokémon X and Y. Similarly, if a Pokémon stored in Pokémon Bank possesses this move, it cannot be withdrawn into Pokémon X and Y. To facilitate transfer to Pokémon X and Y, the Pokémon must first forget Precipice Blades.

When executed by Brendan (Champion) & Groudon, Precipice Blades never misses and consistently lands critical hits, thanks to their passive skill, "Sure Hit | P-Moves & S-Moves Crit." Furthermore, this pair's sync move also invariably results in a critical hit, a benefit of the same passive skill.

In the anime episode "In the Palms of our Hands!", Mew, after Transforming into Groudon, prepared to unleash Precipice Blades but was interrupted before the attack could commence. The move was also featured in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer.

Among all Ground-type moves, Precipice Blades shares the highest base power with Headlong Rush, excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. When used as the basis for a Tectonic Rage, it achieves the highest base power among all Ground-type moves. Moreover, Precipice Blades stands alone as the sole Ground-type "Cool" move.

Despite its nomenclature and descriptive text, which allude to "blades of land," Precipice Blades is not categorized as a slicing move. Therefore, its power is not amplified by the Sharpness ability.

Title: Dragon Ascent (move)

The move known as Dragon Ascent (Japanese: ガリョウテンセイ, or Dragon Ascension) is a Flying-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing in Generation VI's Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. It serves as Rayquaza's exclusive signature technique. Upon use, Dragon Ascent deals damage but subsequently decreases the user's Defense and Special Defense stats by one stage apiece. From Versions 1.0 through 1.2.1 of Pokémon Sword and Shield, this move was unavailable for selection during battles. However, with the release of Version 1.3.0, it became fully functional, mirroring its behavior from the prior generation. Any Rayquaza knowing Dragon Ascent is capable of Mega Evolving. Crucially, a Rayquaza cannot undergo Mega Evolution if it holds a Z-Crystal or has learned Dragon Ascent solely via the move Mimic. Within Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Mega Evolution for any Rayquaza is locked until the specific Rayquaza encountered during the Delta Episode has been successfully captured. The move Dragon Ascent is entirely absent from the game data of Pokémon X and Y. Consequently, a Pokémon possessing Dragon Ascent cannot be traded from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire to Pokémon X and Y; similarly, if a Pokémon stored in Pokémon Bank has this move, it cannot be withdrawn into Pokémon X and Y. To enable such transfers or withdrawals, the Pokémon must first forget the move. Specific Move Tutors are capable of instructing a Rayquaza in Dragon Ascent. In Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon (SMUSUM), this tutor is identified as a man wearing a red shirt, found within the Pokémon Center of Seafolk Village. Regarding Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, even though the Rayquaza battled at Sky Pillar initially lacks Dragon Ascent, Zinnia will automatically impart the move (substituting Fly) to it the moment the player successfully captures it. When used, Dragon Ascent strikes foes situated within a 5x5 square area directly ahead of the user. Its secondary effect is to decrease the user's Defense and Sp. Defense by a single stage. Furthermore, Rayquaza can achieve Mega Evolution if it knows Dragon Ascent and possesses either an Awakening emera or an Empowerment Seed. For Steven (Anniversary 2021) & Rayquaza, their passive skill, Downside Up, alters Dragon Ascent's effect: it causes their Defense and Sp. Def to increase by one stat rank after use, rather than decreasing them. The power boost associated with Zinnia & Rayquaza is exclusively obtainable via the villain event "Team Aqua's Stormy Seas." Within Pokémon GO, Dragon Ascent functions as a Charged Attack, having been introduced on August 27, 2023. It is an essential prerequisite for Mega Evolving Rayquaza in this game. The literal translation of the move's Japanese name signifies "to paint a dragon and dot its eyes," metaphorically representing the final, crucial detail that imbues a creation with "the spark of life." This idiom draws its origins from a legend featuring Zhang Sengyou, a Chinese painter, who, by dotting the eyes of dragons on a mural, literally brought them to life, causing them to emerge from the temple wall and take flight. Following their completion, these dragons promptly ascended into the heavens. Notably, Dragon Ascent stands as the sole move in the English localization containing "dragon" in its name that is not of the Dragon type. Since Zinnia teaches Dragon Ascent to the Sky Pillar Rayquaza in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, specifically replacing Fly, it holds the distinction of being the only move capable of replacing an HM move in a title where that HM move is available. If a Mega Evolved Rayquaza performs this move, an extra animation sequence precedes the primary one, which itself undergoes a subtle alteration. Conversely, when any Pokémon other than Mega Rayquaza (including a standard, non-Mega-Evolved Rayquaza) executes Dragon Ascent, a cyan blue animation is displayed in place of the usual green one. Even when a Shiny Rayquaza utilizes the move in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the visual effect still depicts a normal-colored Rayquaza.

Title: Hyperspace Fury (move)

Known in Japanese as いじげんラッシュ (Interdimensional Rush), Hyperspace Fury is a Dark-type damaging attack that first appeared in Generation VI with the release of Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This attack serves as the exclusive signature move for Hoopa Unbound. After dealing damage, Hyperspace Fury reduces the user's Defense stat by a single stage. It circumvents standard accuracy calculations, guaranteeing a hit unless the opposing Pokémon is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. Furthermore, it strikes targets even when they are shielded by protective moves such as Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, King's Shield, Mat Block, or Baneful Bunker, nullifying their effects for the duration of that turn. Should it connect with a foe guarded by Mat Block, that specific field effect is cleared from the target's side. The move additionally eliminates the impact of Quick Guard, Wide Guard, and Crafty Shield from the opponent's side of the battlefield. A Pokémon's Substitute offers no protection against this attack.

Only Hoopa in its Unbound form (or a Pokémon that has taken on the form of Hoopa Unbound) is capable of successfully executing Hyperspace Fury. Any other Pokémon attempting to use this move, even through abilities like Assist or Sleep Talk, will find it fails, a restriction that also applies within Pokémon Contests. When another Pokémon tries to use it, the battle message reads "But <Pokémon> can't use the move!" while Hoopa Confined attempting the move results in the message "But Hoopa can't use it the way it is now!".

As of Version 3.0.0 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the move can no longer be learned or imitated via Sketch. This particular move is absent from the game data of Pokémon X and Y. Consequently, a Pokémon possessing Hyperspace Fury cannot be transferred from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire to Pokémon X and Y via trade, nor can a Pokémon in Pokémon Bank knowing this move be retrieved in Pokémon X and Y. For successful transfer to Pokémon X and Y, the Pokémon must first unlearn Hyperspace Fury.

Within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, should Hoopa Unbound know Hyperspace Fury and then revert to its Confined form, the move is automatically forgotten and superseded by Hyperspace Hole. Conversely, when Hoopa is in its Unbound state, Hyperspace Fury takes the place of Hyperspace Hole.

Diverging from its core series implementation, in some other titles, activating the move does not reduce the user's Defense. In these alternative settings, the attack also begins at its maximum power and with 13 PP, despite its internal base PP being coded as 5. Within Pokémon UNITE, specifically, Hyperspace Fury functions as Hoopa's primary move when it transforms into its Unbound state. The Pokémon then utilizes its numerous arms to unleash 7 distinct attacks within a specified zone, harming foes caught in the area of effect and rendering them immobile for 1 second.

Based on index number ordering, Hyperspace Fury holds the distinction of being the final Generation VI move. It boasts the highest base power among all Dark-type moves and all moves with guaranteed hits, when excluding Z-Moves and Max Moves. While Aura Wheel possesses greater base power, it is typically an Electric-type attack. A Black Hole Eclipse that derives from Hyperspace Fury shares the distinction with Malicious Moonsault for possessing the maximum base power among all Dark-type attacks. However, Power Trip can achieve a superior power level compared to both Hyperspace Fury and its corresponding Black Hole Eclipse, reaching a peak of 860 under optimal circumstances.

Title: Catastropika (move)

Hailing from Generation VII, Catastropika (Japanese: ひっさつのピカチュート Certain-kill Pikachoot) functions as a potent Electric-type Z-Move designed to inflict damage. This particular Z-Move is unique to Pikachu, serving as an enhanced iteration of its hallmark technique, Volt Tackle. Its sole function is to cause direct harm, lacking any additional effects. For Catastropika to be unleashed, the user—either Pikachu itself or another Pokémon having assumed Pikachu's form—must fulfill specific criteria: possessing the move Volt Tackle, being equipped with a Pikanium Z crystal, and its Trainer donning a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. However, this mighty attack is strictly reserved for standard Pikachu; special variants, such as Pikachu wearing a cap, are unable to execute Catastropika, irrespective of meeting all other prerequisites. Remarkably, Catastropika stands out with 10,000,000 Volt Thunderbolt as the only species-exclusive Z-Move that features an individualized animation, setting it apart from other Z-Moves of the same type. Intriguingly, both of these unique moves are coincidentally exclusive to Pikachu. Furthermore, among all Electric-type attacks, Catastropika boasts an unparalleled base power.

Title: Shore Up (move)

Appearing in Generation VII, Shore Up (すなあつめ, or Sand Gather, in Japanese) is a Ground-type technique that inflicts no direct damage. This unique ability is exclusive to the Pokémon Sandygast and Palossand. Its effect involves replenishing the user's health points, with the amount contingent on the prevailing battlefield weather. If no sandstorm is active, it recuperates one-half (½) of the user's maximum HP; however, within a sandstorm, this increases to two-thirds (⅔) of total HP, always rounded down. When enhanced into Z-Shore Up via a Groundium Z, it additionally nullifies any reductions to the user's stats, returning them to their initial state. From Version 1.0 up to 1.1.1 of the Pokémon Sword and Shield titles, players were unable to choose Shore Up during combat. As of Version 1.2.0 onwards, it became fully accessible, operating identically to its behavior in earlier generations.

Title: First Impression (move)

Known in Japan as であいがしら (Head-on Collision), First Impression is a damage-dealing Bug-type move that debuted in Generation VII. Until Generation VIII, it was Golisopod's exclusive signature move, though other Pokémon could still acquire it through breeding. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, it is identified as TM142. This move inflicts direct damage and possesses a priority value of +2, meaning it is consistently executed before any moves with a lower priority.

However, First Impression invariably fails if attempted after the user's inaugural turn in battle, or if activated via the move Instruct. Furthermore, a Pokémon cannot select First Impression if it has already used any other move since being sent into combat. Outside of battle, its use is unrestricted, enabling activities such as shattering Mega Crystals. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, First Impression otherwise behaves similarly to other priority moves like Extreme Speed or Quick Attack; its user briefly becomes intangible while dashing to make contact with the target.
