Should First Impression be utilized to launch a surprise attack on a Trainer within a battle zone, this instance does not count as an in-battle usage, thereby permitting its immediate reuse without cooldown once the formal battle begins. When employed as a Plus Move, First Impression always lands as a critical hit. In co-operative battles, each switch into the active party is considered a new entry into battle. Importantly, Sync moves are not counted among the actions that would cause First Impression to fail.

First Impression commands the highest base power among all priority moves. The move's inspiration may derive from insect fighting, an activity involving two bugs, most commonly beetles, engaging in combat within a small, ringed arena. These contests typically begin with the insects colliding headfirst. This concept could explain First Impression's Bug typing, its Japanese name (であいがしら Head-on Collision), its elevated priority status, and its strict limitation to the user's first turn on the field. Sirfetch'd's ability to learn this move might also serve as a reference to jousting.

Title: Baneful Bunker (move)

Originating in Generation VII, Baneful Bunker (Japanese: トーチカ Pillbox) is a Poison-type move that causes no direct damage. Toxapex exclusively employs this move as its signature technique. When activated, Baneful Bunker grants its user immunity to all effects from moves specifically targeting them throughout that turn, including damage. This safeguard does not extend to end-of-turn damage, such as that inflicted by weather conditions or persistent status ailments. Should Baneful Bunker successfully intercept a contact move, the initiating Pokémon becomes poisoned, assuming it lacks immunity to the status. Furthermore, attacks that are blocked do not trigger abilities or items dependent on contact, such as Rocky Helmet or Rough Skin. The move will fail if the user acts last during the turn. The probability of Baneful Bunker's success diminishes with each consecutive successful use of Endure, other self-protecting moves, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. This reduction involves dividing the success rate by 3 for each subsequent attempt.

Despite its protective nature, certain attacks can still inflict damage through Baneful Bunker. The majority of offensive Z-Moves and Max Moves will strike for only 25% of their usual power; however, G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow are notable exceptions, dealing their full damage. Attacks specifically designed to pierce protective barriers, such as Feint, will hit at full strength and simultaneously negate Baneful Bunker's protection. With the Unseen Fist Ability, contact-based attacks are able to circumvent Baneful Bunker entirely. A separate listing details status moves that remain unimpeded by Baneful Bunker. Broadly speaking, Baneful Bunker offers no defense against self-targeting moves, moves affecting the user and all allies, moves targeting all Pokémon on the field, or any entry hazard deployments. As an elevated priority move, Baneful Bunker typically executes ahead of the majority of attacks within a given turn. When augmented by a Poisonium Z crystal to become Z-Baneful Bunker, the user gains a one-stage boost to its Defense stat.

Title: Spirit Shackle (move)

Debuting in Generation VII, Spirit Shackle (known as かげぬい, or Shadow Stitching, in Japanese) functions as a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage. This particular move stands as Decidueye's exclusive signature technique. When Decidueye is equipped with Decidium Z, Spirit Shackle undergoes transformation into the distinctive Z-Move, Sinister Arrow Raid. Beyond its damaging capabilities, Spirit Shackle imposes a restriction on the afflicted opponent, barring them from escaping or swapping with another Pokémon for as long as the move's user stays active in combat. However, certain abilities like Wimp Out and Emergency Exit, as well as items such as the Red Card, Shed Shell, and Eject Button, possess the capacity to circumvent this binding restriction.

Shifting to Pokémon UNITE, Spirit Shackle represents Decidueye's initial designated attack. Decidueye acquires this move upon evolving from Dartrix, choosing to enhance Leafage into Spirit Shackle rather than Razor Leaf. The user initiates a power-charging sequence prior to launching a substantial arrow in a specified trajectory. Sustaining the charge enhances the damage output, featuring 6 distinct breakpoints for increased potency; however, this action simultaneously reduces the user's movement speed by 25%. Successfully landing this attack after a full power charge possesses the capability to immobilize foes by "stitching" them to the ground. Foes caught by this maximal charge effect suffer an 80% reduction in movement speed for a brief duration while within the area of effect, and incur additional damage should they depart from it. Up to 3 applications of this ability can be stored for later deployment. Upon reaching level 11, Spirit Shackle gains an enhancement, inflicting 15% greater damage against adversaries whose HP has fallen below 50%.

Pertaining to Pokémon GO, Spirit Shackle functions as a Charged Attack, which first became accessible on January 6, 2024. The Japanese designation, kagenui ("shadow stitching"), draws its inspiration from an ancient ninja legend describing a method to immobilize an opponent by piercing their shadow with a kunai or shuriken.

Title: Darkest Lariat (move)

Darkest Lariat, known in Japan as ＤＤラリアット (DD Lariat), is a damage-dealing Dark-type attack that debuted in Generation VII. It serves as Incineroar's signature move in both Generation VII and Generation IX. The move was also obtainable as TR93 in Pokémon Sword and Shield. When Incineroar holds Incinium Z, Darkest Lariat can be transformed into the powerful special Z-Move, Malicious Moonsault.

This attack uniquely deals damage by completely disregarding any modifications to the target's Defense and evasion stat stages. However, its stat-ignoring effect does not extend to changes that operate outside of stat stages, such as the protective effects of Reflect, certain Abilities like Sand Veil, or held items like Eviolite. Furthermore, it does not bypass alterations to the user's own Attack power or accuracy. A previous in-game description, valid until an unknown version past 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), simply stated: "Ignores the target's raised stats when attacking with this move."

In the context of Super Smash Bros., Darkest Lariat is Incineroar's neutral special, performed by the Pokémon swinging its arms to strike opponents. For Pokémon GO players, Darkest Lariat became available as a Charged Attack starting March 16, 2024. This particular move is exclusively learned by Pokémon that have reached their final evolutionary stage. Among moves with this specific learning distinction, Darkest Lariat boasted the highest availability exclusively within Generation VIII. In all other generations, however, its availability ranks among the lowest, tying with numerous other moves, primarily due to its status as Incineroar's signature technique.

Title: Sparkling Aria (move)

This Water-type, damage-dealing attack, known in Japanese as うたかたのアリア (Bubble Aria), first appeared in Generation VII. Before Generation VIII, it served as Primarina's exclusive signature move. Primarina can amplify it into the special Z-Move, Oceanic Operetta, when holding the Primarium Z crystal.

In combat, Sparkling Aria strikes all Pokémon positioned adjacently, including the user's allies. It possesses a unique secondary effect: any targeted Pokémon suffering from a burn will have that status condition removed. However, this healing only applies if the move successfully impacts the burned Pokémon. Categorized as a sound-based attack, Sparkling Aria bypasses Substitutes, allowing it to affect Pokémon hiding behind them. Typically, the Ability Shield Dust prevents the burn-curing effect; yet, this restriction is overridden when Sparkling Aria targets multiple Pokémon simultaneously in scenarios like Double Battles or Battle Royals.

In Pokémon GO, Sparkling Aria functions as a Charged Attack, made available starting August 31, 2024. Although Ida's Primarina was confirmed to possess Sparkling Aria in the episode 'Evolving Research!' via its use of Oceanic Operetta, the move itself has never been depicted directly. Notably, Sparkling Aria stands alone as the sole sound-based move inherently classified as Water-type.

Title: Ice Hammer (move)

Known in Japanese as アイスハンマー (Ice Hammer), this offensive Ice-type technique first appeared in Generation VII. Until Generation IX, Crabominable exclusively learned this attack as its signature move. While dealing damage, Ice Hammer simultaneously reduces the user's Speed stat by a single stage. However, when employed by Hau & Crabominable, their unique passive skill, Speed ↓ Flip, causes the move to *increase* their Speed by one stat rank, reversing the usual effect. Before Version 2.10.0, released on June 28, 2021, its in-game description simply stated: "Lowers the user's Speed." Its description text is identical to that of Hammer Arm. Visually, in Generation VII, its animation bore a resemblance to Hammer Arm's, distinguishing itself with a blue arm rather than an orange one. By Generation IX, the move's visual execution shifted; it now mimics Dragon Hammer and Gigaton Hammer, provided it's not Tinkaton using the latter, featuring a blue hammer instead of purple or silver, respectively.

Title: Floral Healing (move)

Making its debut in Generation VII, Floral Healing (known in Japanese as フラワーヒール Flower Heal) is a non-damaging Fairy-type move. This technique is exclusive to Comfey, serving as its signature maneuver. Floral Healing typically provides recovery equal to half (½) of the target's maximum HP. Should Grassy Terrain be active, however, the move instead restores two-thirds (⅔) of the target's maximum HP. Floral Healing is guaranteed to connect, disregarding standard accuracy calculations, with the sole exception being when the target is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves such as Dig or Fly. Furthermore, it holds no effect against a Pokémon shielded by a substitute. When amplified by a Fairium Z into Z-Floral Healing, the user's lowered stats are all reset, and the target receives healing irrespective of any Heal Block status. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, from initial release versions 1.0 up to 1.1.1, Floral Healing was unselectable during combat. Its availability and functionality, consistent with prior generations, were reinstated starting with Version 1.2.0. Similarly, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the move could not be chosen in battle across versions 1.0 through 2.0.2. It became selectable and regained its prior generation's mechanics with the release of Version 3.0.0.

Title: High Horsepower (move)

Known in Japanese as １０まんばりき (100,000 Horsepower), High Horsepower debuted in Generation VII as a damage-dealing Ground-type attack. It functioned as TR94 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently serves as TM186 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move's primary effect is to inflict damage, with no secondary consequences. For Pokémon GO players, High Horsepower is a Charged Attack that became available starting November 12, 2022. In Pokémon UNITE, High Horsepower functions as Mamoswine's second offensive option. Piloswine obtains it by reaching level 7 and upgrading Tackle into it rather than Earthquake. The user of the move executes an unrestrained charge in a chosen direction, harming and shoving any adversaries it contacts. Subsequently, the user stomps its feet, damaging foes within the area of effect and briefly incapacitating them. Foes already frozen sustain amplified damage from this stomp. At level 13, the swiftness of the initial rush and the duration enemies are unable to act are both improved.

Title: Strength Sap (move)

Debuting in Generation VII, Strength Sap (Japanese: ちからをすいとる Strength Sap) is a Grass-type technique that deals no direct damage. Before Generation VIII, this move was exclusive to Morelull and Shiinotic; however, other Pokémon could acquire it through the breeding mechanic. Its primary effect simultaneously decreases the opponent's Attack stat by a single stage and recuperates the user's HP by a value equivalent to the target's current effective Attack stat immediately prior to the move's execution (accounting for all existing stat modifiers). The move will not succeed if the target has already reached the minimum of -6 Attack stat stages. Nevertheless, Strength Sap will proceed successfully, and still restore HP, even if the target possesses +6 Attack stat stages alongside the Ability Contrary, or if its Attack stat cannot be reduced through other means (for example, being shielded by Mist or having the Ability Hyper Cutter). Furthermore, it won't fail if the user is already at maximum HP; in such an instance, the target's Attack will still be diminished. A user equipped with a Big Root will experience a 30% increase in the amount of HP recovered by Strength Sap. Should the opponent possess the Ability Liquid Ooze, the user will unfortunately suffer a loss of HP equal to the amount it would have otherwise restored. When amplified by a Grassium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Strength Sap, the user's Defense stat will be boosted by one stage.

Title: Solar Blade (move)

A Grass-type offensive technique introduced in Generation VII, Solar Blade (Japanese: ソーラーブレード Solar Blade) inflicts damage upon its target. Before Generation VIII, it served as the exclusive signature move for Lurantis. This attack typically requires two turns: the first is spent absorbing sunlight, and the second unleashes that stored energy as an assault. Should harsh sunlight be active, Solar Blade's charging phase is bypassed, allowing it to execute instantly. Conversely, its power will be halved if employed during rain, hail, or a sandstorm. A Pokémon equipped with a Power Herb will expend the item to perform Solar Blade in a single turn; however, if harsh sunlight is present, the Power Herb will not be consumed. Solar Blade is notable for being the sole move possessing a base power of 125. Its operational similarities, including its two-turn nature and the ability to forgo charging under harsh sunlight, position Solar Blade as the physical counterpart to the special move Solar Beam.

Title: Leafage (move)

The Grass-type move known as Leafage (Japanese: このは, or Foliage) made its debut in Generation VII, serving as a damage-inflicting attack. Its sole function is to deal damage, as it possesses no secondary effects.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Leafage is the initial move for both Decidueye and Eldegoss. Rowlet, Decidueye's pre-evolution, commences with this ability. When Rowlet uses Leafage, it hurls leaves forward in a designated direction, striking foes within a conical area for damage (each foe is hit a maximum of one time). Following its activation, the user's Attack stat receives a 10% boost (which also impacts Leafage itself), and their basic attack speed accelerates by 25% for a brief period. At level 7, upon Dartrix's evolution, Leafage is superseded by a choice between Razor Leaf or Spirit Shackle.

Gossifleur, Eldegoss's pre-evolution, also begins with Leafage. For Eldegoss, the Pokémon launches a cluster of leaves that detonates upon contact with the initial adversary, causing damage and inflicting a slow effect across an area. After evolving at level 4, this particular move gives way to either Pollen Puff or Leaf Tornado.

As a Fast Attack in Pokémon GO, Leafage has been accessible to players since June 1, 2023.

In the game Pokémon Pokopia, Ditto gains the ability to employ Leafage after acquiring it from a Bulbasaur encountered within the Withered Wasteland. This enables Ditto to cultivate tall grass on both dirt and soil surfaces. When Ditto consumes salads, Leafage temporarily becomes more potent, allowing it to cultivate tall grass across expansive areas, sprout vines on walls, generate moss on rigid surfaces, and produce duckweed upon water.

During The Delivery of Rotom and the Girl, Professor Kukui instructed his Rowlet to perform Leafage; however, its execution occurred outside of view. Interestingly, in Pokémon Pokopia, Bulbasaur is able to utilize Leafage and impart it to Ditto, despite never officially being able to learn the move itself.

Title: Spotlight (move)

This entry details the move Spotlight. For information on the Generation IV Accessory that shares this name, please refer to Accessory → Misc. Spotlight (Japanese: スポットライト Spotlight) is a non-damaging Normal-type move that debuted in Generation VII. When used, Spotlight causes its target to become the focal point of battle, redirecting all attacks from the target's foes towards it, rather than their initial intended recipients. This powerful redirection effect takes priority over all other similar mechanics, whether originating from moves such as Follow Me and Rage Powder, Abilities including Storm Drain and Lightning Rod, or the move Ally Switch. The benefits bestowed by Spotlight cannot be conveyed using Baton Pass. If the user transforms it into Z-Spotlight with a Normalium Z, their Special Defense stat will increase by one stage. Essentially, Spotlight functions by turning the chosen target into a decoy. Its mechanical effect is comparable to that of Substitute in titles released before Gates to Infinity. Curiously, the German title for the move Follow Me is also Spotlight, adopted as a loanword from English. However, this specific move, Spotlight, is rendered as Rampenlicht in German, which is a direct translation of the term. Among all moves introduced in Generation VII, Spotlight holds the distinction of being the sole one (excluding signature moves and Z-Moves) that is not available for use in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Toxic Thread (move)

Known in Japan as どくのいと (Toxic Thread), this non-damaging Poison-type move made its debut in Generation VII. It is the signature technique of Spinarak and Ariados. When used, Toxic Thread inflicts poison upon the target and simultaneously reduces its Speed stat (or Travel Speed) by a single stage. These two effects operate independently; the target's Speed will still decrease even if it cannot be poisoned (such as in the case of a Steel-type Pokémon or one already bearing a status condition). Conversely, the target will still be poisoned even if its Speed cannot be lowered (due to abilities like Clear Body or if the stat has already reached its minimum of -6 stages). If powered up by a Poisonium Z, transforming into Z-Toxic Thread, the user's own Speed stat receives a one-stage increase. Excluding Z-Moves, Toxic Thread holds the distinction of being the sole Poison-type move unusable in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Laser Focus (move)

Known in Japan as とぎすます (Hone), Laser Focus is a Normal-type technique that doesn't inflict damage, having debuted in Generation VII. This maneuver guarantees that the user's subsequent attack will land as a critical hit, with the effect lasting until the end of their next turn. However, this critical hit guarantee is circumvented if the target possesses the abilities Battle Armor or Shell Armor, or is shielded by Lucky Chant. The beneficial status conferred by Laser Focus can be copied by using Psych Up or Transform. When empowered by a Normalium Z Crystal, transforming it into Z-Laser Focus, the user's Attack stat experiences a one-stage increase. Additionally, the user acquires the 'Laser Focus' status condition for a single turn, ensuring their immediate next move is a critical hit.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Laser Focus functions as Duraludon's primary ability. It offers a 20% reduction in incoming damage for a brief period of 0.5 seconds. Following this defensive phase, the user's standard attacks deal augmented damage to any enemies struck. This buff concludes either after 8 seconds have passed or once 3 basic attacks have been successfully performed. Upon Duraludon reaching level 5, this particular move is replaced by the player's choice of either Flash Cannon or Dragon Pulse.

Title: Throat Chop (move)

Throat Chop, known in Japanese as じごくづき (Hell Thrust), is a Dark-type offensive technique first introduced in Generation VII. In the games Pokémon Sword and Shield, it was obtainable as TR95, and it is currently listed as TM221 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This attack inflicts damage upon its target and, for a period of two turns, prevents the affected Pokémon from executing any sound-based moves. Similar to Psychic Noise, repeated applications of Throat Chop will still deal damage but will not extend the duration of this suppressive effect. If the Pokémon using Throat Chop is faster than a target that was poised to use a sound-based move, the target will find itself unable to perform that move, a dynamic comparable to a faster Taunt user preventing a non-attacking move. Should the target be caught in an uproar, the uproar state will cease prematurely. Neither the initiation nor the conclusion of Throat Chop's effect on a Pokémon triggers a battle message display. An affected Pokémon that has no other usable moves besides sound-based ones, or that is locked into a sound-based move by a Choice item or Encore, will instead resort to using Struggle. Throat Chop's execution involves both dealing damage and applying the Throat Chop status condition to the target. This specific status condition prohibits the afflicted Pokémon from utilizing sound-based moves. The Japanese designation for this move, じごくづき Jigokuzuki (Hell Thrust), is a direct reference to a signature technique developed by the renowned professional wrestler, Abdullah the Butcher.

Title: Pollen Puff (move)

Originating in Generation VII, Pollen Puff (Japanese: かふんだんご Pollen Ball) is a Bug-type attack capable of inflicting damage. Prior to Generation VIII, Ribombee held this technique as its unique signature move. It was previously available as TR96 in Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently functions as TM131 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

The impact of Pollen Puff shifts depending on its chosen target. When directed at an opposing Pokémon, it causes offensive damage. Conversely, if aimed at a friendly ally, it has the potential to restore up to 50% of that target's maximum HP.

Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof ability are completely unaffected by both the damaging and restorative properties of Pollen Puff. A Pokémon possessing the Telepathy ability will not be healed by an ally's Pollen Puff. A Pokémon afflicted by Heal Block cannot target allies with Pollen Puff (though opponents can still be targeted); furthermore, if a Pokémon attempts to use Pollen Puff on a friendly target while under the effect of Heal Block, the move will fail. Similarly, a Pokémon suffering from Heal Block cannot be healed by Pollen Puff from another Pokémon.

In the separate environment of Pokémon UNITE, Pollen Puff serves as Eldegoss's initial combat technique. It becomes accessible after evolving from Gossifleur at level 4, specifically by upgrading Leafage instead of Leaf Tornado. The user launches a pollen puff that adheres to any Pokémon it makes contact with. After a short delay, this puff detonates, harming nearby enemies and regenerating health for surrounding allies. Upon reaching level 11, if the puff is attached to an enemy, it will subsequently inflict damage incrementally over time. If attached to an ally, it will grant a 20% reduction in incoming damage for two seconds.

Title: Psychic Terrain (move)

Debuting in Generation VII, Psychic Terrain (known as サイコフィールド Psycho Field in Japanese) is a Psychic-type move that causes no direct damage. It held the TM91 slot in Pokémon Sword and Shield and currently functions as TM138 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Upon activation, Psychic Terrain establishes a special field condition across the battle area, altering the visual background and overriding any existing terrain. This effect persists for five turns before dissipating.

The following effects manifest for Pokémon that are grounded and not currently within a move's semi-invulnerable phase: Psychic-type moves executed by affected Pokémon receive a 50% power increase, irrespective of whether the move's target is also under the terrain's influence. These Pokémon gain immunity to opposing moves that possess increased priority, such as those enhanced by Abilities like Prankster, Gale Wings, or Triage. However, opponents' moves targeting all Pokémon on the field—excluding Perish Song, Flower Shield, and Rototiller—circumvent Psychic Terrain's priority-blocking effect, even if such moves possess increased priority.

Under Psychic Terrain, Secret Power adopts the animation of Confusion and has a chance to reduce its target's Speed stat. Meanwhile, Camouflage will transform the user into a Psychic-type. The Ability Psychic Surge automatically initiates Psychic Terrain upon its holder's entry into battle. Furthermore, Genesis Supernova, after inflicting damage, generates Psychic Terrain on the battlefield.

A Pokémon holding a Psychic Seed will consume the item and have its Special Defense stat raised by one stage when Psychic Terrain is active, even if the Pokémon itself isn't otherwise under the terrain's influence. Should a Pokémon be holding a Terrain Extender upon the deployment of Psychic Terrain, the effect's duration extends to 8 turns instead of the usual 5.

When Psychic Terrain is enhanced by a Psychium Z into Z-Psychic Terrain, the user's Special Attack stat increases by one stage. Under these conditions, the power of Psychic-type moves used by affected Pokémon is boosted by 30%, a change from the standard 50%. Expanding Force's power is amplified by 1.5 times, and in a Double Battle scenario, it will target both opposing Pokémon. Terrain Pulse's type transforms to Psychic, and its base power doubles from 50 to 100. Additionally, a Pokémon with the Ability Mimicry will become Psychic-type.

Max Mindstorm's activation automatically brings Psychic Terrain into existence. Finally, Defog, G-Max Wind Rage, Steel Roller, and Ice Spinner all clear Psychic Terrain, as well as any other active terrain, upon their execution. Notably, Steel Roller will fail if no terrain, including Psychic Terrain, is currently present on the field.

Title: Power Trip (move)

Making its debut in Generation VII, the Dark-type attack known as Power Trip (Japanese: つけあがる Be Audacious) inflicts damage upon its target. Before Generation VIII, Krookodile exclusively possessed this attack as its signature move; however, other Pokémon were still capable of inheriting it through breeding. Its damage output is initially set at a base power of 20, which then increases by 20 for every raised stat stage the user possesses. Stats decreased below their normal level hold no bearing on this move's base power. The highest achievable base power for this attack reaches 860. This peak power is attained when the user has elevated all seven of its stats—Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, Speed, evasion, and accuracy—to their maximum of six stages. Before Version 2.20.0, the in-game description stated: "The more the user's stats are raised, the greater the power of this move." Originally, this move featured a power multiplier beginning at ×1; for every single increase in the user's stats (which encompassed critical-hit rate), this multiplier would rise by one. The multiplier would reach its upper limit of ×12 if the user's stats were boosted by a total of eleven or more ranks.

Title: Burn Up (move)

Burn Up (known in Japanese as もえつきる, or Burn Out) is an offensive Fire-elemental attack first seen in Generation VII. When a Pokémon of the Fire classification utilizes Burn Up, it first unfreezes itself if currently afflicted by the frozen status condition. Subsequently, it deals damage to the opposing Pokémon and divests its own user of its Fire typing. This ability is unsuccessful if the activating Pokémon is not of the Fire category. Consequently, after an initial successful application, the move consistently fails unless the Pokémon recovers its Fire type (via an effect like switching out, or through type-altering abilities such as Protean or Reflect Type). Crucially, Burn Up is incapable of unfreezing the user if the maneuver would otherwise be unsuccessful because the user lacks the Fire classification. Should the Pokémon employing Burn Up possess solely the Fire type, it transitions into a typeless state. In line with other type-altering mechanisms, the Pokémon's elemental assignment resets to its original types upon being withdrawn from battle. Creatures possessing the Protean or Libero abilities cannot execute Burn Up effectively unless they are already of the Fire type; otherwise, the move will be ineffective, and the Protean or Libero ability will not be triggered. Conversely, a Pokémon that has undergone Terastallization (whether transforming into a Fire or Stellar Tera type) retains its Fire classification. Consequently, a Terastallized Burn Up can be deployed repeatedly without the necessity of withdrawing the Pokémon. Since no Pokémon naturally possesses the capacity to learn Burn Up, its utilization is exclusively possible via the Metronome move. Within the titles Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, an existing glitch causes the game to cease functioning if a player attempts to access the summary screen of a Pokémon rendered typeless by Burn Up.

Title: Speed Swap (move)

This non-damaging Psychic-type maneuver, known in Japan as スピードスワップ (Speed Swap), first appeared in Generation VII. It held the TM62 designation throughout Pokémon Sword and Shield. Come Generation IX, it transitioned to become Rabsca's unique signature move. The fundamental effect of Speed Swap is to directly trade the base Speed statistics between the user and its intended recipient. Crucially, however, it leaves untouched any active in-battle speed adjustments, like those stemming from Abilities or current stat stages. Its execution is guaranteed, as it inherently bypasses accuracy checks, unless the chosen target is sheltered by the semi-invulnerable phase of a move like Dig or Fly. When augmented by a Psychium Z to become Z-Speed Swap, the user's Speed stat receives a boost of one stage.

Title: Smart Strike (move)

Smart Strike (Japanese: スマートホーン Smart Horn), a Steel-type move dealing damage, was introduced in Generation VII. It served as TM67 in Pokémon Sun and Moon, and Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. This move inflicts damage and possesses perfect accuracy, always hitting unless the target is in a semi-invulnerable state from moves such as Dig or Fly. During Generation VII, Togetic was able to learn Smart Strike via TM, but its evolution, Togekiss, surprisingly could not. This learning disparity was resolved in Generation VIII.

Title: Purify (move)

This entry pertains to the move Purify. For details on the process of transforming a Shadow Pokémon back to its normal state, refer to Purification.

Purify (Japanese: じょうか Purify) is a Generation VII addition, a non-damaging Poison-type technique. It stands as the signature move of Pyukumuku. Should the target Pokémon be under a non-volatile status condition, Purify will alleviate this affliction. Following the cure, the user's HP is restored by up to 50% of its maximum HP. The move yields no effect if the target is not suffering from a non-volatile status condition; this holds true even for Pokémon possessing the Comatose Ability. When empowered by a Poisonium Z to become Z-Purify, the user experiences a one-stage boost to its Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats.

Title: Revelation Dance (move)

Originating in Generation VII, Revelation Dance (Japanese: めざめるダンス, known as Awakening Dance) is a damaging attack of the Normal type. It stands as Oricorio's exclusive signature technique. Beyond dealing damage, this move possesses no additional effects. Ordinarily, Revelation Dance's elemental type corresponds precisely to the user's principal type. Should a Pokémon lose its primary Fire type through Burn Up, or its primary Electric type from Double Shock, Revelation Dance will instead assume the user's secondary type, if one exists. Absent a secondary type (and if no type was added by Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat), the move will become typeless, consequently failing to benefit from STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus). In a unique interaction, if a Pokémon has forfeited its primary Flying type earlier in the turn by utilizing Roost, and subsequently employs Revelation Dance via the Dancer Ability, the move's type will reflect its present elemental status: either its secondary type (if present) or, failing that, it defaults to Normal-type (ignoring any types potentially granted by Forest's Curse or Trick-or-Treat). The move is immune to the effects of type-changing Abilities such as Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, Aerilate, and Galvanize. Nevertheless, it remains susceptible to Ion Deluge (provided the move would naturally be Normal-type) and Electrify. A typeless Revelation Dance will not trigger the Color Change Ability and is entirely nullified by Wonder Guard. Furthermore, Conversion 2 will invariably fail if the target's most recent attack was a typeless Revelation Dance. When used by a Pokémon with Protean, that Pokémon will relinquish its secondary type, if it possesses one. Irrespective of the user's actual types, for the purpose of Z-Move mechanics, Revelation Dance is consistently regarded as a Normal-type move. It can be transformed into Breakneck Blitz when a Normalium Z is held. Upon Terastallization, Revelation Dance adopts the user's Tera Type, with the sole exception being if the Tera Type is Stellar. Notably, Revelation Dance shares with Judgment the distinction of being the only two battle mechanics where the specific order of a Pokémon's types influences their effect (excluding a known glitch related to Present in Gold and Silver versions).

Title: Core Enforcer (move)

Known in Japan as コアパニッシャー (Core Punisher), Core Enforcer is a Dragon-type attack capable of inflicting damage, having been introduced in the seventh generation of Pokémon games. It stands as one of Zygarde's distinct signature techniques. When Zygarde in its Complete Forme undergoes Mega Evolution, this move transforms into the unique special move Nihil Light, retaining this form until Zygarde reverts from Mega Evolution to its Complete Forme. Fundamentally, Core Enforcer inflicts damage upon its target. Beyond raw damage, this attack possesses a secondary effect: it suppresses the target's Ability for the duration of its presence in battle, provided the target has either acted already or had a Bag item applied to it by its Trainer within the same turn. However, this suppression effect fails if Core Enforcer misses, if the target is immune to or shielded from the move, or if the target possesses one of several specific Abilities, including Multitype, Stance Change, Schooling, Comatose, Shields Down, Disguise, RKS System, Battle Bond, Power Construct, or As One. The visual execution of Core Enforcer varies depending on the user: when unleashed by any Pokémon apart from Complete Forme Zygarde, it manifests as a destructive beam directed at the foe. In contrast, when Complete Forme Zygarde employs the move, it instead draws a large "Z" shape on the ground, reminiscent of its portrayal in other media, which then inflicts delayed damage upon all combatants positioned within the 'Z'. Furthermore, Sygna Suit Serena & Zygarde benefit from the passive skill Piercing Gaze whenever Zygarde assumes its Complete Forme; this ensures that their deployment of Core Enforcer will always strike true, never missing. Notably, Core Enforcer made its debut in the animated series as the first Generation VII move showcased. It was wielded by two Zygarde Cores, known as Squishy and Z2, within the episode "Forming a More Perfect Union!", predating the official launch of Pokémon Sun and Moon.

Title: Trop Kick (move)

Trop Kick (Japanese: トロピカルキック Tropical Kick) is an offensive Grass-type technique that debuted in Generation VII. It functions as the signature move of Tsareena. This attack deals damage and simultaneously reduces the target's Attack by a single stage. Its description, prior to Version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), simply stated: "Lowers the target's Attack."

In Pokémon UNITE, Trop Kick is Tsareena's second available move. It is acquired when Steenee evolves at level 6, at which point Rapid Spin upgrades into Trop Kick instead of Grassy Glide. The user executes a flying kick in a designated direction, inflicting damage on enemies hit and temporarily decreasing their Attack by 25%. Additionally, opponents making contact with the wake of the flying kick sustain damage and have their movement speed reduced for a short duration. If Queenly Majesty is active, this move grants the user a protective shield. At level 12, the move's damage output is enhanced. The name "Trop Kick" is thought to be a pun on "drop kick," an attack where the user jumps and strikes the opponent with both feet while falling.

Title: Instruct (move)

Introduced in Generation VII, Instruct (known in Japanese as さいはい, meaning Command) is a Psychic-type status move that inflicts no damage. It stands as the signature technique of Oranguru. Instruct compels the designated target to promptly re-execute its most recent action, completely disregarding any moves that were initiated indirectly via abilities such as Dancer or Magic Bounce. Moves repeated in this manner will still consume Power Points (PP), and any damage dealt by these re-executed attacks will count towards the effects of Counter, Mirror Coat, and Metal Burst, even if the target had not yet chosen a move for that particular turn.

Should the target be instructed to use its most recent move before it has acted in the current turn (assuming it has acted on a previous turn), it will perform the move from the prior turn. Subsequently, when its natural turn order arrives, it can then execute the move it originally selected for the current round.

Instruct will fail under several conditions: if the target has not performed any action whatsoever; if it is currently engaged in moves like Bide, Focus Punch, Beak Blast, Shell Trap, or a multi-turn attack (with Raging Fury being an exception from Generation IX onwards); if it has been lifted into the air by Sky Drop (even if one of the involved Pokémon possesses No Guard); if the specific move that would be instructed has exhausted all of its PP; if the target no longer remembers the move it would be told to use (for instance, by leveling up and replacing it); or if the target is a Dynamax Pokémon or the boss of a Tera Raid Battle.

Furthermore, Instruct will not succeed if the target's most recently executed move was Instruct itself, Bide, Focus Punch, Beak Blast, Shell Trap, Sketch, Transform, Mimic, King's Shield, Struggle, any move requiring a recharge period (such as Hyper Beam), an attack with an initial charging turn (like Dig or Skull Bash), a move designed to call another move (for example, Metronome), a Z-Move, or a Max Move.

Using an instructed move constitutes a distinct action, meaning that effects tied to a Pokémon's individual actions are processed separately. For instance, a Pokémon might inflict self-damage due to confusion or waste a turn loafing during its original action, yet successfully execute a move during its subsequent instructed action. For action-dependent status conditions from which recovery is possible, such as sleep or confusion, performing an instructed move both provides an opportunity for recovery and contributes to the turn count for any guaranteed recovery mechanisms. Conversely, effects not bound to a Pokémon's individual actions apply uniformly once for the entire turn; for example, if a Pokémon has flinched, this condition will prevent all of its potential actions for the remainder of that turn.

Notably, Instruct can still compel a Pokémon under the effect of Torment to repeat a move, and can force a Pokémon holding an Assault Vest to execute a status move. If Instruct commands the use of Fake Out, Mat Block, or First Impression, the target will attempt to initiate the move, and its PP will be consumed, but the move will ultimately fail. However, if the instructed move would be nullified by conditions such as Disable, Heal Block, Gravity, Throat Chop, Taunt, or Imprison, the target will still attempt its execution, but the move will fail without any corresponding PP deduction.

The re-executed move will automatically target the identical Pokémon slot that was previously aimed at, provided a Pokémon currently occupies that position. Crucially, instructed moves maintain their inherent priority level, entirely independent of Instruct's own priority. For instance, Quick Attack will still be blocked in Psychic Terrain, even if it's called by an Instruct move executed at 0 priority. Likewise, non-priority moves will successfully execute within Psychic Terrain when instructed, even if the Pokémon using Instruct possesses the Prankster ability. Prankster-instructed non-priority status moves used on a Dark-type Pokémon will also succeed, as will Prankster-instructed non-priority moves used on a Pokémon protected by Armor Tail or Queenly Majesty.

When Instruct is amplified by a Psychium Z into its Z-Move variant, Z-Instruct, the user's Special Attack stat receives a one-stage boost.

Title: Beak Blast (move)

Named くちばしキャノン (Beak Cannon) in Japanese, Beak Blast is a damage-dealing Flying-type move that debuted in Generation VII. This technique serves as Toucannon's exclusive signature move. Beak Blast operates with a reduced priority. At the onset of a turn, the user initiates a preparatory charge, then unleashes Beak Blast with a priority of -3. Any Pokémon that makes physical contact with the user while it is in its charging phase will suffer a burn status condition. The notification signaling its charge appears before the display of any other move messages. The user enters this charging phase irrespective of any status conditions that might otherwise hinder Beak Blast's execution. For instance, should the user be afflicted by confusion, the check for confusion takes place just prior to the attack's strike, rather than before the initial charging action. While Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof Ability are impervious to Beak Blast's damage, they remain susceptible to its associated burn effect. During Versions 1.0 through 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Beak Blast was unavailable for selection in battles. However, commencing with Version 3.0.0, it became usable once more, retaining its functionality consistent with prior generations.

Within Pokémon GO, Beak Blast is categorized as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since November 30, 2025. In this game, the user adopts a counterattacking stance. If this move is triggered a second time, the user performs an attack. While in this counterattack-ready state, no other actions are permitted. An attack from an opponent will prompt the user to strike back. Furthermore, if an opponent uses a physical move against the user during this counterattack-ready phase, that opponent will be left burned.

Before Version 2.10.0 (which was released on June 28, 2021), its mechanics differed: The user would adopt a counterattacking posture. Re-activating the move would result in the user attacking, followed by exiting this posture. While in this posture, no other actions were possible. If struck by an attack move while it was in this counterattacking posture, the user would attack and then abandon the posture. Should the triggering move be a physical attack, the attacking opponent would become burned. Alternatively, the user would assume a counterattacking posture. Activating the move again would first cause the user to abandon this posture, then attack. While occupying this posture, other actions were disallowed. If attacked by a move while it was in this counterattacking posture, the user would first leave this posture, then attack. If that attack was a physical move, it would inflict a burn on the attacking foe.

Remarkably, Beak Blast holds the distinction of being the sole Flying-type move capable of inflicting a burn on its target. The move's functionality draws inspiration from real-world toucans, which utilize their beaks as heat sinks to help regulate their body temperature.

Title: Clanging Scales (move)

A Dragon-type attack capable of inflicting damage, Clanging Scales (Japanese: スケイルノイズ, also known as Scale Noise) debuted in Generation VII. It stands as one of Kommo-o's signature techniques. When Kommo-o holds a Kommonium Z in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, this move can be elevated into the exclusive Z-Move, Clangorous Soulblaze. Using Clanging Scales delivers damage but also causes the user's Defense stat to drop by one stage. This ability is categorized as a sound-based move. Notably, Clanging Scales can bypass and strike Pokémon even if they are protected by a substitute. The move's description, before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), simply stated: "Lowers the user's Defense." Furthermore, the 6★ EX effect's description, preceding Version 2.18.5 (from March 15, 2022), read: "Additional Effects: Sync move targets all opponents." In Pokémon GO, Clanging Scales functions as a Charged Attack and has been available since June 21, 2025. Phil's Kommo-o, in the episode "All Out, All of the Time!", was confirmed to possess Clanging Scales due to its deployment of Clangorous Soulblaze. However, the move itself has yet to be directly observed in action.

Title: Dragon Hammer (move)

This Dragon-type damage-dealing move, known in Japanese as ドラゴンハンマー (Dragon Hammer), first debuted in Generation VII. During Generation VII, it served as Alolan Exeggutor's signature attack, though other Pokémon were capable of acquiring it through breeding. Dragon Hammer simply inflicts damage, lacking any secondary effects. Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, specifically from versions 1.0 through 1.1.1, Dragon Hammer could not be chosen during a battle. Starting with version 1.2.0, it became selectable and functioned exactly as it had in the preceding generation. Likewise, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the move was unselectable in battle across versions 1.0 to 2.0.1. From version 3.0.0 onward, it became selectable once more, behaving identically to its function in earlier generations. In Generation IX, its animation shares similarities with Ice Hammer and, when not utilized by Tinkaton, with Gigaton Hammer; however, Dragon Hammer is distinguished by a rose-colored hammer rather than blue or silver, respectively.

Title: Brutal Swing (move)

Making its debut in Generation VII, Brutal Swing (Japanese: ぶんまわす Brutal Swing) is a Dark-type maneuver that inflicts damage. It carried the TM59 designation across Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, later shifting to TM97 within Pokémon Sword and Shield. This move causes harm to every Pokémon positioned nearby, even one's own allies. As a Charged Attack in Pokémon GO, Brutal Swing became accessible on June 25, 2022. It stands alone as the sole contact move capable of striking all adjacent Pokémon simultaneously.

Title: Aurora Veil (move)

Known in Japanese as オーロラベール (Aurora Veil), this Ice-type status move debuted in Generation VII. It held the TM70 slot across Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon. For five turns, Aurora Veil halves the damage taken by the user from both physical and special attacks. In formats beyond Single Battles, it extends this protection to all allies alongside the user, though the damage reduction becomes approximately one-third (precisely, a 2732/4096 multiplier) instead of half. However, this effect does not apply to self-inflicted confusion damage, critical hits, or moves that inflict direct damage.

Its activation is contingent on the presence of hail or snow, yet its protective effects endure even after the weather condition dissipates. Abilities such as Cloud Nine and Air Lock will prevent Aurora Veil from taking effect, even if hail or snow is present. While it can be deployed concurrently with Reflect and Light Screen, the defensive benefits from these moves do not accumulate. Certain actions will dismantle Aurora Veil from a Pokémon's side of the field: being struck by Brick Break, Defog, Psychic Fangs, or Raging Bull, or when a Pokémon possessing the Screen Cleaner Ability enters the battle. Furthermore, Pokémon with the Infiltrator Ability bypass Aurora Veil's effects entirely when launching attacks.

Equipping a Light Clay when Aurora Veil is deployed will prolong its duration from the standard 5 turns to 8 turns. When amplified by an Icium Z crystal, transforming into Z-Aurora Veil, the user receives a one-stage boost to its Speed stat.

In *Pokémon Legends: Arceus*, Aurora Veil targets the user and any allied Pokémon in the same immediate area, applying the "Aurora Veil" status condition. This status lessens damage from *all* incoming moves and persists for 21 turns. In this title, the move will exclusively succeed if the weather is Hail.

Within *Pokémon UNITE*, Alolan Ninetales can acquire Aurora Veil as its second move at level 6, chosen as an evolution from Icy Wind rather than Blizzard. Activating it causes Ninetales to dash a brief distance, then summon an aurora around its location for 5 seconds. This phenomenon decreases damage sustained by Ninetales and its nearby allies by 30%, concurrently restoring Ninetales's HP with each of its boosted attacks. While Ninetales remains within the aurora's radius, its movement speed is elevated by 20%, and all its basic attacks become boosted. Upon reaching level 13, this move's damage reduction receives an additional 5% increase, and its cooldown period is shortened by 2 seconds.

In *Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX*, an error exists within Aurora Veil's Technical Machine description, which incorrectly labels it as a "damage-dealing physical move" instead of accurately identifying it as a status move.

Title: Sinister Arrow Raid (move)

Introduced during Generation VII, Sinister Arrow Raid is a Ghost-type Z-Move designed for inflicting damage. Known in Japanese as シャドーアローズストライク (Shadow Arrows Strike), it functions as Alolan Decidueye's unique Z-Move, serving as an amplified version of its signature attack, Spirit Shackle. This move's effect is purely to deal damage, as it has no additional consequences. Sinister Arrow Raid is not directly selectable during a battle. For a Pokémon to utilize it, the creature must be Decidueye (or a Pokémon transformed into Decidueye), possess knowledge of Spirit Shackle, and be holding a Decidium Z. Additionally, its Trainer must be wearing either a Z-Ring or a Z-Power Ring.

Title: Malicious Moonsault (move)

This offensive Dark-type Z-Move, known in Japanese as ハイパーダーククラッシャー (Hyper Dark Crusher), made its debut in Generation VII. Solely available to Incineroar, it serves as an enhanced form of the Pokémon's prior signature attack, Darkest Lariat. The move deals direct damage. Should the opposing Pokémon have previously employed Minimize, Malicious Moonsault's power is doubled. It is not directly selectable during combat. To execute Malicious Moonsault, Incineroar (or any Pokémon transformed into Incineroar) must be aware of Darkest Lariat, be equipped with an Incinium Z, and its Trainer must be wearing either a Z-Ring or a Z-Power Ring. Incineroar's Final Smash, Max Malicious Moonsault, is depicted as an even quicker and more aggressive rendition of the original maneuver. Prior to Version 2.18.5 (released March 15, 2022), the 6★ EX effect boosted sync move power by 50%. Furthermore, before Version 2.20.0 (dated April 27, 2022), the move's power scaled with the degree of the user's Speed stat increase. It's noteworthy that, despite its English appellation, Malicious Moonsault visually depicts Incineroar performing a 450° splash instead of a traditional moonsault.

Title: Oceanic Operetta (move)

Oceanic Operetta, known in Japanese as わだつみのシンフォニア (Sea God's Symphonia), made its debut in Generation VII as a damage-dealing Water-type Z-Move. This particular Z-Move is exclusive to Primarina, functioning as an enhanced version of its signature attack, Sparkling Aria. Oceanic Operetta deals damage without imparting any secondary effects. For Primarina (or a Pokémon that has transformed into Primarina) to execute Oceanic Operetta, it must fulfill several criteria: knowing Sparkling Aria, holding a Primarium Z, and its Trainer must be wearing a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. During the animation sequence of Oceanic Operetta, Primarina's physical appearance undergoes a symmetrical inversion, resulting in its tail being situated on the right side of its body instead of its usual left. This specific visual modification is unique to Primarina and this Z-Move, not manifesting with any other Pokémon or Z-Move. Despite its nomenclature and its status as an upgraded form of Sparkling Aria, Oceanic Operetta is not classified as a sound-based move. This Z-Move pays homage to Spirit Bomb, a technique originating from the Dragon Ball Series.

Title: Stoked Sparksurfer (move)

Known in Japan as ライトニングサーフライド (Lightning Surf Ride), Stoked Sparksurfer stands as an Electric-type, damage-dealing Z-Move, first appearing in Generation VII. This unique Z-Move belongs solely to Alolan Raichu, serving as an enhanced variant of its signature move, Thunderbolt. Its effect includes not only inflicting damage but also guaranteeing paralysis for the target. Players cannot directly select Stoked Sparksurfer during a battle. To activate Stoked Sparksurfer, an Alolan Raichu (or a Pokémon currently transformed into one) must know Thunderbolt, be holding an Aloraichium Z, and its Trainer must be equipped with either a Z-Ring or a Z-Power Ring. Notably, Stoked Sparksurfer holds the distinction of being the sole damaging Z-Move capable of inflicting a non-volatile status condition. Furthermore, it is the only fixed-power move boasting a power rating of 175. Its power rating precisely matches that of a Gigavolt Havoc when derived from Thunderbolt, the very move required for Stoked Sparksurfer's activation. The crucial distinction, however, lies in its additional effect: paralyzing the target alongside its damage output.

Title: Pulverizing Pancake (move)

Introduced in Generation VII, Pulverizing Pancake is a damage-dealing Normal-type Z-Move, carrying the Japanese name ほんきをだすこうげき (Go All Out Attack). This distinctive attack serves as Snorlax's exclusive Z-Move, representing an augmented iteration of its Giga Impact. Pulverizing Pancake solely delivers damage, devoid of any additional effects. It is not possible for this move to be selected directly within a battle. A Snorlax, or a Pokémon that has taken Snorlax's form, may execute Pulverizing Pancake under specific conditions: if it knows Giga Impact, is equipped with a Snorlium Z, and its Trainer is wearing a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring.

Title: Extreme Evoboost (move)

Unveiled in Generation VII, Extreme Evoboost (Japanese: ナインエボルブースト Nine Evol Boost) is a Normal-type Z-Move that inflicts no damage. This move serves as Eevee's exclusive Z-Move, functioning as an enhanced version of its Last Resort. Upon execution, Extreme Evoboost substantially boosts the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats, each by two stages. It is unable to further elevate any stat already at its maximum of +6. Should all stats be at this +6 threshold, the move can still be performed, yet it will provide no additional increases. Extreme Evoboost operates as a distinct Z-Move, rather than a Z-Power Effect appended to an existing status move, and therefore interacts typically with move-modifying abilities such as Simple and Contrary. For Extreme Evoboost to be unleashed, Eevee—or a Pokémon transformed into Eevee—must know Last Resort, be holding an Eevium Z, and its Trainer must possess a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. Its description, prior to version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), stated that it would charge the user's move gauge by its current move level and sharply raise the Attack, Defense, Sp. Atk, Sp. Def, Speed, and accuracy of all allied sync pairs. Extreme Evoboost is remarkable as the only Pokémon-exclusive Z-Move categorized as a status move. Moreover, it is the sole Z-Move whose category diverges from its base move's category.

Title: Genesis Supernova (move)

Originated in Generation VII, Genesis Supernova (Japanese: オリジンズスーパーノヴァ Origins Supernova) is a Psychic-type Z-Move that deals damage. This Z-Move is unique to Mew and represents an amplified form of its Psychic attack. Beyond inflicting damage, Genesis Supernova also alters the terrain to Psychic Terrain for five turns. To unleash Genesis Supernova, Mew (or a Pokémon transformed into Mew) must know Psychic, hold Mewnium Z, and have its Trainer wear a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. Mew cannot employ Genesis Supernova if transformed into any Pokémon other than itself. Notably, it is the sole Z-Move capable of establishing any terrain type.

Title: Shell Trap (move)

Debuting in Generation VII, Shell Trap (Japanese: トラップシェル Trap Shell) is a Fire-type attack that inflicts damage. It serves as Turtonator's exclusive signature technique and operates with a reduced priority level. At the start of a turn, the user establishes a defensive trap. Should an opposing Pokémon strike the user with a physical attack during that same turn, Shell Trap will activate instantly following the opponent's action. Conversely, if no such physical blow occurs, the move will simply fail when its natural -3 priority turn arrives. Notably, Encore will be ineffective against a target that currently has Shell Trap prepared.

Activation of Shell Trap is prevented under several circumstances: if the Pokémon is struck by an ally's attack; if a substitute absorbs the damage; if the incoming move benefits from the Sheer Force ability; if the hit occurs after the user's own turn has already elapsed (for example, by Dragon Tail or any move with a priority lower than -3); or if the strike results in the user fainting.

A particular glitch exists where, if Encore intercepts Shell Trap, forcing the Pokémon into a different move, and that Pokémon subsequently endures a physical strike on any turn before Encore expires, its subsequent attempt to use Shell Trap will bypass the initial trap setup phase. Instead, the move will execute successfully at its standard priority, irrespective of whether it was hit during that specific turn. Furthermore, if the user is under the effect of Powder and is then subjected to a physical attack from an adversary, it will suffer damage as an alternative outcome.

Title: Fleur Cannon (move)

Fleur Cannon (Japanese: フルールカノン Fleur Cannon) is a damage-dealing Fairy-type attack that debuted in Generation VII. It serves as Magearna's signature technique. When used, Fleur Cannon inflicts damage upon its target but concurrently decreases the user's Special Attack stat by two stages. Initially, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, from Versions 1.0 to 1.1.1, Fleur Cannon could not be chosen during a battle. However, starting with Version 1.2.0, it became available for selection and operated with the same mechanics as it did in the prior generation. A distinct bug meant that Original Color Magearna's unique animation for Fleur Cannon was not displayed in Pokémon Sword and Shield's Versions 1.2.0 through 1.2.1; this graphical error was corrected in Version 1.3.0. Sygna Suit Gladion & Magearna possess a passive skill, Just Give It Up Already, which uniquely prevents this move from lowering their Sp. Atk after its execution.

Title: Psychic Fangs (move)

Originating in Generation VII, Psychic Fangs (サイコファング Psycho Fang in Japanese) is a Psychic-type offense that inflicts damage. Before Generation VIII, it was the exclusive signature technique of Bruxish, although other Pokémon were capable of inheriting it through breeding. The primary function of Psychic Fangs is to dismantle Light Screen, Reflect, and Aurora Veil from the adversary's side of the field, preceding its damage output. This barrier-removing aspect will not activate, however, if the move fails to hit, or if the intended target holds immunity to or protection from Psychic Fangs. In Pokémon GO, this attack functions as a Charged Attack, becoming accessible on November 21, 2021.

Title: Stomping Tantrum (move)

Stomping Tantrum, referred to as じだんだ in Japanese, is a Ground-type attack that inflicts damage, having debuted in Generation VII. It previously held the identifier TM98 within Pokémon Sword and Shield, and currently functions as TM084 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This damage-dealing move boasts a unique property: its power escalates from 75 to a formidable 150 if, during the preceding turn, the user's last executed move either missed its mark, failed to impact all intended targets, or was prevented from activation by an ailment like paralysis. However, Stomping Tantrum's base power does not receive this boost if, also on the preceding turn, the user was in a recharging state, had been seized by Sky Drop, or if any adversary targeted by the user's prior move successfully defended with a protective action such as Protect. Fundamentally, Stomping Tantrum delivers an attack whose destructive potential is amplified when the user's preceding offensive action did not achieve its intended outcome. Earlier in-game descriptions include: "Its power is doubled if the user's previous move failed," as stated before Version 2.10.0 (effective June 28, 2021). Furthermore, preceding Version 2.20.0 (dated April 27, 2022), the text read: "This move's power is doubled when the user's previous move failed."

Title: Shadow Bone (move)

Making its debut in Generation VII, Shadow Bone (Japanese: シャドーボーン Shadow Bone) is a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage. It stands as one of Alolan Marowak's unique signature moves. Beyond its primary function of dealing damage, Shadow Bone carries a 20% probability of reducing the target's Defense stat by a single stage. Within Pokémon Sword and Shield, during versions 1.0 through 1.1.1, this move was unobtainable in combat encounters, as no available Pokémon within those game iterations were capable of learning it. With the release of Version 1.2.0, its usability was restored, and it began operating identically to its performance in the preceding generation. Its in-game description, before Version 2.10.0 (which launched on June 28, 2021), read: "Has a small chance of lowering the target's Defense."

Title: Liquidation (move)

Known in Japan as アクアブレイク (Aqua Break), Liquidation is an offensive Water-type technique that first emerged in Generation VII. This attack delivers damage to the target and carries a 20% probability of diminishing the opponent's Defense stat by one stage. Essentially, it functions as a damage-dealing action that may reduce the target's Defense by a single stage. Before Version 2.10.0, which was released on June 28, 2021, the move's in-game description read: "Has a small chance of lowering the target's Defense." In the mobile game Pokémon GO, Liquidation operates as a Charged Attack, having been introduced on June 1, 2023.

Title: Prismatic Laser (move)

Prismatic Laser, a Psychic-type attack that deals damage, made its debut in Generation VII and is known in Japan as プリズムレーザー (Prism Laser). This move is considered one of Necrozma's exclusive techniques. Executing Prismatic Laser deals damage, but obliges the user to recharge on the following turn. However, the user is exempt from this recharge period if the attack misses, or if the target is either immune to or protected from the assault. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, during versions 1.0 through 2.0.2, Prismatic Laser was unselectable in combat. As of Version 3.0.0, it became accessible and operates identically to its behavior in earlier generations. Excluding Z-Moves, Prismatic Laser possesses a higher base power than any other Psychic-type move. The Z-Move Shattered Psyche, when derived from either Prismatic Laser or Psycho Boost, holds the absolute highest base power among all Psychic-type moves. Furthermore, it stands alone as the sole move requiring a recharge to possess 10 PP.

Title: Spectral Thief (move)

Introduced in Generation VII, Spectral Thief (Japanese: シャドースチール Shadow Steal) is a Ghost-type attack that deals damage. This technique is Marshadow's unique signature move. When Marshadow holds Marshadium Z, Spectral Thief can be upgraded to the powerful special Z-Move, Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike. Beyond its primary function of inflicting damage, if successful, Spectral Thief also purloins any positive stat stages from the target immediately before its damage calculation. This move is capable of bypassing Substitutes, and it can successfully appropriate stat enhancements from a Max Raid Battle boss even if that boss's shield is currently active. Even if the target possesses an Ability like Clear Body, White Smoke, or Hyper Cutter, which typically prevent some or all stats from being lowered, Spectral Thief can still abscond with boosts in those specific stats. The move will steal all positive stat stages; even if the sum of the user's existing stat stages and the target's stat stages would exceed +6 in a particular stat, the target's positive stages will nonetheless be reduced to 0. Should the user possess the Simple Ability, the stolen stat stages will be doubled; conversely, if the user has the Contrary Ability, the stat boosts will become stat drops upon being stolen. In the film I Choose You!, Marshadow's proficiency with Spectral Thief was confirmed via its execution of Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike, though it has never been directly observed utilizing Spectral Thief itself. If Battle Effects are disabled, Spectral Thief will still display a visual effect if it manages to steal any stat boosts.

Title: Sunsteel Strike (move)

Hailing from Generation VII, the Steel-type attack known as Sunsteel Strike (メテオドライブ Meteor Drive in Japan) inflicts damage and serves as the exclusive technique for both Solgaleo and Dusk Mane Necrozma. When utilized by Solgaleo or Dusk Mane Necrozma in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon while holding a Solganium Z, Sunsteel Strike transforms into the formidable Z-Move, Searing Sunraze Smash. This attack delivers damage while disregarding all "ignorable" Abilities active during its performance. Such ignorable Abilities typically comprise those that might hinder a move's effectiveness, power, or outcome if held by the target or their allies, exemplified by skills like Multiscale and Friend Guard. Crucially, its ability-ignoring property is bypassed if Sunsteel Strike is invoked indirectly by another move instead of being chosen firsthand.

Within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, from Versions 1.0 through 2.0.2, Sunsteel Strike was unavailable for selection during combat. However, with the release of Version 3.0.0, it became fully usable, mirroring its functionality from prior generations.

Switching to Pokémon GO, Sunsteel Strike functions as a Charged Attack, introduced on May 30, 2024. Beyond combat, it possesses an Adventure Effect: by expending Necrozma Candy and Stardust, it grants a temporary window for Pokémon that evolve during daylight hours, alongside Dusk Form Rockruff, to undergo evolution. This effect cannot be active concurrently with an Incense, Mystery Box, or Coin Bag. Additionally, it has the power to attract specific Pokémon. Activating this effect consumes three Necrozma Candy and 3,000 Stardust, lasting for a duration of 10 minutes. An earlier description, predating Version 2.10.0 (as of June 28, 2021), stated that Sunsteel Strike disregards passive abilities designed to lessen its damage output, ignores passive abilities safeguarding targets from critical hits, and prevents opponents from enduring the assault.

Title: Moongeist Beam (move)

Known in Japanese as シャドーレイ (Shadow Ray), Moongeist Beam is a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage, making its debut in Generation VII. This particular move serves as the exclusive technique for both Lunala and Dawn Wings Necrozma. When utilized in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, holding the Lunalium Z crystal enables Lunala or Dawn Wings Necrozma to transform Moongeist Beam into the unique Z-Move, Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom. Upon its execution, Moongeist Beam deals damage while bypassing all Abilities that are considered 'ignorable'. These 'ignorable Abilities' generally encompass most Abilities that, if present on a move's target or their allies, could negatively influence the move's success, damage output, or overall effects—notable examples include Multiscale and Friend Guard. Nevertheless, this bypass effect does not activate if Moongeist Beam is invoked by a different attack instead of being directly employed. Across Versions 1.0 through 2.0.2 of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, selecting Moongeist Beam during combat was not possible. From Version 3.0.0 onwards, it became fully usable, operating identically to its behavior in earlier generations. Within the Pokémon GO mobile game, Moongeist Beam serves as a Charged Attack, accessible since May 30, 2024. Outside of battles, its Adventure Effect, activated by consuming Necrozma candy and Stardust, grants a temporary window for any nocturnal-evolving Pokémon to evolve, specifically benefiting creatures like Dusk Rockruff and Ursaring even without a full moon. Furthermore, it possesses the capability to attract specific Pokémon. However, its use is incompatible with Incense, the Mystery Box, or the Coin Bag, preventing simultaneous activation. Activating this effect for 10 minutes requires 3 Necrozma candy and 3,000 Stardust.

Title: Tearful Look (move)
Designated as a non-damaging Normal-type technique, Tearful Look (なみだめ Teary Eyes in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VII. Its primary effect is to decrease the target's Attack and Special Attack stats, each by a single stage. This move inherently possesses perfect accuracy, always connecting with its foe unless they are engaged in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. While it successfully pierces through defensive maneuvers such as Protect, Detect, Spiky Shield, and Baneful Bunker, it is notably rendered ineffective by Crafty Shield. When transformed into Z-Tearful Look through the application of a Normalium Z, the user benefits from a one-stage boost to their own Defense stat. Interestingly, the visual elements within Tearful Look's battle animation appear to draw inspiration from the eyes of Espurr, a Pokémon incapable of learning the move itself. This peculiar characteristic is also observed in Baby-Doll EyesVII+ and Mean LookVII+.

Title: Mind Blown (move)

Known in Japanese as ビックリヘッド (Surprised Head), Mind Blown is a Fire-type, damage-dealing attack initially introduced during Generation VII in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It is the signature technique of Blacephalon. This move inflicts damage upon its target, but the user simultaneously suffers recoil equal to half of its total HP, rounded upwards, unless it possesses the Magic Guard Ability. Mind Blown cannot be initiated if a Pokémon with the Damp Ability is present on the battlefield. The Pokémon performing Mind Blown will still take its self-inflicted damage even if the target is shielded from the attack, if it strikes a substitute, if the move misses altogether, or if the opposing Pokémon has the Flash Fire Ability. Conversely, if Mind Blown fails to execute because there are no available targets (e.g., all Pokémon within range fainted before its use), the user avoids any damage. Abilities like Rock Head and Reckless do not impact Mind Blown. In Single Battles, should the user have Parental Bond, the HP loss occurs after the initial hit, and the secondary hit proceeds only if neither Pokémon fainted from the first impact; notably, no additional HP reduction is incurred for this second strike. The user's trainer will face defeat if all of their remaining Pokémon are knocked out in the same turn as a direct result of using Mind Blown.

Regarding its availability in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Mind Blown was unselectable in battle from Versions 1.0 up to 1.2.1. However, starting with Version 1.3.0, it became fully functional, mirroring its behavior from the previous generation. Mind Blown is not present in the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. Consequently, a Pokémon knowing Mind Blown cannot be traded from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Sun and Moon, nor can a Pokémon with the move be withdrawn from Pokémon Bank into Sun and Moon. To facilitate a transfer to Pokémon Sun and Moon, the Pokémon in question must first forget Mind Blown.

A related table illustrates the probability that "Strange Flames Mind Blown" will amplify the user's "Special Moves ↑ Next" effect by various rank increments. In Pokémon GO, Mind Blown is classified as a Charged Attack and has been available since December 31, 2025. When a Shiny Pokémon deploys Mind Blown, its animation changes, featuring a Shiny Blacephalon's blue head instead of the standard white one. This distinct visual trait is shared with Dragon Darts and Syrup Bomb. This animated difference does not, however, manifest in Pokémon GO; there, the head remains white regardless of whether the user is Shiny.

Title: Plasma Fists (move)

Plasma Fists (Japanese: プラズマフィスト Plasma Fist) is an Electric-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation VII with the release of Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. This move serves as Zeraora's exclusive signature technique. Upon activation, Plasma Fists causes all Normal-type moves used by any Pokémon during the current turn's remainder to become Electric-type, a transformation that also applies to status moves. Max Moves are also subject to this alteration, converting into Max Lightning; however, they will specifically become G-Max Volt Crash for Gigantamax Pikachu and G-Max Stun Shock for Gigantamax Toxtricity, respectively. The type-modifying influence of Plasma Fists occurs subsequent to the effects of move type-changing Abilities. Therefore, it cannot impact an originally Normal-type move if the Pokémon possesses Pixilate, but it is capable of affecting an originally Fairy-type move if the Pokémon has Normalize.

Plasma Fists is not present within the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. As a direct consequence, a Pokémon holding Plasma Fists cannot be transferred via trade from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Sun and Moon. Similarly, if a Pokémon stored in Pokémon Bank knows this move, it cannot be retrieved into Pokémon Sun and Moon. For a Pokémon to be successfully moved to Pokémon Sun and Moon, it must first relinquish Plasma Fists. In the spin-off title Pokémon UNITE, Zeraora's Unite Move, Plasma Gale, bears a resemblance to Plasma Fists.

Title: Photon Geyser (move)

Known in Japan as フォトンゲイザー (Photon Geyser), this Psychic-type attack, which deals damage, debuted in Generation VII, specifically within Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It stands as one of Necrozma's signature moves. When Ultra Necrozma holds the Ultranecrozium Z item, Photon Geyser can be elevated into the unique special Z-Move, Light That Burns the Sky. The attack's damage-dealing classification depends on the user's stats: if its Attack stat surpasses its Special Attack stat, Photon Geyser manifests as a physical move, striking the target's Defense stat. Otherwise, it operates as a special move, affecting the target's Special Defense stat.

During Photon Geyser's execution, all Abilities classified as "ignorable" are bypassed. Ignorable Abilities broadly include most Abilities of the target or its allies that could negatively impact a move's success, damage, or other effects, with examples such as Multiscale and Friend Guard. However, this ability-ignoring property is not active if Photon Geyser is activated indirectly by another move rather than being used directly. For the purpose of determining which of the user's Attack or Special Attack stats is higher, only stat stage modifiers are factored in; other influences like held items (e.g., Choice Band) or Abilities (e.g., Huge Power) are not considered.

In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, from Versions 1.0 up to 2.0.2, Photon Geyser was unselectable during battles. Beginning with Version 3.0.0, it became fully operational and functions identically to its behavior in prior generations. Photon Geyser is absent from the game data of Pokémon Sun and Moon. Consequently, a Pokémon knowing Photon Geyser cannot be traded from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon to Pokémon Sun and Moon. Furthermore, if a Pokémon within Pokémon Bank possesses the move, it cannot be withdrawn into Pokémon Sun and Moon. To enable transfer to Pokémon Sun and Moon, the Pokémon must unlearn Photon Geyser.

Title: Searing Sunraze Smash (move)

Known in Japanese as サンシャインスマッシャー, or Sunshine Smasher, Searing Sunraze Smash represents a potent Steel-type Z-Move that inflicts damage. Its debut occurred in Generation VII, specifically within the games Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. This unique attack is reserved solely for Solgaleo and Dusk Mane Necrozma, functioning as an enhanced iteration of their signature technique, Sunsteel Strike. When Searing Sunraze Smash is executed, it deals damage while simultaneously disregarding any ignorable Abilities present during its activation. (To clarify, 'ignorable Abilities' encompass the majority of Abilities that might unfavorably impact a move's success rate, damage output, or overall effects if held by the move's target or their allied Pokémon—examples include Multiscale and Friend Guard.) It is important to note that Searing Sunraze Smash is not directly selectable during a Pokémon battle. For Searing Sunraze Smash to be unleashed, the user must be Solgaleo, Dusk Mane Necrozma, or a Pokémon that has undergone transformation into one of them. Additionally, it must possess the move Sunsteel Strike, be equipped with a Solganium Z item, and its Trainer must be adorned with a Z-Power Ring. Among all Steel-type moves, Searing Sunraze Smash shares the distinction of possessing the highest base power, a feat it achieves alongside a Corkscrew Crash initiated from Doom Desire.

Title: Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom (move)

The Ghost-type Z-Move known as Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom (Japanese: ムーンライトブラスター Moonlight Blaster) first appeared in Generation VII through Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, functioning as a damage-dealing attack. This move serves as the exclusive Z-Move for both Lunala and Dawn Wings Necrozma, representing an enhanced form of their unique attack, Moongeist Beam. When Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom is performed, it deals damage while bypassing any ignorable Abilities. These "ignorable" Abilities primarily include those that could hinder a move's effectiveness, power, or outcome if held by the opposing Pokémon or its allies, with examples being Multiscale and Friend Guard. A Trainer cannot directly choose Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom from the move menu during combat. Only Lunala, Dawn Wings Necrozma, or a Pokémon transformed into one of them, may unleash Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom. This requires them to have learned Moongeist Beam, be equipped with a Lunalium Z, and for their Trainer to be wearing a Z-Power Ring. With 27 characters in its English title, Menacing Moonraze Maelstrom shares the record for the longest move name alongside Soul-Stealing 7-Star Strike.

Title: Splintered Stormshards (move)
