Known in Japanese as ラジアルエッジストーム (Radial Edgestorm), Splintered Stormshards functions as a damage-dealing Rock-type Z-Move. This special move made its debut in Generation VII, specifically with the release of Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. It serves as Lycanroc's signature Z-Move, representing an enhanced iteration of the Pokémon's Stone Edge attack. When executed, Splintered Stormshards not only deals damage but also clears any active terrain effects from the combat area. It is important to note that Splintered Stormshards cannot be directly chosen during a battle. To activate this move, a Lycanroc of any form—or a Pokémon that has transformed into Lycanroc—must fulfill specific conditions: it needs to have learned Stone Edge, be holding a Lycanium Z, and its Trainer must be equipped with a Z-Power Ring. The sync move's ability to be raised to 6★ EX was unavailable until January 25, 2023, marking a span of 1091 days from its initial launch on January 30, 2020. Prior to Version 2.10.0 (from June 28, 2021), its description indicated it would clear physical and special damage-reducing effects from the opponents' field of play. Despite consistent properties, the visual animation of Splintered Stormshards adapts to the specific Lycanroc form utilizing it, portraying a time of day within the animation that corresponds with that form. Within Pokémon Masters EX, Island Splintered Stormshards uniquely stands as the sole sync move capable of dispelling certain field effects.

Title: Clangorous Soulblaze (move)

A damage-dealing Dragon-type Z-Move, Clangorous Soulblaze (known in Japan as ブレイジングソウルビート Blazing Soulbeat) first appeared in Generation VII through the games Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. This Z-Move is exclusive to Kommo-o and functions as an enhanced form of its signature move, Clanging Scales. When used, Clangorous Soulblaze not only damages all opposing Pokémon but also simultaneously augments the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats by a single stage each. Notably, Clangorous Soulblaze is capable of striking Pokémon even when they are protected by a Substitute. However, the move will fail if the target Pokémon are in the semi-invulnerable turn of another move, or if they are inherently immune to Clangorous Soulblaze; furthermore, the user's stats will not be increased if the move is unable to inflict damage on any of the opposing Pokémon. The stat boosts provided are considered an intrinsic part of the Z-Move's effect, rather than a distinct Z-Power Effect, ensuring they interact normally with move-modifying abilities such as Simple and Contrary. Clangorous Soulblaze is not a move that can be directly chosen in a battle. For Kommo-o, or a Pokémon transformed into Kommo-o, to unleash Clangorous Soulblaze, it must know Clanging Scales, be holding a Kommonium Z Z-Crystal, and its Trainer must be wearing a Z-Power Ring. It uniquely delivers offensive damage while simultaneously granting stat enhancements. In the animated series, Clangorous Soulblaze holds the distinction of being the sole Z-Move that debuted outside of the Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon arc. Although Z-Moves were discontinued from the core series as of Generation VIII, Kommo-o did receive a new signature move in that same generation called Clangorous Soul; due to its identical naming across all languages, this move can be viewed as a lesser variant of Clangorous Soulblaze, sharing the positive effect of increasing the user's stats but introducing the drawback of diminishing the user's HP.

Title: Zippy Zap (move)

Zippy Zap (Japanese: ばちばちアクセル Crackling Accel) is a damage-dealing Electric-type attack that premiered in Generation VII, appearing in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It serves as a unique signature technique for the partner Pikachu. Initially, Zippy Zap was described as inflicting damage and consistently landing a critical hit. It boasts a priority of +2, ensuring its execution before any moves that do not possess increased priority. However, this move cannot be chosen for use during battle. Its parameters have been subsequently altered: its Power Points (PP) were adjusted to 10, and its base power was set at 80. Furthermore, its modified secondary effect is a one-stage increase to the user's evasion, rather than a guaranteed critical hit. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!, the Move Tutor who teaches Zippy Zap can be found inside Cerulean City's Pokémon Center. Zippy Zap currently holds the distinction of being the final move when sorted alphabetically.

Title: Splishy Splash (move)

The move Splishy Splash (known in Japanese as ざぶざぶサーフ, or Splashing Surf) was introduced during Generation VII, making its appearance in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It functions as a damage-dealing Water-type attack. This technique is recognized as one of the signature moves exclusive to the partner Pikachu. In addition to inflicting damage, Splishy Splash carries a 30% chance of paralyzing its target. For trainers playing Let's Go, Pikachu!, the Move Tutor for Splishy Splash is located within the Pokémon Center in Fuchsia City. Notably, Splishy Splash holds the unique distinction of being the only Water-type move capable of inflicting paralysis. It may also be interpreted as an homage to Surfing Pikachu.

Title: Floaty Fall (move)

Known in Japanese as ふわふわフォール, Floaty Fall is a Flying-type attack that inflicts damage. It was first introduced during Generation VII in the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This technique ranks among the signature moves available to the partner Pikachu. When utilized, Floaty Fall not only causes harm but also carries a 30% chance of making the target Pokémon flinch. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!, trainers can find the Move Tutor who teaches Floaty Fall inside the Pokémon Center located in Celadon City. The move itself seems to pay homage to Flying Pikachu.

Title: Pika Papow (move)

Known in Japanese as ピカピカサンダー (Pikapika Thunder), Pika Papow functions as an Electric-type attack that inflicts damage, first appearing within Generation VII, specifically in the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This technique stands as a signature move and a "partner power" exclusive to the partner Pikachu. It functions as an auxiliary "fifth" move, and its activation requires shaking the game controller upon being prompted by a specific on-screen animated icon depicting Pikachu waving a Joy-Con. Not only does Pika Papow inflict harm, but it also completely bypasses accuracy checks, guaranteeing a hit every time. The foundational power stat of Pika Papow scales with the user's friendship level. An increased friendship value directly correlates to a higher base power for the move. Its base power ranges from a minimum of 1 (when friendship is at its lowest value of 0) up to a maximum of 102 (at the highest friendship value of 255).

Title: Bouncy Bubble (move)

Bouncy Bubble, recognized in Japanese as いきいきバブル (Lively Bubble), is a Water-type offensive maneuver. It was first introduced during Generation VII in the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This technique ranks as one of the partner Eevee's distinct signature moves. In addition to dealing damage, Bouncy Bubble simultaneously recovers 50% of the inflicted damage for the user.

Typically, Bouncy Bubble is unavailable for selection during combat. However, it has been modified: its Power Points (PP) were adjusted to 20, its base Power was set at 60, and the HP restoration effect was increased to 100% of the damage dealt to the adversary. The move cannot be performed if the user is under the influence of the Heal Block status condition.

For players of Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor for Bouncy Bubble can be found within the Pokémon Center in Cerulean City. Remarkably, Bouncy Bubble stands as the exclusive Water-type move that drains HP. Among all damage-dealing moves featured in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, it shares with Quick Attack the distinction of having the most variations, totaling eight distinct forms when including the base move itself.

Title: Buzzy Buzz (move)

An Electric-type attack known as Buzzy Buzz (which translates from Japanese as びりびりエレキ, or "Shocking Electricity") first appeared in Generation VII, specifically within the games Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, where it functions as a damage-dealing technique. This move is distinctive to the partner Eevee, being counted among its exclusive signature abilities. Upon a successful strike, Buzzy Buzz not only inflicts harm but also invariably leaves the opposing Pokémon paralyzed. Despite its inability to be chosen during actual combat, the move's attributes have undergone adjustments: its Power now stands at 60, and its PP (Power Points) has been set to 20. For players of Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, the specialized trainer capable of teaching Buzzy Buzz can be found within the Pokémon Center situated in Cerulean City. Although Buzzy Buzz remains unusable in the more recent Pokémon Scarlet and Violet titles, it is commemorated by one of the purchasable camera effects available in the League Club Room, a distinction it shares with the move Sizzly Slide.

Title: Sizzly Slide (move)

Known in Japanese as めらめらバーン (Blazing Burn), Sizzly Slide made its debut as a damaging Fire-type attack in Generation VII, specifically within the games Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move stands as one of the distinctive signature attacks of the player's partner Eevee. When successful, Sizzly Slide not only inflicts damage but also leaves the opposing Pokémon with a burn status condition. Despite its existence, Sizzly Slide is not a move that can be chosen during standard battles. Nevertheless, adjustments were made to its attributes: its Power Points (PP) were set to 20, and its base Power value was established at 60. For players of Let's Go, Eevee!, the specialized Move Tutor capable of teaching Sizzly Slide can be found within the Pokémon Center situated in Cerulean City. While Sizzly Slide itself is unusable in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, its legacy persists through an unlockable camera effect in the League Club Room, which bears its name. This particular characteristic is also shared by the move Buzzy Buzz.

Title: Glitzy Glow (move)

A Psychic-type, damage-dealing attack, Glitzy Glow (Japanese: どばどばオーラ Gushing Aura) debuted in Generation VII, specifically in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It stands as one of the partner Eevee's signature techniques. Glitzy Glow inflicts damage while simultaneously initiating the Light Screen effect on the user's side of the field. However, direct selection of Glitzy Glow in combat is not possible. Nevertheless, it received adjustments, with its power being altered to 80 and its accuracy to 95%. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor for Glitzy Glow is situated within Celadon City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Baddy Bad (move)

The Dark-type offensive move Baddy Bad (Japanese: わるわるゾーン Bad-Bad Zone) first appeared in Generation VII, within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It serves as one of the signature techniques exclusive to the partner Eevee. Baddy Bad not only inflicts damage but also establishes the Reflect effect for the user's side of the battlefield. Although it cannot be manually chosen during combat, this move's power was adjusted to 80 and its accuracy to 95%. In Let's Go, Eevee!, the Move Tutor who teaches Baddy Bad is situated in Celadon City's Pokémon Center.

Title: Sappy Seed (move)

The Grass-type attack known as Sappy Seed (Japanese: すくすくボンバー, translated as Fast-Growing Bomber) made its debut as a damage-dealing technique in Generation VII, specifically within the games Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This particular move is designated as one of the distinctive signature abilities of the partner Eevee. When used, Sappy Seed inflicts damage and additionally affixes a seed onto the opposing Pokémon. Initially, Sappy Seed possessed a power rating of 90, perfect 100% accuracy, and 15 PP. Despite these statistics, the move was originally unselectable during combat. Subsequent modifications, however, have adjusted its characteristics to 100 power, a 90% accuracy rate, and 10 PP. Within the game Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, trainers seeking to teach this move can find its dedicated Move Tutor stationed within the Pokémon Center in Fuchsia City.

Title: Freezy Frost (move)

Freezy Frost, known in Japanese as こちこちフロスト (Frozen-Stiff Frost), is an offensive Ice-type technique that first appeared in Generation VII. It was introduced with the release of Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, serving as one of the partner Eevee's signature moves. This attack both inflicts damage and resets the stat stages of all currently engaged Pokémon on the field to zero. Although Freezy Frost cannot be directly selected by players in battle, its attributes have been defined: it possesses a power of 100, an accuracy of 90%, and 10 PP. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, players seeking to teach this move can find its dedicated Move Tutor within the Pokémon Center located in Fuchsia City.

Title: Sparkly Swirl (move)

Known in Japanese as きらきらストーム (Sparkly Storm), Sparkly Swirl is a Fairy-type offensive technique that debuted in Generation VII, specifically within the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This particular ability serves as a signature move for the dedicated partner Eevee. Beyond inflicting damage, Sparkly Swirl offers a restorative benefit, completely eliminating all non-volatile status ailments—such as sleep, poison, paralysis, freeze, and burn—from the user, their entire active party, and any present allies. During combat, Sparkly Swirl is an inaccessible move via direct selection. Nevertheless, its attributes have undergone adjustments: its power rating stands at 120, its accuracy at 85%, and its PP count at 5. For trainers playing Let's Go, Eevee!, the individual capable of teaching Sparkly Swirl can be found within the Pokémon Center situated in Fuchsia City.

Title: Veevee Volley (move)

Known in Japan as ブイブイブレイク (Vuivui Break), Veevee Volley is a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage, making its debut in Generation VII within the titles Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. This move functions as both a unique signature technique and the dedicated "partner power" for the special partner Eevee. Functioning as a fifth slot ability, its activation is restricted to moments when the game displays an animated Eevee icon playfully waving a Joy-Con, requiring the player to shake their game controller. Veevee Volley consistently inflicts harm and is guaranteed to connect, as it completely ignores typical accuracy computations. The offensive strength, or base power, of Veevee Volley, hinges directly on the user's current friendship level. A higher degree of user friendship correlates with a more potent base power. This value can range from a minimum of 1 (when friendship is at its lowest point of 0) up to a maximum of 102 (achieved with the highest possible friendship value of 255). Should the underlying calculation for its power result in 0, the base power of Veevee Volley is instead fixed at 1.

Title: Double Iron Bash (move)

A Steel-type attack capable of striking multiple times, known as Double Iron Bash (Japanese: ダブルパンツァー Double Panzer), made its debut in Generation VII, specifically within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. It serves as the signature technique for the Pokémon Melmetal. When utilized, Double Iron Bash strikes the opponent two times, dealing damage. Each individual strike possesses a 30% probability of causing the foe to flinch, resulting in a combined 51% overall chance of flinching across both hits. Should a Focus Sash be triggered by a prior hit before the final strike lands, the subsequent hit is guaranteed to make the defending Pokémon faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its ability to activate multiple times to prevent the Pokémon from fainting, though each activation chance remains distinct. The Ability Sturdy will activate with every single hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is set to 1. Historically, if the opponent had used Minimize, Double Iron Bash would inflict twice its normal damage and ignore accuracy checks, ensuring a guaranteed hit, unless the target was in a semi-invulnerable state from moves like Dig or Fly. However, Double Iron Bash no longer possesses this unique interaction with Minimize. By index number, Double Iron Bash holds the position of the final Generation VII move. Its name in Pokémon the Series was bestowed by Professor Kukui. The Japanese title, ダブルパンツァー (Double Panzer), features a linguistic play on words, combining ‘Panzer’ (パンツァー, pantsaa) with ‘Punch’ (パンチ, panchi). This etymological connection is further reinforced by the move's classification as a punching attack. It stands as the sole move originating from Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! that maintains usability in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Furthermore, it is the only move from Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! to have made an appearance in the Pokémon animated series.

Title: Max Guard (move)

Known in Japanese as ダイウォール (Dai Wall), Max Guard is a Normal-type Max Move, introduced in Generation VIII, that inflicts no damage. Upon activation, it shields the user from incoming attacks for the remainder of the turn. This move provides superior defensive capabilities compared to many other protection-based techniques. Notably, it entirely blocks the majority of Max Moves and resists penetration or nullification by most moves typically designed to bypass protective effects. However, G-Max One Blow, G-Max Rapid Flow, and Feint are exceptions, inflicting their full damage; yet, Feint, unlike its usual effect on other defensive moves, does not remove Max Guard's protective barrier. While it still falls victim to many moves that typically ignore other forms of defense, Max Guard uniquely defends against Block, Flower Shield, Gear Up, Magnetic Flux, Phantom Force, Psych Up, Shadow Force, Teatime, Transform, and all contact moves influenced by the Unseen Fist ability. Should the user act last within a turn, Max Guard's activation will invariably fail. Its success rate diminishes with each consecutive successful use of Endure, any single-target protection move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Any Pokémon undergoing Dynamax or Gigantamax transformation gains access to Max Guard, provided it already knows a status move. Furthermore, Max Guard is a teachable move for all Dynamax and Gigantamax Pokémon. A distinct variant, known simply as Guard, is accessible to Crowned Sword Zacian, Crowned Shield Zamazenta, and Eternatus. Players are required to first unlock Max Guard, after which its power can be enhanced, reaching a maximum of Level 3. When Dynamax Cannon's Adventure Effect is active, Max Guard's level receives a temporary +1 boost, a benefit that does not apply to Zacian or Zamazenta. Should Max Guard be in a locked state, Dynamax Cannon will temporarily make it available at Level 1. During a Max Battle, deploying Max Guard grants a minor amount of temporary HP to the user, depicted visually as a shield icon adjacent to their HP bar. Each individual shield corresponds to 20 HP at Level 1, 40 HP at Level 2, 60 HP at Level 3, and 80 HP at Level 4 (which is achieved by a Level 3 Max Guard under the influence of Dynamax Cannon's Adventure Effect). Repeated use of Max Guard accumulates additional shields, capping at three, resulting in a combined maximum of 60 HP at Level 1, 120 HP at Level 2, 180 HP at Level 3, and 240 HP at Level 4. Furthermore, Max Guard draws the boss's aggression, causing it to preferentially target the user with single-target attacks rather than selecting a random opponent. When Guard is unlocked for Crowned Shield Zamazenta, it commences battle with an initial shield already active, instantly drawing the boss's focus. Uniquely, Crowned Shield Zamazenta can sustain up to four concurrent shields, surpassing the typical limit of three, yielding a combined maximum of 80 HP at Level 1, 160 HP at Level 2, and 240 HP at Level 3. Zamazenta's Level 3 Guard achieves the same peak shield capacity of 240 HP as a Level 4 Max Guard used by a Max Pokémon; this represents the highest achievable value, given Zamazenta's immunity to the Dynamax Cannon's Adventure Effect. Within the core series, Max Guard holds the distinction of being the sole Max Move capable of possessing a type different from its originating base move. It also stands as the inaugural Generation VIII move when sorted by index number.

Title: Dynamax Cannon (move)

Hailing from Generation VIII, Dynamax Cannon (known in Japan as ダイマックスほう Daimax Cannon) is a Dragon-type attack that inflicts damage. This move is a distinct signature attack associated with Eternatus. A special property of Dynamax Cannon causes its damage output to double if the Pokémon it targets is currently Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed. However, this increased damage bonus does not extend to Eternamax Eternatus, even though it shares visual characteristics with a Gigantamaxed Pokémon. Furthermore, if Dynamax Cannon was the last move executed by the target, the move Encore will consequently fail. Given the absence of the Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Dynamax Cannon's effect has been simplified to solely dealing damage, without any additional secondary properties.

Turning to Pokémon GO, Dynamax Cannon functions as a Charged Attack, having made its debut on August 17, 2025. Within Max Battles, Dynamax Cannon is employable as a Max Move and possesses the capability to be leveled up in this form. It is noteworthy that Dynamax Cannon cannot be swapped out for a different move through the use of Charged TMs. When utilized as an Adventure Effect beyond combat, it elevates the level of all Max Moves by 1, with the specific exceptions of Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash. Max Moves already at Level 3 will advance to Level 4, which grants a notably significant power boost compared to typical level increases; concurrently, any locked Max Moves will experience a temporary elevation to Level 1. This effect requires a cost of 5,000 and 30, and it persists for 10 minutes. Before Version 2.20.0*, the move's power received a 50% increase whenever the target possessed a sync buff.

Title: Snipe Shot (move)

Known in Japanese as ねらいうち (Snipe Shot), this offensive Water-type maneuver first appeared in Generation VIII, serving as the signature technique of the Pokémon Inteleon. Beyond inflicting harm, Snipe Shot possesses an elevated chance of landing a critical hit. Effects from other moves and Abilities designed to redirect attacks, such as Follow Me and Storm Drain, have no impact on Snipe Shot. Before the release of Version 1.2.0 for the games Sword and Shield, attempting to use Snipe Shot when randomly chosen by Metronome would result in its failure, given that Metronome itself targets the user. Furthermore, a specific glitch in Double Battles causes Snipe Shot to fail if it is initiated through the move Sleep Talk. Within Pokémon Masters EX, among all offensive actions requiring four move-gauge slots, Snipe Shot registers the lowest power.

Title: Jaw Lock (move)

Introduced in Generation VIII, the Dark-type attack Jaw Lock (Japanese: くらいつく, translated as "Seize with Teeth") deals direct damage. Until Generation IX, Chewtle and Drednaw were the exclusive users of this signature technique. While inflicting harm, it additionally establishes a trapping effect, preventing both the user and its target from fleeing battle or switching out. This binding effect applies to allies as well and remains until an affected Pokémon leaves the field, unless either Pokémon is already prevented from exiting. Nevertheless, it fails to impede switches caused by the Abilities Wimp Out or Emergency Exit, or by the effects of Shed Shell, Eject Button, or Red Card.

Title: Stuff Cheeks (move)

Emerging in Generation VIII, Stuff Cheeks (Japanese: ほおばる Stuff Cheeks) is a non-damaging Normal-type move. Prior to Generation IX, this particular move was exclusive to Skwovet and Greedent. Upon execution, the Pokémon consumes its currently held Berry, resulting in a two-stage increase to its Defense stat, alongside the Berry's usual effect. Notably, this action bypasses the effects of Unnerve and Magic Room, allowing the user to consume its Berry unimpeded. Should the user lack a held Berry, Stuff Cheeks becomes unavailable for selection from the move list; in such a scenario, if no other moves can be used, the Pokémon will resort to Struggle. Furthermore, if a Berry is absent at the moment of move execution (for instance, if it was removed by a move like Knock Off), Stuff Cheeks will invariably fail.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Greedent utilizes Stuff Cheeks as its second distinct ability. This skill is acquired when Skwovet evolves into Greedent at level 5, replacing Defense Curl rather than Covet. The Pokémon deposits multiple Oran Berries onto the ground, which it can then consume to restore HP. Any HP restoration from an Oran Berry consumed within a 5-second window that surpasses the user's maximum HP total is transformed into a temporary shield. Observing a Berry during the execution of this move grants a temporary surge in movement speed. Upon reaching level 11, the move's potency increases, causing it to drop an amplified quantity of Berries.

Title: No Retreat (move)

This Fighting-type maneuver, known in Japanese as はいすいのじん (Last Stand), made its debut in Generation VIII. It deals no damage. Falinks exclusively employs this unique technique. When used, No Retreat boosts the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed stats, each by a single stage. A consequence of using this move is that the Pokémon becomes unable to switch out or attempt to flee. Should the Pokémon already be trapped by a prior application of No Retreat, the move will fail. Conversely, if the 'Can't Escape' status has been inflicted by a different move, such as Mean Look, No Retreat will still apply its stat enhancements; however, it will not generate a new status entry to further restrict the user's escape. Under these specific circumstances, repeated uses of No Retreat will result in cumulative stat increases. Despite being trapped, the Pokémon can still depart the battle if it possesses a Shed Shell, if its Eject Button is triggered, if the opposing Pokémon's Red Card activates, or if an opponent employs a move like Whirlwind or Dragon Tail to force a switch. A Ghost-type user retains the ability to switch out; however, it will nonetheless acquire the 'Can't Escape' status on its summary screen, rendering subsequent uses of No Retreat impossible. Notably, within Pokémon Legends: Z-A, No Retreat possesses the longest cooldown period among all available moves.

Title: Tar Shot (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, Tar Shot (タールショット in Japanese) is a Rock-type utility move that inflicts no direct damage. This particular technique is exclusive to Coalossal, serving as its signature move. Upon use, Tar Shot inflicts two distinct effects: it reduces the opposing Pokémon's Speed stat by a single stage, and it causes Fire-type attacks to deal twice their usual damage to that target. It is important to note that the enhanced susceptibility to Fire-type moves does not stack; if a Pokémon is already under the influence of Tar Shot, subsequent uses will not further multiply Fire damage, though the Speed reduction may still occur. Furthermore, the heightened weakness to Fire-type assaults will not apply to a Pokémon that is already Terastallized. Conversely, should a Pokémon previously subjected to this increased vulnerability undergo Terastallization, the effect persists.

Title: Magic Powder (move)

Known as まほうのこな (Magic Powder) in Japanese, Magic Powder is a Psychic-type status move that first appeared in Generation VIII. This particular technique is exclusive to Hatterene. Upon successful execution, it alters the target's elemental affiliation to solely Psychic. The modified typing persists for the duration the Pokémon stays on the field; however, actions such as a form change, transformation, or Terastallization will nullify this alteration. This move cannot be used successfully if the opposing Pokémon is protected by a substitute, already possesses the pure Psychic typing, is currently Terastallized, or has the RKS System Ability. As a technique categorized by its powder nature, it will also fail against Grass-type Pokémon, those with the Overcoat Ability, or targets equipped with Safety Goggles. Uniquely among all powder-based attacks, Magic Powder stands alone as one that cannot be acquired by either a Grass-type or Bug-type Pokémon.

Title: Dragon Darts (move)

Dragon Darts (Japanese: ドラゴンアロー, Dragon Arrow) is a damaging Dragon-type multi-hit attack first introduced in Generation VIII. It serves as the exclusive technique of Dragapult. As a multi-strike maneuver, Dragon Darts inflicts damage through two separate blows. When employed against an opponent in a multi-foe battle scenario, the initial strike of Dragon Darts targets the designated adversary, while its second strike connects with the other opposing Pokémon. Should either the primary target or its ally be unaffected by Dragon Darts' damage, both successive attacks will instead be aimed at the remaining Pokémon. If an allied Pokémon is selected as the target, both impacts of the move will strike that ally. Wide Guard has no influence over Dragon Darts. The following circumstances, leading to Dragon Darts inflicting no damage, will cause it to redirect to the other Pokémon: if Dragon Darts would miss due to the user's accuracy or the target's evasion, or if Dragon Darts would miss due to targeting a Tatsugiri which is currently within its ally Dondozo's mouth. If both potential target Pokémon possess immunity to Dragon Darts, only one of these immunities will be registered. Should one of Dragon Darts' blows be re-aimed to avert a miss caused by accuracy and evasion, this will trigger the user's Blunder Policy (even if both hits successfully land on the alternate Pokémon). When an opponent becomes the sole focus of attention (for instance, via an ability like Follow Me), both impacts will strike that specific Pokémon, even if it holds immunity to Dragon Darts. The ability Pressure will deplete extra PP for each Pokémon hit by the move that also possesses this ability. Within a Max Raid Battle, when executed by the wild Pokémon, each individual strike of Dragon Darts will independently target two opponents, striking one Pokémon and subsequently another Pokémon positioned adjacently to the first target. Should the user possess Prankster and employ Dragon Darts through a move that invokes other attacks, such as Copycat, and one of the intended targets is Dark-type, its inherent type immunity is correctly recognized, resulting in the other target being struck twice. If a Focus Sash is triggered prior to the final strike, the subsequent strike will lead to the defending Pokémon's incapacitation. A Focus Band retains the capacity to activate multiple times to avert fainting, though each activation attempt is separate. Sturdy will take effect with every individual hit if the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. Dragapult's Unite Move in Pokémon UNITE, known as Dreep and Destroy, bears resemblance to Dragon Darts. In Generation VIII, Dragon Darts exhibited specific animation characteristics. The visual presentation of the move would vary under these conditions: if a Pokémon other than Dragapult executed the attack, or if a Shiny Pokémon utilized Dragon Darts, causing the accompanying Dreepy to also manifest as Shiny. This peculiarity was shared with Mind Blown and Syrup Bomb. However, with animation adjustments introduced in Generation IX, neither of these distinct visual effects can be observed any longer.

Title: Teatime (move)

First appearing in Generation VIII, the maneuver Teatime (Japanese: おちゃかい Tea Party) is classified as a non-damaging Normal-type attack. It functions as Polteageist's unique signature technique. Teatime's primary effect is to make all Pokémon on the battlefield consume their currently held Berry. This action successfully bypasses any active Substitutes. However, if a Pokémon has already eaten its Berry, Teatime will have no impact on that specific target. The Berry is ingested even if doing so would provide no actual benefit, such as a Mago Berry being consumed by a Pokémon already at maximum health. Should no Pokémon present on the field possess a held Berry, Teatime will completely fail, accompanied by the message "But nothing happened!". Teatime's compulsion for Pokémon to eat their Berries remains effective even in the presence of Abilities like Unnerve or the environmental effect of Magic Room. Conversely, Pokémon under the protection of Max Guard are immune to this move's influence. Similarly, Pokémon in the semi-invulnerable phase of another move are not affected by Teatime. If Teatime's elemental type is altered by Electrify or Plasma Fists, Pokémon possessing Volt Absorb, Lightning Rod, or Motor Drive will not expend their Berries; instead, they will activate the specific benefit of their Ability, regardless of whether they were actually holding a Berry at that moment.

Title: Fishious Rend (move)

Debuting in Generation VIII, Fishious Rend (Japanese: エラがみ Gill Bite) is a Water-type offensive attack. This technique functions as the exclusive move of the Fossilized Fish Pokémon, dealing damage to its opponent. The move's base power escalates to 170, effectively doubling, if the user acts before the target, or if the target switches into battle during the turn Fishious Rend is employed. Furthermore, the Ability Strong Jaw enhances its power by an additional 50%. Although it is often classified as a biting attack, its official description states that the Pokémon utilizes its gills to perform the attack.

Title: Court Change (move)

A non-damaging Normal-type move, Court Change (コートチェンジ in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VIII. It is one of Cinderace's signature techniques. The move causes any active battle effects to swap their sides of the field. This mechanism impacts protective screens such as Reflect, multi-turn tactical boosts like Tailwind, and deployed entry hazards like Spikes. Court Change does not, however, enable a Pokémon to transmit volatile or non-volatile status conditions to another, nor does it relocate any Pokémon's stat modifications. Effects that encompass the entire battlefield, such as weather conditions, terrains, or Trick Room, remain untouched by Court Change. When targeting a passive effect originating from a G-Max Move, for instance G-Max Volcalith, Court Change will successfully reassign it, provided the effect's influence is restricted to a single side of the field. In situations where an identical effect impacts both sides of the skirmish but possesses differing durations, Court Change will precisely exchange the respective remaining turn counts of these effects. An entry hazard transferred via Court Change will not take effect until a different Pokémon is subsequently sent out. Should a Sticky Web be moved back to its user's side of the field by Court Change, and a Pokémon with Defiant or Competitive then gets sent out onto it, these Abilities initially do not activate. However, Defiant or Competitive will now activate precisely because of a Sticky Web having been swapped back to its user's side of the field.

Title: Max Lightning (move)

Known in Japanese as ダイサンダー (Dai Thunder), Max Lightning is an Electric-type Max Move that deals damage, first appearing in Generation VIII. Beyond its damaging effect, it transforms the battle area into Electric Terrain, which persists for 5 turns, or 8 turns if the user is holding a Terrain Extender. The specific power value and whether it functions as a special or physical attack are determined by the characteristics of the Electric-type move it converts from. A Pokémon in its Dynamax form can execute Max Lightning provided it possesses a damaging Electric-type move. Its previous description, found in versions before 2.21.0 (as of May 26, 2022), simply stated: 'Turns the field into Electric Terrain.'

Title: Max Strike (move)

Hailing from Generation VIII, Max Strike (Japanese: ダイアタック Dai Attack) functions as a Normal-type Max Move that inflicts damage. Beyond its primary offensive role against a target, it concurrently decreases the Speed of all opposing Pokémon by one stage. Both its destructive force and its classification as either a special or physical attack are determined by the foundational move from which it originates. When Max Strike's base is Multi-Attack and the user holds a Memory, or Techno Blast with a held Drive, Max Strike transforms into a Max Move matching the type of that specific Memory or Drive. Similarly, if Weather Ball serves as its progenitor during active weather, Max Strike assumes the Max Move type that Weather Ball would take on in those atmospheric conditions. Following this pattern, should Terrain Pulse be its origin during existing terrain, Max Strike will become a Max Move of the type Terrain Pulse would manifest as under that terrain's influence. A Pokémon possessing Aerilate, Galvanize, Pixilate, or Refrigerate will cause Max Strike to convert into a Max Move whose type aligns with that specific Ability. Any Dynamax Pokémon is capable of unleashing Max Strike, provided it has a damaging Normal-type move in its repertoire.

Title: Max Knuckle (move)

Appearing in Generation VIII, Max Knuckle (Japanese: ダイナックル Dai Knuckle) functions as a damage-dealing Fighting-type Max Move. Beyond its direct damage, this Max Move provides a one-stage boost to the Attack stat for the user and all allied Pokémon. Its base power and whether it performs as a special or physical attack are contingent on the original move from which it is derived. Typically, its power rating is somewhat lower when compared to other available Max Moves. Any Dynamax Pokémon gains the ability to employ Max Knuckle, provided it knows a damaging Fighting-type attack.

Title: Max Ooze (move)

A Generation VIII debut, Max Ooze (Japanese: ダイアシッド Dai Acid) is a damage-dealing, Poison-type Max Move. In addition to damaging foes, it boosts the user's and its ally's Special Attack by one stage. Its power, and whether it functions as a special or physical attack, are contingent on the original move it transforms from. This move generally possesses less power than most other Max Moves. A Dynamax Pokémon may utilize Max Ooze if it has a damaging Poison-type move in its repertoire.

Title: Max Geyser (move)

Known in Japan as ダイストリーム (Dai Stream), Max Geyser is a Water-type Max Move that causes damage, initially introduced in Generation VIII. In addition to its damaging effects, this attack brings forth rain, lasting for 5 turns, or an extended 8 turns if the user is holding a Damp Rock. The specific power of Max Geyser, along with whether it operates as a special or physical attack, is contingent upon the Water-type move it transforms from. Any Pokémon currently in its Dynamax state is capable of utilizing Max Geyser, provided it knows a Water-type move that inflicts damage. It holds the unique distinction of being both the inaugural Max Move and the very first Generation VIII move showcased within the animated series.

Title: Max Rockfall (move)

Max Rockfall, known in Japanese as ダイロック (Dai Rock), made its debut in Generation VIII as a damage-inflicting Max Move of the Rock type. Beyond causing harm, this move also conjures a sandstorm effect, which persists for 5 turns, extending to 8 turns if the user is holding a Smooth Rock. The specific power level and classification as either a special or physical attack for Max Rockfall are determined by the originating move from which it is derived. To execute Max Rockfall, a Dynamax Pokémon simply needs to possess knowledge of any damaging Rock-type technique. Uniquely, when employed by Rose & Copperajah, Max Rockfall strikes all opposing Pokémon, a characteristic granted by their P-Moves & Max Moves Expansion passive skill.

Title: Max Quake (move)

Emerging in Generation VIII, Max Quake (known in Japanese as ダイアース Dai Earth) is a Ground-type Max Move designed to inflict damage. In addition to its offensive properties, this attack simultaneously elevates the Special Defense stat of both the Pokémon using it and its ally by one stage. The specific power and whether it functions as a special or physical move are directly tied to the original damaging move it's based on. Any Pokémon in its Dynamax form can unleash Max Quake, provided it knows a damaging Ground-type technique. Uniquely, when executed by Victor & Rillaboom or Rose & Copperajah, Max Quake strikes all opposing Pokémon, a benefit derived from their passive skills, Targets Maxed and P-Moves & Max Moves Expansion, respectively.

Title: Max Overgrowth (move)

First introduced in Generation VIII, Max Overgrowth is a potent Grass-type Max Move (known in Japanese as ダイソウゲン, or Dai Grassland) designed to inflict damage. Beyond its direct offensive capability, this attack transforms the combat area into Grassy Terrain. This terrain persists for 5 turns, a duration that extends to 8 turns if the Pokémon holds a Terrain Extender. Max Overgrowth's offensive power, along with its classification as either a special or physical move, is determined by the damaging Grass-type attack it originates from. Any Dynamax Pokémon can unleash Max Overgrowth, provided it knows an offensive Grass-type move. In the "Early-Evening Waves" episode, Milo's Eldegoss was revealed to be able to use Max Overgrowth, though its actual execution occurred offscreen. The move's Japanese name is likely a pun on 大草原 (だいそうげん), which translates to "prairie."

Title: Clangorous Soul (move)

Introduced in Generation VIII, Clangorous Soul (known as ソウルビート, or Soul Beat, in Japanese) is a Dragon-type technique that does not inflict damage. This ability stands as one of the distinctive moves associated with Kommo-o. When activated, Clangorous Soul enhances the user's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed by a single stage apiece. However, this power comes with a price, as it consumes 1/3 of the user's maximum HP. The move will fail to execute if the user's aforementioned stats have already reached their peak of +6 stages, or if their current HP falls to or below 1/3 of their maximum health. Despite the removal of Z-Moves from the core series starting in Generation VIII, Clangorous Soul bears a resemblance to a reduced form of Kommo-o's exclusive Z-Move, Clangorous Soulblaze. Their nomenclature across all languages displays notable similarity, and both techniques provide identical stat increases. Nevertheless, Clangorous Soulblaze differentiates itself by requiring no HP expenditure, inflicting damage on the adversary alongside its stat buffs, and being restricted to a single use per battle.

Title: Body Press (move)

First appearing in Generation VIII, the Fighting-type damage-dealing attack known as Body Press (ボディプレス in Japanese) is a formidable move. Players could acquire it as TR99 within Pokémon Sword and Shield; presently, it serves as TM089 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. While inflicting harm, Body Press distinguishes itself by calculating damage based on the user's Defense stat, completely bypassing the standard Attack stat. Crucially, while stage modifiers to the user's Defense stat are factored in (as opposed to those affecting Attack), all other Attack-related modifiers still contribute. This encompasses effects from held items, Abilities, and status conditions like burn. To illustrate, an Ability like Huge Power or a held Choice Band will indeed boost the stat utilized for Body Press's damage calculation. Conversely, Abilities such as Fur Coat or an item like Eviolite will have no such effect. Similarly, Abilities such as Slow Start or Defeatist are capable of diminishing the effective stat employed in this calculation. However, an Ability like Sword of Ruin does not similarly impact the user's calculation stat. Notably, Body Press stands alongside Life Dew as one of the scant few Generation VIII moves available for use in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Decorate (move)

A non-damaging Fairy-type maneuver, Decorate (Japanese: デコレーション Decoration) made its debut in Generation VIII. It stands as Alcremie's unique signature technique. The move's effect is to elevate the target's Attack and Special Attack stats, increasing each by two stages. Distinct from many other techniques, Decorate successfully penetrates defenses such as Protect, Detect, and even Max Guard to reach its intended target, yet it is rendered ineffective by Crafty Shield. The move is unable to impact opponents shielded by a substitute or those currently in a semi-invulnerable phase of another action. Within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, for versions spanning 1.0 to 2.0.2, Decorate was unusable in battle. As of Version 3.0.0, its use became possible once more, exhibiting identical functionality to its previous generation's performance.

Title: Snap Trap (move)

Snap Trap, known in Japanese as トラバサミ (Foothold Trap), is a Grass-type damage-dealing move introduced in Generation VIII. It stands as the signature technique of Galarian Stunfisk. Beyond its initial damage, Snap Trap inflicts a continuous drain of 1/8 of the target's maximum HP per turn for four to five turns. Furthermore, it ensnares the opponent, preventing them from switching out or escaping the battle. Should the move's user withdraw from combat, the trapped Pokémon is freed. If the user holds a Grip Claw, the duration of this effect is extended to a fixed 7 turns. Should the user be equipped with a Binding Band, the periodic damage dealt will increase from 1/8 to 1/6 of the target's maximum HP.

Title: Pyro Ball (move)

Known in Japanese as かえんボール (Flame Ball), Pyro Ball is a damaging Fire-type technique that was first introduced in Generation VIII. It stands as one of Cinderace's distinctive signature moves. Should the user be frozen, Pyro Ball first thaws them out before proceeding to inflict damage upon the target. Additionally, this attack carries a 10% chance of burning the foe. However, it exhibits no effect against Pokémon possessing the Bulletproof Ability.

In Pokémon GO, Pyro Ball functions as a Charged Attack, becoming available to players starting March 14, 2026. For Pokémon UNITE, it is designated as Cinderace's initial move. Players obtain it by evolving Raboot into Cinderace at level 7, choosing to upgrade Ember into Pyro Ball instead of Blaze Kick. The user unleashes a fiery projectile by kicking it in a chosen direction, incinerating and harming any adversaries in its path. Upon reaching level 11, successfully landing this move causes a reduction in the cooldown period for the user's alternate ability.

If Gloria (Alt. 2) & Cinderace employ a Fire-type sync move, their passive skill, Fuel Economy 2, adjusts Pyro Ball's cost to two move gauge slots. Conversely, when their sync move aligns with the Fighting-type, Gloria (Alt. 2) & Cinderace's Piercing Gaze passive skill guarantees that their use of this move will never miss.

Title: Behemoth Blade (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, Behemoth Blade (known as きょじゅうざん or Behemoth Slash in Japanese) is a Steel-type offensive attack. This move serves as Zacian's exclusive signature technique, fundamentally inflicting damage upon its target. Its destructive power is amplified, specifically doubling its damage, when employed against a Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed opponent. However, this damage bonus does not extend to Eternamax Eternatus, notwithstanding its resemblance to a Gigantamaxed Pokémon. Due to the absence of the Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Behemoth Blade currently functions solely as a damaging attack, devoid of any secondary effects. Should Zacian possess Iron Head upon entering combat in its Crowned Sword form, Iron Head transforms into Behemoth Blade for the entire encounter.

Within Pokémon GO, Behemoth Blade manifests as a Charged Attack, first appearing on May 29, 2025. In this game, it supplants Iron Head when Zacian undergoes transformation from its Hero of Many Battles form to its Crowned Sword form. Furthermore, Behemoth Blade is available for use as a Max Move in Max Battles, and can also be enhanced through leveling up in this capacity. It is not possible to substitute Behemoth Blade with an alternative move via Charged TMs. An Adventure Effect, introduced on June 24, 2025, grants an out-of-battle benefit: a 10% boost to the power of player Pokémon attacks during Raids, and a 5% boost during Max Battles. This effect requires an expenditure of 5,000 Stardust and 5 Zacian Candy, persisting for a duration of 6 minutes.

Before Version 2.20.0 (which launched on April 27, 2022), its description stated a 50% power increase when the target possessed a sync buff. When executed by Hop & Zacian, it strikes all opponents and guarantees a critical hit, attributed to their passive skill, The Sword That Saved Galar. The 6★ EX effect description, pre-Version 2.18.5 (released March 15, 2022), detailed an additional effect: the sync move would target all opponents. Earlier descriptions (before Version 2.20.0, dated April 27, 2022) also noted that the move's power would increase if the target had a sync buff.

In the context of Pokémon UNITE, Zacian's Unite Move, Sovereign Sword, shares similarities with Behemoth Blade. When executed by a transformed Pokémon, Behemoth Blade's animation is truncated, showing the Pokémon in a static, default stance. Historically, Behemoth Blade and Behemoth Bash were absent from the mobile version of Pokémon HOME's move Pokédex. This was because they are signature moves of Crowned Sword Zacian and Crowned Shield Zamazenta—forms that appear exclusively in battles and are thus non-depositable in HOME. Additionally, Smeargle could not learn them via Sketch, as Smeargle was not available in Pokémon Sword and Shield. Following Smeargle's inclusion in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, these two moves were eventually integrated into Pokémon HOME. This specific situation is mirrored by Glacial Lance and Astral Barrage for identical reasons. Furthermore, Struggle, which no Pokémon can legitimately acquire, and Light of Ruin, as Pokémon Legends: Z-A lacks compatibility with Pokémon HOME, share similar unique statuses regarding HOME's move data.

Title: Behemoth Bash (move)

Known in Japanese as きょじゅうだん (Behemoth Impact), Behemoth Bash is a Steel-type offensive maneuver first appearing in Generation VIII, which deals damage to opponents. It serves as Zamazenta's exclusive signature attack. When utilized against a Dynamaxed or Gigantamaxed target, the move's damage output doubles. However, this damage bonus does not extend to Eternamax Eternatus, despite its visual and mechanical similarities to Gigantamaxed Pokémon. Following the removal of the Dynamax mechanic in Generation IX, Behemoth Bash now functions purely as a damaging attack, devoid of any secondary effects. Should Zamazenta possess Iron Head upon entering combat in its Crowned Shield form, Iron Head will transform into Behemoth Bash for the entirety of that battle.

Within Pokémon GO, Behemoth Bash operates as a Charged Attack, which made its debut on May 29, 2025. In this mobile title, it replaces Iron Head when Zamazenta undergoes its transformation from its Hero of Many Battles form to its Crowned Shield form. Behemoth Bash is also usable as a Max Move in Max Battles and can be enhanced through leveling. This move cannot be swapped out for a different attack via Charged TMs. An Adventure Effect associated with Behemoth Bash, introduced on June 24, 2025, applies outside of combat. This effect decreases damage received by Pokémon by 10% during Raids and by 5% during Max Battles. Activating it requires 5,000 Stardust and 5 Zamazenta Candy, and its benefits persist for 6 minutes.

Regarding Pokémon Masters EX, the move's description prior to Version 2.20.0 (released on April 27, 2022) indicated its power increased by 50% when the target had a sync buff. A less specific description, also active prior to Version 2.20.0, simply stated its power increased if the target possessed a sync buff. Furthermore, this sync move was not capable of being raised to 6★ EX status until July 14, 2023 – a period of 1 year, 11 months, and 2 days following its debut on August 12, 2021.

Previously, Behemoth Bash, alongside Behemoth Blade, was absent from the move Pokédex within the mobile application of Pokémon HOME. This exclusion occurred because these moves are exclusive to Crowned Shield Zamazenta and Crowned Sword Zacian, forms that are battle-only and thus cannot be transferred into HOME. Additionally, Smeargle could not acquire these moves through Sketch in Pokémon Sword and Shield, as Smeargle itself was unavailable in those titles. Following Smeargle's inclusion in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, both Behemoth Bash and Behemoth Blade were subsequently integrated into Pokémon HOME. Glacial Lance and Astral Barrage faced the same situation for identical reasons. Moreover, Struggle and Light of Ruin also share this distinction, as neither can be legitimately learned by any Pokémon.

Title: Aura Wheel (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, the Electric-type damage-dealing attack known as Aura Wheel (オーラぐるま in Japanese) debuted. This move serves as Morpeko's exclusive signature technique. Beyond causing harm, Aura Wheel boosts the user's Speed stat by a single stage. The move's elemental type adapts according to Morpeko's active form. However, the Speed increase will not occur if the attack fails to connect, or if the target is invulnerable to the move, or shielded from it. Only Morpeko itself, or a Pokémon that has undergone transformation into Morpeko, can successfully execute Aura Wheel. Should any other Pokémon attempt to use this move, for example, via Mimic, it will invariably fail, accompanied by the battle message: "But <Pokémon> can't use the move!" With each use of Aura Wheel, Morpeko now undergoes a form change. Before Version 2.10.0 (released June 28, 2021), the description stated: "Raises the user's Speed. This attack becomes Electric-type when the user is in Full Belly Mode, and Dark-type when in Hangry Mode." Similarly, the description before Version 2.23.0 (dated July 27, 2022) specified: "Raises the user's Speed by one stat rank. While in Full Belly Mode, this attack turns Electric-type; in Hangry Mode, it becomes Dark-type." In its Battle-Only Form, specifically Hangry Mode, Morpeko's Aura Wheel will adopt the Dark type. The visual animation for this move draws inspiration from the mechanism of hamster wheels. Within the games Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Aura Wheel holds a unique distinction as the sole move capable of being learned by Sketch, yet simultaneously unusable by Smeargle itself.

Title: Breaking Swipe (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, Breaking Swipe (known as ワイドブレイカー Wide Breaker in Japanese) functions as a Dragon-type offensive maneuver that deals damage. Its Technical Machine designation was TM99 within Pokémon Sword and Shield, subsequently becoming TM222 for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This particular move inflicts damage upon every opposing combatant. Additionally, it simultaneously decreases the Attack stat of each engaged foe by a single stage. Notably, these stat reductions are independently applied to every combatant successfully struck. When utilized by Iris (Alt.) & Hydreigon, their unique passive ability, Piercing Gaze, guarantees that Breaking Swipe will never fail to connect. Before Version 2.10.0, implemented on June 28, 2021, its description simply read: 'Lowers the target's Attack.' Furthermore, prior to version 2.38.0, this 'buddy move' variant was designated as B Breaking Swipe. Within the mobile game Pokémon GO, Breaking Swipe functions as a Charged Attack, becoming accessible as of February 22, 2023. Notably, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, it stands as the sole Dragon-type move capable of being taught via a Technical Machine.

Title: Grav Apple (move)

The Grass-type attack Grav Apple, known as Ｇのちから (G-Force) in Japanese, made its debut in Generation VIII as a damage-dealing technique. This move is uniquely associated with Flapple, serving as its signature technique. When utilized, Grav Apple not only causes harm but also decreases the opponent's Defense stat by a single stage. Should the field condition Gravity be active, Grav Apple's fundamental power escalates to a formidable 120. Its conception appears to draw inspiration from the well-known anecdote regarding Isaac Newton's discovery of his gravitational theory, prompted by witnessing an apple's descent from a tree. Furthermore, the appellation "Grav Apple" cleverly combines the concept of gravity with "crab apple," a compact fruit often compared to a cherry due to its size.

Title: Life Dew (move)

Appearing in Generation VIII, Life Dew (いのちのしずく, or Life Droplets in Japanese) is a Water-type maneuver that deals no direct damage. Its primary function involves replenishing the hit points of the user and its active allies, restoring 25% of their respective maximum HP. Nevertheless, if an ally possesses the abilities Water Absorb, Storm Drain, or Dry Skin, these will activate, thereby neutralizing the move's beneficial effect on that particular Pokémon. While it is capable of healing allies protected by substitutes, Life Dew cannot influence a Pokémon during the semi-invulnerable stage of another action. This move's origins trace back to an ACE SPEC Item card within the TCG. Uniquely, alongside Body Press, Life Dew is among the select Generation VIII techniques available for use in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Obstruct (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, Obstruct (ブロッキング Blocking in Japanese) is a Dark-type technique that inflicts no damage. This move serves as Obstagoon's unique signature ability. When employed, Obstruct shields the user for that turn, nullifying all effects from both physical and special attacks directed at them. Should Obstruct successfully intercept a contact move, the attacking Pokémon's Defense stat will decrease by 2 stages. Notably, this Defense reduction occurs even if the Obstruct user possesses an immunity to the specific contact-making move. (Blocked moves, it's worth noting, do not trigger contact-dependent effects such as those from Rocky Helmet or the Rough Skin Ability.) The move will fail if the user's turn comes last. Furthermore, Obstruct's success rate diminishes with each consecutive, successful application of Endure, any user-only protection move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. With every such instance, the probability of success is subsequently divided by 3. Certain moves, however, are capable of inflicting damage despite Obstruct's protection. The majority of damaging Max Moves inflict only 25% of their base damage, with the exceptions of G-Max One Blow and G-Max Rapid Flow, which deliver their full damage. Attacks designed to bypass protective barriers, such as Feint, will cause full damage and simultaneously nullify Obstruct's effects. The Unseen Fist Ability provides a mechanism for contact moves to circumvent Obstruct entirely. Functioning as a high-priority move, Obstruct typically executes before the majority of other attacks during a turn. Before version 2.10.0, implemented on June 28, 2021, the move's description specified that if the attack was a physical one, it sharply lowered the Defense of the opposing Pokémon that used it. Within Pokémon GO, Obstruct is classified as a Charged Attack and became available starting August 13, 2022. Obstagoon's Pokémon Sword Pokédex entry indicates that shouting is integral to this technique, a detail visually confirmed by the user's shouting animation. Nevertheless, Obstruct is not categorized as a sound-based move.

Title: Eternabeam (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, Eternabeam (known as ムゲンダイビーム, or Infinity Beam, in Japanese) is a Dragon-type attack that deals damage. This technique is designated as one of Eternatus's exclusive signature moves. Upon successfully landing, Eternabeam delivers damage but subsequently compels the user to spend the following turn recharging. This recharge requirement is waived if the attack does not connect, is nullified by a type immunity, or is intercepted by a protective maneuver. Notably, Eternabeam holds the distinction of being the inaugural signature technique of a Legendary Pokémon to be subsequently withdrawn from the main game series. During Eternatus's execution of Eternabeam, its Eternamax form makes a fleeting appearance within the move's animation sequence. Aside from its encounter during the climactic battle in Pokémon Sword and Shield, this fleeting animation is the sole occasion where Eternamax Eternatus is observed. However, despite the visual manifestation of the Eternamax form throughout the move's duration, the game's internal mechanics do not register this as a genuine transformation. Consequently, Eternatus's inherent stats and its internal form index value persist without alteration, and the act of employing this move will not lead to Eternamax Eternatus being logged in a player's Pokédex. Eternabeam possesses a unique status as the singular move functioning as a standard technique in the core Pokémon series titles, yet classified as a Max Move within Pokémon Masters EX. Its designation inherently incorporates a nod to 'Eternamax' across all localized versions. Furthermore, in English, Japanese, Chinese, and Korean, the name also alludes to Eternatus itself.

Title: Steel Beam (move)

Originating in Generation VIII, the Steel-type attack known as Steel Beam (Japanese: てっていこうせん, translated as 'Beam of Steely Resolve') is classified as a damage-dealing maneuver. This move holds the Technical Machine designation TM170 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM157 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon using Steel Beam, the attacking Pokémon sustains self-inflicted damage equivalent to precisely half of its total HP, always rounded upwards. An exception to this rule applies to Pokémon possessing the Magic Guard ability, which negates this recoil. Notably, the user experiences this self-damage under various circumstances, including when its target is protected from the attack, if it strikes a substitute, or even if the move fails to connect entirely. However, no self-damage is incurred if Steel Beam fails to execute because no valid targets remain within range (for instance, if all opposing Pokémon have fainted prior to the move's activation). Its base power was adjusted from an initial 140 to 120. In its current iteration, Steel Beam is classified as a recoil-inducing move.

A specialized Move Tutor offers instruction for Steel Beam to all Steel-type Pokémon, with the singular exclusion of Dusk Mane Necrozma. Additionally, both the legendary Pokémon Zacian and Zamazenta are eligible to learn this technique from the tutor. Furthermore, Arceus and Silvally possess the unique ability to acquire this move, irrespective of their active type.

Beyond the standard move, certain sync pairs feature sync moves incorporating "Steel Beam" in their titles. It's important to note, however, that the animations for these sync moves are merely generic Steel Sync Beam visuals and do not derive from the actual move Steel Beam.

An earlier description, predating version 2.10.0 (released on June 28, 2021), stated: "The user takes damage equal to half of its maximum HP. The damage is taken even if the attack fails." Steel Beam holds the distinction of being the sole Steel-type move that inflicts harm upon its own user.

In April 2019, preceding the launch of Pokémon Sword and Shield, a competition organized by CoroCoro Comics in its May issue invited fans to suggest a name for the most potent Steel-type move. The Japanese designation for Steel Beam emerged as the victorious submission from this contest. The Japanese title for this maneuver, 徹底抗戦 (tettei-kōsen), literally translates to 'all-out resistance'. It ingeniously incorporates wordplay involving 鉄 *tetsu* (signifying iron or steel), 抵抗 *teikō* (meaning resistance), and 光線 *kōsen* (referring to a beam).

Excluding Max Moves, Steel Beam marked the debut of a Generation VIII move within the animated series, where it was showcased by Raihan's Duraludon during the episode 'Toughing It Out!'. Among all Steel-type Pokémon, Dusk Mane Necrozma stands as the solitary exception unable to acquire the move Steel Beam.

Title: Expanding Force (move)

The Psychic-type damage-dealing attack, Expanding Force (Japanese: ワイドフォース Wide Force), premiered in Generation VIII with The Isle of Armor. This move is listed as TM218 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Ordinarily, it targets just one Pokémon. Nevertheless, if Psychic Terrain is active and the user remains grounded during execution, the move gains a 1.5× power increase (stacking with the regular 1.3× terrain bonus) and expands its target to both opponents. Expanding Force, together with Scorching Sands, marked the final standard move to debut in Pokémon the Series.

Title: Scale Shot (move)

Known in Japanese as スケイルショット (Scale Shot), Scale Shot is a Generation VIII Dragon-type damaging attack, categorized as a multistrike move, and debuted specifically in The Isle of Armor expansion. Its Technical Machine (TM) designation is TM200 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM143 within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon execution, Scale Shot delivers damage by striking the opponent between two and five times. Provided the attack doesn't miss, the Pokémon utilizing Scale Shot experiences a one-stage increase in its Speed statistic, alongside a corresponding one-stage reduction in its Defense. Independently, every individual hit possesses an identical probability of scoring a critical hit. The distribution of hits is as follows: a 35% likelihood for two strikes, another 35% for three strikes, a 15% chance for four strikes, and finally, a 15% chance for five strikes. Taking into account the inherent 10% chance of missing, the calculated average overall base power for this move stands at 69.8. This move has the capability to persist its assault even after shattering a foe's Substitute. Only the final blow of Scale Shot is registered by the move Counter. Should the attacking Pokémon be equipped with a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every subsequent hit carries an independent chance to make the target flinch. A Pokémon possessing the Skill Link Ability will consistently deliver five strikes with Scale Shot, assuming the attack doesn't miss entirely. Should a Focus Sash be consumed prior to the delivery of the move's ultimate strike, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its potential to activate multiple times to avert a KO, though each instance of activation is entirely separate. For a defending Pokémon with a maximum HP of 1, its Sturdy Ability will trigger with every single strike received. When facing a target endowed with either the Weak Armor or Stamina Ability, each individual strike from Scale Shot will successfully activate that Ability. The move Scale Shot remains unaffected by the Ability Sheer Force.

Title: Meteor Beam (move)

Known in Japan as メテオビーム (Meteor Beam), this damage-dealing Rock-type attack was first introduced during Generation VIII within The Isle of Armor expansion. Trainers can teach it using TM220 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, or via TM156 within Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Upon selection, Meteor Beam immediately enhances the user's Special Attack stat by one stage, simultaneously displaying the notification "[User] is overflowing with space power!". The subsequent turn sees Meteor Beam unleash its damage, which benefits from the previously applied Special Attack increase. At this point, its PP is expended, and it registers as the most recently executed action. After Meteor Beam's initial selection, the Pokémon utilizing it becomes unable to withdraw from battle until the move is either interrupted or its full sequence concludes. Conditions such as sleep, freezing, being bound, or flinching will only temporarily halt Meteor Beam's progression without canceling it entirely. However, if the user holds a Power Herb, Meteor Beam's entire process can be completed within a single turn. In this scenario, the Pokémon charges and boosts its Special Attack, then consumes the Power Herb, enabling the immediate execution of the move's damaging phase. The Special Attack increase remains active because the charging component of the move is still carried out. Transitioning to Pokémon GO, Meteor Beam functions as a Charged Attack, having become accessible as of September 18, 2022. Specifically, when paired with Selene (Alt.) & Nihilego, their passive skill "Alola to You!" causes Meteor Beam to reduce each target's Special Defense by two stat ranks instead of one, provided the target is either poisoned or badly poisoned. This move's power amplifies proportionally with each Special Defense reduction on the target, gaining a 5% boost for every stat rank lowered, up to a maximum augmentation of 30%. Many Pokémon with extraterrestrial origins and all Fossil Pokémon acquirable in Pokémon Sword and Shield are capable of learning Meteor Beam.

Title: Shell Side Arm (move)

Known in Japanese as シェルアームズ (Shell Arms), Shell Side Arm is a damage-dealing Poison-type technique that was first introduced during Generation VIII, specifically within The Isle of Armor expansion. This particular move is exclusive to Galarian Slowbro, acting as its signature capability. Beyond inflicting direct damage, Shell Side Arm possesses a 20% chance of poisoning the target it strikes. A unique characteristic of Shell Side Arm is its ability to predict whether its base damage would be higher if executed as a physical move or a special move. It then automatically assumes the category (physical or special) that is anticipated to deal more harm, with a random selection occurring if both categories yield an identical damage forecast. For the purposes of this damage prediction only, stat modifiers other than stat stages are entirely disregarded. Moreover, the effects of Wonder Room are applied to any stat stage modifiers, but not to the Pokémon's original base stats themselves. Shell Side Arm visually changes its animation depending on whether it manifests as a physical or a special attack. If it operates as a physical move, it makes contact with the opponent; however, when it functions as a special move, it does not establish contact.

Title: Misty Explosion (move)

Misty Explosion, also known by its Japanese name ミストバースト (Mist Burst), is a damaging Fairy-type attack that debuted during Generation VIII, specifically in The Isle of Armor expansion. It can be found as TM201 within the Pokémon Scarlet and Violet games. This move deals damage, yet invariably causes the user to faint upon its execution. Its base power receives a significant boost under specific circumstances: if the user is grounded while Misty Terrain is active, Misty Explosion's power is multiplied by 1.5, reaching a formidable 150. However, the move is rendered ineffective, and the user avoids fainting, should a Pokémon possessing the Damp Ability be present on the field. Furthermore, when attempted in Max or Tera Raid Battles, Misty Explosion fails to activate, and the user does not collapse. Uniquely, alongside Self-Destruct and Explosion, Misty Explosion is one of only three offensive moves whose battle animation will fully display even if the attack fails to connect or has no discernible effect.

Title: Grassy Glide (move)

Grassy Glide (Japanese: グラススライダー, Grass Slider) is a Grass-type offensive move that debuted in Generation VIII, specifically within The Isle of Armor expansion. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is identified as TM194. This move inflicts damage upon its target. If Grassy Terrain is present and the user is grounded when initiating the move, its priority increases by one stage. Following the 2.0.1 update for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Grassy Glide's power was further lowered to 55.

In Pokémon UNITE, Grassy Glide serves as Tsareena's second move. Tsareena gains access to it at level 6 after evolving from Steenee, by choosing to upgrade Rapid Spin instead of Trop Kick. The move involves the user sliding in a chosen direction, harming any enemies encountered. This action is immediately followed by a retaliatory strike in the opposite direction, which both damages and shoves enemies. Should Tsareena's Queenly Majesty ability be active, this move will also provide her with a shield. At level 12, the damage output of this move is enhanced.

Grassy Glide holds the distinction of being the only move whose priority can fluctuate as a direct result of its own properties. Furthermore, its base power was adjusted from 60 to 55 in the 2.0.1 patch for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, making it the singular move to have its base power altered through a game update.

Title: Terrain Pulse (move)

Originating in Generation VIII with The Isle of Armor expansion, Terrain Pulse (だいちのはどう in Japanese) is a damage-dealing attack initially classified as Normal-type. By Generation IX, this technique became the exclusive signature move of the Smoliv evolutionary family. Its base classification defaults to Normal-type when no specific terrain effect is present. However, if the Pokémon using it is grounded and any form of terrain is active, Terrain Pulse's base power sees a significant increase, doubling to 100. This power surge operates independently of, and stacks with, any additional type-based boosts conferred by the active terrain itself.

The move's elemental typing and visual animation dynamically adapt based on the environmental terrain present during its execution. For instance, it becomes Electric-type when used on an Electric Terrain-affected battlefield. Similarly, it transforms into a Psychic-type attack under Psychic Terrain conditions. Pokémon possessing the Ability Mega Launcher grant Terrain Pulse a 50% power amplification. It remains impervious to the effects of Abilities like Normalize, Pixilate, Refrigerate, and Galvanize. Conversely, the status-inducing move Electrify does influence Terrain Pulse; when Electrified, it will always inflict Electric-type damage, irrespective of any active terrain. Notably, even under Electrify's influence, its power will still double to 100 if executed within an active terrain.

As a Max Move, Terrain Pulse initially presents as Max Strike. However, its actual type-based transformation into the suitable Max Move or G-Max Move occurs upon its execution, contingent on both the prevailing terrain and the user's grounded status at that precise moment.

Title: Burning Jealousy (move)

The Fire-type attack known as Burning Jealousy (しっとのほのお Flames of Jealousy in Japanese) first emerged in Generation VIII, specifically within The Isle of Armor expansion, and is categorized as a damage-dealing move. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is identified as TM195. This move strikes all adjacent adversaries, inflicting damage, and importantly, it will apply a burn status to any foe who has increased its stats during the current turn. Nevertheless, stat modifications resulting from the Imposter Ability are ignored when assessing whether to apply the burn. If a target is vulnerable to Burning Jealousy and holds a Weakness Policy, the check for burning the opponent is performed before the Weakness Policy is consumed. Should the user possess the Sheer Force Ability, the power boost associated with it is always applied, regardless of whether Burning Jealousy would have burned the target or not.

Title: Lash Out (move)

This Dark-type damage-dealing attack, known as うっぷんばらし (Vent Anger) in Japan, was first introduced in Generation VIII via The Isle of Armor expansion. In the Pokémon Scarlet and Violet games, it is designated as TM199. Lash Out delivers offensive damage, but its true potential emerges when the user's stats have been lowered within the same turn it is activated, causing its base power to double to an impressive 150. It is crucial to understand that this power augmentation does not apply when stat stages are modified directly by specific effects like Haze, Clear Smog, or Topsy-Turvy.

Title: Poltergeist (move)
Originating in Generation VIII's The Isle of Armor, Poltergeist (Japanese: ポルターガイスト Poltergeist) functions as a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage. Its Technical Machine designation in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet is TM198. Damage is only dealt by Poltergeist if the opposing Pokémon holds an item, preceded by the distinct message: "[target] is about to be attacked by its [item]!" Conversely, if the target lacks an item, the move consequently fails. Even when targeting a Pokémon with a Ghost-type weakness holding a Kasib Berry or Weakness Policy, or a Pokémon with a Red Card, Poltergeist's damage output occurs after the item is consumed, yet the item's effect remains active. Within the Pokémon GO mobile game, Poltergeist functions as a Charged Attack, becoming obtainable starting October 15, 2022. Notably, Poltergeist stands alone as the sole Ghost-type maneuver possessing an accuracy rating below 100%.

Title: Corrosive Gas (move)

Corrosive Gas (Japanese: ふしょくガス Corrosive Gas) is a Poison-type move, first introduced in Generation VIII's The Isle of Armor expansion, which deals no direct damage. Its primary effect is to strip targets of their held items, rendering them inaccessible for the remainder of the battle, regardless of whether the Pokémon is switched out. Items lost to Corrosive Gas cannot be retrieved through the moves Recycle or Harvest. This move, however, has no effect on items held by Pokémon with the Sticky Hold Ability, nor does it impact a Pokémon's substitute. Furthermore, it fails to remove specific key items: the Griseous Orb from Giratina, a Drive from Genesect, a Memory from Silvally, the Rusted Sword from Zacian, or the Rusted Shield from Zamazenta. Given that no Pokémon can naturally learn Corrosive Gas, its only in-game use comes via Metronome. The functional outcome of Corrosive Gas mirrors the secondary item-removal effect of Knock Off, as it has been since Generation V. Significantly, Corrosive Gas is the sole Move Tutor move from The Isle of Armor that remains unlearnable by any Pokémon in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Coaching (move)

Known as コーチング (Coaching) in Japanese, this Fighting-type, non-damaging technique debuted in Generation VIII's The Isle of Armor expansion. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Trainers can find it as TM213. Coaching serves to enhance the Attack and Defense stats of all allied Pokémon by one stage apiece. However, this effect does not apply to the move's user or any allies currently in a semi-invulnerable state during a move. Remarkably, it ignores all protective measures, such as Crafty Shield, to reach its allied targets. The move will be unsuccessful if employed in a Single Battle or in the absence of an ally.

Within the world of Pokémon UNITE, Coaching is among Mew's selectable second moves. Mew acquires this ability upon attaining level 3, opting for it instead of Light Screen or Agility.
