Activating Coaching causes Mew to dash towards an allied Pokémon, bestowing a shield upon it and accelerating its basic attack speed. With every deployment of Coaching, the cooldown duration of Mew's Electro Ball, Solar Beam, or Surf is shortened. Furthermore, should Coaching be applied to an ally that has not received its benefits for a predetermined period, the move's own cooldown will instantly reset. Upon reaching level 12, Coaching undergoes an upgrade to Coaching+, which consequently reduces both its overall cooldown and the specific waiting period required before it can be used again on a recently coached ally without re-triggering its cooldown.

Title: Flip Turn (move)

Known in Japanese as クイックターン (Quick Turn), Flip Turn is an offensive Water-type move that made its debut in Generation VIII, specifically with The Isle of Armor expansion. This move is listed as TM196 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and TM008 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. After inflicting damage, Flip Turn causes the user to withdraw from battle. However, this switch-out is prevented under several conditions: if the user is the only Pokémon in the party capable of battling, if Flip Turn fails to connect with its target, if it activates an opponent's Wimp Out or Emergency Exit Ability, or if its use brings the battle to an end. Furthermore, the user will not be switched out if it holds a Red Card or if the target is equipped with an Eject Button. When a player's Pokémon defeats an opposing Trainer's Pokémon using Flip Turn, the subsequent Pokémon sent out by the player will not be credited with participation for experience calculations. Nevertheless, if that replacement Pokémon holds an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense, the item's effect will be applied to the prize money calculation. In battles where switching is allowed, players retain the option to substitute Pokémon after an opponent's Pokémon faints. Notably, Flip Turn's user-switching effect occurs even when affected by hindering conditions such as Ingrain, Arena Trap, Magnet Pull, Shadow Tag, Mean Look, or Block. The term "flip turn" itself, also known as a "tumble turn," refers to a swimming technique employed in freestyle and backstroke events. Swimmers execute this maneuver to quickly and seamlessly pivot in the water and change direction. The French name for Flip Turn incorporates a clever wordplay on "eau" (water) and "au revoir" (goodbye).

Title: Triple Axel (move)

Known in Japan as トリプルアクセル, or Triple Axel, this damage-dealing Ice-type attack debuted in Generation VIII's The Isle of Armor expansion. Trainers can teach it using TM212 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, or TM144 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A.

This attack delivers up to three separate blows to the foe. The initial strike possesses a base power of 20, which then escalates by 20 for each subsequent hit, resulting in the second strike dealing 40 base power and the third a substantial 60. A distinct accuracy check governs each individual strike; should any of these checks fail, the entire move concludes prematurely. Given each strike’s 90% base accuracy (without accuracy/evasion modifiers), the probabilities for hitting are: a 72.9% likelihood of landing all three strikes (for a combined 120 base power), an 8.1% chance of connecting twice (totaling 60 base power), and a 9% probability of hitting just once (20 base power). Factoring in these accuracy rates and base powers, Triple Axel's average effective power calculates to 94.14.

The move interacts uniquely with certain game elements. Contact-based Abilities on the target, like Weak Armor or Stamina, can trigger with each individual strike. Similarly, if the target carries a Rocky Helmet, the user will suffer damage for every hit. However, a user with the Skill Link Ability or holding Loaded Dice bypasses individual accuracy checks, performing only a single one for the entire move. Should the initial hit fail due to an opponent's Disguise, that first contact is still registered as a strike, yet the power for the subsequent two hits will default to 20 and 40, respectively. In scenarios involving fainting prevention: if a Focus Sash activates prior to the final strike, the subsequent strike will inevitably knock out the defending Pokémon. A Focus Band, conversely, offers multiple independent chances to prevent fainting across the strikes. For Pokémon with a maximum HP of 1, Sturdy will consistently activate with every hit delivered by Triple Axel.

Notably, this move was formerly designated as B Triple Axel before reaching version 2.38.0. Shifting to Pokémon UNITE, Triple Axel serves as Tsareena's initial ability, unlocked when Steenee reaches level 5 and Razor Leaf is upgraded to it instead of Stomp. Here, the user executes three spin-kicks in a chosen direction, harming opponents. Successful hits accumulate 'Triple Axel Buff' stacks, which persist for 5 seconds and diminish simultaneously. While this buff is active, the user's basic attack speed improves based on the number of stacks (up to a maximum of 3). Furthermore, any damage inflicted by basic attacks or Triple Axel during the buff gains an extra 5% per stack (with a cap at 6 stacks). If Tsareena's Queenly Majesty is active, each kick provides healing to the user. Upon reaching level 10, the move's damage output further increases.

Distinctively, Triple Axel joins Triple Kick and Population Bomb as one of only three multistrike attacks where the accuracy check for subsequent hits operates independently of the first hit's success or failure. Its in-game description text mirrors that of Triple Kick. Its namesake likely derives from the figure skating maneuver, the Triple Axel jump. Intriguingly, despite its in-game flavor text describing it as a "consecutive three-kick attack," several Pokémon without feet are capable of learning Triple Axel, including Seel, Dewgong, Bellossom, Milotic, Froslass, Cryogonal, the entire Popplio evolutionary line, Frosmoth, and Tatsugiri.

Title: Dual Wingbeat (move)

A Flying-type, damage-dealing attack capable of striking multiple times, Dual Wingbeat (Japanese: ダブルウイング Double Wing) made its debut in Generation VIII as part of The Isle of Armor expansion. This technique can be learned via TM197 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and through TM134 in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. When used, Dual Wingbeat delivers two separate strikes, each of which deals damage. Both individual hits possess an independent and equal probability of landing as a critical hit. Should the initial strike shatter a target's substitute, the move will proceed to land its second hit. Only the second strike of Dual Wingbeat is registered by the move Counter. For targets possessing a contact-activating Ability or holding a Rocky Helmet, each of Dual Wingbeat's strikes registers independently, leading to two potential activations of the Ability or item. Should a Focus Sash be triggered by an earlier strike, the subsequent hit will still result in the defending Pokémon's incapacitation. A Focus Band, however, offers multiple chances to prevent fainting, with each instance being distinct. When facing a defending Pokémon with 1 maximum HP, Sturdy will activate for every hit taken from Dual Wingbeat. The wielder, if holding a King's Rock or Razor Fang, will have an independent and equal chance with each successful strike to make the opposing Pokémon flinch. Abilities such as Weak Armor or Stamina will be triggered by each individual strike of Dual Wingbeat.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Dual Wingbeat serves as an alternative second move for Scyther. To acquire it, Scyther must reach level 5 and select it as an upgrade for Fury Cutter, instead of opting for Bullet Punch. This choice prevents Scyther's evolution into Scizor. The move involves the user slashing forward with both scythes, inflicting damage upon enemy Pokémon within an X-shaped area of effect. Striking an opponent precisely in the center of this zone will restore the user's HP. Upon successfully hitting an enemy Pokémon, Dual Wingbeat can be activated again within a brief timeframe. Re-activating it causes the user to dash in a chosen direction, harming any opposing Pokémon encountered along the path. This dash's damage output increases proportionally to the target's missing HP. At level 11, Dual Wingbeat receives an enhancement, becoming Dual Wingbeat+, which boosts the move's damage output. Notably, Dual Wingbeat stands as the sole Flying-type attack capable of striking multiple times.

Title: Scorching Sands (move)

Known in Japanese as ねっさのだいち (Hot Sand Terrain), Scorching Sands is a damaging Ground-type attack. It made its debut in Generation VIII, introduced specifically within The Isle of Armor expansion. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, this move is assigned TM215. Beyond inflicting damage, Scorching Sands boasts a 30% chance of burning the target. A unique property allows the move to thaw its user if they are frozen upon execution, an effect that holds true even if the attack fails to connect. Moreover, any frozen opponent struck by Scorching Sands will also be defrosted. Notably, it was among the last standard moves to be introduced in Pokémon the Series, sharing this distinction with Expanding Force.

Title: Jungle Healing (move)

Jungle Healing (Japanese: ジャングルヒール Jungle Heal) is a Grass-type maneuver that does not inflict damage. It was brought into existence during Generation VIII, specifically as part of The Isle of Armor expansion. It functions as the signature move for the Pokémon Zarude. Upon activation, Jungle Healing restores 25% of the maximum Hit Points for the Pokémon utilizing it and any allies present. Furthermore, it remedies any non-volatile status conditions afflicting these Pokémon. The animated film Secrets of the Jungle depicted Koko employing Jungle Healing. This event marked an unprecedented occurrence, as it was the first time a human character performed a Pokémon move within the animated series.

Title: Surging Strikes (move)

Originating in Generation VIII with The Isle of Armor expansion, Surging Strikes (Japanese: すいりゅうれんだ Stream Strikes) is a Water-type multistrike attack that inflicts damage. It serves as Rapid Strike Style Urshifu's signature technique. Each application of Surging Strikes causes damage through three separate hits against the chosen target. Every individual strike invariably lands as a critical hit, unless the target Pokémon possesses either the Battle Armor or Shell Armor Abilities. Should a Focus Sash be triggered prior to the final strike connecting, the subsequent hit is guaranteed to knock out the defending Pokémon. A Focus Band, however, can repeatedly prevent a Pokémon from fainting; nevertheless, each opportunity for activation functions independently. Furthermore, Sturdy will take effect with every hit, provided the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is 1. Within the pages of Crackle!! Retrieve the Sword & Shield, it was disclosed that Mustard's second Urshifu — which was subsequently entrusted to Henry and given the nickname Arman — had mastered Surging Strikes following its evolution.

Title: Thunder Cage (move)

Thunder Cage (Japanese: サンダープリズン Thunder Prison) is an Electric-type attack that deals damage, first appearing in Generation VIII as part of The Crown Tundra expansion. It functions as the signature technique of Regieleki. Beyond its initial damaging effect, Thunder Cage afflicts the target with residual damage, subtracting 1/8 of their maximum HP each turn for a duration of four to five turns. Additionally, the move ensnares the opponent, preventing them from switching out or escaping the battle. The trapped Pokémon is freed immediately if the user of Thunder Cage departs the field. If the attacking Pokémon is equipped with a Grip Claw, the trapping effect will consistently last for 7 turns. Should the user be holding a Binding Band, the periodic damage inflicted will intensify from 1/8 of the target's maximum HP to 1/6. The design of Thunder Cage likely draws inspiration from 19th and 20th-century electromagnetic discoveries and devices, such as the Faraday cage or the Tesla coil.

Title: Dragon Energy (move)

A Dragon-type attack capable of inflicting damage, Dragon Energy (Japanese: ドラゴンエナジー Dragon Energy) made its debut in Generation VIII's The Crown Tundra expansion. This technique is the exclusive signature move of the Pokémon Regidrago. Its damage output begins at a considerable 150 base power when the user is at full HP; however, this power diminishes in direct proportion to the user's current health. More precisely, its base power is determined by the calculation ⌊150×HPcurrentHPmax⌋. Should this calculation result in a figure below 1, the move's base power is set to 1, regardless. When engaged in combat against more than one adversary, Dragon Energy strikes all neighboring foes.

Title: Glacial Lance (move)

Known in Japan as ブリザードランス (Blizzard Lance), Glacial Lance is an Ice-type attack that inflicts damage, debuting in Generation VIII within the confines of The Crown Tundra expansion. This technique is notably the signature ability of Ice Rider Calyrex. Its effect targets and harms all foes positioned next to the user. Within Generation VIII titles, when Ice Rider Calyrex unleashes Glacial Lance, its visual representation features a spear being hurled towards an icy mass. Conversely, should any other Pokémon employ this move, the animation shifts to depict a modest icy beam launching at the same ice chunk. To log Glacial Lance into the move Pokédex found in the mobile iteration of Pokémon HOME, the sole method involves a Smeargle having acquired the move via Sketch. This particular action became feasible in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet subsequent to The Indigo Disk DLC's introduction of Smeargle into those games. This characteristic is shared by several other moves: Astral Barrage, Behemoth Blade, Behemoth Bash, Ice Burn, and Freeze Shock.

Title: Astral Barrage (move)

Known in Japanese as アストラルビット (Astral Bit), Astral Barrage is a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage, debuting in Generation VIII with The Crown Tundra expansion. This particular move serves as the exclusive technique of Shadow Rider Calyrex. When employed, Astral Barrage strikes all foes positioned nearby. Among all Ghost-type attacks, Astral Barrage possesses a base power equal to Shadow Force, making them jointly the strongest, provided Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves are not considered. To document Astral Barrage within the move Pokédex of Pokémon HOME's mobile application, a trainer must utilize a Smeargle that has acquired the move via its Sketch technique. This method became feasible in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet subsequent to the introduction of Smeargle in The Indigo Disk DLC. This characteristic is shared by several other moves: Glacial Lance, Behemoth Blade, Behemoth Bash, Ice Burn, and Freeze Shock.

Title: Eerie Spell (move)

A Psychic-type attack causing damage, Eerie Spell (Japanese: ぶきみなじゅもん Eerie Spell) debuted in Generation VIII within The Crown Tundra expansion. It stands as a signature technique for Galarian Slowking. In addition to inflicting damage, this move diminishes the PP of the target's most recently used move in the current battle by 3, if such a move exists. The PP reduction, however, will not take effect if the target is knocked out by Eerie Spell. Being a sound-based move, Eerie Spell is capable of striking Pokémon even when they are protected by a substitute.

Title: Dire Claw (move)

Known in Japanese as フェイタルクロー (Fatal Claw), Dire Claw is a Poison-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation VIII with Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This particular technique is exclusive to Sneasler. Initially, Dire Claw would deal damage and boast an elevated critical-hit chance. Furthermore, it carried a 50% likelihood (or 80% when executed in the strong style) of afflicting the opponent with either poison, paralysis, or drowsiness. Subsequently, its base Power was adjusted, increasing from 60 to 80. In its modified form, the move now has a 50% probability of leaving the foe poisoned, paralyzed, or asleep (each status having an approximate 16.67% individual chance of occurring), while simultaneously losing its boosted critical-hit rate. Remarkably, Dire Claw stands alone as the sole Poison-type attack capable of inducing both paralysis and sleep.

Title: Psyshield Bash (move)

Introduced in Generation VIII's Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Psyshield Bash (Japanese: バリアーラッシュ Barrier Rush) is a Psychic-type attack that deals damage. It functions as the exclusive signature technique for both Stantler and Wyrdeer. This offensive Psychic-type move delivers damage while simultaneously elevating the user's Defense stat by one stage. For a Stantler to evolve into Wyrdeer, it must have utilized Psyshield Bash in the agile style a minimum of 20 times. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the move's in-game description claims it "may raise the user's defensive stats," despite this effect being a 100% guaranteed occurrence. Notably, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Psyshield Bash is unique among evolution-inducing moves originating from Pokémon Legends: Arceus for lacking its evolutionary effect, as Stantler cannot evolve into Wyrdeer in those specific games.

Title: Power Shift (move)

Debuting in Pokémon Legends: Arceus during Generation VIII, Power Shift (Japanese: パワーシフト Power Shift) is a Normal-type move that inflicts no damage. This maneuver causes the user to interchange their Attack and Special Attack stats with their Defense and Special Defense stats, respectively. Crucially, it swaps the raw numerical value of the user's Attack with their raw Defense stat, while any active stat modifiers remain unaffected by this shift. Although Power Shift is technically compatible with Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, no Pokémon naturally capable of learning it are present in those titles, nor is it ever selected by Metronome. This unique set of circumstances establishes Power Shift as the sole move legally playable in only a single game.

Title: Stone Axe (move)

Originating in Generation VIII through Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Stone Axe (Japanese: がんせきアックス Stone Axe) is a Rock-type offensive technique. It serves as Kleavor's signature move. Stone Axe delivers damage and features an increased critical-hit ratio. The attack also afflicts the target with splinters for varying durations: 4 turns when executed in Strong Style, 2 turns in Agile Style, or 3 turns in other scenarios. Beyond direct harm, Stone Axe establishes Stealth Rock hazards on the opposing side of the field. A Pokémon wielding the Sharpness Ability will see this move's power boosted by 50%. However, Stone Axe ultimately does not possess an increased critical-hit ratio.

Title: Springtide Storm (move)

Known in Japanese as はるのあらし (Spring Storm), Springtide Storm is a Fairy-type attack designed to inflict damage. This Generation VIII move made its debut in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, serving as the unique technique of Enamorus. While its fundamental purpose is to deal damage, the initial secondary effects of Springtide Storm varied depending on Enamorus's specific form. When employed by Incarnate Forme Enamorus, or by another Pokémon mimicking its usage, the move had a chance to enhance the user's offensive and defensive attributes. Conversely, if Therian Forme Enamorus unleashed it, a possibility existed for it to reduce the target's offensive and defensive stats. Springtide Storm's base power was later increased to 100. During multi-opponent skirmishes, this attack will strike all neighboring adversaries. As of a subsequent update, Enamorus's form no longer dictates its additional effect; it now consistently possesses a 30% chance to lower each target's Attack by one stage. Distinct from its counterparts, Bleakwind Storm, Wildbolt Storm, and Sandsear Storm, Springtide Storm's accuracy remains unaffected by rain, and its maximum PP did not undergo changes in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Mystical Power (move)

Originating in Generation VIII with its debut in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Mystical Power (Japanese: しんぴのちから, known as Mysterious Power) is a damage-dealing Psychic-type attack. This technique is exclusive to the lake guardians, serving as their signature move. When unleashed, Mystical Power inflicts damage and consistently raises the user's Special Attack by one stage. It also features a conditional stat alteration: if the total of the user's current Attack and Sp. Atk stats equals or exceeds the combined sum of its Defense and Sp. Def stats, its offensive stats will be raised after the move hits. In the alternative scenario, its defensive stats will be elevated.

Title: Raging Fury (move)

Originating in Pokémon Legends: Arceus during Generation VIII, the Fire-type attack known as Raging Fury (Japanese: だいふんげき, Great Infuriation) delivers damage to its target. Once initiated, it compels the Pokémon to repeatedly employ Raging Fury until either the battle concludes or a different action is selected. The move possesses a formidable base power of 120 and perfect accuracy at 100%. It sustains its offensive assault for two to three turns before its immense exertion leads the user into a state of confusion due to fatigue. Its execution can be interrupted if it fails to connect because of a miss, if the user is asleep, paralyzed, frozen, or flinching, or if the target is protected or immune to the attack. Notably, even if such a disruption takes place on the turn that would have concluded the move's duration and induced confusion, the Pokémon will nonetheless experience the confusion effect.

Title: Chloroblast (move)

Chloroblast (Japanese: クロロブラスト Chloroblast) is a damaging technique of the Grass type, first appearing in Generation VIII within the game Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It serves as the exclusive signature move of Hisuian Electrode. When employed, Chloroblast deals damage to an opponent, but also causes the user to suffer recoil damage equivalent to half of its maximum HP, rounded upwards. Furthermore, it diminishes the user's action speed. Chloroblast boasts a formidable base power of 150. Both this recoil damage and the reduction in the user's action speed are averted if the user possesses the Ability Magic Guard or Rock Head, or if the target's Ability is Sap Sipper. The name of this move likely originates as a play on "chloroplast," an organelle abundant in chlorophyll found in plant and algal cells, crucial for the process of photosynthesis. Chloroblast shares the distinction with Frenzy Plant of having the greatest base power among all Grass-type offensive moves, with the exception of Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves. Notably, Chloroblast stands alone as the sole move whose recoil is prevented by Rock Head, yet it does not receive a power bonus from the Reckless Ability.

Title: Mountain Gale (move)

Known in Japan as ひょうざんおろし (Iceberg Drop), Mountain Gale is an Ice-type offensive technique introduced in Generation VIII via Pokémon Legends: Arceus, serving as the signature move of Hisuian Avalugg. When executed, Mountain Gale deals damage while concurrently decreasing the user's action speed. Its Power Points (PP) were subsequently set to 10, and there is a 30% probability of causing the target to flinch. The move's Japanese designation, ひょうざんおろし "Hyōzan Oroshi" (Iceberg Wind), is notable for its inherent double meaning. The term "oroshi" can signify both a wind that descends a mountain (颪) and the act of dropping an object (下ろし). Support for the former interpretation is found in other language translations, which frequently connect the move to wind. Meanwhile, the latter meaning is visually confirmed by the move's animation, which illustrates large ice fragments being dropped upon the opponent.

Title: Victory Dance (move)

Introduced in Generation VIII with Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Victory Dance (Japanese: しょうりのまい Victory Dance) is a Fighting-type move that inflicts no damage. It serves as the exclusive signature technique for Hisuian Lilligant. Its mechanics align it as a physically-oriented equivalent to Quiver Dance. Upon execution, Victory Dance concurrently elevates the user's offensive and defensive attributes, alongside providing a substantial 50% increase to the damage output of the user's subsequent attacks. More precisely, it grants a one-stage augmentation to the user's Attack, Defense, and Speed stats.

Title: Headlong Rush (move)

Known in Japanese as ぶちかまし (Buchikamashi), Headlong Rush debuted as a damage-dealing Ground-type attack during Generation VIII, specifically within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Upon striking an opponent, this move subsequently reduces the user's defensive capabilities for a duration of three turns. More precisely, it deals damage before decreasing both the user's Defense stat and Special Defense stat, each by one stage. Excluding Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves, Headlong Rush shares the distinction with Precipice Blades of possessing the greatest base power among all Ground-type techniques. The Japanese name, ぶちかまし (Buchikamashi), originates from sumo wrestling, where it describes the head-on impact between two rikishi at the commencement of a bout. Its Ground typing likely draws inspiration from a linguistic interplay involving 武 *bu* (signifying bravery or courage), ち *chi* (referring to ground), ちか *chika* (meaning underground), and 噛まし *kamashi* (a blow or hit). This wordplay is also a probable explanation for Headlong Rush being classified as a punching move, and consequently, its learnability by Pokémon such as Hariyama. Interestingly, Charizard's forward smash attack in Super Smash Bros. Ultimate bears the same name, Headlong Rush, despite Charizard being unable to acquire this move in the primary Pokémon game series. It's worth noting that Super Smash Bros. Ultimate predates Headlong Rush's debut as an actual move in the core Pokémon titles.

Title: Barb Barrage (move)

Known in Japanese as どくばりセンボン (Thousand Stingers), Barb Barrage made its debut as a damaging Poison-type attack during Generation VIII, specifically within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This move serves as the signature technique for Qwilfish, Hisuian Qwilfish, and Overqwil. Beyond dealing damage, it initially carries a 30% likelihood of inflicting poison on the opposing Pokémon, with its offensive strength doubling when the foe is already afflicted by a non-volatile status ailment. Its Power Points (PP) have been subsequently adjusted to 10. Further iterations show it possesses a 50% probability of poisoning the target, and its power receives a twofold boost if the opponent is currently afflicted with poison.

Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, a Hisuian Qwilfish becomes capable of evolving into Overqwil after executing Barb Barrage in the strong style a minimum of 20 times. Conversely, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Hisuian Qwilfish's evolution into Overqwil occurs upon gaining a level while Barb Barrage is part of its known moveset. Looking ahead to Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the method for a Hisuian Qwilfish to evolve into Overqwil will involve Barb Barrage successfully striking 20 distinct targets.

Title: Esper Wing (move)

A damaging Psychic-type technique, Esper Wing (known as オーラウイング Aura Wing in Japanese) was first introduced in Generation VIII, making its debut in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It functions as the exclusive signature move for Hisuian Braviary. Initially, Esper Wing caused damage while benefiting from a boosted critical hit ratio and also elevating the user's action speed. In its current iteration, the move possesses a base power of 80 and a flawless 100% accuracy. Alongside its damage output, it still features an increased critical hit rate and now improves the user's Speed by one stage. Prior to the Version 2.10.0 update on June 28, 2021, its description indicated a very minor chance of confusing the opposing Pokémon.

Title: Bitter Malice (move)

Bitter Malice (Japanese: うらみつらみ, or Bitter Grudge) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type attack first introduced during Generation VIII, making its debut in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It functions as the signature technique exclusive to Hisuian Zorua and Hisuian Zoroark. In its original iteration, Bitter Malice delivers damage and carries a 30% chance (elevating to a 50% chance when employed in the strong style) to leave the opposing Pokémon with frostbite. Moreover, its power in that game would double if the target was already suffering from a status condition. Later adjustments changed Bitter Malice's PP to 10 and its base Power to 75. These revisions also removed its ability to gain increased power from status-afflicted foes; instead, it now consistently lowers the target's Attack stat by one stage. Notably, its Japanese name, Urami Tsurami, constitutes a rhyme, suggesting a potential linguistic pun.

Title: Triple Arrows (move)

This Fighting-type, damage-dealing attack, Triple Arrows (Japanese: ３ぼんのや Triple Arrows), made its debut in Generation VIII, specifically within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It functions as the exclusive technique of Hisuian Decidueye. Beyond simply dealing damage, Triple Arrows provides several additional effects: for a duration of three turns, it boosts the user's critical hit ratio by two stages while simultaneously diminishing the target's defensive stats. Its current specifications include a PP of 10 (which represents a reduction), a base power of 90 (an increase), and an inherently heightened critical hit rate. Furthermore, this move carries a 50% likelihood of decreasing the target's Defense stat by one stage, alongside a 30% probability of causing the target to flinch.

Title: Infernal Parade (move)

Originating in Pokémon Legends: Arceus during Generation VIII, Infernal Parade (its Japanese name being ひゃっきやこう Infernal Parade) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type attack. This specific move serves as the signature technique of Hisuian Typhlosion. When employed, Infernal Parade not only inflicts damage but also carries a 30% chance of leaving a burn on the target. Its power doubles if the opposing Pokémon is already affected by a status condition. Infernal Parade holds the unique distinction of being the only Ghost-type move able to induce burns. The Japanese appellation, ひゃっきやこう Hyakki Yakō, alludes to the idiom 百鬼夜行 hyakki yakō (literally "Hundred demons traveling at night"), a phrase from Japanese folklore that finds a conceptual parallel in the English word "pandemonium."

Title: Ceaseless Edge (move)

Ceaseless Edge, a Dark-type, damage-dealing move, debuted in Generation VIII with Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Known in Japanese as ひけん・ちえなみ (Secret Sword - Thousand Waves), it serves as the exclusive signature technique for Hisuian Samurott. This attack inflicts damage and is noted for having an increased critical-hit ratio. Moreover, it afflicts the target with splinters, which persist for 4 turns when used with Strong Style, 2 turns with Agile Style, or 3 turns otherwise. Beyond its direct offensive capability, Ceaseless Edge also deals damage and deploys a single layer of Spikes on the opposing side of the battle arena. When employed by a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness Ability, its power receives a 50% boost. Nevertheless, it does not ultimately feature an increased critical-hit ratio.

Title: Bleakwind Storm (move)

Known in Japanese as こがらしあらし (Wintry Wind Storm), Bleakwind Storm is a Flying-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation VIII within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It serves as the unique signature move of Tornadus. Beyond merely inflicting damage, this attack carries a 30% probability of afflicting its target with frostbite, a likelihood that increases to 50% when executed in the strong style. Its base statistics were adjusted, establishing its PP at 10 and its power at 100. Furthermore, Bleakwind Storm now possesses a 30% chance to decrease the Speed of each affected opponent by one stage. In combat scenarios featuring multiple adversaries, Bleakwind Storm is designed to strike all adjacent foes. Moreover, when unleashed amidst rainfall, the move guarantees a hit by circumventing typical accuracy calculations, provided the target isn't undergoing the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Regarding its presence in Pokémon GO, Bleakwind Storm functions as a Charged Attack and has been accessible to players since January 10, 2024.

Title: Wildbolt Storm (move)

An Electric-type damage-dealing attack, Wildbolt Storm (Japanese: かみなりあらし Thunderous Storm) made its debut in Generation VIII's Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It stands as Thundurus's signature move. Originally, it dealt damage with a 30% chance to inflict paralysis upon the target. Later revisions set its PP to 10 and its power to 100, and it now possesses a 20% chance of paralyzing each target struck. In battles against multiple adversaries, Wildbolt Storm targets all adjacent opponents. When unleashed during rainfall, Wildbolt Storm bypasses all accuracy checks, ensuring an unavoidable hit, except if the target is engaged in a semi-invulnerable turn from moves such as Dig or Fly. Within Pokémon GO, this is a Charged Attack available since January 17, 2024.

Title: Sandsear Storm (move)

Known in Japanese as ねっさのあらし (Hot-Sand Storm), Sandsear Storm is a damage-dealing Ground-type attack introduced during Generation VIII, specifically in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This technique serves as the signature move of Landorus. Initially, Sandsear Storm inflicted damage and presented a 30% likelihood of burning the opponent. Subsequent updates increased its PP to 10 and boosted its base power to 100, while also revising the chance to burn each affected target down to 20%. In battles featuring multiple adversaries, Sandsear Storm targets all adjacent foes. When activated during rain, this move bypasses typical accuracy checks, ensuring a hit unless the target is in the semi-invulnerable turn of another move, such as Dig or Fly. For Pokémon GO players, Sandsear Storm is a Charged Attack that became available starting January 24, 2024. Curiously, the description of Sandsear Storm within Pokémon Legends: Arceus states it "leaves the target with a burn," which inaccurately implies its secondary effect is guaranteed. This particular descriptive error does not appear in the text for its variations.

Title: Lunar Blessing (move)

Hailing from Generation VIII, specifically Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Lunar Blessing (Japanese: みかづきのいのり Crescent Prayer) is a non-damaging Psychic-type move. It stands as one of Cresselia's signature techniques. This maneuver offers several effects: it restores 25% of the user's maximum HP and alleviates any non-volatile status conditions. Crucially, these restorative benefits also extend to the user's allies, healing them for a quarter of their maximum HP and clearing any non-volatile status ailments. Furthermore, Lunar Blessing creates a shroud around the user, increasing the probability of incoming attacks missing their target. The move's Power Points (PP) have been adjusted to 5.

Title: Take Heart (move)

Known in Japanese as ブレイブチャージ (Brave Charge), Take Heart made its debut as a non-damaging Psychic-type attack in Generation VIII, specifically introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This move is recognized as the signature ability for Phione and also stands as one of the signature moves associated with Manaphy. Upon activation, Take Heart grants the user a dual benefit: it concurrently boosts their Special Attack and Special Defense stats by one stage each, and it remedies any non-volatile status conditions they may be afflicted with. These stat enhancements persist for a duration of four turns. Notably, Take Heart holds the distinction of being the final Generation VIII move when sorted by its index number.

Title: Tera Blast (move)

Tera Blast (Japanese: テラバースト Tera Burst) is a Normal-type damaging move first appearing in Generation IX. It is TM171 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move inflicts damage, becoming physical if the user's Attack stat exceeds Special Attack while Terastallized; otherwise, it remains special. For this stat comparison, only stat stage modifiers apply; other effects like held items (e.g., Choice Band) or Abilities (e.g., Huge Power) are ignored. The determination occurs at execution, not selection, meaning its category can change mid-turn, for instance, if an opponent acts first with a move like Charm.

When Terastallized, Tera Blast's type matches the user's Tera Type, is immune to type-changing moves and Abilities, and features a type-specific animation (unless the Tera Type is Normal, where the animation is consistent regardless of Terastallization). Gale Wings does not boost the priority of a Flying-type Tera Blast. If Terastallized into the Stellar type, it deals supereffective damage to Terastallized targets and neutral damage to others. Additionally, its base power becomes 100 (up from 80), and it lowers the user's Attack and Sp. Atk by one stage. When not Terastallized, Abilities like Galvanize can affect Tera Blast. However, if the user Terastallizes into a Normal type, Tera Blast remains Normal.

Despite being composed of Terastal energy and central to the Terastal phenomenon (as referenced by 'Rainbow Jewel Tera Blast' and 'Ancient Indigo Disk Tera Blast'), Terapagos cannot learn Tera Blast. It can, however, use it in Pokémon Masters EX, an acknowledged game move error. By index number, it is the first Generation IX move. It is also the sole move with varying descriptions based on its type. Its creation subtly links Glimmet and Glimmora to the Terastal phenomenon, as eight Glimmet Crystals are needed to craft its TM.

Title: Silk Trap (move)

Debuting in Generation IX, the non-damaging Bug-type technique, Silk Trap (Japanese: スレッドトラップ Thread Trap), was introduced. Spidops possesses this signature technique. For the duration of the turn it is employed, Silk Trap safeguards its user from all effects stemming from targeted physical and special moves. A successful block of an attack that would make contact with the user results in the attacker's Speed stat being lowered by one stage. It's important to note that when moves are blocked this way, effects contingent on direct contact, such as Rocky Helmet or Rough Skin, do not trigger. Should the user be the last to act within a turn, the move will fail. Furthermore, the likelihood of Silk Trap succeeding decreases with each consecutive successful use of Endure, any protective move solely affecting the user, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. In each subsequent instance, its success rate is divided by 3. Not all attacks are fully nullified; certain moves can inflict damage through Silk Trap's protection. For instance, moves designed to break protection, like Feint, will bypass Silk Trap entirely, dealing full damage and canceling its ongoing effects. Moreover, contact attacks used by a Pokémon with the Unseen Fist Ability can bypass Silk Trap. As a high-priority technique, Silk Trap typically activates ahead of the majority of other attacks within a given turn.

Title: Axe Kick (move)

Hailing from Generation IX, Axe Kick (Japanese: かかとおとし Axe Kick) stands as a damaging-dealing Fighting-type move. It carries a 30% probability of confusing the target in addition to inflicting damage. Should Axe Kick fail to connect, the user sustains crash damage equivalent to half of their maximum HP. Furthermore, the user will also take crash damage if this move is deployed against a protected target or one that is immune to it. This move holds the distinction of being the first crash damage move introduced since Generation I.

Title: Last Respects (move)

First appearing in Generation IX, Last Respects (known in Japanese as おはかまいり, or Visit to the Grave) is a Ghost-type attack that inflicts damage. Its foundational base power begins at 50, but this value escalates by an additional 50 for each Pokémon on the user's team that faints during the ongoing battle. Notably, Pokémon that are revived and subsequently faint once more also contribute to this increment. The move's base power reaches its pinnacle at 5,050, a level achieved after 100 Pokémon have fainted on the user's side within a single encounter. This establishes Last Respects as the move capable of reaching the highest base power of any attack. Furthermore, it is unique in being the only move whose base power can exceed a three-digit length.

Title: Order Up (move)

Making its debut in Generation IX, Order Up (Japanese: いっちょうあがり Order Up) is a Dragon-type attack that deals damage. This specific technique is Dondozo's signature move. Order Up causes harm to its target. Should the user be holding a Tatsugiri in its mouth, facilitated by the Commander Ability, Order Up's visual effects undergo a transformation, and the user's statistics are bolstered, with the increase varying depending on Tatsugiri's particular form. The move's potency is enhanced by the Sheer Force Ability, even when Commander is inactive. Any resulting stat buffs, if granted, take effect as usual. Prior to the release of Version 1.2.0 for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, a peculiar behavior caused Order Up to raise the stats of a Dondozo holding a Tatsugiri, even if the attack itself was prevented from landing (such as by Protect). Such an occurrence was attributed to a glitch and was corrected in the Version 1.2.0 update.

Title: Population Bomb (move)

Named ネズミざん (Proliferation) in Japanese, Population Bomb is a Normal-type attack capable of striking multiple times, first appearing in Generation IX. This move is uniquely associated with Tandemaus and Maushold. When used, Population Bomb delivers blows to the opponent, potentially striking them as many as ten times. Every individual strike of Population Bomb undergoes its own distinct accuracy check. Should any strike miss, the entire attack immediately concludes. When the user holds Loaded Dice, a single accuracy check determines the outcome, and the attack will land a total of four to ten times, with an equal probability for each number within that range. A Pokémon possessing the Skill Link Ability will execute this move with just one accuracy check, guaranteeing it strikes the full ten times. When employed by a Pokémon with the Sharpness Ability, Population Bomb's power receives a 50% boost. Should the target possess a contact-activating Ability, each successive strike is counted separately, allowing that Ability to trigger repeatedly. If the target holds a Rocky Helmet, the user sustains damage with every single strike delivered. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to be knocked out. A Focus Band, however, can still repeatedly trigger to avert fainting, though each activation chance remains entirely independent. The Sturdy Ability will trigger with every hit received, provided the defending Pokémon's maximum HP is exactly 1. Under standard conditions, without any changes to accuracy or evasion, Population Bomb boasts an approximate average effective power of ~117.24 and an average of ~6 hits per use. The probabilities of it landing at least a given number of strikes are detailed as follows: Alongside Triple Kick and Triple Axel, Population Bomb stands as one of only three multi-hit attacks where the miss chances for subsequent strikes are not reliant on whether the initial hit connected. The Japanese designation, ねずみざん (nezumizan), carries a dual meaning: it can refer to ねずみ算, "mouse mathematics" (an arithmetic problem illustrating multiplication akin to mice breeding rapidly), or ねずみ斬, "mouse cut." This linguistic duality elucidates why the Sharpness Ability enhances the move's potency. The English name for this attack might draw inspiration from the 1968 publication, The Population Bomb by W, a book that erroneously foretold widespread famine and fatalities globally due to overpopulation anticipated in the 1970s.

Title: Ice Spinner (move)

Ice Spinner (Japanese: アイススピナー Ice Spinner) is an offensive Ice-type move that debuted in Generation IX. For Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, this technique is available as TM124. When used, Ice Spinner deals damage and simultaneously clears any active terrain from the battlefield. However, the terrain will not be removed if the user faints from its held Life Orb, the opponent's held Rocky Helmet, or the foe's Ability Rough Skin or Iron Barbs. Similarly, if the user is forced to switch out by the target's Red Card, the terrain effect persists. Intriguingly, this move, which involves the user "covering its feet in thin ice," can be learned by the entire evolutionary lines of Seel, Shellder, Pineco, Dunsparce, Bronzor, Popplio, and Mareanie, alongside Glalie, Froslass, Cryogonal, Hisuian Sliggoo, and Frosmoth—a notable fact since these Pokémon do not possess feet.

Title: Glaive Rush (move)

Emerging in Generation IX, Glaive Rush (known in Japanese as きょけんとつげき, or Giant Sword Assault) is a Dragon-type attack that inflicts damage. This technique serves as Baxcalibur's exclusive signature move. Upon successfully landing, this maneuver leaves the user vulnerable until their subsequent action: all incoming attacks, including OHKO moves, will inflict twice the normal damage (though this does not apply to direct damage moves) and will universally connect, ignoring all accuracy and evasion parameters. Should the user employ any other move, this self-imposed vulnerability immediately ceases. The inspiration for Glaive Rush possibly stems from the movie *Godzilla vs. Hedorah*, where Godzilla propels itself similarly with its atomic breath. This particular scene was conceived by director Yoshimitsu Banno as an assumed homage to Gamera. Banno later initiated a Gamera project that was ultimately abandoned but then re-envisioned, becoming the *2014 Godzilla* film.

Title: Revival Blessing (move)

Originating in Generation IX, the Normal-type move Revival Blessing (known in Japanese as さいきのいのり Recovery Prayer) inflicts no damage. When utilized, Revival Blessing enables the player to select a fainted Pokémon from their party, which subsequently gets revived with half of its maximum HP restored. This move fails if no fainted Pokémon are present in the party. Sketch is unable to replicate Revival Blessing. Furthermore, Revival Blessing cannot be used if the Pokémon employing it is under the effect of Heal Block caused by Psychic Noise. Excluding Struggle, Revival Blessing holds the distinction of being one of only two moves, the other being Sketch, whose base Power Points (PP) are not a multiple of 5. Neither Sketch nor Revival Blessing can have their maximum PP value augmented. Pawmot and Rabsca are the sole Pokémon capable of learning this particular move; they acquire it upon evolving, a process that requires them to level up outside of their Poké Ball after having accumulated 1,000 steps via the Let's Go! feature. Even Smeargle is unable to learn Revival Blessing, given that Sketch is unsuccessful in copying it. Within battle, Revival Blessing stands alone as the only game mechanic capable of reviving a fainted Pokémon without the use of items from the Bag. This makes it the exclusive game mechanic for reviving a Pokémon during player-versus-player battles in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Salt Cure (move)
Introduced in Generation IX, Salt Cure (Japanese: しおづけ Salting) is a Rock-type move that causes damage. It serves as the signature ability for Naclstack and Garganacl. When utilized, Salt Cure not only inflicts immediate damage but also applies a residual effect, subtracting 1/8 of the target's maximum HP each turn. However, this recurring damage intensifies to ¼ of their maximum HP per turn if the afflicted Pokémon possesses the Steel type, the Water type, or both. This continuous drain ceases when the Pokémon is switched out of battle. Notably, Salt Cure's effect cannot be transferred via Baton Pass. Additionally, Team Star's Starmobiles are impervious to the move's secondary effects.

Title: Triple Dive (move)

Triple Dive, identified in Japanese as トリプルダイブ (Triple Dive), is a damage-dealing Water-type multistrike move that debuted in Generation IX. Wugtrio is the sole Pokémon capable of learning this distinctive signature attack. Upon execution, Triple Dive inflicts damage by striking the target three separate times. Should a Focus Sash activate prior to the final strike being delivered, the subsequent hit will inevitably cause the defending Pokémon to faint. Conversely, a Focus Band possesses the capability to trigger repeatedly to avert a knockout, with each individual chance operating independently. If the defending Pokémon has a maximum HP of 1, the ability Sturdy will be activated by every single hit from Triple Dive.

Title: Mortal Spin (move)

Mortal Spin (Japanese: キラースピン Killer Spin), a Poison-type offensive technique, made its debut in Generation IX. This move deals damage while concurrently clearing the user of any active binding effects, Leech Seed status, and entry hazards present on its side of the battlefield. This cleansing effect is negated if the user faints from the move's recoil or from an ability like Rough Skin. Furthermore, Mortal Spin will inflict the poisoned status condition on every target it hits. When engaged in a multi-opponent skirmish, it will strike all adjacent foes.

In the episode Over the Top!, Geeta's Glimmora was noted to possess Mortal Spin. However, before it could unleash the attack, Liko's Floragato intervened with a Sucker Punch, prompting Geeta to issue a command for Spiky Shield instead. The Japanese designation for Mortal Spin, キラースピン Kirā Supin, likely incorporates a double entendre, merging キラキラ kirakira (an onomatopoeia for sparkling) and キラー kirā (killer), mirroring the linguistic origins of Glimmora's Japanese name. Interestingly, its localization into French, Italian, and Chinese emphasizes the "glittering" aspect over the "killer" connotation.

Title: Doodle (move)

Debuting in Generation IX, Doodle (Japanese: うつしえ Trace) is a Normal-type status move that inflicts no damage. This technique is exclusive to Grafaiai. When utilized, Doodle changes the Abilities of the Pokémon employing it and any allied Pokémon to match the targeted Pokémon's Ability. Doodle will, however, fail if the target, the user, or the user's ally possesses any of the following Abilities: As One, Battle Bond, Comatose, Commander, Disguise, Gulp Missile, Ice Face, Multitype, Poison Puppeteer, Power Construct, Protosynthesis, Quark Drive, RKS System, Schooling, Shields Down, Stance Change, Zen Mode, or Zero to Hero. An additional failure condition occurs if the target holds the Ability Receiver, but not if Receiver is held by the user or its ally.

Title: Fillet Away (move)

In Generation IX, the non-damaging Normal-type move Fillet Away debuted. Its Japanese name is みをけずる (Cut to the Bone), and it serves as Veluza's exclusive signature move. Utilizing Fillet Away requires the user to sacrifice half of its maximum HP (rounded down) from its current HP. This costly action, however, simultaneously bestows a two-stage increase to the user's Attack, Special Attack, and Speed stats. The move will fail to execute under two specific conditions: if the user's current HP is already less than or equal to half its maximum, or if its Attack, Special Attack, and Speed stats have all reached their maximum of +6 stages. Regarding its etymology, the Japanese name みをけずる (Mi o Kezuru) directly translates to 'to chip away at one's body.' This phrase carries the figurative implication of 'enduring severe hardships,' often for the sake of achieving a particular objective. Its European Spanish counterpart, Deslome, presents a similar linguistic duality, being interpretable as both 'cutting fish fillets' and 'wearing one's back out,' typically stemming from excessive toil.

Title: Kowtow Cleave (move)

Originating in Generation IX, Kowtow Cleave (known as ドゲザン, or Kowtow Slash, in Japanese) is an offensive Dark-type maneuver. This specific attack serves as the exclusive signature move for Kingambit. Kowtow Cleave always strikes its intended target, bypassing typical accuracy checks; its only failure to connect occurs if the foe is temporarily invulnerable during a move's effect, such as Dig or Fly. Should a Pokémon with the Sharpness Ability perform this action, its overall power is enhanced by 50%. The move's thematic basis is 'dogeza,' a Japanese act of kneeling to show respect to a person of higher status. However, the word "kowtow" itself more accurately describes a similar ceremonial act found in Chinese culture.

Title: Flower Trick (move)

A Grass-type damaging ability, Flower Trick (Japanese: トリックフラワー Trick Flower) made its debut in Generation IX. Meowscarada possesses this move as its exclusive signature technique. Flower Trick deals damage and bypasses all accuracy checks, ensuring it invariably hits its mark. The sole exceptions are when the target is in the semi-invulnerable state of another move, such as Dig or Fly. Furthermore, this attack will always result in a critical hit, unless the opposing Pokémon is protected by the Battle Armor or Shell Armor Abilities. In the realm of Pokémon GO, Flower Trick is classified as a Charged Attack and became available starting January 5, 2025.

Title: Torch Song (move)

Known as フレアソング (Flare Song) in Japan, Torch Song is a Fire-type offensive move that made its debut in Generation IX. It serves as the signature attack for Skeledirge. This technique is categorized as sound-based. Upon use, it not only deals damage but also increases the user's Special Attack by one stage. Remarkably, Torch Song can affect Pokémon even when they are positioned behind a Substitute. In the mobile title Pokémon GO, Torch Song functions as a Charged Attack, having been accessible since March 8, 2025. It is the sole Fire-type move that is also sound-based.

Title: Aqua Step (move)

Aqua Step, known in Japanese as アクアステップ (Aqua Step), is an offensive Water-type technique that deals damage, first introduced in Generation IX. Quaquaval employs this as its exclusive signature move. When Aqua Step successfully connects, it causes harm to the target and simultaneously enhances the user's Speed by a single stage. Notably, Clavell & Quaquaval's P-Move Stat Raises ×2 passive skill amplifies this effect, resulting in their Speed increasing by two stat ranks after each deployment. In Pokémon GO, Aqua Step operates as a Charged Attack, having been made available on July 20, 2025.

Title: Raging Bull (move)

Known in Japanese as レイジングブル, Raging Bull is a damage-dealing Normal-type attack that debuted in Generation IX. This technique serves as Tauros's exclusive signature move. Raging Bull clears Light Screen, Reflect, and Aurora Veil from the target's side of the field—an effect that occurs even when targeting an ally—before it deals damage. However, if the attack fails to connect, these protective screen effects are not dispelled. Should Paldean Tauros be the user, its type shifts based on its form. The Combat Breed's Raging Bull manifests as Fighting-type, the Blaze Breed's as Fire-type, and the Aqua Breed's as Water-type. If employed by a Pokémon other than Paldean Tauros, the move remains Normal-type.

Title: Make It Rain (move)

Originating in Generation IX, Make It Rain (Japanese: ゴールドラッシュ Gold Rush) is a Steel-type, damage-dealing attack. It holds the distinction of being Gholdengo's signature move. When used, Make It Rain inflicts damage and simultaneously reduces the user's Special Attack by one stage. In encounters with multiple foes, this move targets all adjacent opponents. For every target successfully hit, coins are scattered across the ground. The monetary value dispersed each time equals $5 multiplied by the user's level. However, no coins are scattered during Tera Raid Battles. Should the player emerge victorious, they will collect all scattered coins, thereby receiving the total sum of money. The money obtained from Make It Rain is doubled if any of the player's Pokémon participates in the battle while holding an Amulet Coin or Luck Incense; this bonus, however, applies only once, irrespective of how many additional Pokémon possess such an item. Having multiple Pokémon hold these items will not further increase the money gained. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, coins generated by Make It Rain are instantly picked up. The move does not scatter coins when striking objects like Mega Crystals. Furthermore, the Gold Canari Plush has no effect on the amount of money earned from Make It Rain. Sygna Suit Lear & Gholdengo's passive skill, Golden Authority, prevents their Sp. Atk from being lowered after using this move. The English idiom "Make it rain" refers to either creating wealth or ostentatiously displaying it, often by throwing money into the air to give the appearance of falling currency. Consequently, despite its English name, this move does not actually summon rain. The English renaming of the move from "Gold Rush" may have been prompted by a potential naming conflict with G-Max Gold Rush, a G-Max Move introduced in Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Title: Psyblade (move)

The Psychic-type damage-dealing move known as Psyblade (Japanese: サイコブレイド Psycho Blade) was first introduced in Generation IX. It is the exclusive signature technique of Iron Leaves, and it causes direct damage. If Electric Terrain is active on the field, Psyblade's base power escalates to 120, irrespective of the user's grounded status. Furthermore, when employed by a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness Ability, the move's power is boosted by 50%. Notably, among the unique moves of the six Paradox Pokémon associated with Legendary Pokémon, Psyblade is the sole one that does not align with its user's primary type, namely Grass. This move also holds the distinction of being the only non-Electric-type attack to benefit from a power enhancement due to Electric Terrain.

Title: Hydro Steam (move)

Hydro Steam (ハイドロスチーム Hydro Steam in Japanese) is a damage-dealing Water-type move that debuted in Generation IX. It is the signature technique of Walking Wake. When activated under harsh sunlight, Hydro Steam's power increases by 50%. This effect is unique, as all other Water-type moves would instead have their damage reduced by 50% in such conditions. Should a Pokémon holding a Utility Umbrella use Hydro Steam during harsh sunlight, it bypasses the 50% damage boost and is instead subject to the standard 50% damage reduction for Water-type moves. Hydro Steam stands alone as the only non-Fire-type move to gain a power advantage from harsh sunlight.

Title: Shed Tail (move)

Known in Japanese as しっぽきり (Tail Shedding), Shed Tail is a Normal-type move that causes no direct damage, making its debut in Generation IX. Upon use, Shed Tail forms a substitute, which consumes an amount of the user's maximum HP equal to half, rounded upwards. Immediately after, the user is switched out of battle. This newly generated substitute possesses health equivalent to 1/4 of the user's maximum HP. The Pokémon entering the field subsequently gains possession of this substitute. Should the user be equipped with a Berry that would activate due to the HP loss from Shed Tail (for example, a Sitrus Berry), that Berry is consumed prior to the user's departure from battle. Execution of Shed Tail is unsuccessful (preventing the user's switch-out) under specific conditions: if the user currently possesses a substitute, if no allied Pokémon are available for a switch, or if the user's current HP stands at or below 50% of its maximum capacity. It is important to note that the substitute offers no defense to the Pokémon entering play against hazards placed on the field. The move's Japanese designation, しっぽきり (Shippokiri), derives from the idiomatic phrase 蜥蜴の尻尾切り (tokage no shippo kiri), literally 'cutting lizard's tail,' which conveys the meaning of delegating one's duties or accountability to someone of lower rank.

Title: Chilly Reception (move)

Making its debut in Generation IX, Chilly Reception (known as さむいギャグ, or Cold Gag, in Japanese) is an Ice-type maneuver that inflicts no damage. This particular action is Slowking's exclusive move and also serves as one of Galarian Slowking's unique capabilities. Upon execution, Chilly Reception generates a snowstorm across the arena and subsequently forces the user to withdraw. The snowy conditions brought about by this technique persist for a duration of five turns, extending to eight turns should the user be equipped with an Icy Rock. A user's forced departure from battle occurs regardless of whether snow is already present on the field at the time of the move's activation. Should there be no available allies for a swap, the maneuver's effect will solely be the alteration of the weather to snow.

When this move is chosen directly, a preliminary message appears prior to its execution: "<Pokémon> is preparing to tell a chillingly bad joke!", and this notification shows even if the move ultimately fails. However, if Chilly Reception is invoked indirectly via another move that calls it, the preceding message will not be shown. Across Japanese, Korean, and Chinese cultures, the term "cold joke" frequently denotes a jest lacking a proper punchline or one that elicits no amusement from its listeners. An unresponsive or unimpressed audience might metaphorically describe a sudden drop in room temperature. In English, a comparable comedic trope involves feigning the sound of crickets as the sole reaction to an unfunny anecdote. Media, especially comedic works, sometimes exaggerate this effect by portraying a room as literally chilling due to a terrible joke. Within the game, this cultural reference is manifested as the user conjuring a snowstorm before exiting the battle, ostensibly from embarrassment over its poor humor.

Title: Snowscape (move)

Known in Japanese as ゆきげしき Snowscape, this non-damaging, Ice-type maneuver debuted in Generation IX, taking the place of Hail from previous iterations. Players can find it as TM052 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Upon its activation, a snowstorm immediately blankets the battlefield. This atmospheric condition persists for five rounds. It dissipates any pre-existing weather phenomena but will not take effect if a snowstorm is already present. Nevertheless, overworld weather patterns remain unaffected. While the snowstorm rages, all Ice-type Pokémon experience a 50% boost to their Defense stat. Creatures possessing the Ice Body Ability regain 1⁄16 of their total HP every turn. Those with the Snow Cloak Ability benefit from a 20% increase in their evasiveness. Meanwhile, Pokémon featuring the Slush Rush Ability find their Speed attribute doubled. Should the Pokémon employing Snowscape be holding an Icy Rock when the move is executed, the snowstorm's duration extends from the standard 5 turns to 8 turns. The presence of the snowstorm enables the use of Aurora Veil. Under Snowscape's influence, healing moves like Synthesis, Morning Sun, and Moonlight restore merely ¼ of the user's total HP. Furthermore, the attack Weather Ball transforms into an Ice-type move.

Title: Twin Beam (move)

Known in Japan as ツインビーム (Twin Beam), this Psychic-type attack, which strikes multiple times, made its debut in Generation IX and inflicts damage. Exclusively, Girafarig and Farigiraf possess this characteristic attack. When employed, Twin Beam causes damage by striking the target a total of two times. The probability of either individual strike becoming a critical hit is identical. Should the initial strike shatter a substitute, Twin Beam's subsequent hit will still proceed. Mirror Coat specifically registers damage solely from the second impact of this attack. Should a Focus Sash be triggered prior to the final strike landing, the subsequent strike will inevitably lead to the defending Pokémon's incapacitation. A Focus Band retains its potential to activate multiple times to avert a KO, although each activation opportunity functions discretely. When facing a Shedinja, the ability Sturdy will be triggered by every single hit from Twin Beam. Should the user be equipped with either a King's Rock or a Razor Fang, every successive strike presents an identical likelihood of making the opponent flinch. For targets possessing the Stamina ability, each individual impact will cause it to activate. A Girafarig possessing knowledge of Twin Beam undergoes evolution into Farigiraf upon gaining a level. Notably, Twin Beam stands alone as the sole Psychic-type move capable of striking multiple times.

Title: Rage Fist (move)

Originating in Generation IX, Rage Fist (Japanese: ふんどのこぶし, Rage Fist) is a damage-dealing Ghost-type attack. It is the signature technique for Primeape and its evolved form, Annihilape. The power of this move escalates by 50 points each time the user sustains direct damage from an attack during the battle, culminating at a peak power of 350, achieved after six such impacts. This encompasses hits from both allied and opposing Pokémon, even extending to attacks that do not actually deplete HP, such as those that activate an ability like Disguise. However, Rage Fist's power does not augment if the user strikes itself in confusion, nor when a user's Substitute takes damage. Each individual strike of a multistrike move registers as a single hit. The accumulated hit count persists, unaffected by the user switching out or fainting. A Pokémon undergoing transformation copies the target's current hit counter.

To evolve Primeape into Annihilape, it must level up after executing Rage Fist a minimum of 20 times (with PP expenditure occurring for each activation, regardless of whether damage was inflicted). In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, this condition instead presents the choice to evolve. Any supplementary PP lost due to effects such as Pressure is not counted toward these usage totals. A specific detail for Pokémon Legends: Z-A is that Rage Fist need not connect with a Pokémon for Primeape's evolution criteria to be met. Within Pokémon GO, Rage Fist functions as a Charged Attack, having become available on November 10, 2024.

Title: Bitter Blade (move)

Originating in Generation IX, Bitter Blade (known in Japan as むねんのつるぎ, or Mortification Sword) is a Fire-type offensive maneuver designed to inflict damage. It serves as the unique signature move of Ceruledge. When employed, this attack deals damage to its target while simultaneously regenerating the user's health by an amount equivalent to half of the damage inflicted. Should the attacking Pokémon possess the Sharpness Ability, Bitter Blade's power is boosted by 50%. Furthermore, if the user is holding a Big Root, the HP recovery portion of the move is amplified by 30%, though this item provides no enhancement to the damage dealt itself. Conversely, if the adversary bears the Liquid Ooze Ability, the user will suffer a loss of HP equal to the amount it would have otherwise recovered. Bitter Blade stands as the sole Fire-type attack capable of draining an opponent's HP. Its debut in the animated series marked it as the first Generation IX move to be showcased. An appearance in "Riding the Waves" revealed that when applied to cut food, Bitter Blade extracts its nutrients, yet the food remains suitable for consumption.

Title: Double Shock (move)

Known in Japanese as でんこうそうげき (Double Lightning Attack), Double Shock is a damage-dealing Electric-type move introduced in Generation IX. This technique is exclusive to Pawmot as its signature attack. When executed by an Electric-type Pokémon, Double Shock strikes the opponent while simultaneously causing the user to shed its Electric typing. The move fails if the Pokémon attempting to use it lacks the Electric typing. Consequently, after an initial successful execution, Double Shock will consistently fail until the user recovers its Electric type, which can happen by withdrawing from battle or through other type-altering effects like Protean or Reflect Type. Should the user be a mono-Electric-type creature, it will subsequently possess no type. Upon being switched out of battle, the Pokémon's original, default types are restored. Creatures possessing the abilities Protean or Libero are unable to successfully execute Double Shock unless they are already Electric-type. If not, the attack will fail, and neither Protean nor Libero will trigger. However, a Pokémon that has undergone Terastallization to its Electric Tera Type will retain its Electric typing, despite the customary notification "The <wild/opposing> <Pokémon> used up all its electricity!" appearing. Thus, a Terastallized Pokémon is capable of deploying Double Shock repeatedly without needing to be withdrawn. Before game version 2.38.0, this specific buddy move was identified as B Double Shock. Accessing the summary screen of a Pokémon that has become typeless, for instance by employing Double Shock, carries a risk of the game crashing.

Title: Gigaton Hammer (move)

The Steel-type damaging move Gigaton Hammer (Japanese: デカハンマー Huge Hammer) debuted in Generation IX, serving as the signature attack of Tinkaton. A user cannot typically select Gigaton Hammer on consecutive turns unless the preceding attempt failed. If a trainer attempts to utilize Gigaton Hammer twice in a row, they will be prevented from doing so, and will instead be permitted to choose another move. Once an alternate move is performed, Gigaton Hammer becomes available for use again. In situations where the Pokémon cannot choose another move (for example, due to a Choice item or having no other usable attacks), it will resort to using Struggle on every other turn. However, the game mechanics Instruct and Sleep Talk enable Gigaton Hammer to be employed consecutively. Similarly, Encore permits Gigaton Hammer to be used twice in a row, though on subsequent turns under the Encore effect, the user will be compelled to Struggle. Gigaton Hammer boasts the highest base power of all Steel-type moves, excluding Z-Moves. Its animation changes when a Pokémon other than Tinkaton uses it. In such cases, the animation resembles those of Ice Hammer and Dragon Hammer, but features a silver hammer instead of their respective blue or purple.

Title: Comeuppance (move)

Introduced in Generation IX, Comeuppance (Japanese: ほうふく, meaning 'Retaliation') is a damage-dealing Dark-type move. It retaliates by inflicting 1.5 times the damage received from the adversary's most recent attack. Distinct from Counter or Mirror Coat, this move is not restricted to specific physical or special damage types, nor does it possess reduced priority. However, Comeuppance will fail if its user acts prior to being hit by an opponent's damaging technique, or if a Substitute takes the blow instead of the user itself. In multi-Pokémon encounters, Comeuppance targets the last opposing Pokémon that dealt damage to the user. It cannot impact allied Pokémon. Nevertheless, if the Pokémon that last hit the user is knocked out before Comeuppance is performed, the move will strike an available enemy Pokémon, behaving like a regular single-target damaging move. The damage calculation in this scenario still uses the damage originally taken from the fainted attacker's move. Its description text is identical to that of Metal Burst.

Title: Wicked Torque (move)

Introduced in Generation IX, Wicked Torque, known as ダークアクセル (Dark Accel) in Japanese, is a damage-dealing Dark-type move. It functions as the signature technique of the Segin Starmobile. When deployed, Wicked Torque inflicts harm and possesses a 10% probability of putting the target to sleep. The moves Encore, Instruct, Mimic, and Sketch are all rendered ineffective if Wicked Torque was the last action performed by the target. Additionally, Copycat will also fail under the condition that Wicked Torque was the most recently executed move. Its usage is exclusively confined to the Revavroom found within the Segin Squad's Starmobile.

Title: Magical Torque (move)

Known in Japanese as マジカルアクセル (Magical Accel), Magical Torque is a Fairy-type attack that deals damage, making its debut in Generation IX. This particular technique serves as the exclusive signature move of the Ruchbah Starmobile. Beyond simply inflicting damage, Magical Torque carries a 30% probability of leaving the opposing Pokémon in a state of confusion. Should Magical Torque be the last move utilized by a target, subsequent attempts to use Encore, Instruct, Mimic, or Sketch against it will prove unsuccessful. Similarly, Copycat also fails if Magical Torque was the immediately preceding move. Its use is restricted solely to the Revavroom found within the Ruchbah Squad's Starmobile. Before the launch of The Hidden Treasure of Area Zero expansion for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Magical Torque stood as the sole Fairy-type move introduced within Generation IX. Furthermore, it holds the distinction of being the only physical Fairy-type move that does not make contact, excluding Z-Moves and Max Moves, though it is not accessible in regular gameplay.

Title: Blood Moon (move)

Known in Japanese as ブラッドムーン, Blood Moon is a Normal-type attack that inflicts damage, debuting in Generation IX's The Teal Mask expansion. This technique serves as the exclusive signature move for Bloodmoon Ursaluna. Its primary function is to cause direct damage to an opponent. A Pokémon cannot choose to use Blood Moon on consecutive turns, with the sole exception being if the initial attempt to use the move was unsuccessful. Should a trainer attempt to employ Blood Moon two times without interruption, the game mechanics will prevent this, instead permitting the user to choose an alternative maneuver. After any other move has been executed, Blood Moon becomes available for selection once more. In situations where the user is unable to choose a different move (such as when holding Choice items or possessing no other viable attacks), it will resort to using Struggle on every alternate turn. However, specific moves like Instruct and Sleep Talk bypass this restriction, enabling Blood Moon's consecutive application. Encore similarly permits Blood Moon to be executed twice back-to-back, yet on all subsequent turns that Encore remains active, the Pokémon will be compelled to use Struggle instead.

Title: Matcha Gotcha (move)

Known in Japanese as シャカシャカほう (Rustle-Rustle Cannon), Matcha Gotcha is a Grass-type damage-dealing move that premiered in Generation IX within The Teal Mask. It serves as the exclusive signature move of Sinistcha. This technique inflicts damage and simultaneously restores the user's HP by an amount up to half of the damage it deals. Moreover, it carries a 20% chance of inflicting a burn on affected targets. In Double Battles, Matcha Gotcha targets both opposing Pokémon. If an opponent possesses the Ability Liquid Ooze, the user will lose HP equivalent to the amount it would have otherwise recovered. When deployed in a Double Battle against two opponents, if one opponent has Liquid Ooze, the user will first suffer damage from that specific ability before subsequently regenerating HP from the other opponent. Should the user hold a Big Root, the HP restored is boosted by 30%, leading to a total recovery of 65% of the damage inflicted. The Big Root item, however, does not augment the move's damage output. Upon activation, Matcha Gotcha will thaw the user if they are frozen. Similarly, any frozen target hit by the move will also be thawed. This HP-draining move is capable of striking all adjacent opponents without affecting friendly Pokémon. Interestingly, despite its Japanese name including ほう "cannon," it is not categorized as one of the ball and bomb moves.

Title: Syrup Bomb (move)

Making its debut in Generation IX with The Teal Mask expansion, Syrup Bomb (Japanese: みずあめボム Mizuame Bomb) is a Grass-type attack that inflicts damage. It serves as the signature move of Dipplin and is additionally recognized as one of Hydrapple's signature moves. Beyond dealing immediate damage, Syrup Bomb also causes the opponent's Speed stat to drop by one stage at the end of each of the subsequent three turns. However, foes possessing the Bulletproof Ability remain unaffected by this move. Should the Pokémon using the move withdraw from battle, the Speed-lowering effect ceases instantly. A distinct animation plays to signify the reduction of the target's Speed stat.

Notably, Syrup Bomb exhibits an altered visual appearance when employed by a Shiny Pokémon, a characteristic it shares with Mind Blown and Dragon Darts. This yellow coloration, encompassing both the initial strike and the lingering secondary effect, is exclusively observed when the move is executed by a Shiny Dipplin or a Shiny Hydrapple. Otherwise, the move's visual hue varies, seemingly influenced by the user's current appearance, Shininess, and species. For instance, when Syrup Bomb is utilized by any Pokémon other than Dipplin or Hydrapple (e.g., via Metronome or Sketch), it invariably displays a red coloration, irrespective of whether that Pokémon itself is Shiny. Furthermore, if a Pokémon, regardless of its own Shiny status, transforms into a Shiny Dipplin or Shiny Hydrapple and then uses Syrup Bomb, the initial attack will correctly appear yellow. However, the subsequent Speed-reducing effect applied at the close of each turn will revert to the standard non-Shiny red coloration.

Title: Electro Shot (move)

Known in Japanese as エレクトロビーム (Electro Beam), Electro Shot is an Electric-type attack that inflicts damage, making its debut in Generation IX with The Indigo Disk expansion. This technique serves as Archaludon's exclusive signature move. When chosen, Electro Shot initiates a two-turn process; on the initial turn, the user's Special Attack stat increases by one stage, accompanied by the on-screen message, "[User] absorbed electricity!". The subsequent turn sees the move inflict damage, with the previously gained Special Attack enhancement applied. Concurrently, its PP is expended, and it registers as the most recent move performed. After Electro Shot is activated, the Pokémon employing it is prevented from switching out unless the move is either interrupted or completely carried out. Should the weather condition be rain, Electro Shot bypasses its charging phase, executing instantly. However, if the move undergoes its charging turn, its execution will be prevented if the user is targeted by Disable during that period. Status conditions such as sleep or freezing, alongside flinching, will merely suspend Electro Shot's duration without canceling it outright. When the user holds a Power Herb, Electro Shot's entire process, including both charging and attacking stages, is completed within a single turn. In this scenario, the Pokémon charges, its Special Attack is boosted, the Power Herb is consumed, and the damaging phase of the move all occur in unison. The Special Attack increase remains active because the move's charging component is still considered to have taken place. Intriguingly, Electro Shot's damage output benefits from the Sheer Force ability, even though it possesses no secondary effects for Sheer Force to negate. Among Electric-type attacks, Electro Shot shares the distinction with Bolt Strike for possessing the highest base power, a comparison that excludes Z-Moves, Max Moves, and G-Max Moves.

Title: Tera Starstorm (move)

The offensive technique known as Tera Starstorm (テラクラスター Tera Cluster in Japanese) made its debut in Generation IX, specifically within The Indigo Disk expansion. This move is designed to inflict damage. It serves as the signature attack for the Pokémon Terapagos. As an offensive capability, Tera Starstorm consistently causes harm.
