Should Terapagos execute Tera Starstorm while in its Stellar Form, the attack's type transforms into Stellar, and it strikes both opposing Pokémon simultaneously during a Double Battle. When a user Terastallizes, Tera Starstorm's offensive category is determined dynamically: should the user's Attack stat exceed its Special Attack stat, the move transitions into a physical attack; otherwise, it maintains its status as a special attack. The comparison to ascertain the superior stat includes any active stat stage-modifiers, yet it disregards other influences such as held items (e.g., a Choice Band) or Abilities (e.g., Huge Power). Furthermore, the visual effect of Tera Starstorm transforms when its user has Terastallized.

Title: Burning Bulwark (move)

Known in Japanese as かえんのまもり (Blazing Protection), Burning Bulwark is Gouging Fire's exclusive signature move. This non-damaging Fire-type attack debuted in The Indigo Disk, a part of Generation IX. Upon activation, Burning Bulwark establishes a shield around the user, safeguarding it from all effects originating from both physical and special attacks directed at it for that turn. Should this protective barrier successfully intercept a contact-making attack, the assaulting Pokémon will suffer a burn affliction, provided it isn't immune. Importantly, any blocked moves fail to trigger secondary contact-dependent effects, such as those from Rocky Helmet or the Rough Skin Ability.

However, if the user acts last in the turn, the move's execution will inevitably fail. Furthermore, its chances of success diminish with each consecutive, successful deployment of Endure, any self-targetting protection move, Quick Guard, or Wide Guard. Each subsequent use divides the probability of success by a factor of 3. Certain attacks possess the capability to inflict damage even through Burning Bulwark. Specifically, moves known to breach protective measures, such as Feint, will deal their complete damage and simultaneously nullify Burning Bulwark's ongoing effects. The Unseen Fist Ability presents another exception, enabling contact-based moves to completely circumvent this defensive posture.

This move benefits from increased priority, typically resolving before the majority of other attacks within a turn. Notably, Burning Bulwark holds the distinction of being the sole protection move callable by Metronome. It is also unique as the only signature move belonging to a Paradox Pokémon that functions as a status move.

Title: Thunderclap (move)

Should you be searching for the Pokémon categorized as "Thunderclap," please refer to Zeraora. This particular move, Thunderclap (Japanese: じんらい), is an Electric-type damage-dealing attack that made its debut in Generation IX, specifically within The Indigo Disk. It stands as one of the signature techniques associated with Raging Bolt. Featuring a priority of +1, Thunderclap's execution is conditional: it will hit and deal damage if the designated target has selected either a physical or special attack and has not yet had the opportunity to perform that move. Otherwise, the move will not succeed. The efficacy of Thunderclap is solely determined by the target's *selected* action; it is entirely independent of whether that chosen attack can actually be *executed*, meaning factors such as sleep or freeze status conditions, or Abilities like Truant, do not prevent Thunderclap from activating. This move's title mirrors the category of Zeraora.

Title: Tachyon Cutter (move)

Hailing from Generation IX's The Indigo Disk, Tachyon Cutter (Japanese: タキオンカッター Tachyon Cutter) is a Steel-type, damage-dealing attack notable for its multistrike nature. This move serves as the distinctive signature technique of Iron Crown. Each use of Tachyon Cutter delivers two damaging strikes to inflict harm. Individually, every one of these hits possesses an identical probability of scoring a critical hit. Accuracy calculations are bypassed by this move, guaranteeing a hit in most scenarios, with the exception of targets currently in the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Should a Pokémon possessing the Sharpness ability utilize Tachyon Cutter, its power escalates by 50%. The second strike of Tachyon Cutter will occur even if the first blow obliterates a substitute. Only the second strike of Tachyon Cutter is taken into account by Mirror Coat. In instances where a Focus Sash activates prior to the final strike, the subsequent strike will result in the defending Pokémon's immediate fainting. Conversely, a Focus Band retains its capability to repeatedly prevent a knockout, yet each activation opportunity operates independently. When the user holds either a King's Rock or Razor Fang, every successive hit carries an identical likelihood of causing the adversary to flinch. Should the target possess the Stamina ability, every single strike will trigger its effect. Notably, Tachyon Cutter stands alone as the sole multistrike attack incapable of missing in standard combat situations. The term 'tachyon' refers to a theoretical particle hypothesized to travel at superluminal, or faster-than-light, velocities.

Title: Hard Press (move)

Hard Press, designated ハードプレス in Japanese, is a Steel-type damaging attack that first appeared in Generation IX as part of The Indigo Disk expansion. This move is obtainable as TM225 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Its damage output is determined by the target's remaining HP, with power fluctuating between 1 and 100 – the more HP the target possesses, the stronger the attack. Hard Press was unveiled on November 21st, 2023, during Serebii's hands-on preview writeup of the DLC, appearing alongside Supercell Slam prior to The Indigo Disk's release. Mechanically, it functions identically to Crush Grip and Wring Out, though it boasts a higher PP count and a lower achievable base power. Uniquely among these moves, Hard Press is not a Normal-type.

Title: Dragon Cheer (move)

Known in Japan as ドラゴンエール (Dragon Yell), Dragon Cheer is a non-damaging Dragon-type move that debuted in Generation IX as part of The Indigo Disk expansion. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it functions as Technical Machine TM226. This move boosts the critical-hit rate of the user's allies by one stage. However, for any affected Pokémon that possess the Dragon typing, this critical-hit advantage becomes two stages. The heightened critical-hit chance bestowed by Dragon Cheer can be replicated through abilities such as Psych Up, Transform, Costar, and Opportunist, as well as the item Mirror Herb. The determination of Dragon Cheer's effectiveness is based entirely on the allies' type at the moment the move is executed, remaining unaffected by any subsequent type alterations during the battle. Prior to version 3.0.1, a bug caused the critical-hit boost from Dragon Cheer to persist even if the affected Pokémon switched out and later returned to battle. The move will not execute successfully if there is no neighboring ally, or if the targeted ally is already benefiting from either Focus Energy or Dragon Cheer itself. Dipplin gains the ability to evolve into Hydrapple upon leveling up, provided it has learned Dragon Cheer. The presence of a defect in Scarlet and Violet version 3.0.0, which allowed the critical-hit increase to persist even after a Pokémon had departed the battlefield, led to a temporary prohibition of Dragon Cheer in sanctioned competitive events. This restriction was subsequently removed upon the debut of version 3.0.1. Despite its descriptive terminology and nomenclature, Dragon Cheer does not classify as a sound-based technique.

Title: Temper Flare (move)
Introduced in Generation IX within The Indigo Disk, Temper Flare (Japanese: やけっぱち Complete Desperation) is a damaging Fire-type attack. Trainers can obtain this move as TM207 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. While typically possessing a base power of 75, its potency escalates to 150 under specific conditions: if, on the preceding turn, the user's last executed move either missed, failed to affect each of its targets, or was prevented from being used due to an impediment such as paralysis. This power increase, however, is not triggered if the user was recharging from a previous action, or if any target successfully defended against the user's last move with a protection move like Protect. Temper Flare's Fire typing likely stems from the "やけ" (yake) component in its Japanese name, which translates to "burn." Its English name similarly incorporates a pun referencing "flare," whereas most other linguistic versions commonly employ a portmanteau.

Title: Supercell Slam (move)

Known in Japanese as サンダーダイブ (Thunder Dive), Supercell Slam made its debut as a damaging attack in Generation IX, specifically with the release of The Indigo Disk. Players can find it as TM210 within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. While Supercell Slam inflicts damage upon its opponent, a failed attempt to hit results in recoil. Should the user miss, they incur crash damage equivalent to half of their maximum HP, rounded down. Furthermore, if the target possesses type or Ability-based immunity (for example, Volt Absorb) to Supercell Slam, or if they are protected, the attack is invariably treated as a miss. Against a target that has employed Minimize, Supercell Slam's power doubles, and it unfailingly connects by circumventing standard accuracy checks. This guaranteed hit exception applies only if the foe is within the semi-invulnerable phase of actions like Dig or Fly. Its initial reveal occurred on November 21, 2023, appearing alongside Hard Press before The Indigo Disk's official launch. This happened during Serebii's hands-on preview writeup for the DLC, where it was, perhaps mistakenly, identified as Supercharged Cell. Notably, Supercell Slam stands alone as the sole crash-damage-inflicting move that isn't Fighting-type, and equally unique for lacking "kick" in its designation.

Title: Psychic Noise (move)

Psychic Noise (Japanese: サイコノイズ Psycho Noise) is an offensive Psychic-type attack, first introduced in Generation IX's The Indigo Disk. It can be found as TM228 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move deals damage and subsequently prevents the target from recovering HP for a period of two turns. During these two turns, should the target attempt to use an HP-restoring move, such as Recover or Drain Punch, that move will fail. The Heal Block component of this attack is regarded as an additional effect, meaning it is negated by Shield Dust and suppressed by Sheer Force. The Ability Aroma Veil also prevents this secondary effect from taking hold. Activating Psychic Noise again while the Heal Block is already in effect will not cause its duration to reset.

Title: Upper Hand (move)

Known in Japanese as はやてがえし (Quick Hand Reversal), Upper Hand is a damage-dealing Fighting-type attack that made its debut in Generation IX with The Indigo Disk expansion. Trainers can acquire it as TM229 in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. This move possesses a priority bracket of +3. Its effect dictates that if the opposing Pokémon has chosen an offensive move (either physical or special) with a priority level between +1 and +3 (including Upper Hand itself) and has not yet acted, Upper Hand will strike first, causing the target to flinch. Otherwise, the move will simply fail. It also functions against abilities that grant priority to moves, such as Gale Wings. Within Pokémon GO, Upper Hand is categorized as a Charged Attack and has been available since April 13, 2025. Notably, Pokémon like Samurott, Cobalion, Terrakion, Virizion, Keldeo, Talonflame, Falinks, and Flamigo are able to learn Upper Hand despite their lack of actual hands.

Title: Shadow Rush (move)

Shadow Rush, known in Japanese as ダークラッシュ (Dark Rush), is a unique offensive technique exclusively available in the titles Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. In Pokémon Colosseum, it functions as the game's only Shadow move, with all captured Shadow Pokémon inherently knowing it. Conversely, within Pokémon XD, Shadow Rush is merely one of multiple Shadow moves. Consistent with all Shadow moves, it is permanently forgotten and substituted with an alternate move once the Pokémon undergoes purification.

This attack inflicts direct damage, possessing a base power of 90. The user simultaneously takes recoil damage equivalent to ¹⁄₁₆ of its maximum HP, which is consistently rounded down. The exact recoil amount can randomly be either the calculated result or one point higher or lower. Notably, the ability Rock Head does not prevent this recoil damage.

When a Shadow Pokémon uses Shadow Rush while in Hyper Mode, it gains a substantial 90.625% chance to land a critical hit. More specifically, an additional 90% critical hit chance is applied, behaving similarly to an added effect. Should this heightened probability not trigger, the move still retains the standard 6.25% chance for a critical hit. Despite Shadow moves not gaining super effective status against any type in Colosseum, Shadow Rush is still capable of damaging a Pokémon protected by the Wonder Guard ability.

Due to the absence of Pokémon trading and Pokémon Contests in Colosseum and XD, Shadow Rush cannot be utilized in Contests. However, its summary screen entry still displays Contest-related information, showing both Appeal and Jam scores as 0, with a blank space designated for the Contest condition.

In later contexts, Shadow Rush's base power is reduced to 55, and it ceases to inflict recoil damage. Hyper Mode itself no longer exists, and its counterpart, Reverse Mode, does not provide any critical hit ratio increase for this move. Additionally, the game eventually stops displaying Contest descriptions for any moves whatsoever.

All Shadow Pokémon in Pokémon Colosseum possess this move from the moment they are obtained. A specific glitch present in Pokémon Colosseum allows Shadow Rush to successfully strike an opponent during the semi-invulnerable turn of moves such as Fly, Bounce, Dig, and Dive. This phenomenon occurs only if the Shadow Pokémon is in Hyper Mode and first attempts to use the corresponding move: Thunder for Fly, Surf for Bounce, Earthquake for Dig, or Magnitude for Dive.

Title: Shadow Blast (move)

Shadow Blast (Japanese: ダークブラスト Dark Blast) is an exclusive attack among the various Shadow moves, available only in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Consistent with other Shadow moves, it is forgotten and replaced by another move once the Pokémon is purified. This move serves as the signature technique for Shadow Lugia. Shadow Blast represents a corrupted, Shadow variant of Aeroblast, inflicting damage and featuring an elevated critical hit rate. Due to the inability to trade Shadow Pokémon, and the absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Blast is ineligible for Contest usage. Paradoxically, despite being based on Aeroblast, Shadow Blast transmutes into Psycho Boost when Shadow Lugia is purified. Visually, this move appears to be special; however, it is classified as a physical move, a characteristic derived from Aeroblast also being a physical move in Generation III. Conceptually, it parallels Aeroblast+, the signature move of Apex Shadow Lugia in Pokémon GO. Both are enhanced iterations of Aeroblast, exclusive to a specific individual Shadow Pokémon specimen.

Title: Shadow Blitz (move)

Shadow Blitz (Japanese: ダークアタック Dark Attack) is a distinct Shadow-type attack exclusively featured in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. In line with other Shadow moves, once the Pokémon undergoes purification, this technique is removed from its known moves and superseded by an alternative. This particular ability functions as the Shadow counterpart to the basic offensive maneuvers Pokémon commonly learn at Level 1, such as Pound, Scratch, and Tackle. Shadow Blitz causes direct damage and possesses no supplementary effects. Due to the inability to trade Shadow Pokémon and the complete absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Blitz cannot be utilized in Contests.

Title: Shadow Bolt (move)
Appearing solely in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Bolt (Japanese: ダークサンダー Dark Thunder) is one of a collection of special Shadow moves. Consistent with its Shadow move brethren, this attack is lost and substituted with an alternative once the Shadow Pokémon completes its purification process. Shadow Zapdos commands this move as its unique signature technique. Its nature makes it the Shadow variant of Thunderbolt. This attack inflicts direct damage and also carries a 10% likelihood of paralyzing the opponent. Given the untradeable status of Shadow Pokémon and the complete absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Bolt finds itself ineligible for Contest participation. Among all Shadow moves, it holds the sole distinction of being able to induce paralysis.

Title: Shadow Chill (move)

Shadow Chill, known in Japanese as ダークフリーズ (Dark Freeze), is among the unique Shadow moves accessible solely within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Consistent with other Shadow moves, it is permanently forgotten and substituted with a different technique once the Shadow Pokémon undergoes purification. This move functions as the distinctive signature attack of Shadow Articuno. Effectively, Shadow Chill serves as the Shadow equivalent of Ice Beam, dealing damage and presenting a 10% likelihood of inflicting the frozen status condition on the target. Given that Shadow Pokémon cannot be traded, and Pokémon Contests are absent from Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Chill is consequently unusable in such Contest events. Furthermore, it holds the unique distinction of being the only Shadow move capable of causing freezing.

Title: Shadow End (move)

Exclusively featured in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow End (Japanese: ダークエンド Dark End) stands as one among multiple Shadow moves. Consistent with other Shadow moves, a Pokémon loses access to Shadow End, which is supplanted by a different technique, upon its purification. When executed, Shadow End inflicts damage upon the target, while concurrently causing the user to suffer recoil damage equivalent to 50% of its current HP. Notably, the Ability Rock Head provides no immunity against this specific recoil damage. Its non-tradable nature as a Shadow Pokémon move, coupled with the absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, renders Shadow End unusable in such competitions. Boasting a formidable 120 base power, Shadow End surpasses all other Shadow moves in strength; however, its accuracy is remarkably low at 60%, a figure it shares with Shadow Panic, marking them as the least accurate. Among all Shadow moves present in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow End stands alone as the sole technique that inflicts recoil damage on its user. Its usage is restricted solely to Shadow Pokémon belonging to Greevil and his two lieutenants, Ardos and Eldes, who also serve as his sons.

Title: Shadow Fire (move)

Known in Japanese as ダークファイア (Dark Fire), Shadow Fire stands as one among several special Shadow attacks exclusively found within the game Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. This particular technique, like all other Shadow moves, vanishes from a Pokémon's repertoire, being substituted by an alternative move upon the creature's purification. Notably, it serves as the unique signature attack for Shadow Moltres. Shadow Fire functions as the Shadow counterpart to Flamethrower, inflicting damage and possessing a one-in-ten probability of inflicting a burn status on its target. Due to the inability to trade Shadow Pokémon and the absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Fire is consequently unusable in such events. A distinct characteristic of Shadow Fire is its sole capability among all Shadow moves to induce the burning status condition.

Title: Shadow Storm (move)
Exclusive to Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Storm (Japanese: ダークストーム, or Dark Storm) is among the game's various Shadow moves. Following the pattern of other Shadow moves, purification causes it to be forgotten and substituted with a different attack. The inability to trade Shadow Pokémon, combined with the complete absence of Pokémon Contests in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, prevents Shadow Storm from any Contest usage. Its combat performance matches Surf's, featuring identical power, accuracy, and targeting within Pokémon XD, the sole title where this move is encountered.

Title: Shadow Down (move)
Among the various Shadow moves found exclusively in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness is Shadow Down (Japanese: ダークダウン Dark Down). This technique, like other Shadow abilities, is discarded and substituted with a different move once the Pokémon achieves purification. Shadow Down acts as the corrupted counterpart to Screech. Its effect is to reduce the Defense stat of each opposing Pokémon by two stages. Given that Shadow Pokémon cannot be exchanged, and Pokémon Contests are absent from Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Down is therefore unusable in such competitions.

Title: Shadow Half (move)

This particular Shadow move, known in Japan as ダークハーフ (Dark Half), is exclusively found within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, where it stands as one among several such abilities. Consistent with other Shadow abilities, Shadow Half vanishes from a Pokémon's move set upon its purification, giving way to a different technique. Its primary effect is to inflict damage equivalent to half (50%) of the current hit points (HP) of every single combatant present, including the Pokémon that initiated the move. This damage is always rounded down. Notably, this move is incapable of causing any Pokémon to faint. Following its execution, the user must spend the subsequent turn recharging, unable to act. Should the user be afflicted by the status condition Taunt, Shadow Half becomes unusable. The damage inflicted by Shadow Half is recognized as an offensive action for the mechanics of Bide, Mirror Coat (though not Counter), and Focus Punch. Due to the inability to trade Shadow Pokémon and the complete absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Half is not a move that can be showcased in such events. Uniquely, Shadow Half stands alone as the sole damaging technique capable of affecting every single Pokémon present on the battlefield, the initiator included.

Title: Shadow Hold (move)

Shadow Hold, known in Japanese as ダークホールド (Dark Hold), is one of several Shadow moves that players can only encounter in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. In line with other Shadow moves, a Pokémon will forget Shadow Hold, gaining a different move in its place, once it has undergone purification. Functionally, Shadow Hold mirrors the effects of Spider Web, Mean Look, and Block, serving as their Shadow variant. This move targets all opposing Pokémon on the field, preventing them from being switched out of battle. The trapping effect, however, is contingent upon the Pokémon that used Shadow Hold remaining active in combat. Despite being affected, Pokémon can still be forced to exit the battle if hit by Roar or Whirlwind. Additionally, an affected Pokémon can still switch out using Baton Pass, but in such a scenario, the Pokémon that enters the battle will then become trapped by Shadow Hold. Due to the inability to trade Shadow Pokémon and the absence of Pokémon Contests in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Hold is consequently unusable in Contest events. Shadow Hold holds the distinction of being the only trapping move that targets every opponent, without affecting allies.

Title: Shadow Mist (move)

Bearing the Japanese name ダークミスト (Dark Mist), Shadow Mist is counted among the exclusive Shadow moves obtainable solely in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. In alignment with other Shadow techniques, it vanishes from a Pokémon's repertoire, being superseded by a different move upon purification. It functions as the Shadow counterpart to Sweet Scent, causing a two-stage reduction in the evasion of both opposing Pokémon. The inability to trade Shadow Pokémon, combined with the absence of Pokémon Contests in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, renders Shadow Mist ineligible for use in such events.

Title: Shadow Panic (move)

Introduced exclusively in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Panic (known in Japanese as ダークパニック, or Dark Panic) stands as one of the specialized Shadow attacks. Consistent with other techniques of its kind, a Pokémon loses access to Shadow Panic, which is then substituted by a different move, upon achieving purification. As a sound-based technique, Shadow Panic is rendered ineffectual against Pokémon possessing the Soundproof ability. The inability to trade Shadow Pokémon, coupled with the absence of Pokémon Contests within Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, precludes Shadow Panic's use in such competitive events. Notably, it holds the distinction of being the sole Shadow move classified as sound-based.

Title: Shadow Shed (move)

Exclusively featured in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Shed (known in Japanese as ダークリムーブ Dark Remove) stands among the game's unique roster of Shadow moves. Consistent with other Shadow attacks, this technique is lost, making way for a different move, as soon as the Pokémon undergoes purification. Its primary function involves nullifying the active Safeguard, Reflect, and Light Screen effects present on the opposing team's side of the battlefield. Given that Shadow Pokémon are untradable, and Pokémon Contests are absent from Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Shadow Shed is consequently unavailable for use in Contest events.

Title: Shadow Sky (move)

Shadow Sky (Japanese: ダークウェザー Dark Weather) stands as one of several Shadow-type attacks exclusively accessible in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. As with other Shadow techniques, it is erased from a Pokémon's memory and replaced by an alternative move following purification. Shadow Sky conjures a shadowy atmosphere across the battleground, persisting for five rounds. While active, all Pokémon not of the Shadow type (barring those in the semi-invulnerable phase of Dig or Dive) sustain damage equivalent to 1/16 of their maximum HP at the conclusion of each turn. Additionally, Shadow moves benefit from a 50% power enhancement (though this effect is not stated within the game), and Weather Ball changes to become typeless while its power is doubled.
